# BOOK European Spatial Planning and Territorial Cooperation

## S. 1

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 2

European Spatial Planning and 
Territorial Cooperation
There is a strong international dimension to spatial planning. European integration strengthens
interconnections, development and decision-making across national and regional borders. European
Union (EU) policies in areas such as environment, transport, agriculture and regional policy have farreaching effects on spatial development patterns and planning procedures. Planners in the EU are now
routinely engaged in cooperation across national borders to share and devise effective ways of
intervening in the way our cities, towns and rural areas develop. In short, the EU has become an
important framework for planning practice, research and teaching. Spatial planning in Europe is being
‘Europeanised’, with corresponding changes for the role of planners.
This book explores:
•
large-scale spatial development trends and their inﬂuence on planning;
•
the nature, institutions and actors of the EU from a planning perspective;
•
the history of spatial planning at the transnational scale;
•
the planning tools, perspectives, visions and programmes supporting European cooperation on
spatial planning;
•
the territorial impacts of the EU’s sector policies; and
•
the outcomes of European spatial planning in practice.
Written for students, academics, practitioners and researchers of spatial planning and related
disciplines, this book is essential reading for everybody interested in engaging with the European
dimension of spatial planning and territorial governance.
Stefanie Dühr is Associate Professor in the Department of Spatial Planning, Radboud University
Nijmegen. She holds a Ph.D. in spatial planning from the University of the West of England, Bristol,
and a geography degree from the University of Trier. Her main research interests are comparative
planning systems, cartographic representations in spatial planning, and European territorial
cooperation. She is the author of The Visual Language of Spatial Planning, and editor of the policy
debates section of the journal Regional Studies.
Claire Colomb is Lecturer in Urban Sociology and European Spatial Planning at the Bartlett School
of Planning, University College London. She holds a degree in politics and sociology from the Institut
d’Etudes Politiques de Paris and a Ph.D. in planning from University College London, and previously
worked for the European transnational cooperation programme INTERREG IIIB. Her research
interests include comparative urban governance, planning and urban policies in European cities, urban
sociology, and European territorial cooperation.
Vincent Nadin is Professor of Spatial Planning and Strategy in the Department of Urbanism, Faculty
of Architecture, Delft University of Technology. He is a visiting researcher at Leibniz University,
Hannover. His main research interest is comparative planning systems. He is joint author of Town
and Country Planning in the UK, and editor of the journal Planning Practice and Research.

## S. 20

Preface
It is often argued that there is no EU competence for ‘spatial planning’, but this ignores the great
inﬂuence of EU sectoral policies on patterns of spatial development. These sectoral policies with
a spatial impact can be seen as forming a de facto EU spatial planning policy. EU sectoral policies
and legislation are agreed by the EU member states and then mostly implemented though
national legislation. However, although the effects on the organisation of spatial planning
systems, policies and practices have been considerable, their EU origin often becomes forgotten
during the process of implementation. It is undeniable that the EU has become an important
actor for inﬂuencing spatial planning decisions and shaping the spatial development of the
European territory.
Many aspects of spatial development cross administrative boundaries. Joint responses to
shared development problems across national borders are especially, and increasingly, called for
in the EU single market. Planning actors are often confronted with institutional, administrative
and cultural challenges when cooperating with colleagues in other European regions, and
territorial cooperation is not always given the required political support. However, acknowledging and understanding the European dimension to spatial development is fundamental 
for institutions and actors involved in spatial planning or responsible for sectoral policies with
a territorial dimension, such as environment, economic development, urban regeneration or
transport. Indeed, as a recently published report proclaimed: ‘Territory matters!’ (ESPON
2006a). Our motivation for writing this book derives from the same understanding, namely that
policy-making within the EU, at all levels of scale, across policy sectors and across national
borders, is best considered through a ‘spatial lens’, with spatial planning providing an important
instrument with which policy coherence can be improved.
For anyone involved in teaching students about the European dimension of spatial planning,
there are challenges in ﬁnding suitable study literature. This is despite the remarkable number
of references available on the topics of European spatial planning and territorial cooperation.
However, much of this literature has become rather specialised, and for students it is not always
easy to make the relevant connections in this dynamic and increasingly complex and diverse ﬁeld.
There had not been a comprehensive textbook on the subject since Williams’s 1996 European
Union Spatial Policy and Planning. The idea for this book was born out of our own needs for
an up-to-date, critical textbook on European spatial planning and territorial cooperation
informed by the latest available research. We hope that other lecturers, researchers and
practitioners involved in European issues will also ﬁnd this to be a useful source.
Writing a book on a subject as multi-faceted as European spatial planning and territorial
cooperation, by necessity, imposes limitations on the level of detail that can be provided. For
the reader interested in gaining a deeper understanding on certain topics, references for further
reading are provided at the end of each chapter. There may, of course, be readers who have 
a much more critical view on the ‘European project’ than the one we give in this textbook.

## S. 21

We have tried to give a balanced view of the potentials and challenges that come with European
integration. Our own conclusion is that the beneﬁts for individuals and institutions outweigh
the disadvantages, and that spatial planners in Europe should embrace the opportunities by
engaging with the EU institutions and cooperating with other partners in Europe.
EU policy-making is a dynamic ﬁeld, and given the scope of the initiatives covered in this
book there are changes occurring constantly. Their impact on the ‘European spatial planning
project’ may be variable, but because of the dynamic nature of the topic of European spatial
planning and territorial cooperation, spatial planners in Europe have to be on full alert at all
times. For this textbook, every care has been taken to present information that is up to date to
autumn 2009. Where changes may occur in the near future, as, for example, through the entry
into force of the Lisbon Treaty, references to the expected differences have been made wherever
possible and feasible. However, new policy developments may have occurred since the
completion of the manuscript. The reader is invited to consult the accompanying website to the
book, which provides additional information and updates on EU policies and developments.
The three authors of this book have been involved in research, higher education and practice
in the ﬁeld of European spatial planning and territorial cooperation for many years. In writing
this book, we sought to complement the critical review of the literature with our previous
research and professional (as well as sometimes personal) experiences. Today, all three of us live
and work in a European country other than the one we originate from, and our experiences of
working in and with different planning cultures have undoubtedly shaped this book. We
believe that despite the limitations, uncertainty and unintended consequences surrounding
public intervention through spatial planning, it is nonetheless an absolute primary requirement
of government if we are to address the critical consequences of poor organisation of our
territories and the environmental, economic and social challenges of the twenty-ﬁrst century.
Inadequately guided and unregulated spatial development that relies on market forces is a recipe
for environmental damage and regressive social and economic impacts. We accept that where
it is done poorly, planning can be part of the problem, but we also believe that we have no
alternative but to try to do the job better. And Europeans need to do it together.

## S. 22

Acknowledgements
Writing a textbook such as this requires much time and effort, in particular since the book
involved as much ‘European planning’ and transboundary cooperation among the three authors
as its title implies! We therefore thank our families and friends for their patience and support,
and our colleagues in our home institutions for their encouragement and their interest in this
work. We would also like to thank our students, who engaged with us in lively debates and
discussions on European spatial planning and territorial cooperation and provided us with
inspiration to write this book, in the course of several years of teaching on the topic in the UK,
France, Germany and the Netherlands. We thank our editor, Catherine Lynn at Taylor &
Francis, for her patience and for keeping up her enthusiasm for our book over many months.
As a source of information for this book, we would like to acknowledge a project
commissioned and funded by the UK Department of Environment, Transport and the Regions
(now Department for Communities and Local Government) in 2000–01, entitled A Research
Study in European Spatial Planning. The project, initiated by John Zetter and directed by Prof.
Harry Dimitriou at University College London, entailed the design and curriculum development
of a master’s programme in European Spatial Planning and was prepared by Claire Colomb with
contributions from Prof. Simin Davoudi and assistance from Richard Oades. Many chapters 
of this book have also been inspired and informed by our previous collective research efforts in
the ﬁeld of European spatial planning. There are too many funding bodies and individuals to
mention individually, but we are thankful for all opportunities that have occurred over the years
and the support we have received to allow us to pursue our research agendas.

## S. 30

Introducing the European
dimension of spatial 
planning
Territory matters.
(ESPON 2006a)
The inﬂuence of the European Union (EU) on the spatial development of its member states (MS)
and regions is ever increasing, and parts of national spatial planning policy and procedures are
now of EU origin. This is remarkable because to this day the EU does not have any competence
for spatial planning. However, there are many EU sectoral policies such as in the ﬁelds of
environment, agriculture and transport have a ‘spatial impact’ or are implemented through
domestic planning law and practice. The member states have also accepted the positive beneﬁts
of cooperating on the European dimension of spatial development. Spatial planning ministries
from the EU member states have been working together for many years to agree joint spatial
development strategies for the European territory. There is considerable EU funding available
to support cooperation on spatial development across national borders and to research spatial
development trends and policy impacts.
As a result many thousands of professionals in the built and natural environment at local,
regional and national level are now routinely involved in the European dimension of spatial
development and planning, mostly in cooperation with colleagues in other countries. Introductions to EU policies and actions are now commonly taught in many university courses dealing
with spatial planning, urban and rural development, geography, environmental management,
property and business, and other subjects. The European dimension of spatial planning has
become an essential part of the work of planning practitioners and academics.
In this part of the book, the EU as a planning subject is introduced and the structure of the
book explained. In Chapter 2, an overview of the arguments for and against European spatial
policy and planning is given. As the terminology for spatial planning varies greatly across
Europe, reﬂecting different understandings of spatial planning and spatial policy, Chapter 3 is
dedicated to the language and terminology issues that planners face when they engage in EU
policy-making or cooperation with actors in other regions.

## S. 31

Introducing the European Union 
as a planning subject
All in all, in the past decade, the new institutional context posed by the EU has fundamentally
changed the relationship between Member States and their territory, despite the lack of a formal
European competency to engage in spatial planning. Although it certainly remains necessary to
conduct spatial policy at the national level – if for no other reason than to coordinate EU sectoral
policies and integrate them into the planning system – doing so without regard to the growing
inﬂuence of Brussels will doom it to failure.
(van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 9)
Introduction
The EU of today is a global power to be reckoned with, while at the same time it has become
an important inﬂuence on most aspects of everyday life of its citizens. The EU is a unique
organisation of nation-states. Its membership has grown considerably over the years, from
initially six members in the European Coal and Steel Community (ECSC), which was established
in 1951, to currently 27 member states (see Figure 1.1). In 2009, it covered a territory of 4.32
million square kilometres and had altogether almost 500 million inhabitants. The EU produces
approximately 30 per cent of the world gross domestic product (GDP), and in terms of
population and economic power has now overtaken the USA.
Alongside changes to its membership, there has been a gradual expansion of EU competences
in an increasing number of policy areas over the years. The EU today has a considerable inﬂuence
on most areas of policy-making and decision-making in its member states. A number of
provisions from the EU sectoral policies with a spatial content, such as environment, transport,
agriculture and regional policy, are implemented through national and regional planning
systems. In response to the procedural and substantive requirements of such EU initiatives, the
organisation and practice of spatial planning has changed in many member states. Examples 
are the EU requirement to undertake environmental impact assessments (EIA) for large scale
developments and strategic environmental assessments (SEA) for spatial planning instruments
(as explained in Chapter 21). EU policy also has an inﬂuence on spatial development in European regions. Transnational transport connections, such as the Öresund (or Øresund) Bridge
(supported in policy and ﬁnance by the EU) (Figure 1.2), or the designation of areas of nature
protection through the Natura 2000 network (created under EU environmental law), are
examples of how EU policy has been used by member states and regions in their land use or
territorial management.
The spatial development impacts of these sectoral policies may be positive or negative or both.
A new railway connection supported by European policy and funds may encourage new
investment in one location and divert it from another. Support for regional airports may improve
Chapter 1
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 32

the accessibility of peripheral areas but also create environmental problems. Agricultural subsidies may improve the income of some farmers but contribute to the depopulation of certain
rural areas and an over-intensive use of land. Many policies and actions will have unintended
side effects that lead to less than desirable spatial development patterns or shift the problems
from one region to the next. While it may be inevitable that such problems are created, the issue
is that they are not considered in the policy-making process. That considerable ‘costs of noncoordination’ (Robert et al. 2001) may be resulting from the contradictory spatial effects of EU
sectoral policies is now commonly recognised, as explained in Chapter 2. The question of how
policy integration can best be achieved and coordinated across the territory has consequently
over past years become an important part of the discussions between European institutions and
the member states.
Besides sectoral policies, institutional and geographical changes in the EU play an important
role in shaping planning policies and practices. More than 50 years of economic and social

## S. 33

integration in the pursuit of a ‘Europe without frontiers’ have led to the establishment of a Single
European Market (SEM) and Economic and Monetary Union (EMU), and giving rise to many
intended and unintended spatial development effects (see Chapter 17). The freedoms of the
Single European Market give EU citizens the right to study, live, work and buy a house in another
country, and companies the opportunity to settle anywhere in the EU with few restrictions.
National borders, in the context of ongoing European integration, have been increasingly
perceived as barriers. For planners they can present a considerable challenge in the search for
appropriate responses to large-scale spatial development trends. There are many issues that 
may be best tackled at the transnational or European scale and through cooperation across
national borders, such as interregional transport connections, environmental pollution and the
protection of natural habitats, or water management in the catchment areas of large rivers. The
severe river ﬂoods of the Rhine and Danube that have affected many regions in several member
states over recent years illustrate the necessity for cooperation on the use and management of
space across several countries. Moreover, regions in Europe have become increasingly interconnected, as the growth in infrastructure and transport volumes across the EU demonstrates.
Many EU citizens have bought second homes in another country, thereby contributing to
increasing passenger transport flows and driving up property prices elsewhere in the EU 
(Figure 1.3).
For spatial planners this means that even standard planning tasks, such as residential development, may be signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by factors that originate from other countries as well as
the changes within their own locality or region. The European dimension of spatial planning
arises from a recognised and growing need for the coordination of spatial development trends

## S. 34

and EU spatial policy across policy sectors, across levels of government from the EU to the local
level, and across national borders. Planners in Europe increasingly operate in an interdisciplinary
and international multi-level governance system. Built and natural environment professionals
in many ﬁelds are now routinely engaged in cooperation on spatial development issues with
partners in other regions and countries. Considerable EU funding has been made available to
support such cooperation on spatial planning across national borders, which is now referred to
as ‘territorial cooperation’. Also, since the 1980s spatial planners from across Europe have been
collaborating on spatial development strategies for the European continent as a whole (CEMAT
2000) and for the EU territory (CSD 1999, EU Ministers 2007a).
European spatial planning has thus emerged as a response to the need to address real
problems that are shared across countries, or problems that spread across geographical borders
over large parts of the EU territory (such as water and air pollution). It is also promoted as a
way to help reconcile the potentially contradictory core EU objectives of economic competitiveness, social cohesion and sustainable development. However, not everyone agrees with the 
view that there should be some form of EU intervention in spatial development. Some member
states and regions observe the increasing role of EU institutions in activities related to spatial
policy with unease and argue that there is no justification for such wide-ranging public

## S. 35

intervention. Others are concerned that growing competences at EU level may result in shifts
of power in decision-making and responsibilities for spatial development matters away from
national, regional or local levels – thus weakening their position in policy- and decision-making.
These issues are examined in the next chapter. The reality for spatial planners in Europe today
is, nonetheless, that engaging with the European institutions and with other partners in Europe,
and more fully understanding the European dimension of spatial development, has become an
important part of their work.
Why study European spatial policy and planning?
European spatial planning is a new, signiﬁcant and exciting ﬁeld of research and teaching. It is
not surprising therefore that its study has grown quickly alongside the development of practice,
such that it has become a standard component of the planning curriculum in many universities
across Europe. It also presents considerable challenges to the student, teacher and researcher,
not least because it involves a need for cross-cultural and multi-disciplinary understanding. But
this is what makes the subject so intriguing. Increasing transboundary cooperation in the ﬁeld
of spatial planning has brought to the fore national and regional differences in the understanding
of what spatial planning is about and how it is conducted (CEC 1997a). The motto of the EU,
‘united in diversity’, could be applied equally to the diversity of planning systems in its member
states and the different disciplines involved in spatial planning. For practitioners and researchers
engaged in spatial planning in Europe, it is thus of increasing importance to understand how
member states have and are working together on spatial planning, how they bring their different
experiences of planning to this task, and the potential beneﬁts and challenges of cooperation
on spatial development across national borders. The increasing interdependence of nations is
inevitable and is to be welcomed if the distinctiveness and variety of regional and national
settings can be maintained at the same time. A spatial planning approach has a part to play in
ensuring that diversity in the EU is maintained while the member states join forces to tackle the
urgent problems of spatial development.
Spatial policy initiatives at EU level have led planning researchers and scholars to increasingly
consider the European dimension of spatial planning, to study the effects of a ‘Europeanisation’
of spatial planning (Dühr et al. 2007) and to develop a comparative perspective on planning
systems and policies in Europe. Planning practitioners and other stakeholders are increasingly
confronted by European-scale issues. It is not possible to understand the nature of spatial
development and appropriate intervention in domestic terms only, because of the interconnections between countries and and their joint work through the EU. European-wide collaboration
on spatial planning and urban development has provided a forum to achieve a better
understanding of the role and principles of spatial planning and important benchmarks against
which to compare and stimulate innovation in domestic planning practice.
Within the exciting and growing ﬁeld of European spatial planning and territorial cooperation, there are many opportunities for practitioners and academics to use their knowledge and
understanding of the European and international policy context. Areas of activity for graduates
with knowledge in the ﬁeld of European spatial planning and skills in other European languages
are not restricted by national boundaries, and ‘European spatial planners’ can work in many
parts of the public sector, the private sector and higher education across Europe. This book will
be of relevance to students and researchers interested in European issues in the ﬁelds of urban,
regional, rural and spatial planning, geography, economic development and public policy. It will
also assist present and future practitioners working in different sectors and types of organisation
to increase their understanding of the European dimension of planning and territorial
development (see Table 1.1).

## S. 37

The scope and structure of the book
This book gives an overview of spatial development and spatial policies in Europe with a focus
on the inﬂuence of the activities and policies of the European Union. It provides substantive
information and conceptual tools for understanding the geographical, institutional and planning
perspectives on European integration. While EU initiatives on spatial development and the
relevance of EU policy for planning in the member states form the core focus of this book, 
the wider geographical context for European spatial planning and policy is given due attention.
For example, the activities of other organisations such as the Council of Europe, which has a
much larger membership than the EU and has been promoting cooperation across national
borders and action on spatial development for longer than the EU institutions, are crucial 
to comprehending the European dimension of spatial planning. Cooperation between the EU
and non-member states such as Iceland, Norway, Switzerland and Liechtenstein, which are represented through the European Free Trade Association (EFTA), and negotiations with accession
countries that wish to join the EU in future are all important aspects for a better understanding
of the role of the EU in spatial development.
As the ﬁeld of European spatial planning is highly dynamic and characterised by ongoing
developments in EU policy and legislation, institutional change and EU enlargements, a book
on the topic can never be complete and fully up to date. All important aspects up to 1 December
2009 (when the Lisbon Treaty entered into force) were considered when writing this book.
This book discusses the European dimension of spatial development and planning, and other
relevant activities of the EU. Part II presents the spatial development context for European spatial
policy and introduces the conceptualisations of the European space that have been developed
since the 1980s and inﬂuenced the thinking about European spatial development. Part II also
includes an overview of EU spatial data sets that have become available and that can help
planners to analyse and visualise spatial development trends in Europe.
The institutional framework for European spatial policy making is explained in Part III, which
provides an introduction to theoretical approaches that can help us to understand the processes
of European integration and their relevance for spatial planning and spatial development. Part
III further presents the key EU institutions and other European actors. The policy- and decisionmaking processes in the EU’s system of multi-level governance are explained, and the changing
membership and competences of the EU over time are put in perspective.
Part IV explains the emergence of the EU and of Europe as a ‘planning subject’ in a historical
perspective and discusses whether a ‘model’ of European spatial planning is emerging. It
reviews the shifts in European planning theory and practice and the implications this has for
the way the territory is governed. The instruments of European spatial planning are presented,
which include strategies, visions, programmes and projects.
In Part V, the various EU policy ﬁelds that have major territorial and spatial impacts on EU
regions and cities and planning systems are discussed. The focus is on economic policies, regional
(or cohesion) policy, transport, agricultural and environmental policies, and their inﬂuences on
spatial development and the organisation of spatial planning in the member states.
Part VI provides a discussion of how governance arrangements and the organisation of spatial
planning of nation-states, regions and local authorities have changed as a consequence of their
engagement with the EU and other European partners. Institutional changes are also having an
effect on the culture of planning in EU countries. The effects of a Europeanisation of planning
and of the planning profession, which can be observed as a result of territorial cooperation and
European integration processes, are therefore also discussed in Part VI. The book concludes 
with a critical reﬂection on the need for a European dimension of spatial planning and for a
‘spatialisation’ of European policy.
8
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 38

Arguing for and against European
spatial planning
The arguments for planning . . . cannot be taken as a defence of the status quo in planning but must
serve as a challenge to the profession to learn from its mistakes and build on new and expanded
conceptions of the public interest, information and political action to realize its ultimate potential.
(Klosterman 1985: 13)
Introduction
Why do we cooperate on spatial planning across national borders? Why is spatial planning
needed at the European level? Every chapter in this book contributes in some way to ﬁnding
answers to these simple but rather awkward questions. Some answers are straightforward; others
are more involved. Rarely are they uncontested, and inevitably they beg questions about the very
nature of ‘European spatial planning’. Arguments about whether there is need for spatial
planning at this scale are often intertwined with competing views about its role and scope.
This chapter rehearses the main underlying arguments for and against the idea of spatial
planning at the European and transnational scales and points out some common fallacies. The
discussion introduces recurring themes in the short history of European spatial planning,
particularly in relation to the key concepts of competence, subsidiarity and transnationality.
These and other points are intended to provide tools for reasoning about spatial planning action
at the European scale. The elementary approach is deliberate. Much of the argument that takes
place in and about European spatial planning is much more elaborate, cross-cutting and
potentially confusing. It is bound up with broader questions about European integration and
the nature of the EU, and more detailed questions about the role and scope of planning. Some
of that elaboration follows in later chapters, particularly on the importance of European-wide
spatial development trends in Chapter 4, theories of European integration in Chapters 7 and 8,
explanations of the notion of European spatial planning in Chapter 13, and the territorial
impacts of other EU policies in Part V. In this chapter the main task is to present the main lines
of argument and to foster a critical viewpoint so that the notion of European spatial planning
is not taken for granted.
From time to time, most countries need to cooperate with others across national boundaries
on spatial development and planning issues. A pragmatic approach is needed to manage
development, for example, when transport infrastructure crosses borders into neighbouring
countries. This sort of pragmatic case-by-case bilateral cooperation is not the subject of this
chapter. Pragmatic considerations do play a part in the justiﬁcation for European spatial
planning, but the concern here is with the more intentional and strategic collaborations. As
explained in Chapter 14, prototypes of this type of collaboration can be found from the 1950s
in cooperation among the smaller countries in the Benelux and north-west Europe (Doucet 2002;
Fit and Kragt 1994).

## S. 39

The chapter follows a simple structure, beginning with a brief note on the justiﬁcation for
government intervention through planning per se, that is, spatial planning as conducted by
nation-states. It then moves on to consider more speciﬁc questions about the need to organise
planning action at the European and transnational levels. The concepts of competence,
subsidiarity and proportionality are introduced because they have particular salience to these
questions. Two main lines of argument around the justiﬁcation for European spatial planning
are then discussed: the idea that spatial planning can assist in creating a more even distribution
of spatial development across the European territory, and the need for better spatial coordination
of the combined spatial effects of EU and national policies. Despite the extensive practical action
on European spatial planning, the case is far from decided, and the emphasis of the arguments
continues to shift in line with changing political priorities. Table 2.1 provides a summary of the
main arguments in the debate.
Arguments for and against planning
The title of this chapter is inspired by the well-known 1985 paper by Klosterman in which he
gives an unusually straightforward account of ‘arguments for and against planning’. By planning
he means what is known in the USA as ‘city and regional planning’, corresponding broadly to
notions of spatial planning in the member states of the EU (see Chapter 13). He provides a
summary of the fundamental and persistent economic and political arguments that have been
made to justify or challenge the extent of planning intervention. Elements of this discussion
provide pointers for the discussion of European spatial planning. The idea of government
intervention in the market, including spatial planning, is accepted in all liberal market economies. The question is more what degree of intervention is acceptable that, while respecting
individual free choice, is also able to deal with market failures (examples of market failures
relating to the environment are given in Box 21.1).
Klosterman summarises the economic arguments concerning the provision of public goods
(such as a healthy environment), externality effects (such as the impact of one property
development on neighbouring uses) and political issues concerning the representation of interests
in decision-making. He also explains that in addressing these market failures planning is in
danger of serving the interests of capital and legitimising existing power relations, but argues
that some element of planning is ‘clearly preferable to exclusive reliance on the fundamentally
ﬂawed processes of market and political competition’ (Klosterman 1985: 11).
Klosterman (1985: 17) concludes that there is a consensus on four ‘vital social functions –
promoting the common or collective interests of the community; considering the external effects
of individual and group action, improving the information base for public and private decision
making and considering the distributional effects of public and private action’. All these points
will be applicable at the European and transnational scales, and seemingly provide a sound
argument for planning, but Klosterman stresses some important caveats. The identiﬁcation of
failures in the market as it is currently conceived certainly supports some kind of public
intervention, but that need not necessarily be planning intervention. Indeed, many public interventions are designed to adjust or manipulate markets directly through regulation or incentives
without any spatial planning. Can planning provide any better solution to these problems 
than a regulated market? Experience in centrally directed planned economies provides a 
warning about the limitations of state planning. Furthermore, is it possible for planning to 
deﬁne and act in the common or public interest? The reality even at the local level is of many
competing interests, and experience demonstrates how powerful interests are well represented
in planning decisions, not least in the tendency for government to prioritise economic and
business interests.
10
The European dimension of spatial planning

## S. 41

These uncertainties demonstrate the limitations of theory in justifying planning action. For
Klosterman (1985: 7) ‘the case for planning in a modern market society cannot be made in the
abstract but requires a careful evaluation of planning’s effectiveness relative to alternative
institutional mechanisms for achieving society’s objectives’. This is also an important lesson in
arguing for or against European spatial planning. Theory does not give us an answer but a set
of categories to organise our thinking. Arguments often come down to ideological and cultural
convictions drawing on experience of practical outcomes from cooperation on planning. This
is especially important in the EU context where opposing positions on the role of state and
market come together from the member states (see Chapter 24). There is considerable variation
in the respective roles of state and market in the countries of the EU. In parts of central and
eastern Europe in particular, the legacy of discredited centralised economic planning systems 
is an unwillingness to intervene, especially in spatial planning (UNECE 2000) (as explained in
Chapter 12). For the EU as a whole, Kunzmann (2006a: 19) argues that the differences lie
simply between those who trust the market more than state intervention and those who have
conﬁdence in an enlightened state when it comes to guide longer term spatial development
. . . Experience shows that there is no alternative to the market economy. The question is
rather how socially responsible such market policies should and can be in times of global
competition, how much European or national policies, attempting to redistribute economic
wealth within Europe ﬁnd political and popular support.
While the arguments above have generally been resolved (if not concluded) in favour of a
signiﬁcant degree of spatial planning at nation-state level, and to some extent they can be scaled
up, they do not offer an automatic justiﬁcation for action at the European level. It is perfectly
acceptable to agree with the need for spatial planning at the national and regional levels while
arguing against similar activity at the EU level. This is where the concepts of competence and
subsidiarity come into play.
Competence, subsidiarity and proportionality
This section introduces the principles and criteria that are employed in arguments about the
distribution of competences between levels of government in the EU. It is not the intention to
argue whether or not the EU should have a competence for spatial planning, but rather to use
the general principles as a guideline in considering the case for European and transnational
spatial planning – as it can be used at any level of planning (Scheers 2006).
Nation-state governments (or sometimes in federal systems, regional-state governments)
would normally claim complete authority or ‘sovereignty’ ‘to take decisions without being
subject to external restraints’ (Nugent 2006: 558). Thus they hold all ‘competences’ to make
decisions and take action affecting that territory. The member states of the EU have agreed to
effectively pool aspects of sovereignty by ceding some competences to the Union (see Chapter 7).
This pooling constrains the autonomy of member states and undermines sovereignty. However,
state sovereignty is limited in any case and is being eroded further by global forces such as the
internationalisation of economies and the dominance of certain national cultures such as the
USA. So ceding competencies to shared institutions (such as NATO and the United Nations
(UN)) may help member states to ‘at least re-assert control over aspects of decision-making by
cooperating at levels and in ways that match post-war internationalism’ (Nugent 2006: 2). EU
competences are far greater than those of any other international ‘association’ of countries and
have expanded to involve many spheres of policy, some very close to the heart of national
12
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 42

politics (as explained in Chapter 7). Competences are ceded to, or conferred on the EU by the
Treaties agreed by member states. The EU has no role other than that given to it by the Treaties.
Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union (European Communities 2008) states that the 
EU can act ‘only within the limits of the competences conferred upon it by the Member States’.
The Treaty confers competences primarily as objectives that the EU should achieve, and this
gives a duty to the European Commission and other EU institutions to act to deliver them.
Competences are allocated to the EU as exclusive and non-exclusive or shared. A clariﬁcation
of the allocation of competences is one purpose of the Treaty of Lisbon signed by the member
states in 2007 (see Table 11.2).
Two principles are used in judging questions of competence: ‘subsidiarity’ and ‘proportionality’. The subsidiarity concept has been used in the EU since 1975 and it was ﬁrst mentioned
in the Single European Act (SEA) of 1986. Competences for environmental policy were conferred
on the EU, but at the same time the SEA introduced the concept of subsidiarity to constrain those
powers. EU law making has always acknowledged the principle of proportionality. The main
Treaty provisions on subsidiarity and proportionality are given in Box 2.1.
The subsidiarity concept is known mostly from federal government systems where it
enumerates competences to the state and federal governments, though it is often implicit in other
government systems (Bothe 1993; Toth 1992). The principle is that powers should be retained
at the lowest possible level and not ceded upwards unless action cannot be effectively undertaken
at the lower levels (Nadin and Shaw 1999). As Höffe (1996: 61) explains:
subsidiarity expresses a preference for lower levels . . . no responsibility should be located
higher than is necessary, whatever the individual can do should not be taken over by 
the community, and whatever a smaller social unit can do should not be performed by a
larger one . . .

Arguing for and against European spatial planning
13
Box 2.1 Subsidiarity and proportionality
Treaty on European Union (consolidated version of 2008 integrating the modiﬁcations listed
in the Treaty of Lisbon signed in 2007 and ratiﬁed in 2009):
This Treaty marks a new stage in the process of creating an ever-closer union among the
peoples of Europe, in which decisions are taken as openly as possible and as closely as
possible to the citizen.
(Article 1 of Title I Common Provisions – part)
Under the principle of subsidiarity, in areas which do not fall within its exclusive
competence, the Community shall act only in so far as the objectives of the proposed
action cannot be sufﬁciently achieved by the member states, either at central level or at
regional and local level, but can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the proposed
action, be better achieved at Union level.
Under the principle of proportionality, the content and form of Union action shall not
exceed what is necessary to achieve the objectives of the Treaties.
(Article 5 – parts)
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 43

The onus, therefore, is on demonstrating why competences should ﬂow upwards to the 
EU, and not the other way around. Proportionality contributes to maintaining citizen freedoms by controlling the intensity of intervention. Actions should not go beyond what is necessary to achieve the stated objective (Nadin and Shaw 1999). The two principles are employed
together to ensure that only policy objectives best achieved at the Community level are conferred
on the EU, and that if they are, the minimum of actions are taken by the EU to achieve the
objectives.
The Treaty of Amsterdam of 1997 introduced a Protocol providing guidance on how the
concepts of subsidiarity and proportionality could be applied in practice to particular policy
ﬁelds (partly from previous legal judgements). A summary of the criteria that provide a starting
point for considering the justiﬁcation for action on spatial planning beyond the nation-state is
given in Box 2.2. The application of these criteria is restricted in law to certain types of case
(which are explained in Nadin and Shaw 1999 and Tillotson and Foster 2003). As noted above
14
The European dimension of spatial planning
Box 2.2 Criteria to apply subsidiarity and proportionality
Criteria for testing subsidiarity:
Sufﬁciency
Can objectives not be sufﬁciently achieved by the member states, by reason of
their scale or effects?
Beneﬁt
Can action be better achieved by the Community?
In fulﬁlling the criteria outlined above, the following three guidelines are suggested:
•
The issue should have transnational aspects that cannot be regulated by member states.
•
Actions of member states alone or lack of Community action would conﬂict with other
requirements of the Treaty; and
•
Action at the Community level would produce clear beneﬁts.
Criteria for testing proportionality:
Suitability
Are the least onerous means adopted?
Necessity
Is the action necessary to achieve the objective?
Proportionality
Is any disadvantage imposed by intervention proportionate to the advantage
gained?
Fairness
Is there a fair distribution of the social burden and no disproportionate 
advantage to an individual?
Variation
Does action not prevent member states adopting higher standards?
Source: Nadin and Shaw 1999 and Consolidated Treaty on European Union (European Communities 2008)
Note that the Treaty of Lisbon signed by member states in 2007, which entered into force on 1 December 2009,
includes a Protocol on the application of subsidiarity, which explains that ‘the reasons for concluding that a Union
objective can be better achieved at Union level shall be substantiated by qualitative and, wherever possible,
quantitative indicators’ (Article 5).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 44

the purpose of setting out the criteria here is not to judge the legal arguments for a Community
competence but rather to use them as general norms against which to test European and
transnational level actions on spatial planning. The same norms are used in intergovernmental
debate about what the EU should do, and the EU encourages member states to apply these
principles when they implement Community law in their own jurisdictions. So, for example,
there is a preference for decentralised decision-making (Beckmann et al. 2009).
In summary the criteria say that there is a case for an action beyond the member state level
where objectives cannot be met by the member states acting independently (sufﬁciency), and
where a Community decision will be better (beneﬁt), that is, it will provide added value to
decisions made at national levels. The criteria for testing proportionality are that the means
should be the least onerous (suitability), they must be needed to achieve the objective (necessity)
and be fair in their distribution of costs and beneﬁts (fairness) (Emilou 1996). At the same time
the common provisions of the Treaty add the criteria that EU decisions should be taken as openly
and as closely to the citizen and that the actions should secure greater freedoms for the
individual.
The ﬁeld of spatial planning does not ﬁgure as an objective of the EU in the Treaties and
therefore no competence has been ceded to the European Union. (A mention of ‘town and
country planning’ in the Treaties is related to the implementation of environmental policy; see
Nadin and Shaw 1999.) However, other policy areas where the EU has a competence (such as
agriculture, economic competition and transport) have substantial implications for spatial
development and planning in the member states, and they are explained in Part V of this book.
Despite this apparent lack of competence, there has been a lot of activity on spatial planning
at the EU level involving the member states working ‘intergovernmentally’ with support from
the European Commission. Member states have generally been very reluctant to confer a
competence on the EU while at the same time accepting the European Commission’s close
involvement, though this has often gone by other names, notably ‘spatial development policy’.
This should not disguise the fact that the European Commission has played a key role in
enabling and encouraging a great increase in transnational cooperation on spatial planning since
the 1980s. DG REGIO (the Commission’s Directorate-General for Regional Policy) has
undertaken inﬂuential studies of spatial development patterns and trends at the European scale,
and provided billions of euro to co-ﬁnance cooperation on spatial planning across borders; and
funded a major research programme on spatial planning.
It is very likely that European spatial planning would have evolved more quickly had the
Community been given a free rein in the matter. In the event, responsibility for taking the agenda
forward has switched from country to country every six months with the presidency of the
European Council. Despite the limitations imposed by the competency issue, a group of
committed experts from the Commission and member states have successfully steered European
spatial planning on a course between the member states’ demands to retain control (sovereignty)
over their land use matters and the perceived beneﬁts of promoting and providing joint statements on spatial planning principles and policies. It has required much negotiation and constant
reafﬁrmation that actions were of an exploratory nature and not intended to usurp the powers
of member states.
By 2008 there was more agreement about the EU’s role in spatial planning brokered with
the introduction of the concept of ‘territorial cohesion’. As explained in Chapter 13, this has
clariﬁed the difference between the EU’s role in ‘spatial planning’ and that of the member states.
Nevertheless, the member states will continue to weigh the costs and beneﬁts of any action on
European spatial planning, whether by the EU or not, against criteria such as sufﬁciency, beneﬁt,
suitability and fairness. As Kunzmann (2006a: 28) argues: ‘most likely subsidiarity will rule the
game in the future’. With this in mind, the rest of the chapter examines three main arguments on

Arguing for and against European spatial planning

## S. 45

whether European spatial planning meets the principles and criteria of subsidiarity and proportionality: the growing transnationality of spatial relations; the EU’s goal to achieve more balanced
spatial development; and the need to coordinate the spatial impacts of sectoral policies.
Transnationality
With regard to testing the criteria of sufﬁciency and beneﬁt, the Protocol attached to the Treaty
of Amsterdam invokes the notion of ‘transnational aspects which cannot be regulated by
member states alone’. This is very pertinent for spatial development, but there are few pointers
in ofﬁcial documentation about how to assess whether transnationality exists. Crossing national
borders such as in the case of some water catchments is an obvious indicator, but there are 
also public policy issues that sit inside a country that will have externality effects in other
countries, such as changes in the capacity of seaports, environmental emissions and even urban
containment policies that put pressure on housing in a neighbouring country. Nevertheless, the
deﬁnition of transnational issues has been vague even when considerable EU funding has been
involved, as in the case of the Community Initiative INTERREG IIIB (Dühr and Nadin 2007;
see Chapters 16 and 22).
There are claims that transnationality is an inevitable outcome of the effects of globalisation
reinforced by European integration. Dynamic spatial relations within global capitalism are 
now less hindered by national borders. Nation-states have less control over their territories, there
is a dispersal of power across a multi-level governance system, there are pressures for a
‘rescaling’ or reorganisation of territorial governance, and there is steady progress towards a
‘borderless Europe’. The tendency for increasing autonomy and competences of regional (subnational) governments, especially around city regions, is part of this phenomenon (Eser and
Konstadakopulos 2000).
Bounded territories of nation-states are also being challenged by the network society or
network urbanism (Albrechts and Mandelbaum 2005; Dupuy 2008; Healey 2006a). Expansion
of interconnected infrastructure networks encourages the increasing interaction of ﬁrms and
citizens in larger spaces cutting across borders. Increasing mobility across extensive networks
challenges the containment effect of administrative boundaries (Rooij 2005), although Dupuy
(2008: 66) adds that this new ‘rapidly spreading, genre of territoriality . . . does not mean that
every other form of territoriality has vanished as a result’. Dupuy argues that we have seen
similar shifts in scale before: ‘The railways were a large-scale network and urban planners and
policy-makers learnt to work with them’ (2008: 127).
It has been argued that transnational networks and ‘reterritorialisation’ of social and
economic relations create issues of strategic relevance that cannot be solved by member states
alone but require a joint response by all nation-states and their regions. So it could follow 
that there should be some concomitant response in spatial planning today (and more broadly
territorial governance) that addresses the reality of these changing spatial relations (described
in more detail in Chapter 4) and the externality effects of national and regional spatial planning
decisions across borders.
However, member states retain considerable control and autonomy (sovereignty) and may
argue that they can address transnational issues independently. The idea of ‘functional urban
regions’ that cut across administrative boundaries has been recognised in planning since the
1930s, and there are examples of many cross-boundary collaborations that address them,
especially in relation to travel-to-work areas and water catchments. A more proportionate
response to the changing geography of economic and social relations that cut across borders 
may be through continuation of ad hoc bilateral measures not requiring any elaborate apparatus
of European spatial planning with special transboundary institutions and instruments. Also,
16
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 46

there may be other ‘countervailing issues such as the potentially negative effects of [EU 
action on] harmonization of local standards and cultures’ (Búrca 1999: 24) which indicate 
that disadvantages outweigh the beneﬁt.
Furthermore, member states and/or regions have legitimate authority to act within their
jurisdiction. There are no democratic institutions at the transnational and cross-border levels
to provide accountability, and the idea of planning for many interconnected spatial networks
at varying scales challenges deeply embedded ‘state-centric assumptions’ of democratic
governance that support it (Gualini 2004a, 2006). The European Union offers no solution or
model here since its own ‘democratic deﬁcit’ undermines its authority. For most of Europe,
planning systems, policies, professional allegiances and cultures all continue to have a strong
domestic orientation around the nation-state.
The counter argument to this, of course, is that while nation-states (or regional states) may
have the authority to act within their territory, they may have little real power to shape events
when acting independently. There is little sense in holding on to forms of democratic
accountability that leave the nation-state powerless to tackle critical issues. The willingness of
member states to jointly create a European Single Market illustrates this point. Also, there may
be other ways to ensure accountability, for example by creating cross-border institutions with
indirect political representation. In any case the problem will not go away. The rootedness of
the ‘nation-state mentality’ and the difﬁculty of shifting this is not an argument for ignoring a
growing problem.
Arguments of principle aside, experience on transnational cooperation on spatial planning
so far demonstrates that at least in north-west Europe transnational issues are barely addressed
in national and regional spatial planning (Dühr and Nadin 2007). Spatial planning is deeply
rooted in the ‘nation-state mentality’. Perhaps because of this ambivalence transboundary
institutions rarely exist, and where they do they are very weak, with some notable exceptions
such as those in the Baltic Sea Region (VASAB, HELCOM; see Chapter 15) or the cross-border
arrangements between Denmark and Sweden for the Öresund (or Øresund) region mentioned
in Chapter 1. In contrast, Buunk (2006) argues from four case studies that planning is playing
a part in creating new informal institutions or arenas to negotiate spatial policy issues that cut
across jurisdictions and where there are unclear lines of accountability. These ﬁndings support
a need for a transnational scale to spatial planning to overcome inertia in domestic planning
systems and to support decision-making for matters that cross borders.
Cohesion and more balanced spatial development
European spatial planning is promoted on the basis that it can help to achieve EU goals, and
in particular that it can assist in resolving competing tensions among them. The fundamental
goals of the EU are economic competitiveness, social cohesion and sustainable development. 
The spatial dimension of these objectives is self-evident since they concern spatial disparities
across the EU (as explained in Chapter 4). The Lisbon Treaty of 2007 adds the objective of
territorial cohesion, which gives a more explicit expression of the spatial dimension (as explained
in Chapter 13). Underlying these objectives is a desire for peaceful cooperation and solidarity
among nations.
There are inherent tensions among these goals. The initial need for post-war recovery, global
competition, economic restructuring and the declining inﬂuence of Europe in the world has made
economic competitiveness the paramount goal. Economic competitiveness policies are inﬂuenced
by the neoliberalism paradigm with its strong reliance on the free market economy. However,
the EU is also committed to environmental sustainability and social cohesion (which in short
might be explained as more equal access to opportunities). It is accepted that free market forces

## S. 47

alone fail to deliver social and environmental objectives; that the distribution of the costs and
beneﬁts of the market is very unequal; and that there is a strong spatial dimension to this unequal
distribution. Some places succeed and others fail. The EU is faced with inconsistent objectives
with variable impacts on different parts of the territory (as are member states). Thus, there is
interest in strategic spatial planning to help reconcile economic priorities with social and
territorial cohesion (Eser and Schmeitz 2008; Koresawa and Konvitz 2001). In this role, spatial
planning operates alongside regional policy (see Chapter 18), which supports economic
investment in the weaker regions.
The underlying argument is that a more balanced distribution of development can deliver
social cohesion and environmental sustainability by avoiding the damaging effects of concentrations of economic activity that we see at the European scale. This theme is reﬂected in the 
sub-title of the pre-eminent statement on European spatial planning, The European Spatial
Development Perspective: Towards the Balanced and Sustainable Development of the European
Territory (ESDP) (CSD 1999). It argues for ‘a spatial balance designed to provide a more 
even geographical distribution of growth across the territory of the EU (aiming at cohesion)’
(CSD 1999: 7). Moreover it is argued that more balanced development can improve Europe’s
economic performance as it will make better use of the economic potential of all regions.
Can spatial planning at the European and transnational scales deliver more balanced
development and does more spatially balanced development help to achieve the EU’s goals?
These are difﬁcult questions to answer, not least because the concept of balanced development
is rather vague. Concepts such as polycentric development and global economic integration
zones have been invoked to provide more depth (as explained in Chapter 15) but they are equally
ambiguous. Underlying the notion of balanced development is the principle of spatial equity.
Kunzmann (1998a: 101) claims that spatial equity ‘at whatsoever tier of spatial decision-making
has become the unfulﬁlled dream of the socially minded planning community’, but that more
often than not those aspirations are frustrated. Even where progress on spatial equity is
possible, Kunzmann argues that it could be counterproductive if it is thought to be a levelling
out of regional differences in pursuit of ‘global mainstream investment fashions’ (1998a: 104)
and that, indeed, it may increase intra-regional disparities. The evidence bears this out. Global
markets tend to favour the already dominant urban regions (as explained in Chapter 18).
Regional policy investment has had a positive effect on reducing disparities between member
states, though at the cost of increasing disparities within countries and regions.
The EU seeks to intervene in regional development processes that are complex and uncertain,
with concepts that lack clarity (Markusen 2003 cited in Alden 2006). Given this uncertainty
there is an argument for careful spatial steering of regional policy investment. Therefore spatial
planning may have a part to play, but this would be primarily at the national and regional level.
Kunzmann (2008) notes that contemporary theories of regional development point strongly to
an emphasis on the individual characteristics and potential of regions and to ‘regional embeddedness’, that is, regions beneﬁtting from a more self-sustaining economy with internal economic
circuits building from their own endogenous potential. The focus therefore turns to regions’
distinct identities, values, heritage and products (Morgan 1997). This argument favours local
projects rather than grand supranational visions. European spatial planning has moved towards
this position, calling for EU policy ‘to respond more effectively than it has done so far to the
territorial needs and characteristics, speciﬁc geographic challenges and opportunities of the
regions and cities’ (EU Ministers 2007a). For the orientation of EU Cohesion Policy after 2013,
the focus of discussion has now shifted to considering a ‘place-based approach’ (Barca 2009).
Even for external investment, the speciﬁc qualities of place are important. Nevertheless, it could
be argued that shaping market activity at the EU level and especially promoting weaker regions,
can be supported by European level planning action that helps to provide certainty and
undermine risk for investors.
18
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 48

Arguments that emphasise the distinctive characteristics of regions do not rule out a European
dimension to spatial planning, but in respect to proportionality they do reduce its scope and
weight. A less sympathetic critic of the role of European spatial planning in reconciling
economic, social and environmental objectives would argue that it simply masks the real power
play among economic actors in which social and environmental performance of particular
regions is not important. It is often merely a kind of symbolic reassurance that these goals can
be reconciled when in fact the economic interests dominate (Kunzmann 2006a; Richardson and
Jensen 1999, 2000). In this case we must turn to Klosterman’s (1985) recommendation and to
carefully consider the practical effectiveness of (European) spatial planning action. Tentative
conclusions on the effects of European spatial planning are given in Chapters 22 and 23.
Spatial policy and the costs of non-coordination
The introduction to this chapter mentioned that arguments for and against spatial planning 
at the European and transnational levels are inevitably bound up with the meaning of spatial
planning. Chapter 13 provides one answer to this question by pulling out the distinctive
features of the European dimension in contrast to notions of spatial planning that prevail in the
member states. This underlines that European spatial planning tends more towards inﬂuencing
and coordinating the spatial impacts of other sector policies, rather than pursuing an
independent European spatial policy (as the understanding of spatial planning within member
states would imply). The argument is a simple one. The EU intervenes in policy sectors such 
as agriculture, economic competition, environment, research and transport. It does this in 
ways that are often aspatial in form (co-ﬁnancing, subsidies, guidance, etc.) but that are
decidedly spatial in effect. Take, for example, funding for research and development. More than
EUR 8 billion is distributed annually by the European Commission to research institutions,
mostly through the EU Research Framework programmes. Allocations are principally made on
the basis of criteria that seek the highest scientiﬁc qualities and have little spatial intention, but
the distribution of funding has distinct spatial impacts. It inevitably ﬂows in large measure to
locations with high-quality research institutions (more often than not in larger urban areas), and
may reinforce existing disparities.
Part V of this book explains the territorial effects (or spatial policy effects) of key sector
policies. Funding to support agriculture (about a third of the total community budget), for
example, is distributed to farmers mostly on the basis of historical production levels, which has
the effect of concentrating much support in the richer areas of the Union territory. Some sector
policies do have a more explicit spatial dimension to their policy, but it may be poorly aligned
with, or contradictory to, other EU and national policy goals in particular places. For example,
for a long time there have been concerns about the effect of EU regional and agricultural policies
on the overexploitation of water resources and the loss of valued habitats and landscapes 
(EEB 2008).
Numerous reports since the 1980s have concluded that more attention should be given to
the spatial effects of sectoral policy (for example EP 1983) and to methods of ‘territorial policy
integration’ (Schout and Jordan 2007) (see Chapter 15). The relevance of this for the form or
style of planning at the European level is explained in Chapter 13. Here, two interrelated
implications of the spatial effects of sectoral policy are important. It can be argued that the EU
is already deeply engaged in a form of spatial planning because of the spatially discriminating
effects of its sectoral policies; and that unnecessary costs could be avoided through better spatial
coordination of them.
As early as 1983, the European Parliament’s Gendebien Report noted that the EU ‘institutions
had launched a series of policies whose respective spatial implications, when taken together, form
the constituent parts of a de facto regional policy’ (EP 1983: 11, quoted in Williams 1996: 78).

Arguing for and against European spatial planning
19
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 49

By the 1980s, the EU was heavily engaged in shaping patterns of spatial development across
Europe, but not in an intentional or ‘planned’ way. A resolution on a European Regional
Planning Scheme that followed argued that it was necessary to ‘co-ordinate existing EU
measures and instruments’ in order to ensure their ‘functional and ﬁnancial rationalisation in
time and space’ and that ‘from the spatial point of view, no decision will stand in contradiction
to any other’ (Williams 1996: 77). Similar views have been expressed many times since. The
‘territorial impacts’ of sector policies have become a major theme for research (Albrechts 1997,
2001b). Underlying this argument, although not expressed so explicitly, is the assumption that
a territorial approach to policy coordination (spatial planning) can help to improve the efﬁciency
and value added of Community intervention, and, as explained above, reconcile competing
objectives (Benz 2002).
A major empirical study lends weight to these arguments. Robert et al. (2001) investigated
the spatial impacts of Community policies and the costs of non-coordination. The report
concludes that sectoral compartmentalisation is deeply ingrained in the EU’s culture and that
the institutions are poorly prepared to give more territorial coherence to policies. ‘The degree
of horizontal co-ordination between the various Community Institutions is relatively low and
no procedure exists which aims at creating spatial coherence between all Community policies’
(Robert et al. 2001: 158). In summary, the report ﬁnds much evidence that certain EU policies
favour the already stronger regions of the EU, that they exacerbate existing intra-regional
inequalities, and that they fail to grasp opportunities for positive combined effects.
The authors argue that the ﬁndings provide justiﬁcation for the development of stronger
territorial strategies for cross-sectoral policy coordination at all levels. They recommend new
instruments and procedures at the EU level including spatial impact evaluation for all new Community actions, though recognising that this would require a fundamental change in the culture
of EU policy development. As the report says, ‘these calculations . . . demonstrate the existence
of a co-ordination problem and a lack of synergy which requires a political answer’ (Robert 
et al. 2001: 153). Benz (2002) argues that this can be achieved through a combination of binding
policy statements at the EU level that constrain the actions of EU institutions and member states,
and more speciﬁc targeted interventions to positively promote coordination.
All in all, there appears to be a strong need for greater spatial coordination of sectoral policy.
It is needed to address costs of non-coordination and would bring added beneﬁts in making best
use of available resources. Furthermore, it could be argued that the lack of an EU competence
for spatial planning has unduly perpetuated costs of non-coordination because Commission
Directorates are able to implement their sectoral policies with little consideration of their interrelation with other sector and spatial policies in the same place. However, there are weaknesses
in this argument. First, the spatial disposition of EU funding, including for example Cohesion
Policy, is under the control of member states, and that is why some have been reluctant to cede
a spatial planning competence to the EU: it would interfere with their discretion. So the problem
may be more about linking sector policies and spatial strategies at the member state and regional
levels. Second, although it is an implicit assumption of the discussion, there is no guarantee that
more attention to the spatial coordination of sector policies would lead to better outcomes. A
spatial strategy could not harmonise the varying objectives of sector policies, nor would it have
any legitimate authority to do so. It might inform the development of policy, but even then there
will be different opinions about the likely spatial impacts of sector policies. It is highly unlikely
that member states would agree to a binding policy statement at the EU level, as advocated by
Benz (2002), and the task from a proportionality perspective would entail great investment for
questionable value. Third, there are beneﬁts in a degree of compartmentalisation of policy 
that enables each sector to focus on speciﬁc and manageable objectives. Where there is a need
for improved coordination at the EU level, it might be achieved in other ways (see Chapter 15).
20
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 50

For example, the different sectors could adopt common policy positions in relation to the spatial
impacts of policy. Other methods have been found to provide more coordination of EU and
member state policies in areas such as coastal zone management.
Conclusion
Member states have been consistent in their opposition to any Community competence in spatial
planning in the sense of the regulation of the use of land in their territories, which is seen to 
be very close to questions of sovereignty. On the other hand, there is wide recognition that 
some coordination of aspects of policy that have spatial impacts is needed at the EU level. 
By the mid-2000s member states had generally accepted the need for, and potential beneﬁts of,
more attention to the spatial coordination of sector policies under the banner of territorial
cohesion (Faludi 2007a, 2007b). The objective of territorial cohesion helps to distinguish EU
level action on the spatial dimension of policies from the land use regulation and wider
territorial management regimes in the member states. For some member states it conﬁrms that
the EU will not intervene in ‘spatial planning’ that is the proper competence of the member
states, and that spatial planning at the European level is sufﬁciently distinct from spatial planning
at the national and regional levels. This question is yet to be resolved as, at the time of writing,
the debate on territorial cohesion is far from resolved (see Chapter 13).
Questions about EU competences are central to the progress of European integration in
general. Worries about increasing powers of the Community institutions were a major factor
in the rejection of the European Constitution in 2005 (see Chapters 12 and 14). Although it does
not make the headlines, spatial planning has been part of the same debate. Questions of subsidiarity and proportionality may seem rather abstract – but have real effects. These arguments
have pervaded questions of European spatial planning, in contrast to other policy ﬁelds such 
as environmental regulation where the EU holds a rarely questioned exclusive competence.
Faludi and Waterhout (2002) explain how in the late 1980s there was an expectation that the
Commission would take on a similar role in the development of European spatial development
policy, and that it was assumed by some, including the French protagonists, that no speciﬁc
competence was needed. In the event, arguments for a European dimension to spatial planning
have been more protracted because the member states hold differing views about what the
Commission should be allowed to do. This is a political debate in which there is much horsetrading. During the time that European spatial planning emerged in the early 1990s there was
a general backlash to the rapid expansion of Community competences and EU law over
preceding years. Politics in much of the EU has been more concerned with reducing state
intervention and liberal market economics. That there has been steady growth of transnational
cooperation on spatial planning in this context suggests that most stakeholders have been
convinced of the case for it.
Further reading
The literature that can inform a debate about European spatial planning is extensive though the
arguments are often more assumed than explicit. The ofﬁcial statements on European spatial
planning are a good starting point if read with a critical eye: the ESDP (CSD 1999), the Territorial
Agenda (EU Ministers 2007a) and the Council of Europe’s Guiding Principles (CEMAT 2000).
The arguments about spatial planning are not conﬁned to Europe. Useful insights come from
elsewhere in the world where effective spatial planning is promoted to support progressive
democratic change; see, for example, South African National Development and Planning
Commission (1999) and South African Ministry of Agriculture and Land Affairs (2001).
