## S. 51

The language challenge 
and terminology
English alone is not enough.
Promoting Language Learning (CEC 2003a: 4)
Introduction
The great diversity of language and culture in a European Union of 27 member states within a
continent of 45 nations presents great challenges for territorial cooperation. Williams (1996: 58)
gives a typical example. He explains how a German-speaking conference participant asked a
British speaker: ‘Is there a plan for this project?’ The response was long and complicated and
left the questioner confused. The reason is that the use of the word plan has very different
meanings in the UK and Germany. In Germany the legally binding plan effectively determines
and gives permission for certain forms of development. In the UK the plan provides criteria to
judge proposals but does not give permission. The British speaker did not understand the
question properly. What the questioner meant (in the UK sense) was ‘Does this project have
permission?’ This example illustrates how language, society and culture (including institutional
and planning culture) are interdependent and difﬁcult to separate. Aspects of society including
planning practices and tools may only make sense in the home context and language.
Spatial planning debate is also very dynamic. Meanings and concepts are debated and revised,
and especially so since the growth of cooperation across national borders has encouraged more
exchanges between planning cultures. The vocabulary is growing all the time, particularly as
concepts employed in one country are exported to other countries. There has been little opportunity to reach agreement about the meaning of different terms, and there is considerable
variation in their use, which can lead to ambiguity and confusion.
Consistent use of terminology is difﬁcult because many of the words we use to practise and
explain spatial planning are culturally rooted. Very few terms used in spatial planning have a
universal scientiﬁc meaning, as might be the case in the disciplines of engineering or physics.
The meaning of terms is deeply bound up with their context, especially the history of a society
and its language. Terms can be, and often are, translated literally from one language or context
to another, but their meaning will be distorted because the new language and culture gives a
different frame of reference. Even where the same terms are being used, the social context
matters. Think of the way that concepts such as ‘heritage’, ‘high density housing’ or ‘national
park’ will vary in meaning between Italy and Finland. This is a problem of ‘non-equivalence of
terms or concepts’ (Hantrais and Mangen 1996: 9) and is the great challenge of cross-national
working. Even deceptively simple terms such as ‘land’ and ‘plan’ can cause difﬁculties. And each
participant in processes of transnational cooperation will hold their own assumptions about
meaning according to their place of origin and value system.
Chapter 3
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 52

The problem for European spatial planning (as with other EU policy sectors) is that on the
one hand common languages and terminology are needed to facilitate cooperation across
cultural and linguistic boundaries. But common terminology may fail to distinguish the real
differences in meaning that exist. At the most fundamental level do terms such as ‘territory’ and
‘planning’ mean the same thing on either side of a border? On the other hand, cultural and
linguistic diversity is an asset and so it must be protected and enhanced. But will the promotion
of diversity prevent effective action to tackle border problems and make the most of a region’s
potential? Without a common language how can member states debate common principles,
address shared problems and adopt complementary practices?
A great many European territorial cooperation projects (see Chapter 16) have underestimated
this challenge and relied heavily on a common international language and literal translation,
often involving English. There is a need also to make explicit different meanings attached to
terms. The rest of this chapter explores the challenge of language and terminology, with a view
to explaining how the notion of spatial planning varies from place to place. It also presents the
key common terminology and offers guidance on how to avoid the most common terminological
pitfalls in cross-national spatial planning.
The language challenge
The European Union, candidate countries and neighbours make use of many languages and three
alphabets. Of these, 23 languages are ‘ofﬁcial’ (at the end of 2008). Regulations based on Articles
21 and 290 of the EC Treaty state that citizens are entitled to contact the EU institutions in any
one of the ofﬁcial languages and the reply must be made in the same language. They also require
that regulations and ‘other documents of general application’ and the Ofﬁcial Journal (which
includes all EU legislation and ofﬁcial documents) shall be published in all ofﬁcial languages.
Table 3.1 shows the steady increase in the number of ofﬁcial languages since 1957. The
European Commission does its work in the tree main working languages, English, French and
German with particular Directorates favouring certain languages. DG REGIO, for example, has
had a strong French ﬂavour reﬂecting the stafﬁng and history of the topic. Other institutions
such as the European Parliament and the Committee of the Regions (where politicians are
involved) use all ofﬁcial languages in routine working. Perhaps surprisingly, the translation and
interpretation costs of the EU institutions amount to just over 1 per cent of the EU’s total budget,
that is about EUR 1.3 million per annum (or EUR 2.28 per citizen per year) (CEC 2005a).

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 53

The signiﬁcance of the language issue for Europe is reﬂected in the creation of the post of
the Commissioner for Multilingualism (many policy areas do not have a dedicated commissioner). The Community’s policy on multilingualism is set out in A New Framework for
Multilingualism (CEC 2005a) and Multilingualism: An Asset for Europe (CEC 2008a). They
both take forward the European Council’s commitment signed in 2005 in Barcelona that all
school children should learn at least two other foreign languages from an early age as part of
their compulsory education. This policy has three main strands: to encourage language learning
and linguistic diversity, to promote a multilingual economy, and to give access to European
information in the citizens’ own languages. The Commission gives priority to many direct actions
that encourage multilingualism including the Comenius Programme for languages in schools,
the Leonardo programme for international exchanges in vocational training, the Grundtvig
programme for adults, the Erasmus exchange programme for higher education and the Lingua
programme for language teachers. The Commission also encourages member states to develop
national strategies to guide language learning.
The inﬂuence of European integration and EU policy on language use, especially in providing
opportunities for international exchange, is unquestionable, but the reality of multilingualism
still depends very much on the particular historical circumstances of member states. A
Eurobarometer survey carried out across the whole EU territory in 2005 (CEC 2006a) found
that 28 per cent of respondents would be able to hold a conversation in two languages other
than their mother tongue and 56 per cent would manage one language, a 9 per cent increase
on 2001 ﬁgures. The geographical distribution of language skills is very skewed. Ability to hold
a conversation in one other language was highest in Luxembourg (92 per cent), Latvia and 
Malta (93 per cent) and lowest in Ireland (34 per cent), the United Kingdom (38 per cent), Italy
(41 per cent), Hungary and Portugal (42 per cent), and Spain (44 per cent). The average number
of foreign languages learnt per pupil in higher-level secondary education in Luxembourg in 2005
was 2.3 compared with 0.6 in Malta and 0.1 in the UK. For some countries this performance
may be about to change. In Spain, learning of at least one foreign language is now compulsory
from age three to 18 years. In Sweden learning two foreign languages is compulsory from age
seven to 16 years. By contrast, in England the learning of one foreign language is compulsory
only from age 11 to 14 years.
Of particular concern in EU language policy is the maintenance of lesser-used or minority
languages that are spoken by more than 46 million EU citizens. The European Bureau for Lesserused Languages has identiﬁed 63 languages in use in the territory of the EU.1 This does not
include the many other languages in use that result from immigration (people from more than
175 nationalities reside in the EU). Some regional languages are recognised as second ‘state
languages’. For example, Belgium has three state languages: French, German and Dutch. In
Spain, Spanish (castellano) is the ofﬁcial language of the country but four languages have ofﬁcial
status in their respective regions (Catalan/Valencian, Basque, Galician and Aranese). Italy
recognises 12 other ofﬁcial regional languages (Eurostat et al. 2008: 19). The Council of Europe
has adopted a European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages (CoE 1992a) that makes
a direct link between language and territory and the need to ensure that administrative divisions
‘do not constitute an obstacle to the promotion of the regional or minority language in
question’ (Article 7). It encourages cross-border cooperation where the same language is used
on either side of a border.
In 2008 a report from the EU Group of Intellectuals for Intercultural Dialogue (GIID 2008)
made a stand against the ‘erosion’ of knowledge of neighbouring languages in favour of a
language of international communication. The report is particularly scornful of the pervasive
inﬂuence of the English language, which is by far the most widely used and learnt language in
Europe. Although German is the most widely spoken native language with more than 90 million
24
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 54

native speakers in the EU, English is the most popular second language so in total about half
of all EU citizens (that is around 125 million) claim to be able to hold a conversation in English
(CEC 2006a, 2008b). About a third can converse in German and a quarter of all citizens can
converse in French.
The use of English is dominant and expanding with around 90 per cent of school children
in most EU countries learning English as part of their compulsory education (Eurostat et al.
2008: 12). The Intercultural Dialogue report (GIID 2008) accepts that most citizens will choose
a language of international communication as a second language, particularly English, because
of the ‘virtually essential’ requirement for English in professional circles. It argues that this
competence will become so widespread that it will no longer offer a strong asset on curricula
vitae, and therefore suggests that French, Spanish, Portuguese and Mandarin among others are
worthwhile alternatives to strengthen one’s proﬁle. On the dominance of English it recommends
that each European citizen should also master a ‘personal adoptive language’, which is likely
to be the language of a neighbouring country or region. This would be:
part and parcel of the school and university curriculum of every European citizen, and of
everyone’s occupational curriculum . . . [It would] go hand in hand with familiarity with
the country/countries in which that language is used, along with the literature, culture,
society and history linked with that language and its speakers.
(GIID 2008: 10)
The ‘special challenge’ for the British is noted – ‘the temptation to remain ensconced in a
monolingualism is probably much stronger than elsewhere’ (p. 15) – and the ﬁgures quoted
above do not suggest that this will change.
In a provocative paper about the increasing adoption of the Anglo-Saxon model of higher
education in Europe and its effect on planning education (discussed further in Chapter 24),
Kunzmann (2004) outlines the consequences of the adoption of English as the lingua franca of
the European academic community. In summary, he argues that the prospects are for more
planning schools to teach courses in English (possibly bad English), and academics will continue
to concentrate on publication in English textbooks and journals and on topics of international
interest that will gain status over a ‘second tier’ of regional market topics and publications in
other languages. Planning thought published in other languages will become ‘second class’ and
will be alienated from ‘local milieus’ and thus have less impact on local developments; and ‘the
gap between (international) theory and (regional) practice will unavoidably widen’ (Kunzmann
2004: 6–7). Above all Kunzmann warns of a ‘language driven planning theory development’.
‘Planning theory becomes Anglo-American and planning practise (sic) regional, be it French or
Swedish’ (p. 7). The concepts used in planning theory will tend to be Anglo-American concepts,
even though the social model in Britain and the USA is much more market oriented than
continental models, and therefore not necessarily compatible.
These are real threats, and indeed much of this scenario is already realised. For example,
universities routinely rank English publications above local language publications in assessing
research performance. Anglo-American theoretical texts become widely known in the planning
curriculum long before theory published in other languages. Stiftel and Mukhopadhyay (2007)
have analysed the national afﬁliations of authors in 25 English-language planning journals over
a recent ﬁve-year period to assess the degree of internationalisation in the scholarship published.
They conclude that while the English-language planning literature is substantially multinational
when taken as a whole, non-anglophone scholarship is under-represented, and journals in certain
countries are concerned only with domestic issues. This results in low rates of exchange of
planning scholarship between world regions.

## S. 55

In order to counter the Anglo-American hegemony in planning academia, Kunzmann (2004)
suggests that journals might give priority to authors from non-English speaking countries and
that practitioners need more help to have their ideas promoted in the international languages
and journals. Stiftel and Mukhopadhyay (2007: 569) make a number of proposals that would
improve interregional and inter-language group information ﬂows and improve the richness and
quality of planning scholarship. These include actions by editors to increase non-anglophone
editorial board memberships; greater involvement of international learned societies in the
publication of planning journals; higher levels of international collaboration in the conduct of
planning research; higher levels of planning research funding in developing countries; various
practices in which English-language journals work with non-English original versions before and
after publication; greater availability of translated materials; better non-English search engines
and more comprehensive coverage by English-language search engines; and more language- and
culture-sensitive practices at conferences.
Although these proposals are welcome, they do not address the fundamental problem that
arises from the dominance of English, which is that there is a strong tendency for the world to
be explained using Anglo-American concepts only. There is a widespread use and misuse of
terms that belong speciﬁcally to either American or British planning theory and practice. For
example, authors routinely translate the terms for planning procedures and instruments into
English terms, thus distorting the meaning of the original idea. The solution for this, given that
the English language will continue to dominate international exchanges (and is needed for this
purpose), is to use the ‘home or native language terms’ whenever possible and to be more precise
in explaining complex concepts. This is explained in Box 3.1.
A key principle in EU language policy that links it strongly with European spatial planning
issues is the argument that diversity is an asset and should be nurtured. Respect for and careful
management of cultural differences is a demonstration of fundamental values that drove the
European project after the Second World War. All Europeans should be able to ‘access this
heritage’ in which language plays a primary part. ‘Most European nations have been built on
the platform of their language of identity, the European Union can only build on a platform 
of linguistic diversity . . . [which] is a powerful antidote against the various types of fanaticism
. . . in Europe and elsewhere’ (GIID 2008: 5). The debates on territorial cohesion in the EU 
and speciﬁcally the Territorial Agenda (explained in Chapters 14 and 15) make much the 
same point and stress the role of territorial cooperation and spatial planning in this task. As
explained in Chapter 2, this requires some progress on developing a common understanding or
understandings of the key concept – spatial planning.
The meanings of ‘spatial planning’
The concept of ‘spatial planning’ (in the English language) became common currency across
Europe during the 1990s, but there is confusion about its meaning. This is because it is used in
different ways depending on the context and purpose (Nadin 2000). There are two main ways
in which it is used:
•
as the name of the governance system for managing spatial development and/or physical
land use in a particular place (the national or regional planning system); and
•
as a speciﬁc term to describe a particular idea or deﬁnition of spatial planning as a
coordination mechanism that has arisen through debate at the European level (the spatial
planning approach).
Every EU member state has a national and/or regional planning system that seeks to manage
and regulate spatial development and land uses in pursuit of agreed objectives. Spatial planning
has been widely used as a common English language term to describe these systems, so we might

The language challenge and terminology
27
Box 3.1 Avoiding ambiguous terminology in European 
spatial planning
In European spatial planning there is the problem of translating terms for institutions,
instruments and concepts while avoiding misinterpretation. This is true for any language but
most often occurs when translating terms into English, which is the most common language
for publications that are intended for international circulation. In practice, there tend to be two
approaches to translation that are most commonly used:
•
make literal translations of terms into English, for example, ‘destination plan’ for the Dutch
bestemmingsplan, and vice versa; or
•
use the nearest equivalent Anglo-American term in English, for example, district council for an
Italian commune.
There are dangers in both approaches. Some terms that are literal translations may mean
(more or less) different things, for example, the Danish byggelinie and the English building line
(the former is to do with keeping areas free from development; the latter is the setback of
buildings along a street). Using speciﬁc terms is also difﬁcult: the form and operation of a
commune is very different to a district council though they are both local authorities. Another
example is the Swedish nationalpark and the English national park. This is a literal translation,
but the English and Swedish concepts of national park are quite different. The former can only
be established on state land – the latter is generally on private land. There are even cases where
the same terms in the same language are used in different countries but where the meaning
differs. The problems of meaning are multiplied further with more abstract concepts such as
partnership or participation. Even words such as urban and rural cause problems.
An educated reader will have in mind that context matters and will not take for granted
that the same words mean the same thing in different countries or regions. Also, we should
not assume that the use of a planning term at the European level coincides with the meaning
of a corresponding term at the national level. More signiﬁcantly where English is dominant,
there is a tendency to explain the world through the Anglo-American conceptual lens. There
is no easy solution. A good glossary can help to avoid ambiguity by providing clear explanations
of each term in each language.
In this book we have adopted the approach that was used in the EU Compendium of Spatial
Planning Systems and Policies (CEC 1997a: 25):
the use of literal translations of words with a speciﬁc meaning is avoided in favour of the
use of ‘home language terms’. Where home language terms with speciﬁc meaning are used
they are italicised and explained in the text in their first mention. If necessary the
explanation is repeated in later sections.
Also, in explaining European spatial planning we have tried to use generic terms that are
not speciﬁc to a particular country – and in particular to be aware when we are using primarily
Anglo-American concepts. This is difﬁcult. The meaning of the term ‘planning’, like many others,
is tied to the context in which it is used. So we have sought to ensure that it is clear what
meaning we are giving to terms, and the possible alternative understandings.

## S. 57

refer to ‘the Greek spatial planning system’ or ‘the spatial planning system in Bavaria’. These
spatial planning systems are a sector of government activity alongside others such as transport,
agriculture and environment. This is the notion that national, regional and local governments
generally have in mind when they use the term spatial planning.
When reference is being made to the ‘spatial planning system’ in the home language, more
speciﬁc names are used such as urbanisme or aménagement du territoire (France, Belgium,
Luxembourg), town and country planning (the United Kingdom), Raumplanung (Germany and
Austria) and planowanie przestrzenne (Poland). Table 3.2 gives a list of the names that the EU
translation service uses to denote the notion of ‘spatial planning’ in the ofﬁcial languages (terms
like spatial planning is also translated into other non-ofﬁcial languages, such as Welsh). The
terms listed describe the principal legal and governance regime managing spatial development
in particular countries. In some cases they might be near-literal translations of the English term
‘spatial planning’; others will be quite different.
The important point already made in the introduction to this chapter is that these names
describe different concepts. The meaning of these terms has evolved in the particular legal, socioeconomic, political and cultural conditions of the country or region in question. This is perhaps
most evident in the form of the visual language that different systems use (Dühr 2007). The
problem is further compounded by the numerous variants of ‘planning’ that may exist in
different places, such as environmental planning and landscape planning. Such terms may be
used as virtual synonyms for ‘the spatial planning system’, but they might also refer to some
other speciﬁc ﬁeld of action or professional domain. For example, in Germany the term
Landschaftspﬂege is sometimes translated as landscape planning (or conservation) but while it
is closely integrated with spatial planning, it remains a separate discipline. In the Netherlands
the term stedebouwkundig ontwerp (urban and regional design) must be separated from planologie

## S. 58

(urban and regional planning) (Klaasen 2007). The division of professional domains denoted
by terms will vary in each country. So, strictly speaking, the terms used for each country are
not transferable to other countries, except in the most general sense. The problem of nonequivalence persists even if the same words are used, for example, aménagement du territoire
will have a different meaning in Belgium, France and Luxembourg. (A more detailed explanation
of different forms or models of spatial planning is given in Chapter 13.)
The home language names are speciﬁc terms, and so, when possible, it is good practice to use
them to describe what stands for the spatial planning system in each country, but this will present
obvious communication barriers. So in transnational cooperation it is commonplace to use the
Euro-English term ‘spatial planning’ to describe the system in a particular country. The danger
here is that it suggests that we are talking about the same concept in different countries, and we
are not. So care is needed. This is especially so when we use the term spatial planning as a generic
term to denote the whole class of systems of government that are used to manage spatial development, as for example in The EU Compendium of Spatial Planning Systems and Policies (CEC
1997a). Here, spatial planning systems refers to many, and very different, forms of planning.
The second meaning refers to the use of the term in European level spatial planning debate,
particularly in the creation of the European Spatial Development Perspective (CSD 1999). Here
spatial planning has acquired a different and quite specific meaning that reflects an
understanding of the role of spatial planning at that scale. The European level debates on spatial
planning have not generally been concerned with the idea of planning as the management or
regulation of land use change.2 Rather, the European level concept of spatial planning is much
more concerned with the coordination of the spatial impacts of sectoral policies such as
transport, regional policy and agriculture so as to avoid the ‘costs of non-coordination’ (Robert
et al. 2001).
Recently, the European Commission has begun to favour the use of the term territorial
cohesion and, before that, spatial development policy, because they put some distance between
this European level idea of spatial planning and member states’ concerns with the management
of land use. The reasons for this begin with the problem of competence – as explained in Chapter
2. Regulation of land use is a matter for the member states, as they themselves often argue, and
not the Community. Thus there is little reference to spatial planning in Commission reports
published in the 1990s, and virtually none in documents published over recent years. Also, some
of the new member states are not very positive about using the term planning because its
meaning is tied up with the central planning of former communist regimes. In contrast, the term
territorial cohesion is an objective (rather than the means) against which the Commission can
develop a programme of actions. This ﬁts better the normal working method of the EU, which
is to pursue objectives set out in the Treaties.
The Interim Territorial Cohesion Report (CEC 2004b: Article 16) defines territorial 
cohesion as:
the balanced distribution of human activities across the Union . . . it translates the goal of
sustainable and balanced development of the Union into territorial terms . . . [and] includes
fair access to all citizens and economic operators to Services of General Economic Interest
(SGEI) irrespective of the territory to which they belong.
It could be argued that this is as close to spatial planning (deﬁned as a particular approach to
policy coordination) as possible without using the term. Indeed, national and regional governments routinely link the notion of territorial cohesion to spatial planning as the principal method
of its application.
The discussion above explains that there are two very different meanings given to the idea
of spatial planning. The ﬁrst suggests that the word will be used as shorthand for what are

The language challenge and terminology

## S. 59

different systems in the member states and regions. The second meaning is universal but refers
to a form of spatial planning that is more speciﬁc to concerns at the European level. In practice
the use of the term mixes up these meanings. All national and regional planning systems are
likely to reﬂect both a land use and a national and European level sectoral coordination concept
of planning, as explained in Chapter 13. Indeed, the European debate on ‘spatial planning’ has
been very inﬂuential in recent reforms of spatial planning systems in some member states, for
example England (Nadin 2007) and Italy (Janin Rivolin 2003).
Transnational cooperation
The rapid expansion of collaborative activity across national borders has generated an expanded
vocabulary of terms to describe it, some of which are new and others are the reworking of older
language. Cross-border, interregional, transnational, transfrontier, transboundary, transborder,
trans-European and supranational are all used to describe collaborative activities across national
and regional borders. Often these terms are not used with consistency. The forms of collaboration are explained in Chapter 16. Here we will brieﬂy review the main terms that are based on
the spatial dimension of that collaboration (CEC 1997a; CSD 1999):
•
cross-border – cooperation between geographically contiguous border regions, including
maritime borders;
•
interregional – cooperation among non-contiguous regions; and
•
transnational – cooperation across large multi-national spaces.
The Council of Europe and the Committee of the Regions have traditionally also used transnational to denote all three forms of cooperation across boundaries. It is less confusing to use the
terms transboundary or transborder as the generic form (Nadin and Brown 1999). Transboundary
retains a sense of both transcending boundaries and cooperation across different territorial spaces.
There is also a general European dimension to some spatial planning activities, for example,
in the preparation of the ESDP or Council of Europe’s CEMAT, and it is suggested that these
should be described as supranational. Finally, there is the related term intergovernmental, which
describes the process by which EU member state governments work cooperatively on joint
policies and actions outside the formal Community processes, as in the case of the ESDP (see
Chapters 7 and 11).
Reducing confusion over terminology
The problem of the meaning of terms is multiplied with the many other concepts that are
employed in territorial cooperation. Williams (1996) makes this point with reference to the
concept of ‘inner city’. In British English inner city has a very speciﬁc meaning, which excludes
the city centre. In German the literal translation Innenstadt includes the city centre. Williams
recalls how inner city was translated into the Italian centro storico – the historic centre, ‘the 
logic being that city centres are almost always also historic centres in Italy. A double transposition of meaning had occurred, neatly demonstrating the cultural dimension of professional
terminology’ (p. 60).
Three approaches are used to reduce the difﬁculties of terminology: the use of multilingual
glossaries; the creation of universal conceptual terms; and agreements on speciﬁc nomenclatures
(the naming of things).
30
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 60

Glossaries
The European institutions make extensive use of glossaries. The European Commission has a
general glossary of 233 general concepts used in the European Union, which is provided on the
Europa website in 11 languages.3 There are many other speciﬁc Community glossaries covering
topics such as enlargement, the information society and regional development.4 In recognition
of the widespread use of synonyms for the same concept, the Commission also provides the
EUROVOC multilingual thesaurus. It is possible to search for a term in any one of the 23 ofﬁcial
languages to ﬁnd out its relationships with other terms and a list of synonyms in other
languages.
There have been several attempts to create glossaries speciﬁcally for spatial planning. The
European Conference of Ministers of Spatial Planning (CEMAT) of the Council of Europe
published its Spatial Development Glossary in 2007, with 68 concepts, categories and terms used
in areas related to spatial development (CEMAT 2007). The aim is speciﬁcally ‘to aid communication between professionals of the member states’ (p. 3).5 It provides terms and explanations
in English and French, recognising the dominance of these languages in the debate. Understandably, the glossary makes extensive use of the CEMAT’s own Guiding Principles for the
Sustainable Spatial Development of the European Continent (CEMAT 2000). A number of
transnational cooperation projects funded under INTERREG IIIB have also produced their own
glossaries on speciﬁc planning topics to help to agree common meanings of key terms, but access
to these is rather haphazard as many project websites quickly disappear after the completion of
the projects. One reliable source is the website of COMMIN: the Baltic Spatial Concept Share
(COMMIN 2009). As the name indicates, this source contains information only for the Baltic
Sea Region, but it is proposed that the coverage should be extended with the assistance of the
German Academy for Spatial Research (ARL). The COMMIN information includes a European
glossary and national glossaries for 11 countries that explain a term in the country’s national
language and then translate both the term and its explanation into English.
Preparing glossaries is a notoriously difﬁcult task. The deﬁnition of terms is often contested.
This is because there are different theoretical starting points for explaining concepts such as
spatial planning but also because readers will have their own ‘world view’ determined by the
context and practices that they are familiar with. For that reason, it is essential that the
explanation of terms is sound and that the source of ideas is also made explicit. In this regard,
readers will ﬁnd the CEMAT glossary (CEMAT 2007) to be a useful ﬁrst source, but it is derived
from practical experience and not very theoretically informed. For example, ‘land use planning’
and ‘zoning’ are placed in the same category when they are different concepts,6 and the source
of the deﬁnition of spatial planning is given as the Wikipedia Online Encyclopaedia, itself a
contested source of knowledge. In contrast, in the COMMIN glossaries there is a deﬁnition for
each term and there are often clear references to the literature showing the source (including
page numbers).
Box 3.2 provides a glossary that explains the relationship between key European-level
spatial planning terms.
Universal concepts
A second way in which cross-national differences in meaning can be addressed is to use more
neutral and universal concepts for spatial planning that are not based on a benchmark of any
one country. This does not obviate the need for glossaries or explanations of country speciﬁc
terms, but it provides a way of comparing and classifying particular aspects of spatial planning
in English (or any other language) while avoiding the dominance of Anglo-American concepts

## S. 61

32
The European dimension of spatial planning
Box 3.2 Glossary of keywords in European spatial planning
The Europeanisation of spatial planning has added a further layer of terminology to the language
of planning. It involves bringing forward into the European spatial planning debate concepts and
terms used in other ﬁelds or in national planning contexts, and the creation of wholly new
terms and acronyms. We offer here a brief introduction to some of the main terms in use in
English, especially for those who are beginning investigation of the ﬁeld of European spatial
planning.
Our starting point is the notion of spatial development. This refers ﬁrst to the geographical
distribution of the physical features in the built and natural environment and patterns and 
ﬂows of human activity. It may also embrace the social, economic and cultural aspects of
development. The notion of urban and regional or spatial planning is often equated with the policy
and regulation regimes that governments put in place to intervene in spatial development in
its narrow physical sense. In European spatial planning or spatial development policy geographical
disparities in socio-economic conditions and life chances are particularly important, with a
policy emphasis on balanced development or spatial equity. Therefore there is a close relation
in European spatial planning between regional planning and regional policy. Regional planning is
primarily concerned with inﬂuencing the organisation of the physical spatial structure within a
region. Regional policy, EU Cohesion Policy or national-regional policy concerns disparities between
regions. About one-third of the EU budget is spent on Cohesion Policy with the objectives 
of convergence of the economically weakest regions with stronger economies, improving the
economic competitiveness of weaker regions and strengthening territorial cooperation on spatial
planning.
Patterns of spatial development result from the complex interplay of market decisions
(individuals and ﬁrms) and public intervention through sectoral policy such as agriculture, health
and transport. Williams (1996) described the spatial impacts of sectoral policies as spatial policy.
European spatial planning draws attention to the often neglected spatial impacts of sectoral
policies and especially the costs of non-coordination. For example, agricultural subsidies may
impose environmental costs; transport investment may contradict economic investments; 
EU, national and regional policies may not be well aligned. The European spatial planning
approach concentrates on establishing better coordination of spatial policy: horizontally across
different sectors; vertically among different levels of government; and geographically across
administrative boundaries. This message is spelled out in the pre-eminent statement on
European spatial planning, the European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) (CSD 1999). 
It provides a reference point for spatial policy and recommends policy options to create more
polycentric spatial development, to improve urban-rural relationships, to create more parity
in access to infrastructure and knowledge, and to conserve the natural and cultural heritage.
Polycentricity means ‘many centres’ and is promoted as a way to achieve more balanced spatial
development at scales from the European to the city-region. It draws attention to the importance of the functional complementarity of places, their institutional integration and political
cooperation.
The EU’s INTERREG Initiative has been the principal means of encouraging member states
and regions to address the policy priorities of the ESDP through co-ﬁnancing of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 62

or those of a particular country. One example is the way the EU Compendium of Spatial
Planning Systems and Policies classiﬁed types of planning instrument. Note that the word
‘instrument’ is used so as to incorporate ‘the full range of documents used to express planning
policy as well as those commonly referred to as “plans”’ (CEC 1997a: 51.) It used a fourfold
classiﬁcation of types of instrument not based on scale (which is the usual approach) but on the
purpose of instruments in decision-making on spatial development:
•
statements and perspectives setting out national or regional government policy:
•
spatial perspectives or visions;
•
planning guidance with performance criteria;
•
sectoral plans for particular topics such as infrastructure;
•
strategies that do not identify precise boundaries but guide and coordinate the spatial
impacts of a number of planning and sectoral instruments:
•
prepared for the area of administrative jurisdictions, or functional areas such as
catchment areas or metropolitan regions;
•
framework instruments that identify the pattern of spatial development on detailed maps
and provide policy and performance criteria but need to be implemented through other
regulation instruments or mechanisms;
•
regulation instruments that effectively make the decision to confer development rights.

The language challenge and terminology
33
projects involving partners in different countries. There are three strands of action: cross-border,
between geographically contiguous border regions; transnational, across large multi-national
spaces; and interregional, among non-contiguous regions across the whole territory of the EU.
In the latest phase INTERREG has been incorporated into the mainstream Cohesion Policy
under the objective of territorial cooperation. Under the INTERREG initiative transnational
spatial visions have been prepared to provide an agenda for future transnational cooperation.
The need to work across national borders is justiﬁed with reference to transnationality, which
means having an effect in more than one country.
Transnational actions on spatial planning should meet the subsidiarity principle, which means
that competences should only be ceded to higher jurisdictions when there is demonstrable
need or beneﬁt to be gained. The European treaties that outline the Community’s competences do not make reference to spatial planning, but the Lisbon Treaty (2007) promotes the
overarching objective of territorial cohesion. This makes more explicit the spatial or territorial
dimension to social and economic goals.
A commitment to pursue territorial cohesion and polycentric development of the EU 
has been made by all member states through the Territorial Agenda of the European Union
(EU Ministers 2007a). The Agenda is informed by the ﬁndings from ESPON: the European
Spatial Planning Observation Network. The Territorial Agenda is something of an update of the
ESDP though it pays more attention to economic growth and jobs in the light of the Lisbon-
Gothenburg Agenda, which sets out the overarching priority of the EU to develop the
knowledge economy in the interests of global economic competitiveness.
Source: the glossary is based on an earlier version by Nadin and Dühr (2005) and Nadin and Dühr (2007). All of
the terms introduced here are explained more fully elsewhere in this book as indicated in the index.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 63

There is generally a strong correspondence between the purpose and scale of a planning
instrument, but note that a regulation instrument might operate at the national scale and a
national policy might apply to one particular location.
The use of ‘neutral concepts’ needs to be considered carefully. It should be self-evident that
these terms are not completely neutral and that there is some overlap with terms used (in home
language) in particular countries. Generally, it is more legitimate to explain the different
meanings that are ascribed to the same terms in different places, because they can only be fully
understood in relation to their context, or to put it another way, they must be contextualised.
It is futile to seek complete equivalence of concepts across countries and cultures. However, it
is equally futile to say that there is no basis for comparison. Comparative researchers tend to
fall back on the requirement for ‘functional equivalence’ of concepts, an approach that has been
widely used in comparative law. It requires that ‘concepts selected are broad enough to
encompass heterogeneous legal institutions that are functionally comparable’ (Hantrais 2008:
77). This was the approach adopted in the EU Compendium described above. The 15 countries
that contributed to the Compendium were able to allocate their speciﬁc instruments within a
broad and more neutral and universal conceptual categorisation that was not based on the
system of any one country.
Nomenclature
The European Commission provides extensive guidance on nomenclature, that is, the
speciﬁcation and ‘naming of parts’ so that precise deﬁnitions and names are given to particular
things. Two aspects of nomenclature are considered here: guidance on the choice of some key
terms and abbreviations; and the deﬁnition of regions through the NUTS system.
Guidance on key terminology in English, linguistic conventions and how to correctly express
the working of the European Union are given in the EU English Style Guide: A Handbook for
Authors and Translators in the European Commission (CEC 2007a) and the Interinstitutional
Style Guide (CEC 2008d), both of which are available online. The ﬁrst part of the Handbook
gives a comprehensive overview of the preferred style for EU documents and legislation
including the use of British English spelling and points on grammar. The second part explains
the correct terms to use when writing about the European Union. The Interinstitutional Style
Guide is also in two parts: the ﬁrst deals with writing for the Ofﬁcial Journal and the second
covers similar topics to the handbook. Both sources give very clear advice on the correct terms
to use for the European institutions, various procedures and instruments such as the Treaties
and EU legislation, and even the correct names to use for the member states (it also recommends
using the ‘native form’ of the name when possible). Other useful information includes the 
proper use of acronyms and abbreviations and a guide to the graphics and emblems used in 
the EU. The abbreviations for member states and the various aggregates of the EU are given 
in Table 3.3.
NUTS is the abbreviation for nomenclature des unités territoriales statistiques (nomenclature
of territorial units for statistics). The EU statistical ofﬁce Eurostat established NUTS more than
30 years ago in order to provide a single uniform breakdown of territorial units for the
production of regional statistics for the European Union. It is a five-level hierarchical
classiﬁcation that subdivides each member state into NUTS 1 regions, each of which is in turn
subdivided into NUTS 2 regions and so on. The units are based primarily on existing
institutional subdivisions within the member states, although some regional statistical units 
were speciﬁcally set up for this purpose. At the regional level (without taking the municipalities
into account), the administrative structure of the member states generally comprises two 
main regional levels (Länder and Kreise in Germany, régions and départements in France,
34
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 64

Comunidades autonomas and provincias in Spain, regioni and provincie in Italy, and so on).
The full list of NUTS regions is given in Table 3.4.
Since the 1990s, the European Commission (DG REGIO) has used the spatial data collected
and compiled according to the NUTS units for determining the eligibility of regions for
structural funding and to inform the evaluations of the effectiveness of EU regional policy,
among other purposes (see Chapter 18). The NUTS classiﬁcation has been used in Community
legislation since 1988, but only in 2003 was a Regulation of the European Parliament and of
the Council on the NUTS adopted (1059/2003) (Council 2003a). The NUTS Regulation sets
out the aim to have harmonised and stable regional statistics across the EU.
The Regulation lays down minimum and maximum thresholds for the average size of the
NUTS regions (i.e. NUTS 1 from 3 to 7 million; NUTS 2 from 800,000 to 3 million; and NUTS
3 from 150,000 to 800,000 inhabitants). The Regulation defines two levels of ‘local
administrative units’ (LAUs) below the NUTS-3 regions. These LAUs were called NUTS levels
4 and 5 until July 2003, and although this label was ofﬁcially abolished by the Regulation, they
are sometimes still described as such. It is important to note that not all countries have every
level of division, depending on their size. One of the most extreme cases is Luxembourg, which
has only LAUs; the three NUTS divisions each correspond to the entire country itself. NUTS

## S. 67

applies only to EU member states although similar divisions have been created in the countries
of the European Economic Area and Switzerland.
Conclusion
The aim of this chapter was to raise awareness of the potential problems that arise from working
on European spatial planning across many languages and cultures, and to provide guidelines
for how they might be avoided. Many planning practitioners and researchers in Europe now
routinely work in the English language. This is needed to facilitate cooperation, but it also
presents the additional risk of unwittingly imposing an Anglo-Saxon view of the world on the
rest of Europe. There is lots of scope for misunderstanding from international exchange, but
there is also much to learn from the difﬁculties of translating terms from one place to another.
There are some useful and unambiguous guidelines on aspects of terminology from the
European institutions, and there are other conventions that are introduced above, but there are
few hard and fast rules – indeed, it is best to avoid any rigid directions about how a term in
one language should be translated into another. The issues of terminology and the meaning of
terms crop up in other chapters of this book. The lessons from this chapter are to take care in
writing, to explain meanings carefully with reference to the context if need be, and especially
to recognise that one’s meaning may not be shared by one’s readers.
Further reading
For an overview of EU language policy see the Communication Multilingualism: An Asset for
Europe (CEC 2008a: 3). The chapter on ‘Language and meaning: the Tower of Babel problem’
in Williams (1996: 55–62) is as relevant for spatial planning now as it was when published,
though of course some of the speciﬁc terms have changed. Reference has been made above to
the EU English Style Guide: A Handbook for Authors and Translators in the European
Commission (CEC 2007a), which is indispensable for ensuring that authors use the proper
names for EU institutions and procedures.
38
The European dimension of spatial planning
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 68

The spatial development
context for European spatial
planning
Spatial planners increasingly ﬁnd themselves in situations where they need to consider a
European or transnational dimension of spatial development, and have to ﬁnd appropriate
spatial planning responses in cooperation with other European regions. For instance, the
freedoms of movement of the Single European Market have prompted interlinkages of housing
and labour markets in cross-border regions, and this raises issues for the joint planning and
provision of services such as schools or hospitals, and efﬁcient transport connections across
national borders.
In Chapter 4, the main large-scale spatial development trends that affect the regions in Europe
are presented. Recognising that decisions taken outside one’s own administrative system also
affect the spatial development of domestic territories has prompted discussion on an EU spatial
development policy in the 1990s (discussed in Chapter 14). In turn, this has stimulated
considerable activity on better understanding and conceptualisation of the structure of the 
EU territory. There are now many examples of national and regional spatial plans that present
a transnational perspective on spatial development, as are there conceptualizations of the
European space. These are discussed in Chapter 5. Recognising the European dimension to
spatial development implies the availability of comparable spatial data at EU level. There are
numerous initiatives on spatial data and spatial information for the EU territory under way, and
these are presented in Chapter 6.

## S. 69

Spatial development trends 
in Europe and the key issues 
for spatial planning
The EU is subject to changes of its spatial structure to an extent not normally experienced by
individual nation-states, and not solely the result of successive enlargements . . . Their implications
need to be grasped if the capacity to think European is to be acquired.
(Williams 1996: 91)
Introduction
The process of European integration, alongside the effects of globalisation, has considerable
consequences for spatial development. Regions and cities in the Single European Market are
becoming increasingly interlinked with more and more people living and working in more than
one country. Trade ﬂows in the European Union have considerably increased since the 1950s,
accompanied by signiﬁcant trafﬁc impacts and environmental pollution. In this chapter, major
economic, societal and environmental trends that affect the spatial development of the EU
territory, and the key issues that emerge from these trends for spatial planning, are discussed.
By ‘spatial development’, we refer not merely to spatial impacts in relation to physical infrastructure or changes to the built environment but also more generally to the way in which global
and European trends affect land use, environmental quality and the socio-economic performance
of Europe’s cities and regions. Spatial planners working at all scales have to be increasingly
aware of such trends and their regional and local impacts so as to be able to address undesirable
changes to the spatial structure of their territories.
Given the breadth of the topic and the complexity of spatial development trends and their
impacts on the territory, the discussion in this chapter necessarily has to be selective. Before
giving an overview of the major spatial development trends in Europe, the key characteristics
of the spatial structure of the European territory are discussed.
The spatial structure of Europe
In comparison to the USA or the emerging economies of India or China, the geographical and
cultural diversity of Europe is noteworthy. The European continent is characterised by a highly
diverse spatial structure, with a long coastline, numerous islands and peninsulas, large rivers
(such as the Rhine and Danube), mountain ranges (such as the Alps and Pyrenees) and comparatively large distances between the far ‘corners’ of the continent. There are signiﬁcant climatic
differences between north, south, east and west, with considerable variation in landscapes and
biodiversity. Besides its varied geography, Europe is also characterised by many different
cultures, with their historical development affecting the spatial structure and administrative
organisation of modern-day territories. For example, the decentralised settlement pattern of
Chapter 4
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 70

Germany today, characterised by numerous medium-sized cities rather than one dominant
capital, has its roots in the sovereign states that were only uniﬁed into the German Empire in
1871. More recent historical developments have also left their mark on the spatial structure 
of Europe, as the transformation of the former socialist countries and cities in central and 
eastern Europe shows. The fall of the ‘Iron Curtain’ in 1991 has also brought to the fore the
question of the political, ideological and geographical boundaries of the European continent.
Frequently, these debates ﬂare up in relation to questions over eligibility for EU membership,
as for example in the case of Turkey (Drevet 2007).
Although the EU is, of course, not synonymous with the European continent, successive
enlargements have resulted in most European countries now being members of the EU of 27
(see Figure 1.1). Several countries have expressed an interest in joining the EU in the future,
whereas others, including Switzerland and Norway, are currently not pursuing membership
status. Given the spatial diversity of the European continent and numerous changes to the
boundaries of the EU territory as a consequence of frequent enlargements of the Union,
thinking about spatial development in the EU implies considerable challenges. They are
exacerbated by the fact that the supranational scale is still mostly outside normal professional
experience. In relation to the European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) process in the
1990s (see Chapters 14 and 15), for example, Drevet (2007: 150) has argued that:
instead of having plenty of time to assess a stable European territory and to draw up a strategic
document valid for a couple of decades, EU planners urgently needed to develop among
member states a common perception of the new territorial challenges – for example, the
opening of the eastern borders, the reorganization of Trans-European Networks, and the
importance of depolluting transnational catchment areas and inland seas – and to adapt
Community instruments to rapid changes in the conﬁguration of a rapidly expanding territory.
One of the most important issues for spatial development responses at EU level arises from
the level of urbanisation. The EU territory is highly urbanised, with approximately 75 per cent
of the population living in urban areas (EEA 2006a) (see Plate 1). In comparison to other parts
of the world, the majority of Europe’s cities and towns are comparatively small and the
settlement pattern is decentralised, with Paris and London ranking among the ‘world cities’ (see
Sassen 1991).1
However, there are considerable differences in the level of urbanisation across Europe, with
the north-west being generally more urbanised, while other regions, such as northern Finland
and Sweden, parts of France and the new member states are predominantly rural in character.
The negative effects of ongoing urbanisation and urban sprawl are given considerable attention
in EU policy debates, as the expansion of urban areas may lead to environmental damage and
landscape fragmentation (EEA 2006a). This is of particular concern in coastal regions and the
densely populated part of north-west Europe (CEC 2007b).
Beyond the territory of the ‘mainland’ EU-27 regions there are the ‘outermost regions’ and
overseas territories, and these add considerably to the territorial diversity of the Union. These
are territories that are not located on the European continent but have gained EU membership
alongside the accession of their mainland territories. The seven outermost regions of the EU
member states (Azores and Madeira (Portugal); Canary Islands (Spain); and the French Overseas
Departments (DOMs – départements d’outre-mer – of French Guiana, Guadeloupe, Martinique
and Réunion) are part of the European Union and EU law applies to them. They participate in
EU funding programmes (e.g. three out of 13 transnational territorial cooperation programmes
in the 2007–13 funding period cover the EU’s outermost regions; see Chapter 16). They can elect
members of the European Parliament, and their requirements must be taken into consideration

## S. 71

when EU policies are drawn up. In addition to these outermost regions, there are currently 
21 overseas countries and territories (OCTs) that have a special relationship with one of 
the member states of the EU: 12 with the United Kingdom (e.g. Bermuda, Falkland Islands), 
six with France (e.g. the TOMs – territoires d’outre-mer – of French Polynesia or New
Caledonia), two with the Netherlands (Aruba, Netherlands Antilles) and one with Denmark
(Greenland). Although the nationals of these OCTs are in principle EU citizens, they are not
part of the EU and EU law applies to them only insofar it is necessary to implement 
the association agreements that many of these regions have entered with the EU. They are also
eligible for participation in and funding from EU programmes, such as the Research Framework
Programme, education and training programmes and the Competitiveness and Innovation
Framework Programme. On the other hand, there are parts of the British Isles that are not
particularly peripheral and clearly located on the European continent but are not members 
of the EU. These are Jersey and Guernsey in the English Channel and the Isle of Man in the
Irish Sea. Gibraltar is not a member of the EU either, although it does beneﬁt from some
programmes (Williams 1996).
The geographical, political and cultural characteristics of Europe and the EU territory have
been the starting point for spatial planning debates within the Council of Europe, the EU
institutions and its member states. It has been argued that spatial development responses for
Europe or the EU have to be sufﬁciently differentiated to respect this geographical and cultural
variety (Drevet 2007). The changing political and economic geography of the EU, discussed in
the following sections, adds to the challenge of understanding the EU as a planning subject.
The changing political geography of the EU
Williams (1996: 91) points out that ‘the EU is subject to changes of its spatial structure to an
extent not normally experienced by individual nation-states, and not solely the result of the
successive enlargements’. The inherently dynamic nature of a changing political and economic
geography of the EU territory presents considerable challenges for spatial planners trying to
grasp its characteristics.
The successive enlargements of the EU have resulted in a Union of 27 member states (in
2009), which is one of the most powerful in the world with regard to population and economic
performance. The two most recent EU enlargements in 2004 and 2007 (see Chapter 12) have
become possible only because of major political transitions over the past 20 years: the collapse
of the former USSR and eastern European Communist regimes between 1989 and 1991; the
subsequent end of the Cold War; the reuniﬁcation of Germany of 1990; and the transition to
a market economy and to liberal democracies in most central and eastern European countries.
Alongside the geographical expansion, over the past decades the EU has also increased its role
in international political decision-making and has expanded its inﬂuence on policy- and decisionmaking within the member states. For planners, many policy inﬂuences today, for example in
the ﬁeld of environment or agriculture, originate from EU law and policy.
The expansion of EU competences through successive Treaty revisions has prompted
governance shifts whereby ‘nation states are said to be transferring their power both upwards
to supranational institutions (such as the European Union) and downwards by conceding
power to regional governments’ (Amin and Tomaney 1995: 171). Such ‘rescaling processes’ 
have increased attention on ‘regions’ (Brenner 2004). However, a ‘region’ can be many 
things, as explained in Box 4.1. The concerns of regional governments about their inﬂuence in
the EU, especially powerful ones such as the German Länder, led to the setting up of the
Committee of the Regions (CoR) by the Treaty on the EU (as explained in Chapter 9) and
prompted sub-national governments to set up representation ofﬁces in Brussels (as explained in

Spatial development trends and key issues
43
Box 4.1 What is a region?
The term ‘region’ has numerous deﬁnitions and understandings. The classical concept of regions is
based on the French school of geographers of the early twentieth century, most notably Vidal de la
Blanche, who identiﬁed the notion of pays that were considered to be areas that shared an original
way of life, and speciﬁc local characteristics such as a distinctive economy, pattern of settlement,
building styles and even language and dialect. This is a different meaning to the areas of elected
regional governments or regional administrative units that are now in place in all EU member states,
partly as a result of the EU’s Cohesion Policy. The EU’s NUTS system (see Chapter 3) seeks to
provide a standardised set of ‘regions’, which is different again. The deﬁnitions overlap. The NUTS
regions have their roots in existing sub-national administrative units (or sovereign states that were
later uniﬁed) that have historically had important government functions. In some countries regions
have been newly created as part of a process of decentralisation or speciﬁcally to administer EU
funding, and stimulating a common regional identity for such territories can be challenging.
The complexities of trying to compare ‘regions’ and regional planning across Europe do not
end there. For example, the German Länder are based on the administrative boundaries of federal
states that were deﬁned after the Second World War. Planning at this level is often described
in the English-language literature as regional planning. However, in most of Germany there is in
addition a sub-regional tier of planning between the Länder and the local authority, which 
in German is called Regionalplanung that also literally translates as regional planning. The deﬁnition
of these planning regions is the sole responsibility of the individual German Länder, which results
in very different size, organisation and powers attached to their respective ‘regional’ administrations (Dühr 2005). The 12 Dutch provinces are sometimes described as regions but in comparison
to the German Länder are much smaller. Four administrative ‘regions’ (North, East, West, South)
have been deﬁned for the management of EU funding. In England, regions have gained more
prominence following devolution and decentralisation during the 1990s. This involved devolution to three ‘home countries’ of Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland and decentralisation of
administration to a number of bodies at the ‘regional tier’ in England. The deﬁnition of these
regions is not altogether satisfactory: Greater London, for example, lies in three regions (Crow
2002). Also, this system, like the ‘regions’ of Spain, is a hybrid system. The ‘regions’ here have
very different levels of autonomy; some would be more properly described as nations.
While many regional governments across Europe are in the process of establishing 
their standing, planning processes at the same time are seeking appropriate responses to new
‘functional regions’. These are areas that have functional interdependencies most often related to
transport or water catchments, or economic relations as in city-regions. The need to cooperate
on transport infrastructure or on environmental issues that stretch across administrative
boundaries is self-evident, and this is central to the idea of transnational planning. Functional regions
are given increasing attention in both the academic debate and planning practice, but they are rarely
part of the administrative or cultural geography of a country.
In sum, the word ‘region’ should be used with care, especially when comparing one country
with another. Regions will differ in their size and power. Some regions will form an important
part of the government administration with an elected assembly or parliament; others will be
administrative units with no political power; and others will be deﬁned for statistical, analytical
or policy purposes only. There will also be different types of region within the same country,
especially when sectors organise their regional structures in different ways.
Source: This discussion is based on Dühr (2005).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 73

Chapter 10). Elsewhere, there have been more forceful demands for regional autonomy, as for
instance in the Basque country of Spain. In traditionally highly centralised countries, such as
France, efforts have been made to decentralise state functions to regional authorities. Elsewhere,
the requirements of the EU’s Cohesion Policy (see Chapter 18) have resulted in the creation of
regional administrations in countries where previously there was no regional tier 
(as in Ireland) or in the strengthening of regional competences where regions were traditionally
weak (as in Portugal).
Decisions about the location of EU institutions also affect the political geography of Europe.
Although the EU has neither an acknowledged capital city nor formal processes for deciding 
the location of its institutions (as nation-states do), Brussels has many of the characteristics 
of a capital in terms of the seat of administration (the Commission) and decision-making 
(the Council). The political weight that comes with these institutions, and the effect of this
institutional density on the economy, environment and society of the Brussels urban region is
undeniable (Baeten 2001; Lagrou 2000). Most regions, as well as industry and interest groups,
have a representation in Brussels, and the scale of lobbying to the EU institutions is considerable
(see Chapter 10). While Brussels is thus almost the de facto capital of the EU, several of its
institutions are located elsewhere, and the political debates surrounding the decisions on where
to locate major EU institutions demonstrate the importance attached to them (Lagrou 2000).
The establishment of the European Central Bank in Frankfurt/Main in 1998 for example, has
arguably played a major part in strengthening the city’s role as a major ﬁnancial centre.
Despite the clustering of EU institutions in Brussels and north-west Europe, the system of
rotating EU presidencies has to date ensured that the spotlight regularly also falls on capital cities
and regions in other parts of the EU. At present every member state of the EU has a central role
in EU policy-making by hosting a European Council and other key meetings at regular intervals.
The Lisbon Treaty has changed this arrangement to an 18-month rotating presidency shared by
a trio of member states, and for the European Council to become an institution with its own
permanent President of the European Council (see Chapter 12). Some member states could feel
increasingly marginalised by the proposed changes, and it has been argued that they ‘as a result
may be expected to look even more closely at future locational decisions of EU institutions’
(Williams 1996: 94). But also national decisions on the location of key institutions can 
have spatial development effects at the EU level. For example, the decision to designate Berlin
as the capital of the reunited Federal Republic of Germany in 1991 resulted in a relocation of
the federal ministries and main institutions from Bonn to Berlin. This decision prompted the
improvement of the transport connections of Berlin and the new Länder to western capital
regions and the EU institutions in Brussels, as well as decisions on numerous major infrastructure
projects to connect Germany better to its eastern European neighbours, thus contributing to a
shift in political and economic attention towards the east (Williams 1996).
The changing economic geography of the EU
Since the 1960s, the economic geography of the EU has changed considerably as a result of the
combined processes of European integration, globalisation, liberalisation and economic
restructuring. ‘Globalisation trends’ are variably deﬁned, but among them are: the growth of
global financial trade; the globalization of certain media (e.g. MTV and CNN); global
consumption of certain products (e.g. Starbucks, McDonald’s) and the homogenisation of
cultures; global migration ﬂows; and the growing importance of international institutions such
as the World Bank, NATO and the EU (Baeten 2001; Short and Kim 1999). In 2006, the EU-
27 accounted for almost one-ﬁfth of the world’s imports and exports of goods (see Table 4.1),
with the United States being the most important market for EU-27 exports.
44
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 74

Globalisation of economies is facilitated by information and communication technologies
(ICTs) and modern transport connections. These have considerably reduced the time needed for
travel and communication, with the effect that ‘connectivity’ is now frequently more decisive
for participation in social and economic processes than spatial proximity. As a result, economic
competition between countries and regions for foreign investment and international companies
has signiﬁcantly increased on a global scale. For the EU, globalisation and integration processes
are resulting in a concentration of economic development and the location of investments in the
central (and accessible) ‘core’ of continental Europe and a number of strong urban areas outside
this core region, including Madrid, Barcelona, Athens, Stockholm, Helsinki, Warsaw and
Budapest (ESPON 2006a).
The effects of globalisation trends have been much debated in the literature, particularly in
relation to the role of ‘local places’, which are sometimes portrayed as unable to inﬂuence their
fate in a global economic borderless space. Yet, it has been pointed out that global ﬂows are
also ‘reshaped by the unique social, economic and political settings of cities on which they
“descend”, and how this local transformation of the global in turn creates speciﬁc built
environments, power struggles and governance problems that cannot be understood unless in
terms of “glocalization”’ (Baeten 2001: 120). Cities, thus, ‘are not simply at the mercy of free-
ﬂoating global capital (or international governmental institutions for that matter)’ and while
‘cities may need global companies and institutions . . . the reverse is equally true: global
companies and institutions need the globalized environment of world cities if they are to
maintain their command over the international scale’ (Baeten 2001: 120).
These globalisation trends and their effects on the economic geography of Europe have been
accompanied by major processes of economic restructuring in Western economies during the
second part of the twentieth century. The considerable de-industrialisation with a general decline
in manufacturing production and the relocation of industry to other places, especially southeast Asia, corresponds with a shift to increasing investment and employment in the service sector
and ‘high-tech’ industries across Europe. This has been accompanied by more ﬂexible systems
of production and work procedures and an increase in many small and medium-sized enterprises
(SMEs), with a consequently reduced need for large-scale industrial plants. The changes also
create a more complex labour market with an increasing emphasis on part-time working, jobsharing and temporary contracts. This has prompted a trend towards growing social exclusion
and labour market polarisation, which favours the well-educated and middle-aged, and
disadvantages the younger, older and less well educated. The much debated ‘crisis of the welfare
state’ and trends towards an increasingly ‘neo-liberal Europe’2 (Amin and Tomaney 1995) have
