## S. 75

resulted in a reduction of public expenditure and an increasing withdrawal of the nation-state
from economic intervention. Harvey (2005: 2) has pointed to the ‘emphatic turn towards
neoliberalism in political-economic practices and thinking since the 1970s’. Deregulation,
privatisation, and withdrawal of the state from many areas of social provision have been
common to different degrees across Europe. Almost all states, from those newly created after
the collapse of the Soviet Union to old-style social democracies and welfare states, such as
Sweden, have embraced some version of neoliberal theory and adjusted at least some policies
and practices accordingly (Harvey 2005).
As a consequence there has been a strong trend towards the liberalisation of public services
in Europe (supported by the EU’s single market objectives and accompanying measures – see
Chapter 17), and public policy in many countries has been reshaped to follow an ‘entrepreneurial’ model that relies on ﬁnancial and ﬁscal incentives (rather than public subsidies) to
encourage the start-up of new businesses and investments in economic development. Public–
private partnerships (PPPs) and property-led urban regeneration based on ﬂagship schemes 
are today commonly used to lead major spatial development projects. The privatisation and
restructuring of public services and utilities, such as transport and telecommunication services,
is another result of macroeconomic trends that tend to reduce public budgets in the provision
of public services.
These general economic trends have considerable implications for the economic map of
Europe and its spatial structure. New economic activities and an increasing reliance on market
forces result in changing demands on space – in terms of location, land use and movement –
with a great impact on overall spatial development at local, national and European scales. For
example, they tend to favour a ‘growing concentration of corporate power, high-level business
and banking services, research and development (R&D) and advanced manufacturing producing
the technically sophisticated new products in existing “core” regions’ (Hudson 1999: 61). This
increases the socio-spatial distance between the core and the more peripheral regions. The
implementation of the Single European Market may have further contributed to regional
disparities in the EU:
There are reasons to think that, other things being equal, the removal of non-tariff barriers
and the moves towards economic and monetary union will exacerbate these problems of
unbalanced trade ﬂows, job transfer, and the imposition of constraints on national policies.
. . . differences in the productive capabilities of producers in different regions will be
equalised only slowly in the absence of sizeable inter-regional investments.
(Dunford 1999: 183)
The combination of these economic trends with successive enlargements has contributed to
an EU with great regional disparities. The EU-27 is characterised by an economic core (broadly
covering the area between London, Paris, Milan, Munich and Hamburg) where most of the EU’s
GDP is concentrated. However, from the 1980s socio-economic disparities between the poorer
and the richer member states in the ‘old EU-15’ have reduced (partly, it is argued, because of
EU Cohesion Policy, as explained in Chapter 18). The poorest (so-called cohesion) countries of
Greece, Spain, Ireland and Portugal have to some extent ‘caught up’ with their neighbours. At
the same time, regional disparities at the sub-national level within member states have increased
(see Figure 4.1), with uneven economic growth and considerable variation in the performance
of regional labour markets across the EU. In the wider EU-27, enlargement has in addition meant
an increase in interregional disparities, with considerable differences between old and new
member states (see Plate 2). For instance, the EU enlargement to 25 member countries in 2004
increased the EU territory by 34 per cent and its population by 20 per cent, but added only 
5 per cent to its GDP.
46
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 76

However, while enlargement has thus so far led to a decline of average GDP per capita across
the EU by 18 per cent, the new member states seem to be catching up. Some, such as Slovenia
and Estonia, experienced high growth rates in the early 2000s, and new growth centres outside
the traditional ‘core’ of the EU have been emerging, such as Dublin, Madrid, Helsinki and
Stockholm (ESPON 2003, 2006a). The scale of economic growth experienced in the eastern
member states over past years, especially in comparison to the low growth rates in some of the
old member states, such as Germany and France, is in the longer term expected to shift the
economic centre of gravity of Europe eastwards. However, the process of ‘catching up’ in terms
of GDP per capita may in some parts of Europe take several decades still (or may be thwarted
by the economic crisis), and the current picture is thus one of considerable differences in
standards of living between east and west, but also increasingly between metropolitan and more
rural areas. These polarisation tendencies within the member states are reinforced by the
competition between metropolitan regions acting as ‘global players’ by attracting key companies
and highly skilled labour. Large cities have adopted policies to strengthen their internationally
competitive proﬁle, ‘amongst them explicit place marketing, intended primarily to enhance and
demonstrate their attractiveness to mobile investment and consumption’ (Ward 1998: 2).
However, aside from negative effects for the more peripheral and rural parts of the EU territory
that are lagging behind in terms of economic performance, there are also negative externalities
that come with the economic success of capital cities and metropolitan regions – such as housing
shortages, increasing congestion and accompanying environmental problems.
The long-standing objective to achieve social and economic cohesion in the EU is supported
through considerable regional policy intervention that has, over recent years, been aimed at both
assisting regions that are lagging behind and stimulating the economic competitiveness of the
EU territory as a whole (Chapter 18). The argument for EU intervention through Cohesion
Policy derives from the concern over continuing trends towards larger disparities:
Agglomeration economies accrue in the core, whereas the periphery cannot take full
advantage of economies of scale afforded by integration. So integration needs a helping hand
in improving spatial relations by overcoming distance and/or physical barriers in the
interest of European cohesion.
(Faludi 1999: 338)

## S. 77

The support through EU Cohesion Policy to stimulate economic competitiveness (especially in
the ﬁeld of research, technology and development) in pursuit of the ‘Lisbon’ objectives to become
the most dynamic and competitive knowledge-based economy in the world may, however, have
an ‘in-built tendency to favour the richer regions of the Community, reinforcing the advantages
of “islands of innovation” and the qualitative gap in development potential between the regions
of the EU’ (Hudson 1999: 61). Rather than balancing out disparities, therefore, such EU policy
intervention is feared to exacerbate differences in productivity and standards of living across
the territory.
Trends in transport, accessibility and information and
communication technologies
The processes of globalisation and European integration (with increasing trade and other interdependencies in the EU territory) have led to a considerable increase in the volume of trafﬁc. As
a consequence, congestion is worsening, especially in the ‘core’ of Europe. The major transport
ﬂows are now more multi-directional than they were ten years ago, with east-west ﬂows
complementing the traditional north-south movements. The least sustainable transport modes
of road and air have experienced the largest increase in both passenger and freight volume. The
emergence of low-cost airlines has supported the substantial increase in air travel within Europe
and the expansion of regional airports across the territory, with air passenger trafﬁc growth of
7.3 per cent between 2006 and 2007 alone (De La Fuente Layos 2009). The environmental and
land use implications of such increased mobility are considerable, and illustrated by noise and
air quality concerns and related health problems in areas with heavy trafﬁc. These trends have
prompted EU policy to generally support a ‘modal shift’ to more sustainable modes of transport
and to decouple transport growth from economic growth (see Chapter 19). However, many
trends in transport growth are of a global nature, and the inﬂuence of EU policy measures may
therefore be limited. For example, the continuous growth of container trafﬁc in ports is a result
of the increase in intercontinental rather than European ﬂows.
Across the EU, accessibility to rail, road and air transport infrastructure shows a clear coreperiphery pattern, with Germany, the Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, northern Italy and
the Paris and London regions beneﬁting from high accessibility potential, as shown in Plate 3. 
The implementation of the Single Market and increased freedom of movement across the EU
is complemented by major infrastructure investments that have a signiﬁcant impact on spatial
development. Major infrastructure developments, often realised with European support under
the TEN-T programme (see Chapter 19) have altered the accessibility of certain parts of the EU’s
territory (most notably the more central regions). Of particular signiﬁcance are the many
investments in interregional high-speed train (HST) connections, albeit with considerable
national differences (see Figure 4.2).
The spatial implications of such major infrastructure investments are considerable. The
Channel Tunnel between England and France and the Öresund Bridge between Denmark 
and Sweden (see Figure 1.2) are examples of transport infrastructure connecting regions and
countries previously divided by sea. The ﬁxed links support wider policy objectives beyond
improving transport, such as creating a larger integrated labour market around Malmö 
and Copenhagen.3 They also have other, sometimes unintended, effects, such as challenging
‘cultural boundaries’. For example, Williams (1996: 92) has argued that the UK’s ‘island
mentality’ was put to the test when the Channel Tunnel rail link was implemented. Fixed
infrastructure links may also have implications for the housing markets of the newly connected
regions as a result of large regional variations in house prices (as between Greater London and
the north of France).
48
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 78

There are numerous other examples of major European infrastructure projects that, by connecting previously largely separated parts of the EU territory, alter the spatial-economic structure
of Europe. Many, such as the Fehmarn Belt between Denmark and Germany,5 combine road
connections with both freight and passenger rail lines. Some infrastructure can have very distant
impacts. The largest seaport in Europe and the world’s seventh largest container port, Rotterdam
serves an area stretching far beyond the Alps, with major transport impacts in its Europeanwide ‘hinterland’. Much trafﬁc from Rotterdam will ﬁnd its way to Switzerland, which is one
of Europe’s major junctions for freight, with more than 4,000 heavy lorries crossing the Swiss
Alps daily by road. Two major north-south rail tunnels through the Alps in Switzerland have
therefore been initiated to deal with the increasing transport volumes. The Lötschberg Tunnel
(opened in 2007) and the Gotthard Base Tunnel (expected to open in 2015) are among the
longest rail tunnels worldwide and intended to support the shift from road freight to rail in order
to ease congestion and air pollution in the Alps.
In terms of passenger transport, progress with the implementation of the high-speed train
network has mixed effects on spatial development. On the one hand it signiﬁcantly increases
the accessibility of cities that are directly serviced, but on the other hand it is often accompanied
by the reduction of passenger services on the secondary routes, and this means that many smaller
cities and towns are less well connected. Figure 4.3 shows how shorter travel times between
nodes on the high-speed rail network affect the time-space geography of Europe. In this
illustration, travel time (rather than geographical distance) is taken as the determining
coordinates, showing that through HST investments the densely populated regions in the core

## S. 79

The spatial development context

## S. 80

of Europe become even better connected and travel times between European metropolitan
regions are shrinking as a result. In contrast, the more peripheral parts of the territory are
becoming more marginalised.
The European continent is notable for its many waterways, and recent investments to
improve accessibility for waterborne trade will also have an impact on the spatial-economic
structure of the territory (INE undated). The Main-Danube Canal link, for instance, was
completed in 1992 and is 170 km long. It provides a navigable connection for large barges
between the Rhine Delta (at Rotterdam in the Netherlands) and the Danube Delta in eastern
Romania.
Besides investments in rail and waterway infrastructure (and somewhat at odds with the
general objective to promote sustainable mobility), several major road infrastructure projects
are also currently being co-ﬁnanced by the EU. One example is the proposed motorway
expansion of the existing road from Prague (Czech Republic) to Helsinki (Finland), via Poland,
Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia: Route E 67 between Warsaw and Tallinn, also known as the ‘Via
Baltica’. The expansion is expected to considerably improve the connection of the Baltic States
with other parts of Europe. However, the Via Baltica is a highly controversial project, as the
originally proposed route would have destroyed important wetlands of the Rospuda Valley in
Poland that are protected under the EU Habitats Directive (Council 1992). For these
infringements of EU law the Polish government was called before the European Court of Justice
in March 2007, and a debate has begun on possible alternative routes that would leave the
nature protection sites unharmed (Via Baltica 2007).
Although transport infrastructure is often considered a crucial precondition for economic
competitiveness, the relationship between accessibility and economic development is generally
more complex than assumed. The example of Ireland, which experienced high economic growth
rates during the 1990s despite below average levels of accessibility, illustrates this, although it
should be added that in this case regional air connections have been crucial. Nonetheless, major
investments in transport infrastructure can make a signiﬁcant contribution to furthering regional
differences in levels of accessibility and possibly economic development. This is because such
large-scale investments are usually only implemented in areas where they are expected to be
ﬁnancially viable, namely where a sufﬁciently large user group exists. Therefore, investments in
HST infrastructure, for instance, generally focus on the capital regions and major metropolitan
areas in Europe.
Increasing use of modern information and communications technology (ICT), with its
potential for ‘democratisation’ of the territory might offset polarisation trends to some extent,
but in reality the differences between the densely populated parts of the EU and the more
peripheral areas have often become more intensiﬁed due to uneven access to ICT (ESPON 2003).
There are large disparities in the level of ICT development within the EU, with Sweden,
Denmark and England having a high level of ICT development and Ireland and France (with
the exception of the Paris region) being below average. This ‘digital divide’ is being addressed
by the EU as it is seen as a major obstacle to achieving the ‘Lisbon-objectives’ of becoming the
most competitive knowledge-based economy in the world (CEC 2006b).
The changing socio-demographic structure of the EU
Demographic change is one of the most signiﬁcant challenges for spatial development in the EU.
Recent population trends across Europe have the dual effect of a depopulation of the more rural
and peripheral areas and a concentration of population in the larger urban areas. These
developments have considerable implications for housing markets and transport systems, as well
as other spatial, socio-economic and environmental impacts.

Spatial development trends and key issues
51
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 81

Demographic restructuring is the outcome of two trends: natural population change and
migration which exhibit different spatial patterns. The predominant trend in demographic
change in Europe is the ageing of the population due to a fall in fertility rates, a fall in mortality
rates, and an increase in longevity. Fertility rates across Europe vary, though over past years no
EU member state has achieved the fertility rate threshold of 2.1 children per woman that is
necessary to replace the population. In 2006, the fertility rate varied from 2.0 (France) and 1.90
(Ireland) to 1.27 (Poland) and 1.24 (Slovakia). In general, particularly low fertility rates of less
than 1.4 are found in most of the new EU member states, Germany, Italy, Portugal and Spain.
Immigration into the EU is too low to balance out population decline through low reproduction
rates, so since the late 1990s the EU population has been both shrinking and ageing.
As regards longevity, it is estimated that by 2050 life expectancy at birth in Europe will be
75 years for men and 82 years for women. The old-age dependency ratio (number of people over
65 divided by the number of people aged 15–64) is expected to reach on average around 53 per
cent in 2050 for the EU-25 (up from 25 per cent today), which means that for every two people
in work one will be dependent on them if employment rates remain the same (CEC 2007c).
Also crucial to explaining the changing demographic structure of the EU are migration
movements, but they are difﬁcult to predict or even to measure with precision. The dominant
intra-European migratory ﬂows have over past years been from periphery to core, and from east
to west, and this is resulting in increasing populations in some parts of western Europe, in
particular the larger urban areas. This contrasts with a decline of population in the south and
the east and in Nordic countries (ESPON 2002). Migration from east to west following the
eastern EU enlargement and wider differentials in wages and standards of living, are mostly
responsible (see Chapter 12). In some cases, particularly Poland, this resulted in a shortage of
doctors, carers, builders and engineers following accession. Two million young Poles emigrated
to other EU countries, delaying major building projects in Poland and potentially fuelling inﬂation as wages went up to attract Polish workers back into the country (Connolly 2007). Even
so, and despite some countries such as Poland being considerably affected by outmigration,
mobility ﬂows from the new member states to western EU countries have overall been more
limited than previously assumed (although there are exceptions, such as the UK, which has
experienced a major inﬂux of immigrants from Poland) (see Chapter 12). Moreover, many EU
migrant workers have recently returned to their native countries following the economic downturn in the late 2000s. And despite concerns over in-migration in times of economic growth,
migrant workers from the new EU member states are now generally considered to make a
positive contribution to the overall labour market performance in western Europe, because they
often ﬁll positions in very specialised or low-paid sectors that the domestic population does not
occupy. Much migration within the EU is thus temporary, often for work or study, and inﬂuenced by economic conditions. Moreover, while the idea of free movement in the EU creates
many opportunities, even highly skilled and educated migrants still face certain difﬁculties to
borderless mobility, for example, through language and cultural barriers and administrative
obstacles (Favell 2008).
However, while labour mobility between EU member states is relatively low, there are also
signiﬁcant international migration ﬂows (both legal and illegal) to the EU from other parts of
the world. The exact number of non-EU migrants residing in the EU is unknown, largely because
many European countries collect data on nationality rather than the place or country of birth,
thus making it impossible to identify ﬁrst-generation immigrants after they have obtained the
citizenship of their host country. However, for the year 2005, the United Nations estimated that
there were about 40 million migrants in the EU-27 member states, 3 per cent of which were
refugees. The impact of immigrants on population ageing in the EU will largely depend on how
well they integrate into the labour market and EU society, given that the employment rate for
52
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 82

immigrants is still lower than that of non-immigrants in many member states (CEC 2007c). 
Yet while the EU had a net migration of almost 2 million in 2003/2004, there is also a trend 
of highly qualiﬁed Europeans migrating to North America, Japan, Singapore and elsewhere
(Tritah 2009). This ‘brain drain’, which works against the Lisbon Agenda, adds to the challenge
of a rising dependency ratio in the EU and the challenge of achieving high economic
competitiveness.
The combined effect of migration and natural population change creates a diverse picture of
‘shrinking’ and ‘growing’ regions in Europe as shown in Plate 4. It should be noted that the
universally low fertility rates are of relatively recent origin and have not yet affected all parts
of the territory. Some overall trends are, however, apparent, such as population gains of cities
and the losses in rural areas. The trend towards a depopulation of small towns and rural areas
is of particular concern in many parts of the EU such as rural eastern Germany. This is a result
of the so-called ‘triple demographic time bomb’: too many young people leaving rural areas, too
few children being born, and too many old people remaining. In contrast, in other parts of the
EU, such as the UK, ‘counterurbanisation’ can be observed where during the 1990s on average
more than 1,500 people every week moved out of the cities to live in the countryside surrounding
the major urban areas.
While depopulation, ageing and out-migration affect mainly the more rural areas, most of
the international as well as intra-EU migration ﬂows are aimed at the large metropolitan areas,
thus fuelling the heterogeneous population structure of the big cities. Such polarisation
tendencies tend to concentrate the demand for new housing in and around the growing
metropolitan regions (especially the ‘accessible rural areas’ near metropolitan centres) while the
more peripheral and old industrial areas may have a surfeit of housing. In England, for instance,
the demand for housing far outstrips supply in the south-east of England, whereas the northeast has very low demand with housing market collapse and abandonment in the worst hit areas.
At the same time, second home ownership abroad has become a considerable factor for many
housing markets, affecting property prices in attractive destinations. Retirement migration has
become more signiﬁcant, particularly the ﬂows from northern Europe to tourist areas in the
south (ESPON 2002).
The socio-demographic changes in the EU and their spatial implications are in some areas
exacerbated by lifestyle choices that can result in an increasing demand for housing land and
services despite decreasing populations. For example, most European countries continue to
experience falls in household size with a growing number of single households, often due to
childlessness or divorce. At the same time the housing or urban space occupied per person is
often increasing. This trend is strongly reinforced by the changing age structure, as older
households are more likely to be single or two-person households. The long-term impacts of the
global ﬁnancial crisis from 2008 on housing markets and spatial development in Europe
remains to be seen, but one concern is the increasing demand for affordable housing, which is
currently an acute political and social issue in many European cities.
Climate change, energy and the environment
The attention given to the impact of climate change on European countries has increased
dramatically since the late 1990s and is now very high on the EU’s policy agenda (see 
Chapter 21). The main contributors to climate change are greenhouse gas emissions (such as
carbon dioxide and methane). Key international agreements through the United Nations
Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and the Kyoto Protocol of 1992 and
follow-up activities therefore focus on their reduction, while at the same time planning policy
responses in many EU countries are concentrating on adapting to climate change. Spatial

## S. 83

planners can contribute to achieving mitigation objectives by, for example, promoting carbonneutral housing and sustainable forms of mobility. Besides the mitigation of greenhouse gases,
another important driver for the EU’s ambition to increase the share in renewable energy sources
is the desire to achieve secure energy provision. The majority of countries in the EU depend to
a considerable degree on imported oil and gas, and this raises concerns about dependence on
the supply from Middle Eastern countries or Russia. The increase in the use of renewable energy
will present considerable challenges for most EU countries, where the share is currently
signiﬁcantly below the agreed targets.
The spatial development consequences of climate change are related to the expected increase
in both the frequency and intensity of natural hazards. In particular, changes in precipitation
patterns are assumed to lead to increased incidences of ﬂoods, drought and forest ﬁres, although
parts of the EU territory are affected in different ways, as Plate 5 shows. River ﬂoods are expected
to occur more frequently and with more severe consequences, and given the extended network
of waterways in Europe (many of which cross national borders), integrated ﬂood risk management across borders is therefore of increasing importance for managing spatial development.
Adaptation measures to address likely climate change impacts require a response in spatial
planning policies, for example, in the planning of urban areas and buildings that can resist strong
storms, are well insulated against cold temperatures as well as heatwaves, and are adaptable to
rising water levels. The location of new urban development so as to avoid ﬂood risk in low lying
areas such as the Netherlands and eastern England is of special concern.
There are many environmental trends, such as water, soil and air pollution, that affect the
European territory independent of national boundaries. Levels of pollution are generally 
highest in the core areas where industrial and urban development is concentrated. For example,
a clear core-periphery pattern can be identiﬁed in air pollution with the highest values in 
major industrial and metropolitan areas such as the Benelux countries, the Rhine Ruhr area 
and northern Italy. Many areas of environmental quality are now covered by EU environmental
law and policy. The implications for spatial development and planning are discussed in 
Chapter 21.
Conclusion
From a global perspective the EU appears to be prosperous, but there are signiﬁcant regional
economic disparities within and between countries. Many of these disparities have become more
pronounced with the completion of the Single European Market, European Monetary Union (the
eurozone), the enlargement of the EU, and the resulting increasing integration of European
economies. At present, spatial development trends in the EU suggest a further polarisation of
population and of economic development in the highly accessible ‘core’ region of the EU. These
trends present major challenges for EU policy-making, as they work against the objectives 
of social and economic cohesion and may present considerable obstacles to achieving the 
EU’s Lisbon-Gothenburg objectives. They imply the risk of more imbalances between core and
periphery, urban and rural areas, in terms of access to infrastructure, education and services 
and economic opportunities. But there are also many environmental trends with clear spatial
impacts, which affect different EU regions differently. Landscape fragmentation and other
environmental damage are of particular concern to the strongly urbanised parts of Europe, but
many of the more rural areas are also often strongly affected by environmental pollution, for
instance through the considerable volumes of transit trafﬁc on road, rail and air. The climate
change agenda has brought other concerns to the fore, in particular in relation to vulnerabilities
to natural hazards, with many mitigation and adaptation responses also being a task for spatial
planning.
54
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 84

At present, it is difﬁcult to foresee how the global economic crisis will affect these spatial
development trends in the medium to long term. Some countries have been hit harder than
others, and there are signs that the crisis will accelerate the restructuring of regional economies,
for instance in those regions that depend largely on ﬁnance and banking or the automotive
industry. It is likely that the effects of the crisis will be uneven and lead to an even greater
territorial imbalance in the EU. If unemployment rates rise, there will be a greater demand for
affordable and social housing, and affordable public transport, education and health care
services.
Yet, why should planners and policy-makers consider a European dimension to spatial
development, rather than just focus on the concerns within their own country or region? The
most obvious answers come from the fact that many spatial development issues cannot be
successfully tackled by member states alone. Water management and the prevention of ﬂooding
along transnational rivers such as the Rhine or Danube are obvious examples. Decisions taken
in one country can considerably affect the ﬂood risk of another country and put the public at
risk. Other examples are levels of air and water pollution that are not restricted by administrative
boundaries. The pollution that results from the high density of population, trafﬁc and industrial
activity in the central part of the EU also impacts on other, less urbanised parts of the European
territory, and can only be tackled jointly.
There are also concerns shared by cities and regions across Europe that could be more
effectively dealt with through transboundary cooperation. Many regions are affected by the
impacts of demographic change and migration on regional housing markets. The need to achieve
more energy-efﬁcient urban forms and to promote the production and use of renewable energies
are high on the agenda of all EU member states. There is considerable scope for cooperation, for instance on how to plan carbon-neutral settlements and how to integrate wind farms
into the landscape or solar power sources into the built environment. It is these trends and
interdependencies between regions that make a strong case for a European perspective on spatial
development and require a coordinated response across policy sectors and different levels of
jurisdiction.
Further reading
The European Commission’s regular Reports on Economic and Social Cohesion (available at
http://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/sources/docofﬁc/ofﬁcial/repor_en.htm) provide a comprehensive overview on the trends affecting the EU territory. The ESPON programme has published
numerous reports on topics of relevance to the spatial development of Europe. For a summary
of the ESPON ﬁndings on trends in the spatial structure of Europe see ESPON (2006a)
Synthesis Report III: Territory Matters for Competitiveness and Cohesion and the ESPON Atlas
(2006b) (available on the ESPON website at www.espon.eu).

Spatial development trends and key issues
55
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 85

Understanding and representing
European space
Just as a local planner needs to develop the mental capacity to grasp the spatial scale and interrelationships that exist, or potentially may exist, within a local plan area, so at the European scale
the same ability needs to be developed. The difference is that the European scale is outside the
professional experience of the majority of planners, and those from some of the larger countries
may ﬁnd it difﬁcult to think supranationally for reasons of education and upbringing as well as lack
of direct experience.
(Williams 1996: 91)
Introduction
During the 1990s the need to consider a European or even a global dimension to spatial
development has become increasingly recognised by spatial planners and policy-makers. The
spatial impacts of large-scale spatial development trends (explained in Chapter 4) and EU sector
policies (Part V) on domestic territories are considerable. Recognising the European dimension
to spatial planning has prompted cooperation on pan-European and transnational spatial
strategies (discussed in Part IV). Spatial planners have also given more consideration to the
geographical and socio-economic position of their own territory within the EU and the globalised
world. The interest in better understanding the main characteristics of the spatial structure of
Europe and the key development trends that affect the territory has led to the emergence of a
number of new European spatial concepts, images and metaphors.
Creating spatial concepts at the European scale may seem a bold if not implausible venture.
There are great uncertainties and disagreements about the important characteristics of the spatial
structure of the EU. The distribution of activity is changing as a result of EU enlargements and
because of investment in development in particular locations. The analysis of territorial trends
is hampered by insufﬁcient data (Chapter 6). First and foremost, however, preparing concepts
of European space is a political act. Particular models will favour certain interests and be
promoted by them. Who is involved in the preparation of the spatial concept, and which spatial
information is selected to represent it, will thus signiﬁcantly affect the message.
In principle, conceptualisations of the European territory can be for the purpose of explanation or analysis of the spatial structure and trends, or for the purpose of promoting awareness
of a particular cause or normative ideas about preferred patterns of spatial development that
should be pursued by policy. For example, the dominant concept explaining the European
territory has been the ‘core-periphery model’, an idea that emphasises the relative disadvantages
of regions on the outer edge of the territory in contrast to the economically stronger core. During
the 1990s this dominant view was challenged by an alternative normative model that promotes
a Europe of a more balanced system of major city regions. In practice, however, the use of spatial
concepts as explanatory or as normative and policy oriented is rather blurred.
Chapter 5
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 86

In this chapter, an overview of spatial concepts, metaphors and ways of thinking about
European space is given. The European dimension in national and regional planning strategies
will be discussed and the theoretical approaches introduced that can help to explain differences
in the consideration of a European perspective in different planning cultures. It is useful to begin
with a brief overview of the idea of the ‘power of maps’ (Wood 1992) and a note on the importance of the conception of space as a network of ﬂows for European spatial planning policy.
The power of maps in spatial planning processes
Maps, sketches, diagrams and photographs as well as aerial and satellite images are important
visualisation techniques used in spatial planning. Computer technology has considerably
expanded the range of cartographic possibilities to allow for more dynamic expressions of space
and time. More comparable geographical information at the European level has become
available over recent years (Chapters 6 and 16), and this facilitates the visualisation of largescale spatial development trends and provides a basis for the cartographic representation of
spatial policy in a transnational context.
However, many choices have to be made in selecting, generalising and schematising the
information to be presented on a map. Every visualisation is just one of many possible alternatives, and besides considering what has been presented and how, it can also be of interest to
know what is not visualised. Cartographic representations in planning can thus be seen as
representing (and possibly reinforcing) power structures in society. The choices that affect the
message of a ‘spatial plan’ are inﬂuenced by the dominant paradigm and understanding of spatial
development and planning, and by who is involved in the cartographic process (Dühr 2007).
Maps have a considerable communicative power, and their potential contribution to understanding complex spatial development trends and to communicating the key messages of spatial
strategies to a wide range of actors and interest groups is widely acknowledged. Yet there are
considerable differences in the understanding of the purpose and scope of ‘planning’ across Europe,
and these differences also extend to the use of cartographic representations (Dühr 2007). These
differences come to the fore in debate among member states at the European level where they
frequently lead to considerable controversies about the design and content of maps. Given their
conceptual clarity, however, the power of maps can be helpful in stimulating the discussion on spatial
development trends and policy options for large territories between representatives from different
countries and beyond linguistic barriers (Dühr 2003). Maps and other spatial visualisations can thus
be especially useful when planners and decision-makers are working across local, regional and
national administrative borders and functional interdependencies need to be highlighted.
Conceptions of space and place
A dominant theme in the literature on the maps and cartographic visualisations in planning is
concerned with the underlying conception or understanding of space. It has been widely argued
that spatial plans, policies and concepts embody a particular understanding of space that is
outdated and misrepresents reality, and that this traditional approach should be replaced. The
‘traditional’ approach adopts a ‘Euclidean’ conception of space where objects are represented
as points or boundaries on a set of Cartesian (x and y) co-ordinates. This places the emphasis
in spatial planning on geographical location and proximity. Other aspects of space are largely
neglected, notably the time dimension (in the sense of connections between different places 
and the development over time), the importance of ﬂows between the points and across
boundaries, and the way that such representations are socially constructed. The traditional
approach produces static and object-centred representations that are reinforced by the use of

Understanding and representing European space
57

## S. 87

two-dimensional paper maps. Many economic or geographic theories that have inﬂuenced
planning thought and regional policy, including classical location and agglomeration theory, are
based on such a Euclidean conception of space. Traditional spatial planning instruments such
as master plans and regional plans that follow these theories have been criticised because they
support the impression that single, objective representations of space exist (Graham and Healey
1999; Healey 2006a). In reality, it is argued, each cartographic illustration of spatial policies is
chosen through ‘treacherous selective vision’ (Shields 1995: 245) within a particular cultural and
political context and inﬂuenced by the values and interests of a particular part of society.
Graham and Healey (1999: 627) argued that planning practice ‘remains unable to respond to
the now widespread recognition that spaces and times are effectively produced and created
through social actions within and between places’. Friedmann (1993: 482) even went as far as
to say that the ‘conventional concept of planning is so deeply linked to the Euclidean mode that
it is tempting to argue that if the traditional model has to go, then the very idea of planning
must be abandoned’ (Friedmann 1993: 482).
The debate has thus turned to alternative representations in spatial planning (as well as
mapping more generally) as being socially constructed through power-laden acts that support
certain interests and certain parts of a territory while inevitably neglecting others (Dühr 2007;
Harvey 1996). Space is increasingly conceptualised as a network of ﬂows and as functional
interlinkages between different parts of the territory. A prerequisite and driver for this ‘network
space’ (Castells 2000; Dupuy 2008) is the availability of fast and efﬁcient transport connections
and modern information and communication technologies (ICTs). The consequence is that the
connectivity of places has become more important than their proximity to other places, which
shifts the focus from being in the ‘right place’ to being able to act at the ‘right time’ in the global
economy (O’Brian 1992). For spatial planning, the conception of space as a network of ﬂows
means that as distances are increasingly measured in time, ‘locational strategies tend to opt for
places that are optimal in terms of “connectivity”’ (Hajer and Zonneveld 2000: 348). An
example of how this conception of space affects the visualisation of a territory is given in Figure
4.3. This illustrates the connectivity between cities in Europe through the implementation of the
high-speed train system.
For many planning concepts that are based on theories that use spatial proximity as their
organising principle, the conception of space as network presents considerable challenges.
Examples of such concepts are the German concept of central places (Christaller 1933) or the
compact city policy in the Netherlands. The network approach also highlights the limitations
of spatial strategies for territories deﬁned by administrative boundaries, which are increasingly
inadequate to capture the ﬂows that arise from socio-economic interdependencies of functional
territories (such as ‘commuter regions’ of large urban areas). This is especially the case working
at the transnational and European scales, which stretch across national borders (Healey 2007).
This creates considerable tensions between urban planning based on zones and administrative
boundaries and a view of planning from a network-based conception of space (van Schaick and
Klaasen 2008). Despite the theoretical arguments, planning instruments in practice have to date
been little affected by the shifting understanding of space, although there are now many
examples of (mostly informal) planning strategies that seek to address interconnections across
administrative boundaries (Dühr 2007; Healey 2007).
Spatial concepts and spatial metaphors
More and more societal, private and governmental actors whose actions are not easily controlled
by formal and binding spatial plans are involved in planning processes at all levels of scale. 
As a consequence, the focus of attention for many spatial strategy initiatives has shifted to
58
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 88

persuasion rather than direction, and there are an increasing number of informal planning
strategies now in use across Europe that have been prepared in consultation with a wide range
of actors. In such informal planning processes, ‘concepts that demonstrate rather than actions
that implement’ (van Eeten and Roe 2000: 59) play a central role. Spatial concepts can be deﬁned
as a brief expression in words and images of the way that planning actors view the desirable
spatial organisation of society (strategic concept) and the kind of interventions it requires
(instrumental concept) (Zonneveld 1991a, 1991b).
Spatial concepts are often presented as metaphor, as this ensures that they will be more easily
remembered and recognised. This is particularly relevant in intergovernmental planning
processes, where spatial metaphor can provide an associative framework. Faludi and van der
Valk (1994: 67) analysed the Dutch planning concept of the Groene Hart (Green Heart) which
denotes the open space area that is surrounded by the City ring (Randstad) of The Hague,
Amsterdam, Rotterdam and Utrecht. They argued that ‘metaphor can be a pervasive mode of
understanding by which we use experiences in one domain to structure another. . . . It conveys
meaning and intent’. This is related to the idea of framing, which can be deﬁned as:
a way of selecting, organising, interpreting and making sense of a complex reality so as to
provide guideposts for knowing, analysing, persuading and acting. A frame is a perspective from
which an amorphous, ill-deﬁned problematic situation can be made sense of and acted upon.
(Rein and Schön 1986: 4)
Healey (2004) has shown how metaphors are sometimes used as aids to either understand
place qualities and the spatiality of relations (for example, ‘the web’), or may be used to focus
policy attention and mobilise support (‘growth pole’, ‘clusters’, ‘compact city’). Often they are
put forward in the context of developing a vision for a desirable future of a place (‘polycentric
development’, ‘global integration zones’). These three functions can also be combined in a more
complex and comprehensive spatial metaphor, for instance ‘network’.
The cartographic representation of spatial concepts and spatial metaphors is crucial for their
widespread recognition and to convey the main ideas. Alexander (2001: 91–2), for instance, has
argued that ‘iconographic metaphors are the basic language for communicating about space,
whether physical or conceptual’. Since the subject of spatial plans is mostly ‘physical/territorial
space, maps are necessary for the two-way translation between the diagrammatic abstraction
of the metaphor and the concrete realities of geographic location’ (Alexander 2001: 92). For
example, the use of metaphor in combination with the iconographic representation of the Dutch
concepts of the ‘Green Heart’ and ‘Randstad’ communicates convincingly that there is a rural
and undeveloped open space that is surrounded by large cities, although in reality, ‘of course,
both the concepts and the maps are tautologically derived from each other’ (van Eeten and Roe
2000: 63). The spatial development reality of the Randstad is much more complex – the Green
Heart has considerable urban development, and open space extends into the cities. Spatial
concepts rely on simple memorable visual expression because ‘without such maps, planners and
their users would – literally – be unable to convey what is “the truth of the matter” that interests
them’ (van Eeten and Roe 2000: 65).
Spatial metaphors such as the Green Heart concept in the Netherlands can form the backbone
of a planning doctrine. Planning doctrine can be deﬁned as the ‘consistent theme underlying
successive spatial strategies’ (Williams 1996: 97) (see Chapter 24). There are numerous examples
of spatial metaphor that have been developed as the unifying concept in spatial planning
initiatives across Europe. One example of a longstanding planning concept is the Finger Plan
for the Greater Copenhagen area, which was developed in 1947 to maintain urban sprawl and
protect green space by organising built-up areas along ﬁve main transport corridors (the

## S. 89

‘ﬁngers’). The Finger Plan has since been the basis of urban development and structure in 
the Danish capital region, in spite of an almost doubling of Copenhagen’s population over the
past sixty years. Similar spatial concepts and metaphors have been developed more recently
elsewhere. In Flanders (Belgium) a new form of strategic spatial planning was initiated in the
1990s, and this involved the creation of the spatial metaphor of the ‘Flemish Diamond’, an urban
network area covering Brussels, Ghent, Antwerp and Leuven (Albrechts 2001a; Ministerie van
de Vlaamse Gemeenschap 1997). This concept seeks to position Flanders in north-west Europe
alongside other international networks such as the Randstad or the Rhine-Ruhr area. Spatial
metaphors are also used to structure cross-border initiatives on spatial development, as in the
examples of the ‘German-Polish house’ (Selke 2004) or the ‘Central-European crystal’ of the
Czech-German border region (Leibenath et al. 2007).
Spatial concepts and spatial metaphors for the
European territory
Normative visualisations (that is, visualisations of desired spatial development) are notably
absent in the spatial strategies for the EU territory, such as the Guiding Principles for the
European Continent (CEMAT 2000), the European Spatial Development Perspective (CSD
1999) or the Territorial Agenda of the European Union (EU Ministers 2007a). With the
exception of the Spatial Visions for North-west Europe (NWMA Spatial Vision Group 2000)
and for the Baltic Sea Region (VASAB 2010 1994) there are also few examples of visualisation
of spatial policy in the transnational spatial visions that were prepared for the INTERREG IIC
and IIIB cooperation areas (see Chapter 15). The maps that have been included in these
strategies focus on an analysis of the territory and spatial development trends.
Despite their omission in formal statements, many individuals and organisations have 
used visualisation and promoted spatial concepts to try to explain the spatial structure and
development of Europe. Some of these ideas such as the ‘European megalopolis’ date back to
the early 1960s and have been successful in raising awareness of the spatial structure of the EU
and to ‘help us think spatially in relation to Europe as a whole’ (Williams 1996: 95). They also
demonstrate how thinking has changed over time from a core-periphery perspective to a
conceptualisation of European space as a balanced system of urban clusters.
Core-periphery conceptualisations of Europe
A constant feature in regional policy and planning debates has been the core-periphery conceptualisation of European space (Plate 6). In traditional core-periphery models, it is assumed
that increased travel and transport costs result from remoteness relative to the main centres of
population and economic activity, whereas at same time remote locations are characterised by
the absence of agglomerative advantages (broadly deﬁned as economies of scale) (Copus 2001).
There are numerous examples of core-periphery models of Europe, such as the ‘Golden Triangle’
of the London-Paris-Bonn area (Williams 1993). This core-periphery pattern of economic
potential in the European Community of twelve was identiﬁed by Keeble et al. (1988) on the
basis of a model investigating accessibility and regional GDP.
Concern about the socio-economic disadvantages that a spatial structure with a strong core
and weak periphery implies for economic and social cohesion lies at the heart of EU policymaking, and is the rationale for the considerable support given under the EU Cohesion Policy
to regions that are ‘lagging behind’. Core-periphery thinking explains the traditional policy focus
on improving transport and communications infrastructure and providing ﬁnancial support to
facilitate change in the economic structure of peripheral regions.
60
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 90

Economic models are usually the basis of core-periphery conceptualisations, but to a greater
or lesser extent they also make use of visualisation. Highly generalised conceptualisations using
spatial metaphor have been very inﬂuential in planning and regional policy debates. The
probably best-known example of a spatial metaphor of the European territory is the ‘Blue
Banana’ (Brunet 1989). The Blue Banana is a greatly simpliﬁed and therefore memorable image
of the spatial structure of Europe, and depicts a highly developed area stretching from the south
east of England to the north of Italy (Plate 6). Its core-periphery model reﬂects the understanding
of economically ‘strong’ and ‘weak’ regions that has guided thinking about spatial development
and regional policy in the EU for many decades.
The ‘Blue Banana’ was prepared by a research team for the French government, and was
meant to highlight a potential danger for the Greater Paris region in the late 1980s as economic
growth continued to concentrate in the most densely populated part of Europe, yet excluding
Paris (Rase and Sinz 1993). It has become one of the most-quoted contemporary cartographic
representations and has prompted numerous responses and variations. There have also been
several ‘misinterpretations’ or distortions of the idea; for example, it was suggested that it was
a guide to investment activity in Europe, and that all areas outside the Banana were ‘doomed’.
Williams (1996: 97) has pointed out that rather than as predictions or predestinations, spatial
metaphors such as the Blue Banana should be ‘regarded simply as ways of describing the spatial
structure of Europe in a manner that can be easily grasped, which may help people who ﬁnd it
difﬁcult to think in European spatial terms to gain a sense of spatial positioning’.
The core-periphery concept of the EU has undergone a number of adjustments over time, 
as a result of considerable debate among policy- and decision-makers from across Europe, and
also reﬂecting the growing area covered by the EU following enlargements and changes in the
economic performance of European regions (Plate 6). The European Spatial Development
Perspective (ESDP) (CSD 1999: 61) identiﬁed the concentration of economic activity in the EU-
15 as being located in ‘a pentagon deﬁned by London, Paris, Milan, Munich and Hamburg. This
area represents 20 percent of the total area and contains about 40 percent of EU citizens
producing about 50 percent of the EU’s total GDP’. There is no illustration of the ‘Pentagon’
in the ESDP, reﬂecting the controversies over all types of spatial representation and in particular
the debate about core and periphery in the EU (Dühr 2007; Schön 2000).
The Second Report on Economic and Social Cohesion (CEC 2001a, 2001b) presented
central and peripheral regions in the EU based on an index of accessibility that for each region
measures the time needed to reach other regions weighted by their economic importance. The
report identiﬁed:
[a] very high concentration of activities in central regions, which account for only 14 percent
of the land area but a third of the population and almost half (47 percent) of the GDP.
Population density in these regions is 3.7 times higher than in peripheral regions.
(CEC 2001a: 30)
There is no such explicit conceptualisation of the EU’s core and periphery in subsequent
Cohesion Reports, which signiﬁes the shift in the policy debate towards a more diversiﬁed
perspective on European territorial development.
Towards a balanced and polycentric urban development
Although the ‘Blue Banana’ has often been criticised for the rather simplistic representation of
core and periphery in Europe, during the 1990s this powerful image became central to
transnational and national planning discourses. It prompted numerous modiﬁcations, reﬂecting

Understanding and representing European space
61
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 91

the desire to belong to the European core area and to better understand the relationship between
economic performance and accessibility. Responses include the ‘Sunbelt’ growth zone from
Valencia in Spain along the Mediterranean coast to northern Italy or the ‘Blue Star’, which is
an extension of the Blue Banana and the Sunbelt towards central and eastern Europe, along a
number of development axes including Hamburg-Copenhagen, Berlin-Warsaw, Munich-Vienna
and Paris-Madrid (IAURIF 1991). Another variation is the ‘Red Octopus’ (van der Meer 1998),
which visualises the expansion of development along trans-European transport and ICT
networks, towards the north, south and east of the enlarged EU.
From the late 1990s the core-periphery model has been joined and to some extent replaced
by a more diversiﬁed and balanced concept of ‘Europe of the regions’. The concept was
promoted by the Committee of the Regions and the European Commission, and gives more
emphasis to the potential of each individual region. Consequently it presents a more differentiated view of the spatial and economic structure of the European territory. An accompanying
argument for a more diversiﬁed ‘network’ perspective on the spatial structure of Europe is that
improvements in transport infrastructure (through technological advances and publicly funded
infrastructure such as the trans-European transport networks [TEN-Ts]) and technological
improvements in ICTs are making location advantages in relation to the ‘core’ less relevant than
before (Copus 2001). Moreover, ‘soft’ location factors such as natural and cultural amenities,
the quality of human capital, regional identity, and the quality of business links to European
and global markets and information networks play an increasingly important role in determining
the economic success of regions (Copus 2001). They are much less closely related to location
and agglomeration factors.
In EU policy discourse, the simple core-periphery model that seeks to respond to peripheral
disadvantage (in the conventional sense) through investment in physical infrastructure, has 
since the late 1990s been increasingly replaced with the notion of a Europe of the regions 
with policy directed to the particular characteristics of each region. This is in the interests of a
more balanced polycentric system that will ‘help to avoid further excessive economic and
demographic concentration in the core area of the EU’ (CSD 1999: 20). Alternative spatial
conceptualisations of the European territory, particularly the ‘European bunch of grapes’ model
(Kunzmann and Wegener 1991) (Figure 5.1), have played a role in shifting attention. This
concept was prepared by academics and has a strong normative dimension. It presents the 
vision of a polycentric structure of competitive urban agglomerations instead of one economic
core region.
Although it lacks visualisation, the ESDP (CSD 1999) also promoted an alternative spatial
concept for Europe – the ideal of a balanced and polycentric spatial structure. The ESDP called
for strengthening of the overall competitiveness of the EU to improve the spatial balance in
Europe through:
the creation of several dynamic zones of global economic integration, well distributed
throughout the EU territory and comprising a network of internationally accessible
metropolitan regions and their linked hinterlands (towns, cities and rural areas of varying
sizes).
(CSD 1999: 20)
Such concepts of ‘polycentricity’ and ‘global economic integration zone’ have proven
sufﬁciently vague to allow for consensus in the intergovernmental ESDP process. However, this
also implies that the concepts mean different things to different people and are interpreted and
62
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 92

used differently depending on the circumstances. Their lack of visualisation has supported 
this vagueness in meaning, which is why these spatial concepts have stimulated considerable
academic and political debate after the adoption of the ESDP in an attempt to clarify their
meaning and implications. This has involved seeking appropriate graphic expressions, as the
example of the ‘long-term polycentric vision of the European space’ (Titecat et al. 2000) that
was prepared during the French EU presidency in 2000 shows (Figure 5.2). The debate on the
ESDP concepts also inspired national and transnational spatial strategies, such as the study
Aménager la France de 2020 (DATAR 2002), and a study on polycentrism in peripheral urban
areas that was prepared by the Conference of Peripheral and Maritime Regions (CPMR 2003)
(Figure 5.3). The CPMR scenarios are interesting examples of an alternative perspective on the
need to counteract polarisation trends and to promote policy responses for polycentric
development in Europe (see also Chapter 15).
Although the ambition to develop alternative global economic integration zones is not
explicitly pursued in the current EU Cohesion Policy (2007–13), the combination of the
objectives of cohesion and competitiveness is evident, alongside the tensions that marrying such
potentially contradictory ideas implies (see Chapter 18).
Spatial positioning: national and regional territories in
a European perspective
European initiatives in the ﬁeld of spatial development have prompted nation-states and regions
to consider their territories in the wider geographical context. Williams (1996: 97) suggested
the term ‘spatial positioning’ for a process that would help to identify opportunities and
comparative advantages, and make it possible to grasp spatial relationships in a wider territory
than the particular nation-state or region. He argued that spatial positioning at the EU scale may
‘help to overcome the mental blocks of orthodoxy’ (1996: 97) by giving regional cities and towns

Understanding and representing European space
65
Figure 5.3 Study of the Conference of Peripheral Maritime Regions (CPMR): towards the polycentric
development of the European territory

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 95

more scope to promote their European proﬁle. This may be particularly relevant in countries
with a dominant capital-city region, such as France and the UK, where smaller regional centres
have traditionally been used to accepting that they have second-order status and that
communication with the rest of Europe must be through the national capital ‘because of the
transport infrastructure or hierarchy of government responsibilities’ (Williams 1996: 98).
These processes of regions and cities being expected to ‘stand on their own feet’ have 
been accompanied by considerable governance shifts in the EU over the past decades that have
resulted in increasing emphasis being given to the role of cities and regions in the global
economy. The setting up of the Committee of the Regions, and the inception of policy
instruments such as the Community Initiative INTERREG (Chapter 16), have enabled a direct
dialogue between the European institutions and regional and local authorities within the
member states, effectively bypassing national governments. Since the late 1980s, many European
cities and regions have therefore pursued strategies that promote their strengths, often in the
form of spatial strategies or frameworks and sometimes connected with place marketing
strategies (Ward 1998). Such urban and regional spatial strategies often include a representation
of the position of the territory not only in the national, but frequently also the European spatial
structure. Marseille, Montpellier and Barcelona, for instance, have all claimed to be the
‘gateway to the Mediterranean’, and Lille and Brussels have marketed their location in the heart
of north-west Europe.
Inﬂuenced by the debates at EU level, many countries and regions have also begun to position
their domestic territory in the urban hierarchy and in relation to economic growth areas in
planning strategies. The visualisation of surrounding territories was particularly noticeable in
the spatial strategies of north-west European countries, including Denmark, France and the
Netherlands, that were prepared in the 1980s and 1990s. More recently, conceptualising the
position of cities and regions in the globalised economy in relation to gateways and hubs has
become popular, as shown in the examples of national spatial strategies prepared at the turn of
the century, such as those for Ireland (Davoudi and Wishardt 2005), Poland (Korcelli 2005) and
Greece (Angelidis 2005). These examples show that despite the policy discourse on polycentricity, the location in (or near) the economic core area of the EU and on major transport
corridors is still commonly seen as the prerequisite for economic success and for attracting major
investments. Other examples that reﬂect the debates at EU level on polycentricity and economic
competitiveness are the pôles de compétitivité (competitiveness poles) policy initiative in France,
the Northern Way initiative in England and the concept of Europäische Metropolregionen
(European metropolitan regions) in Germany.
However, although positioning of the national territories in a wider geographical context has
become more common, they are usually presented on a separate diagram in the planning
strategies. Truly transnational perspectives of national spatial policies remain scarce. Even those
EU member states that have been noteworthy for presenting a wider European perspective 
in their national spatial strategies during the 1980s and 1990s have more recently turned to
consider the strengths of their region or nation-state vis-à-vis the rest of the world. Moreover,
while national and regional spatial strategies that were prepared in the 2000s consider the
competitiveness of national economies within the wider European or global context, they also
show a stronger concern for domestic issues and focus on those cities and (urban) regions that
are considered the most important powerhouses within the national spatial-economic structure.
For instance, since the 1990s the National Planning Reports for Denmark have visualised the
position of the national territory within Europe, but in the current report the focus has shifted
to ‘spatial planning under new conditions’ with a more national view of the country’s economic
growth potentials and a preoccupation with local government reform (Danish Ministry of the
66
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 96

Environment 2006). In light of the ongoing integration of the Öresund region following the
completion of the transport connection, the report notes that the national priority is to ensure
that ‘spatial planning strengthens the international competiveness of Greater Copenhagen’
(Danish Ministry of the Environment 2006: 16). Within the French national planning agency,
which has for years been one of the driving forces for European spatial planning initiatives and
has frequently conceptualised the French territory in the European context, there has also been
a shift in emphasis away from substantive concerns towards a stronger horizontal interdepartmental coordination and the regional decentralization agenda, with research and scenariobuilding now being undertaken by a new Observatoire (Baudelle and Peyrony 2005; Bovar and
Peyrony 2006). The new German Leitbilder und Handlungsstrategien für die Raumentwicklung
(BMVBS 2006) place strong emphasis on aspects of economic growth and innovation (Aring
and Sinz 2006). Another example, the current National Spatial Strategy for the Netherlands
(VROM et al. 2004), has in comparison to previous national strategies been criticised as being
considerably more inward-looking and offering no strategic view for the integration of EU
policies or a common planning agenda with neighbouring countries (Zonneveld 2005a;
Zonneveld and Waterhout 2007).
Many of the concerns at the EU level about achieving stronger global competitiveness are thus
also reﬂected in recent national spatial strategies and national planning policy. This is not
surprising given the intergovernmental nature of European spatial planning that leads to
dynamic inﬂuences between the different levels through an ‘uploading’ of national and regional
ideas and the subsequent application of European policy concepts in domestic planning systems
and policies (see Dühr et al., 2007). Nonetheless, the changing spatial structure of the EU
territory through successive enlargements or major infrastructure investments, such as the
Öresund Bridge or the Channel Tunnel (Chapter 4), requires planners to continuously readjust
their thinking about spatial development and the strategic position of their territories. This has
prompted considerable cooperation across national borders on informal spatial strategies, and
often with a view to attracting economic investments to more peripheral regions. However, a
cross-border or transnational perspective inevitably results in more complex planning processes,
especially in regions with signiﬁcant institutional and cultural differences. Tatzberger (2008), for
example, has described the governance and cooperation challenges involved in developing a joint
spatial development strategy for neighbouring EU regions in Austria, Hungary and the Slovak
Republic, and the role of spatial concepts and spatial metaphor in fostering a transnational
identity between ‘East and West’. There are also numerous examples of cooperation among
regions that seek to strengthen the argument for major infrastructure corridor investments across
the EU. Examples are the North European Trade Axis (NETA), which stretches from the Irish
Republic to the North of Poland, including parts of the North Sea and the Baltic Sea, and the
Via Baltica Nordica Development Zone, which reaches from Finland to central and eastern
Europe. These examples illustrate once again how keen cities and regions are to position
themselves in the enlarged EU and to ensure that they are well connected. Inevitably, in many
cases this presents a strong argument for cooperation with other regions and countries in Europe.
Conclusion
Policy maps and visualisations are highly sensitive instruments for the communication of policy
because they are clearer than verbal expressions of policy. Whereas vague concepts in the text
of planning policy documents can be subject to a multitude of interpretations, cartographic
representations of spatial policy demand a much higher degree of consensus (Faludi 2002a). Yet
if used appropriately they can help to gain widespread support for a strategy.

Understanding and representing European space
67
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 97

In many parts of Europe, there is still comparatively little experience of planning at
transnational or cross-border scale and it has become clear that transnational spatial planning
strategies present major challenges to the organisation of new and established institutional forms,
policy processes, methods and instruments. The pioneering work of some member states in
addressing the European and transnational dimension to spatial planning has helped to
demonstrate added value through collaborative initiatives and thus increased political support
for European spatial planning initiatives (Chapter 14).
Since the 1990s there have been an increasing number of studies of the spatial development
structure of the European territory that have provided the basis for the development of spatial
concepts. The aim of these studies, as Williams (1996: 91) has pointed out, can be seen ‘as much
to aid the supranational thinking process as to prescribe policy as such’. Indeed, spatial planning
initiatives at European level have prompted a revival of strategic planning across Europe
(Healey 1997), with spatial strategies often including a positioning of the domestic territory in
the wider geographical context. In particular, the more peripheral regions have sought to deﬁne
their position and place qualities in relation to the European ‘core’ region and in relation to
major transport corridors. The thinking about the ‘network society’ has in this respect so far
had little effect on planning strategies, where concerns over relative location disadvantages seem
to prevail. The spatial concentration of economic activities in the European territory is and
remains a highly controversial topic.
Moreover, reﬂecting the policy debates at EU level as expressed in the Lisbon-Gothenburg
Agenda, spatial planning in many European countries and regions appears to have become more
inward-looking again over recent years, emphasising the competitive advantages of domestic
territories to attract investment. This has also affected the spatial conceptualisation in domestic
planning strategies, which at present rarely seek to emphasise the need for and advantages 
of cooperation across administrative boundaries. This current ‘domestic turn’ is somewhat at
odds with the cooperation activities on joint cross-border strategies or corridor initiatives that
are going on in many EU regions, and spatial planners may eventually come to realise again the
necessity and beneﬁts of recognising the ‘European dimension’ in their spatial strategies.
Further reading
The Visual Language of Spatial Planning (Dühr 2007) provides a comprehensive overview of
conceptualisation and visualisation in different planning traditions in Europe and at
transnational level. Healey’s (2007) Urban Complexity and Spatial Strategies discusses the
‘relational turn’ in spatial planning and how this has affected visualisation in spatial strategies
in different European countries. Planning Imagery – The Emergence and Development of New
Planning Concepts in Dutch National Spatial Policy (van Duinen 2004) gives a good insight into
the ‘conceptual paradise for spatial planners’ that is the Netherlands. Key references on the
communicative aspects of maps (without an explicit focus on spatial planning) are, for example,
Monmonier (1996) How to Lie with Maps and Wood (1992) The Power of Maps.
68
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 98

Spatial data and spatial 
information
To make plans, you need information. And to make effective plans, you need accurate, objective
information.
(EEA 1999: 8)
The general situation on spatial information in Europe is one of fragmentation of datasets and
sources, gaps in availability, lack of harmonisation between datasets at different geographical scales
and duplication of information collection. These problems make it difﬁcult to identify, access and
use data that is available. Fortunately, awareness is growing at national and at EU level about the
need for quality geo-referenced information to support understanding of the complexity and
interactions between human activities and environmental pressures and impacts.
(INSPIRE undated)
Introduction
Without the use of maps, spatial planning is difﬁcult to conceive. At all levels of scale, they can
help to understand the ‘space to be planned’ by showing the geographical features of the territory
and how spatial development trends may affect it. However, in intergovernmental European
spatial planning initiatives, mapping has been to date characterised by controversy.
This can be explained by the absence of a clear institutional framework for spatial planning
at the European level and different traditions of visualising spatial policy (Dühr 2007). Another
critical factor is the lack of comparative spatial data to analyse, understand and represent
European space. During the process of preparing the European Spatial Development Perspective
(ESDP) (CSD 1999) concern over incompatible or missing geographic information on the
European territory coloured the discussions – with the result that no policy maps were included
in this spatial strategy document (Dühr 2007; Faludi and Waterhout 2002). In response to these
identiﬁed shortcomings to understanding spatial development trends, and to inform future spatial
policy initiatives, the ESPON programme was set up (discussed in Chapter 16).
The availability of spatial data is crucial for the preparation of all maps, be they of a topographic, analytical or thematic nature, or normative visualisations of policy options. Reﬂecting
the growing interest in monitoring land use change and environmental quality, there has been
considerable progress since the 1990s on providing comparative spatial and environmental data
for the European territory. Comprehensive spatial monitoring systems or ‘spatial observatories’
have also been set up by national planning agencies in some EU countries, and these analyses
also increasingly consider wider European trends and their effects on domestic territories (see,
for example, BBR 2005; Bovar and Peyrony 2006).
In this chapter, the discussion concentrates on the data sources that help to better understand
spatial development trends at the European scale, and the initiatives that are being promoted
to harmonise spatial data from different sources. As well as statistical information, several new
