## S. 99

initiatives on land use information have been launched over recent years and there is now a
growing amount of geographical data available. The chapter concludes with a discussion of some
of the challenges of providing up-to date, comprehensive and coherent spatial information for
the European territory. First, however, the data requirements for the monitoring and evaluation
of EU policy are brieﬂy discussed and the key terms deﬁned.
Evaluation, monitoring, indicators and data: some 
definitions
In EU policy making and implementation, increasing attention is given to policy evaluation, and
consequently to setting up monitoring systems to assess progress towards agreed targets. This
requires the definition of appropriate indicators and the collection of comparable and
harmonised data, much of which needs to be standardised spatial reference data to support the
implementation and monitoring of a growing number of geographically relevant EU policies,
such as environment, agriculture and regional policy (Feranec et al. 2007).
Evaluation involves the compiling of information from monitoring and other sources ‘to ﬁnd
out and explain the effects of the interventions’ (CEC 2006c: 9), such as the impact of the
Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) on land use. Monitoring is the regular and systematic
collection of information on performance, based on appropriate indicators with comprehensive
and up-to-date data to underpin these indicators. An indicator is a model that quantiﬁes and
simpliﬁes phenomena and thus allows a better understanding of complex realities. It can be
deﬁned as the ‘measurement of an objective to be met, a resource mobilised, an effect obtained,
a gauge of quality or a context variable. An indicator should be made up by a deﬁnition, a value
and a measurement unit’ (CEC 2006c: 5).
There are many long-standing and well-established indicators such as gross domestic product,
which is a general indicator of the wealth of a region or country. Since the 1990s there has been
a trend towards increasingly complex indicators, ‘moving away from a larger to a very small
number of highly aggregated values, through the construction of an index’ (EEA 2002: 28). For
example, sustainability indicators have been developed that seek to reﬂect the often complex
interaction between social, economic and environmental factors. In the ﬁeld of EU environmental
policy, monitoring of implementation effectiveness and environmental change has been pursued
for a long time (see Chapter 21). In the ﬁeld of regional policy or Cohesion Policy, the use of
indicators for monitoring and evaluation in the framework of Structural Funds became
established practice in the mid-1990s. Since then, there has been an ongoing debate on how best
to evaluate the effectiveness of Structural Funds intervention. For the period 2007–13, a
hierarchy of indicators has been deﬁned to evaluate inputs, outputs, results and impacts of all
Structural Funds programmes. There is an expectation for those indicators to be ‘SMART’ (see
INTERACT 2006), that is, in order to be able to measure the effectiveness of regional policy
intervention they need to be:
•
speciﬁc to the objective they are supposed to measure;
•
measurable (either quantitatively or qualitatively);
•
available at an acceptable cost (i.e. in relation to data needs);
•
relevant to the information needs of managers; and
•
time-bound so as to know when the expected target will be achieved.
There are other considerations to bear in mind:
The main importance of an indicator, in fact, does not lie in the explicit value itself, but in
the implicit information it contains. The concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
70
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 100

is an example: despite the fact that it is only a measured value (a statistic), it can tell us about
the use of energy, the depletion of natural resources, the diminishing capability of
absorption of the forest etc. . . . However, selecting the appropriate indicator is not an easy
task. In fact, there are many parameters that can be measured, but only a few of them are
able to translate complex relationships about phenomena in a simple way.
(EEA 2002: 24)
Moreover, indicators are increasingly expected to help with planning and communication as
well as monitoring. The indicator’s potential to simplify is thereby crucial. It should be:
easily understood, transparent and give reliable signals of the important trends in the actual
situation to be compared across time. . . . Different users have different understandings and
needs as regards indicators and, thus, it is necessary to translate the real world in different
ways. Indicators are a compromise between scientiﬁc accuracy and the demand for concise
information.
(EEA 2002: 25)
However, the increasing emphasis on communication with different user groups presents
additional challenges for the development of sound indicators:
Even where there is full knowledge of the system, there is still a gap between a ‘theoretically
sound indicator’ and an ‘operational indicator’. Indicators are generally built up by
scientists, e.g. environmental experts, to be used by decision-makers, e.g. city managers, and
represent an effective vehicle of knowledge between these two categories only when they
are fully understandable to the latter. [. . .] This gap between scientiﬁc knowledge on the
one hand, and the effectiveness on the other, is mostly linked to quality and quantity of
available information.
(EEA 2002: 24)
The increasing emphasis on evaluation and indicators to measure change in EU policy raises
questions about the statistical data and geographical information underpinning the monitoring
systems. The terms data and information are in practice often used interchangeably, although
data are commonly deﬁned as the lowest level of abstraction from which information and
knowledge are derived. For instance, statistics about the level of unemployment of a region are
generally considered as ‘data’, whereas a report on the economic situation of the region or
country would be seen as ‘information’. Thus, information refers to data that is organised or
analysed in order to report to someone about a particular matter of concern (Hills 2002;
Stephenson 1999). Stephenson (1999) further distinguishes between ‘hard’ and ‘soft’ information, with ‘hard’ information being based on data that is usually quantitative. ‘Soft’ information,
in contrast, is information usually built from qualitative data such as concepts or perspectives
in, or attitudes towards, public policy.
Most data collected for use in EU policy-making and monitoring is of a quantitative nature,
and intended to provide an ‘evidence base’ for policy-making (see Chapter 16). However, there
are EU initiatives explicitly aimed at gathering ‘soft information’, such as the European
Commission’s Eurobarometer survey of public opinion that has been undertaken since the
1970s. The emphasis on perception research acknowledges that any assessment of the living
conditions of EU citizens should not only consider objectively measurable aspects but also
recognise that perceptions, such as quality of life, are to a certain extent culturally determined
and context dependent.

Spatial data and spatial information
71
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 101

The standardisation of data collection including spatial and temporal scales and the harmonisation of indicators has been of ongoing concern for EU institutions and is particularly
important for geographical representation. Data series (collected at regular intervals) need to be
available across the EU, and data needs to be compatible in terms of definitions used,
geographical units for which the data are collected and standards applied for data storage. There
are EU standards, such as the NUTS system (see Chapter 3), which seek to ensure a certain
comparability of administrative units across the EU, but overall there continues to be considerable diversity, which affects the quality and use of data available at EU level. The temporal scale
is issue speciﬁc as ‘it does not make sense to collect data of urban sprawl on a daily basis, but
it does for carbon dioxide concentrations’ (EEA 2002: 29), so agreements across the EU have
to be reached for collection of individual datasets to ensure their harmonised use.
Given increasing concerns about diversity in data collection and the lack of harmonised and
comparable data, there are proposals to work towards improved comparability and compatibility. These initiatives will be discussed below, following an overview of statistical and
geographical data sources that are available for spatial policy-making in the EU.
Statistical information for the EU
The collection and collation of statistical information for the EU member states and regions has
been an important part of the work of the Community since its beginning. The Eurostat service
was established for this purpose in 1953. Since the mid-1990s, the European Environment Agency (EEA) has collected comprehensive data on the state of the environment through
a network of contact points across Europe. There have been other initiatives of relevance to
European spatial planning, often initiated by the European Commission’s Directorates-General
for Environment and for Regional Policy. These are directed to monitoring the effectiveness of
EU policy implementation and informing future policy-making. As the majority of Europeanwide data are collected by statistical ofﬁces in the EU member states and then centrally compiled
for use by EU institutions, information and communication technologies (ICTs) are crucial for
any initiatives to improve data exchange and to set up online databases of relevance to EU
policy-making. Most statistical and geographical data for the EU is now also publicly (and freely)
available on the websites of the European Commission and its agencies.
Regional statistics for the EU: Eurostat
The EU’s Eurostat service, based in Luxembourg, is the statistical ofﬁce of the European Communities. It is the most important source of statistical information on the EU member states and
regions. Eurostat’s mission is to gather and analyse data from national and regional statistics
ofﬁces in the EU member states and other sources and to make them available to the European
institutions to support the deﬁnition and analysis of Community policies. In order to ensure that
data from different sources is compatible and comparable, common standards have been agreed.
According to Eurostat, more than 300 million data are now available to the public free of
charge via its website, and many of these are also available for regional and sub-regional levels
(NUTS classiﬁcation levels 2 and 3). The databases are accessible under different themes such
as: general and regional statistics; economy and ﬁnance; population and social conditions;
industry, trade and services; agriculture and ﬁsheries; external trade; transport; environment and
energy; and science and technology. Moreover, data are categorised according to a number of
‘key indicators on EU policy’:
•
‘Euro-indicators’ on topics such as: balance of payments; consumer prices; external trade;
industry, commerce and services; labour market; and monetary and ﬁnancial indicators;
72
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 102

•
long-term indicators on many areas of life, work, the economy and the environment in 
the EU;
•
structural indicators on employment, innovation and research, economic reform, social
cohesion and the environment, which feed into the Commission’s annual synthesis report
to the European Council on progress towards achieving the objectives of the Lisbon
Strategy (see Chapter 17);
•
sustainable development indicators to monitor progress towards achieving the objectives of
the EU Sustainable Development Strategy (see Chapter 21), on economic development,
poverty and social exclusion, ageing society, public health, climate change and energy,
production and consumption patterns, management of natural resources, transport, good
governance and global partnership.
European Information and Observation Network (EIONET) and
Shared Environmental Information System (SEIS)
As more than 70 of the several hundred pieces of EU environmental legislation explicitly require
the member states to report on speciﬁc aspects of environmental trends within their territory,
an increasing amount of environmental data is now being collected throughout the EU. The
European Commission and the EEA have set up complex monitoring systems and online
databases to analyse environmental trends, to inform new policy and to monitor implementation. The EU institutions are also promoting initiatives for the collection and sharing of
environmental information between different data providers.
The EEA has collected and disseminated environmental data and information since 1994
through the European Information and Observation Network (EIONET). Today, EIONET
consists of the EEA, ﬁve European topic centres (ETCs) and a network of around 900 experts
from 39 countries in over 300 national environment agencies and other bodies dealing with
environmental information. The European topic centres facilitate the provision of data and
information from the countries and deliver reports and other services to the EEA and EIONET
on key policy issues as identiﬁed in the EU’s Environmental Action Programmes.
Through EIONET and its other initiatives, the EEA coordinates the delivery of environmental
data from individual countries on a wide range of environmental issues related to EU policy and
legislation, such as air pollution, biodiversity, chemicals, climate change, public health, land use,
natural resources, noise, soil, waste and material resources, and water. The EEA also monitors
the environmental impacts of EU policy sectors and activities, such as agriculture, energy,
ﬁsheries, household consumption, industry, population and economy, tourism and transport.
The EEA also pays attention to environmental issues in different regions and speciﬁc areas, such
as coastal zones, seas and the urban environment.
The EEA reports that in 2006 there were on average 6,000 downloads per month for
geospatial datasets alone. This is an indication of the increasing interest of a variety of user
groups in up-to-date and comparable environmental information for the EU. Nevertheless:
while there is a vast amount of data collected by public authorities across the EU (whether
at local, regional, national or European level), these data are not always used efﬁciently,
either because the existence of such data is not widely known or because of a range of
obstacles of a legal, ﬁnancial, technical and procedural nature.
(CEC 2008e: 3)
In recognition of the need to improve the quality and availability of timely, relevant and
reliable information on the EU environment based on a decentralised system of data collection,

## S. 103

the European Commission published in 2008 the Communication Towards a Shared Environmental Information System (SEIS) (CEC 2008e). SEIS aims to:
modernise and simplify the collection, exchange and use of the data and information
required for the design and implementation of environmental policy, according to which
the current, mostly centralised systems for reporting are progressively replaced by systems
based on access, sharing and interoperability.
(CEC 2008e: 2)
It is envisaged that SEIS will draw on existing initiatives (such as EIONET) and integrate all existing
data and information ﬂows related to EU environmental policy. Coordinated responses to natural
risks and emergencies, such as forest ﬁres, ﬂoods and droughts, will thus be facilitated. The focus
is on providing real-time environmental data through the use of Internet and satellite systems and
on information that is easier to understand for policy-makers and the public. SEIS is intended to
complement the INSPIRE Directive (Council 2007a; see below), which contains provisions aiming
to improve the accessibility and interoperability of spatial data, and to integrate EIONET with
other EU initiatives, such as GMES – Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (see below).
Urban Audit
The lack of comparable local-level urban data for the EU has been of long-standing concern,
especially considering that three-quarters of EU citizens live in cities and towns. In order to ﬁll
the information gap on city living, the Urban Audit was set up by Eurostat on behalf of DG
REGIO in the late 1990s. The main goals of the Urban Audit are to support EU urban policy,
to facilitate comparisons between cities, to support exchanges of experiences between cities, and
to inform the public about the quality of urban life.
Following a pilot study with 58 cities in 1999, the ﬁrst Urban Audit was completed in 2003
(with reference year 2001). It included data for almost 270 European cities from the EU-15 
and the then accession countries on demography, socio-economic and civic aspects, training and
education, environment, travel and transport, the information society, and culture and recreation. Some of the indicators are well established (e.g. population, GDP and unemployment),
whereas others have been created to capture ‘quality of life’ aspects of urban areas across the
EU (e.g. crime rate, days of sunshine, cinema places, noise level and mode of transport to work).
The State of the European Cities Report presents the results of the ﬁrst Urban Audit and was
published in 2007 (CEC 2007d).
In 2005 it was decided that Eurostat should be responsible for updating the Urban Audit every
three years (with an annual update for a smaller number of targeted variables). The second fullscale Urban Audit took place in 2006/07 (for the reference year 2004), and involved 321 European
cities in the EU-27 and 36 cities in Norway, Switzerland and Turkey. More than 300 quantitative
indicators were used, complemented by an updated qualitative survey of citizens’ perception on
the quality of life in 75 European cities and towns (EU-27, Croatia and Turkey) (CEC 2007e).
The Urban Audit data is available online through different applications, such as city proﬁles (based
on a selection of indicators across different domains) and ranking and comparisons of cities
according to different indicators. The publication of a report on the results of the second Urban
Audit is planned for autumn 2009, when the third Urban Audit will be undertaken.
Prior to the Urban Audit project, there were no comparative urban statistics available at EU
level and few at national level. The Urban Audit is therefore a valuable source of information
for policy-makers and researchers, and its relevance will grow when the ‘Urban Atlas’ (see below)
is completed and geographical information is available to complement the sectoral data. 
Although the Urban Audit is a major step towards providing comparable information on the
74
The spatial development context

## S. 104

quality of life in Europe, there is more to be done. First, the lack of attention given to rural areas
(including semi-urban or intermediate areas) has prompted suggestions that a ‘Rural Audit’
should be created alongside the Urban Audit. Due to their heterogeneity, rural areas are more
difﬁcult to analyse, yet they are an important aspect of the living space of EU citizens. Second,
comparability of urban data is not up to the standard of national or even regional data, and there
is considerable variation in the deﬁnitions of urban, metropolitan or rural areas used across
Europe. The European Commission has argued that a legal framework may be required to ensure
that Urban Audit data are fully compatible and comparable in future. A third challenge relates
to the coverage of data submitted by local authorities for the Urban Audit, which are usually in
the range of 30 to 90 per cent. This means that despite best intentions there are considerable
gaps in data for many indicators. The European Commission has suggested that the transmission
of Urban Audit data should be mandatory for local and regional authorities. However, whether
(or when) such legal frameworks will be agreed among the member states remains to be seen.
Geographical information for the EU
In order to be able to map statistical information, data need to refer to objects or phenomena
with a speciﬁc location in space. Only such geo-referenced data can be stored in geographic
information systems (GIS) as points, lines and areas. This geographic information can later be
represented on maps (with dots, dashes and patches). When stored in a GIS, spatial data are
usually divided into geometric aspects (location and dimensions) and attribute data (which refer
to other, non-geometrical characteristics, such as the composition of the population in an area)
(Kraak and Ormeling 1996). Facilitated by the increasing use of computers, over recent years
geographic information has grown in signiﬁcance in describing and analysing spatial patterns
and in supporting policy and decision-making (Maguire and Longley 2005).
Traditionally, the EU member states collect and maintain statistical and geographical data
for their own territories. As a result, there are considerable differences across Europe in the
deﬁnitions used, the timetables for data collection and the size and political status of the
administrative units for which data are collected. However, when data from different sources
are combined and used in a GIS application, common referencing systems and standards for data
collection and categorisation are crucial. This is an important concern for the EU, where more
and more geographic data from different sources (satellite imagery and surveying) and for
different spatial scales (nation-states, regions and urban areas) are integrated in spatial databases
for the European territory.
An increasing amount of comparative geographical data has become available at the EU level,
in response to perceived needs for particular information and new policy agendas (e.g. climate
change) or resulting from the evolution of policy (as in the ﬁeld of environmental policy). ‘EU
data’ are usually compiled on the basis of national and regional sources, although these are
increasingly complemented by satellite data that monitor land cover and land use for the whole
of Europe. What has emerged from this growing number of initiatives is a wealth of information
that is, however, not always well coordinated and therefore does not necessarily add up to
comprehensive and comparative information for different spatial scales. Consequently, initiatives
are being set up that seek to improve the quality, coverage and compatibility of geographical
data. Those most relevant to spatial planning are discussed below.
The European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON)
The ESPON programme is an important initiative for spatial planners as it is directly aimed at
informing spatial development policy. It is discussed in more detail in Chapter 16. The ESPON

## S. 105

programme was set up in 2002 and is ﬁnanced through the European Regional Development
Fund (ERDF). ESPON funds cooperative research projects between national spatial planning
institutes, universities and research institutes across Europe, with a focus on analysing spatial
development trends and the territorial impacts of EU policies. The ESPON 2006 programme
(2002–06) funded 35 projects in total (Table 6.1).
Numerous maps, often based on complex aggregated indicators, have been prepared by
ESPON project teams to analyse and characterise the ‘EU-29’ (i.e. EU-27 plus Norway and
Switzerland). Much of the information used in ESPON projects is based on Eurostat data and
complemented by national and regional data sources. Project reports that were produced under
the ﬁrst ESPON programme (2002–06) frequently reported the problem of missing comparable
spatial data for the EU-29. Given the current organisation of data collection and maintenance in
Europe this is not likely to be resolved easily, and may indeed be a reason for variable project
outcomes. Some initiatives have therefore been launched that aim to develop a more coordinated
and structured approach to spatial monitoring, including ‘scientiﬁc tools’ (Data Navigator;
Database Public Files) and web-based visualisations of spatial information (ESPON HyperAtlas;
ESPON WebGIS). For the current programming period (2007–13), there is an expectation that
altogether around 80 projects will be funded under four main funding priorities and covering the
EU-31 territory (EU-27, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway and Switzerland) (see Box 6.1). Under
Priority 3, there is an ambition to establish a territorial monitoring system at the European level,
which should cover different types of territories, such as metropolitan urban regions and rural
areas (see ESPON 2007c). This has prompted a debate on the need for meaningful indicators for
the analysis of spatial development trends, a call for agreed deﬁnitions of spatial structures (such
as metropolitan areas), and a proposal to set up an ‘ESPON 2013 database’. Given parallel
initiatives by the European Commission on spatial and environmental monitoring, the question
of compatibility and comparability of ESPON databases with other EU systems may soon arise.
GISCO – Geographic Information System of the European
Commission
Not all of the data compiled by Eurostat are geographically referenced, and this data therefore
cannot be mapped easily. The European Commission’s geographic information system GISCO
(a permanent service of Eurostat) was set up to promote geo-referencing of statistics and
collaboration between national statistical institutes and mapping agencies in the pursuit of
standardisation and harmonisation in the exchange of geographic information. GISCO provides

## S. 107

geographic information on, for instance, administrative boundaries, rivers, lakes, elevation 
and transport networks that is crucial for many EU sector policies with a spatial impact, 
as well as statistical maps on a wide range of topics (e.g. socio-economic indicators or 
results from the Urban Audit). GISCO products (alongside other Eurostat statistics) are, for
example, used by DG REGIO to prepare the regular reports on economic and social cohesion
(see Chapter 18).
CORINE Land Cover
The CORINE project for the ‘coordination of information on the environment’ was established
in 1985 by the European Commission to monitor the state of the European environment. Since
1994 it has been administered by the EEA. An important part of CORINE is the pan-European
project CORINE Land Cover (CLC), which is an inventory of land cover and land use, usually
cartographically presented at a scale of 1:1,000,000 (although higher resolution data are
available for a fee). The ﬁrst CLC database (CLC1990) was ﬁnalised in the 1990s, and updated
with CORINE Land Cover 2000 (CLC2000). In 2006, a third update of CORINE Land Cover
data was undertaken.
CLC is based on a satellite imaging programme (IMAGE2000) and coordinated by the Joint
Research Centre of the European Commission together with the EEA. Today, 29 countries and
more than 100 organisations are involved in the production and dissemination of CLC data.
The CORINE Land Cover network, which consists of the national ministries for the environment, national environment agencies and national centres for geographic information and/or
environmental research, is one of the oldest and most active components of EIONET.
Across the European territory, 44 different land cover types are distinguished through
photo-interpretation of satellite images by national teams in the participating countries, using
a standard methodology and nomenclature at three levels (see Plate 7a). These national land
cover inventories are then integrated into a seamless land cover map of Europe. Given the large
volume of satellite data and other information used, processing and validation across the 29
participating countries takes several years to complete.
CORINE Land Cover provides comparable data and allows an analysis of changes between
reference years (1990, 2000, 2006) on, for example, the distribution and possible deterioration
of major ecosystems, the spread of urbanisation, or changes to farming practices and agricultural
land use. Plate 7b shows such an analysis of land use change using the example of the new highspeed train line between Cologne and Frankfurt Airport in Germany. The spatial impact of this
major infrastructure investment on the landscape of the Westerwald mountains near Montabaur
is easy to see on the CLC maps.
CLC products, albeit only in low resolution, are publicly available through the EEA website
and many national institutes. The information is used for many purposes, including research,
environment, agriculture, land use planning, forestry, education, transport, demography,
tourism, energy and health. CORINE Land Cover information also helps with monitoring the
implementation of EU policy (such as the EU Sixth Environmental Action Programme or the
EU Water Framework Directive), and has been used in some commercial applications, such as
atlases and in-vehicle navigation systems. However, there are some limitations due the resolution
of the satellite data used. The smallest mapped and classiﬁed unit in CLC is 25 ha, which
prevents a very accurate assessment of surfaces (such as is needed for agriculture statistics or
urban development) (Feranec et al. 2007).
CORINE Land Cover data are an important building block for two recent initiatives, GMES
and INSPIRE, which seek to integrate European spatial and environmental information.
78
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 108

Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES)
GMES was launched in 2008 as a joint initiative of the European Commission and the European Space Agency. GMES compiles geo-spatial information from satellites, air and ground
stations and analyses in order to support decision-making of environmental agencies, civil protection organisations, and local, regional and national authorities on environment and security
matters. GMES services will be provided for three major categories:
•
mapping to support the management of natural resources (including topography, land use,
forestry monitoring, mineral and water resources);
•
support for emergency management in case of natural hazards and man-made disasters
(ﬂoods, forest ﬁres, water pollution, etc.);
•
forecasting to support the modelling of prediction of events, for instance, for marine zones,
air quality or crop yields.
The GMES initiative comprises a group of vertical services aimed at monitoring Earth subsystems (land, ocean and atmosphere) and horizontal services addressing emergency and security
issues (including in relation to climate change). One of the vertical services, the GMES Land
Monitoring Core Service (LMCS), is intended to provide geo-information on land use and land
cover changes for the global, continental and local level. The EEA, in collaboration with the
European Space Agency and the European Commission, is responsible for the implementation
of the LMCS. EU fast track funding has been provided to ensure that the service becomes quickly
available (CEC 2005b). The data will be freely accessible on the internet.
For all EU member states and neighbouring countries GMES LCMS will provide land cover
data and thematic information that will be regularly updated on, for example, water quality,
crop monitoring and forest categorisations. GMES LCMS consists of three components: local,
continental and global. On the global level, it is envisaged that GMES Land Services will support
reporting obligations from international agreements, such as the United Nations Framework
Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and the United Nations Convention on Biological
Diversity (UNCBD). The continental component of LCMS will be provided by CORINE Land
Cover information, combined with new high-resolution land cover satellite data, and it is
intended that this will support the implementation of EU directives and strategies (such as the
Water Framework Directive, Natura 2000 and the Common Agricultural Policy). The local
component of LCMS will be provided by an ‘Urban Atlas’, which adds geo-spatial data to Urban
Audit information, and is expected to be completed by 2011. Based on satellite imagery and
complemented by aerial photography and other information, it is envisaged that the Urban Atlas
will provide comprehensive land use information for 500 urban areas in Europe. The high
resolution of information will become an important new source of information on land use
change in urban areas across Europe. CORINE, which is to date the main source of EU-wide
land cover information, was initially used to monitor natural areas and agricultural land, and
therefore does not allow the monitoring of change in and around urban areas due to the
relatively coarse resolution of the satellite data.
INSPIRE: towards a European Spatial Data
Infrastructure
If these various initiatives on providing statistics and spatial information for the European
territory seem somewhat confusing, then this is because they are. As the vast majority of spatially
relevant information is collected and processed within the EU member states, standardisation

## S. 109

and harmonisation of different data sources for use at European level are therefore major
concerns. According to Maguire and Longley (2005: 11), current obstacles to the provision of
high-quality European spatial information include:
•
gaps in spatial data including missing or incomplete data;
•
a lack of documentation including metadata, which limits the reuse of data;
•
incompatibility of spatial datasets, which restricts the ability to combine multiple datasets;
•
incompatibility of geographic information systems, which makes it difﬁcult to ﬁnd, access
and reuse spatial data;
•
obstacles to sharing and reusing spatial data such as cultural, institutional, ﬁnancial and
legal barriers, which prevent or delay the use of existing spatial data.
The INSPIRE initiative intends to address these problems of ‘fragmentation of datasets and
sources, gaps in availability, lack of harmonisation between datasets at different geographical
scales and duplication of information collection’ (INSPIRE undated). INSPIRE stands for
‘Infrastructure for Spatial Information in the European Community’. It is a cross-sectoral
initiative that was established in 2007 by an EU Directive (Council 2007a). It is being set up
jointly by DG Environment, the Joint Research Centre (JRC) and Eurostat/GISCO.
The target users of INSPIRE include policy-makers, planners and managers at European,
national and local level, and citizens and their organisations, with an emphasis on policy-making
that ‘may have a direct or indirect impact on the environment’ (Council 2007a: 1). The focus
of INSPIRE is on ensuring the compatibility of different national and regional data sources by
setting out common rules and standards that EU member states will have to comply with from
2009. It is thus not envisaged that INSPIRE will require the collection of new data, but rather
that existing spatial information will be used more effectively and made more accessible and
interoperable across Europe.
INSPIRE is connected to the EU Directives on public access to environmental information
(Council 2003b; see Chapter 21) and on the re-use of public sector information (Council 2003c).
It is intended to complement existing Community action on providing spatial information (such
as CORINE Land Cover and GMES) by giving a framework that should ensure the interoperability of data across Europe. Data will be made available through an online Community
‘geo-portal’ operated by the European Commission. It is envisaged that INSPIRE will pave the
way for a progressive harmonisation of spatial data in the Member States. This should also
facilitate cross-border cooperation and planning for catchment areas of large transnational
rivers, as required by the Water Framework Directive (see Chapter 21).
Conclusion
The importance attached to an ‘evidence base’ for policy-making (Faludi and Waterhout 2006;
see Chapter 16) and the increasing emphasis on evaluation and monitoring of the effectiveness
of EU policy intervention imply ever greater demands for comparable and up-to-date statistical
and geo-referenced information on an ever wider range of topics. The timely provision of spatial
and environmental information on a variety of topics and for different European scales has been
made possible through technological advances in ICTs and GIS. This has led to a situation
whereby ‘technology is now the least pressing problem as far as policy makers are concerned.
The focus has shifted instead to legal, economic and social issues’ (Maguire and Longley 2005:
11). Yet, while current debates on revisions of EU Directives demonstrate that legal issues in
relation to public access to spatial and environmental information are an important aspect, the
80
The spatial development context
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 110

coordination of data across Europe remains the primary concern for the provision of integrated
and interoperable spatial information at the EU level.
As the vast majority of ‘European’ data are collected within the member states, the need for
improved harmonisation and standardisation of statistical and geographical data across the EU
has long been recognised. NUTS regions are mostly based on existing sub-national territorial
units, and this heterogeneity across the EU can be problematic for comparative analyses. 
The lack of comparable spatial data at the European level remains an ongoing challenge for
monitoring land use change and improving the understanding of spatial development dynamics
in Europe (for example, through migration ﬂows). As each country brings in its own tradition
of data collection, existing monitoring systems are put to the test with every enlargement of the
EU. The collection of data for use at the EU level can be complicated by changes within nationstates, be it because administrative boundaries are altered (as through local government reform
or deﬁnition of regions in new member states) or because changes to data collection are initiated
and new indicators created. New demands for data emerge continuously in response to new
policy directions and a changing spatial development context. For example, there is now a need
for harmonised data on the spatial impacts of ICT development and use, such as information
on the uptake of broadband across Europe.
Problems with deﬁnitions, such as variable interpretations of ‘agglomeration area’, often come
to the fore in transboundary cooperation activities on spatial planning where they lead to
misunderstandings and practical problems. Data are traditionally collected for administrative
units at different levels of scale and therefore largely ignore interlinkages between territories and
functional regions. This is a major limitation for transnational and metropolitan level spatial
planning, as data presented only for administrative areas are fast becoming inadequate to
describe spatial structures and trends. Therefore, although data analysis for NUTS regions will
continue for the foreseeable future,1 calls have been made for a shift from regional to spatial
analysis, including a representation of large-scale spatial development trends independent of
administrative boundaries (Schmidt-Seiwert 2003).
Recent efforts at the EU level to coordinate and harmonise spatial information at different
levels of scale therefore present important progress. There are indications that stronger legal
frameworks may be proposed for data collection and transmission from the member states to
the EU institutions. However, EU initiatives such as INSPIRE, GMES and SEIS that aim 
to coordinate varying sources of information at the European level have emerged from different camps within the EU institutions. While there is recognition that these activities should
reinforce and complement each other, it remains to be seen how this will be achieved. This 
is acknowledged in the recent Communication on SEIS, which states that ‘these activities will 
be carefully monitored in order to assess whether complementary initiatives need to be 
launched. This is to ensure that SEIS, INSPIRE and GMES will be mutually supportive’ (CEC
2008e: 9).
In parallel with initiatives to collect pan-European satellite data and to coordinate quantitative statistics, there has been a move towards more qualitative indicators. These are related
to perception studies on the quality of life in different parts of the EU, but may increasingly also
be consulted to capture, for example, the ‘softer’ outcomes of regional policy intervention, such
as learning processes through territorial cooperation networks (see Chapter 22). In terms of data
collection, interoperability and representation, the variety of information collected will thus
continue to present some challenges.

## S. 111

Further reading
•
The EU’s statistical service Eurostat prides itself on providing the ‘latest and most complete
statistical information available on the European Union, the EU Member States, the
eurozone and other countries’. Besides online databases, the website also offers access to
numerous online publications: see http://epp.eurostat.ec.europa.eu/.
•
GISCO is the Geographic Information System of the European Commission, and a
permanent service of Eurostat. Geographic data and maps are available for download from
the website: http://epp.eurostat.ec.europa.eu/portal/page/portal/gisco/introduction.
•
Reports on the state of the environment and other environmental information are available
on the EEA website: see www.eea.europa.eu/.
•
EIONET, the European Environment Information and Observation Network, aims to
provide timely and quality-assured data, information and expertise for assessing the state
of the environment in Europe: see www.eionet.europa.eu/.
•
CORINE Land Cover is an inventory of 44 land cover types for the European territory,
based on satellite imagery and available for reference years 1990, 2000 and 2006: see
www.eea.europa.eu/themes/landuse/clc-download.
•
INSPIRE – Infrastructure for Spatial Information in the European Community – is an EU
Directive aimed at making relevant, harmonised and quality geographic information
available to support the formulation, implementation, monitoring and evaluation of
Community policies with a territorial impact: see www.ec-gis.org/inspire.
•
GMES – Global Monitoring for Environment and Security – is a joint initiative of the
European Commission and the European Space Agency: see www.gmes.info.
•
The GMES Land Information Service is accessible through an online data portal, which will
be further developed over the coming years. At present it provides an overview of the
datasets available in a large variety of data repositories across Europe (e.g. on the websites
of the EEA, JRC and relevant projects): see www.land.eu/.
•
The Urban Audit presents information on cities and towns in Europe according to a wide
range of indicators: see www.urbanaudit.org.
•
ESPON – the European Spatial Planning Observation Network – is a programme co-
ﬁnanced by the European Regional Development Fund. It funds cooperation projects of
researchers across Europe on thematic and policy impact studies: see www.espon.eu.
•
While not specifically aimed at providing geographic information, the European
Commission’s Eurobarometer has provided since 1973 interesting insights into the opinions
of EU citizens on a range of EU topics, including enlargement, social situation, health,
culture, information technology, environment, the euro and defence. Data are presented by
country, and reports are published twice a year. Each survey consists of an average of 1,000
face-to-face interviews per member state: see http://ec.europa.eu/public_opinion/index_en.
htm.
There are other initiatives related to the use and provision of spatially relevant data that have
not been discussed in this chapter. The interested reader may wish to consider the following
websites:
•
EU Public Sector Information Directive, which establishes a minimum set of rules governing
the commercial and non-commercial exploitation of existing information held by the
public sector: see http://ec.europa.eu/information_society/policy/psi/index_en.htm.
•
Galileo, Europe’s global positioning service, which will be interoperable with GPS and
Glonass, the two other global satellite navigation systems: see http://ec.europa.eu/transport/
galileo/index_en.htm.

EuroGeographics is an association that represents National Mapping and Cadastral
Agencies (NMCAs) from currently 43 European countries and aims to achieve better
interoperability of geographic information across Europe: see www.eurogeographics.org/
eng/00_home.asp.
•
GoogleEarth, while not an EU initiative, has become an important source of free worldwide
geographical information, combining satellite imagery, maps, terrain information and 3D
buildings: see http://earth.google.com/.

## S. 113

## S. 114

The institutional framework
for European Union spatial
policy-making
The subject matter of EU spatial planning is inextricably linked to ‘the changing structure of
Europe and to theories of European integration, the creation of the single market and key words
such as harmonisation and subsidiarity’ (Williams 1996: 9). It is therefore important to
understand the institutional context in which the various policies and developments that will
be examined in Parts IV and V have developed over time. For the reader who is not familiar
with political science, international relations or European integration studies, this section
provides a short introduction to the nature of the European Union as a political system and as
a regional integration process. More speciﬁcally, the focus is on the most prominent theoretical
approaches of European ‘integration theories’, which seek to explain how the EU is evolving
and what the role of key players is in complex EU policy-making processes.
Over time, EU policy competences have expanded and EU institutions and decision-making
mechanisms have evolved into a complex structure. Key historical milestones in the construction
of the EU are outlined in Chapter 7. Chapters 7 and 8 present the key concepts and theories of
European integration following Nugent (2006), who distinguishes three types of conceptual and
theoretical work on European integration: attempts to conceptualise the organisational nature
of the EU as a political system; attempts to conceptualise the nature of the integration process
(both introduced in Chapter 7); and approaches to conceptualise and theorise speciﬁc aspects
of the functioning of the EU, such as policy-making processes and decision-making processes
(introduced in Chapter 8). The key institutions of the EU are presented in Chapter 9. The focus
is on the actors and their roles in those policy ﬁelds that are explored in the book, such as
regional and environmental policies. Other actors involved in European processes and interacting
with EU institutions are introduced in Chapter 10. The various forms of EU policy-making
processes and instruments of EU action are described in Chapter 11. Finally, Chapter 12
discusses the challenges that have arisen as result of ‘widening’ and ‘deepening’ the European
Union, looking at past and future enlargements, institutional reform in the EU and the future
of the EU as a political system.

Part III
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 115

European integration and the
European Union as a political 
system
The idea of a united Europe is a recurring theme in the long and often violent history of the
continent. The Holy Roman Emperors, Napoleon, Hitler, and others all sought, in sometimes
horrifying ways, to achieve a continental unity based variously on princely alliances, ethnic
cohesion, ideology, or raw power. Ever since the emergence of the modern state, philosophers and
political thinkers have also imagined a united Europe triumphing over narrow national interests and
allegiances. Today’s European Union (EU) is unique among these competing visions. Tempering the
nationalist ethos that had become the ruling principle of European political development, the
countries that formed the European Communities, the basis of the European Union, chose to limit
(but not eliminate) their own sovereignty, the hallmark of a modern nation-state, in favour of
collective peace, economic integration, and supranational governance.
(Dinan 2004: 1)
The European Union is, by all accounts, a remarkable creation. Its authority and scope resemble
those of a weak federal state. Globally, it stands out as the major exception in the thinly
institutionalized world of international politics. Something has led Europeans – and arguably only
Europeans – beyond the political framework of the nation-state
(Parsons 2003: 1)
Introduction
At the start of the twenty-ﬁrst century it seems unthinkable that any two EU member states
would enter into an armed conﬂict. This ‘unbroken peace’ (Nugent 2006: 8) is remarkable, as
it was achieved in only a few decades, following the two most destructive global wars that
humanity had ever known. This chapter brieﬂy outlines the historical context for the creation
of the EU and presents the key theoretical concepts developed to analyse the rationale and nature
of European integration and the complex, multi-level political system of governance that has
arisen as a result. For several reasons it is no easy task to deﬁne what kind of political
organisation the EU is: EU leaders have not sought to deﬁne the EU as a clear project with a
deﬁned ‘end’; the EU has always been in constant transition, evolving in an incremental way;
and it is a complex, multi-faceted and altogether unique system (Nugent 2006: 546–7) that
combines some of the features of two radically different models of political integration between
nations, often referred to as the ‘supranational’ and the ‘intergovernmental’ models.
The historical origin and development of the 
European Union
The idea of an integrated, united Europe that would transcend nationalism dates back to 
before the twentieth century. The development of a distinctively European self-understanding
Chapter 7
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 116

of Europe as ‘civilisation’ can be traced back to Ancient Greece and medieval times. In the
nineteenth century visions and ideas of a united European political system began to emerge
among philosophers and intellectuals, such as Victor Hugo and his concept of the ‘United States
of Europe’ (Dinan 2004; Pagden 2002; Wilson and Van der Dussen 1995). Yet for centuries
Europe had been crippled by wars, conﬂicts and tensions created by divisions of all sorts:
language, religion, culture, power unbalances, and political and economic divisions between
nation-states (Nugent 2006: 5–6). In the twentieth century the disastrous impact of two world
wars gave rise to a widespread determination among west European politicians ‘to put an end
to international hatred and rivalry in Europe and create the conditions for lasting peace’
(Fontaine 2006: 5). The contemporary European Union thus emerged out of the ashes of 
the Second World War. Winston Churchill had, in a speech of 19 September 1946 in Zürich,
called for a ‘United States of Europe’ in the form of a federation of states that would unify the
continent ‘in a manner unknown since the fall of the Roman Empire’ (Mauter 1998). Between
1945 and 1950 various ideas, visions and models of European cooperation and integration were
debated (Parsons 2003), sometimes in stark opposition to one another. But the founding
fathers of the European Union – the Germans Konrad Adenauer and Walter Hallstein, the British
Winston Churchill, the Italians Alcide de Gasperi and Altiero Spinelli, the French Jean Monnet
and Robert Schuman, and the Belgian Paul-Henri Spaak – all sought to persuade their nations 
to create new forms of cooperation and integration between European countries to avoid 
the reoccurrence of armed conflicts. Securing cooperation between France and Germany,
traditionally hostile countries, was in particular seen as a necessary route towards European
stability.
The various movements promoting European unity formed an International Committee of
the Movements for European Unity and organised a congress in The Hague on 7 May 1948. 
It was known as ‘The Congress of Europe’ and gathered 1,000 participants from 20 countries.
At the end of the congress a number of resolutions were adopted calling for:
the creation of an economic and political union to guarantee security, economic independence and social progress, the establishment of a consultative assembly elected by national
parliaments, the drafting of a European charter of human rights and the setting up of a court
to enforce its decisions.
(CoE 2009)
These are the themes around which the EU was to be built. The debates in the Congress,
however, revealed the main divergences between radically different visions of European
integration as explained below. On the one hand, the ‘federalists’ or ‘supranationalists’ (led by
Albert Spinelli, with strong support from France and Belgium) were calling for a proper
‘government of Europe’; on the other, the ‘intergovernmentalists’ or ‘unionists’ (led by Winston
Churchill, and backed by the UK, Ireland and the Scandinavian countries) favoured simple
intergovernmental cooperation and promoted a European consultative body (CoE 2009).
Out of the Congress emerged a new institution, created on 5 May 1949 by ten countries: the
Council of Europe (not to be confused with the European Council – see Chapters 9 and 10).
The Council of Europe was founded as a purely intergovernmental institution – and remains so
to this day. The Statute of the Council of Europe did not include any reference to a potential
constitution or pooling of national sovereignty in order to achieve the ‘economic and political
union’ called for by The Hague delegates. For many politicians in those countries most
supportive to the ideal of a strong form of European integration, there was a need to set up
separate bodies to move integration forward. On 9 May 1950, Robert Schuman, the then French
foreign minister, made a historic declaration inviting France and Germany to pool their

## S. 117

production of coal and steel under a common high authority, hoping that ‘the solidarity in
production thus established will make it plain that any war between France and Germany
becomes not merely unthinkable, but materially impossible’ (Schuman 2003, cited in Nelsen and
Stubb 2003). This proposal was based on the understanding that no single country would be
able to make weapons of war on its own. The day of the ‘Schuman Declaration’ is since regarded
as the date of birth of the European Union and now celebrated annually as ‘Europe Day’. As a
result of the proposal, in 1951 the Treaty of Paris establishing the European Coal and Steel
Community (ECSC) was signed between six countries: France, Germany, Italy, Belgium,
Luxembourg and The Netherlands. The ECSC was characterised by the creation of a
supranational ‘high authority’ that had power over coal and steel production in the six member
countries.
Following the ECSC Treaty, cooperation was expanded to all branches of the economy with
the signature of the two Treaties of Rome in 1957, which established respectively the European
Economic Community (EEC) and the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom). Box 7.1
deﬁnes what a treaty is and outlines how European treaties are made. Box 7.2 clariﬁes the use
of the terms EEC, EC and EU. The Treaty of Rome establishing the EEC was a landmark and
88
The institutional framework
Box 7.1 What is a (European) treaty?
A treaty is a binding formal agreement that establishes obligations between two or more
subjects of international law, usually states and international organisations. The use of
multilateral treaties increased dramatically during the twentieth century (e.g. the 1919 Treaty
of Versailles and the 1945 United Nations Charter). They have proved to be an effective 
way of establishing new rules of international law, for example, addressing environmental 
issues of international importance (Vienna Convention for the Protection of the Ozone Layer,
1985) or human rights protection (European Convention on Human Rights, 1950). Treaties can
include political treaties, commercial treaties, constitutional and administrative treaties, treaties
relating to criminal and civil justice, and treaties codifying international law, such as the
procedures for the peaceful settlement of international disputes (Encyclopædia Britannica 2008).
EU treaties are a blend of several of these categories.
The Treaty of Paris in 1951, which created the ﬁrst European ‘Community’, the European
Coal and Steel Community (ECSC), was the starting point for over 50 years of European treatymaking (see Table 7.1). The EU treaties constitute the supreme law of the EU. They lay down
the fundamental formal and substantive rules of the Union, in particular the division of powers
or competences between the Union and the member states, the powers of the EU institutions
and the decision-making processes.
EU treaties are made through an intergovernmental process, i.e. through negotiations and
bargaining between the national governments of the EU taking the form of ‘intergovernmental
conferences’ (IGCs), which are convened by the European Council (see Chapter 9). IGCs bring
together representatives of the governments of the member states and representatives from
the Commission and the European Parliament. Treaties have to be endorsed unanimously and
signed by all the heads of state or government of the countries that take part (see Nugent 2006).
As a consequence European treaties always represent a form of compromise between nationstates: ‘compromises that have included aspects of different visions of the future of Europe, and
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

Integration and the EU as a political system
89
compromises that, while failing to advance integration as much as some governments had hoped,
have advanced it further than others would have liked’ (Nugent 2006: 146).
Once signed by the heads of state and government, a European treaty has to be ratiﬁed in
each member state through national procedures that vary from one state to another
(parliamentary vote or referendum). When all countries have ratiﬁed a treaty, it then enters
into force on a set date. A treaty can subsequently be modiﬁed or amended through the
process described above. New EU treaties usually do not repeal previous ones, but amend and
expand them. New member states may become parties to the European treaties through a
process known as ‘accession’ (see Chapter 12). When a country joins the EU, it has to adopt
the acquis communautaire, i.e. all the existing body of EU law.
The European Treaty in force until 1 December 2009 was a consolidated version of the
Treaty of Nice (signed on 26 February 2001), which included the Treaty of Nice itself, the
Treaty on European Union (TEU) and the Treaty establishing the European Community (TEC)
in their latest amended version. The TEC and the TEU are collectively referred to as ‘the
Treaties’. A new Treaty was signed in Lisbon in December 2007 and, once ratiﬁed by all member
states, it entered into force on 1 December 2009 (see Chapter 12).
As a matter of convention the date used to refer to EU treaties in most textbooks and
ofﬁcial EU documents is the date of the signature of the treaty by EU heads of state and
government (although treaties usually enter into force up to a few years after their signature,
once ratiﬁed by all the member states).
The EU Treaties can be found online at http://europa.eu/abc/treaties/index_en.htm.
Box 7.2 Clarifying the use of terms: EEC, EC or EU?
The term ‘European Communities’ (EC) initially referred to the three organisations created by
the Treaty of Paris (1951) and the two Treaties of Rome (1957): the European Coal and Steel
Community (ECSC); the European Economic Community (EEC); and the European Atomic
Energy Community (Euratom). In 1992, a new European treaty (known as the Maastricht Treaty)
created the EU by expanding the competences of the European Community institutions and
introducing new forms of cooperation between national governments. As a result the EEC
became the European Community (EC), often referred to as ‘the Community’. The European
Community represented the so-called ‘ﬁrst pillar’ of the EU, alongside two other pillars that
function through intergovernmental cooperation (see Chapter 11). Most decisions of relevance
for spatial development were made in the ‘Community pillar’. The Treaty of Lisbon, which
entered into force in December 2009, simpliﬁes and uniﬁes the legal structure of the European
Union; the three pillars were merged into one single European Union and the distinction
between EC and EU disappeared. Only the term ‘EU’ is now in use. In this book the generic
term used by default is ‘EU’, but the term ‘EC’ or ‘the Community’ is sometimes used to refer
both to the EEC pre-1992 and to the EC when speciﬁcally referring to the ﬁrst pillar of the EU
between 1992 and the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon. CEC is the abbreviation for
the European Commission, or ‘Commission of the European Communities’.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 119

has since formed the basis for 50 years of gradual European integration. The Treaty deﬁned the
main institutions of the European Economic Community: the European Parliament, the
European Commission and the Council of Ministers. These are, to this day, the key institutions
of the European Union, although their roles have evolved over time. The EU institutions are
described in more detail in Chapter 9. The vision of an integrated Europe promoted by the
pioneers of European construction was thus rooted in a desire for peace, security and economic
prosperity and based on the ﬁrm belief that, for member states, working together rather than
separately on speciﬁc issues would be a more efﬁcient and secure path leading to these objectives.
As ‘federalist’ or ‘supranationalist’ interests took precedence among the founding countries of
the EEC, integration, rather than mere cooperation, was chosen as the way forward. The newly
founded institutions of the EEC became the primary drivers for European economic and
political integration, alongside the initiatives pursued by the Council of Europe, which continued
to play an important role in human rights and cultural cooperation.
The European Community was initially built around economic issues, as stated in Article 2
of the EEC Treaty of Rome:
The Community shall have as its task, by establishing a common market and progressively
approximating the economic policies of Member States, to promote throughout the
Community a harmonious development of economic activities, a continuous and balanced
expansion, an increase in stability, an accelerated raising of the standards of living and closer
relations between the states belonging to it.
(EEC 1957)
The primary policy concerns of the EEC in the ﬁrst decade following the Treaty of Rome
were competition, trade policy and the Common Agricultural Policy (see Part V). There was little
talk of European intervention in regional or environmental policy, or in other policy ﬁelds of
relevance to spatial planning. The construction of the European Common Market started with
the removal of restrictions on internal trade, a customs union through a Common External Tariff
and various measures to promote fair competition in the EEC. The United States government
supported the process of European economic recovery and cooperation through large-scale
ﬁnancial support to western European countries in the form of the Marshall Plan (1947–53).
This support was intended to help European states carry out their reconstruction and keep them
away from ‘communism’. The Marshall Plan, which involved the abolition of tariff trade barriers
and the management of USD 13 billion of funding by a joint European ‘Economic Cooperation
Administration’, played a signiﬁcant role in the early steps of European integration.
In the decades that followed the foundation of the EEC, the process of EU integration was
far from smooth:
Although the logic of circumstances and of political and economic changes brought the
states much more closely together, there can hardly be said to have been a common and
coherent integrationist force at work in Western Europe in the post-war years. Far from
the states being bound together in the pursuit of a shared visionary mission, relations
between them have frequently been extremely uncomfortable and uneasy, based as they have
been on a host of different national needs and perceptions of what is possible and necessary.
In consequence, the processes of cooperation and integration have operated in many
different forums, at many different levels, in many different ways, and at many different
speeds.
(Nugent 2006: 34)
90
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 120

From the 1960s onwards, there were, in particular, tensions between the supporters of a
federalist, supranational approach to European integration and those who staunchly defended
an intergovernmental approach (see below for a deﬁnition of those terms). While Germany,
Belgium and Italy supported the idea of European federalism, countries such as France and the
UK opposed it. The mid-1960s saw a major crisis opposing the French President de Gaulle and
his European partners with regard to the future path of European integration, in particular how
national sovereignty would be affected in the process. National sovereignty is ‘the legal capacity
of national decision-makers to take decisions without being subject to external constraints’
(Nugent 2006: 558). In the gradual process of European integration, ‘participant nations 
were not required to relinquish sovereignty in its totality’ but had to accept that sovereignty
could be ‘divided’ between the nation-state and the EC (Williams 1996: 14). This, however, 
did not happen without controversies and debates within nation-states about partial loss of
sovereignty and the appropriateness and desirability of EC intervention on certain issues 
(see Chapter 2 on the arguments for and against European spatial policy and planning). In brief,
the European integration process was not inevitable but the result of a mix of ‘ideas, intellectual
fashion, opportunity, chance, conviction, calculation, personal predilection, and ambition’
(Dinan 2004: 1).
As a consequence, the European integration process has moved forward through periods of
‘slow integrationist advance’ (e.g. the mid-1970s to the early 1980s), followed by periods of fastpaced integrationist developments (e.g. the mid-1980s until the early 1990s) (Nugent 2006: 35).
The integration process is characterised by four areas of development (Nugent 2006: Ch. 3):
Treaty development (see Table 7.1); geographical enlargement (Chapter 12); development of
policy processes (i.e. the way decisions and policies are made (see Chapter 11); and development
of policies (see Part V). Over 50 years the EU has grown from the six founding countries of 1957
to 27 member states in 2007. The member states that joined the EEC (later the EU) since its
initiation have done so for a variety of political and economic reasons, and sometimes faced
strong opposition from existing members. The debates over the advantages and disadvantages
of European integration have often been coloured by populist rhetoric and media misrepresentations of the EU (CEC 2009a, 2009b). The pros and cons of integration relate to a fundamental
tension between:
on the one hand, member-state governments trying to preserve their sovereignty by relating
to each other as equals and, on the other hand, the forces and impulses that compel or
encourage them to transfer sovereignty to a new supranational identity.
(McCormick 1999: 141)
Yet ‘although all the states have reservations about, and some have fundamental criticisms
of, certain aspects of the EU, each has judged that membership enhances its ability to achieve
certain state objectives’, such as ‘the promotion of economic growth and prosperity, the control
of economic and social forces that are not conﬁned to national boundaries, and the strengthening
of political inﬂuence’ (Nugent 2006: 561). The UK, for example, ﬁrst decided not to join the
founding member states of the EEC for fear that membership would impact on patterns of trade
with the Commonwealth. When in 1961 and 1967 the UK eventually applied to join the EEC
for economic reasons, its application was vetoed twice by French President De Gaulle, but the
UK eventually joined the EEC in 1973.
Besides geographical expansion, the ﬁelds of competence of the EC gradually broadened,
although this was not a linear and regular process. From the purely economic focus of the Treaty
of Rome, EU competences have been subsequently expanded to integrate, among others,
policies dealing with regional, social, environmental, cultural, consumer protection, health and

## S. 122

immigration issues. These expansions of EU competences were enshrined in the successive
revisions of the European treaties (Table 7.1), although treaty revisions have often involved the
‘formal consolidation of emergent practices’ shaped during the ‘everyday politics’ of the EU
(Rosamond 2000: 5).
Theoretical perspectives on the EU as a political
system and European integration as a process
The nature of the EU as ‘a political system, but not a state’
Political scientists and international relations theorists often refer to the characteristics of a state,
or to types of inter-state and intergovernmental organisations, when seeking to describe the
features of the EU as a political system.1 The key features of a state are territoriality, sovereignty,
legitimacy, monopoly of governance (Nugent 2006: 548) and monopoly over the legitimate use
of physical force and coercion (Weber 1919). These features are present only in a partial way
in the EU, which therefore cannot be characterised as a ‘state’ in its own right. The EU, for
example, does not have a ‘monopoly over the use of coercion’ (Hix 2005: 4): this power still
lies with the national governments of EU member states, which are responsible for policing,
security, and the judicial and penal system. It also does not, strictly speaking, have a territory,

## S. 123

as decisions over the use of space are the sovereignty of the member states. However, the EU
has some degrees of sovereignty since EU jurisdiction in the policy areas deﬁned by the EU
Treaties applies to the whole of the EU territory.
If the EU is compared to other inter-state organisations, it is thus more complex, more
developed and more extended in terms of competences than any other existing international
organisations that are solely intergovernmental in nature. Due to the hybrid nature of the EU,
it has been debated whether the EU is similar to a federal system such as the United States and
the Federal Republic of Germany, in which ‘general (or national) and local administrative units
coexist with shared and independent powers, neither having supreme authority over the other’
(McCormick 1999: 7). Yet in spite of some federal features the EU is not a ‘classical federal
system’ (Nugent 2006: 550–3). Lawyers talk about the EU as a sui generis construct, neither
international organisation nor federal state. The EU is thus a unique, complex and hybrid form
of international organisation and a political system that combines ‘intergovernmental’ and
‘supranational’ features. These two terms refer on the one hand to theories of integration
(summarised below) and on the other to methods of decision-making in international
organisations, which are explored in Chapter 11.
Intergovernmentalism refers to ‘arrangements whereby nation-states, in situations and
conditions they control, cooperate with one another on matters of common interest’ without
their sovereignty being undermined (Nugent 2006: 558). Decisions are made by unanimity.
Intergovernmentalism is used by most contemporary international organisations such as the
United Nations. Supranationalism refers to a system in which a collection of states collectively
and voluntarily decide to transfer some sovereignty to a higher institution in certain ﬁelds,
leading to a situation in which they ‘may be obliged to do things against their preferences and
their will because they do not have the power to stop decisions’ (Nugent 2006: 558). Table 7.2
summarises the ‘intergovernmental’ and ‘supranational’ features of the EU, highlighting the
unique nature of the EU as a political system.
Who and what drives European integration? The grand theories
The process of European integration has surpassed all existing forms of international integration
in the world. Since the 1950s, political scientists and international relation theorists have
attempted to explain the key drivers and direction of European integration. These ‘grand
theories’ of integration have sought to explain the scope and dynamics in three dimensions:
vertical (the deepening of EU competences in particular ﬁelds through a transfer of domestic
decision-making competences from the nation-state to the EU); horizontal (integration of 
new policy areas); and geographical (territorial widening or enlargement) (Schimmelfennig and
Rittberger 2006: 74). Such theories have traditionally been broadly divided into two strands:
the ‘neo-functionalist’ view, and the ‘intergovernmentalist’ or ‘state-centric’ approach. These two
perspectives have since the mid-1960s formed ‘the two great monoliths at the gate of the study
of European integration’ (Hix 2005: 15). The key concepts and arguments of each school of
thought are briefly introduced here. A more detailed overview of theories of European
integration and their recent developments is provided by Rosamond (2000), Chryssochoou
(2001), Wiener and Diez (2004), Nelsen and Stubb (2003), Schimmelfennig and Rittberger
(2006), Cini (2006) and Eilstrup-Sangiovanni (2006).
Neo-functionalism: European integration as ‘spillover process’
Ernst Haas (1958), in a study of the early development of the ECSC, later followed by Lindberg
(1963), Lindberg and Scheingold (1970) and others, was the ﬁrst scholar to argue that European
