## S. 125

integration has a deterministic dynamic of its own. He contended that integration in one economic
sector creates incentives for integration in another sector through a continuous process of
‘functional spillover’, because policy sectors are closely connected to each other. Additionally, neofunctionalists argue that economic integration inevitably leads to the need for increased political
integration (the so-called ‘political spillover’). Major advances in European integration have indeed
been rooted in the integration of speciﬁc aspects of economic activities with national politicians
‘then, at times seemingly as an afterthought, realising that this requires political integration too if
there is to be political direction and control’ (Nugent 2006: 135). The completion of the Single
European Market (SEM) in the late 1980s has, for example, stimulated the movement towards an
economic and monetary union (Chapter 17), as the harmonisation of macroeconomic and
ﬁnancial policies appeared to be necessary for the SEM to realise its full potential (Nugent 2006:
54). The SEM programme also encouraged the rapid development of EU legislation in the
environmental ﬁeld, in order to ensure that there was no distortion of competition between member
states through different standards of environmental protection (see Chapter 21).
The drivers of the spillover process are both domestic interest groups (such as business
associations, trade unions and political parties) and the supranational European institutions 
(in particular the European Commission) (Hix 2005: 15). From the mid-1960s onwards neofunctionalism was heavily criticised for not being able to explain the periods of slowdown of
European integration (such as the crisis of 1965–66) and the strengthening of the intergovernmental aspects of the EC (Hix 2005: 15). The early proponents of neo-functionalism, such
as Haas, retreated from their earlier position, and ‘state-centric theories’ of European integration
became more prominent from the mid-1960s onwards.
Intergovernmentalism: a state-centric view of European integration
Intergovernmentalism posits that the European integration process is driven primarily by
individual nation-states that voluntarily choose to cooperate in speciﬁc ﬁelds while retaining 
their sovereignty. Scholars such as Stanley Hoffmann (1966, 1982) and Alan Milward (1984,
1992) argued that states have ultimate control over the process and direction of European
integration, and that they act in accordance with their perceived national self-interest. When
‘national interests’ between several states coincide with one another, national governments
support more integration in limited areas characterised as ‘low politics’ (rather than the ‘high
politics’ functions of security and defence, over which nation-states ﬁercely wish to retain
independence). European institutions are controlled by states, which are the ultimate decision
makers and only cede power to supranational institutions to achieve speciﬁc policy aims that best
serve their interests.
The spillover theory is thus rejected, since national governments control the pace and extent
of the integration process, with EU institutions merely ‘facilitating’ the process. It is argued that
supranational organisations have much less political inﬂuence than national governments.
According to Moravcsik (1998: 18):
EU integration can best be understood as a series of rational choices made by national
leaders. These choices responded to constraints and opportunities stemming from the
economic interests of powerful domestic constituents, the relative power of each state in the
international system, and the role of institutions in bolstering the credibility of interstate
commitments.
A ‘logic of diversity’ rather than ‘integration’ prevails (Hoffmann 1966: 869). Consequently, in
a state-centric view of European integration, the interests, preferences, agendas, actions,
96
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 126

decisions of nation-states and the process of negotiation and bargaining between them became
the focus of academic investigation to understand the dynamics of European integration.
Examples of analyses following the intergovernmental approach are studies of intergovernmental
conferences and of the decision-making processes in the Council of Ministers.
Since the 1980s both strands of theories (neo-functionalism and intergovernmentalism) have
been expanded and diversiﬁed through various contributions that will not be discussed here;
among others: ‘supranational institutionalism’ (Sandholtz and Stone Sweet 1998), ‘liberalintergovernmentalism’ (Moravcsik 1998), social constructivist (Checkel 2003), and discursive
perspectives on EU politics (T. Diez 1999, 2001). For an overview see Eilstrup-Sangiovanni
2006; Hix 2005; Schimmelfennig and Rittberger 2006). Among this complex landscape of
divergent theoretical perspectives on European integration, not one single theory ‘can 
claim to possess the explanatory power to account for “European integration” in its entirety’
(Schimmelfennig and Rittberger 2006: 92). Contemporary scholars of EU integration now
mostly agree that both strands can be mobilised at different times to explain different things.
This requires ‘being aware of the empirical domain where the theory is expected to do “best”
in explaining integration phenomena’ (Schimmelfennig and Rittberger 2006: 93). This in itself
is no easy task:
deciding what theory is ‘right’ is not a case of deciding which theory’s assumptions about
actors, institutions and information are closest to the reality. How good a theory is depends
on how much and how efﬁciently it can explain for a particular set of facts.
(Hix 2005: 18)
In order to understand how the European spatial planning agenda has developed, state-centric
perspectives tend to be more appropriate than a neo-functionalist view, because this agenda
(discussed in Chapter 14) has been strongly inﬂuenced by intergovernmental cooperation.
In the 1990s a new generation of scholarly work shifted the focus away from the study of
the overall dynamics of European integration to look in more detail at the day-to-day
functioning of the EU as a political system (George and Bache 2001), and in particular how EU
institutions interact with other levels of government in Europe – national, regional and local –
giving way to new forms of ‘multi-level governance’.
The EU and other tiers of governments: understanding
multi-level governance
The EU can be thought of as a political system with a European layer (European Commission,
European Council and European Parliament), a national layer, and a regional and local layer.
These layers interact with each other across different levels of government (vertical dimension)
as well as with relevant non-governmental actors within the same level (horizontal dimension).
Gary Marks and Lisbet Hooghe (Hooghe 1996; Hooghe and Marks 2001, 2003; Marks et al.
1996) argue that a new conceptual framework, namely ‘multi-level governance’, is needed to
capture the increasingly fragmented and polycentric form of the EU political system and explain
how the relationships between actors at different territorial levels (and their reconﬁguration as
part of the evolving process of EU integration) inﬂuence policy outcomes. The concept of ‘multilevel governance’ was ﬁrst developed in relation to the design and management of EU Cohesion
Policy and the Structural Funds, which have been based on the principle of ‘partnership’ between
European, national and regional authorities (see Chapter 18). The concept of multi-level
governance has since been extended to other EU policy ﬁelds.

Integration and the EU as a political system
97
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 127

The concept of multi-level governance has two key dimensions: the ‘multi-level’ aspect, which
refers to the interdependence between different levels of government; and the ‘governance’
element, referring to the interdependence between governmental and non-governmental actors
(Bache and Flinders 2004: 3). Proponents of the concept of multi-level governance challenge 
the hierarchical, state-centric approach of early European integration studies by arguing that on
different issues and in different arenas, changing combinations of supranational, national and
sub-national institutional actors collaborate and network in the policy formulation and implementation process (Hooghe 1996; Ward and Williams 1997). Decision-making competences are
not only exercised by national governments but also by institutions and actors at other levels:
supranational actors at EU level (the Commission, the European Parliament and the European
Court of Justice); and actors at the sub-national levels, such as regional and local authorities
actively engaged in policy activities. The multi-level governance concept therefore challenges
hierarchical models of the organisation of the nation-state in which policy development is
supposed to take place primarily at the national level while implementation takes place at the
sub-national level.
At the European level, multi-level governance stands in contrast with intergovernmentalist
approaches by emphasising that authority – rather than being concentrated – is gradually
dispersing across different sectors and levels of action, and that political action occurs within
and between various levels of governance. National political activity is no longer seen as being
conﬁned to the national arena, and national inputs into EU decision-making are no longer seen
as predominantly channelled via state-level actors. Instead, a variety of connections and channels
exist between different levels of government – supranational, national and sub-national. The
concept of multi-level governance, therefore, views the EU as:
a polity, or at least as a polity in the making, in which power and inﬂuence are exercised
at multiple levels of government. National state executives are seen as extremely important
actors in the EU arena, but the almost semi-monopolistic position that is ascribed to them
by many state-centrists is ﬁrmly rejected.
(Nugent 2006: 557)
The concept of multi-level governance also emphasises the increasingly important, frequent
and complex interactions between governmental actors and non-state actors that are mobilised
in policy-making in the EU. ‘Governance’ as a concept is rooted in institutional economics,
organisational sociology and public management (rational and public choice theory) (Jessop
1995; Le Galès 1998). The concept became increasingly used in political science to refer to
changes in the way public action and the exercise of politics changed:
government now involves a wide variety of actors and processes beyond the state, the
relationships between state and non-state actors have become less hierarchical and more
interactive, and the essential ‘business’ of government has become the regulation of public
activities rather than the redistribution of resources.
(Nugent 2006: 556)
To achieve this regulation, ‘the capacity to organize collective action, to build coalitions and
partnerships directed towards speciﬁc goals’ (Le Galès 1998: 496) has become a fundamental
aspect of government, and has led political scientists to coin the new concept of ‘governance’.
In urban politics, the term has gained particular currency to refer to new forms of political
regulation and territorial management in cities and to new forms of public–private partnerships
98
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 128

in urban regeneration, planning and economic development. At the EU level, some authors have
argued that the EU is ‘transforming politics and government at the European and national 
level into a system of multi-level, non-hierarchical, deliberative and apolitical governance, 
via a complex web of public/private networks and quasi-autonomous executive agencies’ (Hix
1998: 54) or ‘policy networks’ (see Chapter 8). Jordan (2001) has argued that within the EU
governance, i.e. a political system that is fractured, decentred and often lacking in clear spatial
as well as functional authority, has replaced government dominated by sovereign states.
A good example of ‘multi-level governance’ in a policy ﬁeld of particular relevance for
European spatial planning is the case of EU environmental policy, which is discussed in detail
in Chapter 21:
we have national ministers making decisions in the Council of Ministers, but we also have
decisions being inﬂuenced by information being generated by the European Environment
Agency, policy development being driven by technical experts in the European Commission,
pressure being brought to bear on the policy process by increasingly well-organised interest
groups, policies being implemented by national and local authorities (whose cooperation
is thus critical), and a signiﬁcant body of law and policy being generated by agreements
reached in international organizations which are themselves being heavily inﬂuenced by the
economic power of the European Union.
(McCormick 2001: 7–8)
Scholars of multi-level governance, by deliberately embracing the discourse of governance, have
encouraged a healthy dialogue between branches of political science and public policy that 
had, for a long time, evolved quite separately (Jordan 2001). This dialogue is vital, since the EU
‘neither resembles domestic polities nor international organisations, and therefore defies
explanations from approaches applied to either politics within states or politics between states’
(Bache and Flinders 2004: 1). However, unlike the ‘grand theories’ of European integration
referred to above, multi-level governance does not try to explain the dynamics of European
integration. It has been criticised for being ‘more metaphor than theory’ (Rosamond 2000: 197),
and for lacking ‘a set of testable hypotheses’ (Jordan 2001: 201). One of the main questions of
the ‘grand integration theories’ presented above, namely the reasons behind the transfer of loyalty
and sovereignty between national and supranational entities and the future of this relationship
in the EU, is not addressed by multi-level governance scholars. The concept remains weak in
explaining ‘which levels are the most important and why, and what actually motivated the
experiment in governance in the ﬁrst place’ (Jordan 2001: 194). Some authors have consequently
labelled multi-level governance a ‘(dis)ordering framework’ (Rosamond 2000: 111) that identiﬁes
certain attributes such as ‘multi-actorness’, unpredictability and complexity without really
explaining them.
In spite of these criticisms, the added value of the concept of multi-level governance for spatial
planning research has to be recognised. It is now frequently used as an explanatory framework
in planning research in the EU, or at least to describe the dynamic and complex interrelationships between different tiers of government and scales of planning. The debates surrounding 
the concept of multi-level governance echo some of the shifts in urban studies, political geography and urban politics about the ‘rescaling’ or the ‘hollowing out’ of the nation-state 
(Brenner 1999, 2003, 2004) and the rise to prominence of sub-national interests (Le Galès 2002)
(see Chapters 2 and 4).

Integration and the EU as a political system

## S. 129

Conclusion
The process of European integration has gone beyond all existing forms of inter-state integration
in the world. The EU represents a unique political system, more than a simple intergovernmental
association of countries, but it is still less than a federation (Wallace 1983; Williams 1996: 30).
Its creation has changed the life of Europeans and the political and economic organisation of
the EU territory, as EU policies have created unprecedented possibilities and forms of mobility
for goods, ideas, capital and people. The European integration process until now has been far
from smooth, but its achievements in only half a century, if looked at in a wider historical
perspective, are extraordinary. Yet the EU is not a stable construct that is nearing some form
of ‘completion’. There are major challenges for the future of the EU, which are discussed in
Chapter 12.
As the last two sections of this chapter have shown, ‘different kinds of theories are
appropriate for different pieces of the EU puzzle’ (Sandholtz 1996: 426). Grand theories look
at the dynamics of the integration process as a whole, while other conceptual frameworks, often
referred to as ‘middle-range theories’, focus on the functioning of the complex, multi-level
political system that has emerged in the EU in various policy ﬁelds. These different kinds of
theories, presented in the next chapter, offer complementary, rather than competing, frameworks
to explain different aspects of the EU political system (Nugent 2006: 576–7; Peterson and
Bomberg 1999; Warleigh 2000).
Further reading
On the history of European integration, see Dinan 2004, on theories of European integration,
Eilstrup-Sangiovanni 2006, Nelsen and Stubb 2003 and Rosamond 2000, and on multi-level
governance, Bache and Flinders 2004. A clear timeline and history of the EU is presented on
the EU website at http://europa.eu/abc/history/index_en.htm.
100
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 130

Theoretical approaches to EU
governance and policy-making
EU politics are complex, so unsurprisingly, a large number and variety of middle-range theoretical
approaches have been used to assist explanation of them. This is intellectually healthy since no 
one approach is likely to be capable of capturing the essential nature of the many different 
facets of EU politics.
(Nugent 2006: 572)
Introduction
The ‘grand theories’ of neo-functionalism and intergovernmentalism, introduced in the 
previous chapter, seek to explain how the EU as a political construct has emerged and how 
it is evolving. Because the focus of such ‘grand theories’ is on the EU polity and thus the 
‘macro level’ of European integration, they cannot provide detailed explanations for how the
multi-level governance system in the EU works and how policy and decisions are made. 
This is the level of analysis of the so-called ‘middle-range’ theories of integration, which are 
more appropriate for analysis of the governance and politics of a ﬁeld such as EU spatial policy.
Middle-range theoretical approaches of European integration deal with aspects related to the
actual workings of EU institutions, the inﬂuence of different institutional settings and actor
constellations on policy outcomes, and the impact of EU policy-making and decision-making
on the member states and regions, rather than trying to explain the processes of European
integration as a whole.
However, the concern with understanding the complexity of European governance and policy
processes has resulted in a rather confusing array of different approaches, with ‘a number of
different theories that are only in part mutually exclusive or competing with each other’
(Jachtenfuchs 2002: 651). Perhaps this variety of approaches is inevitable, because as Nugent
(2006: 572) reminds us ‘EU politics are complex’ and:
no one approach is likely to be capable of capturing the essential nature of the many
different facets of EU politics. To take, for example, an area of activity that has been the
main focus for middle-range theorising, policy processes, there is no reason to expect any
one theoretical approach to be wholly satisfactory. After all, there are many different 
types of EU policy process with some, for instance, being essentially intergovernmental 
in character and others being more supranational. Within each policy process there are
several stages – from the initial stages of problem identiﬁcation and then policy initiation
through to the ﬁnal stage of policy evaluation. And each policy area has its own troupe of
policy actors.

Chapter 8
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 131

European integration is an ongoing and a dynamic ﬁeld of study. Diez and Wiener (2004)
have shown how different phases of European integration theory have emerged in their historical
disciplinary context, with ongoing widening and deepening of European integration requiring
more and more complex conceptualisations. Nugent (2006: 572) has therefore suggested that
it may be best to speak of ‘theoretical approaches rather than full-blown theories because they
are still very much in development, arguably have greater descriptive than explanatory uses and
powers, and have only a very limited predictive capacity’.
In this chapter selected theoretical approaches will be presented that can help to explain the
policy and decision-making processes in the EU and to analyse European governance. Given the
ever-growing body of literature on middle-range theories on European integration, the selection
has been based on their previous application to the ﬁeld of spatial planning. They are presented
as an overview of possible theoretical tools for the analysis of different stages of the policy
process.
Theoretical approaches to understanding (spatial) 
policy-making in the EU
Planning scholars have borrowed ideas from other disciplines, such as international relations
or political science, to try to understand the dynamics of decision-making within the multi-level
governance system of the EU, how they can inﬂuence policy outcomes and how EU policy may
affect their own work. The complexity of EU policies and politics is a particularly interesting
ﬁeld for analysis for an area such as spatial planning, where spatially relevant policy is prepared
both following the Community method as well as intergovernmentally. Some authors have
begun to apply middle-range theories of European integration to the ﬁeld of intergovernmental
spatial planning processes, although there are many more examples of their application in those
policy areas where the EU has a competence, such as environment.
However, besides analysing direct regulatory inﬂuences of the EU (e.g. through binding policy
and law), the academic debate has also turned to consider the sociological aspects of European
integration and the ‘frame of reference’ that the EU provides and through which it promotes
‘European’ values and norms. Kohler-Koch (1999: 29) explains that the ‘formulation of European policies enmeshes national and Community actors in a complex discursive process. It
incorporates a shared understanding of the basic rationale of the aims and purpose of European
political regulation’. The four theoretical approaches that may be useful in explaining the EU
policy process and that have been applied to the ﬁeld of European spatial planning are summarised
in Table 8.1. Though mostly conceived in the context of ‘formal’ EU policy-making (i.e. sector
policies such as environment), these approaches have proven ﬂexible enough to offer explanations
for spatial planning policy and intergovernmental cooperation processes, as the examples of their
previous application to the ﬁeld of European spatial planning in Table 8.1 show.
The literature on Europeanisation uses a range of theoretical approaches to explain EU and
member-state interrelationships in terms of institutional adaptation and changing policies and
practices. The ‘new institutionalisms’ are based on the assertion that institutions (deﬁned
broadly as formal and informal procedures, practices, relationships, customs and norms) matter
in determining policy and decisional outcomes (Nugent 2006). The ‘policy network’ approach
is, to a certain extent, an application of new institutionalism, and focuses on the role of policy
networks in the policy process. The epistemic communities approach is a variant of this, and is
particularly concerned with the role of specialist knowledge in complex policy processes.
Because the policy network and epistemic communities approaches are based on institutionalist
perspectives and will therefore beneﬁt from a prior discussion of the new institutionalisms, the
four theoretical tools will be discussed in reverse order to that presented in Table 8.1.
102
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 132

In using this categorisation, the four approaches should not be viewed as a predetermined
‘theoretical toolkit to map the development of a piece of legislation or the emergence of
European-level competence in a given area’ (Rosamond 2000: 113). While the approaches
presented here share a focus on the role of actors in the multi-level governance process of policymaking in the EU and an institutionalist (and increasingly a sociological and cognitive)
perspective on explaining the sharing of ideas and ongoing integration in Europe, it should be
remembered that each theoretical approach has its own merits and that they should be seen as
potentially complementary, rather than competing, tools of analysis.
Europeanisation
The concept of Europeanisation has, since the 1990s, received considerable interest in academic
debate, in particular in the ﬁeld of environmental policies, but more recently also in spatial planning (see Dühr et al. 2007). The attention given to a possible ‘Europeanisation’ of spatial
planning comes in recognition of the increasing inﬂuence of EU policy on planning within the
member states, the support given to transnational cooperation on spatial development over the
last decade, and the learning effects that are expected to come with such cooperation.
There are many deﬁnitions and applications of the concept of ‘Europeanisation’ (see for
instance Olsen 2002; Radaelli 2004; Wishlade et al. 2003), but in its broadest sense it describes
the impacts of the EU on national polities, policies and politics, on the one hand, and the
inﬂuence of national discourses on the development of governance at the European level
through the process of ‘uploading’, on the other. Wishlade et al. (2003) have suggested categorising the concept of Europeanisation into top-down inﬂuences (imposition or transposition
of EU policies to domestic policy areas) and bottom-up processes (uploading of national ideas
to the EU level). Horizontal (between EU member states) and circular Europeanisation processes
(from the national level to the EU and back to the national level, for example, in the form 
of new EU Directives or initiatives) can be added to this (Lenschow 2006). The conception of

## S. 133

horizontal and circular Europeanisation processes is closely related to ideas about policy
transfer. The degree of policy transfer can vary from copying (direct and complete transfer),
emulation (transfer of ideas behind a policy or programme) and combinations (mixtures of
several different policies) to inspiration (where the ﬁnal outcome is inspired by, but does not
actually draw on, the original policy) (Dolowitz and Marsh 2000).
Despite the increasing recognition of the interactive or circular nature of Europeanisation
processes, the top-down conceptualisation of Europeanisation is still widely used in the
literature. It refers to the ‘process by which domestic policy areas become increasingly subject
to European policy-making’ (Börzel 1999: 574). Knill and Lehmkuhl (1999) have identiﬁed three
mechanisms of change for institutional adaptation processes in response to European
requirements to occur. These are not mutually exclusive, and can to varying degrees all play a
role in adaptation processes. The ﬁrst mechanism concerns the imposition or prescription of an
institutional model by the EU to which domestic arrangements have to be adjusted. This implies
limited discretion at national level in order to comply with European requirements. The second
mechanism involves altering the ‘rules of the game’ (Knill and Lehmkuhl 1999: 2) by changing
domestic opportunity structures and a corresponding redistribution of resources and power. The
weakest mechanism is the third, through which European policy affects domestic arrangements
indirectly through altering the beliefs and expectations of domestic actors, by providing a
cognitive logic and normative frame for meaning and action (Radaelli 2004). Such ‘actionoriented intentional learning’ (Evans and Davies 1999: 368) is considered to play a crucial role
in transnational and interregional cooperation on spatial development such as those supported
by EU Cohesion Policy (see Dühr and Nadin 2007).
Wishlade et al. (2003) have pointed out that there is considerable variation in domestic
responses to EU pressures, which can range from absorption to accommodation or transformation. Such variation can be explained by three key factors. The ﬁrst factor is the ‘goodness of
ﬁt’ between European institutions and policies and domestic structures. Börzel and Risse (2000)
have argued that if the ﬁt between European institutions and national institutions is low,
adaptational pressure will be high and demanding transformation as nation-states will be forced
to alter domestic policy in response to EU requirements. If, however, the institutional and policy
ﬁt is high, then adaptational pressure is low and absorption can be expected as member states
incorporate EU requirements without substantially changing existing practices.
The second factor relates to domestic institutional structures, such as unitary or federal
territorial structures and the mix of public and private actors. Particular attention is given to
political obstacles because:
the more power is dispersed across the political system and the more actors have a say 
in political decision making, the more difﬁcult it is to foster the domestic consensus or
‘winning coalition’ necessary to introduce institutional changes in response to Europeanisation pressures.
(Risse et al. 2001: 9)
The structure of the state can be of particular relevance in this context as, for example, in federal
countries such as Germany and Belgium the regions have greater responsibility in the ﬁeld of
regional development, and this could lead to complications in decision-making processes
(Wishlade et al. 2003). Other ‘facilitating’ institutional structures, such as a ‘consensus-oriented
or cooperative decision-making culture’ (Börzel and Risse 2000: 9) can, however, help to
overcome such political obstacles.
The preferences and strategies of actors present a third factor that determines the effect of
Europeanisation. The processes leading to changes in actors’ preferences can, according to Börzel
104
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 134

and Risse (2000), be conceptualised either from a rationalist institutionalist perspective or from
a sociological institutionalist perspective. The two logics are not mutually exclusive, and Börzel
and Risse (2000) argue that they often occur simultaneously or characterise different phases in
a process of adaptational change. The rationalist institutionalist perspective follows the ‘logic
of consequentialism’ (March and Olsen 1998) whereby the misﬁt between European and
domestic processes, policies and institutions provides societal and political actors with new
opportunities and constraints in pursuing their interests. Whether such changes in the political
opportunity structure lead to a redistribution of power within the domestic territory depends
on the capacity of actors to exploit these opportunities and to avoid the constraints. The logic
of rationalist institutionalism thus suggests that Europeanisation ‘leads to domestic change
through a differential empowerment of actors resulting from a redistribution of resources at the
domestic level’ (Börzel and Risse 2000: 2). A sociologist institutionalist perspective, in contrast,
emphasises a ‘logic of appropriateness’ (March and Olsen 1998) and a process of persuasion.
The EU exerts adaptational pressure on domestic-level processes, because European policies,
norms and the collective understandings attached to them do not resonate well with domestic
norms and collective understandings. According to this sociologist institutionalist understanding,
Europeanisation thus leads to domestic change through ‘a socialization and collective learning
process resulting in norm internalization and the development of new identities’ (Börzel and
Risse 2000: 2).
These conceptualisations of top-down Europeanisation provide a useful framework for
understanding the potential impact of EU policy-making and transboundary cooperation on
policy transfer and institutional change. However, the top-down approach has been criticised
for ignoring the interactive nature of Europeanisation through mutual inﬂuence between EU and
domestic institutions. Knill (2005: 767), for example, has pointed out that ‘governments do not
learn about policy practices randomly, but rather through common afﬁliations, negotiations and
institutional membership’. Increasingly, therefore, Europeanisation is conceptualised as an
interactive and iterative process of both institution building at the EU level and corresponding
changes at the domestic level, which allows for an exploration of ‘feedback loops’ and the
‘uploading’ of domestic preferences. More recently the research focus has shifted accordingly
to also consider the involvement of sub-national levels of governance in Europeanisation
processes, as well as the relevance of non-binding policy (besides the EU regulatory framework)
in affecting domestic change. For instance, researchers have analysed the growing ‘Europeanisation’ of local government because of its involvement in EU-funded programmes (Marshall
2005), and the impact of Structural Funds on institutional capacity at regional levels (Boland
2001; Gualini 2004a). The emergence of new, non-binding forms of EU policy-making and
integration which depart from the traditional forms of hierarchical governance through formal
EU decision-making and law making processes (described in Chapter 11) have been conceptualised as prompting new modes of ‘facilitated coordination’ or ‘horizontal Europeanization’
(Bulmer and Padgett 2004; Knill and Lehmkuhl 1999; Radaelli 2004; Wishlade et al. 2003).
Non-regulatory policies have prompted a varied set of instruments and processes to ensure their
application, such as the ‘Open Method of Coordination’ (Bomberg and Peterson 2000; Borrás
and Greve 2004; Faludi 2004a – see Chapter 15), benchmarking, mainstreaming, policy transfer
and lesson-drawing (see Bruno et al. 2006). What these instruments have in common is that they
are aimed at triggering the production of a consensus on a ‘common path’ through a non-binding
process of cooperation (Bruno et al. 2006). They constitute new forms of ‘non-coercive processes
based on the will of the participants to agree, by way of collective deliberation, on procedural
norms, modes of regulation and common political objectives and, at the same time, to preserve
the diversity of national and even local experiences’ (Bruno et al. 2006). The sharing and learning
process can take place between national levels of government (Bomberg and Peterson 2000),

Theoretical approaches to EU governance

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 135

but also between sub-national levels of governments (Marshall 2005; Ward and Williams 
1997). In this process the EU plays the role of ‘mediator or facilitator of cross national policy
transfer’ that supports broader European reform objectives (Knill and Lehmkuhl 1999). The
‘channels of change’ involved are ideas, discourses, information, learning and socialisation
processes (Giannakourou 2005; Knill and Lehmkuhl 1999; Radaelli 2000) that lead to norm
internalisation (Börzel and Risse 2000).
Analysing such horizontal or circular processes of Europeanisation requires a consideration
of how the cognitive mechanisms of Europeanisation work through knowledge sharing, peer
review, mutual learning and exchange of ‘good practices’. Bruno et al. (2006: 531) underline
the point that these mechanisms are not neutral and that they have strong normative
implications, prescribing norms of governmental conduct, through a subtle shift ‘from the
harmonization of orientations to the normalization of “good” policies’. This may, or may not,
lead to convergence between policies and practices across Europe. Such ‘soft law’ mechanisms
and other coordination tools offer advantages for the EU institutions because they are more 
cost-effective than legal instruments and prevent the implementation problems that arise from 
non-compliance with regulatory policies (Ward and Williams 1997). They are encouraged by
the EU institutions in those ﬁelds where the Community method does not apply because they
require member states ‘to look at issues through European lenses, slowly integrating these lenses
into national policy’ (Faludi 2007c: 14), thereby contributing to processes of Europeanisation.
Europeanisation processes are not limited to the direct impact of EU legislation or to
spillover effects (Bruno et al. 2006) but also encompass values and policy paradigms, shared
norms and rules (Börzel and Risse 2000; March and Olsen 1998; Olsen 2002). Europeanisation
is thus increasingly conceptualised as involving complex processes of horizontal and vertical
integration, and these cannot always be easily distinguished. This is particularly so in a ﬁeld such
as spatial planning, where EU institutions have no competence and where European integration and Europeanisation processes are therefore by nature ‘softer’ than in areas where the EU
has regulatory or prescriptive powers. The EU’s regulatory framework has been described as a
‘patchwork’ that reﬂects no one preferred model but the result of compromise between the
member states (Héritier 1996), which is why tracing the inﬂuence of particular member states
or actors on EU policy initiatives can be difﬁcult. For a policy area such as spatial planning,
which is characterised by intergovernmental cooperation processes and steering through EU
regional policy intervention (rather than a strong regulatory framework), understanding complex
processes of Europeanisation requires a consideration of horizontal and bottom-up policy
diffusion and policy transfer alongside the top-down ‘ﬁltering’ of EU ideas. ‘Through the processes of organizational learning and policy transfer these changes may affect strategies and
preferences of other domestic actors, potentially leading to corresponding institutional
adaptation’ (Dühr and Nadin 2007: 377).
New institutionalism
The ‘new institutionalist’ approaches have their roots in political science and emerged in the
1980s and 1990s in response to the recognition that ‘institutions’ are key to the outcome of
decision-making processes. Institutions are deﬁned broadly as ‘formal and informal procedures,
routines, norms and conventions embedded in the political structure of the polity or political
economy’ (Hall and Taylor 1996: 6). The term ‘institution’ in the context of theoretical debates
on new institutionalism theory is thus different (and broader) than what is described by the
notion of EU institutions as actors, such as the European Parliament or the European
Commission. New institutionalism recognises that institutions operate in an environment
consisting of other institutions, and are inﬂuenced by this broader institutional environment.
106
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 136

The institutionalist literature is increasingly diverse, and as well as other applications, much
attention is given to analysing EU governance and integration processes. Indeed, as Rosamond
(2000: 114) has argued, ‘the EU, with its mixture of formal and informal institutions is often
seen as an ideal testing ground for the various forms of institutional analysis’. There are different
sub-ﬁelds of new institutionalism that have developed from within separate disciplines and that
(beyond a core shared interest in institutions) pursue different lines of enquiry. Hall and Taylor
(1996) have identiﬁed three different analytical approaches that have developed more or less
independently of each other under the label of ‘new institutionalism’. These schools of thought
are rational choice, historical, and sociological institutionalism. They have been applied by
different scholars to analyse European integration and the functioning of the EU.
The key concern for rational choice institutionalism is the extent to which and the ways in
which institutions shape, direct and constrain the actions of political actors, who – it is assumed
– will seek to pursue their self-interests in a rational way. Nugent (2006: 573) has argued that:
a common theme of rational choice analysis is that governments actively participate in the
EU and cede powers to it because they derive a variety of beneﬁts from so doing, prominent
amongst which are reducing transaction costs (i.e. the risks and penalties that arise when
actors engage in negotiation with one another) through enhanced policy development, policy
effectiveness, and policy compliance.
Thus institutions in the EU are created because they allow ‘governments to become intimately
acquainted with the goals, aversions, tastes and domestic constraints of each other’ (Sandholtz
1996: 406). However, policy actors are faced with different restrictions under different decisionmaking procedures, and depending on what actor relations exist, outcomes may not always be
beneﬁcial for all actors involved. The rational choices of political actors are thus constrained
(‘bounded rationality’) by the rules (institutions) that govern the behaviour between individuals.
Political decision-making in the Council of Ministers, for instance, has been conceived as a 
series of ‘collective action dilemmas’, which can be deﬁned as:
instances when individuals acting to maximize the attainment of their own preferences are
likely to produce an outcome that is collectively suboptimal (in the sense that another
outcome could be found that would make at least one of the actors better off without
making any of the others worse off).
(Hall and Taylor 1996: 12)
In 1988, Fritz Scharpf had already identiﬁed ‘joint-decision traps’ in the EU and other federal
systems as situations in which decisions of interdependent governments are taken at the lowest
common denominator because other governments may otherwise veto them.
The rational-choice literature has been criticised for largely ignoring the question of how
institutions evolve and shape political outcomes over time (see Pollack 2004). This is an
important question for any policy or political development, but especially for the inherently
dynamic process of EU integration. In response to the limitations of the rational-choice
approach, historical institutionalism states that ‘history matters’, and that paths chosen early
on in the existence of an institution tend to be followed throughout the institution’s development. Institutions, as a consequence, have the capacity to also shape the goals and preferences
of actors, to structure political situations, and to a certain extent determine possible political
outcomes (Rosamond 2000). Institutions will have an inherent agenda based on the pattern 
of development, both in informal (the way things are generally done) and in formal terms 
(laws, rules and institutional interaction). A key concept in historical institutionalism is ‘path

Theoretical approaches to EU governance

## S. 137

dependency’, which implies that the historical track of a given institution or polity will result
in almost inevitable occurrences. Despite the importance of institutional paths for determining
future action, however, they may still change, for instance through ‘critical junctures’ such as
economic crises or military conﬂict. Historical institutionalism has been used, for example, to
show how decisions of member state governments have strengthened the positions of supranational institutions and thus effectively constrained their own future actions (Nugent 2006):
In the context of European integration, historical institutionalists tend to argue that the
construction of supranational institutions was rooted in a very particular historical context
(post-war reconstruction and looming bipolarity in the case of the ECSC). So, the
institutional architects of the Community made their decisions to formalise integration on
the basis of particular motivations and preferences. But, the very act of creating particular
sorts of institutions, with identiﬁable competencies and powers, unleashed logics that could
not necessarily be predicted at the time. The path of subsequent European integration is
determined, therefore, by these context-bound decisions.
(Rosamond 2000: 117)
The formal institutions and less formal patterns of interactions that have emerged since imply
that:
subsequent actors have to operate within these self-reproducing institutional scripts. So, the
single market programme that emerged in the EC in the mid-1980s cannot simply be put
down to a neoliberal turn in economic thinking across Western Europe. . . . its precise
manifestation can only be understood within the already existing EC institutional context.
(Rosamond 2000: 117)
Over recent years, another school of thought, sociological institutionalism, has emerged. This
focuses on how institutional forms and practices can be culturally explained. According to Hall
and Taylor (1996), the behaviour of actors within an institution is guided by a ‘logic of
appropriateness’, which refers to the spoken and unspoken rules, cultural beliefs and norms of
the institution. Sociological institutionalism places considerable emphasis on the cognitive
dimension of decision-making: ‘Compliance occurs in many circumstances because other types
of behavior are inconceivable; routines are followed because they are taken for granted as “the
way we do these things”’ (Scott 2001: 57). Individuals make certain choices or perform certain
actions not because they fear punishment or feel some sort of social obligation but because they
can conceive of no alternative within this institutional framework. ‘Institutions (which are
broadly deﬁned) become the mechanisms through which the world is rendered meaningful to
social actors’ (Rosamond 2000: 119). Sociological institutionalism, as applied to the EU, may
at present still be the most underused of the three branches, and to date it has been applied
mainly to examine the attitudes, motivations and behaviour of people working for and in the
EU institutions. For instance, research on Commission ofﬁcials has shown how they identify
much more with Europe and with a supranational perspective of how the EU should function
than ‘ordinary citizens’ (Nugent 2006: 574). The afﬁnity with social constructivism is evident:
the idea that the reality is socially constructed rather than objectively given directs the
researcher towards the subjective aspects of social life: how actors construe the world in
which they operate and what the implications of those construals might be in terms of
choices and constraints on action.
(Rosamond 2000: 119)
108
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 138

New institutionalism, and in particular sociological institutionalism with its emphasis on the
cultural context and social learning processes, is a promising approach for the ﬁeld of European
spatial planning. There are several examples of the application of new institutionalist approaches
to analyse trends in spatial planning across Europe (see Healey 1999; Healey et al. 1997) and
to the intergovernmental European spatial planning processes (see Faludi 2002b for an
overview). For example, Faludi et al. (2000) have argued that the ESDP was prepared in an
‘institutional vacuum’, yet that the process of discussing the spatial strategy over many years
has prompted the institutionalisation ‘of evolving practices until, by being used repeatedly, they
shape the thoughts and actions of the actors involved’ (Faludi 2002b: 903).
In addition to the three institutionalist approaches presented in this chapter, more recently
‘discursive institutionalism’ has been proposed as an approach that ‘lends insight into the role
of ideas and discourse in politics while providing a more dynamic approach to institutional
change than the older three new institutionalisms’ (Schmidt 2008: 303). Discourse theory and
discourse analysis are established tools of spatial planners, with discourse being deﬁned as the
interactive process of conveying ideas and a policy discourse functioning as a frame of reference
that is supported by distinctive concepts, metaphors and terminology (Fischer and Forester 1993;
Hajer 1995). In the new discursive institutionalism, the power of discourses is connected to 
the ‘logic of communication’ of institutions. Two forms of discourse are differentiated: the
coordinative discourse among policy actors; and the communicative discourse between political
actors and the public. These actors operate (and communicate) within a given ‘meaning context’
that can help to explain how institutions are created, change or persist (Schmidt 2008). In
explaining the combination of external (formal) and internal (context- and culture-speciﬁc)
institutions, the discursive institutionalism thus shows important parallels with the sociological
school.
Policy discourses may draw on speciﬁc intellectual roots, but they may also evolve through
the absorption and transfer of fashionable vocabularies from particular favoured practice
communities. They may become so embedded and taken for granted that they are not
consciously understood as such: these are the hegemonic discourses that drive practices over long
periods. For the ESDP process, Benz (2002) has, for instance, pointed out that mutual learning
has led to the emergence of a common discourse on ESDP themes, such as polycentricity or
urban-rural relationships. This has succeeded in reaching actor groups beyond the policy
network that was involved in preparing the document. This ‘ESDP discourse’ has been variably
conceptualised as a broad discourse on European spatial development policy in general (Faludi
2002b) or as a number of partly overlapping discourses on issues addressed in the ESDP, such
as polycentricity or accessibility (Richardson and Jensen 2000). There has also been some debate
about how discourses of European spatial planning, such as those promoted by the ESDP, have
inﬂuenced the frameworks and practices of planning in European member states (Böhme 2003;
Jensen 2002; Jensen and Richardson 2004).
Policy networks
The policy networks approach can be understood as an application of new institutionalism in
its broadest sense. Generally speaking, policy networks can be deﬁned as ‘arenas in which
decision-makers and interests come together to mediate differences and search for solutions’
(Nugent, 2006: 574). However, the term ‘policy network’ has been used variably in the
literature, and it has been pointed out that ‘neither is there a common understanding of what
policy networks actually are, nor has it been agreed whether policy networks constitute a mere
metaphor, a method, an analytical tool or a proper theory’ (Börzel 1998: 253). Despite the
fuzziness of the term and the lack of an ‘agreed “theory” of policy networks that would lead

## S. 139

us to predictive claims about European integration or EU policy-making’ (Peterson 2004: 118),
the policy network approach has proven useful to conceptualise the way in which actor
coalitions are structured in different EU policy sectors and how they inﬂuence policy outcomes,
including in the ﬁeld of European spatial planning.
Several scholars have referred to the actors involved in the preparation of the European
Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) (CSD 1999) as a policy network. Faludi (1997) 
has labelled those involved in the preparation of the ESDP as a ‘roving band of planners’, 
who shaped the document according to their respective planning traditions (Faludi 2004a). 
In reference to European spatial planning policy networks, Williams (2000: 363) has pointed
out that ‘such groups often develop their own social bonds and subculture, often outside structures of formal political accountability, adopting a managerial style of working and achieving
legitimation by results’. Böhme (2002) has explored the relation of multi-level governance and
policy networks to European spatial planning, and analysed the links between policy networks
at European level and national levels in the Nordic countries (Böhme 2003).
Due to the informal nature of much EU policy-making, the multiplicity of interests at EU
level, the highly technical nature of much EU policy content, the powerful policy positions of
senior ofﬁcials (especially in the European Commission), and the reliance of ofﬁcials on other
actors and outside interests for information and advice about policy content and policy
implementation, the EU is seen by many as a prime seedbed for policy networks. These can be
conceptualised according to three key variables: how stable (or unstable) the network
membership is; how permeable (or impermeable) the network is; and how strong (or weak) its
resource dependencies are (Peterson 1995 in Nugent 2006). From these variables, policy
networks can be thought of as a continuum, with integrated policy communities in wellestablished areas of EU policy (for example, agriculture) at one end of the spectrum and loosely
integrated issue networks in areas where the debate is still emerging at the other end (Faludi
2002b).
In the ﬁeld of European spatial planning, with no direct competences, policy networks are
more ﬂuid than in other, more established EU policy sectors. In intergovernmental working, the
Commission is generally the only actor to identify itself wholly with Europe (Faludi 2002b),
whereas all other actors pursue divergent but interdependent interests through voluntary
cooperation. As a characteristic of policy networks in the ﬁeld of spatial planning, Faludi (2002b:
903) mentions the ‘strategy of the ﬁrst move’ as an attempt by member states to set the agenda
and shape the discussions (for instance, France taking the initiative in the ESDP process). This
strategy requires a high degree of national expertise but also the support of the Commission as
a ‘gatekeeper’ (Faludi 2002b: 903) and process manager. Faludi (2002b: 904) has argued that
‘high-regulation countries are like innovative policy entrepreneurs. If a proposal gains approval,
the ﬁrst mover is at an advantage’.
Policy networks are characterised by processes of mutual learning, which – especially in
committees that live longer – may lead to the development of epistemic communities (Héritier
et al. 1996). For the ﬁeld of European spatial planning, in particular, this means that policies
become effective not by imposition but by means of negotiation, or by ‘attraction’, that is,
dissemination of good practices. For policy networks to function successfully, shared concepts
are important in intergovernmental processes where the European Commission is largely
restricted to agenda-setting and policy formulation and cannot inﬂuence policy outcomes
through regulatory means. Guiding principles and convincing policy concepts are therefore
needed that are ‘capable of papering over the cracks between the various views’ (Faludi 2002b:
904). In the ESDP process, for instance, ‘bridging concepts’ such as polycentricity have played
an important role ‘by supplying persuasive concepts to gain the ear of policy-makers’ (Faludi
2002b: 904).
110
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 140

Epistemic communities
Epistemic communities and policy networks are closely related. However, the idea of epistemic
communities as proposed by Haas (1992) adds the aspect of knowledge to the network concept,
recognising that in many areas policy-makers require specialist advice in order to make decisions.
Decision-makers are increasingly uncertain about their policy environments, due to the growing
complexity of policy issues, which prompts them to seek advice from external experts, or an
‘epistemic community’. An epistemic community is deﬁned as ‘a network of professionals with
recognized expertise and competence in a particular domain and an authoritative claim to policyrelevant knowledge within that domain’ (Haas 1992: 3).1
The epistemic communities approach is based on the assumption that EU policy processes
are shaped by political and cultural structures and the collective interpretation and deﬁnition
of values and the meaning of action (March and Olsen 1984). The members of an epistemic
community may come from a wide variety of disciplines and backgrounds, but they hold a
common set of causal beliefs and share notions of validity based on internally deﬁned criteria
for evaluation, common policy projects and shared normative commitments. Epistemic communities are able to inﬂuence policy-making processes by framing the range of political controversy
surrounding an issue, deﬁning interests and setting standards. They can play both indirect and
direct roles in international policy coordination by diffusing ideas and inﬂuencing the positions
adopted by a wide range of actors, including domestic and international agencies, government
ofﬁcials and decision-makers, legislative and corporate bodies, and the public.
Crucial for the continuing existence and the authority of an epistemic community is the degree
of consensus among community members. The collective involvement in policy processes
prompts learning processes, not just within the epistemic community but, more importantly,
within the wider policy community where new meanings and frames of reference are being
created:
Various political groups and institutions may learn different lessons or interpret reality
differently. It is therefore crucial to know who learns what; whose learning gets translated
into policy and why; whose learning gets a chance to affect other countries; and how
political processes determine whose interpretations of reality are more viable in a particular
historical context. At the international level, the capacity of interacting institutions in
different countries to learn, share norms and practices, and effectively modify their
behaviour depends on the diffusion of cause-and-effect understanding from country to
country. The importance of these understandings lies not merely in being true, but in being
shared.
(Adler and Haas 1992: 386)
Peterson (2004: 121) has argued that the epistemic communities approach can help to explain
why EU policy outcomes in a particular sector:
reﬂect purely technocratic reality or, alternatively, the overtly political agenda of key actors
. . . For example, the [EU’s Research] Framework programme for funding collaborative
research has quietly expanded to become the third largest item of expenditure in the
Community’s budget, not least because much decision-making about precisely who gets
what from the programme has been delegated to epistemic communities of researchers and
scientists . . . Alternatively, highly politicized environmental policy debates over auto
emissions, packaging waste or biotechnology can be viewed as battles between competing
advocacy coalitions – broadly advocating environmental protection versus industrial
interests – for inﬂuence within EU environmental policy networks.

## S. 141

Both the policy network and epistemic communities approaches provide useful ideas for the
analysis of intergovernmental workings in the ﬁeld of European spatial planning, as they discuss
issues of complexity and uncertainty in decision-making and the importance of mutual learning.
Epistemic communities typically emerge from the demands for information and knowledge in
situations of international policy-making that are characterised by uncertainty. This is the case
across all areas of EU policy-making, as the widespread existence of expert committees as a
typical feature of the EU’s semi-autonomous decision making processes shows (Pollack 2001),
although again the spectrum ranges from loose networks to well-established communities. For
example, the concept of epistemic communities has been extensively applied to the analysis of
developments in EU environmental policy-making (Adler and Haas 1992; Buunk 2003).
However, the ‘politics of expertise’ may be even more important in situations where loose
networks and a high level of uncertainty exist (Richardson 2005a), such as the ‘anarchic ﬁeld’
(Faludi 2000a: 249) of European spatial planning. The discussions surrounding the ESDP in
the 1990s, for instance, demonstrated considerable ‘uncertainty regarding content as well as on
the positions of the various actors’ (Faludi 2000a: 249). Reﬂecting the limited competences for
spatial planning at EU level, and the small number of spatial planners within the European
Commission, external experts played a key role in shaping the discussions. Faludi (2000a: 249)
has argued that this process of mutual learning has led to the emergence of an ‘“epistemic
community”, admittedly with its roots in Northwest Europe’.
Conclusion
There are a growing number of middle-range theoretical approaches on European integration,
many of which have been used to investigate governance and policy processes in the ﬁeld 
of European spatial planning. Given this variety of approaches, this chapter therefore had to
be very selective and concentrate on those theoretical approaches that may offer explanations
for developments and initiatives in the ﬁeld of spatial planning. Conceptualising European
integration is under much development, and approaches are constantly developing in response
to criticisms and the ongoing geographical and political expansion of the EU. It is therefore now
widely acknowledged that no single body of theory can capture all aspects of integration (Nugent
2006) and that ‘European integration as a coherent ﬁeld of study is disappearing’ (Jachtenfuchs
2001: 255). Any practitioner and researcher interested in understanding the inﬂuence of the EU
on domestic change and the processes of EU policy-making will therefore have to choose from
different theoretical approaches that may offer an explanation for one aspect of the ‘mosaic of
integration theory’ (Diez and Wiener 2004).
The theoretical approaches presented in this chapter represent an institutionalist and
governance perspective on processes of European integration, which may be useful in understanding spatial planning. Jachtenfuchs and Kohler-Koch (2004: 100), in their argument for 
a governance perspective as an integrated approach, contend that the ‘way policy is made
changes the institutional framework in a broader sense. The institutional structure of the EU in
this view does not only comprise the text of the basic treaties but also informal components 
of institutions’. Rules and behaviour are being deﬁned and have changed over the years of EU
integration, and these ‘rules change normative and cognitive expectations as well as the
behaviour of actors in the EU system’ (Jachtenfuchs and Kohler-Koch 2004: 100). Moreover,
‘changes in governance also change the course of integration’ (Jachtenfuchs and Kohler-Koch
2004: 100) whereby the behaviour of members (in terms of, for instance, non-compliance 
with EU regulations) affects the processes of policy-making and implementation and the 
future choice of policy instruments. The processes of ‘naming and shaming’ of non-conformers,
and the increasing emphasis on good practice examples, are an important part of policy-making
in the EU’s ‘communicative universe’ (von Bogdandy 2003).
112
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 142

It is widely acknowledged that there is considerable variation in how EU law and policy is
being implemented in different EU member states:
Member states have responded quite differently to identical EU input even under similar
external and internal context conditions. The two most obvious reasons are that national
and sub-national actors have considerable latitude when implementing an EU policy and
that costs of adaptation differ. It makes a difference whether alterations are restricted to
modiﬁcations within a given pattern of sector regulation or whether they strike at the core
of national administrative traditions.
(Jachtenfuchs and Kohler-Koch 2004: 110)
Following the recent EU enlargements, the heterogeneity in the EU has increased considerably
– in terms of economic development, administrative structures and member state preferences –
and this has added another dimension of complexity to policy-making and increased the
diversity in the implementation of EU legislation and policy. Jachtenfuchs and Kohler-Koch
(2004) expect that in order to cope with this growing diversity, the trend towards more ﬂexible
and interactive forms of European governance (through exchanges of good practice and mutual
learning etc.) will likely continue. Increasingly, theoretical approaches therefore also emphasise
the importance of EU institutions as the frame of reference. The development of a common
discourse can, for example, be observed in relation to the Lisbon-Gothenburg Strategy with its
emphasis on fostering innovation and a knowledge-based economy. These overarching policy
goals, agreed by the Council in 2000 and 2001 (European Council 2000a, 2001), have since
been taken up in all EU programmes including EU Cohesion Policy, and currently guide
territorial cooperation across Europe (see Chapters 16 and 18). In summary, the ‘cognitive turn’
in integration theory presents an ever-growing number of useful approaches to analysing the
complex ﬁeld of European spatial planning.
Further reading
There is a burgeoning literature on middle-range theoretical approaches to European integration.
Reviews include Rosamond (2000) Theories of European Integration and Wiener and Diez
(2004) European Integration Theory. Nugent’s comprehensive book on The Government and
Politics of the European Union (2006) has a chapter on ‘Conceptualising and theorising’. For
an overview of how middle-range theories of European integration have been applied to the ﬁeld
of spatial planning see Faludi (2002b) and Pallagst (2006a). A special issue of the journal
Planning Practice and Research edited by Dühr, Stead and Zonneveld (Vol. 22, No. 3 of 2007),
brings together a range of papers on the topic of ‘Europeanisation of spatial planning’.

## S. 143

The institutions of the 
European Union
In all member states the governments are and remain the core actors in EC/EU affairs. Through their
representation in the Council of Ministers and the participation of the heads of state and government
in the meetings of the European Council they clearly dominate the decision-making process of the
European Union.
(Wessels and Rometsch 1996: 330)
Europe continues to progress and one sees, in the Brussels institutions, the emergence of working
styles which cannot be related to any one national dimension . . . Forty years of personnel management within a common institution has not disconnected the European bloc from the national
contingencies which constitute it. Attempts to create a common culture led, in the 1960s, to the
adoption of the neologism ‘Eurocrat’. At ﬁrst intended to designate the ‘new race of men’ which
Jean Monnet saw at work in the European institutions, it came to be regarded as a pejorative term.
Yet, do not Eurocrats exist in a positive dimension?
(Bellier 2000: 149)
Introduction
The EU as a political system is a jurisdiction in its own right, with speciﬁc institutions, the power
to enact legislation binding on all member states and its own symbols (Box 9.1). The founding
Treaties deﬁned ﬁve European institutions (in the strictest sense), which are described in this
chapter: the Council of the European Union; the European Parliament; the European
Commission (these ﬁrst three forming the ‘decision-making’ triangle); the Court of Justice of
the European Communities; and the European Court of Auditors. A number of other EU bodies
perform speciﬁc roles and are brieﬂy introduced in the second part of the chapter: advisory
bodies (the European Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions);
ﬁnancial institutions (the European Investment Bank, the European Investment Fund and the
European Central Bank); the European Ombudsman; various support services; and a range of
specialised European agencies.
Particular emphasis is put on the ways in which the working of these European institutions
is relevant to the policy ﬁelds explored in the book (in particular those policy areas discussed
in Part V). This chapter should therefore be read in conjunction with Chapter 11, which presents
the policy-making processes underpinning EU activities in various fields. An important 
caveat should be made at the outset: the EU is a constantly evolving political system, and
successive treaty revisions brought about minor or major changes to the composition, powers,
functioning and mutual relationship of EU institutions. The information presented in this chapter
includes the major institutional changes brought about by the Treaty of Lisbon, which was
Chapter 9
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 144

signed in 2007 and entered into force on 1 December 2009 (following ratiﬁcation by all EU
member states). For readers who would like a completely up-to-date overview, the website of
the European Union is the most reliable source of information on the composition, structure,
powers and decision-making processes of EU institutions.
The five institutions of the European Union
As explained in Chapter 7, the EU is a hybrid political system in which intergovernmental and
supranational features coexist. This is reﬂected in its institutional structure. The Council of the
European Union represents the intergovernmental side of the EU, while the Commission and
the European Parliament have supranational characteristics. The EU has 23 ofﬁcial languages,
as explained in Chapter 3 but, in practice, several institutions have adopted ‘limited multilingualism’ in their internal operations, using two or three communication and drafting
languages (usually English, French and German). However, all ofﬁcial and publicly circulated
documents produced by EU institutions are translated into all ofﬁcial languages. The Directorate-
General for Interpretation of the European Commission provides interpreters for more than
11,000 Commission meetings every year and translated 1,324,231 pages in 2005. Contrary to
widespread popular belief and frequently inaccurate media reports, the costs of administering
the EU (i.e. of running the European Commission, the Council of the European Union and the
European Parliament and operating in all the EU languages) takes less than 6 per cent of the
total EU budget, i.e. EUR 6.9 billion in 2007.
The Council of the European Union (or Council of Ministers)
The Council of the European Union (or Council of Ministers) represents the governments of 
the member states of the EU. It is not to be confused with the Council of Europe, which is an
intergovernmental body but not an EU institution (see Chapter 10), or the European Council
(explained below). It is the primary decision-making body of the EU, although it shares this role
with the European Parliament in certain areas (see Chapter 11). The Council is a single body,
but it meets in nine different conﬁgurations depending on the topic under discussion. Each
conﬁguration brings together the ministers responsible for a particular policy area from the 27
member states and the European Commissioner(s) responsible for the area(s) concerned. The
ministers are accountable to their national parliaments. In the 1990s there were 22 Council
conﬁgurations, which were reduced to nine in June 2002, namely: General Affairs and External
Relations; Economic and Financial Affairs; Cooperation in the ﬁelds of Justice and Home
Affairs; Employment; Social Policy, Health and Consumer Affairs; Competitiveness; Transport,
Telecommunications and Energy; Agriculture and Fisheries; Environment; Education, Youth and
Culture. The frequency of Council meetings varies according to the urgency of the subject matter
dealt with and the European interest and activity in each ﬁeld. The General Affairs and External
Relations, the Economic and Financial Affairs and the Agriculture Councils meet once a month,
while the other Councils meet four to six times a year (usually in Brussels, sometimes in
Luxembourg). There is no Council of Spatial Planning Ministers, because the EU does not have
competence in this ﬁeld (see Chapters 2 and 11). But a competence does not guarantee that a
Council will be established. For example, there is no Council of Regional Policy Ministers where
the EU has a strong competence. This policy area is considered by General Affairs and External
Relations. The explanation for this is given by Faludi and Waterhout (2002) and is to do with
stronger ministries wanting to retain control over spending. However, since 1989, ministers
responsible for spatial planning and regional policy have met periodically in so-called ‘informal

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 146

ministerial meetings’ to discuss issues of common concern and cooperation. These meetings have
played a crucial role in developing a spatial planning agenda for the EU, in particular in the
preparation of the European Spatial Development Perspective (see Chapter 14).
The Council has a dual role – of legislative decision-making and of political coordination.
The Council ﬁrst passes EU legislation, usually jointly with the European Parliament. Strictly
speaking the Council has no formal power to initiate new proposals for legislation – this is the
Commission’s role – but its political weight has had an increasing inﬂuence on the EU policy
agenda since the 1980s. In particular, the Council can ask the Commission to produce studies
and proposals considered desirable for the attainment of common European objectives. The
Council takes decisions on new EU legislation by a vote of ministers from the member states,
using a simple majority (for procedural matters), a qualiﬁed majority or unanimity, depending
on the subject dealt with. The Treaties deﬁne which voting procedure should be used in which
case and set the number of votes that each member state has in the Council (see Chapter 11).
On a broad range of policy issues, the Council now adopts legislation jointly with the European
Parliament under the so-called ‘co-decision procedure’ (Chapter 11). The Council and the
European Parliament are jointly responsible for adopting the EU budget.
Besides its legislative function, the Council coordinates the economic policies of the member
states as well their activities in the area of police and judicial cooperation in criminal matters.
It deﬁnes and implements the EU’s common foreign and security policy. Finally, the Council can,
on behalf of the EU, conclude international agreements between the EU and one or more states
or international organisations.
The Secretariat General, composed of 2,400 staff based in Brussels, provides administrative
support to the Council. Additionally, each member state of the EU has a national delegation 
in Brussels of about 30–40 diplomats and civil servants led by a Permanent Representative. 
The Committee of Permanent Representatives (COREPER) meets every week to prepare and
coordinate the work of the Council of Ministers. The COREPER acts as a key link between 
the EU institutions in Brussels and the 27 national governments, and clears many issues for
discussion so that only the most politically sensitive matters remain for the ministers to discuss
in Council meetings (Nugent 2006: 208) (although all new EU laws have to be formally adopted
at ministerial level). In its work the COREPER draws from a large number of specialised,
thematic committees and working groups (over 150 at any one time) consisting of experts, 
civil servants and delegates from the member states as well as representatives of sectoral
interests or European umbrella groups. The informal processes and relationships between
senior diplomats and civil servants within the various committees and working parties are crucial
for the work of the whole Council (Nugent 2006: 217). These committees work on the
technical aspects of proposed EU legislation and policies but are ‘by no means conﬁned to trivial
or purely technical matters’ (Williams 2000: 363). Committees and groups ‘often develop their
own social bonds and subculture, often outside structures of formal political accountability,
adopting a managerial style of working and achieving legitimation by results’ (Williams 2000:
363) in terms of inﬂuence on the drafting and wording of EU legislation.
The Council is presided over by each member state in turn for a period of six months (from
January to June, and from July to December) according to a pre-established rota. The presidency
plays an important role for setting the agenda and inﬂuencing the political debates at EU level.
The President of the Council, i.e. the relevant national minister depending on the form of
Council, chairs meetings and acts as a ‘mediator’ who negotiates compromises to solve
difﬁculties between member states. Continuity in EU policy development is ensured through
coordination with previous and forthcoming presidencies. Certain presidencies have championed
particular policy agendas or issues and have inﬂuenced EU policy developments markedly:
