The institutions of the EU
117
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 147

member states with strong ‘green’ credentials and agenda, for example, were instrumental in
pushing for stricter EU environmental legislation in various ﬁelds (see Chapter 21).
In addition to the regular meetings of the Council of Ministers in its various conﬁgurations, the
Heads of State or Government of the member states of the EU and the President of the Commission
meet twice a year in a so-called European Council summit meeting. Such meetings set the ‘overall
parameters of the EU system’ (Nugent 2006: 223) by deﬁning the broad policy directions and
impetus for the EU. No provision was made in the founding Treaties for such summit meetings of
heads of state and government (Nugent 2006: 219), yet these meetings were institutionalised in
1974 as ‘European Councils’ that would ‘bring the Heads of Government together on a relatively
informal basis to exchange ideas, further mutual understanding at the highest political level, give
direction to policy development, and perhaps sometimes to break deadlocks and clear logjams’
(Nugent 2006: 219). The European Council is responsible for political decisions on constitutional
and institutional matters, on EU enlargement and external relations. This is why the European
Council summit meetings usually have a high proﬁle in the European media:
This can give the impression that the EU mainly operates through periodic ‘summitry’, like
other international organisations. However, the real essence of EU politics lies in the
constant interactions within and between the EU institutions in Brussels, between national
governments and Brussels, within the various departments in national governments, in
bilateral meetings between governments, and between private interests and governmental
ofﬁcials in Brussels and at the national level. Hence unlike other international organizations,
EU business is conducted in multiple settings on virtually every day of the year.
(Hix 2005: 4)
One of the major innovations brought about by the Treaty of Lisbon is the creation of a new
function of President of the European Council elected for two and a half years, whose
responsibilities are to prepare and chair the summit meetings of the European Council and
represent EU leaders on the global stage. The ﬁrst President of the European Council, Herman
Van Rompuy, took ofﬁce on 1 December 2009.
The European Parliament
The European Parliament (EP) was initially conceived in the founding Treaties as a European
assembly made of elected members of the national parliaments of the member states. It only
became a directly elected chamber in 1979. Since then members of the European Parliament
(MEPs) have been directly elected by the citizens of the EU by universal suffrage every ﬁve years
(the most recent European election was in June 2009). The 2009 EP elections were the largest
simultaneous transnational elections ever held anywhere in the world, since nearly 380 million
citizens were eligible to vote. All EU citizens can vote and stand for a European election wherever
they live in the EU. The EP represents the will of European citizens, as opposed to that of their
national governments, which are represented in the Council. The EP is thus a unique body – ‘the
only real multinational legislative assembly in the world’ (Moussis 2006). The EP’s size grew
with successive enlargements, from 410 members in 1979 to 785 members in 2008 (Table 9.1).
Seats are allocated according to a ‘degressive proportionality’ principle: this means that the bigger
member states have fewer seats than they would receive if the total number of seats was divided
in proportion to population. This allows for the better representation of smaller and less densely
populated states: while Luxembourg had around 67,000 inhabitants per MEP in 2008, Germany
had 830,000 inhabitants per MEP, while a medium-sized country such as Portugal had 412,000.
For the 2009–14 term, the maximum number of MEPs was set at 750, the maximum number
of seats attributed to a member state was set at 96 and the minimum number of seats allocated
118
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 148

to any member state was set at six (which guarantees each member state enough seats to
represent all major strands in the national political landscape) (see Box 9.1).
The EP is organised along party political lines in seven Europe-wide political groups. The
important role of these European political groups is recognised in the EU Treaty itself (Art. 10
TEU): ‘political parties at European level contribute to forming European political awareness
and to expressing the will of citizens of the Union’ (European Communities 2008). These
political groups are, in order of importance (number of MEPs) for the 2009–14 term: the 
Group of the European People’s Party (Christian Democrats); the Group of the Progressive
Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament; Group of the Alliance of
Liberals and Democrats for Europe; Group of the Greens/European Free Alliance; the European
Conservatives and Reformists Group; the Confederal Group of the European United Left –
Nordic Green Left; the Europe of Freedom and Democracy Group; and non-attached members
(known as ‘non-inscrits’).

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 149

Turnout to European election reﬂects the general lack of knowledge and interest of European
citizens in the work of the EP. European elections have often been fought over national 
political issues and failed to attract high levels of interests from EU citizens: at the 2004
European elections the participation ﬁgure was 45.5 per cent; in the 2009 EP elections, the 
voter turnout for the EU-27 was on average 43 per cent. 
The Treaty of Rome had given the EP only a consultative role. The powers of the EP were
substantially expanded with the treaty revisions of 1987, 1992 and 1997, and now include
legislative, budgetary and supervisory powers. The Treaty of Amsterdam (1997), in particular,
put the EP and the Council on an equal footing as joint legislative bodies. Since the 1986 Single
European Act, external and trade agreements negotiated by the Council and the Commission
(e.g. association agreements and agreements governing accession of new countries to the
European Union) are subject to the Parliament’s approval through the ‘assent’ procedure,
without which the Council cannot adopt the agreement.
The European Parliament has three locations: Strasbourg (France), where most plenary
sessions take place once a month; Brussels (Belgium), where committee meetings take place over
two weeks every month; and Luxembourg, home of the General Secretariat, the administrative
service of the EP. In 2007 the Parliament employed 5,900 people (among which 1,300 were in
linguistic services covering 23 working languages). The legislative work of the EP is prepared
by 20 thematic parliamentary committees (similar to those of national parliaments), which
comprise between 28 and 86 MEPs. It is in committee meetings that most of the discussions on
legislative proposals take place: committees play a key role in scrutinising the legislative
proposals made by the Commission and in drafting resolutions and opinions that are then
considered in the plenary parliamentary assembly. The committees that are particularly relevant
for the subject matter of European spatial planning and territorial development policies are those
on: Environment, Public Health and Food Safety; Industry, Research and Energy; Transport and
Tourism; Regional Development; and Agriculture and Rural Development.
The EP does not have the power to propose new legislation. It can only request the
Commission to submit proposals ‘on matters on which it considers that a Union act is required
for the purpose of implementing the Treaties’ (Article 225 of the TEC, European Communities
2008). In many policy areas the EP shares the decision-making power over new EU legislation
with the Council of Ministers, under a procedure known as ‘co-decision’ (see Chapter 11). This
means that new legislation approved by the Council cannot go ahead if it is not voted for by
the EP. A number of mechanisms exist to allow consensus to be reached between the two institutions: successive readings, conciliation committee meetings and the possibility of amendments.
Although it is difﬁcult to assess the inﬂuence of the EP on EU legislation, there is evidence that
‘only a handful of legislative proposals are actually blocked by the European Parliament – under
co-decision only nine had been blocked up to the end of 2005’ and that ‘many proposals are
signiﬁcantly altered, including on matters of political substance’ (Nugent 2006: 246) as a result
of amendments made by the EP. The Treaty of Lisbon has further strengthened the legislative
role of the EP by extending the co-decision procedure (renamed ‘ordinary legislative procedure’)
to new ﬁelds. Under two other procedures – the ‘consultation’ and ‘cooperation procedures’,
which are used in a smaller number of policy areas (see Chapter 11), the Council can overturn
the EP’s amendments.
One of the main powers of the EP is that it has to approve the EU budget as a whole every
year. The budget is prepared by the Commission and approved by the Council, but the EP can
propose modiﬁcations and amendments. On spending related to agriculture and international
agreements, the Council has the last word. Exceptionally the EP can reject the budget at a twothirds majority and ask for the Commission and Council to produce a new draft – a situation
120
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 150

that occurred in January 2006 during the preparation of the budget for 2007–13. The EP also
has to ‘discharge’ the implementation of previous budgets, and has refused to approve the budget
twice – in 1984 and in 1998.
The ﬁnal role of the EP is the control of the EU executive, i.e. the European Commission.
The EP is consulted on, and approves the choice of the President and members of the
Commission every ﬁve years. MEPs can question the Commission at any time (some 3,000
questions are put every year, most of which are to the Commission and some to the Council).
The Commission presents a report on the activities of the EU to the EP every year. The EP can
pass a motion of censure against the Commission as a whole by a two-thirds majority and force
the College of Commissioners (see below) to resign. To date this power has never been used,
although the EP threatened to do so in March 1999 when various factors had led MEPs to be
increasingly dissatisﬁed with the Santer College of Commissioners, in particular because of
allegations of mismanagement of EU funds, fraud and nepotism. After a critical report by a
committee of independent experts and an (unsuccessful) vote over a motion of censure, the
Santer College of Commissioners resigned, which was widely commented upon as a triumph
for the EP, symbolising its rising political control over the Commission (Nugent 2006: 252). 
This political control was reinforced by the Treaty of Lisbon, according to which the President
of the Commission is now elected by the European Parliament on the basis of a proposal from
the European Council.
In contrast to its inﬂuence over the activities of the European Commission, the EP has much
less political control over the Council of Ministers because the Council represents the voice of
the national governments of the member states and ‘to make [the Council], or any of its
members, directly responsible to the EP would be to introduce a measure of supranationalism
into the EU that has been, and is, unacceptable to most governments’ (Nugent 2006: 255). The
EP therefore does not have too much input into the agenda of the Council. Although the powers
of the EP have been strengthened, they are still limited compared to national parliaments:
it can ask the Commission to propose a new law or policy, it has equal power with the
Council of Ministers to amend legislative proposals and approve the EU budget, it must
approve and can ﬁre the Commission, and it can veto applications from aspirant EU
members. But the Commission still holds the power of initiation, and the Council shares
the ability to approve new laws and the EU budget. In short, Parliament either shares or
cancels the power of other EU institutions, so it is best described as a colegislature.
(McCormick 2004: 151)
As a result, the EP is in a constant process of ‘legislative bargaining’ with the Commission
and the Council of Ministers (Hix 2005: 99–102). The Treaty of Lisbon, ratiﬁed in 2009, gives
more powers to the EP.
The European Commission
The European Commission, based in Brussels, is the ‘civil service’ of the EU, although its powers
extend beyond those of a regular ministerial bureaucracy. The Commission has four roles: ﬁrst,
to draft new legislation in the areas of EU competence deﬁned by the Treaties; second, to manage
EU policies, programmes and activities; third, to be the ‘Guardian of the Treaties’ by ensuring
that adopted EU legislation is applied correctly and evenly in all EU countries (jointly with the
Court of Justice); fourth to represent the EU on the global stage and negotiate agreements
between the EU and other countries.

The institutions of the EU

## S. 151

Until 2004 the Commission was composed of a college of ‘Commissioners’ comprising two
nationals from each of the big member states and one from each small member state. Since the
2004 enlargement the Commission has one Commissioner for each member state (currently 27),
each responsible for a speciﬁc policy ﬁeld. According to the Treaty of Lisbon, after 2014 the
number of Commissioners will be reduced, although the ﬁnal ﬁgure remains to be determined
by a Council decision. Commissioners will then be appointed by rotation.
Commissioners are proposed by national governments and appointed by the Council by a
qualiﬁed majority with the approval of the EP for a period of ﬁve years. They are experienced
politicians in their own country, and pledge to serve the interests of the EU as a whole, not of
their nation of origin. The Commission is answerable to the European Parliament alone. The
Commission is led by a President appointed by the European Council and approved by the EP.
Since the mid-1980s and the high-proﬁle presidencies of Jacques Delors, the President of the
Commission has played an important leadership role for the EU as a whole.
The word ‘Commission’ refers to both to the College of Commissioners and to the institution
(and its administration) as a whole. Each Commissioner heads a Directorate-General (DG),
assisted by a cabinet of half a dozen advisors which act as a think-tank to develop new policy
initiatives and play a key role in policy development. The Directorates-General (DGs), which
can be compared to departments of government, are responsible for the implementation of EU
policies and general administration in speciﬁc areas (Table 9.2). The most relevant DGs for the
subject matter of European spatial policy are DG Regional Policy, DG Environment, DG Energy
and Transport and DG Agriculture and Rural Development – which are referred to in Part V.
The Commission has a total staff of around 34,000 as of 2009 (of which 24,000 are
permanent ofﬁcials), and they come from all the member states of the EU. This is not a large
number when assessed against the scope of the policy ﬁelds that the Commission deals with: it
is as big as the local administration of a medium-sized European city or a single government
ministry in many member states. About half of the staff are administrators involved in the

## S. 152

drafting of policies, 40 per cent are support staff and 10 per cent are translators and interpreters.
Recruitment is done through EU-wide competitive examinations based on quotas of posts per
national origin. Some staff are ‘detached’ by their national administration to work for the
European Commission for a limited period of time. Most Commission staff work in Brussels,
while the rest are based in Luxembourg and in representation ofﬁces in the 27 EU member states.
The size of each DG varies between 300 and 2,800 staff depending on the policy ﬁeld.
The ﬁrst role of the Commission is its right of initiative in the EU legislative process: in other
words, it proposes and drafts the legislation on which the Council of Ministers and the European Parliament debate and decide. In so doing, the Commission relies on an extensive network
of expert committees composed of national ofﬁcials and experts on a whole range of subjects
(the ‘comitology system’) (see Chapter 11 for more details). For example, in recent years the
‘Expert Group on the Urban Environment’ set up by DG REGIO has played a proactive role in
shaping the EU’s urban sustainability agenda by drafting the Thematic Strategy on the Urban
Environment approved in 2006 (see Chapter 21).
The second key function of the Commission is an executive role, i.e. the implementation of
Community policies (such as the Common Agricultural Policy or the Cohesion Policy) and the
management of the EU budget. It has a relatively high degree of independence in performing its
implementation duties and has signiﬁcant powers in the conduct of policies such as competition
policy. It can, for example, deﬁne what constitute breaches of EU competition law and scrutinise
the ‘state aids’ given by member states to industries and regions (see Chapter 17). An important
prerogative of the Commission in its executive function is its ability to make rules (what might
be secondary legislation in some nation-states) to ﬁll in the details of primary EU legislation
approved by the Council (and Parliament) (see Chapter 11).
The third role of ‘Guardian of the Treaties’ means that the Commission is legally obliged to
ensure that the principles of the Treaties are turned into laws and policies, and that EU
regulations and directives are properly implemented in the member states. The Commission 
can take action against member states or individuals, ﬁrms and organisations through the
European Court of Justice (a power used in particular against cartels and illegal price-ﬁxing).
Finally, the European Commission manages international trade relationships. One of the 
major innovations brought about by the Treaty of Lisbon, which entered into force in 2009, 
is the creation of a post of EU high representative for foreign and security policy who is also
Commissioner for external relations and Commission vice-president. This will ensure consistency
in the EU’s dealings with foreign countries and international bodies, assisted by a new European
external action service.
The Court of Justice of the European Communities
The Court of Justice of the European Communities, based in Luxembourg, is the judicial
institution of the EU. It is made up of three courts: the Court of Justice, the Court of First
Instance and the Civil Service Tribunal. The Court of Justice (ECJ) is composed of 27 judges
and eight advocates-general appointed by the Council of Ministers for a renewable term of six
years. They are lawyers with signiﬁcant experience in the highest judicial spheres of their
country. The role of the ECJ is twofold: ﬁrst, it ensures that Community law is interpreted and
implemented in line with the Treaties (i.e. the legality of Community measures). It is the only
body allowed to give an opinion on the correct interpretation of the Treaties and on the legality
of the decisions made by the Council of Ministers and Commission. Second, the Court ensures
that Community law is uniformly interpreted and applied by all member states of the EU. As
such it has authority over national courts and determines violations of Treaties and Community
law by the member states, and can impose ﬁnes or compensation payments on individual

## S. 153

member states. The European Court of Justice is not to be confused with the European Court
of Human Rights (which belongs to the Council of Europe), which deals with cases brought
forward under the European Charter of Human Rights.
The ECJ may be called upon to decide on a speciﬁc case by a member state, by EU
institutions, by companies and by individuals. By virtue of the principle of direct application of
Community law, European citizens can rely directly on EU legal provisions before their national
courts. National courts that need clariﬁcations on certain aspects of EU law or its interpretation
can seek a ‘preliminary ruling’ from the ECJ. The case law produced by the ECJ, which is
binding on member states and individuals, has had important consequences for the daily life of
European citizens in areas such as freedom of movement and social rights. Some of the ECJ’s
rulings have had signiﬁcant implications for regional development issues (see Chapter 17 on state
aid). The Court of First Instance, composed of at least one judge from each member state, deals
with disputes between the Commission and individuals or businesses, and administrative
disputes within the EU institutions. The Civil Service Tribunal deals with disputes involving the
European Union institutions and their civil servants.
The Court of Auditors
The Court of Auditors was established in 1975 and was given the formal status of an ‘EU
institution’ by the Maastricht Treaty in 1992. It has one member for each member state
appointed for a six-year term by the Council after consultation with the European Parliament.
It is based in Luxembourg. The Court of Auditors is responsible for the audit of EU ﬁnances.
124
The institutional framework
Box 9.1 The symbols of the EU
The EU, although not a state in its own right, has a number of ofﬁcial symbols. The European
flag symbolises Europe’s unity through a circle of gold stars representing solidarity and
harmony between the member states, regions and citizens of Europe. The number of stars does
not represent the number of member states (the number twelve is a symbol of perfection,
completeness and unity). The ﬂag was originally created by the Council of Europe in 1955, and
adopted by the EU heads of state and government as the ofﬁcial emblem of the European
Communities (later European Union) in 1985.
The motto of the European Union is ‘United in diversity’. Europe Day takes place every year
on 9 May, the day on which in 1950 Robert Schuman presented his proposal for the European
Coal and Steel Community (see Chapter 7), which was the forerunner of the present EU.
Europe Day is an occasion for activities and festivities that bring the EU closer to its citizens.
The ofﬁcial anthem of the EU is the ﬁnal movement of the Ninth Symphony composed in 1823
by Ludwig Van Beethoven, who set to music the ‘Ode to Joy’ written in 1785 by Friedrich von
Schiller. The poem expresses an idealistic vision of the human race becoming brothers. The
Council of Europe adopted the theme as its anthem in 1972, and in 1985 EU heads of state
and government designated it as the ofﬁcial anthem of the EU (without it replacing the national
anthems of the member states).
Source: adapted by the authors from http://europa.eu/abc/symbols/index_en.htm.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 154

It acts as independent guardian of the ﬁnancial interests of the citizens of the Union by checking
that the revenue and expenditure of the EU is received and spent in a lawful and regular 
manner. It can scrutinise the budget and ﬁnances of European institutions but also of national,
regional and local authorities in charge of managing EU funds, in particular Structural and
Cohesion Funds (Chapter 18). It can carry out ‘on-the-spot’ audits. The Court of Auditors
produces opinions to suggest improvements to EU ﬁnances and assists the European Parliament
and Council in overseeing the implementation of the EU budget.
Other EU bodies
Apart from the ﬁve formal legislative, executive and judicial institutions of the EU described
above, a number of other EU bodies perform speciﬁc roles important to the functioning of the
EU: advisory bodies, ﬁnancial institutions, various support services and a range of specialised
European agencies.
The European Economic and Social Committee
The European Economic and Social Committee (EESC) is a consultative body set up by the
Treaty of Rome to represent economic and social interest groups in Europe and act as a bridge
between the EU and organised civil society. It has 344 members divided into three groups:
‘employers’ and their organisations; ‘employees’ and trade unions; and ‘other interests’ (e.g.
consumer protection, farmers, retailers, and independent and liberal professions). Members are
nominated by national governments and appointed by the Council for a renewable four-year
term. The Committee has six sections: Agriculture, Rural Development and the Environment;
Economic and Monetary Union and Economic and Social Cohesion (which deals with Cohesion
Policy, spatial planning and urban policy); Employment, Social Affairs and Citizenship; External
Relations; Single Market, Production and Consumption; and Transport, Energy, Infrastructure
and the Information Society.
The role of the EESC is a consultative one: in speciﬁc policy ﬁelds that have been expanded
by the Single European Act (1987), Maastricht Treaty (1992) and Amsterdam Treaty (1997),
the European Commission and the Council must consult the EESC. This is the case for cohesion
and environment policies, among others. The EESC can submit opinions on its own initiative on any Community matter and thus contribute to shape the European policy agenda. It
delivers on average 170 advisory documents and opinions a year (of which about 15 per cent
are issued on its own initiative). Its Single Market Monitoring Unit reviews the development 
of the Single Market in the member states. Through an opinion on a charter of social rights
published in 1989, the EESC strongly influenced the subsequent ‘Community Charter of
Fundamental Social Rights for Workers’ of the EU-12, which the Heads of State of 11 Member
States adopted in December 1989 (the UK only signed in 1998). The charter establishes major
principles of European labour law, and the social rights enshrined in it have to be implemented
by the member states of the EU.
The Committee of the Regions
The Committee of the Regions (CoR) began operating in 1994, after being created by the
Maastricht Treaty (1992) following pressures by federal member states such as Germany and
Spain for more involvement of local and regional authorities in EU policy-making processes.
Three-quarters of EU legislation is implemented at the local or regional level, so it makes sense
for local and regional representatives to have a say in the development of new EU policy and

## S. 155

laws, in particular in the policy ﬁelds dealt with in Part V of the book. The CoR is composed
of (usually elected) representatives of the local and regional authorities of the EU member states.
Its 344 members are appointed for four years by the Council on the basis of nominations by
member states.
The CoR is a consultative body. The Treaty of Maastricht (1992) set out that the Commission
and the Council have to consult the CoR whenever new legislative proposals are made in areas
that have repercussions at regional or local level, namely economic and social cohesion, trans-
European transport, energy and telecommunication networks, public health, education, youth
and culture. The Treaty of Amsterdam (1997) extended the list of policy ﬁelds in which the CoR
has to be consulted when new EU legislation is proposed to include employment, social policy,
environment and vocational training. In other policy ﬁelds the Commission, the Council and
the European Parliament can consult the CoR if a legislative proposal has important regional
or local implications. The CoR can additionally prepare opinions and commission studies on
its own initiative in order to inﬂuence the agenda of the Commission and of the Council. 
Due to its regional and local interest base, the CoR pays particular attention to the application
of the principle of subsidiarity in EU policies (see Chapters 2 and 11). For example, recent
activities of the CoR have focused on the topics of territorial cooperation, in particular the
European grouping of territorial cooperation (EGTC) (discussed in Chapter 16), and multi-level
governance.
The work of the CoR is prepared by six Commissions specialising in particular policy areas:
Territorial Cohesion; Economic and Social Policy; Sustainable Development; Culture, Education
and Research; Constitutional Affairs, European Governance and the Area of Freedom, Security
and Justice; and External Relations and Decentralised Cooperation. The Commission for
Territorial Cohesion (COTER) is of particular importance as it covers Cohesion Policy, spatial
planning, urban policy and transport policy. Many of the CoR’s opinions and reports have
contributed strongly to debates in the Council (see, for example, CoR 2008), sometimes leading
to amendments to draft legislation. Studies produced by the CoR on the role of local and
regional authorities in EU policies and on transboundary cooperation will be of particular
interest for students, researchers and professionals involved in European spatial planning; they
are available on the ofﬁcial website of the CoR.
EU financial institutions
The European Central Bank (ECB) was established in 1998 and is located in Frankfurt am Main,
Germany. The ECB became a powerful institution in the wake of Economic and Monetary
Union and the introduction of a single currency (the euro) in a number of European countries
in 2001 (Chapter 17). The ECB is part of the European System of Central Banks alongside the
national central banks of the member states. Its task is to deﬁne and implement the monetary
policy of the member countries of the ‘eurozone’, conduct foreign exchange operations and set
base interest rates.
The European Investment Bank (EIB) was established by the Treaty of Rome as an
autonomous ﬁnancial institution in charge of supporting capital investment for the economic
development of the EU. Based in Luxembourg, it is the largest international ﬁnancial institution
in the world and provides long-term loans at low interest rates to support projects (including
ones that are of importance for spatial planning and territorial development) across Europe, 
in particular in less favoured regions. Projects in the ﬁelds of transport, energy, telecommunications, environmental protection, heritage and urban regeneration have been funded, often
complementing Structural and Cohesion funding (see Chapter 18). The EIB provides ﬁnancial

The institutions of the EU
127
Box 9.2 Working for the EU institutions
The majority of permanent jobs in the EU institutions are ﬁlled by competitions, which are open
to all citizens of EU member states. The different types of careers, staff categories (such as
administrators, linguists, interpreters and lawyer-linguists) and grades are presented on the
website of the European Personnel Selection Ofﬁce (EPSO). Open competitions are announced
in the Ofﬁcial Journal of the European Union and listed online on the EPSO website (given
below). The competition process is very selective and highly competitive. Staff have good
salaries and allowances. In addition to their permanent staff, some institutions also take on a
limited number of individuals on temporary contracts. Finally, civil servants or ofﬁcials in
national, regional or local administrations (even experts from the private and voluntary sectors
or international organisations) can become a ‘seconded national expert’ and work in the European Commission for a few years, bringing their experience and expertise to the Commission.
Enquiries for such opportunities should be made to the Permanent Representation to the EU
of one’s country.
For university graduates (EU and non-EU citizens), EU institutions offer various traineeship
schemes (‘stages’) lasting three to ﬁve months. Candidates must have a university degree. 
To know more about a career or traineeship opportunities in the European institutions, 
refer to the website of the European Personnel Selection Ofﬁce: http://europa.eu/epso/index_
en.htm.
Besides the EU institutions themselves, there are plenty of jobs in organisations of all kinds
that deal with EU affairs or gravitate around the EU institutions, many of which are based in
Brussels: think-tanks, lobbies, NGOs, consultancies specialising in public affairs, EU project
management and EU policy evaluation. In the member states there are many employment
opportunities in the managing bodies of EU-funded programmes (such as Structural Funds
programmes) at national or regional level, and in the national representations of the European
Commission in the member states. The two largest websites for jobs in EU affairs are
http://eurobrussels.com/ and www.europeanvoice.com
To facilitate job search, the EU has promoted a common EU curriculum vitae template,
which can be accessed at http://europass.cedefop.europa.eu.
For those interested in combining their interest and expertise in spatial planning, territorial
development and European transboundary cooperation, the technical secretariats and managing
authorities of the various programmes funded under the ‘European Territorial Cooperation’
objective of EU Cohesion Policy (see Chapter 16) offer interesting opportunities of employment and traineeships. Links to the websites of the 52 cross-border cooperation programmes
(INTERREG IVA), 13 transnational cooperation programmes (INTERREG IVB) and the interregional cooperation programme (INTERREG IVC) can be found at http://ec.europa.
eu/regional_policy/cooperation/index_en.htm. Additionally, three networking programmes
frequently offer job opportunities:
•
the European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON) – http://www.espon.eu
•
the European programme for sustainable urban development (URBACT II) – http://
urbact.eu
•
the INTERACT II programme of support for European Territorial Cooperation Programmes
– www.interact-eu.net (see ‘jobs and experts’).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 157

support outside the EU to eastern European, Mediterranean, African, Caribbean, Paciﬁc, Asian
and Latin American countries. The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development
(EBRD), located in London, was founded in 1991 to support eastern and central European
countries in the process of transition to a market economy.
Other EU bodies, support services and agencies
The European Ombudsman, who is elected by the European Parliament, investigates complaints
lodged by EU citizens and residents, businesses and associations about maladministration in the
institutions and bodies of the European Union (except the Courts). Maladministration occurs
if an institution fails to act in accordance with the law, fails to respect the principles of good
administration or violates human rights through discrimination.
The Ofﬁce for Ofﬁcial Publications of the European Communities is the publishing house 
of the EU. It publishes EU ofﬁcial documents and a variety of information sources about the
EU. Many of its publications are available free of charge in electronic version on the Europa
website.
In addition to the ofﬁcial institutions of the EU, a number of specialised Community agencies
have been set up to fulﬁl speciﬁc technical, scientiﬁc or managerial tasks. These agencies are
bodies governed by European public law, but not strictly speaking EU institutions. Agencies of
particular relevance to the policy ﬁelds covered in Part V of the book are the European
Environment Agency (EEA) (see Chapter 21), the European Foundation for the Improvement of Living and Working Conditions (EUROFOUND), the European Maritime Safety
Agency (EMSA) and the European Railway Agency (ERA). In addition to these, a small number
of ‘executive agencies’ were established for a ﬁxed period of time to manage one or more
Community programmes. For example, the Trans-European Transport Network Executive
Agency (TEN-T EA) was set up in 2006 to assist with the completion of the TEN-Ts described
in Chapter 19. Finally a number of agencies were set up in the ﬁelds of Common Foreign and
Security Policy and Police and Judicial Cooperation in Criminal Matters to assist cooperation
between member states in those ﬁelds, for example the European Police Ofﬁce (EUROPOL),
which works against organised international crime.
Box 9.2 gives an overview of the employment opportunities in the various EU institutions
and agencies described in this chapter, with particular references to agencies and programmes
of interest to spatial planners.
Conclusion
The EU has three main institutions forming the decision-making triangle – the Council of
Ministers, the European Parliament and the European Commission. Each of these represents
different types of interests: those of national governments, those of European citizens, and the
‘EU’ interest as a whole. The structure and powers of these three institutions have evolved over
time, as well as their mutual relationships, and will continue to evolve in future in light of
recurring debates about EU competences, subsidiarity, democracy and transparency (see Chapter
12). As mentioned in Chapter 7, the EU political system is characterised by multiple levels of
governance, in which EU institutions, national governments, local and regional authorities and
other stakeholders interact in policy design, policy-making and implementation. The next
chapter presents the constellation of non-EU actors that are involved in EU policy-making and/or
interact with EU institutions and agencies.
128
The institutional framework

## S. 158

Further reading
Most introductory textbooks on the European Union offer a clear presentation of the European
institutions. Readers should make sure they always select the latest edition of a textbook, as 
the EU political system evolves constantly and most books are regularly updated to reﬂect this.
See in particular Hix 2005, Nugent 2006 and Cini 2006 (and their future revised editions). For
up-to-date information on the composition, powers, activities of the EU institutions, agencies
and bodies, refer to the website of the European Union: http://europa.eu. It has links to all the
EU institutions, agencies and bodies mentioned in this chapter.

## S. 159

Non-EU actors, organisations 
and networks
The EU institutions are probably more open to a variety of interests than most national
administrations, since they are aware of the need to recover public support for the integration
process. The Commission has always had a strong incentive to widen support for its proposals, for
which social, environmental, regional or consumer interests may be particularly important. These
civic interests present the Commission with opportunities to expand its inﬂuence and legitimacy.
(EP 2003: 53–4)
Introduction
A larger number of actors and interests are involved in the EU policy process than are involved
in policy (in general) in member states, so much so that it is often referred to as messy or
congested (Richardson 2005a). One reason is self-evident. The EU is comprised of 27 member
states, each with many interest groups that have a view about EU affairs. There is also extensive
interaction between the EU institutions, national and regional governments and other
international bodies. To be effective, and because of their relatively weaker position compared
to member state governments, the EU institutions depend on building a wide consensus among
many parties. Richardson (2005a: 6) argues that:
one of the central features of the EU policy process, which seems to hold good for a high
percentage of cases is that it ‘works’ only by mobilising a large number of public and private
actors from different nations and policy domains and, somehow, persuading them to move
from the status quo to a new policy settlement.
This demands an approach that pays as much attention to ‘ideas, knowledge and expertise,
rather than just pure interest’ (Richardson 2005a: 6). He is emphasising here that the
engagement of stakeholders can lead to better policy outcomes. The expressed need by the EU
of a wide involvement of ‘civil society’1 is also a reaction to what a traditional view of
democratic government regards as a lack of legitimacy in EU decision-making, or a ‘democratic
deﬁcit’. The legitimate authority of the EU is underpinned much less by representative government and more on the direct engagement of civil society, or participatory governance (Finke
2007).
The chapter begins by introducing the range of actors from government and civil society that
are involved in EU affairs and then explains why their involvement is so signiﬁcant to the
legitimacy of EU policy. The next sections consider the role of European networks of interests
and their lobbying activity. The role of national and regional governments in EU policy-making
is considered next, and the chapter ends with reference to the main international organisations
relevant to European spatial planning, particularly the Council of Europe.
Chapter 10
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 160

Non-EU actors involved in EU policy-making
The emergence of European spatial planning has involved a complex web of government,
private, academic and non-governmental organisations and actors. These include international
agencies, national and regional governments, non-governmental organisations (NGOs), private
consultancies, academics and professional bodies. Some of these are formally represented in 
the EU institutions, notably: national governments in the European Council and Council of
Ministers; regional governments in the Committee of the Regions; some non-governmental
organisations in the Economic and Social Committee; and scientists (consultants, academics and
professionals) in advisory bodies and expert committees.
Aside from the 27 member states, their constituent regions and other applicant or neighbouring countries, the number of organisations that are involved in European policy-making is
vast. In 1970 it was estimated that there were about 300 ‘Euro-groups’ directing their attention
to the EU. By 1986, this had increased to more than 650, and by the 2000s the estimate of the
total number of interest groups has been put at somewhere between ‘three to ten thousand
depending on how they are deﬁned’ (Andersen and Eliassen 2001). The European Parliament
(EP 2003) estimates that about 2,600 interest groups have an ofﬁce in Brussels; Greenwood
(2003) makes a more conservative estimate of 1,450; and Nugent 2006 estimates that ‘between
15,000 and 20,000 people make a living from “lobbying Brussels”’.
European non-governmental interest groups range from ad hoc, single issue, nationally or
regionally based pressure groups seeking to promote their interests (for example, local groups
seeking funding to conserve a local heritage asset or protect the name of local food products),
to highly specialised associations of national and regional bodies with large secretariats dealing
with a wide portfolio of issues and based in Brussels. Such large-scale associations are exempli-
ﬁed by the European Environmental Bureau (EEB), a federation of 143 national environmental
citizens’ organisations with a budget of over EUR 2 million (EEB 2007).
Professional experts and their representative bodies have been important in the development
of European spatial planning (as explained in Chapter 14). In the 1960s a Town Planners’
Liaison Committee provided a means of communication between the European Commission and
the relevant professions. This was to become, under the auspices of the International Society of
City and Regional Planners (ISOCARP), the European Council of Spatial Planners (ECTP)
(ISOCARP 2007).2 The principles of the ECTP are set out in its Charter of Athens, ﬁrst
published in 1998 and revised in 2003. It recognises the variety of styles of spatial planning 
in Europe and the diverse roles of the town planner as scientist, designer, political mediator and
urban manager. The ECTP has made important contributions to the European planning
discourse, including, for example, its response to the 2007 Territorial Agenda and the
publication of Strategic Planning Towards Territorial Cohesion (ECTP 2007).
Business and industry is very well represented in various forms in the wider EU policy process
(especially in extensive discussions related to the creation of the Single Market). This is,
however, arguably the sector of civil society that is least well represented in the emergence of
European spatial planning while at the same time being highly inﬂuential for spatial development. The lack of involvement of the private sector will not be a surprise as the same would 
be typical in most member states where planning tends to be regarded as primarily the preserve
of the public sector. In some cases – for example, the European Union of Housebuilders 
and Developers (UEPC), which keeps a ‘watching brief’ on European spatial planning –
representative organisations of businesses are keen to stay informed about the policy debate.
For other property companies the professional associations provide a channel of communication
on regional policy and territorial cooperation – for example, the UK Royal Institution of
Chartered Surveyors (RICS) and the global International Federation of Surveyors (FIG).3

## S. 161

Civil society and the ‘democratic deficit’
The extensive involvement of very many interest groups and actors arises in part as a response
to the criticisms of a ‘democratic deﬁcit’ in the EU. The Council is not directly elected,
appointed Commissioners have the right of initiative in bringing forward legislation, and even
the Parliament is elected by a very low proportion of the electorate (see Chapter 9). Goldmann
(2001: 157) sets out the paradox succinctly:
a number of democracies have joined forces for the purpose of increasing their ability to
solve problems for their citizens, but they have done so by setting up institutions not quite
compatible with the standards of democracy taken for granted within each of them.
This has a direct bearing on the involvement of other actors: ‘the more the policy-making
legitimacy of the EU has been challenged the more the Commission has mobilised stakeholder
participation in the process’ (Richardson 2005a: 8).
However, the effect of interest group participation on the legitimacy of the EU is not clearcut. The uncertain way in which inﬂuence is gained over decisions raises concerns about the
democratic process in the EU (Majone 1998). As consultation and lobbying activity has
increased, concerns about the disproportionate inﬂuence of certain interests has led to calls for
more transparency in policy-making (Friends of the Earth Europe 2006) (discussed further
below).
Since the mid-1990s there has been a growing commitment to external consultation and
citizen involvement in decision-making in the European institutions. Provisions in the
Amsterdam Treaty (1997) sought to open up the decision-making process and provide citizens
with a right of access to EU documentation. More concerted action followed the White Paper
on European Governance (CEC 2001e), which recognised that ‘many Europeans feel alienated
from the Union’s work’ (p. 7) and the need for a ‘reinforced culture of consultation and dialogue’
(p. 16). It made proposals for improving the openness of the policy process and the direct
participation of citizens and representative organisations, in particular, associations of regional
and local government, international networks of interests, and ‘civil society’, meaning broadly
non-government and non-corporate business. Subsequently the EU has invoked the notion 
of ‘participatory democracy’ and provided means such as internet platforms for citizen
engagement.4 The Lisbon Treaty lends more weight through its provisions for an ‘open,
transparent and regular dialogue’ and consultation with citizens and representative associations (Article 11.1–3, Council 2007c). In principle, the greater participation of civil society
complements the representative role of the European Parliament in decision-making, but 
many questions remain about the real effect of ‘civil society’ in democratising the EU institutions
(Finke 2007).
In practice, there is no doubt that the efforts on both sides have created much more
interaction between interest groups and EU institutions, particularly the Commission. Andersen
and Eliassen (2001) suggest that the Commission needs the input of non-EU interests and
networks because political organisation and representation is much less developed in the EU than
in nation-states. Moreover, the EU institutions have not yet established deﬁnitive networks and
sources of advice; fewer interests have got deeply embedded in the system, and there are few
regulations to prevent direct lobbying to ofﬁcers. Indeed Commission ofﬁcials sometimes build
strong personal contacts with interest representatives (Williams 1996: 36), a relationship that
would be frowned on in the civil service of some member states. Aside from inter-institutional
relations within the EU, consultation processes also tend to be relatively ad hoc in comparison
with the very formal arrangements that will apply for most policy areas in the member states.
132
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 162

There is then an incentive for interests and networks to form because there is more chance
of them ‘ﬁnding a way in’ and inﬂuencing the policy process or simply getting their concerns
on the European agenda (Greenwood and Aspinwall 1997).5 This has been the objective of many
organisations that have a ‘territorial interest’ or have a concern with ‘the spatial dimension’, such
as geographical disparities in prosperity and the concentration of environmental problems in
certain areas. However, in spatial planning and territorial cohesion, although interest groups
have played a part, the role of experts has been more important. In the spatial planning ﬁeld,
a loose association of experts with a similar interest (a policy community) was formed in the
early 1990s and has become a more tightly knit network of experts or ‘epistemic community’
(Haas 1992; Richardson 2005a) (explained further in Chapter 8). A small group of experts 
has collaborated to consistently promulgate a very positive understanding about the value and
role of a spatial planning approach. In this they have been supported by a wide range of
predominantly government actors often acting in networks.
Networks and their role in EU policy-making
Territorial cooperation facilitates the formation of networks that have played a central role in
the development of European spatial planning (explained brieﬂy in Chapter 14). European
networking of public bodies has a long history, and in the context of the emergence of the EU,
has expanded steadily since the 1970s (Camhis and Fox 1992; Gottman 1991). Table 10.1 shows
the main characteristics of examples of European-wide networks of relevance to spatial planning.
The list could be lengthened considerably, with many more diverse and overlapping interest
networks operating at the level of the whole of Europe, the EU or smaller geographical zones.
Our main concern here is with the pan-European networks. A summary of one of the larger and
most inﬂuential networks, the Assembly of European Regions (AER), is given in Box 10.1.
The variety of forms of networks makes them difﬁcult to categorise, but they tend to differ
in three ways: the form of membership organisation, the nature of the ‘common interest’, and
their objectives and methods of working. Some networks, such as the AER and METREX, are
made up of a number (large or small) of independent members. Others, such as the Council of
European Municipalities and Regions (CEMR) and the European Sustainable Cities and Towns
Campaign (ESCTC) are networks of other associations or representative bodies. A special case
here is the Association of European Border Regions, which is a network of other networks of
authorities working across borders. Most networks have a membership structure and subscriptions that support a central secretariat. Others, such as the Conference of European Regions with
Legislative Power (REGLEG), are managed by successive ‘presidencies’ drawn from the member
organisations and have no central secretariat.
Members of a network are drawn together by a common interest that may be primarily
thematic – for example, the Association of Wine Producing Regions – or primarily geographic
– such as the Atlantic Arc. Although many thematic networks are ‘European or EU-wide’ the
theme may lend a geographical dimension to the network – as in the Network of European
Municipalities and Rivers. The motivation to participate in a network has been explained with
reference to authorities wanting to strengthen their know-how and capacity to engage in the
economic realities of international economic competition (Dawson 1992; Robson 1992). But
principal characteristics of networks in planning are also cooperation, learning and lobbying
(CEC 2006d; Dawson 1992). There are three general areas of interest or added value that
encourage networking:
•
Cooperation helps to develop knowledge and experience and provides a pool of fresh ideas
and comparative cases. The transposition of policies and actions from one place to another

Notes: These are selected examples of the primary regional and local government networks in the EU. All are independent from the EU institutions though they cultivate strong links with
them and there may be overlapping membership with, for example, the Committee of the Regions. There are many other geographically or thematically deﬁned networks of cities and regions.

## S. 166

requires much care, but there is no doubt about the value that practitioners ﬁnd in
exchanging experience (De Jong et al. 2002).
•
Networks can provide information, contacts and partnerships for bidding for EU and other
joint funding for speciﬁc projects. Partners can pool resources to make bids or to undertake
research and other activities on their subject of interest.
•
Networks are able to lobby for a particular interest in the processes of EU law and 
policy-making and resource allocation. Members will beneﬁt from joint collection of
information about legislation and policy and collectively they will have a stronger voice 
in the EU.
In the context of more open EU policy-making, the European Commission has also played an
important ‘demand-side’ role in encouraging and funding the creation of networks of interest
groups so that they can assist in the policy-making and implementation process (Mahoney 
2004: 443), and where regional government is concerned, to accelerate innovation. Ofﬁcials 
of the EU bodies will be keen in many cases to receive ideas from others, to foster a ‘European
dimension’ or understanding to problems, and to establish direct links with regional and local
authorities.
The Commission accepts that effective networks require time to build trust and ‘mutual
comprehension of objectives and concepts . . . in spite of different cultures and languages’ 
(DG REGIO 2006: 24). So it supports network formation with organisational, political and
ﬁnancing support. For example, in 1989 the Commission provided ECU 10 million to establish
12 inter-city networks with a view to contributing to, and raising awareness of, an urban agenda

Non-EU actors, organisations and networks
137
Box 10.1 Example of a network: the Assembly of European
Regions (AER)
The Assembly of European Regions (AER) was established in 1985 and by 2009 had more than
270 member regions from 33 countries. Its main objective is to increase the inﬂuence of
regions, deﬁned as ‘local authorities immediately below the level of central government with
a political power of representation, as embodied by an elected regional assembly’ (AER 2007:
2). It argues that a regional tier enhances the legitimacy and efﬁciency of government generally,
and therefore it is particularly concerned with establishing democratic regional governments
and the application of the principle of subsidiarity so that ‘all those activities best carried out
at the regional level should be under the control of regional politicians’ (AER 2007: 6) (see
Chapter 11 for an explanation of subsidiarity). So important is this issue that the AER presents
on its website a list of dictionaries that have failed to include the term.
Given its size and the need to promote the concerns of all its members, the AER agenda
is fairly general, with more speciﬁc issues being taken up by smaller networks of interest, for
example the Association of European Border Regions (AEBR). As the number of more speciﬁc
regional interest networks has multiplied, the AER’s role has waned. The AER now wants to
provide a stronger ‘umbrella’ function for other regional networks.
The AER agenda of issues mirrors closely that of the EU but emphasises the role of regions
and the need for effective regional governance. Along with other regional networks the AER
lobbies hard to see the continuation of Cohesion Policy (AER 2008). It has lent strong support
for the notion of territorial cohesion and the 2007 Territorial Agenda.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 167

at the EU level. From 1991 to 2001, the RECITE Programme (Regions and Cities for Europe)
supported cooperation networks between regions and cities to undertake joint economic projects
and to promote the transfer of know-how. Similarly, between 1991 and 2002 the ECOS-
Overture Programme funded EU bodies to network with the then accession countries (whose
participation was funded under the PHARE Programme) to promote projects on regional and
local development strategies, protecting the environment and developing the economic potential
of cultural heritage.
DG REGIO has used its Innovative Actions Programme to fund three networks on ‘experimental actions’ beyond the scope of the Structural Funds. The history of one such network, the
Welsh Assembly Government-led Sustainable European Regions Network (SERN), illustrates
the importance of Commission support. The SERN started out in 2002, undertaking substantive
research to support the sustainability agenda for regions, including a series of studies co-ﬁnanced
by the Commission (SERN 2004). Subsequent funding bids to the Commission were unsuccessful, and the Innovative Actions Programme was discontinued in 2006 as priorities changed.
Without the Commission’s support the SERN network is now reduced to ‘a series of events’,
and there has been little activity since 2006. From the Commission’s perspective, EU funding
is intended only as ‘start-up’ support, and networks and innovative actions are expected to
become self-sustaining. The reality is that many such networks maintain activity primarily 
by making use of other EU funding sources, particularly those available under the INTERREG
initiative and research and development framework programmes.
During the funding period 2007–13 the Commission is promoting cooperation on ‘testing
best practice for economic modernisation and increased competitiveness’ through the Regions
for Economic Change Programme with a budget of EUR 375 million (CEC 2006e: 3). The
Commission will play a stronger steering role in these networks, setting themes with the 
Lisbon Agenda in mind. Otherwise, the task of funding networks, and speciﬁcally the RECITE
and ECOS-Overture objectives, is from 2000 incorporated into the work of strand C of the
INTERREG initiative. These initiatives are explained in Chapter 16. Three speciﬁc networks 
(or support programmes) are also funded under the ‘European Territorial Cooperation’ objective
(INTERREG IV): URBACT II, which facilitates networking of cities; INTERACT II, which
facilitates cooperation of bodies involved in managing other territorial cooperation programmes;
and the European Spatial Planning Observation Programme (ESPON).
The scale and influence of lobbying in the EU
Most networks are essentially political organisations. They seek to inﬂuence the political
process through providing information and case examples, by sponsoring research, by raising
public awareness, and by lobbying in the political process. Where government bodies are
involved, elected representatives attend the principal meetings. Initiatives are usually led by a
key politician, although ofﬁcers employed by the network or in its member organisations 
will often prepare the ground. Many organisations will produce research and policy statements, which the Commission will often welcome as a complement to its own limited internal
staff resources. Lobbying can be very intense and can involve external experts and specialist
consultancy firms with public awareness campaigns. For spatial planning and territorial
cooperation it generally takes the form of routine responses to consultative documents, for 
which the Commission may not be the key player as most initiatives in the ﬁeld of European
spatial planning are intergovernmental in nature. Lobbying to the Commission on ‘territorial’
issues is often connected with the spatial distribution of policies and spending as illustrated 
in Box 10.2.
138
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 168

The level of lobbying activity of interest groups is not at all typical of national politics in
Europe and may be more akin to the situation in the United States, but there are fewer bureaucrats in the EU to liaise with lobby groups. The number of interest groups actively seeking to
inﬂuence EU policy is probably about the same as the number of senior ofﬁcers in the Commission: about 3,500. This is a clear indicator of the great signiﬁcance of actors and organisations
outside the main EU institutions in policy-making. Much of this activity takes place in Brussels
out of the public eye, leading to concerns about openness and accountability (see, for example,
Friends of the Earth Europe 2006). The Alliance for Lobbying Transparency and Ethics in the
EU (Alter-EU) represents 160 NGOs and has campaigned for more accountability because of
alleged biases to industry and conﬂicts of interest in the membership of expert groups who advise
the Commission (Alter-EU 2008). Greer et al. (2008: 427) claim conclusive evidence in the health
sector of bias ‘in favor of those who are already powerful in the member states and the Commission, and against poor, post-communist or Mediterranean states’. However, not everyone
agrees. Greenwood (2003: 13) doubts the consistent dominance of business interests or any other
interest in policy-making partly because of ‘the fragmented nature of the EU decision making’.
In 2008, the Commission launched ‘the register of interests’, commonly referred to as the
lobby register, as part of the ‘European transparency initiative’ (CEC 2008g). The objective is
to open up the policy-making process and expose more clearly the sometimes closeted relations
between lobby organisations and the Commission. Interest representatives are expected to

Non-EU actors, organisations and networks
139
Box 10.2 Lobbying on European territorial issues
Member states will typically lobby for their areas to receive more funding and more favourable
treatment from EU policy. This means understanding the spatial consequences of sectoral
policy. For example, changes to the rules that allocate funding from the Common Agricultural
Policy will have profound effects on particular regions, depending on their geography and
farming practices. Those adversely affected will want to promote their interest. They will work
with others in networks to make a general case for types of places – peripheral regions, wine
producing regions, regions relying on the car industry, etc.
For example, the 2007 Territorial Agenda of the EU was made available in draft form by the
German government that was leading the intergovernmental process at this time. All the key
European-level planning networks made responses. The response of the Association of
European Border Regions was rather critical of the ‘small-scale’ ambitions of the Agenda and
called for a ‘large scale priority . . . considering Europe as an integrated system’ (AEBR 2007:
11). The response advocated joint cross-border land use plans. Underpinning the response is
the main interest and rationale of the network: borders continue to be a signiﬁcant barrier for
realising the potential of border regions, and more attention should be paid to them.
One critical issue in lobbying for more recognition and support for particular places is the
EU’s competition policy. This does the opposite: it works to reduce state aids to speciﬁc
locations in favour of open competition. So interest groups, primarily regions, have to lobby
hard to make the case for differentiation on geographical lines. For example, when commenting
on the draft Territorial Agenda, the Council of European Municipalities and Regions called for
‘enhanced complementarity . . . between the EU’s internal market policies and the objectives
of sustainable urban and regional planning’ (CEMR 2007: 2).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 169

provide information on their organisations and activities in the register and then to abide by a
Code of Conduct, shown in Box 10.3. Relevant organisations are those that undertake ‘activities
carried out with the objective of inﬂuencing the policy formulation and decision-making
processes of the European institutions’ (CEC 2008g: 3), or more succinctly lobbying. Public
authorities have the option but are not ‘expected’ to join the register. At the end of 2008, more
than 800 interest organisations had registered. The register provides a valuable insight into the
diversity of organisations that seek to inﬂuence EU law and policy. If anyone suspects that
interest representatives have not complied with the Code, they can raise a complaint and the
Commission can sanction the offender by suspension or removal from the register.
National and regional governments
During the early 1990s the European Commission was very much in the lead of policy development in the EU under the leadership of the Commission President Jacques Delors 
(as explained in Chapter 14). From the late 1990s this position was eroded, and by the early
2000s national governments had reasserted a dominant position in the European Union 
policy-making process, especially through the European Council, which had assumed the main
agenda-setting role by ‘reducing the power and manoeuvrability of both the Council of 
Ministers and the Commission’ (Nugent 2006: 52). Furthermore, as Richardson (2005a: 4)
argues ‘increasing Euroscepticism appears to be causing some of the key stakeholders,
particularly the member states, to apply the brakes to the seemingly inexorable extension of the
EU’s policy-making competence’. We should therefore consider the ‘non-EU position’ of the EU
member governments.
European spatial planning has emerged primarily through the collaboration of a small team
of experts drawn from member state governments, the Commission’s Directorate-General for
Regional Policy (DG REGIO), consultant ﬁrms and universities. The national interests for spatial
140
The institutional framework
Box 10.3 Code of conduct for interest representation: 
seven rules
Interest representatives shall always:
1
identify themselves by name and by the entity(ies) they work for or represent;
2
not misrepresent themselves as to the effect of registration to mislead third parties and/or
EU staff;
3
declare the interests, and where applicable the clients or the members, that they represent;
4
ensure that, to the best of their knowledge, information that they provide is unbiased,
complete, up to date and not misleading;
5
not obtain or try to obtain information, or any decision, dishonestly;
6
not induce EU staff to contravene rules and standards of behaviour applicable to them;
7
if employing former EU staff, respect their obligation to abide by the rules and conﬁdentiality
requirements that apply to them.
Source: CEC 2008g: 7.

## S. 170

planning have a symbiotic relationship with the Commission. Community Initiatives related to
spatial planning, in particular INTERREG, have assisted in making the case for spatial planning
at the national level by providing funding. For the Commission the member states are the key
players in the implementation of EU policies and legislation. The Commission also needs the
ideas and resources held by the larger national agencies to develop policy. The shift to the open
method of coordination (discussed in Chapter 15) encourages the Commission to build stronger
relationships with member states.
It was national and regional governments that ﬁrst demonstrated the potential of crossborder and transnational cooperation, especially in the Benelux countries. Subsequently, they
have developed the European dimension in national and regional planning instruments, by 
co-ﬁnancing transnational level studies and cooperation, and by providing the intellectual
underpinning for the notion of European spatial planning and territorial cohesion. Ministries
or agencies with responsibilities for spatial planning in France, Germany and the Netherlands
have been particularly inﬂuential with continued involvement over many years (Faludi 2002a).
However, it should not be assumed that any government speaks with one voice; there will be
different attitudes to spatial planning within member states. Representative civil servants (and
these are few in number) may have to put as much effort into selling ideas ‘back home’ as they
do in the intergovernmental meetings.6
Member states have a particular opportunity to shape the policy agenda when they hold the
six-month Council presidency, bringing forward topics on which they have special expertise or
interest and avoiding topics and policy issues they disagree with. (The workings of the Council
are explained in Chapter 9.) Small groups of member states tend to collaborate in bringing
forward policy and actions. For some time, particular initiatives related to spatial planning have
employed the ‘troika method’ whereby the current presidency, together with the former and 
next presidencies work cooperatively to ensure continuity of policy development from one presidency to the next. It is no coincidence that critical decisions and publications tend to appear
under particular presidencies. Faludi and Waterhout (2002) explain the effect of successive presidencies on the evolution of the ESDP: ﬁrst steps were taken under the French and Dutch
presidencies in 1989 and 1991; the Leipzig Principles and ﬁnal ESDP were published under 
the German presidencies in 1994 and 1999.7 The Territorial Agenda was published under the
German presidency in 2007. (The contributions of individual member states to key European
spatial planning initiatives are discussed in Chapters 13 and 14.)
Janin Rivolin and Faludi (2005) also point out the bias in the leadership of the European
agenda towards north-west Europe. The Nordic countries did not join the EU until 1995 but
have had influence through providing models of transnational cooperation through the
Framework for the Spatial Development in the Baltic Sea Region (VASAB 2010 1994). Although
southern European countries have hosted numerous meetings and undertaken studies they have
not made any significant substantive contribution. One explanation is that although the
southern country representatives have been largely supportive of the dialogue, they have been
‘enemies of the crude rationality of a Scheme and very cautious about the risk of changing the
methods for the allocation of Structural Funds of which they were major beneﬁciaries’ (Rusca
1998, quoted in Janin Rivolin and Faludi 2005: 204). Other factors play a part too, of course:
widely varying resources and capacities at national and regional levels reﬂecting the size of the
country and the distribution of competences for planning; past experience of international
cooperation on planning; and national priorities and politics. For example, the federal
government of Belgium has no competence for planning and thus is likely to be less enthusiastic
(although this is made up in part by the three Belgian regions where planning powers lie), and
changes in party political control in the UK have meant that a sceptical view was replaced by
a more positive view from 1997.

Non-EU actors, organisations and networks
