## S. 171

The involvement of regional governments in European spatial planning is strongly tied to the
process of regionalism. Wagstaff (1999: 5) argues that:
regional identity has made itself felt at so many levels across Europe in recent years . . .
regionalist movements have sought to shake off the more or less oppressive yoke of central
control and to stake their claim to varying degrees of autonomy and regional self-expression.
Decentralisation and devolution of government in some member states in the context of the
debate on subsidiarity and a more democratic EU has fostered a ‘renaissance’ of the region in
European political discourse and in practical politics (Rhodes 1995). The European Union 
has assisted this process through regional funding, which in turn has created the EU ‘regional 
lobby’ with its extensive representation in Brussels and networks of interest. Subsequently, the
regions have been prominent in spatial planning and territorial cooperation partly because
spatial plans can help to build regional identity (Zonneveld and Waterhout 2005). The spatial
planning agenda has been effectively used by regions to promote their position in comparison
with national governments.
The Conference of Peripheral Maritime Regions of Europe (CPMR) and particularly its
Atlantic Commission played an important part in expanding the INTERREG initiative from
minor town-twinning programme to a multi–billion euro set of programmes on transnational
and cross-border cooperation on spatial planning (Nadin and Shaw 2000). The goal was to
direct the attention of policy-makers and funding to territorial issues – to the peripheral regions
– so as to strengthen economic competitiveness and create more balanced development of the
European territory overall in the context of the classic core-periphery understanding of the European territory. As noted above, the beneﬁts of regional involvement in policy development also
accrue to the EU, because it helps to legitimise its policy and action.
International bodies
European member states are also engaged in many other international organisations. Although
none has anything like the breadth of responsibilities and power of the EU these other
organisations can be critically important in speciﬁc policy ﬁelds. For example, the World Trade
Organization (WTO) establishes global policies on trade (mostly to reduce tariffs and open up
markets) that have major implications for EU agricultural policy. EU institutions have extensive
interaction with these other bodies, and national and regional representatives in the EU will often
do the same job in these other organisations. The following section introduces three international
bodies that have had some involvement or interest in spatial planning and territorial cooperation:
the Council of Europe; the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe; and the
Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development.
The Council of Europe
The Council of Europe (CoE) brings together representatives from (in 2009) 47 countries with
the intention of promoting unity and cooperation between the nations and people of Europe.
It must be emphasised that the Council of Europe is not connected in any way with the European
Union save for having good working relations on topics of common interest. Ten initial
member countries created the CoE in 1949 following a major ‘Congress of Europe’ a year earlier.
It was at this conference that the ﬁrst lines of debate were drawn between the federalists or
supranationalists seeking a government of Europe (led by Albert Spinelli) and intergovernmentalists or unionists, promoting a consultative body (led by Winston Churchill) as explained
142
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 172

in Chapter 7. The unionists won the day as far as the Council of Europe is concerned. The
Council of Europe was pledged:
to achieve a closer union between its members in order to protect and promote the ideals
and principles which constitute their common heritage and to further their economic and
social progress [but] did little more than exchange ideas and information on social, legal,
and cultural matters.
(Dinan 1998: 102)
The federalists meanwhile turned their attention to the Economic Community, which was to
become the EU.
Despite Dinan’s commentary, the value of a pan-European forum for exchange of ideas
should not be underestimated, especially in the immediate years after the Second World War.
The Council of Europe has provided an avenue for exchange of a very wide range of perspectives
on social, cultural and scientiﬁc issues, and helped to raise standards and harmonise legal and
other practices. Since 1989 the membership of the Council of Europe has expanded as eastern
European states have joined, including Russia in 1996. However, the breadth of its membership
has counted against the depth of the ‘union’ created, with debate limited to the ‘softer topics’
that are not crucial to the dominant social or economic model of its member states.
The CoE’s primary successes have been in the ﬁeld of human rights and particularly the
creation of the European Court of Human Rights (see Box 10.4). Again, this should not be
confused with the EU’s Court of Justice. The many high-proﬁle cases that go to the Court of
Human Rights do so on the basis of agreements reached between the members of the Council
of Europe, not the EU. The Council has also played an important part in the transition of postcommunist regimes, assisting with political, constitutional and legal reform.
The Council of Europe works through reaching agreements or common standpoints and
produces many publications with advice and guidance to its members. It does not make
legislation, nor does it provide substantial funding or make levies on its members. Membership
is open to any democratic country. It is based in Strasbourg (where the European Parliament is
also based). Above all, the annual budget of the Council of Europe at about EUR 100 million is
only about 2 per cent of the budget of the EU. Their roles are complementary, and there is a good
deal of synergy between the agenda for spatial planning pursued by the EU and that of the Council
of Europe, promoted in part by involvement of many of the same national representatives.
Within the Council of Europe, the Foreign Ministers of member states make up the Committee
of Ministers that issues recommendations and adopts conventions. The most well known is the
European Convention of Human Rights of 1950. The Parliamentary Assembly consists of
delegates appointed by national parliaments and makes recommendations to the Council of
Ministers. The Assembly has a specialist committee concerned with regional/territorial planning:
the CDAT (Comité Directeur pour l’Aménagement du Territoire). The Congress of Local and
Regional Authorities in Europe (CLRAE) was created in 1994 as a consultative body within the
CoE to ensure the participation and voice of local and regional authorities in European
integration. It has produced a European Urban Charter (CoE 1992b, 2008).
A number of standing conferences within the CoE deal with various topics. Of special
relevance here is the Conference of European Ministers of Spatial and Regional Planning
(Conférence Européenne des Ministres pour l’Aménagement du Territoire) (CEMAT), which has
met regularly since 1970. CEMAT has played a defining role in strengthening environmental and heritage protection and the emergence of European spatial planning, alongside
agreements on matters such as sustainability, the representation of women, and environmental 
protection (notably the Bern Convention on the Conservation of Wildlife and Habitats). It led

## S. 173

the European Campaign for Urban Renaissance during 1980–82 that started a wide-ranging
debate on healthy environments, urban regeneration and community development. It also
adopted the European Urban Charter in 1982, a forerunner of the EU’s Thematic Strategy on
the Urban Environment. The CEMAT adopted the Torremolinos Charter in 1983 (CEMAT
1983) calling for a European-wide ‘regional planning concept’. This aspiration was ﬁnally
achieved in 2000 with the publication of the Guiding Principles for Sustainable Spatial
Development of the European Continent (CEMAT 2000) (as explained in Chapter 15).
The United Nations Economic Commission for Europe
The United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) was set up in 1947 by the
United Nations Economic and Social Council (not to be confused with the EU advisory body,
the Economic and Social Committee) and is based in Geneva. It played a major role in
maintaining contact between eastern and western European countries (and with other countries
outside Europe when relevant) during the Cold War years and subsequently in the transition
process. It promotes UN objectives in Europe and particularly European economic integration,
so non-governmental interests are also involved. Its activities are open to any country recognised
by the UN, and it has 56 member states.
144
The institutional framework
Box 10.4 The European Court of Human Rights
The European Court of Human Rights was established in 1959 to make judgements on
allegations of violations of the Convention on Human Rights. The Convention was signed by
the member states of the Council of Europe in 1950 and secures fundamental civil and political
rights to everyone within the jurisdiction of the signatories. The Court ensures that those who
signed the Convention comply with it. The Court of Human Rights should not be confused
with the EU Court of Justice, which ensures that member states comply with EU law
(introduced in Chapter 9).
The rights protected are: the right to life; the right to a fair hearing; respect for private and
family life; freedom of expression; freedom of thought, conscience and religion; the right to an
effective remedy; the right to peaceful enjoyment of one’s possessions; and the right to vote
and stand for election.
The Court of Human Rights is based in Strasbourg and has one judge for each of the states
who have signed the Convention, and who are elected by the Assembly of the Council of
Europe. In 2008 they numbered 47. In 2008 the Court of Human Rights received 42,376
applications for cases to be considered. The highest number came from Russia (27 per cent),
followed by Turkey, Romania and Ukraine. In over 83 per cent of judgements made between
1998 and 2008 the Court found at least one contravention of the Convention.
Cases related to spatial planning do come forward, where the actions of planning 
authorities may infringe fundamental rights, for example, in relation to the failure of planning authorities to allocate land for Gypsies or Roma, or in relation to development plans
prohibiting building on land that would, arguably, infringe the right of peaceful enjoyment of
possessions.
Source: see European Court of Human Rights (2008) and example cases: Mathews v The United Kingdom No.
24833/94 18 February 1999; case of Elia S.r.l. v. Italy No. 37710/97, 21 August 2001.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 174

UNECE undertakes studies and provides policy guidance (including norms and standards)
to its members, with a particular focus on the former communist bloc countries. Its interests in
energy, environment housing, land management, transport and trade all have implications for
spatial planning. In 2008 the UNECE published Spatial Planning: Key Instrument for
Development and Effective Governance with Special Reference to Countries in Transition (Stead
and Nadin 2008).
The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development is more commonly known by
its abbreviation, OECD. It is a global organisation but has made important statements on
territorial issues for European countries (and the headquarters of important functions are in
Paris). Of particular interest are the territorial reviews. These are produced on a country-bycountry basis and also for important regions, for example, the Territorial Review of the
Randstad Holland, The Netherlands (OECD 2007a). The emphasis is on economic issues,
reﬂecting the OECD’s mission ‘to improve the global economy’. The inﬂuence of such statements
and the primarily economic orientation of the OECD is evident in the debates on EU spatial
planning, particularly the 2007 Territorial Agenda. The OECD makes statements on other
economic sectoral issues that are important for planning, such as regional governance, energy,
environment and sustainable development. Its report on The New Rural Paradigm (2006) has
been widely quoted in EU institutions, reﬂecting the signiﬁcance of agricultural policy.
Conclusion
The student of European spatial planning and territorial cooperation has to cope with
understanding a huge number of organisations in a very fragmented and complex institutional
context centred on Brussels. Government and non-government interests come together to gather
information and intelligence and lobby for their cause. It may seem at ﬁrst to be a complex,
impenetrable and perhaps wasteful exercise compared to the situation in member states. But the
breadth of interests represented and the depth of interaction is an essential ingredient for European
integration. It is a three-way system: it allows for the participation of a wide range of interests in
the EU decision-making process and thereby helps to legitimise the actions of the EU in the context
of the democratic deﬁcit; it provides many channels through which the EU institutions can
disseminate policy and thus extend their inﬂuence in the member states and build support for
proposals; and the interest organisations, government bodies and networks themselves are able to
communicate and mix with each other, providing a fertile environment for the formulation and
critique of policy. This complex web will undoubtedly mature in time as the value of particular
organisations becomes clearer: some will disappear and others will prosper. In any event, the
particularly complex and congested nature of EU political life will undoubtedly continue.
Further reading
General introductions of the policy-making process and role of non-EU actors are provided by
Richardson (2000, 2005b), Chapters 14 and 16 of Nugent (2006), various entries in the Encyclopedia
of the European Union (Dinan 2000) and Eising (2008). For a more sceptical view of the role of
interests see Greenwood (2003). Academic commentary on the democratic deﬁcit and the involvement of civil society in EU matters is summarised by Finke (2007). The websites of the many
organisations mentioned in this chapter are very useful but are, for obvious reasons, promotional.
For practical advice about networking and lobbying in Brussels, see Goergen (2004 and 2008).

## S. 175

Decision-making and 
policy-making processes 
in the European Union
Under the principle of subsidiarity, in areas which do not fall within its exclusive competence, the
Union shall act only if and in so far as the objectives of the proposed action cannot be sufﬁciently
achieved by the Member States, either at central level or at regional and local level, but can rather,
by reason of the scale or effects of the proposed action, be better achieved at Union level.
(Treaty on the European Union, Article 5.3; European Communities 2008)
Introduction
This chapter introduces the processes through which decisions on EU policies and legislation are
made as well as the instruments of EU action. A large number of actors are involved in the
European policy-making process: European institutions, national governments, and a variety of
interest groups such as policy experts, lobby groups and non-governmental organisations at the
European, national and regional levels (see Chapters 9 and 10). It is crucial for every European
citizen to understand the ways in which decisions are made by the EU institutions and the role
of the member states in this process, since the media often pejoratively use the word ‘Brussels’
to refer in a loose and imprecise way to the EU institutions as a whole, making no distinction
between the European Council, the Parliament and the Commission. In this chapter, the term
‘policy-making’ is used, which has a wider meaning than the production of European legislation
(law-making). For example, in the ﬁelds of environment, competition or agriculture, besides EU
legislation binding on all member states EU policies can take the shape of intergovernmental
coordination or cooperation between national policies (e.g. macroeconomic policies, justice and
foreign policy), of redistributive policies backed by ﬁnancial support instruments (the Common
Agricultural Policy, EU Cohesion Policy), of infrastructure development programmes (the
Trans-European Networks), of support for research, innovation and transfer of know-how
through ﬁnancial and technical support, or of non-binding guidelines and strategies (see Part V).
EU decision-making processes are complex and vary according to the policy ﬁeld. Two broad
types of European decision-making processes can be identiﬁed, as introduced in Chapter 7: the
supranational decision-making process and the intergovernmental decision-making process
(Table 11.1). The combination of both processes is unique, as is the international organisation
of the EU as a whole. Within these two broad categories a variety of procedures are used. Nugent
(2006) notes that nearly 30 decision-making procedures are mentioned in the Treaties and
identiﬁes several factors that determine what policy process is used for a decision over a
particular matter: what is stated in the treaty; the proposed status of the matter under
consideration; the degree of generality or speciﬁcity of the policy issues; the newness, importance,
controversial nature or political sensitivity of the issue in question; and the balance of policy
responsibilities between EU and national levels.
Chapter 11
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 176

In the ﬁrst part of the chapter the ﬁelds of EU competence are discussed, followed by a
presentation of the two forms of decision-making in the EU: the ‘Community’ or supranational
form and the intergovernmental form. The Treaty of Lisbon, which entered into force on 
1 December 2009, has changed several aspects of the decision- and policy-making processes.
Major changes are outlined where relevant.
Domains of EU competence
The ﬁelds of competences of the EU have been gradually increased, although this was not a linear
and regular process. From an economic orientation focusing on the customs union and the
Common Agricultural Policy (the original focus of the EEC), EU competences have been
expanded to integrate regional, social, environmental, cultural, consumer protection, health and
immigration policies, among others. This expansion of EU competences was enshrined in the
successive revisions of the European treaties. In 1992, the Treaty of Maastricht introduced the
notion of ‘pillar’. Until the Lisbon Treaty entered into force, three pillars formed the basic
structure of the European Union:
•
the ‘Community pillar’, corresponding to the three Communities: the European Community,
the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom) and the former European Coal and
Steel Community (ECSC);

## S. 177

•
the second pillar, devoted to the Common Foreign and Security Policy, which came under
Title V of the EU Treaty;
•
the third pillar, devoted to police and judicial cooperation in criminal matters, which came
under Title VI of the EU Treaty.
The ﬁrst pillar was primarily regulated through the supranational decision-making process, while
the other two were mostly regulated through intergovernmental cooperation. The Treaty of
Lisbon, which entered into force on 1 December 2009, simpliﬁes and uniﬁes the legal structure
of the European Union by abolishing the three-pillar structure.
The possibility for the EU to act on an issue is determined by the competences conferred by
the Treaties and the application of the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality as explained
in Chapter 2. In summary, the EU can only exercise competences that are conferred by the
Treaties. The Treaties do this by expressing a set of key objectives (or goals) for the EU, for
example ‘economic, social and territorial cohesion’. When the Community legislates, it indicates
the articles of the Treaties that give it a legal base for action. There is no objective in the Treaties
in relation to ‘spatial policy’ or ‘spatial planning’, but there are several policy areas that have
spatial implications, as explored in Part V, in particular agriculture, the single market, transport,
economic and social cohesion (regional policy), research, and environment. If the EU does not
have an exclusive competence but shares the competence with the member states, then it must
apply the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality before acting.
The subsidiarity principle asserts that competences should rest at the lowest possible level of
government and should not be ceded upwards to higher levels unless action cannot be effectively
undertaken at the lower levels. This may include policies ‘where state decision could have a
negative impact on a neighbouring state (an “externality”), such as environmental or product
standards’ (Hix 2005: 18–19). The principle of proportionality then determines the extent and
intensity of EU intervention. The principles of subsidiarity and proportionality are used both
in a legal sense, where the EU’s actions are tested by the Courts, and as general norms in political
negotiation about what ﬁelds of action are legitimately the province of the EU. The Treaty 
of Lisbon reinforces the importance and application of the principle of subsidiarity by requiring
a detailed statement with qualitative and/or quantitative justiﬁcation of the need for action at
EU level.
It is important to note that the existence of Treaty provisions for a speciﬁc policy ﬁeld does
not guarantee that policy development will occur in this area (Nugent 2006: 351). For several
decades, for example, there was no development in the Common Transport Policy (see Chapter
19), until pressures from the European Parliament forced the Council of Ministers to take action
in this ﬁeld. Conversely, the lack of provision for a speciﬁc policy area in the Treaties does not
mean that there is no European policy development in this domain (Nugent 2006: 352). This
is because the Treaties state that the EU has general powers for the adoption of measures that
contribute to establishing the common market or to attaining any one of the Treaty objectives.
Many EU actions have been taken on the basis of these general provisions in the past: early EU
environmental legislation was developed in this way, prior to the Single European Act, which
institutionalised the EU competence in this ﬁeld (see Chapter 21). Often the incremental
extension of EU activities into new policy areas through informal actions is translated into an
explicit provision in subsequent treaty revisions that formalises the creation of a Community
competence.
In those areas that are not deﬁned as EU objectives in the Treaties or as being within the remit
of EU legislative competences, European policy developments also arise through non-legislative
actions based on intergovernmental cooperation and the voluntary coordination of national
policies. This is the case in the ﬁelds of Common Foreign and Security Policy and of Cooperation
148
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 178

on Justice and Home Affairs. The emergence and development of spatial planning activities at
EU level has also arisen as a result of intergovernmental cooperation (see Chapter 14). The
development of non-legislative action depends on the agenda and wishes of the member states
and their recognition that acting together in one ﬁeld is better than acting separately.
Over the years, what Hix (2005) refers to as a ‘European constitutional settlement’ has been
established:
whereby the European level of government is responsible for the creation and regulation
of the market (and the related external trade policies); the domestic level of government is
responsible for taxation and redistribution (within constraints agreed at European level);
and the domestic governments are collectively responsible for policies on internal security
(justice and crime) and external security (defence and foreign).
(Hix 2005: 22)
However, there has been increasing consensus, after years of incremental treaty revisions, that
the division of competences between the EU and the member states needs clariﬁcation. One of
the novelties brought about by the Treaty of Lisbon is that it clearly determines the areas of,
delimitation of, and arrangements for the EU to exercise its competences. Instead of a ‘pillar’
structure, the Lisbon Treaty deﬁnes four types of competences for the EU: exclusive EU
competences; shared competences between the EU and the member states; coordination competences; and exclusive competences of the member states (Table 11.2). The Treaty of Lisbon
thus clariﬁes the relations between the EU and its member countries by stipulating who is to
act in which domain.
For each of the policy areas listed in Table 11.2, the scope of and arrangements for exercising
the Union’s competences are determined by the provisions of the Treaties relating to each area,
organised in policy-speciﬁc ‘Titles’ (for example, Title XVI on Trans-European Networks, Title
XVIII on economic, social and territorial cohesion or Title XX on environment).
The supranational law-making process (or ‘Community 
process’)
More than 100 pieces of formal EU legislation are passed every year. EU law, quantitatively,
represents a large proportion of the legislation in place in all member states, directly or
indirectly. It has been estimated that the EU produces over ‘80 percent of the rules governing
the production, distribution and exchange of goods, services and capital in the member states’
markets’ (Hix 2005: 3). It is thus crucial to understand how EU law comes into being. The next
sections brieﬂy introduce the stages in the EU law-making process, starting by deﬁning the forms
of EU legislation, before brieﬂy describing the law-making process from the initiation and
drafting of new legislation, to decision, implementation and control.
The body of EU law includes the Treaties, international agreements, the binding legislative
instruments adopted by the European institutions, and case law (i.e. all the decisions made by
the Court of Justice of the European Communities). New legislation is labelled ‘acts of the
Council’ (even when legislation is jointly approved by the Council and the European Parliament),
and can take the form of regulations, directives, decisions (all three types are binding), as well
as non-binding recommendations or opinions. Regulations are directly binding on all member
states and immediately applicable, which means that no additional implementing legislation is
required (examples are the Structural Funds Regulations; see Chapter 18). Directives are
directly binding on member states but have to be ‘transposed’ into national legislations within
a certain time frame before they take full force. A good example is the Environmental Impact

## S. 180

Assessment Directive of 1985 (Council 1985), which has had a noticeable impact on planning
systems and procedures in EU member states (see Chapter 21). The rationale for using directives
(instead of regulations) is that they state generic objectives and targets but leave a choice of
means to individual member states in terms of implementation and policy instruments. The
beneﬁts of this approach in the ﬁeld of environmental policy are discussed in Chapter 21.
Decisions on a particular matter are binding only for the organisations, ﬁrms or individuals to
which they are addressed. Finally, recommendations and opinions are non-binding and allow
the Council (and other European institutions) to make their views known and suggest a line of
action without imposing any legal obligation.
The so-called ‘Community’ decision-making process (i.e. the decision-making process used
to pass EU legislation) is described in the next section (Figure 11.1 provides an overview of the
process). The decision-making processes used to approve other elements of EU law are not
addressed here: the process for treaty approval and revisions has been addressed brieﬂy in
Chapter 7 (Box 7.1), while external trade agreements are the object of a speciﬁc procedure
whereby they are decided upon by the Commission and the Council with advice from the EP.
Initiation: who proposes and drafts new legislation?
Strictly speaking, according to the EU treaties only the Commission has the power to initiate
and draft new EU legislation. However, the Council of Ministers (or the European Council for
the most important issues) has frequently given policy directions to the Commission (and does
so increasingly). The Parliament can also request the Commission to produce legislative
proposals. Proposals for new EU laws are drafted by the civil servants of the Commission’s
Directorates-General in collaboration with the relevant Commissioner and their cabinet. Various
stakeholders are consulted by the Commission in the process: other DGs, national governments,
the Council of Ministers, the European Parliament, industrial and business lobbies, regional and
local authorities, and special interest groups (see Chapter 10). The Commission, as an
administration, is relatively open to external parties wanting to meet ofﬁcials to voice opinions
in relation to legislation and policies under preparation. This explains the presence of a myriad
of interest groups, lobbies and NGOs in Brussels. The production of the European legislative
agenda is thus a shared arena in which various pressures compete:
As the EU institutions have taken on these powers of government, an increasing number of
groups attempt to make demands on the system – ranging from individual corporations and
business associations to trade unions, environment and consumer and political parties . . .
In the EU the member state governments do not have a monopoly on political demands.
As in all democratic polities, demands in the EU arise from a complex network of public
and private groups, each competing to inﬂuence the EU policy process to promote or protect
their own interests and desires.
(Hix 2005: 3)
The process of drafting a new legislative proposal can take from a few months to several
years. The work of the Commission is supported by a network of expert committees composed
of national ofﬁcials and other experts on a whole range of subjects. In a system known as
‘comitology’, experts and civil servants from national governments scrutinise the Commission’s
implementation measures through a complex system of committees. In 2004 there were 248
separate committees (Nugent 2006: 179), the highest number in the transport, energy, environment, enterprise and agriculture policy ﬁelds. The membership of the committees:

## S. 182

depends on their role: committees composed of national civil servants monitor the
implementation of legislation; temporary committees composed of representatives of private
interest groups consider matters for which the Commission feels wider consultation is
necessary; and committees composed of scientists and experts give advice on technical issues.
(Hix 2005: 52)
The comitology system allows ‘the Commission and national experts [to] work together to solve
policy issues in a non hierarchical and deliberative policy style’ (Hix 2005: 58), which, however,
does not always prevent conﬂict on high-proﬁle issues between the Commission and national
experts from different member states.
The EP has criticised the comitology system on many occasions for its lack of transparency
due to the closed nature of committee proceedings (Hix 2005). This complex system was clariﬁed
by two Council decisions – in 1987 (1987/373/EEC) and in 1999 (1999/468/EC) – which deﬁned
three types of committee: advisory, management and regulatory. To each type corresponds a
set of procedures establishing the degree of inﬂuence of the committees’ decisions over the Commission. Advisory committees can only advise the Commission, which has to take the ‘utmost
account’ of the committee’s opinion but can bypass it; management committees can block
Commission decisions; while under the ‘regulatory procedure’ (which in 2000 applied to 50 per
cent of the committees) measures have to be approved by the committee or, if not, sent to the
Council. In practice less than 1 per cent of the up to 3,000 legal instruments presented to
committees every year are referred (Nugent 2006).
Legislative proposals, once approved by the College of Commissioners, take the form of
‘Communications from the Commission’. In addition the Commission can also produce White
Papers, which contain a set of proposals or an overarching strategy for EU action in a particular
ﬁeld (e.g. transport policy; see Chapter 19) and Green Papers, which are intended to stimulate
thinking and launch consultation at European level on a particular subject. The ‘Green Paper
on Territorial Cohesion’ published by the Commission in the autumn of 2008 (CEC 2008c) is
an example of a document aimed at stimulating debate and thinking about future policy
developments in a ﬁeld of particular relevance for the themes addressed in this book. A White
or a Green Paper can lead to an EU action programme (AP) and to legislation in the ﬁeld
concerned if it gains the support of the Council.
Who decides on proposed legislation?
Since the Treaty of Amsterdam, the Council and the European Parliament are the two legislative
bodies of the EU. The EP was initially a consultative body but the Single European Act of 1986,
the Maastricht Treaty (1992) and the Amsterdam Treaty (1997) considerably expanded its
powers. Once a legislative proposal has been approved by the Commission, it is passed on to
the Council where it is discussed in the COREPER (the Permanent Representatives Committee),
which tries to clear political problems in working groups of national experts. The Commission
in its role as facilitator can exercise considerable inﬂuence. In this process, pressure groups can
play a significant role and the ‘social partners’ (represented in the Economic and Social
Committee) and local and regional authorities (represented in the Committee of the Regions)
are consulted in a number of ﬁelds, particularly in those detailed in Part V of the book. The
proposal is then put forward to the relevant Council of Ministers, which can adopt, amend or
reject the proposal. A number of factors determine the progress of a proposal through the
Council machinery (Nugent 2006: 207), in particular its urgency, controversial character and
‘compromise potential’.

Decision-making and policy-making in the EU
153
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 183

The Treaties deﬁne the cases when the Council acts at a simple majority of its members, at
a qualiﬁed majority or unanimously. A simple majority is used for procedural rules and
Common Commercial Policy. Unanimity is used for issues of constitutional importance, such
as amendments to the Treaties or accession of a new member state, and for a small number of
sensitive policy areas such as foreign policy, defence, judicial and police cooperation and
taxation. The ‘qualiﬁed majority vote’ was introduced by the Single European Act in 1986. It
is based on a system whereby each minister in the Council is given several votes in proportion
to the population of his member state. A qualiﬁed majority is a minimum number of votes set
in the Treaties. Qualiﬁed majority vote is used for decisions in a majority of policy ﬁelds, for
example, Cohesion Policy, environment policy, the internal market, economic affairs and trade,
agriculture, transport, competition, public health and consumer protection. In those ﬁelds the
introduction of qualiﬁed majority voting has reinforced the supranational operation of the EU,
as a majority of member states can override the opposition of a minority of member states and
vote legislation that will be binding on all parties.
Since 1 January 2007, in the Council of Ministers a qualiﬁed majority is reached if a majority
of member states approve the proposal (by a two-thirds majority) and if a minimum of 255 votes
is cast in favour of the proposal out of a total of 345 votes. In addition a member state may
ask for confirmation that the votes in favour represent at least 62 per cent of the total
population of the Union. The Treaties set out the number of votes that each member state has
in the Council (Table 11.3). This number of votes broadly (but not only) reﬂects the size of each
country’s population. This voting ‘weight’ has been the subject of difﬁcult negotiations and
bargaining following the institutional reforms that accompanied successive EU enlargements and
Treaty revisions.
The Treaty of Lisbon, ratiﬁed in 2009, introduces a new voting method for 2014, called
‘double majority voting’. Proposed EU laws will require a majority of the member states 
(55 per cent, i.e. 15 countries) but also a majority of the EU population (65 per cent). This will
be accompanied by a new mechanism enabling a small number of member governments (close
to a blocking minority, one-third of member states or 25 per cent of the EU population) to
demonstrate their opposition to a decision.
After the Council adopts a common position, there are four standard procedures determining
the fate of the proposed legislation. These procedures correspond to the degree of involvement of the European Parliament in the legislative process: on certain subjects it has only a
consultative role and cannot block a Council decision; on others it shares full legislative powers
with the Council and can amend or block a Council decision. The range of policy areas in which

## S. 184

the European Parliament now adopts legislation jointly with the Council has been expanded
signiﬁcantly since the 1986 Single European Act.
The four procedures (in ascending order of importance of the EP’s control power) are
consultation, cooperation, co-decision and assent. Under the consultation procedure, the opinion
of the EP is required before a Commission’s proposal can be adopted by the Council of
Ministers, but the Council can ignore the EP’s opinion. The use of the consultation procedure
has steadily declined over the past two decades. Under the cooperation procedure, established
by the SEA in 1986, the Commission’s proposals are subject to a ﬁrst reading in the EP, which
may propose amendments. At the second reading the EP can amend the Council’s position or
reject it at an absolute majority. But the Council can, acting unanimously, disregard the
opinion of the Parliament. So under this procedure the ﬁnal decision lies with the Council alone.
The amendments made by the EP, however, had a real inﬂuence in the 1990s on legislation in
the ﬁeld of health and safety at work and environmental policy (Hix 2005). Since the Treaty of
Amsterdam of 1997, the cooperation procedure applies to fewer policy areas, as many areas
previously subject to this procedure now fall under the co-decision procedure.
Under the co-decision procedure (introduced by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1992) the
decision-making power is shared equally between the EP and the Council of Ministers. Any
proposed legislation must be adopted by the EP and the Council. In the event of disagreement,
a conciliation committee made up of representatives of the Council and of Parliament has to
reach a compromise text acceptable to the two institutions. If no agreement is found, the EP
can reject the proposal and the Council cannot adopt a common position. Since 1997 the 
co-decision procedure has become the most widely used procedure and applies to most areas of
EU legislation, including those of relevance to the themes addressed in this book: transport, the
internal market, trans-European networks, economic and social cohesion and the environment.
Finally, there are certain matters on which the Council cannot legislate unless Parliament gives
its consent (or ‘assent’) by absolute majority of its members. This assent procedure requires 
the EP to give in one single vote its approval. It applies to fundamental decisions, such as the
accession of new member states, and to certain international agreements. The Council and the
European Parliament are additionally jointly responsible for adopting the EU budget.
Implementation
EU law has ‘direct effect’, which means it applies to citizens, legal entities and states. If the 
EU legislation adopted is a directive, however, it ﬁrst has to be transposed into national law by
the member states. For example, the 1985 Directive on Environmental Impact Assessment
entered into force in 1988 and was transposed in the years that followed into national legislation in all EU countries, often through planning legislation. It is now a routine procedure 
in the assessment of applications for developments of a certain size in EU member states (see
Chapter 21). The transposition of EU directives and the implementation of EU legislation 
is, however, a complex process in which member states have some discretion. This has led to
very uneven degrees of transposition and/or compliance in the member states, prompting
critiques of an ‘implementation deﬁcit’ in speciﬁc ﬁelds such as EU environmental policy
(discussed in Chapter 21).
The European Commission is responsible for monitoring whether a directive has been
transposed into national legislation within a certain time frame, and if it has been transposed
completely and correctly. If this is not the case, the Commission can challenge a member state
in front of the Court of Justice of the EC. Additionally the Commission is the executive body
in charge of implementing EU decisions. In performing this role the Commission has a degree
of discretion in the interpretation of the legislation since it has the power to supplement EU

Decision-making and policy-making in the EU

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 185

legislation approved by the Council and the EP by detailed technical and administrative
legislation (the so-called ‘Commission legislation’). Because it is not possible for EU legislation
to cover all areas and eventualities, under conditions deﬁned by the treaties the Commission
possesses delegated rule-making powers that allow it to make quick decisions in administrative
and technical law (areas ‘where policy overlaps with administration’ [Nugent 2006: 171–2])
without a vote by the Council and the EP. This ‘Commission legislation’, primarily applying to
the Common Agricultural Policy and to EU competition policy, represented in the past up to
4,000 pieces of legislation a year, but since the late 1990s has considerably decreased in number
(in 2004 it represented 672 regulations, 408 decision and 59 directives) (Nugent 2006).
Control
The judicial control over the application of EU law lies in the hands of the Court of Justice of
the European Communities (see Chapter 9), which is in charge of ensuring that EU law is
interpreted and implemented in line with the EU Treaties. It increasingly makes wide-ranging
judgments that constitute binding case law. Citizens can directly call upon the application of
EU law before their national courts.
Financial controls are carried out by the European Court of Auditors, and to a certain extent
by the European Parliament. All EU expenditure is subject to audit and evaluation by the
European Court of Auditors and to investigation by the EU’s independent fraud ofﬁce if there
is suspected fraud. The Court of Auditors can verify the proper use of European public funding
by carrying out spot checks in any EU-funded programme or project.
A specific case: decision over the EU budget
The EU budget funds the expenditure of the EU Institutions and EU policies (see Part V). It is
negotiated between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the basis of a
proposal by the Commission. The annual scrutiny of expenditure plans starts every spring when
the European Commission makes proposals for the following ﬁnancial year. The proposals are
debated by the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers – negotiating with each other
about revisions and ﬁne-tuning – before the budget is ﬁnally adopted in December each year.
The budget is approved if three-ﬁfths of the members of the European Parliament vote in favour
of it. The ﬁnal outcome has to be a balanced budget (deﬁcits are not allowed). Each year’s 
budget falls within a seven-year ‘ﬁnancial framework’ (currently running from 2007 to 2013).
Under the Treaty of Nice the Parliament has the last word on half of the individual items 
of EU expenditure (Cohesion Policy, the environment, research and education programmes). 
The ministers have the ﬁnal say on agricultural expenditure and on decisions affecting justice,
freedom, citizenship and security. The Treaty of Lisbon, ratified in 2009, abolishes the
distinction between these two categories and gives equal powers to the EP and Council in the
vote over all elements of the budget.
The EU budget for the year 2007 was EUR 126 billion (EUR 0.70 per day per EU resident).
This is roughly 1 per cent of the EU GDP, which is signiﬁcantly lower than the total amount
of public spending in many EU member states. In 2007, the total expenditure of the governments
in the eurozone was 46.3 per cent of the GDP for this area (CEC 2008t). So the scale of the EU
budget in relation to the EU’s tasks is very small. The EU budget is ﬁnanced from three sources.
Contributions of the member states, based on a uniform rate applied to the gross national
income of each EU country, make up 69 per cent. Adjustments are made if this seems to be
producing an excessive burden on some member states (the UK has since 1984 beneﬁted from
a rebate on its contribution). About 15 per cent of the EU budget comes from customs duties,
156
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 186

agricultural levies and sugar levies, which are imposed on imports into the EU. The last 15 per
cent of the budget come from a ﬁxed proportion of the value added tax (VAT) that member
states collect. Finally, 1 per cent of the budget comes from unspent amounts from previous years.
The intergovernmental policy-making process
Many policy domains are not subject to European legislation but to intergovernmental
cooperation, for example, in the areas of Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) and
cooperation on Justice and Home Affairs (JHA) (the second and third pillar). In those ﬁelds,
member states retain the competence for action and have not agreed to transfer their exclusive
competence to the EU. However, cooperation between national policies or other forms of joint
action between governments can be decided upon by the Council usually by unanimity. In these
areas, the instruments of action of the Council are joint actions, decisions, framework decisions
and common positions. A joint action (in the area of CFSP) is an intergovernmental legal
instrument adopted by the Council unanimously or, in certain cases, by a qualiﬁed majority. 
It is binding on the member states that have agreed to it, and their governments have to achieve
the objectives set unless major difficulties arise. Decisions and framework decisions are
intergovernmental instruments that have been used in the ﬁeld of police and judicial cooperation in criminal matters since the Treaty of Amsterdam. They are adopted by the Council of 
the European Union unanimously on the initiative of the Commission or a member state. A
framework decision is binding on the member states with regard to the result to be achieved
but give the national authorities the choice of form and methods. Finally a common position is
an intergovernmental legal instrument adopted unanimously by the Council that determines the
Union’s approach to particular questions of foreign and security policy or police and judicial
cooperation and gives guidance for the pursuit of national policies in these ﬁelds. For example,
in 2003, the EU member states did not succeed in reaching a common position on the invasion
of Iraq by US troops and the war in Iraq.
One additional method of intergovernmental coordination that has gained prominence in the
past decade is the so-called ‘Open Method of Coordination’ (OMC), whereby the member states
agree on a set of goals to be achieved independently of EU legal instruments (see also Chapter
15). The member states’ domestic policies are then collectively monitored by EU institutions in
the framework of broad policy guidelines set by the Council (for example, in the ﬁeld of
employment, economic policies, research and education) (Hix 2005: 37). The monitoring
process helps to ‘name and shame’ those lagging behind in terms of progress towards those goals
(Hix 2005: 247). ‘Warnings’ can be sent to member states that deviate from the set objectives,
although policy guidelines are not binding.
The process of intergovernmental cooperation has increasingly been used to develop policy
in domains that do not formally belong to the EU competences spelled out in the Treaties.
European spatial planning is a good example. It has since its early days been based on intergovernmental initiatives and cooperation (Chapter 14). It is interesting to note, however, that
in the past speciﬁc policies developed through intergovernmental cooperation were often subsequently formalised into EU competences: since the 1990s there has been some debate as to
whether this might be the case in future for European spatial planning (see Chapters 2 and 14).
In certain cases, intergovernmental cooperation has allowed a small number of member 
states to move forward by agreeing on common policies or integrating measures in certain 
ﬁelds without the full participation of all EU member states. In other words, in a process 
of intergovernmental cooperation individual member states are not obliged to agree, and
follow, a particular decision made by other member states in a cooperative way. A case in point
is that of Economic and Monetary Union (EMU) and the introduction of a common currency

Decision-making and policy-making in the EU
157
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 187

by 11 member states in 2002 (Chapter 17). The UK and Denmark opted out of EMU. Consequently, intergovernmental cooperation has allowed the development of a ‘Europe of variable
geometry’ in various domains: the EMU, the Social Charter and the Schengen Agreements are
examples of this. This process of ‘enhanced cooperation’, formalised in the Treaty of Amsterdam
in 1997 under very strict conditions, has allowed those countries of the Union that wish to
continue to work more closely together to do so, without an extension of the EU powers as laid
down by the Treaties. The concept of an EU in which subsets of states decide to cooperate and
integrate more (or faster) than others, leading to different levels of integration, has been given
different names: ‘variable geometry’, ‘differentiated integration’, ‘closer (or enhanced)
cooperation’, ‘concentric circles’, ‘Europe à la carte’, and ‘two-speed’ or ‘multi-speed’ Europe.
Supporters of such ‘variable geometries’ can either be integrationists wishing to deepen European
integration, possibly with only a small core of countries, or on the contrary, countries eager to
slow down the integration process to protect their national interests, but nevertheless willing to
allow others to move forward.
Conclusion
There is not one EU policy-making model but a combination of two main models with several
procedural variants, as this chapter has shown. Alongside the supranational or ‘Community
method’, which was developed for the CAP and Customs Union in the early days of the EU,
other models of policy-making and regulation emerged: an EU regulatory model (for the single
market), an EU distributional model (for Cohesion Policy), a ‘soft policy cooperation’ model
(through benchmarking and the OMC), and intensive ‘intergovernmentalism’ in ﬁelds such as
Economic and Monetary Union and security policy (Wallace 2005: 29). This brief overview of
policy-making processes in the EU can help to view EU institutions ‘not as external agencies
imposing their views on unwilling national authorities but rather as frameworks or networks
in which national actors attempt to coordinate their interests and policy preferences’ (Nugent
2006: 549), even if in the supranational decision-making process individual member states can
be overridden in certain cases.
Further reading
A good overview of the decision- and policy-making processes in the EU is provided by Hix
2005, Bomberg and Stubb 2002, Wallace and Wallace 2005, Cini 2006 and Nugent 2006. All
ofﬁcial texts of EU law (in all EU languages) are available online on the EUR-LEX website:
http://eur-lex.europa.eu.
158
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 188

Widening, deepening and 
broadening the EU
Past experiences and future prospects
The task ahead of us will be anything but easy and will require all our strength; in the coming
decade, we will have to enlarge the EU to the east and south-east, and this will, in the end, mean
a doubling in the number of members. And at the same time, if we are to be able to meet this historic
challenge and integrate the new Member States without substantially denting the EU’s capacity for
action, we must put into place the last brick in the building of European integration, namely political
integration. The need to organise these two processes in parallel is undoubtedly the biggest
challenge the Union has faced since its creation.
(Fischer 2003)
Introduction
The EU is not a static system; it has been in constant evolution since its creation. This chapter
examines the issues and challenges arising from the geographical ‘widening’ of the EU, on 
the one hand, and from the ‘deepening and broadening’ of its institutions, competences 
and policies, on the other. These processes are interlinked: successive enlargements have 
affected the functioning of the EU institutions and the balance of competences, power and 
policy concerns within the EU (Nugent 2006; Wallace 2005). First, the 2004 and 2007 enlargements are brieﬂy presented, with particular emphasis on the challenges that the enlargement
process posed for EU institutions and policies and for territorial development and spatial
planning in the old and new member states. The ﬁrst section ends with a brief discussion of 
the prospects for future enlargement(s) of the EU. The second section analyses the institutional
issues that the EU as a political system has been facing, in particular the debates surrounding
the need for EU institutional reforms, and the changes brought about by the Treaty of Lisbon
signed in 2007, which entered into force on 1 December 2009.
‘Widening’: the challenges of geographical enlargement, 
past and future
Since its foundation in 1957 by six founding countries – France, Germany, Italy, Belgium,
Luxembourg and The Netherlands – the EU has known six successive enlargements. In 1973
the United Kingdom, Denmark and Ireland joined the (then) EC, followed by Greece in 1980,
Spain and Portugal in 1986, and Austria, Finland and Sweden in 1995. The biggest single
enlargement took place in 2004 with the accession of ten states from central and eastern Europe
(Poland, Hungary, Slovenia, the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Estonia, Lithuania, Latvia, Malta and
Cyprus), followed in 2007 by Bulgaria and Romania, leading to an EU of 27 member states in
2009 (Figure 1.1).

## S. 189

The EU enlargement process
The 2004 and 2007 ‘eastern’ enlargement of the EU1 was prepared for during more than a decade
of complex negotiations and procedures aimed at bringing the candidate countries into line with
the EU requirements for accession. Following the breakdown of the Union of Soviet Socialist
Republics (USSR) in 1991, the collapse of communist regimes in central and eastern European countries (CEECs), the fall of the Iron Curtain that had divided Europe for 40 years and
the reuniﬁcation of Germany in 1990, the EU heads of state and government made a historic
decision at the Copenhagen European Council in 1993, stating that the countries in central and 
eastern Europe that so desired could become members of the Union. The overarching aim 
was the achievement of political stability in the continent of Europe after two World Wars and
40 years of Cold War division, as well as the strengthening of Europe as a major economic power
on the global stage in the face of competition from the United States, Japan, China, India and
other south-east Asian economies. Yet between the early 1990s and the mid-2000s, there were
intense debates on the beneﬁts and costs of EU enlargement within the EU-15 member states as
well as within the candidate countries. These debates were fuelled by a variety of (sometimes
contradictory) arguments and speculations about the potential impacts of the enlargement process. The main arguments for and against the 2004 and 2007 EU enlargements are summarised
in Table 12.1.
At the Copenhagen European Council of 1993 the decision was made that the accession of
a new member state to the EU would take place as soon as the candidate country would be able
to assume the obligations of membership by satisfying three economic and political conditions
– the so-called ‘Copenhagen criteria’. These criteria have since then been applied to all candidate
countries for EU membership:
•
First, EU membership requires a candidate country to have achieved stability of institutions,
guaranteeing democracy, the rule of law, human rights and the respect for and protection
of minorities.
•
Second, a candidate country needs to have a functioning market economy in which prices,
as well as trade, are liberalised and in which an enforceable legal system, including property
rights, is in place.
•
Third, a candidate country needs to demonstrate the capacity to withstand competitive
pressure and market forces within the Union, which requires a stable macro-economic
framework and predictability, a sufﬁcient amount of human and physical infrastructure, and
the restructuring of state enterprise and ﬁrm efﬁciency.
The model of integration embedded in these three principles is a west European model that
presumes viable states, mixed market economies and pluralist democracies (Wallace 2005).
Following the Copenhagen European Council of 1993, 13 countries submitted an application
for EU membership to the Council (ten CEECs plus Cyprus, Malta and Turkey). The Commission has been in charge of assessing each candidate country’s ability to meet the three conditions
of membership. A candidate country will join the EU only when it is deemed to have met all
requirements of the Commission, when the unanimous consent of the governments of the EU
member states in the Council has been secured, when the European Parliament has given its
assent, and when the ‘accession treaty’ is ratiﬁed in the acceding country.
Historically all the enlargements of the EU have been based on the principle that it is up 
to the candidate countries to make unilateral adjustments towards the adoption of the laws, 
rules and practices of the EU. In the case of the eastern enlargement, the EU insisted that 
adjustments by candidate countries should be made ‘pre-accession’ rather than ‘post-accession’
(as had mostly been the case in previous EU enlargements such as the Mediterranean ones of

## S. 191

1980 and 1986). The eastern enlargement was thus characterised by a very tough conditionality
(Wallace 2005: 26), whereby the candidate countries were required to have created the conditions for their integration into the EU by adapting their administrative and judicial structures
prior to accession. This is why it is only in 2001 that a ‘roadmap’ setting precise target dates
for the accession of the majority of candidate countries was drawn up. The EU pre-accession
strategy encompassed a number of instruments: ‘association agreements’, accession partnerships,
national programmes for the adoption of EU legislation, EU ﬁnancial assistance channelled
through speciﬁc structural programmes, and participation in EU initiatives and agencies.
The ‘Europe agreements’, later renamed ‘association agreements’, were trade agreements
signed between 1991 and 1998 between the EU and the CEECs in order to gradually bring 
the CEECs into a free trade area with the EU and commit them to adopt competition policies
compatible with those of the Single Market. The formal accession negotiations only began 
in 1998 with a number of candidate countries, and the ﬁrst wave of negotiations was completed by the end of 2002. Negotiations took the shape of bilateral discussions between the 
EU (led by the Commission) and each candidate country, organised in 31 ‘chapters’ or speciﬁc
policy ﬁelds. The negotiations focused on the terms under which candidate countries should
adopt, implement and enforce the ‘acquis communautaire’, a French legal term used to refer 
to the body of EU legislation and regulations collectively applying to all EU member states. 
The accession negotiations were complex, difﬁcult and sometimes characterised by dispute, in
particular regarding issues such as the free movement of workers or the future level of ﬁnancial
aid to be granted by the EU to new member states under agricultural and cohesion policies.
Transitional measures were negotiated by several candidate countries in speciﬁc ﬁelds, whereby
the requirement for the full application of the acquis communautaire was delayed for a speciﬁed
period. Yet on the whole, candidate countries were required to transform very rapidly to adopt
the full EU acquis over the period of a few years, and had to ‘prove a propensity to “EU-ise”
by adopting, importing and embedding EU policy and institutional templates’ (Wallace 2005:
31). This was a hugely challenging task that was monitored annually by the Commission
through reports on the progress made by individual countries in the gradual adoption and implementation of the acquis. However, ‘transposition’ of EU law does not necessarily mean effective
implementation (see Chapter 11).
Candidate countries were assisted by EU technical and financial assistance channelled
through speciﬁc pre-accession programmes. The EU began to provide ﬁnancial assistance to
central European countries in 1990 through a programme named PHARE (standing for ‘Poland
Hungary Assistance in the Restructuring of Economies’), which was gradually expanded to cover
13 candidate countries. The PHARE programme aimed to strengthen public administrations and
institutions in the candidate countries and support their adoption of the EU acquis through
capacity building, twinning and technical assistance. A speciﬁc strand was created in 1994 to
support cross-border cooperation at the external borders of the EU-15 in the fields of
infrastructure (transport and environment) and economic development: the PHARE Central
European Countries and the Baltic States Cross-border Cooperation Programme (PHARE-CBC).
In 2000 the budget allocated by the EU to pre-accession aid was doubled to over EUR 3 billion
per year. Two new funding programmes were set up for the period 2000–06: ISPA and
SAPARD. ISPA (Instrument Structurel de Pré-adhésion – Pre-Accession Structural Instrument),
with a budget of over EUR 1 billion per year, aimed at enabling candidate countries to meet
EU environmental standards (e.g. through the provision of waste water treatment plants) and
improve their transport infrastructure. The SAPARD programme (Structural Adjustment
Programme for Agriculture and Rural Development), with an allocation of over EUR 500
million per year, supported measures to modernise the agricultural sector and promote rural
development in candidate countries. PHARE, ISPA and SAPARD all aimed at building
162
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 192

institutional capacity within the candidate countries to prepare them for the administrative and
ﬁnancial management of the Structural Funds and agricultural subsidies that they would start
receiving after accession. When candidate countries joined the EU in 2004 and 2007, funding
under the above mentioned programmes ceased and these countries became eligible for the
Structural and Cohesion Funds (see Chapter 18) and for payments under the Common
Agricultural Policy (Chapter 20). In addition to pre-accession programmes, European and
international financial institutions such as the European Bank for Reconstruction and
Development and the European Investment Bank have helped ﬁnance large-scale infrastructure
projects in the candidate countries before and after accession.
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, while candidate countries had to adapt their economic,
political, administrative and judicial systems as a condition for EU accession, the 15 member
states of the EU were busy preparing the reforms of the EU budget, policies and institutions that
were necessary to deal with the forthcoming enlargement. The unprecedented scale of the 2004
enlargement (ten new countries) prompted controversial political debates on the budgetary,
policy and institutional reforms that were needed. In 1999, at the Berlin European Council, the
EU heads of state and government agreed on a programme of reforms called Agenda 2000,
which deﬁned the ﬁnancial framework for the period 2000–06 in light of the forthcoming
enlargement, following long political negotiations over the reform of the Common Agricultural
Policy and of EU Cohesion Policy (see Chapters 18 and 20). At the Nice European Council in
2000, a set of institutional reforms perceived as necessary prior to the 2004 enlargement were
subsequently agreed upon (see section on ‘Deepening and broadening’ later in the chapter)
(European Council 2000b).
Spatial planning and territorial development challenges arising
from enlargement
An EU of 27 member states, as opposed to 15, has increased the land area by 34 per cent and
the population by over 30 per cent but increased GDP by only 10 per cent. This is due to the
much lower levels of GDP per capita in the accession countries. The EU with its 27 member
states now has the third largest population in the world after China and India (almost half a
billion inhabitants) and produces one-third of the world’s GDP. The recent enlargements of the
EU have raised a number of spatial planning and territorial development challenges for the EU
as a whole, for the ‘old’ EU-15 member states and for the accession countries themselves. These
are considered in the next sections.
Impact of enlargement on EU policies
Prior to EU accession the CEECs had a per capita GDP much lower than that of the average
EU-15 (see Figure 4.1 and Plate 2). The level of regional inequalities in the enlarged EU
consequently increased signiﬁcantly. Between 1995 and 2005, however, GDP growth rates
varied markedly between the candidate countries, with some countries growing particularly fast
at 7–8 per cent a year – for example, the three Baltic States doubled their GDP per head in 
real terms in ten years – but with Bulgaria and Romania showing a 5–6 per cent annual 
growth rate between 2001 and 2006 (CEC 2007b: 4). At such rates it would take another 
20 years for the GDP per head of these two countries to reach 75 per cent of the EU average
(CEC 2007b: 3).
Enlargement consequently posed a huge challenge for the future balanced spatial development
of the EU territory, and for the role of EU policies therein – in particular, cohesion and
agricultural policies. Most of the political discussions at EU level in the run-up to the 2004

Widening, deepening and broadening the EU
163
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 193

enlargement therefore focused on the budgetary implications of enlarging the EU. If the criteria
used for the allocation of structural and cohesion funding prior to 2004 were not changed,
virtually all regions in the CEECs would have fallen below the threshold of 75 per cent of the
EU GDP that deﬁned eligibility for Objective 1 funding. At the same time many EU-15 regions
previously below the 75 per cent threshold in the EU-15 (e.g. Spain, southern Italy, northern
Greece, eastern Germany, Wallonia and Scotland) would suddenly stand well above this
threshold in relative terms in an enlarged Europe with poorer new members, due to the so-called
‘statistical effect’ arising from the lowering of the average GDP in the enlarged EU. Without a
reform of the eligibility criteria, the budget for EU cohesion and agricultural policies would have
increased signiﬁcantly as a result of enlargement. This, for obvious reasons, was unacceptable
to most EU-15 member states. The reforms of EU cohesion and agricultural policies were
consequently the object of long and difﬁcult negotiations between 1997 and 2006 (Bachtler and
Wishlade 2004, 2005). The reformed EU Cohesion Policy that was agreed upon for the period
2007–13 represents a compromise between the interests of the ‘net donor countries’ of western
Europe, those of the southern European states (until 2006 net beneﬁciaries of EU funds) and
those of the new member states (Grabbe and Hughes 1998) (see Chapter 18 for an overview of
EU Structural and Cohesion funding post-2007).
Impacts of enlargement on the EU-15 member states
Prior to 2004 there was much uncertainty with regard to the potential impacts of the eastern
enlargement on the economy and territorial development patterns of the EU-15 member states.
This was, in part, due to the methodological difﬁculties in forecasting and spatially locating the
impacts of enlargement on patterns of investment, business and industrial relocation and
migration in Europe. The political leaders of the regions bordering the eastern frontier of the
then EU-15 in Germany, Austria, Greece, Italy and Finland were particularly concerned about
the impact on their territory of increased migration levels from east to west and of increased
competition from the labour force and ﬁrms of the accession countries. Prior to enlargement
these regions lobbied for special EU support, arguing that they would be on the front line of
economic and territorial restructuring. The European Commission recognised that in the short
term EU border regions would need extra support, in particular since many of them (for
example, the Greek border regions and the East German Länder) lagged behind the EU average
in economic and infrastructural terms (CEC 2001c: 3). In the period 2000–06, a total of EUR
16 billion was made available for Objective 1 and 2 border areas in Germany, Austria, Greece,
Italy and Finland. The Commission made a series of proposals to better coordinate EU actions
in those border regions (for example, by aligning the priority themes of the PHARE-CBC
programme and of INTERREG IIIA programmes) and proposed an additional EUR 195 million
for EU-15 border regions for the period 2001–06 (CEC 2001c).
In other parts of the EU-15, the regions that had been the net beneﬁciaries of structural and
cohesion funding since the late 1980s, primarily in Spain, Italy, Portugal, Greece and Ireland,
became highly concerned about the likely diversion of such funds away from them towards new
central and eastern European member states. The Agenda 2000 agreement signed in 1999 at
the European Council (deﬁning a ﬁnancial framework for the 2000–06 period) and later on the
guidelines for EU Cohesion Policy for the 2007–13 period represent a compromise whereby
many of these regions will still beneﬁt from some form of EU funding, albeit at a lower rate or
for a transitional period of six years only (see Chapter 18).
It is still too early to draw solid conclusions on the long-term impact of the 2004 and 2007
enlargements on the economic geography and territorial development of the EU-15 countries
164
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 194

as a whole. It is worth stressing, however, that some of the most dramatic trends and processes
that had been forecast and feared by some commentators, politicians and part of the electorate
prior to 2004 did not happen to the extent that had been foreseen (see Chapter 4). Patterns of
population migration from eastern to western Europe are a prime example. Since one of the basic
principles of the EU is the free movement of citizens and workers across the national borders
of its member states, huge migration waves were foreseen after enlargement due to the signiﬁcant
differences in living standards between western and eastern European countries. Estimates
predicted by various studies in the second half of the 1990s for the movement of people to
western Europe from the eastern European candidate countries within a period of 10 to 15 years
ranged from 5 to 50 million people (Krieger 2004). A Commission study (CEC 2001d) and a
study by the European Foundation for the Improvement of Living and Working Conditions
(Krieger 2004) both predicted an increase of around 1.1 million migrants from candidate countries in the EU-15 within the ﬁrst ﬁve years after enlargement. The Commission study (2001d:
105) predicted that two-thirds of migrants would aim for Germany, 10 per cent for Austria,
less than 5 per cent for Italy and the UK and 3 per cent for Sweden.
Such forecasts led several EU-15 member states to lobby for (and obtain) the possibility of
setting temporary restrictions over the free movements of workers from the accession countries
for a transitional period of up to seven years following enlargement. Eleven countries set up 
such restrictions, four of which (Finland, Greece, Portugal and Spain) removed them in 2006.
Denmark, Ireland, Sweden and the UK did not set up any restrictions on incoming migrant
workers from the new EU member states. These measures have consequently directed migrations ﬂows from central and eastern European workers away from ‘traditional’ destinations
(Germany, Italy, Austria) towards other countries, in particular the UK, Ireland, Denmark and
Sweden. Barrell et al. (2007) estimate that between 2004 and the end of 2006, the percentage
of residents coming from the new EU member states increased by 265 per cent in the UK, 
63 per cent in Ireland, 83 per cent in Germany and 38.5 per cent in Italy. In the UK ofﬁcial
ﬁgures for the ﬁrst two years that followed the accession in May 2004 estimated the number
of people given the right to live and/or work in the UK from the new EU member countries at
above 600,000 (Wheeler 2006). This is the largest single wave of in-migration ever experienced,
according to the UK Ofﬁce for National Statistics.
Altogether, however, the overall volume of east-west migrations after the EU 2004 and 2007
enlargements has been less signiﬁcant than predicted by some. Four factors have been limiting
migrations (Krieger 2004). First, a signiﬁcant proportion of those wishing to migrate away from
the accession countries had moved to EU-15 member states before EU enlargement. Second,
demographic trends in the new member states point towards a decreasing population in the next
30 to 40 years, resulting in more employment chances for younger and better-educated people.
Third, EU ﬁnancial transfers to new member states and foreign direct investment slowly
stimulate the economic development process and help create jobs, retaining or attracting the
workforce. Finally, ‘contrary to the predictions of economic theory, European employees have
scarcely reacted to the opportunity of free movement within a common labour market’ (Krieger
2004: 4). In recent years – and in particular with the global ﬁnancial and economic downturn
that began in 2008 – there has been increasing evidence that a large proportion of all east-west
intra-EU migrations are of a temporary nature. There have been strong signs of ‘return
migration’ as the economic and social conditions improve in the new member states and as the
exchange rates ﬂuctuate (for example, between the British sterling pound and the Polish s∏oty),
making it less attractive for central and eastern European workers to work in western European
countries. The likely scenario predicted by the European Commission prior to enlargement seems
to have been a realistic one: ‘one in which labour mobility will be moderate to limited and will,

Widening, deepening and broadening the EU
