## S. 195

after a likely short upsurge, just after the accession period . . . start declining again to fall below
100,000 persons per year before the end of the decade’ (Krieger 2004: 1).
This is not to say that in-migration patterns have not raised particular challenges in speciﬁc
regions of the EU-15 in terms of labour markets, public service provision, housing pressures and
social relations. The impact of migration from central and eastern European workers and citizens
on the economy, regions and cities of countries such as the UK (Poles) and Spain (Romanians)
has been highly visible (Samuels 2008). In both countries migrants have ﬁlled shortages in the
labour market and contributed positively to the growth of the economy (prior to 2008). At the
same time concentrations of new migrants in some areas have generated local pressures on
housing, infrastructure and public services, which local authorities (in particular their housing,
planning, education and health services) have had to respond to at short notice and without
adequate funding or support from central government. Yet in some areas that suffered from
demographic decline, the arrival of new migrants has brought about positive beneﬁts: in Spain,
villages threatened by depopulation and youth drain have been given a new life by migrant
families from Romania, largely encouraged by local politicians (Lungescu 2006). Perhaps the
biggest social and economic challenges raised by east-west migrations in the enlarged EU are
therefore not to be found in the countries to which migrant workers travel, but in the central
and eastern European cities, regions and countries that have been losing population, in particular
their young and skilled workforce (Krieger 2004: 2).
Spatial planning and territorial development issues in the new EU 
member states
The countries that joined the EU in 2004 and 2007 vary enormously in their geography and
spatial structures, population size, state of the economy and ethnic composition. Yet in all the
new member states of central and eastern Europe, integration into the Single European 
Market has intensiﬁed the processes of economic restructuring and liberalisation that had 
been unleashed during the abrupt transition from a state socialist to a free market economy in
the early part of the 1990s. This transition entailed, to a varying degree, the creation of new
political and administrative structures, the privatisation of productive means, of land ownership
and of housing (which had in many cases been nationalised after the Second World War), 
the conversion and decline of heavy industry, the transformation of structures of agricultural
production, the reorientation of trade patterns towards western Europe, the opening up to
foreign investment, and so on. Processes of economic restructuring, accompanied by a transformation of the role of the state and the dismantling of socialist systems of welfare provision,
led to massive falls in outputs, increasing rates of unemployment and rising socio-economic
inequalities.
In spatial and territorial terms these restructuring processes have led to a reorganisation of
the national urban hierarchy and to increasing interregional disparities within the new member
states (Altrock et al. 2006a; ESPON 2006c; Heidenreich 2003; Traistaru et al. 2003; Turnock
2003). While highly urbanised regions with a diversiﬁed economy and good infrastructure
(usually capital city regions) and the border regions close to the EU-15 member states have
suffered less from the transition, formerly mono-industrial regions have experienced a deep
economic, social and environmental crisis (for example, Silesia in Poland). Rural regions have
faced pressures for the transformation of agricultural production. In the eastern part of Poland,
Romania or Bulgaria, most rural regions are still dominated by subsistence agriculture with low
productivity and very low GDP per capita. Within metropolitan areas, unfettered market-led
urban development and uncoordinated private investments have generated unsustainable forms
of urban sprawl and new forms of socio-spatial segregation.
166
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 196

A number of key planning and spatial development policy issues consequently have to be
addressed at various scales in the new member states of central and eastern Europe: growing
social and spatial polarisation at the intraregional and interregional levels; the challenge of
economic development in declining industrial regions or in rural regions; demographic decline
and outmigration; insufﬁcient transport infrastructure; environmental damage; and nature
conservation, to name but a few (Davoudi 2006a; Kunzmann 2006a). Yet many obstacles stand
in the way of the development of effective spatial planning systems and regional development
policies in the new member states. There has been a widespread reluctance towards ‘planning’
as a form of state intervention in former state-socialist countries (Davoudi 2006a). This is an
inheritance of the negative experiences of centrally planned economies, which explains ‘the
rather liberal approach to the transition launched after the end of socialism’ (Altrock et al.
2006b: 9). The lack of adequate registers of land uses, uncertainties over ownership and the
primacy given by politicians to unregulated investment at all costs has led to unfettered
development and urban sprawl in many areas. Other challenges have included the sometimes
difﬁcult reform of territorial governance structures and the lack of institutional capacity and
skills in local and regional development policies. In many central European countries that had
very centralised state systems during the communist period, a regional level of administration
or governance had to be recreated, in particular as a prerequisite for receiving and managing
pre-accession aid and, after accession, the Structural Funds (see Chapter 18) (Bachtler and
Downes 2000; Bachtler et al. 2000; Gorzelak 1999; Keating and Hughes 2003). In recent years
the participation of the new member states in EU programmes such as ESPON (see Chapters 6
and 16) has improved the knowledge base about spatial and regional development trends in
those countries.
The scope of the present book does not allow for a more detailed discussion of the evolution
of planning systems, and urban and regional policies in the new member states (see Altrock et
al. 2006a; Andrusz et al. 1996; Van Kempen et al. 2005 for a good overview). The forces
shaping spatial development trends in the new member states have resulted from integration into
the global economy and the Single Market, the administrative restructuring and regulatory
requirements of EU membership, plus the speciﬁc challenges of the transition from a state
socialist to a free market economy. As a result the territorial and spatial development patterns
in the new member states are becoming more, rather than less, unbalanced and uneven. This
raises spatial planning issues at the national and regional level but also generates Europe-wide
territorial development issues. There are ‘transformation winners’ and ‘transformation losers’
as a result of EU enlargement:
Without targeted structural assistance programs, there is a danger that those countries with
very dominant capitals located close to the old EU will experience an increasing division
into a booming West and a poor East. This polarization is being exacerbated by the
additional contrast between urbanized and rural-agrarian lifestyles.
(Altrock et al. 2006b: 5)
This begs the question as to what level of ﬁnancial and institutional support should ﬂow from
the EU to the new member states to support more balanced forms of territorial development
and help address the short-term negative impacts of EU integration. Although many of the issues
highlighted above are primarily within the remit of national and regional authorities, the EU
can help in a number of areas. It has done so through pre-accession aid (prior to 2004 and 
2007) and now through the Structural, Cohesion and Rural Development Funds. EU funding has supported infrastructure development, but also institution building and transfer of 
know-how in regional, urban and rural development policies that promoted an integrated

## S. 197

vision of economic, social, environmental and territorial cohesion. In addition, transnational 
and cross-border cooperation programmes funded by PHARE and INTERREG have supported
joint spatial development activities between the new and the old EU member states before and
after accession. From 2007 onwards the new member states were integrated into INTERREG
programmes as part of the ‘European Territorial Cooperation’ objective of the new EU Regional
Policy 2007–13 (see Chapter 16).
During the preparation of the ESDP, in 1997, it was decided to add a chapter on enlargement
in the ﬁnal version of the ESDP. A study by the European Commission published in 2000 
(CEC 2000a) formed the basis for this chapter, arguing that the three aims of the ESDP broadly
applied to the accession countries as well. The study also highlighted a number of issues speciﬁc
to the new member states: how the transition of political and administrative systems affects 
the ways in which spatial issues are dealt with; how the rapid process of economic restructuring
has considerable potential for spatial polarisation; the slow development of technical infrastructure; the extent of environmental damage; the lack of resources in the public sector. The
ESDP chapter concluded that spatial development processes in the enlargement area would 
not take place as simple ‘replicas’ of development processes within EU-15, but would give rise
to new and speciﬁc tasks for European spatial development policy. The idea of an ‘ESDP+’ was
eventually abandoned, and after 2007 the European debate shifted towards the ‘territorial
cohesion agenda’ (see Chapter 14). This has not prevented some of the ESDP’s objectives,
concepts and principles to be integrated into the national planning strategies of new member
states such as Slovenia, Poland, Slovakia, Hungary and Malta (Peterlin and Kreitmayer-
McKenzie 2007; Zaucha 2007). However, spatial planning remains a generally weaker part of
government activity in central and eastern Europe.
Will the EU enlarge further?
Following the last two enlargements, the EU now reaches the Black Sea and completely encircles
the western Balkans (Figure 1.1). As of 2009 there were three candidate countries for EU
accession: Turkey, Croatia and the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia. Turkey became
an EU candidate country in 1999 and entered an ‘accession partnership’ with the EU in 2001.
Croatia and Turkey started accession negotiations with the EU in 2005. In 2006 the European
Council granted the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia the status of a candidate country,
but accession negotiations have not started yet. All the other western Balkan countries are
potential candidate countries: Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, and Serbia
including Kosovo under UN Security Council Resolution 1244. The EU has repeatedly reafﬁrmed its commitment for eventual EU membership of the western Balkan countries provided
that they fulﬁl the accession criteria. In March 2008 the Commission adopted a Communication
on Western Balkans: Enhancing the European perspective (CEC 2008f) to help accelerate the
progress of these countries towards EU membership. The EU has established a ‘Stabilisation and
Association Process’ with each of those countries to bring their economies and legal systems
progressively closer to the EU through a free trade area, reforms designed to support the
adoption of EU standards and EU ﬁnancial support.
In 2007 EU pre-accession funding programmes were reformed and simpliﬁed into a single,
uniﬁed instrument designed to deliver support to both candidate and potential candidate
countries: the Instrument for Pre-accession Assistance (IPA), which replaces the PHARE, ISPA
and SAPARD programmes. IPA consists of the following ﬁve components: transition assistance
and institution building; cross-border cooperation with EU member states and other countries
eligible for IPA; regional development (i.e. measures in the ﬁelds of transport, environment 
and economic development); human resources development (strengthening human capital and
168
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 198

combating exclusion); and rural development. The total pre-accession funding for the current
ﬁnancial framework period (2007–13) is EUR 11.5 billion.
The prospect for further enlargement of the EU raises a number of questions and challenges.
Given the current issues with the institutional functioning of an EU of 27 members, should 
the EU be enlarged further without a period of institutional consolidation? What are the 
borders of Europe – historically, culturally, politically? Where should EU enlargement stop
(Debardeleben 2007)? Attempts to deﬁne the borders of ‘Europe’ have to be understood not 
as ‘an objective scientiﬁc reality but a social and historical production’ (ESPON 2007a: 4). 
Some of the arguments presented in Table 12.1 in relation to the eastern enlargement continue
to permeate the current debates on future enlargements, albeit with less intensity since the EU
will probably never again have to face an enlargement of the scale of the 2004 one. Turkey’s
application for membership, however, has been particularly controversial, in relation to issues
surrounding democracy and human rights. Turkey’s application is grounded in the fact that it
has territory on the European continent and a market economy, but there are several outstanding
issues in the way of the country meeting the political criteria for EU membership: the Cyprus
question; the Kurdish question (the Kurdish minority is estimated to a ﬁfth of the population);
human rights; and the involvement of the military in public life. Although many reforms have
been made (e.g. abolition of the death penalty, reform of the penal code, reforms in the areas
of women’s rights and Kurdish cultural rights), there are still a number of contentious issues
slowing down accession negotiations. From a spatial planning and territorial development point
of view, the potential accession of Turkey would fuel the debates on regional disparities in the
EU, on the role of EU agricultural and regional policies in reducing these and on the size of 
the EU budget to be committed to the objective of cohesion.
What criteria should, or should not, be used to try and deﬁne where ‘Europe’ starts and stops?
Does Israel belong to Europe? Could North African countries apply for EU membership? 
What is the importance assigned to religion in those debates? The concept of a ‘Union for the
Mediterranean’, launched by French President Sarkozy in July 2008 to revive the existing 
‘Euro-Mediterranean Partnership’ with 16 partners across the southern Mediterranean and the
Middle East, has been an attempt to deal with these contentious issues. Initially the concept of
a Mediterranean union was put forward as an alternative to the accession of Turkey to the EU
by promoting transnational cooperation and partnership between the EU and the countries of
the Mediterranean and the Middle East. However, Turkey made it clear it would join the Union
for the Mediterranean only if this process did not come instead of EU accession. The enlarged
EU and its member states have thus struggled to reach consensus and deﬁne clear policies
towards their eastern and southern neighbours:
One major explanation of the difﬁculty for Europe to develop strategies towards the 
south and east is probably the mental barriers that exist in the minds of most of its citizens
and decision makers. The Mediterranean Sea is by many seen as a ‘natural barrier’ between
two continents. . . . The fact that European Union citizens and decision makers are 
reluctant to open their southern border to migrants from Africa or to invest in the African
economies is partly related to their perception of European borders and European identity.
The situation is also unclear for eastern borders where very different perceptions exist
concerning the belonging of the neighbouring countries to ‘Europe’.
(ESPON 2007a: 8)
Following the 2004 enlargement the EU prepared a new ‘European Neighbourhood Policy’
(ENP) to avoid the emergence of new dividing lines with its immediate eastern and southern
neighbours: Algeria, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Egypt, Georgia, Israel, Jordan, Lebanon,

## S. 199

Libya, Moldova, Morocco, Occupied Palestinian Territory, Syria, Tunisia and Ukraine. Russia’s
relationships with the EU are part of a separate ‘Strategic Partnership’. The ENP supports
cooperation programmes that uphold values of democracy and human rights, rule of law, 
good governance, market economy principles and sustainable development. Until 2006, the 
EU provided ﬁnancial assistance to the countries of the European Neighbourhood Policy under
various geographical programmes including: Technical Aid to the Commonwealth of Independent States (TACIS) created in 1996 for eastern neighbours and Russia, which included a
cross-border strand MEDA (launched in 1996 for southern Mediterranean neighbours); and
Community Aid for Reconstruction, Development and Stability (CARDS) launched in 2000 
in the western Balkan countries of the former Yugoslavia. For the budgetary period 2000–06,
the funds available were approximately EUR 5.3 billion for MEDA and EUR 3.1 billion for
TACIS. From 1 January 2007 those various programmes were replaced by a single instrument
– the European Neighbourhood and Partnership Instrument (ENPI), which has a budget of EUR
12 billion for 2007–13. The ENPI supports individual country programmes to help promote
sustainable development, institution building and approximation to EU policies and standards.
Cross-border cooperation with countries along the EU’s external land and maritime borders
remains a priority of the ENP: the ENPI funds 15 Cross-border Cooperation (CBC) programmes
(receiving EUR 1.18 billion for the period 2007–13).
Outside the EU’s immediate neighbours, the EU is involved in humanitarian and development aid programmes in more than 160 countries, territories or organisations worldwide 
to ﬁght poverty and promote economic development and democracy. Development assistance 
and cooperation was originally concentrated in Africa but then extended to Asia, Latin 
America and the southern and eastern Mediterranean countries in the mid-1970s. The EU is the
world’s largest donor of development aid (60 per cent of the world’s ofﬁcial development assistance) and will collectively be spending around EUR 30 billion more per year on development
assistance from 2010. The European Commission has a dedicated agency, EuropeAid, to
manage some its EU external aid programmes. For the funding period 2007–13, there are 
six instruments of external assistance: the ENPI, mentioned above, which applies to the EU’s
17 neighbours; the European Development Fund (EDF), which provides the bedrock of EU
cooperation with 78 African, Caribbean and Paciﬁc countries and the overseas countries and
territories of Member States; the Development Cooperation Instrument (DCI), which provides
assistance to South Africa and 47 developing countries in Latin America, Asia and Central Asia
and the Middle East not covered by the ENPI and EDF; the European Instrument for Democracy
and Human Rights (EIDHR); the Instrument for Stability (IfS) in countries in crisis; and the
Nuclear Safety Cooperation Instrument (NSCI).
‘Deepening and broadening’: the institutional and democratic 
challenge
The founding Treaties of 1957 deﬁned ‘a pattern of policy-making and decision-making in 
which the Commission would propose, the Parliament would advise, the Council would decide,
and – when law was made – the Court of Justice would interpret’ (Nugent 2006: 52). Yet ‘the
logic and momentum of the integration process has come to require periodic revision of the
treaties so as both to “catch up” with evolving realities and to enable desired development to
occur’ (Nugent 2006: 129). Since the mid-1980s a series of successive changes to the European
treaties have expanded EU competences, modiﬁed the composition and functioning of EU
institutions and the relationships between them towards a more widespread sharing of powers,
as discussed in Chapter 11. The Council has taken an increasing lead over the Commission in
170
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 200

setting the European agenda and deﬁning priorities for EU action. The European Parliament now
shares decision-making powers with the Council in many EU policy areas. A wider range of
interests have been increasingly included in the European decision-making process.
Yet in spite of treaty reforms there are recurring debates and criticisms about the composition
and workings of EU institutions, in particular around issues of representation, transparency,
efficiency, democracy and the balance between European and national competences. The
European Commission (often pejoratively referred to as ‘Brussels’ by the media) has long been
the target of such criticisms, accused by Euro-sceptics of red tape and bureaucracy, too much
independence in the exercise of its duties, lack of transparency and accountability (in particular
with regard to the complex ‘comitology system’ referred to in Chapter 11) and lack of
coordination and integration between the policies developed by its various DGs. More widely,
the EU political system as a whole has often come under criticisms of ‘democratic deﬁcit’. Hix
(2005: 177–8) summarises the arguments embedded in the notion of an EU ‘democratic deﬁcit’
as a combination of increased executive power versus decreased national parliamentary control,
a weak European parliament, a lack of proper citizens’ vote over EU policies, the distance
between the EU and its citizens, and a policy drift characterised by the adoption of EU policies
not widely supported by a majority of citizens (e.g. neoliberal deregulation). Hix concludes his
review of electoral processes in EU politics (2005: Chapter 6) by arguing that the EU is not a
very democratic system of government, in the sense that ‘the EU polity can only be considered
democratic if competitive elections determine the direction of the EU policy agenda’ (2005: 180),
which is not (yet) the case:
We certainly elect our governments, who negotiate on our behalf in Brussels and decide who
forms the EU executive. However, national government elections are about national issues,
fought by national parties, and about who controls national government ofﬁce. EP elections,
moreover, are by-products of these national electoral contests: fought on domestic issues
rather than the EU policy agenda or executive ofﬁce-holders at the European level. In no
sense, therefore, can Europe’s voters choose between rival policy programmes for the EU
or ‘throw out’ those who exercise political power at the European level.
(Hix 2005: 206)
This is why some politicians and MEPs have called for more powers for the EP (to make it
on par with the Council in all areas of EU action) and for the right to elect the President of the
Commission. Both measures could raise the proﬁle of the European agenda among European
citizens. Yet member states are unlikely to push for reforms that would strengthen the EP or
lead to the election of the president of the Commission as this would weaken the power of
national governments over the EU agenda (Hix 2005).
The subsidiarity issue also re-emerged in political debates about the EU in the 1990s. Eurosceptic political parties on the one hand, and regional interests in federal states on the other,
argued that too much power had been shifted to the EU institutions away from national and
regional governments (Wallace 2005). Yet at the same time, other politicians were eager to 
take the European integration process further without being stalled by more reluctant member
states. The possibility of ‘closer cooperation’ between a small number of EU member states 
was consequently enshrined in the Treaty of Amsterdam. It has been argued that such forms 
of cooperation are the most realistic way forward, but ‘variable geometries’ has its critics 
too: federalists fear it will lead to a more complex EU, weaken EU institutions and lead to 
the unravelling of the acquis communautaire by allowing some countries to ‘pick and choose’
(Grant 2005).

Widening, deepening and broadening the EU
171
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 201

From the end of the 1990s onwards, the prospect of the eastern enlargement of the EU made
the question of institutional reform of the EU system more pressing. Enlargement did not only
trigger incremental reforms to the EU budget and policies but it also posed major challenges for
the EU institutional setting and decision-making processes. The conventional view held by
political leaders and EU institutions in the 1990s was that there was an inevitable tension
between geographical ‘widening’ of the EU and political ‘deepening’ of the integration process
(Wallace 2005). Without reform of the Commission structure, of the voting system in the
Council, and of the role of the EP, it was argued that decision-making mechanisms would be
crippled and paralysed in a Union of 27. The Treaty of Amsterdam (1997) had enhanced the
role of the EP in EU decision-making and extended the use of qualiﬁed majority voting in the
Council to various policy areas. Yet the Amsterdam reforms did not solve the institutional issues
raised by the prospect of enlargement.
As the timetable of enlargement became more precise, an intergovernmental conference (IGC)
was convened in 2000 to ‘ensure that the European Union’s institutions can continue to work
efﬁciently after enlargement’ (Laursen 2005: 153). This IGC eventually led to the signing of the
Treaty of Nice in February 2001. Considerations of efﬁciency (capacity to reach decisions in a
Union of 27) and legitimacy (balance of power between small and big member states) dominated
the negotiations, which focused on four key issues that had not been solved by the Amsterdam
Treaty – known as the ‘Amsterdam leftovers’ (Laursen 2005):
•
The re-weighting of votes in the Council and of the number of seats in the EP (see 
Chapter 9) created tensions between larger and smaller member states. How many votes are
necessary to form a ‘blocking minority’ in decisions to be reached at qualiﬁed majority
voting in the Council is a crucial issue. In the EU-15, the voting weights were far from
proportional to the population of each member state, with an under-representation of big
states such as Germany and an over-representation of small states such as Luxemburg
(Laursen 2005) in terms of ‘citizens per vote’. Without changes, after enlargement this
situation would have been reinforced, which led bigger member states to call for reform
before enlargement. In the European Parliament, equally, the issue of the relative representation of the new member states came to the fore, as smaller countries were over-represented
(in terms of the number of citizens per MEP) at the expense of larger ones.
•
The extension of the use of qualiﬁed majority vote in the Council to new policy ﬁelds in
the areas of immigration, asylum, social policy and trade was controversial.
•
Changes in the size and composition of the Commission were required to substitute the
system of two Commissioners from each of the ﬁve largest member states and one for all
remaining member states with one Commissioner from each member state.
•
The simpliﬁcation of the procedure for ‘closer’ or ‘enhanced’ cooperation had been made
possible in the Treaty of Amsterdam under strict conditions.
In spite of these changes, the Nice Treaty left many crucial institutional questions unanswered.
The heads of state and governments of the EU at the Laeken European Council in December
2001 acknowledged that the debate on EU institutional reform was not closed, and planned for
a new IGC in 2004 with the participation of the accession countries. The Council set up a
‘convention’ (a working group in charge of drafting a constitution) under the chairmanship of
former French president Valéry Giscard d’Estaing that aspired to ‘put an end to the continuous
Treaty-revision process that started with the Treaty of Maastricht in 1992 and then led to
Amsterdam and Nice’ (Crum 2005: 200) and forge consensus for the need to ‘re-found the Union
on the basis of a single comprehensive Constitutional Treaty’ (Crum 2005: 201). This was a
172
The institutional framework
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 202

bold ambition acknowledging prior resistance to the idea of a ‘European constitution’ from
member states. In June 2003 the Convention presented the result of its work to the European
Council, in the shape of a ‘draft Treaty establishing a constitution for Europe’. The draft constitutional Treaty was a single text that aimed to replace all the existing European treaties and
included a number of signiﬁcant proposals for change (see Crum 2005 for an overview), among
others a new voting system and the extension of qualiﬁed majority voting in the Council, a new
system of representation in the Commission, a recognition of the EU’s legal personality (merged
with that of the EC) and the abolition of the pillars structure, the incorporation of the Charter
of Fundamental Rights as of the Constitutional Treaty, a simpliﬁcation of EU legal instruments
and procedures, a clariﬁcation of the competences of the EU versus those of member states, the
election of the president of the Commission by the EP, the extension of the co-decision legislative procedure to most policy areas, the creation of a new political ﬁgure – the President of
the European Council – and the creation of a position of Minister for the Union’s Foreign Affairs
(European Communities 2004).
The inter-governmental conference that convened to review the draft Constitutional Treaty
rejected it in December 2003, in particular due to strong opposition from Spain and Poland. A
year later, after some revisions, a new ‘Treaty establishing a Constitution for Europe’ was signed
by the heads of state and government in Rome in October 2004. This treaty was, however,
rejected by voters during a referendum in France (May 2005) and in the Netherlands (June
2005), and consequently never entered into force. This setback led the EU heads of state and
government to declare, at the European Council of June 2005, a ‘period of reﬂection’ on the
future of Europe to take stock of the opposition raised during these referenda. There were
various reasons for the rejection of the draft constitution: fears about rapid integration and
enlargement, fear of a dumping down of the European welfare state model (in France), and
failure, on the part of EU leaders, to provide a text that was intelligible to the average citizen.
Offering an ofﬁcial text of 448 articles, 470 pages and 75,000 words (ten times the length of
the American Constitution) was a ‘severe case of constitutional delusion’ (Shaw 2005: 7). At a
European Council meeting in June 2007, European leaders agreed to convene another Inter-
Governmental Conference to ﬁnalise not a ‘constitution’ but a ‘reform treaty’ for the European
Union. A revised Treaty was signed by the European heads of state and government in Lisbon
on 13 December 2007, bringing an end to several years of negotiation and debates about EU
institutional reform. The Treaty of Lisbon amends the EU’s two core treaties, the Treaty on
European Union (TEU) and the Treaty establishing the European Community (TEC), without
replacing them. The shift from a ‘constitution’ to a revised treaty responded to the pressures by
countries such as the UK, the Netherlands and the Czech Republic which wanted to get rid of
the ‘constitutional’ symbols and vocabulary. Yet many of the proposals made in the draft
Constitutional Treaty of 2004 are present in the Lisbon Treaty. The Treaty of Lisbon does not
fundamentally change the EU’s institutional set-up, but introduces a number of new elements,
as summarised in Box 12.1. Its supporters argue that many of the changes brought about by
the Treaty contribute to make the EU more democratic and more efﬁcient in comparison 
with the Nice Treaty system. It was envisaged that the Lisbon Treaty, after ratiﬁcation by all
27 EU member states through a referendum or a parliamentary vote, would enter into force on 
1 January 2009 before the European Parliament elections of June 2009. However, ratiﬁcation
was stalled by the rejection of the Treaty by Irish voters in a referendum in June 2008.
Subsequent negotiations with the Irish government helped to break out of the impasse and Irish
voters approved the Treaty in a second referendum in October 2009. The Treaty of Lisbon
entered into force on 1 December 2009.

## S. 203

174
The institutional framework
Box 12.1 Major changes introduced by the Treaty
of Lisbon
•
The ‘three pillars’ are abolished and the relations between the EU and its member countries
are clariﬁed: deﬁnition of exclusive powers, shared powers, supporting, coordinating or
complementary action (see Chapter 11).
•
The possibility for a member state to withdraw from the Union is recognised (inclusion of a
voluntary withdrawal clause).
•
The Treaty guarantees the enforcement of the Charter of Fundamental Rights (which will be
legally binding on the member states except the UK and Poland, which opted out).
•
The EU is to be a single legal personality, which will enable the EU to conclude international
agreements and join international organisations.
European Parliament:
•
The EP is given more powers in relation to law making, budget and international agreements:
the co-decision procedure (renamed ‘ordinary legislative procedure’) is extended to several
new ﬁelds (legal immigration, penal judicial cooperation, police cooperation and some aspects
of trade policy and agriculture); the EP and Council will determine all expenditure of the EU
budget together; the EP’s assent will be required for all international agreements in ﬁelds
governed by the ordinary legislative procedure.
•
The composition of the Parliament is changed (see Chapter 9).
•
The EP has an increased role in the areas of justice, freedom and security.
Council of Ministers:
•
Qualiﬁed majority voting in the Council is extended to new policy areas (e.g. immigration and
culture). QMV will become the default voting method except where the treaties require a
different procedure (e.g. a unanimous vote).
•
In 2014, a new voting method is to be introduced: ‘double majority voting’. Proposed EU laws
will require a majority not only of the EU’s member countries (55 per cent, i.e. 15 countries)
but also of the EU population (65 per cent). This will be accompanied by a new mechanism
enabling a small number of member governments (close to a blocking minority, one-third of
member states or 25 per cent of the EU population) to demonstrate their opposition to a
decision.
•
A new function of President of the European Council is created, elected for two and a half
years to prepare the Council’s work, ensure its continuity and secure consensus among
member countries.
•
National parliaments and citizens will be able to see which decisions have been taken by which
national ministers in the Council (all deliberations on legislative matters are to be made public).
European Commission:
•
From 2014: the number of Commissioners is to be reduced to two-thirds of member
countries, but posts will rotate between all countries.

Widening, deepening and broadening the EU
175
•
A post of EU high representative for foreign and security policy and Commissioner for
external relations (Commission vice-president) is created to ensure consistency in the EU’s
dealings with foreign countries and international bodies, which is assisted by a new European
external action service.
•
The EP has to approve the Commission President after their proposal by the Council (creating
a direct link between the results of the European elections and the choice of candidate for
president of the Commission).
National parliaments:
•
New clause sets out the rights and duties of the national parliaments within the EU, i.e. right
to information, the way they monitor subsidiarity, mechanisms for evaluating policy in the ﬁeld
of freedom, security and justice, procedures for reforming the treaties.
•
If one-third of national parliaments consider that a proposal is not in line with subsidiarity,
the Commission will have to re-examine it and decide whether to maintain, adjust or
withdraw it, and the European Parliament and the Council will have to decide whether or
not to continue the legislative procedure.
A stronger voice for citizens:
•
Citizens’ Initiative: 1 million citizens from any number of member states (0.2 per cent of the
EU population) will have the opportunity to ask the Commission to present a proposal in
any of the EU’s areas of responsibility.
Policy competences:
•
The EU’s ability to act in several policy areas will be enhanced: freedom, security and justice,
terrorism or crime; energy policy, public health, civil protection, climate change, services of
general interest, research, space, territorial cohesion, commercial policy, humanitarian aid,
sport, tourism and administrative cooperation.
•
Fields of relevance for European spatial planning include:
•
reinforcement of EU action in the ﬁeld of sustainable development, climate change and
the environment;
•
a speciﬁc chapter on energy that deﬁnes the key competencies and the overall objectives
of energy policy;
•
recognition of the role of public services in social and regional cohesion (transport,
schooling, health care) and a special protocol attached to the Treaty on services of
general interest (see Chapter 17);
•
Cohesion Policy: the objective of ‘territorial cohesion’ is added to the EU’s objectives
of economic and social cohesion, and as a shared competence between the EU and
member states;
•
Tourism: the Treaty creates a new legal basis entirely devoted to tourism.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 205

Conclusion
Successive enlargements, in particular the 2004 and 2007 ones, have affected the institutional
structure and policy agenda of the EU. Political debates on EU reforms over the past ten years
have focused on inﬂuence, control, subsidiarity and democracy in the enlarged EU. In the short
term, the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon on 1 December 2009 has led to a number of
changes. In the medium term, a period of reﬂection over the future of the European integration
project is likely to happen. In a speech delivered in 2000, Joschka Fischer, the former minister
of foreign affairs of Germany, stressed that for 50 years the European integration process
advanced incrementally without a deﬁnite institutional end goal – or what he called a ‘ﬁnality’
for the European integration project (Fischer 2003). The convention on the future of Europe
was supposed to determine this ﬁnality, but the failure of the draft constitution left the issue
unresolved. The question of the ‘ﬁnality of the EU’ is not just a matter of institutional reform.
It has to do with the philosophy and ethos of the European project as a whole: What kind of
EU do citizens want? What do we want a united and integrated EU for? What goals should the
EU prioritise: a socially just, a liberal, a competitive, an environmentally sustainable Europe?
How do EU member states and institutions best achieve this? What role should the EU play as
a global actor in international negotiations, regulations and policies to address the key geopolitical and global environmental challenges of the twenty-ﬁrst century? These strategic issues
are likely to permeate the EU political agenda in the next decades.
Further reading
A plethora of academic books were published in the mid-1990s and early 2000s in the run-up
to enlargement. For a recent (retrospective) account of the process, see Mayhew 2004, Nugent
2004, Vassiliou 2007 and Sajdik 2008. The website of DG Enlargement, http://ec.europa.eu/
enlargement/index_en.htm, is a good source of up-to-date information on the current candidate
entries, and links to an archive of all documents related to the 2004 and 2007 enlargements (and
their impact). The Treaty of Lisbon, which entered into force on 1 December 2009, can be
accessed at http://europa.eu/lisbon_treaty/index_en.htm. The European Neighbourhood Policy
and other forms of action of the EU in the world are presented at http://ec.europa.eu/world/
index_en.htm.

## S. 206

The European spatial 
planning agenda
The ﬁrst 12 chapters of this book have set the context for European spatial planning and
territorial cooperation by introducing some key concepts and terms, explaining the patterns 
and trends of spatial development at the European scale, and laying out the complex web of
relevant organisations and processes through which policy and decisions are made. Part IV now
concentrates on the core issue of European spatial planning and what it means in theory and
practice.
This part asks the question: what is European spatial planning? It attempts to answer that
deceptively simple question in three ways. First, in Chapter 13 the notion of European spatial
planning is compared to the predominant styles or models of spatial planning that are found in
the member countries of the EU. These are ideas about planning that readers may be more
familiar with. Does European spatial planning correspond to domestic ideas about planning?
Does the European dimension have any distinctive characteristics that set it apart from planning
in the member states?
Second, Chapter 14 provides a historical review of the emergence of European spatial
planning, explaining the incremental and sometimes accidental way that events come together
in important deﬁning moments. European spatial planning is not a static concept; it is alive and
constantly evolving in the multitude of ongoing collaborations and debates. The mix of organisations, statements, attitudes, beliefs and more that make up the notion of European spatial
planning in 2010 cannot be appreciated without some understanding of these historical origins
and how these have interconnected with the development of the European Union itself.
Third, Chapters 15 and 16 provide a catalogue and critical review of the tools of European
spatial planning. European spatial planning is perhaps best deﬁned by what we claim as its
products or outputs. What things (be they activities, documents, programmes or plans) come
under the banner of European spatial planning? Chapter 15 describes the major supranational
and transnational statements that provide the cornerstones of a common European perspective
on spatial planning. They come in the form of spatial perspectives, strategies and visions.
Chapter 16 then considers the tools of transnational cooperation in its many programmes and
activities. Transnational cooperation is a bridge between the grand and rather general ideas at
the European level with the practical concerns and problems faced by planning practitioners in
regional and local planning authorities. Again, this is evidence of the real practice where a
deﬁnition of European spatial planning can be found.
Although the preceding discussion in the ﬁrst three parts of this book may give an overall
impression of the inexactness of the notion of European spatial planning, Part IV should help
to paint a clearer picture and show that it has many tangible outputs.

## S. 207

A model of European spatial 
planning?
Instead of dreaming the unrealistic (and undemocratic) dream of compressive vertical and horizontal
coordination, a more indirect and soft approach is needed to concert policy actions at all tiers of
decision-making.
(Kunzmann 1998a: 118)
Introduction
The question mark in the title of this chapter indicates some uncertainty. We use the term
‘model’ here in the sense of providing a simpliﬁed but systematic representation of the main
properties of the (in reality) very complex mix of phenomena and ideas that make up European
spatial planning. But is it possible to describe a model of European spatial planning? And if so,
what is the value of such a model? Is it required? We do not want to give the impression that
there is a universally agreed model of European spatial planning. It may be more appropriate
to talk about the ‘notion’ of spatial planning – the more general and contradictory mix of
opinions and understandings. However, there is in our view sufﬁcient agreement about the
distinctive characteristics of European spatial planning to give an account of its main characteristics. Thus we use the word ‘model’.
In this chapter, the ‘model’ of European spatial planning is examined by contrasting it to
models of planning that predominate at the national and regional levels. So the chapter begins
by explaining how styles or models of spatial planning vary across Europe. The chapter moves
on to consider in more detail some of those distinctive characteristics and raise questions about
the operation of spatial planning that are particularly salient in relation to the European
dimension. It concludes by summarising the main characteristics of ‘European spatial planning’
according to the same criteria that are used to consider spatial planning at the national level.
Models of spatial planning in Europe
The subtle but critical variation in wording here needs to be emphasised. This section is about
models of spatial planning in Europe, that is, the varying forms of planning thought and practice
at the national and regional levels. Take care to distinguish this idea from the European model
of spatial planning, which is considered later. This book is not primarily concerned with ‘spatial
planning in Europe’ (which would involve explanations of how planning is carried out in
different European countries) but about European spatial planning – the cooperative activity on
spatial planning among member states and regions at the supranational and transboundary
levels. However, at this point it is useful to brieﬂy review variation in the theory and practice
of spatial planning in the countries of Europe.
Chapter 13
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 208

Cross-national comparative studies have created classiﬁcations or ideal-type taxonomies 
that generalise about the differences and similarities in the way planning is thought about and
practised. Such general typologies are useful for identifying trends, particularly evidence of
convergence and the diffusion of ideas (Hantrais 2008). Comparative typologies (sometimes
called families) have been popular in ﬁelds such as comparative law (Zweigert and Kötz 1998),
social policy (Wilensky et al.1985) and welfare provision (Esping Andersen 1990; see Chapter
24). In Europe this type of analysis is encouraged by European integration. EU law and policy
has to be implemented in national contexts so knowledge of differences in policy regimes in
member states is particularly useful.
There has long been an interest in cross-national comparison of aspects of spatial planning (see, for example, Bunbury 1938). European integration and transnational cooperation
have given an impetus for new studies to assist directly with the formulation and application
of supranational planning measures and transnational cooperation (Williams 1996), especially
to support the European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) and INTERREG projects. For
example, the COMMIN INTERREG project was designed speciﬁcally to support transnational
and cross-border working in the Baltic Sea region by providing comparative information on
spatial planning systems (COMMIN 2009). Cross-national comparisons have been also
employed to help in the ‘positioning’ of planning practice in one place compared to others, as
a form of benchmarking.
Undertaking cross-national comparison and generalising about similarity and difference is a
considerable methodological challenge and ﬁndings are often limited and partial (Nadin 2008;
Williams 1984). There are a number of reasons for this that should be spelled out to caution
against the reaching of ill-considered judgments. Most studies are small-scale and concentrate
on the analysis of two or three systems (see, for example, Booth 1993; Booth et al. 2007; Dühr
2007; Masser 1984; Spaans and de Wolff 2005; and Thomas et al. 1983, among others). While
such studies may inform the creation of more general taxonomies, the ﬁndings will often be
limited to the countries concerned. Second, most comparative studies have been undertaken 
in, or led from, north-west Europe (for example, Davies et al. 1989) and may have helped to
perpetuate the dominant stream of northern European thinking in European spatial planning
(Janin Rivolin and Faludi 2005). Third, comparative studies have for many years focused on
Western planning systems, although over recent years since the fall of the Iron Curtain there
has been increasing attention given to approaches to planning in the new member states in
central and eastern Europe (see, for example, Adams et al. 2006; Altrock et al. 2006a). Fourth,
many studies are mostly descriptive. While they provide very helpful information about aspects
of spatial planning they give little attention to comparing notions of planning or explaining the
differences (Dal Cin and Lyddon 1996; GMA Planning 1993; Ryser and Franchini 2008;
Schmidt-Eichstaedt 1996). Other studies that have engaged in comparison and explanation tend
to be based on limited empirical evidence (Healey and Williams 1993) or one aspect of planning
(Healey et al. 1997; Seaton et al. 2000; Spaans et al. 1996; Thomas et al. 1983) One useful and
well-used classiﬁcation of planning systems by Newman and Thornley (1996) is based only on
the criterion of the form of the legal system, which is brought forward from Zweigert and Kötz
(1998, originally published 1987).
The last point on criteria is critical. Comparing spatial planning from place to place entails
choosing meaningful characteristics to compare. The choice should be based on some general
proposition about the important characteristics of systems and the particular questions at hand.
In practice much emphasis is placed on formal aspects of systems such as the division of
competences among tiers of government and the legal status of plans. More meaningful criteria,
such as the ‘planning doctrine’ – the dominant ‘spatial organising principles’ that guide intervention
(Faludi and van der Valk 1994) – are more difﬁcult to research and much less often considered.

## S. 209

Nadin and Stead (2008) summarise the major cross-national comparative studies that have
produced typologies of spatial planning. The EU Compendium of Spatial Planning Systems and
Policies (CEC 1997a; Nadin et al. 1995) is by far the largest cross-national comparative
research project on spatial planning in Europe, but shares some of the limitations of other studies
– it is mostly descriptive and it only covers the EU-15, but it does provide an elementary typology
of spatial planning that is based on a wider set of criteria. The Compendium proposed four ideal
types of spatial planning in Europe. An ideal type takes certain meaningful characteristics or
attributes of an object and accentuates them to create a clearly deﬁned type that does not exist
in reality. Ideal types provide a benchmark or measuring rod against which to compare reality,
and as such they prove useful for a discussion about the characteristics of European spatial
planning.
The Compendium used the term ‘tradition’ for its ideal types (CEC 1997a: 33) to emphasise
the way that forms of planning are deeply embedded in the complex historical conditions of
particular places. The form of system of law and six other variables were used to distinguish
types:
•
the scope of the system in terms of policy topics covered over which it has some competence
or seeks inﬂuence (such as social, economic and environmental sectors);
•
the extent of national and regional strategic planning as well as the ubiquitous local
regulation of development;
•
the locus of power or relative competences of levels of government (which is a question
about subsidiarity, explained in Chapter 2);
•
the relative roles of the public and private sectors and the extent to which planning can be
described as plan-led or market-led;
•
the maturity or completeness of the system including the existence in practice of plans and
the level of public acceptance of planning intervention; and
•
the apparent distance between expressed goals for spatial development and outcomes.
On the basis of these criteria four traditions (or styles or models) of spatial planning were
proposed and are summarised in Figure 13.1. All national spatial planning approaches will
exhibit a combination of traditions or models. Some places might exhibit a strong tendency to
one tradition and be much weaker in others, whereas some places may exhibit a more complex
combination. Figure 13.1 is used here to show that the four ideal type models overlap in some
way. They each have dominant characteristics that set them apart, but they also share a common
concern with organising space or territory. The ESPON Project 2.3.2: Governance of Territorial
and Urban Policies from EU to Local Level took forward the Compendium ideal types and
applied them to the new member states and neighbouring countries, 29 countries in all. It also
seeks to ‘give a modest update on the movements that took place’ since the Compendium project
was published (ESPON 2006d: 112).
Box 13.1 gives more explanation of the four models and also gives a very tentative list of
countries under each one. This is not to suggest that national planning systems can be ‘pigeon
holed’ into classes but rather gives an indication of the predominant model or style of planning
in that country. For example, the French system has a very strong vein of regional economic
planning but also reﬂects the urbanism model. There is a particular difﬁculty with the former
communist countries that have undergone, and in some places continue to undergo, radical
change. The ESPON 2.3.2 Governance project (2006d) does cover the new EU member states,
but readers should be aware that this study sometimes gives a quite different understanding 
to the four ideal types than was originally intended. These problems, which are recognised in
many studies, illustrate the vagaries and inconsistencies of cross-national comparative planning.

The European spatial planning agenda

## S. 210

The following section considers how the European dimension of spatial planning compares with
the four models described in the Compendium (CEC 1997a).
The European Model?
In this section, the idea of a ‘European model of spatial planning’ is compared with the varying
models described above. To recap and to avoid misunderstanding, there is no European model
in the sense of a clear deﬁnition of scope and role. But various statements published since the
1980s have converged towards ‘a European model’ in terms of some common assumptions and
principles. This is not simply a fusion or lowest common denominator of the various models
and approaches to spatial planning across Europe. It has distinctive characteristics that are
relevant to EU objectives and the transnational and European scales. But the emergence of a

## S. 211

182
The European spatial planning agenda
Box 13.1 Traditions or models of European spatial
planning
The comprehensive integrated model is about spatial coordination. It has wide scope and its main
task is to provide horizontal (across sectors), vertical (between levels) and geographical (across
borders) integration of the spatial impacts of sectoral policies. It does this by using a multilevel arrangement of plans that are intended to coordinate spatial development. It has a strong
public sector component. It is characterised by mature planning institutions and mechanisms
in a context of political commitment to and public trust in planning.
European countries that show features of this ideal type include: AT, DK, FI, NL, SE, DE,
BG, EE, HU, LV, LT, PL, RO, SL, SV.
The land use management model is about the regulation of changes of use of land and property.
The operation of planning is geared to managing physical development, mostly at the local level,
though some regulation may be done at higher levels. This is a narrow scope in terms of the
role of planning, but development is managed in order to meet general planning principles and
wider societal goals such as housing provision and protecting environmental heritage. It makes
use of policy statements and decision rules and there are extensive mechanisms for citizen
involvement. This style or model of planning is particularly associated with private sector-led
development and land value capture.
European countries that show features of this ideal type include: BE, IE, UK, LU, CY, 
CZ, MT.
The regional economic planning model of spatial planning is about mitigating social and economic
disparities in the interests of ‘spatial equity’ especially at the regional scale. It has a wide scope
in terms of its desired inﬂuence on other policy sectors. There is a strong public-sector
orientation, and there are mature planning institutions. The national and regional levels of
planning are important, though with vertical integration to local actors. It makes use of strategies, programmes and economic support instruments alongside plans and direct investment
in infrastructure projects.
European countries that show features of this ideal type include: FR, DE, PT, HU, LV, 
LT, SK.
The urbanism model is about the design and creation of places. It has a narrow scope in terms
of methods of intervention through zoning instruments, design codes, building control and
development projects. There is little interest in strategy or planning at the national and regional
scales. Private actions tend to dominate. There is recognition of the relationship between
physical environmental change and wider societal objectives. The model has a strong bias to
design and professional solutions with only limited mechanisms for citizen involvement and
managing conﬂict.
European countries that show features of this ideal type include: GR, IT, ES, CY.
Note: The countries are listed according to the predominant model or ideal type of spatial planning. All countries
will exhibit a mixture of ideal types. The sources for the allocation of countries are: ESPON 2006d and Nadin
2008. See Table 3.3 for a list of country abbreviations.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 212

European spatial planning approach has been strongly inﬂuenced by certain ideas from the
member states. In short, these come predominantly from the regional economic and comprehensive integrated models, as explained below. First, consideration is given to the land use
management and urbanism approaches.
Perhaps it is self-evident that the ‘land use management’ and ‘urbanism’ approaches are less
relevant for the European Union if the principle of subsidiarity is applied, given their concern
with predominantly local and regional planning issues (see Chapter 2). However, this has not
been the view in some member states where there are concerns about how European laws and
other actions impinge on the detailed operation of land use regulation. For example, DG
Environment has been keen to ensure that member states establish and effectively implement
land use regulation in so far as it serves the objective of sustainable development (see Chapter
21). It may be that there is insufﬁcient attention to detailed questions of regulation and
enforcement at the European level given the land use impacts of climate change, urban sprawl
and other relevant problems. However, this is not the realm of the model of European spatial
planning that is discussed here. The detailed operation of land use regulation or urban design
has never been central to the European dimension of spatial planning. Nevertheless, the fear of
such potential ‘interference’ has been one of the reasons for member states to consistently oppose
the granting of an explicit competence to the EU for spatial planning, while continuing to
cooperate on planning initiatives, as explained in Chapter 2.
The origins and rationale for spatial planning at the European level are quite different to those
that led to the strengthening of public regulation and planning of land use in many countries
in the early twentieth century. At national level spatial planning was established as a response
to the damaging effects of rapid and uncoordinated urban development on the social and
economic state of cities and the wellbeing of citizens. (These problems continue for much of
Europe.) The result was the creation of varying systems of regulation through land use zoning
and other means of intervening in state and market development processes. Local regulation was
later supplemented with regional and strategic planning (Hall 2002). The underlying goals are
in part social and economic, but the means and the day-to-day objectives were, and still are,
about controlling physical development. The Council of Europe’s European Regional/Spatial
Planning Charter (Torremolinos Charter) (CEMAT 1983) follows this line:
Regional/spatial planning gives geographical expression to the economic, social, cultural and
ecological policies of society. It is . . . an interdisciplinary and comprehensive approach
directed towards a balanced regional development and the physical organisation of space
according to an overall strategy.
(CEMAT 1983: Section 5, emphasis added)
The findings from the EU Compendium project suggest that the control of physical
development at the local level still lies at the core of the meaning of spatial planning for most
countries, with very few exceptions. In all EU countries there is a sector of government activity
that is concerned with regulating physical land use change. This sector is represented by a
government ministry or department that to a greater or lesser extent also seeks to coordinate
the land use impacts of other sectors of government, such as transport, economic development,
agriculture, environment, health and others. Planning is just one sector of government activity
that rarely has any special inﬂuence over the activities of other departments other than the
competence to regulate physical development. It is often relatively weak (CEC 1997a).
Professional planners in member states have compared many aspects of domestic spatial
planning and made joint statements about their relevance and value so as to raise its status 
and to promote better land use regulation more widely in Europe. This is not ‘European

A model of European spatial planning?
183
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 213

spatial planning’ because it involves a fundamentally different conception of planning to this
conventional understanding.
In the European model suggested here, spatial planning is not positioned as a sector of
government regulating physical development but as a cross-governmental activity intended to
guide and integrate the spatial development impacts of all sectoral policies. It is not about
passively regulating the physical development activities of sectors but about proactively
anticipating and integrating the spatial impacts of sectoral policies. It identiﬁes and seeks to
address the contradictions and opportunities for synergy and complementarity of spatial policy.
It is a method of securing ‘convergence and co-ordination between various sectoral policies’
through a territorial development strategy (CEC 1998a: 7) (see also Bastrup-Birk and Doucet
1997). The object of planning is not land use but spatial organisation, which is concerned with
spatial development in a broader sense including the quality of physical, social and economic
changes and their relationships. It can operate at different scales though it is particularly closely
related to the EU’s Regional or Cohesion Policy. One of the sectors subject to spatial
coordination would be land-use planning.
The distinction made here is by no means deﬁnitive or unqualiﬁed, but rather a starting point
that reﬂects the general tenor of the literature on the nature of European spatial planning. 
For example, both Albrechts (1997) and Faludi (2002a) make the distinction between conventional (or traditional) regulatory forms of planning, which tend to dominate at national levels, 
and the European model of spatial planning. Faludi (2002a: 7) explains how in the 1990s the
European Commission favoured the terms ‘spatial development policy’ because of the ‘restrictive
connotations of spatial planning as a concept’. Thus, spatial development ‘signals a move away
from regulation toward capital programs and the like . . . [and] a willingness to engage in publicprivate partnership to achieve public goals’ (Faludi 2002a: 8). European spatial planning (or
spatial development policy) will not (and cannot) replace the regulation of land use and design
of place, which are necessary for the implementation of cross-sectoral spatial planning. However,
member states have shared in the resurgence of interest in strategic spatial planning (Healey 
et al. 1997), informed in part by their joint activity at the EU level.
The contrast being made may seem rather ﬁne. The models are simpliﬁcations of the complex
mixture of approaches in reality. Also, this is a moving target; practices in member states are
changing. Kunzmann (2006b: 43) argues that ‘in essence, there is not much difference’, but this
underplays the variety of approaches to planning in the member states. Also, there are distinct
emphases about the scope and role of European spatial planning at the core of the principal
supranational and transnational statements such as the ESDP, the Territorial Agenda and the
CEMAT Guiding Principles. These and other statements offer guidance for the way planning is
done at the national level, but they also promote a particular model or style of spatial planning.
European spatial planning has emerged as response to the contradictory impacts of spatial
impacts of EU sectoral policies, which require coordination in all types of territories, and 
as an expression of the political objective of achieving more balanced development across the
Community in order to address disparities. The ‘model’ of European spatial planning as it 
has been pursued to date is thus centrally concerned with conﬂicting spatial policies. Williams
(1996: 7) explains spatial policy as ‘any Community policy that is spatially speciﬁc in practice,
whether or not it is deliberately designed to be’. The impact of spatial policies demands
cooperation and coordination of policy horizontally (between sectors), vertically (between levels
of government) and geographically (across borders). As the ESDP states, ‘cooperation is the key
to an integrated spatial development policy and represents added value over sectoral policies
acting in isolation’ (CSD 1999: 35).
A reminder should be given here about the quotation at the beginning of this chapter.
Positioning spatial planning in this way may seem unrealistic or even naïve. Kunzmann (1998a:
184
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 214

101) suggests that planners’ ‘strategic policy papers are full of quests for more, and better
horizontal and vertical coordination of actors, institutions or interest groups . . . to achieve
spatial equity . . . the unfulﬁlled dream of the socially minded planning community’. While this
is a valid warning, it is not an argument against addressing the obvious contradictory spatial
effects of non-coordination (Robert et al. 2001). This argument is explained in Chapter 2. In
the next section we explain how the European ‘model’ has been informed by French and Dutch
contributions.
The French and Dutch contributions
The regional economic and integrated comprehensive models of planning have been most
important in the European debate and parallel respective contributions by the French and the
Dutch. The French system of aménagement du territoire is by far the strongest example of
regional economic planning model. It is a proactive approach concerned with ‘interventions of
the national administration to promote development in less favored regions’ (Faludi 2002a: 6).
The term ‘regional planning’ as used in the Torremolinos Charter (CEMAT 1983) has been
interpreted as referring to this model of planning, that is, in the sense of reducing regional
disparities in European regions so as to achieve cohesion of the territory as a whole (Faludi
2002a). Care must be taken not to confuse this with the regional planning that is undertaken
by the regions and seeks to organise land use within sub-national territories. An alternative term
for interregional planning (as described by the Torremolinos Charter) is ‘national-regional
planning’ (Hall 2002). Though some countries now reject regional policy for all but the most
critical situations, it remains at the core of the French model of spatial planning, supplemented
by a regulatory code-based urbanism model. It is associated with the use of indicative plans and
spatial scenarios and is concerned with the whole of the national territory. It is bound up with
the concept of spatial equity in the sense of an ‘obligation on the part of the central state to
guarantee equal living conditions to its citizens’, which ‘echoes the same concern’ as European
Cohesion Policy (Faludi 2009a: 11). Signiﬁcantly, it was the model of planning on the table at
the ﬁrst meeting concerning the ESDP (Faludi 2004a). The French have continued to inject this
model into the European spatial planning debate subsequently, particularly in relation to the
concept of territorial cohesion, as explained below.
Spatial planning in the Netherlands has had a strong regulatory and generally restrictive style,
but it is also an exemplar of the comprehensive integrated model. It has, since the 1970s,
followed a ‘sector facet model’ where ‘planning was considered a multidimensional policy . . .
and therefore had been given a sort of coordinating role’ (Waterhout 2008: 163). It has also
exhibited strong vertical relations between national and local levels (as the centre has sought to
determine outcomes) and successive national spatial planning documents have sought to
coordinate the spatial dimension of other sectors (Nadin and Stead 2008). In relative terms the
Netherlands exhibits a model of integrated spatial planning. As a small and prosperous country,
it has also understood the importance of cross-border transnational impacts, and taken a leading
role in the emergence of European spatial planning ‘arguably to try to promote or export
concepts and processes from the Dutch spatial planning system’ (Nadin and Stead 2008: 43).
Although the Netherlands is much less likely to support a strong regional policy planning
approach than France, the two models are mutually reinforcing in their effect on the European
model. Waterhout (2008: 45) argues that the European spatial planning approach ‘comes 
very close to the comprehensive integrated planning approach that is common practice in, for
example, Germany and the Netherlands, but which is in turn increasingly inspired by French
ideas too’. In different ways they focus attention on spatial planning as spatial policy coordination. Thus, ‘spatial planning adds an extra dimension to spatial policies: one of deliberateness
