## S. 215

. . . Spatial planning is, thus, the systematic preparation of spatial policies’ (Faludi 2002a: 6,
emphasis in original). Faludi (2002a) offers an alternative view of the impact of the four models
of spatial planning at the EU level and emphasises more the question of competence. He links
the comprehensive integrated approach and its organisation or hierarchy of plans much more
to a regulatory mode of planning and thus one that requires specific allocation of legal
competence because it will interfere with property rights. He argues that the regional economic
model does not require any deﬁnition in law to underpin government action because it is ‘implied
in the powers to engage in public works, grant subsidies and engage in other policies that help
improve the quality of locations’ (Faludi 2002a: 7). Therefore he sees the regional economic
approach as more amenable to the EU situation where legal competences are missing because
they are not needed to improve spatial policy coordination. However, this may underestimate
the importance of strategic ‘spatial organising’ within an integrated model and the identiﬁcation
of the spatial impacts of sectoral policies. The view taken here is that both models offer ways
to develop spatial strategies that can inﬂuence sectoral policy.
Transnational strategies, collaboration and learning
The nature of a transnational planning instrument or ‘plan’ will differ from the various forms
of strategies and plans that are employed at the national and regional levels. The scale, scope
and range of interests involved at the transnational level are obviously very different to the norm
for plans and strategies within member states. Also, there is no law or guidance that speciﬁes
the characteristics of transnational strategies. Research on the characteristics of transnational
planning instruments is slowly emerging, for example, on spatial visioning (see Nadin 2002;
Zonneveld 2005b, and Chapter 16) and transnational cooperation (see, for example, De Vries
2002). This work builds on well-established concepts to provide some theoretical pointers on
planning instruments at the transnational level. The distinction between ‘strategic plans’ and
‘project plans’ is a useful starting point for explaining how the nature of European and
transnational planning instruments will differ from national practice. Faludi (2000b) (drawing
on Mastop 2000 and Faludi and Mastop 1982) approaches this from the perspective of planning as a decision-making procedure. He explains that a typical project plan is a blueprint type
plan, and its success is evaluated on the extent to which outcomes conform to the plan. This
approach is to be found in the traditional continental European approach to zoning. Faludi
(2000b: 302) quotes the Centre for Environmental Studies (1970: 16) to explain how a strategic
plan differs: it is ‘not so much concerned with producing a plan as with gaining a better understanding of the problems with which we are faced now and in the future, in order that we can
make better decisions now’. European spatial planning is concerned with such strategic plans.
The strategic planning process in this sense is concerned with the learning experience that the
strategy offers and the interaction between planners and other actors involved in spatial development. Faludi (2001a: 664) has suggested that ‘applying any strategic planning document aims
at shaping the minds of actors involved in spatial development’. A strategy is concerned with
‘framing’ the understanding of actors (who may have much stronger powers of implementation)
to particular understandings of problem and possible solutions, not prescribing solutions. It will
draw different sectoral actors together around a shared understanding.
From the perspective of strategies as framing mechanisms, Faludi (2001a) has therefore
argued that it is more appropriate to speak of their ‘application’ than their ‘implementation’.
Implementation assumes a direct and measurable impact of the plan, whereas application refers
to a reinterpretation of policy options in an interactive process. Similarly, in evaluating European
spatial planning we should talk of performance rather than conformance (Faludi 2000b; Janin
Rivolin 2008). European and transnational strategies are not plans whose performance can be
186
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 216

measured directly against outcomes; rather, they will be performing if they play ‘a tangible role
in the choices of the actors’ (Faludi 2000b: 306). De Vries (2002) makes a similar point in
proposing two ways that planning instruments seek to inﬂuence change – a ‘programming’
approach and a ‘communication’ approach. Programming refers to the direct implementation
of plans with instruments that can guide and control the decisions of others. The communication
approach gives emphasis instead to providing an interpretive framework. De Vries (2002)
reaches the same conclusion as Faludi regarding the evaluation of the effects of planning, which
must be sought in changes to the reference framework of actors rather than the direct outcomes
in spatial development. De Vries (2002) further notes the lack of tools and powers in European
spatial planning to directly implement policy. Indeed, even at the national and regional levels
strategic spatial planning is relatively weak, and it is difﬁcult to impose even apparently
compulsory elements on other actors who have discretion to decide where and when they act
or not. For these reasons attention in strategic planning generally has turned more to its potential
role in inﬂuencing the reference framework of crucial actors.
An example of how actors’ understanding of spatial development or their worldviews can
be inﬂuenced that is particularly important at the transnational scale is ‘spatial positioning’
(Williams 1996: 97). Chapter 5 explains how administrative boundaries deﬁne the geographical
horizon of planning practice (or spatial positioning) and how European and transnational spatial
planning has brought other transboundary spatial relationships into the picture, though generally
only to a limited degree. In this framing and reframing task, spatial concepts and images are
especially useful and powerful tools (Dühr 2007; Faludi 1996). Spatial concepts and
visualisations are part of what Needham et al. (1997) have described as the ‘vision model’ of
strategy-making. This is the primary approach to strategy-making in transnational spatial
planning, where plan-makers try to make a plan ‘so powerful and attractive that all those whose
support is needed willingly range up behind it. The plan challenges and motivates, it convinces’
(Needham et al. 1997: 876) through promotion of a strong vision of how the territory should
develop. In the alternative ‘consultation model’ the plan-makers enter into discussions with those
who can directly shape the physical environment (the target groups) at an early stage of the plan
preparation, with the aim of generating support and commitment for the new plan. With the
multitude of actors and interests involved, the consultation model has limited applicability at
the transnational scale. Instead the ‘vision model’ is a much better approximation, and explains
too why ‘packaging’ and presentation, and spatial concepts and visualisations are so important,
albeit politically difﬁcult to negotiate (Dühr 2007; see Chapter 15).
Healey (2006b) takes the notion of a strategy as a way of understanding the world much
further. She draws on different sources in social and political thought to explain that planning
is a social process ‘through which ways of thinking, ways of valuing and ways of acting are
actively constructed by participants’ (p. 29). She deepens the notion of strategy as ‘inclusive
argumentation’ (p. 261). Strategies are ‘deliberate efforts in collaborative social mobilisation,
the objective of which is to transform policy discourse’. Successful strategies are about changing
the way participants see the world by establishing a new discourse. They innovate by ‘re-framing
issues and enabling new types of actions to be thought about’ (p. 267). The communicative role
of strategies is reﬂected in the notion of ‘planning as learning’ that has developed strongly in
relation to transnational spatial planning, highlighting the importance of interaction among
groups of professional experts or epistemic communities (see, for example, Böhme 2006;
Colomb 2007; Dühr et al. 2007; Farthing and Carrière 2007; Pallagst 2006b). Through this
interaction, learning takes place and a common ‘discourse’ of assumptions and concepts
becomes accepted such that participants share understandings or core beliefs about issues and
how they should be addressed and jointly promoted (Hajer 1995; Sabatier and Jenkins-Smith
1999). Thus writers now commonly talk of a European spatial planning discourse (Jensen and

## S. 217

Richardson 2004). The European discourse pervades this book, of course, but readers are
encouraged to maintain a sceptical and critical perspective on the coherence of this discourse.
In particular, the claim that the hegemony (or dominance in thinking) of the European model
naturally leads to greater similarity in policy systems (Falkner 2001) is not so certain insofar as
it applies to spatial planning models (see Chapters 23 and 24).
Territorial cohesion
From the 2000s the problem of deﬁning a form of European spatial planning has shifted to the
objective of territorial cohesion. Article 2.3 of the Treaty of Lisbon includes the statement that
‘it [the Union] shall promote economic, social and territorial cohesion, and solidarity among
Member States’ (Council 2007c).
There is no clear and politically agreed deﬁnition of the objective of territorial cohesion (see
Davoudi 2005a; Faludi 2005a; Molle 2007), but there is an acknowledgement in the academic
and policy debate that territorial cohesion is a multi-dimensional concept with at least three main
components:
•
territorial quality: the quality of the living and working environment; comparable living
standards across territories; similar levels of access to services of general interest and to
knowledge;
•
territorial efﬁciency: resource efﬁciency with respect to energy, land and natural resources;
competitiveness of the economic fabric and attractiveness of the territory; internal and
external accessibility; capacity for resistance against de-structuring forces related to the
globalisation process; territorial integration and cooperation between regions; and
•
territorial identity: presence of ‘social capital’ and a sense of belonging that translate into
a competitive advantage of each place and territory (Battis and Kersten 2008).
Throughout the 2000s the discussion in European planning circles was dominated by the elaboration of territorial cohesion. Early discussions had made a strong connection between this
concept and the European model of spatial planning. The Third Cohesion Report (CEC 
2004a) and the special Interim Report on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2004b) deﬁned territorial
cohesion in terms of ‘the balanced distribution of human activities across the Union [and] . . .
fair access to all citizens and economic operators to services of general economic interest
irrespective of the territory to which they belong’. The Committee of the Regions deﬁnes
territorial cohesion in direct relation to spatial planning as ‘reducing development disparities and
physical or economic dislocation within European regions by means of spatial planning and
other public policies with a territorial impact’ (CoR 2003: 1). The Dutch took a similar view,
arguing that territorial cohesion means ‘incorporating a spatial planning perspective into
decisions that are now made primarily on economic and social grounds’ (VROM 2004: 4). Thus
territorial cohesion draws attention to the ‘de facto’ territorial consequences (the spatial
development impacts) of the interaction of disparate EU policies in particular places. The Fourth
Report on Economic and Social Cohesion (CEC 2007b) refers to territorial cohesion in the same
vein, pointing to continuing disparities and calling for a deeper integration of spatial policy –
the territorial impacts of sectoral policies.
Faludi (2007a: 12) argues that the publication of the Territorial Agenda (EU Ministers 2007a)
and the Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2008c) are indications that the ‘ministers
[of the EU member states] have come to ﬁnally accept that the EU – and thus the Commission
– needs to have a territorial cohesion policy’. The member states have accepted a model of spatial
planning that is different and separate from the spatial planning that is undertaken in their
188
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 218

territories. The Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2008c: 10) explains that the debate
on territorial cohesion:
has not progressed as far as it might . . . partly because of a perception in the Member States
that national – or regional – competence over matters concerning land use and development
planning was open for discussion. It should be made clear that it is in no way intended to
call into question national and regional competences in these areas.
Needless to say, the notion of territorial cohesion was welcomed, indeed partly instigated,
by the planning policy communities. From the early 2000s territorial cohesion was fully
embedded in the European spatial planning discourse. The appearance of territorial cohesion
in the draft Constitution is another example of the inﬂuence on the management of regional 
and territorial issues in the Commission. Faludi (2009a) explains how it was the French
national planning agency DATAR that proposed the inclusion of territorial cohesion (a popular
concept in French regional policy) into the debate on the Constitution. He notes also how the
Commission’s interest in the ESDP waned in the belief that the notion of territorial cohesion
would give it an alternative means to bring the spatial dimension into sectoral policy. In this
respect, it has been argued that territorial cohesion ‘is taking over the role of spatial development
policy in the post-ESDP process’ (David 2007: 12), with the discourse on territorial cohesion
adding ‘a spatial justice dimension to European spatial policy’ (Davoudi 2007: 84) or providing
a spatial dimension of the European social model (Davoudi 2009). But to invoke territorial
cohesion policy is much the same as invoking spatial planning or a particular model of European spatial planning, as explained above. The ongoing debate around the concept of territorial
cohesion is helping to distinguish the characteristics of the European spatial planning model from
other domestic national models.
Conclusion
This chapter concludes by applying the criteria used to distinguish national models or styles of
spatial planning, introduced above, to the European model of spatial planning. This will
provide a systematic review of the nature of the model and allow for comment on some
remaining questions.
First, the scope of the model is very wide. The subject is not land use but spatial development
in its widest sense. It embraces the spatial distribution of physical development and human
activities, but also, because of the underlying principle of cohesion, the changing qualities of
economic and social life and fair access to opportunities in employment, health, recreation and
the like. Second, European spatial planning is not concerned with land use regulation but is
about strategic spatial planning, which provides positive direction to mobilise and integrate
coalitions of interests around a common agenda, and shared deﬁnitions of decision situations
(Albrechts 2001b; Faludi 2009a; Healey 2006b). Note that European policies and actions do
have implications for government regimes regulating physical land use change, but this is not
the concern of the European spatial planning model. The spatial focus is on the region at
different scales. These are primarily regions deﬁned by functional relations that cut across
boundaries, either cross-border or transnational.
Third, the locus of power is in principle with the member states and regions that collaborate
supranationally or transnationally on strategic spatial planning. In practice the initiative rests
with a small group of professional experts and there is little involvement of stakeholders.
Questions are raised about the legitimacy of statements in European spatial planning because
it is ‘a very closed shop’ (Albrechts 2001b: 300). The emergence of European spatial planning

A model of European spatial planning?

## S. 219

has also been marked by continuing debate about the application of the subsidiarity principle
and the legitimate scope and role of spatial planning at the EU level (explained in Chapter 2).
This is now changing with acceptance by the member states of their joint ‘spatial planning
competences’ at the EU level centered in the concept of territorial cohesion, which helps to
distance the idea of spatial policy coordination to spatial planning in the member states.
The European spatial planning model is deeply embedded in a public sector directed notion
of spatial planning, demonstrating the inﬂuence of the French regional economic planning and
Dutch comprehensive integrated models. Both have a strong public sector plan-led rationale.
Planning is inevitably a government-led activity, but the need to incorporate market and civil
society interests is a challenge yet to be addressed by the European model. The tensions that
there may be between the market and ‘active interventions’ (Albrechts 2001b: 297) in the
interests of sustainable development and social cohesion are not very well addressed so far. The
territorial focus of strategic spatial planning ‘provides a promising basis for encouraging levels
of government to work together and in partnership with actors in diverse positions in the
economy and civil society’ (Albrechts et al. 2003, with reference to Fürst 2001; Kunzmann
2001). Finally, the European spatial planning model is very much an unﬁnished project that has
in practical terms barely entered the political arena. However, the foregoing discussion is
intended to convince readers that there is sufﬁcient agreement about its general and distinctive
characteristics to warrant the term ‘model’.
Further reading
For an alternative approach to explaining the European model of spatial planning see Waterhout
(2008), Chapters 2 and 5, which explain four underlying storylines or discourses. The series of
papers in which Albrechts (1997, 1999, 2001b, 2004, 2006) has examined the theory and
practice of strategic spatial planning covers the points above and many more. This discussion
can usefully be informed by understanding the inﬂuence of many streams of social and political
theory on planning and the primary ‘traditions’ of planning thought that reﬂect them, as
explained by Friedmann (1987) and Healey (2006b). Collaborative Planning (Healey 2006b) is
to be particularly recommended for its review of the inﬂuence on planning of many streams of
social and political theories and its advocacy of a collaborative and inclusive model of planning
practice.
190
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 220

The emergence of the European
spatial planning agenda
Planners are notorious internationalists.
(Faludi and Waterhout 2002: 2)
Introduction
The European dimension of spatial planning has emerged in incremental tentative steps over
many years. This chapter presents a brief chronology of the emergence of European spatial
planning, setting this in the broader context of the evolution of the EU. The major events in the
history of the EU and European spatial planning are compared in Table 14.1.
Davies et al. (1994) divide up the history of the EU (when discussing spatial planning) into
ﬁve periods relating to the successive Treaties extending the powers of the Community (see also
Nadin 1999). This review follows a similar approach, dividing the emergence of European
spatial planning into four major periods separated by major Treaty revisions:
•
the formative years to 1985 of early experiments in cross-border and transnational planning;
•
from 1986 and the Single European Act with major extensions of Community competences;
•
from 1992 and the Treaty on European Union (1992) with expansion of cooperation on
spatial planning; and
•
the 2000s with a shifting agenda towards territorial cohesion.
1949 to 1985: laying the foundations of spatial policy
The story begins not with the EU but with the Council of Europe (CoE). The Council was
created in 1949 to provide a forum for exchanges on social, legal and cultural issues and to
promote and uphold common values (see Chapter 10 for further explanation). Until the late
1980s it provided the main arena for international debate on spatial development and planning
in Europe – and although the actions of the EU tend to dominate now, it still plays an
important role. In 1970 the Council established a conference of European Ministers of
aménagement du territoire (CEMAT) (using the French term used to describe the national and
regional planning system). This followed publication of a Council of Europe study calling for
a European level dimension to regional policy (Fit and Kragt 1994; Kunzman et al. 1977). The
rationale was simply that a common approach was needed to regional planning so that
European integration did not aggravate existing geographical differences (Williams 1996).
CEMAT began work in the 1970s on a European regional/spatial planning strategy to cover
the whole of Europe, though as explained below, that was not completed until 2000. In the

## S. 225

meantime, CEMAT promoted a string of conferences on aspects of European spatial development (Martin and Robert 2002), the publication of Towards a European Regional Planning
Concept in 1981 and the European Regional Planning Charter in 1983, which is also known
as the Torremolinos Charter after the town where it was adopted by Ministers (CEMAT 1983).
This was the ﬁrst major joint international statement on European spatial planning.
The CoE also promoted a European dimension in domestic planning issues including the
‘urban environment’, which eventually led to the adoption of the European Urban Charter
(1992b). The poor state of many of Europe’s cities and towns promoted dialogue on the
principles of good urban management, and a ‘European Campaign for Urban Renaissance’ was
launched in 1980, including resolutions on health in towns and community development. Later
on the European Commission’s Directorate-General (DG) for Environment was to take up a
similar agenda (see Chapter 21). These activities brought together ‘experts’ in spatial planning,
mostly senior civil servants, academics and consultants, in regular meetings in various guises.
The Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), the United Nations
Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) and the institutions of the EU, among others, were
raising questions about regional spatial development and planning. Through this extensive
interaction a small and exclusive community of experts on European spatial planning began to
form. Many of these people were (and remain) convinced of their task and they seized on
opportunities to move forward on ‘the long road to European spatial planning’ (Fit and Kragt
1994: 461).
In the same year as the CoE Torremolinos Charter was adopted (CEMAT 1983) the
European Parliament took up the case for the spatial and temporal coordination of EU sector
policies by means of a European Regional Planning Scheme, proposed in the Gendebien Report
(EP 1983). Although the recommendations for the Commission to prepare such a scheme did
not progress, the territorial objectives it outlined of balanced regional development and the
protection of Europe’s cultural and environmental diversity were to be reiterated in later policy
documents. It was during this period that EU membership expanded, introducing more variety
to its geography and increasing economic disparities. Denmark, Ireland and the UK joined in
1973 (negotiations for entry had begun in 1961). Greece joined in the second enlargement in
1981. The inclusion of Ireland and Greece in particular raised awareness of regional differentials
in environmental and economic conditions.
The Gendebien Report argued that no additional powers were necessary for the Community
to act on regional planning. The Treaty of Rome speciﬁcally addressed disparities of wealth
between regions, ‘requiring the Community to ensure harmonious development by reducing
regional differences and the backwardness of less-favoured regions’ (Michie and Fitzgerald 1997:
14). Spatial issues were addressed in the early stages of the EU by, for example, special measures to promote employment in poorer regions using the European Social Fund. The European
Regional Development Fund (ERDF) was created in 1975 with the aim of correcting regional
imbalances (see Chapter 18). As early as 1972 the United Nations conference on the Human
Environment in Stockholm prompted the EU to agree to joint action on environmental issues,
again with a strong spatial dimension. Williams (1996: 67) argues that the creation of the
European Investment Bank (EIB) was the ‘most explicit recognition of the need for spatial policy’
because it is ‘an instrument of regional development’. European spatial policies, in the sense of
concrete interventions that shape the spatial distribution of investment and activity, were
therefore in place before 1985.
In planning practice, transboundary cooperation has a long history, starting with cooperation
on roads and railways in the nineteenth century (Fit and Kragt 1994). North-west Europe in
particular has had a number of practical cooperation agreements involving spatial planning. The
Conference of Regions of North-west Europe (CRONWE) was established in 1955 with a board
196
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 226

of planning ofﬁcials from ﬁve countries (the UK joined in the 1970s) (Doucet 2002). It produced
a Structure Sketch of North-west Europe and ‘deliberated about a Channel tunnel’ (Faludi and
Waterhout 2002: 2). Six countries of north-west Europe set up a European Institute for
Regional and Urban Planning (ERIPLAN) in 1970 (Waterhout 2008: 166), and the European
Commission funded a Prospective Study on Physical Planning and the Environment in the
Megalopolis in Formation in North-west Europe (ERIPLAN 1975). This had no lasting impact
(Martin and Robert 2002) – indeed, most initiatives at this time were exploratory. However,
the Economic Union of Benelux Countries’ (spatial) Structural Outline of 1986 was of sufﬁ-
cient interest to warrant updating in 1996 (Secrétariat Général de l’Union Economique Benelux
1996). By the 1980s numerous transnational or cross-border planning studies had been
undertaken by EU member states, including France, Germany and the Netherlands (Martin and
Robert 2002).
1986 to 1991: grasping opportunities after the Single European
Act
The Single European Act (SEA) of 1986 and the accession of Spain and Portugal in the third
enlargement of the Community in the same year marked the start of a distinctive period in
European integration (see Chapter 17), and with it, in European spatial planning. EU policy
development and legislative actions increased sharply, deepening the inﬂuence of the European
institutions in national affairs. It is no coincidence that this burst of activity coincides with the
term of ofﬁce of Jacques Delors as President of the European Commission. Delors, a former
French ﬁnance minister, was an ambitious and capable politician. He championed the Single
Market and was the driving force behind the policy changes and new laws needed for its
implementation (Drake 2000; Grant 1996).
Delors sought a balance between the interests of the market, the state and society to ensure
that economic growth is combined with social cohesion in a mixed economy with high levels
of social welfare and protection from inequality. Recently, this has been termed ‘the European
model of society’ (Alber 2006; Jepsen and Serrano Pascual 2005; Nadin and Stead 2008). He
promoted an equal role for the state and its role in regulation alongside the market and its role
in promoting enterprise. Planning was thus seen a natural part of the EU’s role ‘to work with
the market, but also where necessary to contain it’ (Faludi 2007b: 574). Alongside the Single
Market and European Monetary Union, Delors gave priority to strategic European scale infrastructure, especially in transport (see Chapter 19), and to the spatial reallocation of resources
through a much stronger EU regional policy (Chapter 18). The Community budget allocated 
to the Structural Funds was increased substantially. The Structural Funds were also given a
stronger spatial orientation, concentrating spending on the regions where per capita income was
less than 75 per cent of the EU average (see Chapter 18), in anticipation of the potential increase
in regional imbalances arising from the Single Market (Padoa-Schioppa 1987). Indeed, Delors
saw the management of the territory and spatial development as a natural complement to his
other policies. He took an interest in the early development of spatial planning and in 1989
attended the ﬁrst informal meeting of EU spatial planning ministers in Nantes when important
decisions were made for the EU to take forward a spatial planning agenda, as explained below
(Faludi and Waterhout 2002).
The SEA also included new formal powers for the EU to promote common actions to
‘preserve and protect the quality of the environment and . . . ensure the prudent and rational
utilisation of natural resources’. A steady ﬂow of European legislation on environmental issues
followed, including the well-known Directives on environmental assessment (1985), wild birds

The emergence of the European spatial planning agenda

## S. 227

(1979) and natural habitats (1992) (as explained in Chapter 21). These are probably the bestknown European laws in the spatial planning community. Indeed, planning professionals were
centrally involved in their creation. This is an important point. The impetus given to European
integration under the Delors presidency was an opportunity seen and taken by advocates of
environmental management and spatial planning in the member states and the European
institutions. It is at about this time, too, that there was a rapid expansion of representation of
lobby groups in Brussels (as explained in Chapter 10).
Given the growing strength of the EU from the mid-1980s following the completion of the
Single Market programme and its relatively strong resource base (in the context of favourable
international economic conditions), it is not surprising that the main arena for debate on the
European dimension to spatial planning moved from the Council of Europe’s CEMAT to the EU.
Here we should emphasise that when authors refer to the Commission, DG REGIO, the member states and other ‘actors’ in this process, they actually refer to a small number of ‘experts’
who were convinced of the argument for spatial planning at the EU level. They were civil servants
working in the Commission and member states, a few national ofﬁcials who were seconded to
the European Commission as ‘national experts’, and leading ﬁgures from planning consultancies 
and academia. The responsibility (and credit) for the emergence of European spatial planning
rests very much with this small group.
The full story of the emerging agenda on European spatial planning from 1989 to 2000 is
told in detail in Faludi and Waterhout (2002). An outline of the plot is given here. The key
players were French and Dutch. They began to draw the attention of their own organisations
to the supranational level of spatial development and planning, to cooperate and share ideas in
loose networks and to sponsor speciﬁc studies and research to develop a better understanding
of the trends affecting spatial development at the European level. In 1989, the French
government planning agency Délegation à l’Aménagement du Territoire et à l’Action Régionale
(DATAR) (now Délegation Interministérielle à l’Aménagement et à la Compétitivité des
Territoires (DIACT)) published an analysis of spatial development trends at the supranational
scale that were important to France (Brunet 1989). The report pointed out the increasing
concentration of economic growth in the heartland of Europe – the now infamous Dorsale or
‘Blue Banana’ – an area of high economic development stretching from London through the
Benelux and Germany to northern Italy, but bypassing Paris (as explained in Chapter 5). The
report argued that areas outside the Blue Banana might be at a competitive disadvantage, and
this included most of France. Thus the report was inﬂuential in France and elsewhere in drawing
attention to the national implications of what were transnational development trends.
The Netherlands followed with a report entitled Perspectives on Europe (Rijksplanologische
Dienst 1991), which made an explicit call for attention to spatial planning at the Community
level to address the consequences of the Single Market. The argument was that active spatial
development policies were needed to maximise beneﬁts from the reduced effects of national
borders. Other member states were also beginning to recognise the transnational aspects of
spatial development. The German government produced a number of planning studies on the
effects of reuniﬁcation and development patterns on the east (Fit and Kragt 1994). The Danish
government published a national plan Denmark Towards the Year 2018 (Ministry of Spatial
Planning (Denmark) 1994) with an analysis of the Danish spatial structure in its European
context. The countries around the Baltic Sea region began to cooperate on an analysis of
changing spatial development trends in the region as the former eastern bloc states were
opened to the west. This led to the production of the joint Vision and Strategies around the
Baltic Sea 2010 (VASAB 2010 1994).
Contributions also came from the regional level with cooperation among regions beginning
in the 1980s to lobby the EU for support to address their speciﬁc problems, especially for those
198
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 228

regions in the periphery. The French Region of Brittany initiated the Conference of Peripheral
and Maritime Regions (CPMR) in the early 1970s, which argued against growing disparity 
in prosperity between the core and periphery (see Chapter 10). Within CPMR the political
grouping of regions in the Atlantic Arc (or Espace Atlantique or Atlantic Area) was particularly
important, arguing successfully for Community funding to support cooperative activities among
its member regions to tackle the problems of peripherality (Nadin and Shaw 2000). Once this
funding was in place, the Atlantic Arc partnership of regions drafted an Atlantis Programme of
cooperative projects on transport, tourism and other themes. This was never realised, but the
argument was won. The European Commission later extended the idea of transnational
cooperation on spatial planning to the whole of the EU through the INTERREG initiative. Other
transnational coalitions of interests with varying interests in spatial planning and urban development were also formed around this time, especially among regional and local governments
sharing similar types of problems. The creation of networks such as Eurocities, METREX 
and Quartiers en Crise is discussed in Chapter 10. Cooperation among universities was also stimulated through the setting up of the Erasmus programme for student mobility and the creation
of the Association of European Schools of Planning in 1987 (as explained in Chapter 24).
The actions of member states and regions in recognising transnational spatial development
issues and in establishing cooperation created the right conditions for more formal action to be
taken at the EU level. In 1989 EU ministers responsible for spatial and regional planning met
together for the ﬁrst time in Nantes. Such meetings were to become regular events, as shown in
Table 14.2. The ministerial meetings are ‘informal’, which means they are not part of the
business of regular European Council meetings described in Chapter 9, but they have provided
an impetus to further cooperation on supranational planning (and continue to do so). The work
of analysis, negotiation and consensus building is done by senior government ofﬁcials: from 1991
this took place in regular meetings of the Committee on Spatial Development (CSD). The CSD,
which has since then been disbanded, was an intergovernmental body composed of senior
ofﬁcials and planners from national ministries concerned with spatial planning (two per member
state) (Faludi and Waterhout 2002: 50). The country holding the presidency of the EU chaired
the CSD and led its work for a period of six months. The European Commission, DG REGIO,
acted as a secretariat for the committee, although technically the CSD was an informal
arrangement outside the ‘comitology’ system of the EU (described in Chapter 11).
The initial idea for ministerial meetings on spatial planning came from French and Dutch
planning experts with experience in both their national governments and in the European
Commission. The French broadly supported more spatial coordination of EU sector policies in
the model of the French system of aménagement du territoire. Faludi and Waterhout (2002)
report that there was some uncertainty about what was on the table for discussion at that ﬁrst
meeting in Nantes. The example of transnational cooperation in the Atlantic Arc area was
presented to illustrate the possibilities for other territories and the EU as a whole. The meeting
was not well attended, with only six ministers from the then 12 member states, but Jacques
Delors, a close colleague of the French host, attended and lent his strong support to the very
general proposals. He understood the French system of aménagement du territoire very well
from previous government experience in France, and he saw the introduction of this idea to the
EU level in a very positive way.
Faludi and Waterhout (2002) explain how other factors played a part in forming an
‘opportunity structure’ within which progress could be made towards developing a European
spatial planning agenda: key actors in France and the Netherlands believed they would proﬁt
from cooperation on spatial planning, as in much of Europe dramatic changes in spatial development were arising from the demise of the communist states, and the possible spatial
implications of the Single Market and monetary union were widely recognised (see Chapter 17).

## S. 229

In Germany the upheaval brought about by reuniﬁcation was a factor contributing to the
country’s support for the general approach. However, there was a general unwillingness among
member states to accept that the Commission had a competence in spatial planning (Faludi and
Zonneveld 1997; Nadin and Shaw 1999). So from 1989, the CSD steered the production of key
documents in an ‘intergovernmental’ approach with each presidency taking the lead in turn on
a shared agenda. This intergovernmental approach has sometimes been a precursor for the later
extension of EU competence in other ﬁelds (Edwards and Spence 1997: 17). However, intergovernmental working is slow, all parties need to agree any action, and there may be a hiatus
with each succession of the presidency.
The Commission made important contributions. Spurred on by the regional networks noted
above, in 1991 it undertook a broader analysis of European spatial development trends
published as Europe 2000: Outlook for the Development of the Community’s Territory (CEC
1991; Martin 1992). It adopted an approach to spatial analysis of functional rather than
administrative regions to encourage ‘new ways of thinking about spatial prospects which are
not limited by national boundaries’ (CEC 1994a: 169). It proposed seven transnational regions
(later to become eight with the inclusion of the former East German Länder) on the basis that

## S. 230

they encompassed areas of similar dominant spatial development issues. This was a clear
development of the arguments made by the CPMR. The coherence of these so-called ‘megaregions’ is debatable but they served the purpose of drawing attention to patterns of spatial
development that are not restricted by national borders, to the spatial issues where member
states and regions should cooperate, and thus to the potential role for a European level of spatial
planning. The transnational regions later formed the basis for cooperation zones as explained
below and shown in Figure 16.3.
1992 to 1999: from the Treaty on European Union
Through a long series of CSD meetings, ministerials, conferences and publications, the fundamental arguments for tackling spatial planning issues at the transnational and European 
scales were set down during the 1990s. The Treaty on European Union (TEU) (or the Maastricht
Treaty), signed in 1992, gave an initial boost to transnational cooperation on planning by 
strengthening the EU’s commitment to ‘cohesion’ as a primary objective alongside economic
competitiveness and environmental sustainability. The Treaty is best known for establishing the
actual expression ‘European Union’ and requiring more adherence to the principle of subsidiarity
(see Chapters 7 and 11). It also introduced into the English version of the Treaty the words ‘town
and country planning’ and ‘land use’, but this was only to make clear that whenever the Council
enacted environmental legislation affecting these fields, it had to do so by voting for it
unanimously and not by qualiﬁed majority voting (Bastrup-Birk and Doucet 1997). Nevertheless,
the TEU had substantial indirect effects in the short term through: the creation of the Committee
of the Regions (CoR) (a staunch supporter of cooperation on spatial planning); strengthening
the measures and funding for Cohesion Policy; and enhancing competences relating to the
environment and trans-European networks. In the longer term it strengthened commitments to
the Single Market and Economic and Monetary Union (EMU).
Further analysis of spatial development trends: Europe 2000+
DG REGIO continued its work on the analysis of spatial development trends at the European
scale through detailed studies of the transnational regions such as the ‘centre capitals region’
(CEC 1996a). The studies have not been widely reported though they contain striking scenario
maps illustrating spatial development patterns and trends across national and regional borders.
The evidence on which the scenarios were based is rather limited compared with the much
deeper analyses conducted during the 2000s, but they point to sometimes obvious linkages
between places separated by national borders and to shared problems and opportunities. The
ﬁndings from these and other studies were brought together in Europe 2000+: Cooperation for
European Territorial Development (CEC 1994a). The principal contributions of Europe 2000+
were its clear and bold statements on the priority spatial development issues with a European
dimension. These have, by and large, persisted through to the end of the 2000s. Europe 2000+
makes unambiguous statements about: a preferred polycentric pattern of development in
Europe; the need to avoid urban sprawl; a more rigorous protection of areas of environmental
importance; the avoidance of the abandonment of rural areas where agriculture becomes
uneconomic; the revitalisation of poor neighbourhoods; and the protection of local heritage and
traditional patterns of development.
One chapter of Europe 2000+ is dedicated to the principal means of government intervention
and implementation in spatial development: the set of tools and procedures by which
governments plan and regulate development, generally known as spatial planning systems. This
chapter was the ﬁrst output of a major DG REGIO sponsored study to produce an EU

The emergence of the European spatial planning agenda

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 231

Compendium of Spatial Planning Systems and Policies (CEC 1997a). Questions about the
capacity for public intervention in spatial development have been mostly overlooked in the
debate since then.
Emerging policies on European spatial planning and the ESDP
A series of ministerial meetings on spatial planning led to a ﬁrst formal Ministerial statement
on spatial planning in 1995: the Leipzig Principles, published by the German government
(BMBau 1995). This was a cautious statement. While the principles asserted the potential role
of spatial planning in delivering economic and social goals and sustainable development, they
also made clear the limited ambitions and non-binding nature of the European spatial planning
project. The principles foresaw a strategic policy statement on spatial development but this was
not to constrain the actions of member states and EU institutions, and it had to respect the
principle of subsidiarity. This ﬁrst major step on an emerging European spatial planning policy
should be seen in the context of the then relatively positive attitudes in the EU about the role of
the European Commission and the value of European integration.
The following work of the CSD included the preparation of (unpublished) European-wide
trendscenarios for the year 2015 coordinated by the French planning agency DATAR (see
Chapter 15). They forecast increasing polarisation of urban development as functions became
more concentrated in the core of Europe and the Alpine Arc, with a corresponding decline
through depopulation in many rural regions. New proposals came forward to use funding under
Article 10 of the ERDF regulations for spatial planning related initiatives and pilot projects.
Article 10 allowed for ‘innovative actions’ or experimental programmes to be funded under EU
Cohesion Policy (see Chapter 18).
By 1996 the EU was again discussing its constitution. An intergovernmental conference
(which was to lead to the Amsterdam Treaty) considered incorporating reference to spatial
planning in the Treaty but this was not taken forward. Progress of the CSD was intermittent,
with apparent progress at one meeting dissolving (or being ignored) at the next. Each six-month
presidential period asserted its own priorities, largely reﬂecting national priorities, while the
French, Dutch and Germans (primarily) tried to keep the project of a European spatial planning
policy moving forward. Variation in attitudes was partly about capacity (large countries can do
more) and partly about political priorities and attitudes to planning. To improve consistency,
work was delegated to a ‘troika’ of members of the CSD, which represented the ongoing,
previous and forthcoming presidencies of the EU, with the ongoing presidency taking the lead.
The CSD’s First Ofﬁcial Draft of the European Spatial Development Perspective, describing
itself as ‘an integrated multisectoral and indicative strategy for the spatial development of the
Union’, was published in 1997 under the Dutch presidency (CSD 1997: 7). Its stated aims were
‘both to raise the awareness of the various partners involved . . . and to promote the organisation
among them of a wide-ranging discussion on long-term guidelines for European Spatial
Development’ (CSD 1997: 7). A Complete Draft was published under the UK presidency in 
the following year. The ﬁnal version of the ESDP was presented to an informal ministerial at
Potsdam in 1999 under the German presidency (CSD 1999). Although the document was
prepared intergovernmentally, it was published by the European Commission’s publication ofﬁce
and translated into the then eleven ofﬁcial languages of the EU. It is without doubt the most
explicit statement of the argument for and principles of a European dimension to spatial
planning. (A fuller explanation of the content is given in Chapter 15.) The main themes
established early in the process are clearly recognisable: calls for ‘more balanced development’
and ‘a polycentric approach’ remain, with stronger statements about the need to ‘avoid further
excessive economic and demographic concentration in the core area of the EU’ (CSD 1999: 20).
202
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 232

The content reﬂects the concerns of its key protagonists in the EU-15, so it tends to have a northwestern European and urban orientation. The ESDP was prepared by 15 member states of the
EU, and following the eastern enlargement of the Union there were calls for a revision of the
document, although this was not pursued.
In the case of the ESDP preparation, the routine planning challenge of ﬁnding consensus was
on a grand scale, which explains its long gestation. At the time of its inception there were 12
member states, and by 1994 15, with more observers from future member states and EFTA
countries. The ESDP was prepared by the member states and the Commission working in close
cooperation within the CSD over a period of ten years. The working method has been unique,
based on shared decision-making and consensus building (see Faludi and Waterhout 2002 for
a detailed account). The level of input from various member states in the ESDP process varied
over time, and was inﬂuenced by domestic conceptions of spatial planning, planning cultures
and national agendas towards the EU and European integration. French, Dutch and German
planning traditions played an inﬂuential role (Faludi 2004a). Within each country there were
different attitudes to such a project from powerful regions and policy sectors depending on 
their position in European spatial development, regional cultures and planning traditions. The
ESDP process has thus been very dependent on the personalities, linguistic skills, dedication 
and support given by various national administrations and a small ‘roving band of planners’
(Faludi 1997). The ESDP is undoubtedly a major achievement that has had a considerable
impact on planning thought and practice in many places, for example in Italy (Janin Rivolin
2003), the United Kingdom (Shaw and Sykes 2003, 2005) and even the United States (Yaro
2002). It remains a central reference for scholars of European spatial planning.
Publication of the ESDP spurred on the Council of Europe’s CEMAT (which shared some
of the same actors) into a new bout of activity at the end of the 1990s and eventually publication of Guiding Principles for Sustainable Spatial Development of the European Continent
(CEMAT 2000). This advisory document presents strategic spatial policy options much in 
the same way as the ESDP with the intention of improving social cohesion among its then 
41 member states. It is able to refer back to the longer history of CoE interest in spatial development including the European Regional Planning Strategy of 1983. Like the ESDP it promotes
balanced polycentric development.
Following completion of the final version of the ESDP, the CSD prepared an action
programme to explain and apply the document (see Table 15.1). The ﬁrst of three sets of actions
was directed to promoting consideration of the spatial impacts of sectoral policies in sectoral
strategies and plans at all levels. The second part made proposals for improving knowledge,
research and information on territorial development (including getting spatial development issues
into school curricula); and the third part was concerned with preparing for the enlargement of
the territory of the EU, including more research examining the spatial development impacts of
enlargement.
The Action Programme also included a pilot action to test proposals for a research network
on spatial development. The CSD had for some time expressed the need for more concerted and
coordinated European level research on spatial development to complement its policy development work. Initially a European observatory was proposed, but it was not possible to reach
agreement on a legal framework (Faludi and Waterhout 2002). The pilot action ran from
December 1998 to the spring of 2000 as the ‘Study Programme on European Spatial Planning’
(SPESP). It was built on ‘focal points’ in each member state led by the Nordic Research Institute
Nordregio. Its primary work was on deﬁning indicators of spatial development at the European
scale and on studying urban-rural relationships, and on cartographically representing the ESDP
policy options. The work was extensive and certainly paved the way for much more research
activity later.

The emergence of the European spatial planning agenda
203
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 233

Notwithstanding the Action Programme, the most signiﬁcant application of the ESDP has
been through the Community Initiative INTERREG, which ran in parallel with the emergence
of the ESDP. It was to become the principal means by which many thousands of planning
practitioners across Europe were to engage with European spatial planning. INTERREG, as one
of the main instruments of European spatial planning and territorial cooperation, is explained
in detail in Chapters 16 and 22. It was launched in 1990 with a budget of EUR 1 billion, 
with an emphasis on cross-border cooperation. A second phase from 1994 increased funding
to EUR 3.5 billion and extended the areas for cooperation. In 1997 transnational and interregional components were added. The ﬁrst enabled cooperation among bodies in large transnational zones that were based around the Europe 2000 mega-regions; the second provided for
cooperation across the whole of the EU.
The guidelines for INTERREG also called for transnational strategies or ‘visions’ following
the model of the Baltic States’ Vision and Strategies around the Baltic Sea 2010 (VASAB 2010
1994, 1996; CSD-BSR 1995). Five such visions with quite different characteristics were prepared
and published around 2000. Each seeks to link the broad principles of the ESDP with the many
cooperation projects taking place under INTERREG programmes by setting an agenda for
cooperation (Dühr et al. 2005; Nadin 2002). The role of the visions (explained more fully in
Chapter 15) was not carried forward into the current round of territorial cooperation, though
they certainly had an impact on national and regional planning in some countries.
Environment and transport
Developments related to spatial planning were also under way during the 1990s in the ﬁelds of
environment and transport. The signing of the Maastricht Treaty, which coincided with the 1992
Earth Summit and its strategy for the implementation of the sustainable development concept,
strengthened the environmental protection powers of the EU. The Fifth Environmental Action
Programme: Towards Sustainability (Council 1993) proposed ambitious measures that could
assist in delivering more sustainable development, among them strategic environmental assessment. Some of these ideas came from the earlier Green Paper on the Urban Environment (CEC
1990a) and the EU Expert Group on the Urban Environment (see Chapter 21). Its report on
European Sustainable Cities (EU Expert Group 1996) contributed to the creation of the
European Sustainable Cities and Towns Campaign, which continues to be an important lobby
in European spatial planning and urban and environmental policy.
Mention should also be made here of developments in EU transport policy, on which there
had been little action before the 1990s (see Chapter 19). The opening up of central and eastern
Europe and the accession of three relatively peripheral countries in 1995 (Austria, Finland and
Sweden) gave added weight to transport issues, especially the need to improve linkages beyond
the core of Europe. The Maastricht Treaty gave some shape to the general EU transport competence by making the creation of guidelines for ‘trans-European networks’ in energy, telecommunications and transport a specific task for the Community. From this came the
designation of priority transport projects of European interest, explained further in Chapter 19.
Amsterdam Treaty
The 1990s ended in the same way as they started – looking east. The Amsterdam Treaty of 
1997 prepared the Community’s institutions for enlargement, and extended EU competences 
in employment, social policy and justice. The Treaty also made sustainable development one 
of the Union’s core objectives, and required that environmental protection be integrated into 
the deﬁnition and implementation of all other Community policies. Above all, the Amsterdam
204
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 234

Treaty introduced the notion of territorial cohesion, which was to move to centre stage in the
2000s (as explained in Chapter 13). The strategy on enlargement was taken forward in the
Community document Agenda 2000. Enlargement was to force a major readjustment of policy
and spending, in particular a reorganisation of the Structural Funds (see Chapter 12).
Enlargement was to make the need for a European dimension to spatial planning even more
evident, while at the same time making it more difﬁcult to achieve.
2000 onwards: a shift in thinking about European
spatial development
At the end of the 1990s the direction of European spatial planning became much less clear in
the context of enlargement and the increase of disparities across the EU. There was also less
conﬁdence about economic growth and more unwillingness of most member states to accept a
European lead on these issues. The ESDP was starting to gain ground in member states, with
many taking actions with reference to the document. But by the mid-2000s the agenda had
shifted decisively, with territorial cooperation programmes such as INTERREG being directed
more to economic competitiveness than to the broader policy concerns of the ESDP.
The Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda
The dominant policy theme during the 2000s was the (weak) economic position of the EU in
comparison to the United States and Asian economies. The Lisbon Agenda or Strategy (not to
be confused with the Treaty of Lisbon, see below) is the Commission’s response to the need to
strengthen the EU’s global competitiveness. It has become the overriding policy concern of the
EU and has had major impacts on the arguments for and operation of European spatial
planning and territorial cooperation. The Lisbon Agenda was adopted by the European Council
in 2000 and provided a new strategic goal for the EU: ‘to become the most competitive and
dynamic knowledge-based economy in the world capable of sustainable economic growth with
more and better jobs and greater social cohesion’ (European Council 2000a: paragraph 1.5).
The Lisbon Strategy was augmented at the Gothenburg Council in 2001 to also embrace
sustainable development, especially in relation to climate change. However, there is little doubt
that the Lisbon part of the strategy has been dominant in the rhetoric and action. Not that this
has led to any considerable achievement. A review of the strategy in 2004 pointed to the
worsening position of Europe in relation to its global competitors. The Agenda was re-launched
in 2005 (CEC 2005c) with more emphasis on directing EU funding programmes (including the
Structural Funds) towards growth and jobs.
The implications of the Lisbon and Gothenburg Agenda were that much of the EUR 340
billion for the 2007–13 period of EU Cohesion Policy was directed to the knowledge economy
(CEC 2004a), as explained further in Chapter 18. Radical changes were made to the
organisation of Cohesion Policy, with nine objectives (or categories of funding support) reduced
to three. The territorial cooperation initiative INTERREG was incorporated into the mainstream
of Cohesion Policy under a new objective, ‘European Territorial Cooperation’.
The continuing challenge from environmental policy
Although the overriding themes of EU policy-making in the 2000s have continued to be
economic, the steady growth of environmental policy and action has continued. In 2001 the ﬁrst
EU Sustainable Development Strategy was published (and contributed to the Gothenburg
Agenda). Its emphasis (explained more fully in Chapter 21) is on better policy integration, the

The emergence of the European spatial planning agenda
205
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 235

internalisation of costs of environmental damage and promoting technological innovation. The
link with spatial planning is strong. The Strategy promotes balanced regional development, the
‘greening’ of planning systems and the improvement of land-use management. It recognises that
many of its priorities rely on planning for implementation, such as integrated development
strategies for environmentally sensitive areas. The Sixth Environmental Action Programme
followed in 2002 and took a stronger line than its predecessors, including reference to ‘better
land use planning’. The Commission’s DG Environment began work on a proposed Communication on Planning and Environment quite independently from the work on spatial planning in
DG REGIO. The initiative for a communication did not progress, though similar concerns were
followed up in the Thematic Strategy on the Urban Environment published in 2006.
DG Environment ﬁrst consulted on a strategy on the urban environment in 2003, and spatial
planning has ﬁgured prominently in the proposals, with early proposals for ‘urban management
or action plans’ to improve performance on sustainability. Attitudes of member states to
radical solutions were mixed, with Germany and France in support and the UK against
(Atkinson and Mills 2005). After extensive negotiation and interim reports, the ﬁnal and much
diluted thematic strategy was published in 2006 and is guiding rather than regulatory (see
Chapter 21). Delay in the creation of the strategy was damaging. By the mid-2000s attitudes
against further environmental regulation had hardened. EU legislation was giving way to the
open method of coordination (as explained in Chapters 11 and 15), and the Lisbon Strategy
priorities became dominant.
European Constitution, the Treaty of Lisbon and territorial cohesion
The backdrop to all EU affairs during the 2000s has been the saga over the draft Constitution
for Europe and its successor, the Treaty of Lisbon. The 2004 and 2007 enlargements have put
considerable strain on the institutions and procedures of the EU, and it was widely agreed that
change was needed. Progress of a draft European Constitution, agreed by member states in 
2004, came to an abrupt halt in June 2005 when it was rejected in referenda in France and the
Netherlands (Podolnjak 2007) (see Chapter 12). A new intergovernmental conference prepared
a Reform Treaty, which was signed by heads of government on 13 December 2007 in Lisbon,
and thus it is known as the Treaty of Lisbon, fully described in Chapter 12. By November 2009
all EU member states had ratiﬁed the Treaty (after some difﬁculties in Ireland and the Czech
Republic), which entered into force on 1 December 2009.
The progress of constitutional reform is of more than passing relevance to spatial planning
since the draft Constitution had clariﬁed the difference between the Community and national
interests in spatial development and planning. It did this by inserting the concept of ‘territorial
cohesion’ into Article 3 (the Union’s objectives) of the draft Constitution. The Treaty of Lisbon
now includes ‘economic, social and territorial cohesion’ as core objectives of the EU (emphasis
added). The concept is discussed more fully in Chapter 13.
Application of the ESDP and ESPON
Early in the 2000s the CSD became a sub-group of the committee responsible for managing the
Structural Funds: the Committee on the Development and Conversion of the Regions (CDCR).
The reformed working group created on 31 May 2001 was called the Sub-Committee on 
Spatial and Urban Development (SUD). This formal status allowed the Commission to support
the new committee, and thus spatial planning, though the committee’s role has remained
‘advisory’, requiring close cooperation with the CDCR. The Commission now has a more central
role (as chair), and the relationship between spatial planning and Cohesion Policy funding is
206
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 236

reinforced. In the new member states attention has been focused on the acquis communautaire,
the full body of European law that had to be enacted before membership, and that does not
include spatial planning (see Chapter 12).
While the ESDP itself has made a considerable mark on thinking about spatial planning, the
action programme produced only limited results and quickly ran out of steam. One aspect has
proved more durable. The outcomes of SPESP (Study Programme on European Spatial Planning)
in 2000 provided sufﬁcient evidence to the Commission and member states that a more
substantial and better-funded research programme on European spatial planning was needed.
This has been addressed by the European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON)
(explained in Chapters 6 and 16). Over four years to 2006, ESPON funded 34 major studies
involving more than 600 researchers and 130 institutions and has become a major plank of
European spatial planning. Its agenda is tied closely to the ESDP (Hague and Hachmann 2008)
and subsequent statements. Sufﬁcient momentum and credibility had been gained to carry
ESPON forward into the 2007–13 funding period with a budget of EUR 45 million.
Territorial Agenda (TA)
With the pendulum swinging in the early 2000s towards economic competitiveness and the
Lisbon Agenda, the momentum behind spatial planning stalled with no informal meetings of
spatial planning ministers. In 2002 the Danish government hosted a meeting of member states
to take stock of the territorial cohesion notion and the role of the member states. The EU
enlargement of 2004, as well as the prospect of the European Commission getting the single
‘right of initiative’ to propose territorial cohesion policy if the draft EU Constitution had been
ratiﬁed, ‘urged the member states to come together and made them agree on a forward defence
strategy’ (Waterhout 2008: 10).
Subsequently a paper titled Managing the Territorial Dimension of EU Policies after
Enlargement was presented in 2003 to the formal SUD committee chaired by the Commission,
and the French held a high-level meeting of ofﬁcials in 2004. Informal meetings of ministers then
resumed, starting with the Dutch presidency in 2004 at Rotterdam. This meeting was set up to
reach agreement on the preparation of the Territorial State and Perspectives of the EU (BMVBS
2007a), a synthesis of ‘evidence’ from the ESPON programme and elsewhere. Over the next
three years this proposal was to evolve into two separate documents, the ‘state’ document and
a separate and much more political Territorial Agenda of the EU (EU Ministers 2007a). The
preparation of this document differed from the ESDP because of the emphasis on ESPON
ﬁndings and the role of a small ‘drafting group’ set up under the triple presidency.
The important milestones following the Rotterdam meeting were: a ministerial meeting under
the Luxembourg presidency where a scoping document or outline of the State and Perspectives
document was endorsed; a London meeting of ofﬁcials in 2005 at which the separation of the
political and evidence documents was agreed; and a stakeholders conference in Amsterdam in
2006. The ﬁnal drafting was led by ofﬁcers in the German government, and draft documents
were considered at subsequent meetings in Espoo (Finland) and Berlin, and (unlike for the ESDP
process) were made available on the German government website for a period of general
consultation.
The Territorial Agenda of the European Union: Towards a More Competitive and Sustainable
Europe of Diverse Regions (EU Ministers 2007a) was presented to a ministerial meeting in Leipzig
in May 2007. Its title reﬂects the important change in reasoning for spatial planning in the context
of the Lisbon Agenda, with more attention to economic competitiveness. The contents of the
Territorial Agenda are discussed more fully in Chapter 15. The ﬁnal version of the longer
statement on The Territorial State and Perspectives was made available at the same time but was

The emergence of the European spatial planning agenda

## S. 237

not formally presented to Ministers. At the same meeting, ministers endorsed the Leipzig Charter
(EU Ministers 2007b), a statement of principles to guide urban policy. In 2008 the Council of
Europe approved a new version of the European Urban Charter (Manifesto for a new urbanity),
with a strong emphasis on urban governance for sustainable development (CoE 2008).
In the second half of 2007 the Portuguese presidency hosted an informal meeting of ministers
in November 2007 in the Azores to agree the Territorial Agenda Action Plan. The Commission’s
intervention at this meeting raised the question of making the meetings of ministers relating to
territorial cohesion a part of the formal Council of Ministers – a question that had not been
discussed since the late 1980s.
Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion
After being in the doldrums for the ﬁrst part of the 2000s spatial planning was very much back
on the agenda in the second half, although mostly because of its association with the notion of
territorial cohesion. As noted above the concept was ﬁrmly embedded in European debates by
2007, but there was considerable ambiguity about its meaning and status. In anticipation of the
Lisbon Treaty, the Commission launched a consultation exercise in October 2008 through a
Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion: Turning Territorial Diversity into Strength (CEC 2008c).
This ensured that the debate on the meaning of territorial cohesion and the role of European
spatial planning would continue (see Chapter 15).
Conclusion
The 2000s began rather hesitantly for European spatial planning, with the ESDP published, no
real plan for what was to come next, and the Commission turning its attention to territorial
cohesion. The decade ends with four pillars of European spatial planning (as Waterhout 2008
describes them) in place: the ESDP, the INTERREG Programme, the ESPON programme and
the Territorial Agenda of the EU (with its accent on territorial cohesion). The ESPON and
INTERREG initiatives have drawn in a very large number of critical but sympathetic practitioners and academics. The member states have supported the notion of territorial cohesion,
though ‘nation-centred policies are still dominating policies in the interest of Europe as a whole’
(Kunzmann 2006a: 20). EU funding continues to support many transnational spatial planning
projects though their rationale is more often to do with economic growth.
The fallout of the global ﬁnancial crisis in 2008 will have profound effects. The private sector
is less likely to be able to deliver development and there will be a stronger emphasis on public
sector stimulation of economic and social cohesion through investment in infrastructure. EU
Cohesion Policy will be reviewed for the period post-2013 and, if some countries have their way,
may be substantially weakened (save for the transition countries) (see Chapter 18). This leaves
the stage open for more attention to the territorial coordination of sectoral policies and to
cooperation across borders through spatial planning.
Further reading
Andreas Faludi offers many useful sources, two of which are particularly relevant for the
discussion in this chapter. The ﬁrst is his 2002 book with Waterhout on the Making of the
European Spatial Development Perspective, and the second is the Progress in Planning paper
(2009a), which discusses the making of the Territorial Agenda. Kunzmann (2006a) offers a
critical view encompassing the new member states. A view from those most closely involved in
the process during the 2000s is Eser and Schmeitz (2008). For a ﬁne explanation of progress in
the early years see Fit and Kragt (1994).
208
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 238

The instruments of European 
spatial planning
Spatial strategies, spatial visions and the 
quest for spatial coordination
. . . to work cooperatively at the local, regional, national and continental levels, to make sure that
future spatial planning across the European landmass is compatible with Europe’s dream of
inclusivity, diversity, sustainability, quality of life, universal human rights, the rights of nature, and
peace among people.
(Rifkin 2005: 156)
Introduction
The expanding inﬂuence of the EU on spatial development has raised awareness of the need to
coordinate the spatial impacts of EU sectoral policies across levels of government and across
national borders. For spatial planners, especially those from north-west European countries, this
has meant proposing integrated spatial development frameworks for the European territory
(CEMAT 2000) and for the EU territory (CSD 1999, EU Ministers 2007a). Related instruments
of integrated spatial visions were also prepared for large transnational territories, such as those
deﬁned under the INTERREG IIC and IIIB Community Initiatives. Such spatial strategies
combine substantive policy and procedures for their application,1 placing emphasis on spatial
planning as a coordination mechanism. In this chapter, those coordination instruments directly
concerned with European spatial planning are explained and critically discussed.
With the policy shift since the 2000s towards the objectives of the Lisbon-Gothenburg
Agenda and territorial cohesion (discussed in Chapter 14), the focus of attention has largely
shifted away from providing integrated spatial development frameworks for the EU. Yet the need
for spatial coordination remains, and has prompted a debate on other, more procedural, instruments. These include the open method of coordination (OMC) and territorial impact assessment
(TIA). Such procedural instruments are discussed in the second part of this chapter.
Spatial development frameworks for the pan-European 
and the EU territory
Within Europe, the interest in developing spatial development strategies or spatial planning
frameworks increased considerably during the 1990s for all levels of scale, from the EU to the
regions. These instruments are intended to provide a shared vision and strategy for the territory
in question for a certain time horizon. However, there is no universally accepted deﬁnition 
of what a spatial development strategy is and what it contains, nor of its role in inﬂuencing 
land use patterns and governance arrangements at different levels of scale. Kunzmann (2008:
12–13) has described such spatial development frameworks as ‘well worded documents on the
aims and processes of spatial development, decorated by persuasive narratives, success stories

Chapter 15

## S. 239

and “best practice” examples, [which] are written by highly qualiﬁed experts in international
politico-administrative committees’. The need for consensus in such transnational processes is
paramount, and as a consequence, as Kunzmann (2008: 13) points out, ‘such documents try 
to be neutral, non-ideological and well balanced. That is why they rarely touch on the real
challenges and why they tend to forget to articulate or to express the contradictions of
mainstream policies and statements’.
The non-binding status of such spatial strategies raises the question of how they can achieve
their policy objectives without having regulatory or ﬁnancial instruments at their disposal. To
become effective, they have to rely on the power of consensus, and on the shared understanding
of all actors involved that the stated ‘vision’ is in everyone’s best interest. Faludi (2001a: 664)
has argued that ‘applying any strategic planning document aims at shaping the minds of actors
involved in spatial development’. Kunzmann (2008: 13) describes it as the ‘discourse power of
spatial planning’ (see Chapter 13). However, ensuring that non-binding strategies are applied is
not always easy, as the capacity of planning ‘to integrate is limited by the power of sectoral policy
communities and the weakness of the conceptions of socio-spatial relations that underpin the
thinking of the planning community’ (Healey 1999: 116). Moreover, such strategies are
primarily read by academic experts or by communities of spatial planning practice and as a
consequence ‘have little inﬂuence on the corporate worlds of ﬁnance and global corporations’
(Kunzmann 2008: 13).
The consensus-led process of preparing spatial development frameworks for the European
territory implies that guiding principles are needed that are ‘capable of papering over the cracks
between the various views prevailing’ (Faludi 2002b: 904). These ‘bridging concepts’ may be
rather vague, but they ensure that the document is acceptable for all involved. As in the case of
the ESDP, however, they may lead to a considerable debate after the strategy has been adopted
on what policy principles such as polycentricity actually mean in different territorial contexts
and how these objectives can be achieved. In this section, the content and purpose of the spatial
strategies that have been prepared for the pan-European and EU territories are presented. Their
application and potential impact on domestic planning systems, policies and practices is
discussed in Chapter 23.
The CEMAT Guiding Principles for Sustainable Spatial Development 
of the European Continent
The Council of Europe’s activities in the ﬁeld of spatial development since the late 1960s are
informed by the understanding that cooperation across national borders is a prerequisite for
peace and prosperity on the European continent. Based on the Torremolinos Charter, and
undertaken in parallel to the work on the ESDP for the EU-15 during the 1990s, the CEMAT
Guiding Principles for Sustainable Spatial Development of the European Continent were
approved by the 41 Ministers for Regional Planning of the member countries of the Council of
Europe in September 2000. Its content is described in Box 15.1. There are some obvious parallels
between the work of EU Ministers and CEMAT activities in the ﬁeld of spatial development,
which are a result of cross-fertilisation between the two European spatial planning communities.
Guiding Principles is a non-binding document that sets out a policy framework for the
activities of the Council of Europe and its members and intends to provide a coherent strategy
for an integrated and regionally balanced development of the European continent. There have
been some follow-up activities on its application. The CEMAT ‘Ljubljana Declaration’ (CEMAT
2003) and the ‘Lisbon Declaration’ (CEMAT 2006) emphasised the role of transnational 
and interregional cooperation through development projects. A review of the effectiveness 
of implementing the Guiding Principles to date, presented at the Lisbon meeting, noted that
