## S. 241

‘the spirit of the Guiding Principles had been widely taken into consideration’ in territorial
development policies at the various levels, albeit with a greater impact in the countries that
joined the Council of Europe over the past 15 years than in the ‘old’ members. In some cases,
the Guiding Principles had been included in planning legislation and in strategic planning
documents. It was noted, however, that public engagement and coordination with sectoral
departments in the planning processes required more effort.
The European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) and the 
ESDP Action Programme
The European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP) (CSD 1999) is the ﬁrst ever EU spatial
development framework, and was prepared for the territory of EU-15 (Figure 15.1). The ESDP
was prepared by the EU member states in cooperation with the European Commission over 
a period of ten years (see Chapter 14 and Faludi and Waterhout 2002). Among planners the
ESDP is widely regarded as a key milestone in the debate on a European dimension for spatial
planning.
The ESDP consists of two main parts as described in Box 15.2. Part A sets out the policy
framework of the ESDP, and Part B provides some analyses and background information. The
argument for an EU spatial development framework is derived from three main concerns:
growing regional disparities in the EU; increasing interdependencies between cities and regions;
and existing inconsistencies between Community sectoral policies. At the end of the 1990s, a
number of central and eastern European countries were preparing to join the EU. As they were
not directly involved in the work of the CSD, a speciﬁc chapter on enlargement was added to
the ESDP. It was envisaged that the ESDP would be regularly updated, with the ﬁrst revision
to take full account of the enlarged EU territory. For various reasons, these updates were not
prepared.

## S. 243

The ESDP seeks to inject a spatial dimension to EU policy, through three main integrated
policy guidelines for spatial development:
•
development of a balanced and polycentric urban system and a new urban-rural relationship;
•
securing parity of access to infrastructure and knowledge; and
•
sustainable development, prudent management and protection of nature and cultural
heritage.
These three policy guidelines are each further detailed through policy aims. A further 60 speciﬁc
policy options are attached (see Box 15.3 for an example).
The ESDP has been criticised for some inherent tensions. These are an effect of its intergovernmental process of production where the need to reach agreement was paramount.
Statements made in the ESDP are at a very general level, and the ESDP does not offer a vision
of what the EU territory should look like. Visualising spatial policy options has been a highly
controversial and difﬁcult issue in the ESDP process. In the ﬁnal ESDP, policy maps are notably
absent (Dühr 2007). After the adoption of the ESDP concepts such as ‘polycentricity’ and ‘global
214
The European spatial planning agenda
Box 15.3 Example of ESDP policy guidelines, policy aims and
policy options
Policy guideline: ‘development of a balanced and polycentric urban system and a new urban-rural
relationship’.
There are four policy aims attached to this guideline, including: ‘Polycentric and Balanced Spatial
Development in the EU’.
This policy aim is speciﬁed through the following ﬁve policy options:
1
Strengthening of several larger zones of global economic integration in the EU, equipped with
high-quality global functions and services, including the peripheral areas, through transnational
spatial development strategies.
2
Strengthening a polycentric and more balanced system of metropolitan regions, city clusters
and city networks through closer cooperation between structural policy and the policy on
the Trans-European Networks (TENs) and improvement of the links between international/
national and regional/local transport networks.
3
Promoting integrated spatial development strategies for city clusters in individual member
states, within the framework of transnational and cross-border cooperation, including corresponding rural areas and their small cities and towns.
4
Strengthening cooperation on particular topics in the ﬁeld of spatial development through
cross-border and transnational networks.
5
Promoting cooperation at regional, cross-border and transnational levels; with towns and
cities in the countries of northern, central and eastern Europe and the Mediterranean region;
strengthening north–south links in central and eastern Europe and west–east links in northern
Europe.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 244

economic integration zones’ prompted considerable debate, as their meaning was not clearly
deﬁned. For example, polycentricity can be used as an analytical concept (i.e. to help describe
and explain the distribution of settlement and its growth) or as a normative concept (i.e. a policy
objective to be pursued through government action) (Davoudi 2003). The understanding of
‘polycentricity’ varies at different spatial scales: polycentricity at the European level is understood
as the promotion of other global economic integration zones outside the European core area,
or the Pentagon (discussed in Chapter 5); at the interregional scale it implies the development
of complementary urban functions; and at the intra-regional scale (within a region) it may refer
to urban networks or city clusters. Accordingly, possible policies and relevant stakeholders also
vary, and contradictions may occur if polycentricity is pursued at different levels.
Moreover, although the rhetoric of the ESDP emphasises a harmonious and balanced spatial
development, the economic competitiveness discourse is dominant, and it has been argued that this
is at the expense of a debate focusing more on social and environmental interests (Giannakourou
1996; Richardson and Jensen 2000). As an illustration, the ESDP does not emphasise redistribution
as a mechanism to achieve balanced spatial development but rather the development of ‘dynamic
zones of economic integration’ throughout the EU territory, that is, support for those cities and
regions with potential to become major players in the global economy.
The ESDP is intended to ensure coherence and complementarity of the member states’ spatial
development strategies and to coordinate the spatial aspects of Community sectoral policies.
Given the lack of an EU competence, the emphasis is on dialogue and debate. The ESDP is an
‘informal reference framework’ that promotes integrated spatial policy at Community level, at
transnational/national level, and at regional/local level through three types of coordination:
•
horizontal coordination between sectoral policies at different levels of scale;
•
geographical integration and coordination at the transnational level, i.e. for the whole of
the European territory and/or for large transnational areas that transcend national borders,
such as river basins, mountain ranges and maritime basins;
•
vertical coordination between administrative levels to ensure complementarity between
different combinations of policy options applicable at different administrative levels but
addressing the same geographical area.
The envisaged horizontal coordination of the spatial impacts of EU sector policies remains a
considerable challenge. The ESDP’s call for more policy coherence has been reinforced by other
Commission documents, such as the White Paper on European Governance (CEC 2001e), and
prompted the discussion on other coordination instruments (discussed in the last section of this
chapter).
At a meeting of the EU Ministers for spatial planning under the Finnish presidency in 1999,
shortly after the adoption of the ESDP, a12-point Action Programme for the ESDP was agreed.
This had three central aims:
1
to promote the spatial dimension in Community and national policies;
2
to improve the level of knowledge, research and information on territorial development
issues; and
3
to prepare for EU enlargement.
It was envisaged that the actions would be implemented in a voluntary manner between 1999
and 2003. Member states were assigned as lead partners to each action to coordinate the work
(Table 15.1).
The results and impacts of these actions have been uneven, although some of the activities
(such as the INTERREG and ESPON programmes, both discussed in Chapter 16) have

## S. 245

signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced the debate on European spatial planning. The lack of available knowledge and spatial information on large-scale spatial development trends in Europe and the spatial
impacts of EU sector policies came to the fore acutely in the ESDP process. This prompted 
the setting up of the European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON), intended to 
provide the relevant technical support on European spatial policy-making and give input into
the envisaged periodic updates of the ESDP. The Community Initiative INTERREG IIC was 
set up in 1997 to facilitate transnational cooperation on spatial planning in large mega-regions.
According to Kunzmann (1998b: 54), linking Community funding to the ESDP policy options
may have saved it from becoming a ‘paper tiger’. In terms of the application of the ESDP to
national and regional planning policy, the impact has overall been variable (ESPON 2007b;
Faludi 2001a, 2003; 2004b). In some countries, such as the Netherlands and Germany, which
have played important roles in the ESDP process and succeeded in ‘uploading’ domestic policy
ideas, the ESDP has, arguably, not had far-reaching effects because adaptation was required. In
other countries, the EU debates on spatial planning and the work on the ESDP have prompted
changes to planning policies and practices, as in Italy, or even informed the reform of the
planning system, as in the UK (see Chapter 23).
The Territorial Agenda of the European Union (TAEU) and the
First Action Programme

## S. 246

of the century, the motivation for developing an ‘ESDP+’ to pursue the spatial integrated
approach was lost. Yet there were some member states, especially from north-west Europe, that
were concerned about the ‘non-spatial turn’ in EU policy, and in 2004 they initiated a process
of preparing a Territorial Agenda of the European Union (TAEU). This led to the adoption of
the document at a meeting of Ministers in Leipzig in 2007 (EU Ministers 2007a; see Figure
15.1b). The TAEU is accompanied by a more voluminous background report on the Territorial
State and Perspectives of the EU (BMVBS 2007a). The TAEU contains references to the ESDP
policy principles, such as polycentric territorial development, but combines these with the
objectives of the Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda to promote economic growth and jobs in the EU
(see Box 15.4). Yet, rather than an update, the TAEU is seen as the operationalisation of the
ESDP. The shift to the overt pursuit of economic competitiveness is evident. This corresponds
to the paradigm shift in European regional policy from the traditional focus on structurally weak
and disadvantaged regions to strategies aimed at making better use of the development of
potential and opportunities in all regions (see Chapter 18).
The TAEU promotes the objective of territorial cohesion, and talks of ‘territorial solidarity’,
deﬁned as the need to secure better living conditions and quality of life with equal opportunities,
oriented towards regional and local potentials, irrespective of where people live. European
cohesion and sectoral policies should take more account of the particular geographic characteristics, challenges and potentials of regions (the ‘territorial capital’). The need to address 
new challenges, strengthen regional identities and make better use of territorial diversity is
emphasised. These new challenges include climate change, energy, the integration of regions and
enlargement, over-exploitation of environmental resources, and demographic change. To address
these challenges, ‘development strategies should explicitly take more account of their national
and regional contexts’ and ‘intertwine’ with EU policies, especially rural development, environment, transport and cohesion. In this, the TAEU is more explicit than the ESDP was in
acknowledging the importance of coordinating the sector policies by engaging with the debates
on the budget reviews. The TAEU sets out the following policy priorities: to strengthen polycentric development and innovation through networking of city regions and cities; to support
the development of new forms of partnership and territorial governance between rural and urban
areas; to promote regional clusters of competition and innovation; to strengthen and extend 
the trans-European networks; to promote trans-European risk management (including in
relation to the impacts of climate change); and to strengthen ecological structures and cultural
resources with a view to providing an economic added value (Box 15.4). The importance of
‘territorial governance’ as an intensive and continuous dialogue between all stakeholders of
territorial development is acknowledged.
A ‘First Action Programme’ for the implementation of the Territorial Agenda between 2007
and 2011 was agreed later in 2007 under the Portuguese EU presidency, with lead partner
countries being allocated to individual actions under the Slovenian presidency in 2008 (see Box
15.5). Future presidencies are given explicit tasks to monitor and review implementation. The
actions are expected to contribute to:
•
better coordination between the spatial policies of the member states by introducing a
European dimension and deﬁning and implementing common priorities;
•
improved coherence between European Union policies and the spatial development policies
within the member states by inﬂuencing European Union policies from a territorial cohesion
point of view;
•
better understanding of the spatial system and territorial trends at European Union level;
•
improved vertical and horizontal coordination and participation of private and public
sectors towards a more responsive territorial governance.

## S. 247

The actions of the Territorial Agenda are more clearly speciﬁed than those for the ESDP (Faludi
2009b), supported at the time by the expected ratiﬁcation of the Lisbon Treaty in 2009 (which
includes the objective of territorial cohesion) and the Commission’s activities in preparing the
Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2008c). Indeed, the main value of the TAEU lies
arguably not in its substantive content (which is a combination of ESDP policy and the Lisbon-
Gothenburg Strategy) but rather in its procedural provisions and the political support it has
received. National territorial cohesion-related contact points (NTCCPs) were nominated in all
EU member states, which give more stability to the process than the usual approach of semipermanent working groups of an informal nature offers. Faludi (2009b: 27) has interpreted the
TAEU process even as prompting ‘a sea change in the institutionalisation – not in a formal 
but rather in an informal sense – of the territorial cohesion discourse’. Nevertheless, ‘the future
is uncertain’ (Faludi 2009b: 28), and besides the requirement of ongoing support from the
member states, much will depend on how the objective of territorial cohesion will be deﬁned
and applied to EU policy.

## S. 248

The Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion
The Commission’s Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion: Turning Territorial Diversity into
Strength is a consultation document that was published in October 2008 (CEC 2008c). It was
drafted in response to the invitation of the member states ‘to prepare a report on territorial
cohesion by 2008’ (European Council 2007) and is aimed at initiating a debate on the objective
of territorial cohesion and the territorial implications of EU policy (Box 15.6). The Green Paper
is notable in the context of a debate on European spatial development for two main reasons:
ﬁrst, because after a phase of predominantly intergovernmental activity, and supported by
objective of territorial cohesion in the Lisbon Treaty, the European Commission is now

## S. 249

taking a more active role in the debate on EU territorial matters; second, and largely dodging
the competence issue, the TAEU and the Green Paper mark the ﬁrm shift towards the debate
on territorial (rather than spatial) development. This also opens the door to instruments 
other than spatial planning to help achieve the objective of territorial cohesion and policy
coordination.2
The Green Paper is accompanied by a Commission Staff working document (CEC 2008h),
which includes responses of the member states to a Commission survey on how the concept of
territorial cohesion is understood and implemented across the EU. Although member states 
do not usually have a policy that is labelled ‘territorial cohesion’, most countries have policies,
or elements of policies, that they consider relevant in this context. Spatial planning is regarded
by many member states to be the strongest mechanism at national level for the coordination
between actors in different sectors and across different administrative levels.

review in 2011

## S. 250

The Green Paper’s main message is that ‘place and geography matter’. Several elements of
territorial cohesion are deﬁned: equity concerns (i.e. where people live should not fundamentally
determine their opportunities nor their quality of life); competitiveness (regions and localities,
each in their own way, play a crucial role in growth and job creation); sustainability; and good
governance. These elements are potentially contradictory, which is why the emphasis is on
ﬁnding ﬂexible and ‘place-based’ responses. In spatial (territorial) terms, the concept of territorial
cohesion translates into the need to reduce regional disparities, ensuring the provision of goods
and services across the territory in small towns and cities to prevent rural depopulation and
suitable transport and ICT services for all parts of the territory and society. Cooperation between
regions is seen as an important aspect of achieving these goals.
Numerous questions for consultation were set out by the Green Paper (see Box 15.7). By the
end of the consultation period in February 2009, 388 responses had been received altogether,
and these were made available on the Commission’s website. At the time of writing, DG REGIO
was preparing a synthesis report on the basis of the responses, to be made publicly available
later in 2009. The results will feed into a White Paper on Territorial Cohesion. It is expected
that the concept of territorial cohesion will be clearly deﬁned in this policy document. The debate
on territorial cohesion, launched by the TAEU and the Green Paper, is expected to inform the
reform of EU Cohesion Policy post-2013; and perhaps even stimulate the setting up of a
‘territorial cohesion policy’. This would imply that the territorial impacts of EU sector policies
are considered comprehensively, and that their implementation may require local adaptation for
them to be most effective, as different territories may require different approaches. The ﬂexibility
in implementation that is inherent in this has clear governance implications and, alongside the
emphasis on regional networks and territorial cooperation, promises a different approach to EU
policy-making and implementation in future. This may also prompt a different approach to 
the allocation of Cohesion Policy funding. Several ideas have been put forward in the debate,
whereby the shortcomings of deciding eligible areas on the basis of NUTS 2 administrative
regions are widely acknowledged. An alternative could be a more ﬂexible approach to geography
and the allocation of Cohesion Policy funding. This would require considering smaller regions
of geographical coherence (e.g. cities or islands), but also larger regions, such as the Danube

## S. 251

Basin and the Black Sea region. In this context, spatial or territorial visions may gain in importance again as an instrument to coordinate policies at all levels and across all sectors.
The Green Paper, although by many regarded as an important step towards an EU 
territorial cohesion policy, has also met with criticism. The European Parliament, since the 1980s
a ﬁrm supporter of a stronger EU involvement in the coordination of spatial development,
commented on the ‘overly cautious’ message of the Green Paper and has argued that it lacks 
ambition:
to the extent that it fails to propose either a clear deﬁnition of or an objective for territorial
cohesion and does not advance signiﬁcantly the understanding of that new concept, so that
it can effectively contribute to the alleviation of disparities between regions.
(EP 2009)
However, an alternative interpretation of the Commission’s modest approach could be that it
wants to ensure that EU policy proposals are backed by a wide range of stakeholders by inviting
a broad debate.

## S. 252

Spatial visions and spatial strategies for transnational territories
In the 1990s new instruments, such as spatial visions or scenarios, were increasingly introduced
in national and transnational strategic spatial planning processes. They are instruments to coordinate many (and often divergent) interests and to reach agreement where many uncertainties
about complex spatial processes and future developments exist. Preparing a spatial vision has
become popular also in the context of transnational cooperation on spatial development,
funded by the Community Initiatives INTERREG IIC and IIIB (see Chapter 16).
Besides such bottom-up exercises, the EU institutions have, over recent years, become
involved in the preparation of strategies for parts of the EU territory. Examples include the 
EU Baltic Sea Region Strategy and the Integrated Maritime Policy for the European Union, which
are currently being tested as instruments to achieve policy coherence in the multi-level governance system of the Union. In comparison to the intergovernmental activities, the emphasis is
more explicitly on the coordination of sector policies and the integration of policy across the
different levels of governance, with spatial development playing a role in this (but not being at
the core). Such initiatives are predominantly governance instruments, aimed at involving the
various participating parties as much as possible in the decision-making process, in order to gain
their consent before introducing speciﬁc measures. 
‘Bottom-up’ transnational spatial visions
Spatial planning strategies can take various forms, and have been described as ‘spatial vision’,
‘structural outline’, ‘long-term spatial development perspective’ or Leitbild, depending on the
context and the planning cultures involved in their preparation. Since the mid-1980s, spatial
visioning has become increasingly popular in planning practice. The word ‘vision’ generally
refers to a visualisation of an ideal long-term future state of affairs. The idea of visioning has
become closely linked to public participation in strategic spatial planning exercises at different
levels of scale, with attractive images playing an important role in getting support for the proposals. Based on a review of visioning exercises in planning, Shipley (2000) has argued that
visioning usually has only a weak theoretical basis, with planners working largely from a set of
tacit assumptions about the usefulness of preparing spatial visions.
Such ‘learning-by-doing’ has resulted in a wide variety of different ‘models’ of spatial visions
(Shipley and Newkirk 1999):
•
The vision as master plan provides the collective view of a wide range of interests of what
the region should be like in x years’ time. This type of spatial vision tends to concentrate
on physical development with maps setting out the future spatial distribution of different
land use functions and connections between them.
•
The vision as the truth is an authoritative forecast of what the region will be like in x years’
time. They are often presented as ‘worst case scenarios’ (for example, showing the negative
effects of increasing polarisation of development). On this basis, actions are agreed to
counter negative trends.
•
The vision as utopia or dystopia intends to stimulate debate and prompt action by showing
extreme versions of spatial development, either from a positive (showing the perfect world)
or a negative perspective (showing the worst horrors the future could hold).
•
The vision as mission statement, rather than presenting a picture of the future, sets down
fundamental principles that should govern future actions. These may cover substantive
principles (for example, sustainability), or governance principles (for example, transparent
decision-making processes) (Nadin 2002).

## S. 253

The ‘model’ for transnational spatial vision exercises in Europe under INTERREG IIC and 
IIIB is commonly acknowledged to be the Vision and Strategies around the Baltic Sea 2010
(VASAB 2010 1994) document. Cooperation on spatial development in the BSR (Baltic Sea
Region) through VASAB 2010 began before any EU funding for territorial cooperation became
available, and was only later backed by EU programmes. Indeed, VASAB provided a model for
the INTERREG IIC Community Initiative. The achievements of VASAB in the ﬁeld of joint
spatial planning, especially considering the political, administrative and cultural differences in
the region, warrant particular attention. In the early 1990s concerns over environmental
pollution of the shallow sea and an interest in ﬁnding policy responses to the shared issues of
a largely peripheral region of Europe after the fall of the Iron Curtain led to the setting up 
of the Committee on Spatial Development in the BSR (CSD-BSR) of the 11 countries around
the Baltic Sea. An action programme, entitled ‘From Vision to Action’ (VASAB 2010 1996)
proposed measures for the application of the spatial vision. In 1997, EU funding was made
available for transnational cooperation and to support the process of updating of the VASAB
2010 strategy (VASAB 2010 2001). The CSD-BSR, which is supported by a VASAB Secretariat,
to this day facilitates the coordination of joint actions and prepares the Ministerial Conferences that take place every four to ﬁve years in the Baltic Sea Region. Cooperation on spatial
development in the Baltic Sea Region has since become further institutionalised, and work is
under way to develop a new long-term spatial development perspective for the BSR (expected
time horizon 15 to 20 years). In comparison to earlier VASAB initiatives, the new visioning
processes place greater emphasis on cooperation between the CSD-BSR, regional authorities and
pan-Baltic organisations, and wide public consultation is planned.
Elsewhere in Europe, preparing a strategic framework for transnational territories has been
applied more widely only in the context of the Community Initiatives INTERREG IIC (1997–99)
and INTERREG IIIB (2000–06) (see Chapter 16). Transnational INTERREG cooperation areas
were expected to elaborate operational spatial development strategies on a transnational scale.
Besides the Baltic Sea Region, there are only four INTERREG programmes that prepared such
a spatial vision. These are CADSES (BBR 2000),3 North-west Europe (NWMA Spatial Vision
Group 2000), North Sea Region (Vision Working Group 2000) and the Atlantic Area (CPMR
2005). Updates have been undertaken on some of these spatial visions since, partly aimed at
arriving at an agreed action programme.
The cooperation areas have very different characteristics, as do the resulting spatial visions.
In different ways, they each sought to draw together the broad principles of the ESDP with the
disparate planning activity of national and regional governments and many hundreds of
cooperation projects funded by the INTERREG programme (Nadin 2002; Zonneveld 2005b).
The results were not considered to be particularly ‘visionary’; rather, they displayed the existing
state of spatial development in the transnational cooperation area (that is, they represented the
type of ‘mission statement’). The INTERREG transnational spatial visions have been criticised
for a lack of transparency on how the spatial visions were prepared, and their often limited
consultation processes (ESPON and INTERACT 2006). Arguably, their role in inﬂuencing
decisions on INTERREG project selection was limited (Nadin 2002).
Few of the INTERREG examples attempt to present the ‘vision’ in cartographic terms,
although they all contain a series of analytical maps. Indeed, as for the ESDP process, mapping
of normative policy has been a controversial matter, reﬂecting uncertainty about the status of
the vision document, and about the competency for spatial planning at transnational level and
appropriate mechanisms for implementation. Three spatial visions attempted to visualise the
spatial policy options for the transnational territory. The VASAB 2010 document (VASAB 2010
1994) presented the now famous ‘pearls’ (the system of cities and urban settlements), ‘strings’
(the interlinking infrastructure networks) and ‘patches’ (selected types of land uses in non-urban
224
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

areas). Spatial policy has also been visualised for the Spatial Vision for North-west Europe (see
Figure 15.2) and the Atlantic Spatial Development Perspective.
Preparing the transnational spatial visions required intensive cooperation between the
national and regional actors involved in the transnational INTERREG programmes in order to
reach agreement on the objectives and directions for spatial development in these mega-regions
(see Doucet 2002; Dühr et al. 2005; ESPON and INTERACT 2006). In retrospect, this may 
have been their main value as they have arguably played a very important role in starting a

## S. 255

process of ‘policy-oriented learning’ about the transnational space in which the actors are
involved (Farthing and Carrière 2007). This is particularly so because the effect of most of the
transnational spatial visions on planning practice appears to be limited. They raised interest for
a short time but were not carried forward into the 2000–06 round of territorial cooperation in
any substantial way, although they are frequently referred to in national documents. In the
current transnational territorial cooperation programmes (2007–13) there is little interest in
visioning instruments as a possible coordination mechanism. However, transnational spatial
visions may receive a new lease of life in EU Cohesion Policy post-2013, where macro-regions
and integrated strategies are being debated as a suitable approach to achieve the objective of
territorial cohesion. The experiences with the EU Baltic Sea Region Strategy could provide the
example for how this might be organised.
The EU Baltic Sea Region Strategy
The Baltic Sea Region (BSR), a pioneer in the ﬁeld of transnational spatial planning, is currently
subject to an innovative approach initiated by the European Commission. The BSR territory
includes EU member states (Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Germany, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania,
Poland – although the eastern members only joined the EU in 2004) and the non-member states
Norway, Russia and Belarus. Since the 1990s, transnational cooperation in the BSR has become
increasingly institutionalised, and this is particularly remarkable because the region was divided
for 40 years during the Cold War, which resulted in considerable differences in political and
economic systems. Besides cooperation on spatial planning through VASAB, there are numerous
other cross-border and transnational initiatives in the BSR. They have considerable inﬂuence on
policy- and decision-making in the BSR states and at transnational level, for example HELCOM
in the ﬁeld of environmental policy.
In December 2007, the European Council launched an initiative to develop an ‘EU Strategy
for the Baltic Sea Region’, as a ‘testing ground for the rethinking of the nature of territorial
cooperation which emphasises more coherent strategies, shared community objectives and robust
implementation tools’ (CEC 2008r: 5). The Strategy is an innovative approach whereby a 
comprehensive strategy is prepared for a ‘macro-region’ covering several Community policies
and aimed at coordinating action by member states, regions, the EU, pan-Baltic organisations,
ﬁnancing institutions and non-governmental bodies.
A draft strategy for the Baltic Sea Region was published in 2008, which was followed by a
period of public consultation. The four cornerstones of the Strategy are to make the BSR:
•
more environmentally sustainable (e.g. reducing pollution in the sea);
•
more prosperous (e.g. promoting innovation in small and medium-sized enterprises);
•
more accessible and attractive (e.g. providing better transport links);
•
more safe and secure (e.g. improving accident response).
In June 2009, the European Commission presented the ﬁnal strategy as a Communication to
the Council (CEC 2009c). It is accompanied by an Action Plan that lists 80 ﬂagship projects.
The EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region was adopted by the European Council in October
2009.
The EU Baltic Sea Region Strategy is not explicitly a spatial planning document (although 
it has clear spatial development implications), and it does not seek to replace existing arrangements in the ﬁeld of transnational spatial planning through VASAB and the CSD-BSR. Rather,
the argument for EU involvement derives from the recognition that appropriate ways have to
be found to coordinate sector policies across different levels of government (and among the
226
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 256

relevant Directorates-General of the Commission) and through joint action across national
borders. These are ideas that had already been put forward by the ESDP, so the fact that a wellestablished transnational cooperation area is used as a ‘pilot’ for the preparation of a comprehensive integrated strategy may not be surprising. There have been suggestions to formulate
similar strategies for the Danube River Basin and possibly for the Mediterranean Union. If it
proves successful, the EU Baltic Sea Region Strategy may also provide inspiration for the
territorial cooperation objective of the EU Cohesion Policy post-2013.
‘An Integrated Maritime Policy for the European Union’ and maritime 
spatial planning
Europe has a 70,000 km coastline along two oceans and four seas: the Atlantic and Arctic
Oceans, the Baltic, the North Sea, the Mediterranean and the Black Sea. The EU’s maritime
regions account for some 40 per cent of its GDP and population, and are subject to a multitude
of activities and intensive use, such as shipbuilding and shipping, ports and ﬁsheries, offshore
energy (including oil, gas and renewables), coastal and maritime tourism, and exploitation of
mineral resources. Maritime regions are vitally important for the EU, but activities and policies
related to the seas and the oceans have to date been managed largely on sectoral lines. The 
EU’s ‘Integrated Maritime Policy for the European Union’ seeks to change this and promotes
coordination to avoid further deterioration of the marine environment, especially in the face of
climate change effects. A ‘Blue Book’, consisting of two Commission Communications on policy
and two working documents (an action plan and an impact study), was published after a period
of consultation in October 2007 (CEC 2007f). The Blue Book sets out a ‘governance framework’
and proposes cross-sectoral tools necessary for the EU Integrated Maritime Policy.
The Communication and accompanying action plan list a range of concrete actions to be
launched by 2009. Five main objectives are set out, to which around 30 measures are attached:
1
To encourage sustainable exploitation of the seas and oceans.
2
To create a basis of knowledge and innovation for maritime policy.
3
To provide better quality of life in the coastal regions.
4
To promote the leading position of the Union in international maritime affairs.
5
To increase the visibility of maritime Europe.
While some of the measures are ‘structural’ in nature and covered by existing EU policies such
as transport and ﬁsheries policy, others are new and deliberately cross-cutting. Of particular
interest for spatial planners are actions in the ﬁeld of maritime spatial planning, the integrated
management of coastal areas, maritime energy policy and the establishment of a European
maritime observation and data network. Some legislative action is being discussed, including a
‘Marine Strategy Directive’. A number of additional Commission communications have meanwhile been published on the proposed activities, including a Communication on the ‘Energy
Policy of the Union’ (CEC 2007g) or the Communication on a ‘Roadmap for Maritime Spatial
Planning: Achieving common principles in the EU’ in November 2008 (CEC 2008i).
Maritime spatial planning is expected to bring economic beneﬁts (by generating ‘a favourable
climate for investment in maritime activities’ through a strategic approach and planning
security). Several member states (e.g. Poland and Germany) have already launched maritime
spatial planning processes. The need is evident for cooperation among countries to achieve
effective coordination of all sea-related policies at the different decision-making levels when
preparing comprehensive and integrated plans. The Communication on Maritime Spatial Planning (CEC 2008i) has launched a debate on how a common approach to maritime spatial

## S. 257

planning can be achieved in the EU. A system for exchange of best practice among authorities
engaged in maritime spatial planning and Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM) will
be set up (for more on ICZM see Chapter 16). Pilot actions to test adaptation strategies to
climate change in coastal zones will be supported. The European Commission explicitly
promotes the use of the European territorial cooperation programmes (see Chapter 16) to
support interregional maritime development and cooperation between EU regions. The results
of national activities and of international cooperation are expected to demonstrate that there
would be added value in pursuing maritime spatial planning at EU level.
Spatial planning under complex conditions: other policy 
coordination instruments
An instrument that is closely connected to the preparation of spatial development strategies and
visions is that of spatial scenarios. Scenarios have a strong communicative function, and seek
to stimulate debate among a wide range of interests about the future spatial structure of a
territory. Attention has also been given to governance-oriented and technical instruments as
coordination mechanisms. Among those ‘new modes of governance’ (CEC 2001e) is the ‘Open
Method of Coordination’, while territorial impact assessment (TIA) is an example of a more
technical mechanism to integrate the spatial impacts of sector policies.
Spatial scenarios in European spatial planning
Spatial scenarios are instruments for the exploration of future spatial development trends, and
have been described as a particularly suitable way to visualise and communicate complex and
slow processes such as those of territorial development (BBR 2003). The preparation of a series
of alternative spatial development scenarios can facilitate the discussion of a ‘desirable future’
and help to reach consensus on a spatial vision or spatial strategy. Stiens (2004) has
differentiated three main categories of scenarios:
•
descriptive scenarios or trendscenarios, which show the continuation of existing development trends, and the impact on the territory if no policy intervention occurs;
•
Leitbild or strategy scenarios, which are normative scenarios to illustrate the desired spatial
future; and
•
alternative scenarios, which show favourable spatial structures in comparison to the present
situation, and are often radical and utopian in character.
For spatial planning purposes, scenarios are usually presented in cartographic form as this
facilitates the discussion of their main content with a wider group of actors. Although scenarios
are based on quantitative data and prognoses, they are usually complemented by qualitative
information, such as expert knowledge. The rising popularity of spatial scenarios since the
1990s, especially at transnational levels, can be ascribed to the scenarios published by the
European Commission in the Europe 2000 (CEC 1991) and Europe 2000+ (CEC 1994a)
reports. In 1995, the French presidency of the EU initiated the preparation of spatial scenarios
as an input into the ESDP process to stimulate the thinking about policy options for the EU-
15 territory. Although the ESDP ‘trendscenarios’, which sought to show the future spatial
structure of Europe without policy intervention (by extrapolating current spatial development
trends up to the year 2015), did not lead to policy maps in the ESDP, they have reportedly
helped in the discussions across language and cultural boundaries on spatial development
options (Sinz 1997).
228
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 258

Other scenario exercises for transnational and national territories followed these pioneers
(Dühr 2007). The European experiences have prompted a number of national-level scenario
exercises that provided the basis for the preparation of informal planning strategies. Although
prepared by different actor groups and for different territories, what many of the following
spatial scenarios have highlighted is the need for policy intervention to avoid undesired
polarisation tendencies. The French national planning agency, having initiated the work on the
ESDP trendscenarios, subsequently published Aménager la France de 2020 (DATAR 2002),
which included four scenarios for France in Europe in 2020. The scenarios range from
unhindered continuation of current trends (described as the ‘The Shattered Archipelago – a
neoliberal scenario’) to a policy scenario called ‘The Networked Polycentrality’, which has
subsequently been operationalised as a model for the future spatial development of France
(Guigou and Peyrony 2004). For the German territory, four long-term spatial scenarios up to
2040 were prepared in 2003 (BBR 2003), which informed the preparation of the new spatial
Leitbilder (guiding principles) for the federal territory (BMVBS 2006).
A notable example of an attempt to translate the ESDP concept of polycentricity into a
cartographic representation for a transnational territory is the study undertaken by the
‘Conference of Peripheral and Maritime Regions’ (CPMR 2003; see Figure 5.3). At the European
level, a project funded by the ESPON Programme has developed ‘Spatial Scenarios and
Orientations in relation to the ESDP and Cohesion Policy’ (ESPON 2006e) with a time horizon
of 2030. The ESPON scenarios, while not used systematically in communicative planning processes, are interesting because of their comprehensive consideration of the challenges affecting
Europe’s spatial structure, and for highlighting the need for policy adaptation to deal with 
new challenges. An integrated ‘baseline’ scenario shows the probable evolution of the European territory in a situation of no major changes or policy intervention. In this, the Pentagon
is expected to expand, along the main transport corridors, in the direction of other capital
regions such as Barcelona and Madrid, Rome, Glasgow, Copenhagen, Stockholm and Oslo,
Berlin and Warsaw, Prague, Vienna and Budapest. The trend towards marginalisation 
and depopulation of rural areas is expected to continue, as is economic decline, although with
considerable regional variation. Starting from the baseline scenario, two prospective policy
scenarios explore the effects of EU policy. In one scenario, cohesion objectives are given top
priority, which is expected to result in a reduction of regional disparities, albeit at the cost 
of considerable public intervention. In contrast to this ‘cohesion-oriented scenario’, the
‘competitiveness-oriented scenario’ places the global competitiveness of the European economy
at the core. This is expected to result in a further concentration of economic activity and population in the core region of the EU. 
The Open Method of Coordination (OMC)
The Open Method of Coordination (OMC) (see also Chapter 11), has been promoted in policy
areas that fall within the competence of the member states and where the Community method
does not apply. It was ﬁrst introduced for the ﬁelds of social and employment policy, but has,
through the Commission’s White Paper on European Governance (CEC 2001e), also been
promoted for the ﬁeld of spatial development. Although preceding the EU White Paper on
Governance, Faludi (2007c: 11) has argued that the ESDP showed features of OMC. He
describes the process as ‘an approach encouraging adaptive, open-ended horizontal and vertical
mutual learning’. The OMC relies on ‘soft law’ mechanisms of ‘frameworks and guidelines for
member states to follow’, and benchmarks to measure progress using ‘agreed EU-wide
indicators’ (Gore 2004: 125). It places emphasis on policy learning through target setting and
sharing good practice, while leaving room for member states to develop their own policy

The instruments: spatial strategies and visions

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 259

responses within the agreed framework. ‘Naming and shaming’ of low-performing member
states in league tables is expected to pressure them to better their achievements. Member states
are thus evaluated by one another, while the Commission’s role is limited to surveillance and
other EU institutions play virtually no part in the process. According to De la Porte and Pochet
(2002) OMC is an expression of a subtle and complex balance of power between the
Commission and the member states. OMC is expected to lead to indirect, incremental
Europeanisation, because ‘member states would be invited to look at issues through European
lenses, slowly integrating these lenses into national policy’ (Faludi 2007c: 14). OMC is, in that
sense, part of a wider set of instruments of ‘soft’ or ‘cognitive’ Europeanisation (Bruno et al.
2006), discussed in further detail in Chapter 8.
Following the ratiﬁcation of the Lisbon Treaty, which makes territorial cohesion a shared
competence of the EU and the member states (meaning the Community method will apply), the
OMC is still expected to play a role in setting up an EU territorial cohesion policy. Faludi
(2007c: 22) for instance has argued that should there be a decision to prepare an EU Territorial
Cohesion Strategy as a framework for Cohesion Policy spending and to coordinate the spatial
impacts of sector policies once the Lisbon Treaty is ratiﬁed, then this ‘would necessarily imply
some form of vision of the territory of the EU and its future development as a framework for
the relevant policies of member states and the EU alike’. As the Commission lacks capacity to
produce such a strategy, and the support from the member states would be crucial to ensure its
implementation, the process would have to be organised with input from the member states 
and other stakeholders through a process such as OMC. However, experiences from other policy
ﬁelds have shown that so far the implementation effectiveness of OMC has been highly variable
across the member states (Kroger 2009; Radaelli 2003). Thus, while much hope is put on 
OMC as a coordination mechanism for the ﬁeld of spatial development and territorial cohesion,
there is at present little evidence as to its suitability for this policy area.
Territorial impact assessment (TIA)
Impact assessments are meant to support political decision making by gathering and presenting
relevant information. There are several impact assessment tools in use in EU governance,
including the environmental impact assessment (EIA) and the strategic environmental assessment
(SEA), which are both regulated by EU Directives and of great relevance for territorial policies
(see Chapter 21). Since 2005, a formal impact assessment is also required for all EU regulatory
proposals, White Papers and expenditure programmes.
The ESDP has prompted a debate on territorial impact assessment (TIA) as an instrument
to coordinate the spatial impacts of sector policies (horizontally) as well as across different levels
of governance in the EU (vertically). TIA is, in some member states, already established as a
planning or policy-making instrument, as in Germany with a legal procedure called Raumordnungsverfahren (spatial planning procedure).
TIA can be best described as a tool for assessing the impact of major development projects
and of spatial plans against the spatial policy objectives for this territory (including cross-border
contexts). While the ESDP recommendations primarily referred to applications of TIA at project
level, since then consideration has also been given to the potential value of TIA for assessing
the impact of plans and programmes (Böhme and Eser 2008). Some ESPON projects have tried
to develop methodologies for the territorial impact analysis of policies, too.
TIA has been given considerable attention again in the Territorial Agenda of the European
Union (EU Ministers 2007a) and the Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2008c). Yet
at present there are no agreed deﬁnitions and methodologies that could be applied across Europe,
and given that a number of impact assessment instruments already exist at EU level within the
230
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 260

member states, proposals have more recently focused on connecting TIA to existing tools and
procedures. For example, there have been suggestions to consider extending the existing EIA
and SEA procedures to cover social and economic impacts in order to arrive at a sustainability
assessment process that would cover all impacts (environmental, social and economic) of
programmes and projects and the effects of the interactions between activities and space over
speciﬁed time periods.
Conclusion
The complex situation of coordinating policies for European spatial planning has been characterised by Faludi (2009b) as a ‘two-level game’, whereby for decisions relating to spatial
development, at the EU level agreements have to be reached among Commissioners and
Directorates-General responsible for different sector policies, and at the national level agreements
must be reached among sector ministries. In both cases, coordinating institutions such as spatial
planning are weak. Moreover, there also needs to be some agreement between the levels. Thus,
while the disadvantages of the sectoral policy implementation logic are widely recognised, the
obvious complexity of EU spatial policy-making rather undermines any simple ideas about
integrated policy.
From the early 2000s, marked by the ‘paradigm shift’ of the Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda,
there appeared to be less faith in the coordinating role of spatial strategies or spatial visions,
but there are numerous initiatives for spatial coordination under way, framed in the context of
territorial cohesion and territorial cooperation. Some have the European Commission in a
leading role. Territorial cohesion strategies could play a part in the future, but visualisation has
proved notoriously difﬁcult in policy processes to date. The differences in understanding about
strategy-making and visualisation will continue, as will concerns that such strategies may be
connected to the allocation of EU funding (Dühr 2007). The objective of territorial cohesion is
expected to boost the quest for spatial coordination further, especially if it is applied through
an EU territorial cohesion policy post-2013. Experiences that have been gathered with instruments such as spatial visions and spatial scenarios may then receive more attention again as 
they are considered to be ‘particularly relevant to setting a strategic framework for the
implementation of the concept of territorial cohesion’ (Davoudi 2005b: 688).
Further reading
On the making of the ESDP, see the detailed account by Faludi and Waterhout (2002). A discussion of the concept of territorial cohesion and the EU territorial cohesion agenda is provided
in Faludi 2007d and Faludi 2009a. On the Open Method of Coordination, see Faludi 2007c;
on transnational spatial visions see Nadin 2002 and Zonneveld 2005b.

## S. 261

The instruments of European 
spatial planning
Transboundary territorial cooperation
programmes and projects
. . . it makes no sense for planning to stop artiﬁcially at national borders.
(CEC 1991: 3)
European integration notwithstanding, borders are still important for the way people perceive 
social-political questions.
(De Vries 2008: 50)
Introduction
In this chapter, the main Community Initiatives and programmes of relevance to the ﬁeld of
European spatial planning are discussed. The application of the ESDP through the cooperation
projects funded under the INTERREG IIC and IIIB programmes has led to a wide dissemination
of the ESDP’s spatial concepts and ideas among planning practitioners across Europe. The
evolution of the INTERREG initiative involves a change in thinking about territorial cooperation, and provides a basis for discussing the ‘rescaling’ processes that may be at work in the EU’s
system of multi-level governance (see Brenner 2004; Gualini 2004b).
The ESPON programme, funded under the INTERREG initiatives, is also a direct brainchild
of the ESDP process. ESPON was set up to provide comprehensive and comparable information
on the spatial trends affecting the European territory and the spatial impacts of EU policy, and
supports the cooperation of researchers in the ﬁeld of spatial planning. Results from ESPON
projects were, for example, used for the preparation of the Territorial Agenda of the European
Union (EU Ministers 2007a). In this context, the notion of evidence-based planning received
much attention, and this is discussed at the end of the chapter.
EU support for transboundary cooperation: INTERREG 
programmes and projects
While the regions and nation-states were the ﬁrst to promote cooperation on spatial development
with the objective of tackling obvious and urgent shared problems through informal intergovernmental committees (CoR 2002; Dühr et al. 2007), the support of the European institutions through formal funding and institutional mechanisms has been crucial in widening
involvement in transboundary cooperation. In particular, since the 1990s the funding provided
by the Community Initiative INTERREG has led to increasing attention being given to the
beneﬁts and challenges of cooperation across national borders. As a consequence, the number
of cross-border and transnational cooperation initiatives across Europe has increased manifold.
The scope and objectives for cooperation have expanded alongside, although spatial development continues to be at the core of many cooperation initiatives.
Chapter 16
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 262

INTERREG is ﬁnanced through the EU’s ‘European Regional Development Fund’ (ERDF) (see
Chapter 18). It was launched as a ‘Community Initiative’ in 1990, but in the current Cohesion
Policy period (2007–13) it has ceased to be a Community Initiative and has become a main
funding objective in its own right. Through Community Initiatives such as INTERREG the EU
has been able to focus attention on particular European problems and opportunities concerning
regional development, experimenting with new approaches and emphasising actions to promote
the development of networks and cooperation between regions across national frontiers.
INTERREG has evolved over the years (see Figure 16.1), reﬂecting the search for the most
efﬁcient and appropriate organisation and management of the Structural Funds and INTERREG
programmes, alongside changing expectations with regard to the programmes’ impact. Since the
inception of INTERREG I in 1990, successive EU enlargements have meant that the number of

## S. 263

internal EU borders has grown, alongside increasing institutional and socio-economic differences
across the territory. The objectives and scope of transboundary cooperation programmes have
broadened over time, and much of the understanding about the role of territorial cooperation
and how best to do it has developed in a process of ‘learning by doing’.
As with Community Cohesion Policy in general, INTERREG funding is expected to deliver
value ‘additional to that which would have been secured by national and regional authorities
and the private sector’ (CEC 2001g: 4). Consequently projects funded under INTERREG should
focus on actions that member states and regions would not otherwise undertake (in particular
in relation to the ‘additional’ aspect of cooperation across national borders) and that can have
signiﬁcant leverage effects by bringing in funds from other sources. INTERREG projects require
matching funding (co-ﬁnancing) from public or private sector sources to complement the ERDF
contribution, but grant rates vary (in most programme areas they are between 50 per cent and
75 or 80 per cent of ERDF funding).
The scale of territorial cooperation activity across the EU today is enormous, but it is difﬁcult
to assess exactly how many participants have been involved in, or beneﬁted from, cooperation
projects funded under INTERREG. Just for the ﬁve INTERREG IIIB programmes in which
Germany was involved it has been estimated that more than 6,500 partners have worked
together on around 500 projects (Battis and Kersten 2008). It is impossible to give a comprehensive account of all the projects that have been funded with the support of INTERREG over
the years. Many projects have pursued a range of objectives that make it difﬁcult to sum them
up in a short review. There is also considerable variation in the activities that have been funded
in different parts of Europe and different programmes areas, reﬂecting the ‘local cultures’ of cooperation within the overall framework provided by the EU. For example, it has been reported
that under INTERREG III some transnational cooperation areas (e.g. the Baltic Sea Region or
the eastern part of the Alpine Space) have displayed a much higher ‘intensity’ of cooperation
than others (ESPON 2006f).
This scale of activity frequently raises the question of what INTERREG funding has achieved.
Given the comparatively modest ﬁnancial provisions of INTERREG (especially for transnational
and interregional cooperation), the policy and academic debate over recent years has come to
acknowledge that the main (added) value of trans-European cooperation may lie in ‘soft’, more
qualitative, learning outcomes and the overcoming of institutional barriers for cooperation
(rather than ‘hard’ infrastructure investments). This is discussed in detail in Chapter 22. In the
following sections, an overview of the INTERREG initiative as it has evolved over the years is
given, with particular emphasis on those programmes with an explicit focus on spatial planning:
INTERREG IIC and IIIB, and ESPON.
INTERREG I and related initiatives (1990–93)
The Single European Act (1986), the adoption of the Single Market programme, the accession
of Greece (1981), and of Spain and Portugal (1986) were the main drivers for EU regional policy
during the 1980s, and prompted the setting up of the Community Initiative INTERREG to support cross-border cooperation between neighbouring regions with a budget of ECU 1.1 billion.
Altogether 31 INTERREG I Operational Programmes (1991–93) were approved, which represented a variety of border regions within the EU.
The launch of INTERREG I may represent the ﬁrst steps towards multi-level governance, as
INTERREG funding did not necessarily have to be awarded separately to nation-states or regions
but could be allocated to speciﬁc cross-border structures, such as the ‘Euroregions’ or ‘Euregios’
between Belgium, the Netherlands, Germany, Luxembourg and France. As such, it built on
234
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 264

existing cross-border structures and supported their further development, and enabled a direct
dialogue between the European institutions and regional and local authorities within the
member states. As Brenner (2004: 288) has pointed out, this approach to involving regions and
municipalities and existing cross-border structures directly, was:
viewed both by municipalities and by the European Commission as a means to circumvent
national governments in pursuit of their own regulatory agendas. Thus, through such
networks, European municipalities [and regions] attempt to establish transnational lobbying
platforms without directly involving their respective national governments. Concomitantly,
the European Commission attempts to capitalize upon such networks in order to inﬂuence
local development outcomes without the direct mediation of national state institutions.
As a consequence of INTERREG funding, the ‘quantity, organizational density, and regulatory signiﬁcance’ (Brenner 2004: 286) of cross-border activities has considerably expanded
since the 1990s. Moreover, although interregional cooperation has been an important aspect
of EU policies and programmes for a number of years, the fall of the Iron Curtain prompted
the setting up of additional ‘innovative actions’ funded by the ERDF and involving partners 
from outside the EU. In this context, the ECOS-Ouverture initiative was launched in 1991 by
the European Commission in response to the signiﬁcant political, social and economic changes
taking place in central and eastern Europe. The RECITE (Regions and Cities for Europe)
programme, initiated in 1991 by DG REGIO, explicitly aimed to encourage inter-urban networking and exchange of experience between local and regional authorities across the Community. The experiences of these two programmes were later incorporated into the objectives of
INTERREG IIIC.
INTERREG II, Article 10 pilot actions and related initiatives 
(1994–99)
The Treaty on European Union (1992) and the accession of Austria, Sweden and Finland in
1995, which increased the number of internal and external borders, prompted a new phase of
trans-European cooperation with a signiﬁcantly increased EU regional policy budget for the
1994–99 funding period. Besides six mainstream Structural Funds objectives (see Table 18.2),
a total of ECU 14 billion (8 per cent of the total budget) was made available during this period
for altogether 13 Community Initiatives to co-ﬁnance innovative projects. The total budget
allocation for INTERREG II was ECU 3.5 billion in 1996 prices. This is not a great amount
compared to total spending on regional policy: it was initially intended only for ‘softer’ projects
such as joint studies and strategies, which are much less expensive than physical infrastructure.
There were initially two strands of INTERREG programmes: INTERREG IIA on cross-border
cooperation (which continued the previous INTERREG I programme with a higher budget of
ECU 2.4 billion for altogether 59 INTERREG IIA Operational Programmes); and INTERREG
IIB on the completion of selected energy networks (the former REGEN initiative), which had a
budget of ECU 500 million (1994 prices). INTERREG IIA incorporated for the ﬁrst time all
border regions along the internal and external borders of the EU, and also placed stronger
emphasis on cooperation across maritime borders. A third strand, INTERREG IIC, was added
in 1997, focusing for the ﬁrst time on transnational cooperation on spatial planning, and on
the management of water resources (see Figure 16.1).
The aims of the INTERREG IIA initiative were to assist border regions to overcome speciﬁc
problems concerning development resulting from their border position and their relative

The instruments: territorial cooperation
235
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 265

isolation from national and European centres of economic activity. The themes for INTERREG
IIA covered almost all socio-economic activities, such as the creation of SMEs, tourism, energy
and environment, agriculture and rural development, transport and telecommunications, R&D,
health, training and employment.
INTERREG IIC, on transnational cooperation on spatial planning including management of
water resources (ﬂood mitigation and drought prevention), was launched in 1997 with a
budget of ECU 420 million. There had been severe ﬂoods in Europe, and the need for more
cooperation on the issue was self-evident. This provided a window of opportunity for the
Commission to extend the initiative for cooperation on spatial development and planning despite
questions of competence in the ﬁeld. While quite a few examples of cross-border and transnational planning had been emerging across Europe (as explained in Chapter 14), this was the
ﬁrst time that Community funding was made available speciﬁcally for this task. The aims of
INTERREG IIC were: to promote a harmonious and balanced spatial development of the territory of the Union; to foster transnational cooperation within a common framework in the ﬁeld
of spatial planning by the member states, regions and other authorities and actors; to contribute
to improving the impact of Community policies on spatial development; and to help member
states and their regions cooperate on a proactive approach to common problems in the ﬁeld of
spatial development and water resource management.
Three main areas of action were supported by the INTERREG IIC initiative. Transnational
cooperation on spatial development was supported in seven cooperation areas: Western
Mediterranean and Latin Alps; South-Western Europe; Atlantic Area; North Western
Metropolitan Area (NWMA); North Sea Area; Baltic Sea Area; and Central, Adriatic, Danube
and Southeast European Space (CADSES). Flood mitigation programmes of ECU 148.15
million were set up for transnational river basins such as the Rhein-Maas basin (IRMA) and
the border area between Italy and France. For the Mediterranean countries ECU 114 million
were made available for the prevention of drought under INTERREG IIC. In addition to the
INTERREG IIC transnational cooperation programmes, altogether four so-called ‘ERDF Article
10 Pilot Actions’ were set up for additional large transnational cooperation areas (Northern
Periphery; Western Mediterranean/Latin Alps; Alpine Space; and Mediterranean Gateway)
following the same objectives and type of cooperation, although with less funding than the IIC
programmes. According to Article 10 of the ERDF regulations then in force, up to 1 per cent
of the annual ERDF budget could be used to provide support for studies or pilot schemes
concerning regional development at Community level, especially where regions along the EU’s
external border were involved. During the 1990s, the European Commission made good use of
this opportunity to fund innovative demonstration projects and studies on spatial development
(including the Europe 2000 (CEC 1991) and Europe 2000+ (CEC 1994a) reports).
With INTERREG IIC, transnational cooperation on spatial development was ‘being tested
for the ﬁrst time, using common organisation, administrative and ﬁnancial structures’ (CSD
1999: 39). INTERREG IIC was explicitly presented as one of the key operational mechanisms
for the application of the ESDP. The large mega-regions that were deﬁned as cooperation zones
for INTERREG IIC were based on the Europe 2000 studies undertaken by the European
Commission in the early 1990s (CEC 1991, 1994a) with the intention ‘to encourage new ways
of thinking about spatial prospects which is not limited by national boundaries and to stimulate
a bottom-up approach to the development of links between regions’ (CEC 1994a: 69). This new
approach made a signiﬁcant contribution to raising awareness about wider processes and
changes shaping development at the transnational and European scale, and of spatial development issues that countries cannot address independently but that require cooperation on spatial
planning.
236
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 266

However, as is often the case where Community funding is involved, there was intensive
lobbying on the designation of transnational cooperation areas within which funding would be
made available. In this case the most signiﬁcant amendment to the transnational regions deﬁned
in the Europe 2000 reports was the extension of the ‘Central and Capital Cities’ region to
incorporate the whole of the UK and the Republic of Ireland in a very large region called the
‘North-Western Metropolitan Area’. The Irish government argued that Dublin should be
included as part of the EU’s metropolitan core (Nadin and Shaw 1998: 284). The Commission,
under intense lobbying, also created new transnational zones for areas that were not originally
included. It used Article 10 funding to create other ‘pilot actions’, with the same objectives as
INTERREG, one of which – Archimed (Archipelago Mediterraneo) – included Malta.
For each of the transnational regions, Community Initiative Programmes (CIPs) setting out
speciﬁc objectives for the different programme areas and priorities for funding were prepared.
Partnerships of bodies involving at least two member states could then make bids for funding
under the thematic priorities deﬁned in the CIP. In this early stage the Commission gave emphasis to the preparation of transnational strategies such as the identiﬁcation of environmentally
sensitive areas crossing borders and the promotion of technology transfer and cooperation
networks. An important theme of INTERREG IIC was to complement and help to deliver the
aims of the ESDP through the elaboration of transnational planning strategies or ‘spatial visions’
(see Chapter 15).
During the practical implementation of INTERREG IIC some of the critical questions about
the nature of cooperation across borders came to the fore, particularly the meaning of the central
concept of ‘transnationality’. At the root of initiatives that encourage transnational or European
cooperation on spatial planning is the recognition that there are some issues that countries
cannot take on independently. Examples of such ‘transnational issues’ are inter-city infrastructure and movement, ﬂood prevention along large river basins and ecological networks. In
addition, INTERREG funding was frequently used to support cooperation on ‘common issues’,
that is, shared concerns of local or regional importance that can beneﬁt from cooperation but
do not necessarily require it (for example, waterfront regeneration or the management of urban
parks). In practice, most of the projects funded under INTERREG IIC focused on cooperation
on common issues (see Chapter 22).
While INTERREG supported cooperation of the border regions of the EU through the
Structural Funds, additional ﬁnancial instruments were made available to support cooperation
of EU regions with accession countries and other regions outside the EU following the fall of
the Iron Curtain. A speciﬁc strand in the PHARE programme (see Chapter 12) was devoted to
supporting cross-border cooperation (CBC). Although PHARE-CBC was intended to provide
‘mirroring’ ﬁnancial assistance to INTERREG in EU external border regions, many problems
with the joint management of the two funding streams (due to their different regulatory
regimes) were reported (despite the new PHARE CBC Regulation of 1998, which sought to
address these shortcomings) (see AEBR 2000).There were even greater challenges with some of
the other external funding programmes, such as TACIS, MEDA and CARDS assistance, which
lacked a programmatic approach and were implemented within very different regulatory frameworks from INTERREG. It has for instance been argued that coordination between cross-border
actions under TACIS CBC and INTERREG has in practice proved almost impossible to achieve
(AEBR 2000).
Besides INTERREG II, there were other initiatives during this funding period that are
important for understanding the evolution of EU-funded territorial cooperation. The RECITE
II programme (Regions and Cities of Europe), funded through Article 10 of the ERDF Regulation, continued between 1997 and 2001 to support interregional projects and networks of local

The instruments: territorial cooperation
