## S. 267

and regional authorities in the European Union from the previous period. ECOS-Ouverture II
(1997–03) supported interregional cooperation between local authorities in the EU and central
and eastern Europe, with a particular view to strengthening local and regional governance in,
and economic development of, transition countries. EU support for interregional cooperation
was subsequently continued under the Community Initiative INTERREG IIIC.
Moreover, the European Commission funded two experimental programmes in the 1990s to
test the spatial integrated approach as promoted by the ESDP in practice: TERRA and ICZM,
both funded through Article 10 ERDF. The TERRA Programme was launched in 1997, and
promoted cooperation on spatial planning in areas that were thought to be especially ‘vulnerable’
(e.g. mountain areas, rural areas, river basins, coastal zones). TERRA supported altogether 
15 interregional networks of local and regional authorities with responsibility for spatial
development (CEC 2000b). Cooperation and exchange of experience between geographically
comparable local authorities was expected to lead to innovative solutions to shared or joint
problems. While the Community Initiative INTERREG IIC and the pilot actions under Article
10 were concerned with transnational cooperation, TERRA allowed experimental action on
interregional cooperation and spatial planning at the local level, especially in Objective 1 regions.
Lessons from TERRA have informed the objectives of the INTERREG III Community Initiative
(2000–06).
The Demonstration Programme on Integrated Coastal Zone Management, which was undertaken between 1996 and 1999, also sought to apply the ESDP principles practically. Altogether
35 demonstration projects were selected for funding, and these represented the range and
diversity of Europe’s coastal zones. ‘Territorial management’ was promoted by the EU institutions as the appropriate approach to coordination across policy sectors and governance levels
in coastal areas (Faludi and Waterhout 2002; see CEC 2000c). In 2000, based on the demonstration projects, the Commission published a Communication on Integrated Coastal Zone
Management: A Strategy for Europe (CEC 2000c), followed in 2002 by a Council Recommendation for the implementation of ICZM in Europe (Council 2002). Given that decisionmaking competence in this area lies with the member states, it was proposed that the EU’s role
would focus on coordination tasks. Recently, ICZM has been given more attention again within
the context of the EU’s Integrated Maritime Policy (see Chapter 15).
INTERREG III (2000–06)
The negotiations on the Cohesion Policy funding period 2000–06 were strongly inﬂuenced by
preparations for the eastern enlargement and considerations about the future ﬁnancial framework of the EU. This was accompanied by shifts towards simpliﬁed programme management
procedures, and the intention to achieve more ﬁnancial discipline in Structural Funds programmes. The number of Structural Funds objectives was reduced from six to three, and the
number of Community Initiatives from 13 to four (see Chapter 18). Altogether EUR 11.5 billion
(about 4.9 per cent of the Structural Funds budget) were allocated to INTERREG III, URBAN
II, EQUAL, Leader+ and innovative actions during the 2000–06 period. For the Community
Initiative INTERREG III, three strands of cooperation were deﬁned: cross-border (INTERREG
IIIA), transnational (IIIB) and interregional (IIIC). The total budget for INTERREG III in the
funding period 2000–06 was EUR 4.875 billion (1999 prices), i.e. about 2.5 per cent of the total
Structural Funds Budget (against EUR 3.6 billion for 1994–99).
A number of changes were made to the way INTERREG functioned. With INTERREG III,
particular attention was given to territorial integration with candidate and other neighbouring
countries, while achieving greater social and economic cohesion of the European territory as a
238
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 268

whole. A number of principles were set out in the INTERREG III guidelines as a framework
for cooperation (CEC 2004c). All measures and operations had to be based on a joint crossborder or transnational strategy and development programme with a clear Community added
value. Partnerships for the management of INTERREG III programmes were expected to be 
wide and to follow a ‘bottom-up approach’ by involving national, regional and local authorities, economic and social partners and other relevant bodies. The INTERREG III guidelines
placed great emphasis on ‘truly joint structures’ (CEC 2004c: point 8) to prepare, manage and 
implement cooperation programmes. INTERREG programmes had to be complementary to 
the ‘mainstream’ Structural Funds programmes and other Community Initiatives, and effective coordination between INTERREG III and external Community policy instruments (such 
as PHARE, TACIS, ISPA, MEDA, CARDS, EDF or other Community programmes with an
external policy dimension – see Chapter 12) was encouraged. The evaluation undertaken on
INTERREG II showed that the monitoring and evaluation systems in place were insufﬁcient
(LRDP 2003). For the 2000–06 programming period, three types of evaluations consequently
became a requirement: ex ante and mid-term (both organised by INTERREG programmes
themselves), and an ex post evaluation (which is the responsibility of the Commission).
Community Initiative Programmes (CIPs) were prepared by joint cross-border or transnational committees and submitted to the European Commission for approval. The EU member
states involved in INTERREG III programmes were responsible for their efﬁcient and correct
management and implementation. In partnership with regional and local authorities, they
designated a managing authority (MA) (which could be any public or private authority or body
at national, regional or local level) with overall responsibility for managing the programme. 
A paying authority (PA) was responsible for the programme’s ﬁnancial administration. A Programme Monitoring Committee (PMC), comprising representatives of the national, regional and
local authorities involved in the cooperation area, was responsible for overall strategic guidance
of the programme. The joint selection of projects and the coordinated monitoring of their
implementation were carried out by a common programme steering committee (PSC), which 
was also made up of national, regional and local representatives. For each of the INTERREG
IIIA and IIIB programmes, a ‘joint technical secretariat’ was set up, charged with the overall
management of the programme and the evaluation of project applications. 
In this programming period, in order to improve the ﬁnancial performance of Structural
Funds programmes, the automatic ‘decommitment’ rule (‘N+2’ rule) was introduced. At the
beginning of each programming period, annual financial allocations are made to each
INTERREG programme. Programmes then have to spend funds (and provide proof of payment
for that) by the end of the second year following the year in which they are allocated. This target
is called the N+2 rule. Failure to meet it means that money will be returned to the budget of
the European Commission. It has been argued that, as a consequence, decision-making processes
on which projects to ﬁnance were sometimes signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by concerns over the
automatic decommitment of allocated funds, leading to agreements on projects that under other
circumstances might have not been considered to be of high relevance to the cooperation area
(Graute 1998).
INTERREG IIIA on cross-border cooperation supported integrated regional development
between neighbouring national, regional and local authorities (as well as other partners, such 
as NGOs) along national borders. The majority of the ﬁnancial resources for INTERREG III 
were devoted to this strand (67 per cent of the total budget). There were altogether 64 INTERREG
IIIA programmes, and they provided funding for projects under a wide range of priority
objectives, including: urban, rural and coastal development; social, environmental and economic
issues; transport, information and communication technologies; and support for cooperation in
the legal and administrative spheres to promote economic development and social cohesion.

The instruments: territorial cooperation

## S. 269

INTERREG IIIB supported transnational cooperation between national, regional and local
authorities and other actors to contribute to harmonious territorial integration ‘across large
groupings of European regions, with a view to achieving sustainable, harmonious and balanced
development in the Community and better territorial integration with candidate and other
neighbouring countries’ (CEC 2004c: point 12). INTERREG IIIB was presented as the main
instrument for the application of the ESDP. It built on the experiences of the INTERREG IIC
programme (1997–99), on related programmes on ﬂood and drought prevention, and on pilot
actions ﬁnanced by ERDF Article 10 in the previous funding period. Thirteen transnational
cooperation areas were deﬁned: Northern Periphery; North Sea; Baltic Sea; North West Europe;
Atlantic Area; Central, Adriatic, Danube and Southeast European Space (CADSES); South West
Europe; Western Mediterranean; Alpine Space; ARCHIMED (Archipelago Mediterraneo);
Caribbean Area; Indian Ocean Area; and Açores-Madeira-Canarias Area. This type of cooperation was allocated 27 per cent of the INTERREG III budget. Some of the programme areas
covered neighbouring non-EU regions, for which coordination with the Community’s external
policy instruments (such as PHARE-CBC or TACIS) was required.
Project partners from within the identiﬁed areas could apply for funding for transnational
projects related to a wide range of priorities and measures with a spatial impact, based on the
policy priorities of the ESDP (CSD 1999), namely: polycentric development and urban-rural
partnerships; sustainable transport and improved access to the information society; the
promotion of the environment and the good management of cultural heritage and of natural
resources, in particular water resources; and the promotion of integration between maritime
regions and of insular regions. As with INTERREG IIC, it was envisaged that spatial visions
would be prepared to provide a transnational strategy for the programme (see Chapter 15).
INTERREG IIIB placed great emphasis on promoting an ‘integrated spatial approach’ through
transnational cooperation projects, underpinned by appropriate measures in response to
common problems and opportunities, which would lead to genuine beneﬁts for the transnational
area. The projects selected were expected to demonstrate concrete, visible and innovative
results. Infrastructure investments were only eligible if they could demonstrate a transnational
impact or be jointly implemented by partners. It is impossible to give an overview of the full
range of INTERREG IIIB projects that were funded, but Box 16.1 gives some illustrative
examples. As in the previous programming period, one of the key criteria in the process of
project selection was ‘transnationality’, which was interpreted in a rather strict sense in some
programme areas. The critical issue was the difﬁculty of deﬁning projects that have genuine
impacts in more than one country. In spite of that, many of the projects funded across all
programmes were arguably of national rather than transnational signiﬁcance.
INTERREG IIIC on interregional cooperation was set up to ‘improve the effectiveness of
policies and instruments for regional development and cohesion through networking,
particularly for regions whose development is lagging behind and those undergoing conversion’
(CEC 2004c: point 17). INTERREG IIIC built on the experiences of the RECITE and ECOS-
Ouverture programmes, and offered opportunities for cooperation among actors in noncontiguous regions. The whole of the (enlarged) Community was eligible for INTERREG IIIC
funding. Four programme areas were set up for management purposes, which did not, however,
determine the location of project partners in eligible zones (as was case for the A and B strands).
Of the total INTERREG III budget, 6 per cent was allocated to Strand C on interregional
cooperation. The topics for interregional cooperation were very broad, and generally more
related to ‘regional governance’ issues, although there are several examples of projects that
focused on spatial development.

Box 16.1 Examples of INTERREG IIIB projects
In the 2000–06 programming period, INTERREG funding provided an opportunity for a very
large number of public authorities and other planning related bodies to get involved in transnational cooperation on spatial planning. There are estimates that more than 10,000 people
were involved in the transnational strand alone (Müller et al. 2005), and many hundreds of
projects were funded. In terms of awareness raising alone, this could be considered very good
value for an initiative that takes only a fraction of the Structural Funds budget. The variety of
INTERREG IIIB projects that have been funded makes it impossible to provide a short summary
of their content and achievements. Most programmes have project databases on their websites,
and have prepared synthesis reports on projects and their results (see, for example, Brol et al.
2007; Jancˇicˇ et al. 2008; Stephenson 2008; Zaucha and Matczak 2007). The examples in this 
box are taken from a background document to the TAEU (Hartz and Saad 2007) and other
synthesis reports. The selection of projects is merely illustrative to give a ﬂavour of the types
of projects that have been funded.
MSUO: Maritime Safety Umbrella Operation (www.maritime-safety.org)
The Maritime Safety Umbrella Operation is an umbrella project bringing together several
INTERREG IIIB cooperation areas to facilitate cooperation among actors and projects in the
ﬁeld of maritime safety. It is coordinated by the North Sea Programme Secretariat, but also
involved projects in the Baltic Sea Region, North West Europe and the Northern Periphery.
It was set up in response to the perceived limitations of the cooperation boundaries for certain
types of spatial issues, particularly maritime safety.
BEEN: Baltic Energy Efficiency Network for the Building Stock 
(INTERREG IIIB Baltic Sea Region, www.been-online.net)
Due to the high signiﬁcance of the topic of energy efﬁciency in residential buildings on the
European and national level, the participants of the BEEN project committed themselves to
continue the communication and exchange on that topic. The maintenance of the ‘BEENetwork’
and the exchange of knowledge and experiences with regard to new approaches and strategies
for energy efﬁciency in buildings have reportedly helped partners to achieve their national and
local targets in the ﬁeld of energy efﬁciency in residential buildings. After completion of the
INTERREG project, a declaration on the ‘further continuation of the BEENetwork’ was signed.
AlpFRail: Alpine Freight Railway (INTERREG IIIB Alpine Space, 
www.alpfrail.com)
The objective of the AlpFRail project was to transfer goods transport from road to rail in the
whole Alpine area by developing innovative concepts. The entire logistics chain of freight ﬂows,
from the origin to the destination across Europe, was analysed to find ways to better
coordinate railway logistics ‘from one single provider’ through coordination measures between
all relevant actors.
ESPACE: European spatial planning – adapting to climate change
(INTERREG IIIB North West Europe, www.espace-project.org)
The ESPACE project developed tools and mechanisms to integrate techniques for coping with
climate change into planning systems and processes. Its ‘decision testing tool’ allowed strategic 
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 271

Complementary to the three INTERREG strands, a budget of EUR 50 million (2001 prices)
was made available under Article 53 of the INTERREG III Guidelines (CEC 2004c) to ﬁnance
two additional horizontal programmes aimed at promoting the sharing of experience and good
practice:
•
The European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON), set up in 2001, is a
programme that funds cooperative research projects and is discussed below.
•
The programme INTERACT (INTERREG – Animation, Coordination, Transfer) was set
up to improve the effectiveness of the implementation of INTERREG III across the different
strands. The focus of INTERACT was on providing a platform for exchange of experience
and networking, information dissemination and support to those involved in managing
INTERREG III programmes, and on providing information on INTERREG activities to
national, regional and local actors and the public in general.
European Territorial Cooperation (2007–13) (‘INTERREG IV’)
and the objective of territorial cohesion
Prompted by the enlargement of the EU to 27 member states and to support the implementation
of the Lisbon-Gothenburg Strategy, there were several signiﬁcant substantive and procedural
changes to the framework for territorial cooperation from 2007. INTERREG ceased to be a
separate Community Initiative and was integrated into the new mainstream objective of
‘European territorial cooperation’ (unofﬁcially also referred to as ‘INTERREG IV’), next to the
two other main objectives of ‘Convergence’ and ‘Competitiveness’ (see Chapter 18). The budget
for European territorial cooperation increased to EUR 8.7 billion (about 2.5 per cent of the total
Structural Funds budget), although this is now for 27 member states. The three strands of
cooperation remain, but the emphasis on cross-border working has again been strengthened. The
INTERACT and ESPON programmes have also been continued in the 2007–13 programming
period, and with increased budgets. Funding for cooperation with non-EU member states and
regions is provided with the European Neighbourhood and Partnership Instrument (ENPI),
which replaces previous external instruments and is expected to facilitate the coordination
between different EU funds along the EU external border (see Chapter 12).
242
The European spatial planning agenda
Box 16.1 continued
decisions to be tested against a range of future scenarios. Committed to engaging civil society,
the project carried out a ‘caring for climate’ competition, encouraging schools to develop
educational toolkits showing climate change impacts.
FLOWS: Flood Plain Land Use Optimising Workable Sustainability
(INTERREG IIIB North Sea Region, www.flows.nu)
The FLOWS project developed web-based applications and study modules for regional and
sector planners in the ﬁeld of ﬂood control. The topics covered are mathematical-technical
modelling and forecasting of ﬂood events, adaptive strategies for ﬂood risks, risk awareness
within the affected population and a system for experts in urban and regional planning
participating in water management-related tasks.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 272

The reform of EU Cohesion Policy sought to encourage a more strategic approach and further
decentralisation, and to reinforce the performance and quality of programmes and simplify their
management. For each territorial cooperation programme, an operational programme was
prepared by the member states and regions involved and submitted to the European Commission
for approval. Since 2008, the member states have had to present regular reports on the progress
of implementation, which are compiled into summary reports by the Commission.
The funding priorities for all three strands of INTERREG IV are drawn from the 
Lisbon-Gothenburg Strategy and set out in the Community Strategic Guidelines on Cohesion
(Council 2006a). The thematic priorities for all territorial cooperation programmes now focus
on innovation, environment, accessibility and sustainable urban development. For transnational
cooperation programmes (the former B Strand), there is no longer a requirement for an
integrated spatial planning approach in cooperation projects, and many proposed activities are
not even ‘spatial’ in character. There is an emphasis on projects providing more concrete outputs
and on projects of a ‘strategic’ nature of relevance to the cooperation area.
Besides the changes to budget and funding priorities, there have again been changes to the scope
and deﬁnition of cooperation areas. The INTERREG IVA cross-border areas have become larger
by combining programmes along the same national borders (‘one border, one programme’) (see
Figure 16.2). There are now 52 INTERREG IVA cooperation programmes. The interregional
programme (C Strand) is being administered by one central Secretariat, based in Lille (rather than
the previous four zones). There have also been changes to some of the INTERREG IVB
transnational cooperation areas (e.g. the split of the previous CADSES programme into the ‘East
Central Europe’ and ‘South East Europe’ programmes), but there are still 13 transnational programming areas (see Figure 16.3).
The effects of INTERREG programmes and projects have so far proved difﬁcult to assess
comprehensively (see Chapter 22). It is widely acknowledged that results were variable, however,
because there are great differences in the types of border region and cooperation areas to begin
with, and these have resulted in different approaches to territorial cooperation and sometimes considerable variations in the deﬁnition of ‘transnationality’. Alongside a changing policy
framework, many cooperation areas have also undergone changes over time in the areas 
eligible for support, and this has may have affected learning processes in cooperation and 
the maturing of cooperation structures. For transnational cooperation areas in particular, the
deﬁnition of the relevant areas is important for determining the agenda for cooperation.
Changes to the cooperation areas have led to a considerable ‘blurring of the initial intentions
for cooperation . . . They are too large to suggest speciﬁc transnational issues’ (Dühr and Nadin
2007: 379). 
The changing regulatory framework for territorial cooperation has raised the expectations
for cooperation structures and tangible results, but as Bachtler and Polverari (2007: 125) have
argued:
the current regulations and strategic guidelines [in the 2007–13 period] do not seem to solve
the constraints that typically have hindered the effectiveness of territorial cooperation, 
like the intangible nature of the outcomes of cooperation and the difﬁculty in quantifying
the money value of cooperation projects. On a more operational level, the complexity,
vagueness, and lack of proportionality of the bureaucratic requirements associated with 
cooperation projects is often a constraint, making it difficult, especially for smaller
organisations, to be active partners. Communication between partners also tends to be
problematic, not just because of the different languages spoken, but also because of the
different domestic institutional settings and traditions.

## S. 275

An innovation in the 2007–13 funding period was introduced to try and address some of these issues
more effectively: the Regulation on a ‘European grouping of territorial cooperation’ (EGTC).
EU Regulation on a European grouping of territorial
cooperation (EGTC)
The Regulation on an EGTC (Council 2006b) enables regional and local authorities from
different EU countries to set up groupings of territorial authorities across the EU to create a
transboundary authority with legal personality. The Regulation on EGTC is a response to two
main drivers: bottom-up, reflecting the perceived need for a stronger legal basis to take
established cooperation activities to the next level by providing more institutional, legal and
administrative certainty; and top-down, responding to the increasingly decentralised and
partnership-based approach to EU Cohesion Policy that potentially increased the complexity 
of managing INTERREG programmes. However, EGTCs are not restricted to the management
of EU programmes. They can be used to organise and manage cross-border, transnational or
interregional cooperation measures, with or without a ﬁnancial contribution from the EU, such
as the management of a cross-border transport service or a joint cross-border hospital. To carry
out these tasks, an EGTC may create its own structure, have assets and hire staff.
The EGTC Regulation required member states to adopt national legislation by 1 August 2007
to facilitate the implementation of EGTCs. At the time of writing, some member states had still
not adopted national provisions to make the regulation effective. Where national provisions are
in place, an EGTC can be set up by territorial authorities from at least two EU member states,
as well as involve third countries if these have appropriate legal frameworks in place. Those
involved can be at any level of government; they can be regional or local authorities, associations
or any other public body. Setting up an EGTC can be a rather complex process, but the beneﬁts
are expected to outweigh this, by overcoming the obstacles to territorial cooperation that different legal systems, administrative structures and cultures present, and replacing them through an
agreed set of regulations at a supranational level. The use of EGTCs is optional, and the uptake
has initially been hesitant. In practice EGTCs are mostly of interest to those regions where no
bi- or multilateral agreements have been signed to date. In areas where well-established cooperation structures already exist their value may be more limited and may not necessarily be applied
to replace existing legal agreements (for example under the Madrid Outline Convention).1
The ﬁrst EGTC to be implemented was the EGTC of Lille-Kortrijk-Tournai between France
and Belgium (set up in January 2008). Other EGTCs have since been set up between Hungary
and Slovakia in the region of Ister-Granum, and between Spain and Portugal at the border of
Galicia and Norte. The Grande Région SaarLorLux (Belgium/Luxembourg/France/Germany) is
in the process of setting up an EGTC to take over the INTERREG programme management for
this cross-border region. The scope and membership of the EGTCs that have been set up to date
varies, and not all have a spatial development objective. In any case, the Regulation presents
considerable progress towards the institutionalisation of territorial governance structures for
cross-border and transnational cooperation.
The European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON) 
and the notion of evidence-based policy-making
The European Spatial Planning Observation Network (ESPON) was conceived in parallel to
INTERREG IIC during the ESDP process. In preparation for ESPON, a Study Programme on
European Spatial Planning (SPESP) had been undertaken between 1998 and 1999 to gain
246
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 276

experience with a European network of research institutes on spatial planning (CEC 2000d).
There were considerable discussions about the organisation of ESPON, with the European
Parliament, for example, supporting a more institutionalised form (e.g. as an agency or
‘observatory’), because it considered ‘the intergovernmental dynamic’ to have ‘exhausted 
its opportunities for action’ (EP 1998a: 6). Yet some member states were doubtful about the
role of such an observatory, as Williams (2000: 362) has argued, ‘not merely because some
governments cannot see the point of it, but precisely because they can see it playing such a role,
which may not be welcome as existing levels of beneﬁt may come under scrutiny’. To date, the
ESPON programme has not been institutionalised, and continues as a funding programme in
the 2007–13 period. However, Böhme and Schön (2006: 67) have discussed whether, following
the ratiﬁcation of the Lisbon Treaty, with territorial cohesion becoming a shared competence,
a European agency on spatial research would be more appropriate than a network.
ESPON 2002–06
The creation of ESPON was a major step in European spatial planning. It was set up in 2002,
funded from the INTERREG programme allocation for 2000–06. It aimed at providing detailed
spatial information for the EU territory and to set up a decentralised network of spatial
research institutes across Europe. The ‘ESPON space’ that projects covered was that of the 
‘EU-29’: 27 countries that are now members of the EU (with 12 of these still accession countries
when ESPON started) plus Norway and Switzerland. As with the INTERREG programmes,
ESPON is subject to the ERDF regulations, and control and management are in the hands of a
managing authority, a paying authority and a monitoring committee. The monitoring committee
is the body responsible for substantive content. It is supported by a small coordination unit,
based in Esch-sur-Alzette (Luxembourg).
Over four years, 35 major studies were completed under the ESPON 2006 programme,
involving more than 600 researchers and 130 institutions (see Chapter 6 for an overview of the
projects that have been funded). Much effort was concentrated on spatial analysis and a more
quantitative approach to the representation of spatial trends (for an example of an ‘ESPON map’
see Plate 9). Only a few individual projects gave room for other, more qualitative, methods of
understanding European space and its future development, such as spatial scenarios (Chapter
15). ESPON has become a mainstay of European spatial planning, perhaps the most signiﬁcant
element during a period when there was otherwise little action on carrying forward the ESDP.
The mid-term review of the ESPON programme (MVA 2003) pointed to the limitations of
funding – enabling breadth and posing new questions but not allowing for sufﬁcient depth 
of enquiry on some topics. This view has been reinforced elsewhere (Bengs 2006). A study on
ESPON prepared for the European Commission (Rambøll Management 2006) argued that
ESPON 2006 had created added value for the scientiﬁc community, but less so for practitioners.
The processes of funding ESPON 2006 projects have been described as overly complex and
bureaucratic, and the decision-making process on which projects to fund as highly politicised
(Rambøll Management 2006). This, in combination with limited project budgets, led to a
geographical imbalance in participation with an overrepresentation of large research institutes
and universities, mostly from north-west Europe.
ESPON’s agenda was at the outset very strongly tied to the ESDP (see Table 6.1). There were
expectations that project results should quickly be available to feed into the Commission’s work
programme, which led to ‘squeals of anguish’ among the researchers involved in complex
multinational collaborative projects and coping with great problems of data compatibility
(Hague 2008: 5). ESPON results have, however, been used in various documents, including the
Third Cohesion Report (CEC 2004a), and in the drafting of the Territorial State and Perspectives

## S. 277

in 2007 (BMVBS 2007a). The selection of project results for further use has been met with
concern. Bengs (2006: 8) for instance has pointed out that
one of the most interesting results of the whole programme was the ﬁnding that the EU support
of agriculture tends to be concentrated in centrally located and wealthy areas of the union where
agriculture is most productive. When this result was presented at a project conference, the
representative of the [European] Commission told the audience that, despite the obvious ‘truth
value’ of the proposition, such results could jeopardize the future of ‘territorial cohesion’, an
aim that the Commission had been ﬁghting for. One conclusion of such incidents is to ask
whether there is any point in studying the spatial implications of EU policies if inconvenient
research results cannot be presented. The whole exercise seems to lose its rationale.
ESPON 2007–13
Expectations for ESPON 2006 were rather ambitious considering the limited budget and the
‘learning-by-doing’ nature of research cooperation and management structures. This may
explain some of the criticisms. In any case, sufﬁcient momentum and credibility had been gained
to carry ESPON forward into the 2007–13 funding period with a higher budget of EUR 
45 million. Projects are meant to cover the ‘EU-31 space’ (EU-27, plus Switzerland, Norway
Iceland and Liechtenstein). In parallel with other shifts during the ﬁrst half of the 2000s, the
emphasis changed and so did the name: ESPON became the European Observation Network
for Territorial Development and Cohesion (ESPON 2013). Projects under the ESPON 2013
programme will be funded under four main priorities:
1
applied research on territorial development, competitiveness and cohesion to provide
evidence on trends, perspectives and policy impacts;
2
targeted analysis based on user demand;
3
scientiﬁc platform and tools: territorial indicators, data, analytical tools and scientiﬁc
support; and
4
capitalisation, ownership and participation through capacity building, dialogue and networking.
The theme devoted to ‘targeted analysis’, where potential clients (including INTERREG programmes) are invited to put forward research project ideas, was included in answer to criticism
that ESPON has to become more relevant for a practitioner audience. The approach taken 
in the ESPON 2013 programme in this respect has, however, not been accepted universally as 
the appropriate response. Faludi (2008a: 14), for example, has argued that although some 
of the practical problems may have been overcome, ‘critics worried about the interface between
research and policy-making will not be satisﬁed to hear that, if anything, the short-term, userdriven and policy-driven emphasis will increase. Presumably, this will be at the expense of
opportunities for long-term, fundamental, and inevitably critical pursuits’.
The complex relationship between research results and policy-making has been addressed in
debates on ‘evidence-based policy-making’ at EU level.
The notion of evidence-based policy-making
The notion of evidence-based policy-making has gained prominence in the context of the ESPON
2006 programme and related work. The Territorial State and Perspectives document (BMVBS
2007a), for example, has been presented as providing the evidence base for the Territorial
Agenda of the EU (EU Ministers 2007a), although the timescales of ﬁnalising the two documents
248
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 278

made such a direct link practically impossible (Dühr and Müller 2009). Faludi (2009a: 21)
explains that:
the logic underlying the Territorial Agenda as an evidence-based document has [. . .] been
that of objective, scientiﬁc information providing an unambiguous and thus unquestionable
basis for action. It is a logic suiting the Commission as it seeks to manage hugely complex
operations with inadequate resources. Within this constellation, once agreed, ‘objective’
indicators and so forth reduce the burden of decision-making. The logic is also to the liking
of experts. It promises to shield them from political interference . . .
There are considerable tensions in the concept of evidence-based policy in complex policymaking processes, which by most are now accepted as being socially constructed. Faludi
(2009a: 19) explains that ‘the advocacy of evidence-based planning, or evidence-based public
policy for that matter, ignores the reams of publications criticising the idea that scientiﬁc
evidence could form an unambiguous guide to action’. Davoudi (2006b) suggested that it would
be more appropriate to speak of policies as being ‘evidence-informed’ (rather than evidencebased, which assumes a much more direct relationship between analysis and policy).
Given this emphasis on ‘evidence-based policy-making’ in the ESPON programme, it is not
surprising that a frequent complaint of ESPON researchers was that policy-makers have tended
to ask for scientiﬁc results ‘on demand’ (see Bengs 2006; Gløersen et al. 2007; Prezioso 2007).
The ESPON 2006 programme was based on ‘a research model dominated by the effort to
quantify spatial patterns and processes’ (Waterhout 2008: 194), reﬂecting the understanding 
of ‘objective science’. This has been considered problematic in two respects. First, complex
indicator-based maps may be too abstract for the envisaged dialogue with policy-makers, while
in-depth case studies, which could allow a better link to policy and practice, were disregarded
at least in the ESPON 2006 programme as too ‘time consuming, descriptive, and atypical’
(Hague and Hachmann 2008: 35). Second, the lack of comprehensive and up-to-date spatial data
for 29 European countries has been a major concern in most ESPON 2006 projects. As a
consequence, as Davoudi (2007: 93) has argued:
the nature and scope of analyses have been almost totally dictated by the availability of
harmonised, comparable data for the whole of Europe. This drawback has inevitably led
to a selective approach to the development of indicators, criteria, models, and typologies,
using those that can be quantiﬁed by pan-European data.
Finding long-term solutions to the lack of harmonised data for the European territory is
considered imperative for the future impact and credibility of ESPON research (Bengs 2006), 
in particular if claims about the analytical evidence underpinning policy-making are to be
maintained. While the challenge of comparable and up-to-date spatial data remains an issue in
the ESPON 2013 programme, the relationship between research and policy is now more carefully
presented as providing comparable information and evidence to support policy development.
Conclusion
The previous sections have shown that EU-funded territorial cooperation across national borders
has evolved in response to changing circumstances (such as enlargement and ongoing European
integration) and with the intention of achieving a more efﬁcient management of the programmes.
More recently, these readjustments have been likened to a paradigm shift in the orientation of
the programmes towards the objectives expressed in the Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda in the

The instruments: territorial cooperation

## S. 279

2007–13 period. This shifted the emphasis away from spatial planning in the INTERREG IVB
programmes towards more thematic priorities. Moreover, the primary objective of more balanced
development (the cohesion objective) has been complemented with a uniform economic growth
policy (the competitiveness objective) in the 2007–13 Cohesion Policy period (see Dühr and Nadin
2007). The tension between these objectives is arguably most pronounced in territorial
cooperation programmes, where on the one hand cooperation is seen as a tool to help overcome
the barrier effect of national borders in order to achieve economic competitiveness, and on the
other hand it is seen as a means to strengthen economic and social cohesion in the Community.
The objective of ‘territorial cohesion’ included in the Lisbon Treaty is expected to play an
important role in determining the scope and objectives of future territorial cooperation programmes. It certainly will give new stimulus to the debate on the added value of territorial
cooperation and how programmes can play an important role post-2013 for achieving the
objective of territorial cohesion. The Open Method of Coordination (OMC), discussed in
Chapter 15, is frequently mentioned as an appropriate governance model for EU territorial
cohesion (see Faludi 2007c). Within the context of OMC, ‘cross-border and transnational
cooperation, such as those facilitated by the Community Initiative INTERREG, have a major
role to play’ (Davoudi 2005b: 687), as could the implementation of transnational strategies 
such as for the Baltic Sea Region or the Danube corridor. It is possible that such large-scale
geographical approaches will be linked more strongly to INTERREG-funded cooperation in
future. Monitoring of spatial trends and the assessment of territorial impacts are also likely to
play an important role in a future ‘EU territorial cohesion policy’ post-2013, and experiences
with the ESPON programmes will be useful in this context.
Further reading
Information on INTERREG programmes, including ex post evaluations and related studies, can
be found on the website of the European Commission DG REGIO (http://ec.europa.eu/regional_
policy/index_en.htm). Information on INTERREG projects are available from the individual
websites of the INTERREG programmes, most of which have a project database.
The Committee of the Regions’ report Trans-European Cooperation Between Territorial
Authorities (CoR 2002) gives a good overview of the emergence of the initiative. The website
of the AEBR (www.aebr.net/) lists a number of studies and practical handbooks on cross-border
cooperation. There are also a number of studies on INTERREG and ESPON cooperation that
have been funded by the INTERACT programme (www.interact-eu.net/).
There is at present no comprehensive academic review of INTERREG and ESPON programmes and projects, and some programme areas are arguably more frequently subject to
analysis in the English-language literature than others. A Special Issue on the ‘Europeanization
of spatial planning through territorial cooperation’ (Dühr et al. 2007) includes articles on
territorial cooperation and ESPON. The German Bundesamt für Bauwesen und Raumordnung
(BBR) has published a number of studies on the effects of the ﬁve transnational cooperation
programmes in which Germany is involved and on the relevance of the ESPON programme for
Germany (see Görmar et al. 2005; Hartz and Saad 2007; Huebner und Stellfeldt-Koch 2009;
Kujath et al. 2009; Müller et al. 2005; available on the BBR website: www.bbr.bund.de).
Information on the ESPON programme and projects is available from the website www.espon.eu.
A Special Issue on evidence-based planning provides further debate on this concept (Faludi and
Waterhout 2006). The edited volume European Spatial Research and Planning (Faludi 2008b)
includes a number of contributions on the ESPON programme and projects. Information and
updates on EGTCs can be found on the website of the Committee of the Regions (www.cor.
europa.eu/) and on the website of the INTERACT programme (www.interact-eu.net/).
250
The European spatial planning agenda
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 280

EU spatial policy
Sectoral policies and their 
territorial effects
Other parts of this book explain how the European dimension of spatial planning arises in part
from the effects of a wide range of policies on spatial development and the operation of planning
in the member states. This part reviews the main EU sector policies that have territorial effects.
A spatial impact occurs when ‘Community measures modify the spatial structure and potentials
in the economy and society thereby altering land use patterns and landscapes’ or ‘inﬂuence the
competitive position or spatial signiﬁcance of a city or region within the European economic
system and settlement pattern’ (CSD 1999: 13). EU policies contribute to the inﬂuence of
government on locational and land-use decisions, on the spatial distribution of social and
economic activity at all spatial scales (Williams 1996), and on land value and mobility (van
Ravesteyn and Evers 2004).
Four types of EU policies are relevant for spatial planning and territorial development:
•
EU sectoral policies that are based on the spatially targeted distribution of financial
resources and investments such as EU regional policy (through the Structural and Cohesion
Funds) and the Trans-European Networks, or on the geographical designation of certain
areas in which particular regulations apply (e.g. under the EU nature protection policy);
•
EU sectoral policies that are not deﬁned in spatial terms but have an inﬂuence on the spatial
structure and development of the EU territory, such as the Common Agricultural Policy
(CAP), which has strongly shaped rural areas; or the Single Market, which has inﬂuenced
the location decisions of businesses and individuals;
•
EU sectoral policies that are not expressed in spatial terms but change the context for action
of public authorities and private businesses and their capacity to intervene in, or carry out,
urban planning and development tasks (e.g. EU competition policies); and
•
EU sectoral policies that are implemented through the planning system and domestic
planning legislation in the member states, of which there are only a few (e.g. Environmental
Impact Assessment and Strategic Environmental Assessment Directives).
In the next ﬁve chapters, EU sectoral policies with the most signiﬁcant spatial impacts and
relevance for national planning systems and practices are presented: economic and competition
policies, regional (or cohesion) policy, agricultural and rural policies, transport policy and
environmental policy. With every Treaty revision, the EU has usually expanded its competences
in these spatially relevant sectors, often in relation to the argument that such intervention is
needed to implement the Single European Market (e.g. by ensuring comparable environmental
standards across the EU territory). Thus, altogether ‘the EU has not been unwilling to conduct
spatially relevant policy through its sectoral competences, usually arguing that establishing a
level playing ﬁeld for competition, addressing Community-wide problems and dealing with cross
border problems justiﬁes intervention’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 17).

Part V

## S. 281

These policy sectors have direct impact: through various types of EU law (the types are
explained in Chapter 11); through ﬁnancial support instruments, such as income support (for
example, through subsidies to farmers under the Common Agricultural Policy or investments
in infrastructure or training in geographically targeted areas); in the ﬁnancing of thematic
research programmes; and through other European initiatives in culture, energy or technological
innovation (CEC 1998a: 9). EU policies can also support exchanges of experience between
member states, regions and cities, and data collection and monitoring activities at the European
level. The development of non-binding resolutions, guidelines or strategic documents such as
white and green papers is also a key part of EU policy-making. European policies can ﬁnally
take the shape of common guidelines for public action agreed through processes of intergovernmental cooperation (as opposed to the supranational decision-making process described
in Chapter 11) between a sub-group of member states (for example, the euro). In the subsequent
chapters the word ‘policy’ or ‘policies’ will consequently be used not only to refer to EU
legislation but also to all those other forms of action.
European policy-makers and academic researchers began to pay attention to the territorial
impacts and spatial planning relevance of EU sectoral policies during the years leading to the
preparation of the ESDP (see Chapter 14), starting in the late 1980s. The ﬁrst comprehensive
study that attempted to identify EU policy sectors with an impact on ‘spatial planning’ was a
working report from the Commission (CEC 1998a). It was followed by a number of studies by
the Commission looking at the impact of speciﬁc policies on social and economic cohesion (e.g.
Robert et al. 2001), by studies on the territorial impacts of various EU policies carried out under
the ESPON programme (2002–06), and by academic studies that looked in a systematic way at
the overall impact of EU policies on the domestic planning systems of speciﬁc European
member states, such as Tewdwr-Jones and Williams (2001) for the UK and van Ravesteyn and
Evers (2004) for the Netherlands. Some researchers have argued that although spatial impacts
of EU sector policies can be indirect or ‘unseen’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004), because they
are implemented through national law and domestic planning systems, they collectively have a
much stronger impact on planning systems than actual ‘European spatial planning’ initiatives
such as the ESDP. In Chapter 23 the impact of EU policies on planning systems, policies and
practices of EU member states is discussed in more depth.
252
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 282

EU economic and competition 
policies
Although primarily a matter of the free ﬂow of people, goods and capital, the creation of a common
market has produced some tangible effects on physical space as well . . . Regulation of competition
by the EU (e.g. restrictions on state aid, liberalisation of markets and anti-monopoly legislation) can
also indirectly affect spatial development patterns by inﬂuencing business location decisions.
(van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 73)
Introduction
From the very beginnings of the European Coal and Steel Community (Chapter 7) the EU has
been driven by dynamics of economic integration and market liberalisation, ﬁrst through a
customs union, later through a common market ﬁnally leading to an economic and monetary
union. EU macroeconomic and competition policies have had a major inﬂuence on economic
activity, trade, investment ﬂows and human mobility in Europe, and thus on the economic
geography of the continent. Although this inﬂuence was acknowledged in the European Spatial
Development Perspective (CSD 1999), the territorial impacts and spatial planning implications
of these policies remain largely unaddressed in the literature on European spatial planning and
spatial development. The broad label of EU economic and competition policies encompasses
competition, internal market and services, taxation and customs union, economic and ﬁnancial
affairs, enterprise and industry and external trade. EU economic and competition policies have
territorial and spatial impacts in two distinct ways (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004):
•
They directly affect the functioning of markets, the behaviour of economic players (ﬁrms
and consumers), the location decisions of businesses and individuals, ﬂows of capital,
labour, goods and ideas, and consequently inﬂuence the geography of investment, economic
activity and migrations.
•
They indirectly affect the ability of public authorities to intervene in the economy and
conduct economic, environmental and spatial planning policies in support of regions, cities
and neighbourhoods in need, as EU regulations constrain the design and implementation
of public policies and public service provision.
These two types of impacts are considered in turn in this chapter: ﬁrst by looking at the impact
of the Single Market and Economic and Monetary Union on the economic geography of the EU
territory; second, by looking at the inﬂuence of EU competition policy – in particular the
regulation of state aid and the liberalisation of public services – on territorial cohesion and
spatial planning in the EU. Particular emphasis is put on the tension between the objective of
market integration and economic competitiveness that underpins EU economic and competition
policies and the objectives of social/territorial cohesion and sustainable development that form
another key part of the EU agenda.

## S. 283

The territorial impact of the Single Market and 
of Economic and Monetary Union
In 1957 the Treaty of Rome laid down four goals to fulﬁl the long-term establishment of a Single
Market in the (then) EEC: a customs union; the progressive approximation of legislation
between member states; the establishment of the four freedoms of movement for goods, services,
capital and labour; and a uniﬁed system of competition rules (Young and Wallace 2000: 87).
These goals were gradually achieved over several decades (Table 17.1).
254
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Table 17.1 Key steps of European economic integration
1929
German politician Gustav Stresemann suggested to the League of Nations the idea
of a common European currency.
1951
Treaty of Paris establishing the European Coal and Steel Community: pooling
production of coal and steel between six countries.
1957
Treaty of Rome founding the EEC.
1 July 1968
Customs Union: abolition of customs duties at the internal borders of the EEC and
creation of a uniform system for taxing imports.
1979
Creation of the European Monetary System (EMS) under the impetus of France and
Germany. Currency ﬂuctuations contained within the margins of a ﬁxed (but
periodically adjustable) exchange rate tied to a newly created unit, the European
currency unit (ECU).
1985
Signing of the Single European Act, which formalised the steps towards the
completion of the Single Market and made ‘Economic and Monetary Union’ an
eventual goal of the EU.
1988
Expert committee on Economic and Monetary Union chaired by Jacques Delors
(then President of the European Commission) proposed to set up the EMU in three
phases and advocated the creation of a European Central Bank.
1989
Madrid European Council launched the ﬁrst stage of EMU, i.e. the full liberalisation
of capital movements and the beginning of the gradual convergence of national
economic policies.
1 July 1990
Stage I of EMU: all restrictions on the movement of capital between member states
were abolished.
1991
Maastricht Treaty: set out the timetable for the completion of EMU, deﬁned four
convergence criteria, a new institutional structure and procedures for the
functioning of monetary policy under EMU.
31 December 1992
Deadline for the completion of the Single Market programme agreed in the SEA. 
280 items of European legislation passed between 1986 and 1992 (harmonisation or
mutual recognition of technical and product standards).
1 January 1994
Beginning of Phase II of EMU: member countries began to work towards economic
convergence on the basis of ‘convergence criteria’. Creation of a European
Monetary Institute (EMI) to strengthen the coordination of monetary policies
between national central banks.
1997
Beginning of the coordination of national economic policies through guidelines:
‘Stability and Growth Pact’ (SGP) and non-binding broad economic policy guidelines
(BEPGs).
1 January 1999
Phase III of EMU: irrevocable ﬁxing of the exchange rates of the currencies of the 
11 member states initially participating in EMU.
A single monetary policy under the responsibility of the European Central Bank.
1 January 2002
Introduction of the single currency, the euro, in 12 member states (old national
currencies progressively phased out).
2006
Services Directive (free market for services).
2009
The eurozone has 16 members.
Source: authors’ own.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 284

Stages of European economic integration
The process of economic integration started with a free trade area and customs union that took
effect in 1968. It abolished internal tariffs on all goods traded between the six EEC member
states and established common external customs tariffs for goods imported from third countries
into the EEC. Yet so-called ‘non-tariff trade barriers’ remained between the EEC countries, such
as differences between safety and technical standards or national environmental regulations. In
the early 1980s, a growing consensus emerged between member state governments around the
need for more economic integration and market liberalisation as a solution to the economic
slowdown, recession and high unemployment inherited from the 1970s. The dominant paradigm
that had shaped post-war economic policies in most European countries, based on a Keynesian
conception of the role of the state as market regulator and provider of public and welfare
services, became increasingly challenged. This model began to be gradually replaced by a
neoliberal paradigm based on the premise that less state intervention and greater competition
in markets would result in lower prices, higher economic growth and increased employment.
The rationale for the completion of a Single European Market (SEM) was thus based on the
argument that freedom of movement of people, goods, capital, labour and services within the
EC would lead to a reduction in prices, economies of scale, an increase in efﬁciency, greater
choice for consumers, more innovation, more growth and job creation across the EC (Cecchini
et al. 1988).
The Single European Act of 1986 and the subsequent completion of the ‘Single Market
programme’ in the years that followed were critical turning points in the history of European
integration. The Single Market implemented the principles of the free movement of people,
goods, capital, businesses and services:
•
The principle of free movement of people means that all citizens of EU member states were
given the right to live, study and work anywhere in the EU as well as the possibility of
claiming social beneﬁts in their EU country of residence. Mutual recognition of academic
and professional qualiﬁcations was gradually developed. Additionally a number of European
countries signed the ‘Schengen accords’ in 1985 and 1990, which abolished systematic
passport and border control between those countries and established common visa
requirements for non-EU citizens entering the ‘Schengen zone’.1 The free right of movement
of people and workers has led increasing numbers of citizens to live, study or work outside
their native country, affecting labour and property markets, although ‘the degree to which
EU citizens avail themselves of the opportunity to live and work abroad, and the territorial
consequences this may have’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 146) have been underresearched to date. Sometimes relocation decisions in border regions have been motivated
by differences in ﬁscal systems or even planning systems, e.g. Dutch citizens choosing to live
in Flanders or Germany because of the differences in housing markets (van Ravesteyn and
Evers 2004).
•
The free movement of goods between EU countries was facilitated by the harmonisation of
national product standards across the EU (for about 50 per cent of all traded goods). Where
no harmonisation of standards has taken place, according to the ‘mutual recognition’
principle, products legally manufactured or marketed in one EU country can in principle
be traded freely in other EU countries.
•
Controls on the movement of capital between member states were abolished. Value added
tax (VAT) was harmonised through a minimum European standard rate of 15 per cent
(which can be increased by member state governments).

49222
EU economic and competition policies
255
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 285

•
The transboundary activities of businesses were facilitated through reforms in company and
intellectual property law and the opening up of public procurement procedures to nondomestic companies.
•
Finally, the liberalisation of services began in the late 1980s and was recently accelerated
by the 2006 Services Directive (explained below). In many sectors, service providers now
have the right to establish themselves and offer their services in all EU countries. ‘Network
industries’ (which organise and manage ﬂows of energy, information, goods or people
through a network, for example electricity, telecommunications, transport or postal services)
were forced to open to competition from other European providers.
A study carried out by the European Commission in 1996 estimated that the completion of the
Single Market programme led to the creation of 300,000 to 900,000 additional jobs, increased
EU GDP by 1.1–1.5 per cent between 1987 and 1993, lowered inflation rates, boosted
investments by 1–3 per cent, increased trade volume between member states and boosted
transnational investment within and into the EU (Young and Wallace 2000). Large businesses
and corporations benefited more from the Single Market than small and medium-sized
enterprises, taking better advantage of new market opportunities and economies of scale
(Young and Wallace 2000).
The European Single Market has not been entirely completed to date, since the degree of
transposition and effective application of EU legislation and rules into national law varies highly
between member states. Besides, large parts of the member states’ economies remain ‘highly
regulated and beyond the direct harmonization efforts of EU regulatory legislation or the indirect
liberalisation effects of the Single Market and EU competition policies’ (Hix 2005: 245), for
example national labour legislation. Yet the creation of the Single Market has strongly inﬂuenced
developments in other EU policy ﬁelds (Young and Wallace 2000). For example, the efﬁciency
of the Single Market depended on the corollary development of a tough EU competition policy
to ensure a ‘level playing ﬁeld’ between European ﬁrms (see below). Additionally, the objective
of abolishing ‘non-tariff trade barriers’ provided the rationale for the adoption of harmonised,
EU-wide legislation in environmental policy (see Chapter 21). Finally, the political acceptability
and legitimacy of the Single Market programme had to be accompanied by reforms in EU
regional policy (Chapter 18), since the economic restructuring processes fuelled by the Single
Market adversely affected many regions in the EU.
But the most important follow-up to the Single Market was the move towards an economic
and monetary union. The Economic and Monetary Union (EMU) involves a single currency and
a single monetary policy (for those EU countries that opted in), as well as the coordination of
national macroeconomic policies between all EU member states. Advocates of an economic and
monetary union perceive the transaction costs that stem from the conversion between different
national currencies and exchange rate ﬂuctuations as a non-tariff barrier. It was argued that a
single European currency would boost trade and growth through cheaper international ﬁnancial
transactions, push inflation and interest rates down, create an internationally recognised
currency with high credibility with investors, and trigger a decrease in consumer prices. Yet the
EMU was driven as much by political arguments as by economic ones, as many economists,
central bank ofﬁcials and experts were initially sceptical about the degree of readiness of EU
member states to embark on a monetary union. Many had highlighted the risks inherent in the
adoption of a single currency and the associated loss of exchange rate adjustment mechanisms,
with ‘no easy exit option if things go wrong’ (Tsoukalis 2000: 177).
The timetable and criteria framing the move towards EMU were set in the Treaty of
Maastricht adopted by the European Council in December 1991. Between 1994 and 1999 EU
member states worked towards economic convergence on the basis of four criteria that governed
256
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 286

the transition to the ﬁnal phase of EMU: a low inﬂation rate; the control of government deﬁcit
and government debt within limits; the stability of long-term interest rates; and the stability of
exchange rates. These criteria imposed a strong budgetary discipline on governments and led
to a general decline in public investment relative to GDP in most member states of the EU-15.
The European common currency, the euro, was eventually introduced in January 2002 in
countries that chose to adopt it. In 2009 the eurozone had 16 members. The EMU is an example
of the ‘variable geometry’ Europe referred to in Chapter 11. While all EU member states
coordinate their national economic policies under EMU, not all of them decided to adopt the
euro: Denmark and the United Kingdom have opted out for fear of losing national economic
sovereignty. Sweden is not yet in the euro area but should, according to the Maastricht Treaty,
ultimately adopt the euro. Slovenia, Cyprus and Malta adopted the euro in 2008, Slovakia in
2009. Denmark, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania are part of an exchange rate system as a prerequisite for the eventual adoption of the euro. The other new EU member states have committed
themselves to joining the euro area as soon as they fulﬁl the entry conditions.
EMU creates a ‘new and unique economic policy regime’ in which national governments of
the eurozone no longer have the ability to control monetary policy and the exchange rate (Ardy
et al. 2002). The EMU does not only involve a single currency, but also the coordination of
national economic policies through a ‘Stability and Growth Pact’ and non-binding ‘broad
economic policy guidelines’ to keep national public deﬁcits within limits, although economic and
ﬁscal policies ofﬁcially remain primarily a national responsibility. The Pact effectively imposes
constraints on national ﬁscal autonomy and public expenditure, since the Council can require
ﬁnancial sanctions from member states that have excessive deﬁcits. In 2000, the Lisbon European Council agreed on a package of reforms and proposals known as the ‘Lisbon Agenda’ to
make the EU ‘the most competitive and dynamic knowledge-based economy in the world’ by
2010. The Lisbon Agenda is supported by a commitment from EU member states to modernise the ‘European social model’ and reform labour markets, welfare systems and macroeconomic
policies to support growth. The ‘growth and competitiveness’ objectives embedded in the Lisbon
Agenda have become very prominent in the overall EU policy agenda since the early 2000s, and
have permeated policy reforms in sectoral ﬁelds such as EU Cohesion Policy post-2007 (see
Chapter 18).
In macroeconomic terms EMU can be judged as a relative success if assessed against the
initially pessimistic predictions of its opponents and of many economists. EMU has helped lower
inﬂation and interest rates in member countries and triggered a reduction in public deﬁcits 
to 0.6 per cent of the EU GDP in 2007 (CEC 2008j). The euro has become the second most
important international currency behind the dollar for international transactions and
investments. Yet in the eurozone economic growth rates have remained low, and levels of
unemployment high. The global ﬁnancial crisis which began in 2008 has put to test the ability
of the EMU and the eurozone governance system to restore stability and respond to the crisis.
At the time of writing, it was difﬁcult to predict the full extent of the global ﬁnancial crisis, its
duration and impact on the economy of the EU, and the success (or failure) of EU responses.
Many observers have stressed that the system of ‘economic governance’ that underpins EMU is
rather weak, in particular since the possibility of inter-state ﬁscal transfers remains very limited.
In a period of global economic recession, the euro prevents asymmetric exchange-rate movements in the eurozone, but the Stability Pact that underpins EMU forces national governments
to pursue restrictive ﬁscal policies – which has led the European Commission to relax the
restrictions on national public debt temporarily. Beyond the economic crisis, other global
challenges will affect the future of economic and monetary integration within the EU: ongoing
globalisation; an ageing population and the consequences this has for public expenditure; rising
food and energy costs; and climate change (CEC 2008j).

EU economic and competition policies

## S. 287

The impacts of European economic integration on economic,
social and territorial cohesion
The gradual development of the Single Market and of Economic and Monetary Union has had
signiﬁcant impacts on spatial development trends and on the cohesion of the European territory
as a whole. Existing research on the impacts of the SEM and EMU has primarily focused on
macroeconomic impacts, much less on the long-term impact on the regional economic geography
and spatial organisation of the EU territory.2 The main issue, from a spatial policy point of view,
revolves around which types of regions beneﬁt most from market integration and which regions
lose out in the process. The potential social, environmental and regional impacts of the Single
Market programme in terms of ‘winners and losers’ were often ignored by the early studies and
reports published by the European Commission (Bremm and Ache 1993; Young and Wallace
2000). Such reports were based on the assumption that the growth generated by the Single
Market would be relatively evenly distributed throughout European regions, or that regions that
had hitherto been disadvantaged would proﬁt more from the Single Market than the already
prosperous regions of Europe (Cecchini et al. 1988).
Assessing and measuring the differentiated territorial impacts of the SEM and EMU on
European regions is a difﬁcult task, as recognised by the researchers who have attempted to
investigate the topic. Economists emphasise the trade-inducing effects of the SEM and EMU but
cannot easily predict the regional distribution of such gains (Begg 2003: 172) or assess whether
spatial imbalances in economic performance between regions are diminished or aggravated
(Barry and Begg 2003: 781). Market and monetary integration can have a positive impact on
a given region if ﬁrms seize new opportunities thanks to access to a larger market or to the
exploitation of economies of scale. But economic integration can also have a negative impact if
less competitive ﬁrms struggle in the face of increased competition from outside the region. The
aggregate net effect on a given region of more market integration is thus uncertain and cannot
be determined ex ante (Begg 2003; Molle 2007). In addition, a complex set of economic,
technical, cultural and political factors affect the spatial development of cities and regions in
Europe as outlined in Chapter 4 (Bohan and Muylle 2001). There are many EU sectoral policies
that, combined with the Single Market, have an impact on patterns of regional economic
development in the EU. The aggregate impact of EU policies on the trajectory of individual
regions is thus very complex to assess (ESPON 2006c), as different EU policies pull in different
directions and interact with national policies and other global trends (see Chapter 4). Finally,
and perhaps more importantly from a spatial policy point of view, depending on what deﬁnition
or dimension of ‘cohesion’ one considers, one may come up with very different evaluations of
the impact of the SEM and EMU on the cohesion of the EU. Molle (2007: 261–2), in his review
of the effects of EU policies on cohesion, breaks down ‘cohesion’ into three dimensions –
economic, social and territorial. Net economic growth (i.e. progress in terms of economic
cohesion) may be accompanied by an uneven spatial and social distribution of beneﬁts, leading
to a widening of inter- and intra-regional inequalities, i.e. negative impacts in terms of social
and territorial cohesion.
There is, consequently, little consensus in the academic literature on the impacts of the 
SEM and EMU on different types of regions, and whether economic integration favours or
disadvantages less competitive economies in the long run (Barry and Begg 2003; Begg 2003).
The assumption that the economic growth generated by the SEM and EMU would be evenly
distributed between European regions or would beneﬁt disadvantaged regions the most was,
however, proven wrong by researchers early on. Since the 1980s, theoretical and empirical
evidence from economic geography questioned ‘whether the translation of economic tendencies
into spatial structures and developments is as linear as neoclassical theory assumes’ (Bremm and
258
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 288

Ache 1993: 994). Taking into account variables such as the political regulation, institutional
capacity, innovation potential and social capital of speciﬁc regions, economic geographers and
sociologists (Massey 1984; Scott 1988; Storper 1997; Storper and Walker 1989, among others)
demonstrated that a complex mix of concentration and dispersion, centralisation and decentralisation trends characterised the territorial restructuring of economic activity and spatial
division of labour induced by the SEM:
on the one hand, agglomeration economies are attracting other socio-economic functions
towards dynamic centres, and on the other hand, agglomeration diseconomies are driving
people and ﬁrms to peripheral locations. Both forces are reproducing spatial disparities on
different levels and scales.
(Bremm and Ache 1993: 994)
Consequently the SEM does not hold out ‘the same economic potential beneﬁts to all the
disadvantaged regions’ (Bohan and Muylle 2001: 59).
When the Single Market programme was launched in the late 1980s, a small number of EU
ofﬁcials acknowledged that the distributional impacts of the SEM would not be felt evenly across
regions and might even negatively affect disadvantaged regions. Two ofﬁcial EU reports (Delors
1989; Padoa Schioppa 1987) called for compensatory mechanisms in the form of an EU
regional (or cohesion) policy to ‘ensure that the losers from integration have an incentive to
persevere with it’ (Begg 2003: 162). As a result, the Single Market programme was accompanied
by a signiﬁcant increase in the budget allocated to the Structural and Cohesion funds (see
Chapter 18). If one looks at national convergence between EU member states measured through
GDP per capita, the creation of the Single Market may have supported growth in the so-called
Cohesion countries – Spain, Portugal, Greece and Ireland: in 1993 their GDP per capita was 10
per cent higher than it would have been without the Single Market (Molle 2007). Yet the picture
is less clear cut when one looks at convergence between regional GDP levels. Inequalities in terms
of regional GDP per capita have not signiﬁcantly narrowed (Ardy et al. 2002; see also Chapter
4). The relative position of different kinds of regions has remained quite stable over the last three
decades (ESPON 2006c). Additionally, there is evidence of increased interregional disparities
within many member states of southern and eastern Europe, in particular because their capital
cities and large metropolitan regions have performed better than the rest of the national
economy (ESPON 2006c). Consequently the Single Market has, at best, not counteracted the
divergent economic development trajectories and interregional disparities in the EU; at worse
it may have reinforced the spatial impacts of economic globalisation processes and the new
spatial and functional division of labour arising as a result.
Turning to EMU, again few studies are available on the spatial and territorial impact of
monetary union in terms of regional development and territorial cohesion. Existing research has
mainly focused on the macroeconomic impact of EMU (Ardy et al. 2002; ARL and DATAR
2004; Barry and Begg 2003; Begg 2003; Molle 2007). Most authors conclude that monetary
policies that beneﬁt national territories may work against regional convergence if the beneﬁts
are gained by a small number of already strong regions. Molle (2007) studied the impact of EMU
on the so-called ‘cohesion countries’ of southern and eastern Europe, and concluded that a
combination of positive impacts (e.g. lower inﬂation and interest rates) and negative impacts
(e.g. elimination of devaluation as a tool for adjustment) have affected less-developed regions
and countries. Altogether, however, in the early 1990s the GDP growth rates of the four
Cohesion countries (Greece, Portugal, Spain and Italy) were much higher than in other EU
member states, showing that in the early years of EMU, it was the periphery of the EU that
appeared to be the main beneﬁciary of monetary union (Begg 2003). Whether this will last

EU economic and competition policies
