## S. 289

is an open question: favourable macroeconomic impacts and the advantages of improved
market access have to be set against continuing competitive weaknesses, problems in the
labour market, the productivity gap, and the lack of potential of the less competitive regions.
(Begg 2003: 177)
One ﬁnal impact of EMU has to be highlighted. EU macroeconomic policies (in particular
the Stability and Growth Pact), combined with the competition and liberalisation policies
described in the next section, have put pressure on all EU member states to reduce overall public
expenditure, with signiﬁcant implications for infrastructure development and public service
provision. In relation to spatial planning and urban and regional development, this has
contributed to a decrease in the direct role of the public sector in development processes and
to an increase in public-private partnerships.
In conclusion, while there is evidence that the SEM and EMU have had positive effects in
terms of macroeconomic stability and national economic convergence, the picture becomes much
more shaded if we look at regional convergence and social and territorial cohesion. Macroeconomic convergence between member states has not been accompanied by a visible reduction
in interregional disparities within and between member states. In many regions the European
economic integration process has actually reinforced economic and social restructuring processes
(such as deindustrialisation or brain drain) with long-term adverse social consequences such 
as inter-generational unemployment, poverty, social exclusion and demographic decline. There
is thus a clear tension between the (primarily economic) objective of the Single Market and 
EMU and the objectives of territorial and social cohesion. This tension has been fuelled by
developments in EU competition policy, in particular the EU regulation of ‘state aid’ and the
liberalisation of network industries and public services.
EU competition policy and territorial cohesion: an impossible 
reconciliation?
In this section two elements of EU competition policy are explored in light of their signiﬁcant
implications for spatial development policies and ‘territorial cohesion’: the regulation of ‘state
aid’ and the liberalisation of ‘services of general interest’. EU competition policy encompasses
a set of rules developed from the mid-1980s onwards to ensure that economic competition in
the Single European Market is not distorted. These rules prevent ﬁrms and governments from
carrying out certain practices that, according to classical economics, break the free, fair and
transparent functioning of the market. EU competition policy forms one of the most extensive
cornerstones of European legislation (McGowan 2000). The European Commission plays a
prominent role in rule-making and rule interpretation in this ﬁeld, in particular because it can
supplement EU legislation approved by the Council and the European Parliament with detailed
technical and administrative legislation (the so-called ‘Commission legislation’; see Chapter 11).
EU competition policy has four elements: anti-trust regulations, merger regulations, regulations
on state aid, and the liberalisation of monopolies and state enterprises. The ﬁrst two types of
regulations focus on competition between ﬁrms by prohibiting business practices that are deﬁned
as ‘anti-competitive’ (for example, price ﬁxing and cartel arrangements between companies to
secure dominance over a market). The other two elements of EU competition policy focus on
the regulation of the ﬁnancial aid given by governments, public authorities and state-owned
enterprises (called ‘state aid’) and on the liberalisation of monopolies and state enterprises. State
aid regulations and EU liberalisation policies have had a growing impact on public policies and
public service delivery in EU member states, and have signiﬁcant, although under-researched,
consequences for territorial development policies at the local and regional level.
260
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 290

The impact of EU state aid regulations on the member states’
regional and urban policies
Many EU governments have traditionally steered their national and regional economies through
various means aimed at supporting the development of new industries, attracting foreign
investment, maintaining employment in sectors and regions facing structural decline or reducing
interregional imbalances (McGowan 2000: 128). Molle (2007: 172) distinguishes between two
types of instruments of regional policy used by national and regional public authorities:
•
instruments of ﬁnancial support targeting ﬁrms or speciﬁc economic sectors, which aim at
attracting economic activity to specific areas or to support existing industries facing
difﬁculties;
•
instruments targeting the public sector or individual persons, for example improvements in
transport and educational infrastructure or training measures, which aim at improving the
structural factors underpinning economic development in speciﬁcally targeted regions (see
Chapter 18).
Both types of instruments support regional spatial planning objectives in EU member states. 
The ﬁrst type of instrument is deﬁned by the European Commission as ‘state aid’, and can come
from any public body or agency at local, regional and national level. Such state aid can take
the form of subsidies, investment grants, tax exemptions, loans with preferential interest rates,
acquisition of land and buildings on favourable terms or loan guarantees. The total amount of
state aid spent annually by EU member states has been estimated to amount to 1 per cent of
the total EU GDP (Molle 2007). One-third of this aid is targeted towards the manufacturing
sector. By comparison, the budget for EU Cohesion Policy for the period 2007–13 represents
0.45 per cent of the total EU GDP (see Chapter 18).
According to classical economic theory, state aid distorts competition in the EU Single 
Market by giving an advantage to less efficient firms in a specific sector or region, and
encourages a ‘rent-seeking behaviour of ﬁrms trying to justify continued support by poor
performance’ (Molle 2007: 172). State subsidies to ﬁrms that would otherwise go bankrupt are
perceived to be indirect instruments of national protectionism in the Single European Market
(Wishlade 2003). This is why the EU has sought to control these forms of aid strictly. The
Commission Directorate-General for Competition is in charge of monitoring the application of
EU competition law (including state aid rules) across EU member states. Member states have
an obligation to inform the Commission of any plans to grant state aid and cannot implement
those plans without the Commission’s explicit approval. The Commission can request a member
state to abolish or alter aid schemes deemed unlawful, and prosecute or ﬁne states.
Because EU state aid rules involve a degree of control by the Commission over the spending
activities of national governments, the topic of state aid is very controversial and politicised, with
recurrent conﬂicts between national governments and the European Commission over speciﬁc
aid plans. According to the EC Treaty all state aid is in principle forbidden. However, exceptions
are recognised, particularly in those cases when state aid is thought necessary to promote the
economic development of areas where the standard of living is abnormally low or where there
is serious underemployment. Speciﬁc types of public subsidies are considered acceptable, for
example funding for the provision of public goods (infrastructure and education), social beneﬁts
granted to individuals and aid given in response to natural disasters. Through 30 years of
legislation and case law, the European Commission has developed a comprehensive set of rules
that differentiate between prohibited and authorised state aid (Wishlade 2003: 5–18). A number
of ‘block exemption regulations’ allow member states to implement aid without notifying it to
the Commission, e.g. in the areas of training, employment and support to SMEs.

EU economic and competition policies

## S. 291

One particular type of state aid is of speciﬁc relevance for spatial planning and territorial
development: the so-called ‘regional state aid’ granted by public authorities to large companies
in speciﬁc regions with a view to redressing regional disparities. This type of aid will be referred
to hereafter as domestic regional aid (in other words, national) in order to distinguish it from
the aid provided to the regions by the EU through the Structural and Cohesion Funds. The
average yearly amount of domestic regional aid spent by the 27 EU member states was EUR
9.5 billion over the period 2005–07 (CEC 2009d).
EU regulations on the regional aid given by member states are a crucial element of EU policy
that is little known to planners, but that is of strategic importance for regional and territorial
development policies. In practice it means that national governments are constrained in their
ability to inﬂuence the situation of their ‘problem regions’ through ﬁnancial support to mobile
ﬁrms (Molle 2007: 176). The current EU Guidelines on National Regional Aid for 2007–13 (CEC
2006f) determine the types of regions that are eligible for domestic regional aid, the types of aid
that are authorised, the types of ﬁrms that can get such aid, eligible costs, and maximum levels
of aid for each type of ‘problem region’ (Wishlade 2003, Molle 2007). Table 17.2 shows which
regions can receive which level of domestic regional aid for the period 2007–13 (see also the
‘Regional Aid Map’, CEC 2009e). From an EU perspective, the aim is to encourage member states
to concentrate their domestic regional aid on the most deprived regions and to prevent
governments from outbidding one another in a subsidy race to attract mobile investment and jobs.
EU rules on state aid have been of central importance to the design of regional, urban and
rural development policies in all member states. The impact of EU state aid regulations on such
policies has been felt in three ﬁelds: the overall amount of public expenditure spent on such aid;
the spatial coverage of regional aid; and the form of such aid, i.e. the instruments used by public
authorities in their urban and regional development policies.
Across EU member states there has been a steady decrease in the overall level of domestic
regional aid measured as a percentage of the EU GDP, from just above 1.1 per cent in 1992 to
below 0.6 per cent in 2007 (CEC 2009d). This is due to the combined effect of EU state aid
rules and a global neoliberal push for reductions in public expenditure in EU member states
(Molle 2007; Wishlade 2003), although there have been wide variations between countries. In
2007, for example, France, Germany and Spain spent respectively EUR 2.5, 2.2 and 1.8 billion
on domestic regional aid, while the UK and Poland spent only EUR 0.5 and 0.3 billion (CEC
2009d). In recent years, the European Commission and the Council have continuously
encouraged member states to ‘spend less but better’.

## S. 292

Second, the consequence of the existence of EU state aid rules is that the spatially targeted
regional aid policies of the member states were effectively all brought under the umbrella of a
single map of ‘EU-authorised regional aid’ (Wishlade 2003: 217), under pressures from the
Commission for the harmonisation of the geographical coverage of areas eligible for national
regional aid and those eligible for EU Structural Funds. The ‘Regional Aid Map’ is based on a
model of area designation often radically different from the former national distribution of aid.
In the current programming period 2007–13 the spatial coverage of national (domestic) regional
policies is driven by EU Cohesion Policy objectives, and the possibility of using domestic 
regional aid instruments outside the disadvantaged regions deﬁned in the ‘Regional Aid Map’
is severely constrained. This leads to a ‘policy vacuum’ in those urban and rural areas ‘that are
neither well placed to beneﬁt from policies focused on innovation or other horizontal priorities,
nor sufﬁciently disadvantaged to qualify for regional aid, either at the national or Community
levels’ (Wishlade 2008: 763). Some member states have thus called for a ‘renationalisation’ of
regional policy to free them from the constraints of the ‘Regional Aid Map’, in exchange for
less ﬁnancial support from the EU under EU Cohesion Policy.
Third, EU state aid rules have led member states, in some cases, to modify the type of policy
instruments used in regional and urban policies. A controversial example from Belgium is
summarised in Box 17.1.

EU economic and competition policies
263
Box 17.1 The Ryanair Charleroi case
In 2000, the Walloon regional authority of Belgium and Brussels South Charleroi Airport made
an agreement granting the low-cost airline Ryanair preferential rates for landing charges and
ground handling services, incentives for each new route opened and other contributions
towards promotional activities. In 2004 the European Commission ruled that the ﬁrst two types
of subsidies constituted state aid and would have to be repaid by Ryanair to the regional and
airport authorities. The Commission nonetheless allowed aid given in support of promotional
activities, on the ground that it contributed to the objectives of EU transport policy:
it benefits the regions by ensuring a better return from existing public goods and
facilitating regional economic development, in particular through job-creation and tourism;
it beneﬁts the member state concerned by encouraging spatial planning and better use of
existing airports as opposed to the construction of new infrastructure or extensions; and
it beneﬁts the European Union whose need to develop airport capacity was pointed out
in the White Paper on Transport and which provides part of the ﬁnancing for airports
through its regional policy, the TENs and the EIB.
(CEC 2004d)
In December 2008, the Court of First Instance overturned the European Commission’s
decision and ruled that the deal with Ryanair was part of ‘normal economic activity’ on the
part of Walloon regional authorities. This means that the low-cost airline will not have to pay
back the EUR 4.5 million that it received from the Belgian state. This agreement will have an
impact on other regions as the Commission has launched similar proceedings against
agreements made between Ryanair and other secondary regional airports across Europe.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 293

EU state aid rules have thus contributed to a shift in the paradigm of regional development
policies away from the support of individual companies or sectors towards horizontal objectives
such as improving skills, environmental quality or the research infrastructure in targeted 
regions (Molle 2007). This shift has been advocated by the EU in its own Cohesion Policy (see
Chapter 18). Besides regional policy instruments, urban regeneration schemes and programmes
designed by national or local authorities have also been affected by EU state aid rules.
Instruments such as tax incentives to encourage investors to locate in designated urban areas
or preferential rates on the sale of public land fall under the EU deﬁnition of ‘state aid’ and are
thus subject to restrictions (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004). The increasing complexity of
instruments used for urban regeneration policies (e.g. public-private partnerships) has generated
new challenges in terms of compliance with EU state aid rules. The extent to which the design
and operation of national urban regeneration policies has been affected by EU state aid rules is
illustrated in Box 17.2 with a UK case study. In other EU countries, public banks supported by
guarantees from national governments have traditionally played a key role in providing loans
on favourable conditions for the ﬁnancing of public infrastructure and local urban development
projects, such as the German and Austrian Landesbanken and the French Caisse des Dépôts 
et Consignations. The operations of these banks were affected by EU state aid rules: the
European Commission forced governments to phase out the guarantees offered to these banks
to restore ‘fair competition’ in the banking sector. This has had an impact on the funding sources
available to local authorities for infrastructure investment.
EU competition policy has also affected the conduct of urban development policies in
another way. As part of the development of the Single Market, the EU has, since the early 1990s,
developed extensive rules on public procurement procedures to ensure fair competition between
companies bidding for government and public sector contracts. When a contract for works,
supplies or services above a certain threshold value is commissioned by a public body (currently
EUR 5.15 million net of VAT for works, and EUR 133,000 for supplies and services), it has to
be publicised in the Ofﬁcial Journal of the European Union. The contract has to be allocated
to the lowest bidder. This means that governments are no longer completely free to choose which
company they wish to contract out for public works (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004). Reserving
a proportion of public works for local or regional sub-contractors is thus not allowed (Korthals
Altes 2006), and failure to abide by these rules can delay large-scale development projects. In
the Netherlands, a project involving gravel extraction and enlargement of the Meuse river was
delayed and threatened because it had not been properly put to tender according to EU public
procurement rules (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004).
EU state aid and public procurement rules interfere with the provision of development
incentives by public authorities and complicates ‘the ability of local authorities to become
actively involved in the development process by entering into public-private partnerships,
which goes against the general trend in planning’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 79). Korthals
Altes (2006) explored how different land and urban development practices have been affected
by EU state aid and public procurement rules, focusing in particular on land purchase, assembly,
preparation, development and disposition. Since state aid rules severely constrain the ability of
governments to subsidise urban regeneration schemes, an alternative mechanism available for
public authorities to intervene in land and urban development is through ‘direct development’
or provision of ‘infrastructure in kind’ (e.g. public space and sewerage systems) to facilitate the
development of a site (Korthals Altes 2006). In many European countries, it is common practice
for landowners and local government ‘to enter into an agreement in which the land-owning
developer builds the infrastructure and transfers this “for free” to the local government as a
development obligation’ (Korthals Altes 2006: 253). This practice is referred to as ‘planning
264
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 294

obligations’ in the UK, ‘städtebauliche Verträge’ in Germany or ‘conventions d’aménagement’
in France. Yet these practices are increasingly constrained by EU rules too. When public works
are realised like this, they are not put out to tender as required by European public procurement
rules. In 2001 the Court of Justice of the EC ruled, in relation to an Italian case, that land
developers who had a contract with the local government to provide infrastructure on their own
land ought to be regarded as contractors of public works (Korthals Altes 2006), which meant
that normal public procurement rules should apply. The reasoning embedded in this ruling has
not yet been applied widely to the practice of development obligations in EU member states
(Korthals Altes 2006). If this changes in future, it may severely constrain the long-standing
practice of development obligations that exists in many European countries as a planning tool
to achieve the delivery of infrastructure in kind (Korthals Altes 2006).

EU economic and competition policies
265
Box 17.2 The impact of EU state aid rules on urban
regeneration programmes in the UK in the 
1990s
In 1994 the English national agency for urban regeneration, then called English Partnerships (EP),
put forward a scheme named ‘Partnership Investment Programme’ (PIP) to support the
physical regeneration of deprived urban areas. The programme provided gap funding to enable
developers in designated urban areas to undertake schemes that under normal market
conditions would not have been proﬁtable due to high development costs and low end value.
Gap funding refers to the ‘difference between the development cost and the developed value
of the land and the proﬁt made by the developer’ (Plender 2004: 14). In 1999 the European
Commission investigated the compliance of the scheme with EU state aid rules following
concerns that the measure had been used by investors who were part of a large-scale motor
vehicle manufacturing company. The UK authorities argued that ‘PIP did not constitute state
aid because: the grant given was the minimum necessary to bridge the gap between
development costs and the market value of the regeneration site; it did not confer an unfair
competitive advantage on the developer because any undertaking could apply for assistance
under PIP, and all costs and values were assessed at open market rates; and there was negligible
(if any) intra-Community trade in the development of derelict land and buildings’ (Wishlade
2003: 259).
But the Commission ruled that the gap funding provided by public authorities as part of the
PIP was state aid and breached EU state aid rules because it provided ‘a quantiﬁable ﬁnancial
incentive to a developer to invest in an area or location in which little or no private investment
would otherwise have happened’, and thus favoured certain ﬁrms (Wishlade 2003: 259). The
PIP scheme was forced to stop, although projects already approved were allowed to continue.
The scheme was then signiﬁcantly revised to comply with EU state aid rules. This meant that
where the property developer was not an SME, gap funding was only allowed if the project was
located in an assisted area as deﬁned by the EU ‘Regional Aid Map’, and there were maximum
ceilings to the funding (Wishlade 2003: 259). The PIP case led the UK House of Commons to
ask the European Commission to clarify the state aid rules applying to urban regeneration
funding schemes (UK Parliament 2000, 2002). The Commission’s DG Competition later
produced a guide to clarify how urban regeneration aid provided by member states can be
compatible with EU state aid rules (CEC 2006g).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 295

The liberalisation of ‘services of general interest’: challenges to
public service provision and territorial cohesion in the EU
Public authorities at all levels are heavily involved in providing, regulating, organising or funding
public services such as health care, education, postal services, transport and energy supply
(Rouillon 2008). Such activities are essential for economic, social and territorial cohesion
because they ensure the universal provision of key services at a cost and of a quality that market
forces cannot always provide on a commercial basis, in regions that are remote, or to users that
do not have the necessary ﬁnancial means. These services are therefore crucial to the fulﬁlment
of the objective of ‘territorial cohesion’ deﬁned in Article 16 of the Treaty of Amsterdam as a
situation in which ‘people and ﬁrms are not unduly handicapped by spatial differences in access
to basic services’ (Molle 2007: 283). In addition, from a territorial development perspective,
public services underpin and complement the role of regional aid instruments described in the
previous section, since ‘basic infrastructure, communications and energy supplies are a
prerequisite for regional economic development’ (Wishlade 2003: 263).
Although public services are not a primary concern in the EU Treaties per se, their provision
has been affected by the development of the EU Single Market (in particular the free movement
of goods, the right of establishment and the freedom to provide services) and by EU state aid
rules. The term used by the Commission and the Council to refer to public services is that of
‘services of general interest’ (SGIs). The ‘general interest’ is deﬁned in the 2003 Green Paper on
Services of General Interest as ‘the satisfaction of citizens’ essential needs and the preservation
of public goods where the market fails’ (CEC 2003b: 3). These SGIs cover a wide range of
activities that vary from one country to the other. The European Commission distinguishes
between two types of services of general interest (CEC 2007h). Services of general economic
interest (SGEIs) are activities that are economic in nature, such as postal services, telecommunications, transport services and the supply of electricity and gas, and are consequently
subject to EU Single Market and competition rules. In many EU countries, such SGEIs were
provided by publicly owned organisations with a monopoly situation, such as state-owned
railway, energy, postal and telecommunication companies. From the 1980s onwards, such
services were forced to open to competition in the wake of the Single Market programme – in
other words, to be liberalised. By contrast, non-economic services, such as health, education,
police, justice and statutory social security schemes, are theoretically not subject to EU Single
Market and competition rules.
The argument of pro-liberalisation supporters is that by opening up services of general
economic interest to international competition, consumer choice and the quality of services 
will improve and prices will decrease, making the EU economy more competitive. European air
transport and telecommunications were the ﬁrst sectors to be opened up to competition and were
fully liberalised by the late 1990s, resulting in a visible decrease in consumer prices and in the
rapid expansion of low-cost airlines and regional airports. This has had visible spatial impacts
in a number of peripheral regions, in particular through increased tourism ﬂows, which often
contradict EU environmental and transport policy objectives (see Chapters 19 and 21). In the
railway, electricity and gas sectors, the European Commission has required network operators
to give competitors fair access to their networks. The gradual liberalisation of ﬁnancial services
(banking, securities and insurance) began in the early 2000s. In other sectors, such as postal
services and rail freight transport, liberalisation has been stalled or incomplete due to resistance from some member states. The European Parliament has opposed the liberalisation of 
water supply and waste services (EP 2004). Liberalisation is a contested process, because forced
competition clashes with ‘the special arrangements introduced by all countries to protect certain
activities in the public interest’ (Wishlade 2003: 262). Attacks on national service providers (in
particular in the utilities sector) have generated a lot of resistance in some member states such
266
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 296

as France: the liberalisation of public services was one of the bones of contention leading to the
French ‘no’ vote on the proposed Constitutional Treaty in 2005.
Gradual liberalisation has indeed brought considerable uncertainty to the capacity of nationstates to secure the conditions in which accessibility, continuity and affordability of network
and public service obligations can be assured (Wishlade 2003). The liberalisation of the postal
sector, for instance, has an impact on the quality of postal services and threatens the existence
of post ofﬁces in rural areas in many countries. In the telecommunications sector, studies have
shown that while the net direct impact of liberalisation on economic cohesion is positive, the
impact on social and territorial cohesion is negative because small users and customers in remote
areas often pay higher access charges (Young et al. 2001). Consequently the Council, the
Commission and the Parliament have promoted measures and provisions to protect the ‘general
interest’ and help service providers to meet public service obligations and deliver a basic,
affordable ‘universal service’ in all regions. Because network services (e.g. telecommunications
or energy supply) are most efﬁcient in densely populated and highly developed areas, the price
of network services is highest in less developed, remote areas (Molle 2007: 260), so it is often
not commercially viable for service providers to offer services in such areas. The Commission
has thus allowed companies with a public service duty (such as postal services) to be compensated by the state for costs incurred when assuming their public service responsibility, e.g. the
provision of postal services in remote rural regions.
Additionally the Commission produced a number of communications (CEC 1996b, 2000e,
2003b, 2004e, 2007h) in which it recognised the key role played by services of general interest
in the ‘European model of society’ (Faludi 2007d; Nadin and Stead 2008) and in social and
territorial cohesion. The Commission’s argument is that ‘the existence of a network of services
of general interest is an essential element of social cohesion; conversely, the disappearance of
such services is a telling sign of the desertiﬁcation of a rural area or the degradation of a town’
(Wishlade 2003: 264). This quote clearly illustrates the spatial planning dimension of the debates
on services of general interest. The Presidency Conclusions of the June 1995 Cannes European
Council stated that the introduction of greater competition into various sectors should be
compatible with, among other considerations, ‘balanced town and country planning’ (quoted
in Wishlade 2003: 263–4). It was indeed recognised in the ESDP that market liberalisation can
aggravate spatial and regional disparities (CSD 1999: 14). French policy-makers and planning
ofﬁcials were particularly keen to call for a stronger protection of public service obligations in
EU law and policies. The French national advisory body on spatial planning (Conseil National
d’Aménagement et de Développement du Territoire, or CNADT) expressed the view that
competition policy objectives had been prioritised at the expense of spatial planning and
territorial cohesion objectives. In a joint recommendation of 2002 with the German Beirat für
Raumordnung, the CNADT stated that ‘competition policy should not threaten the provision
of SGEI’ and should be ‘sufﬁciently ﬂexible to enable Member States to intervene to address
regional inequalities or speciﬁc problems resulting from trade liberalisation’ (Wishlade 2003:
265, 268).
In recent years, EU action has shifted toward the liberalisation of commercial services –
deﬁned as any service provided for economic return – which represent 70 per cent of the EU’s
GDP but only 20 per cent of internal EU trade. The Commission adopted the ﬁrst proposal for
a Services Directive in 2004, a text sometimes referred to as the Bolkestein Directive after the
name of the then Commissioner for the Internal Market. The ﬁrst draft of the Services Directive
was the focus of major public controversy in various member states, in particular because of
the principle of the ‘country of origin’. A company active in a country other than its own would
have been able to apply the norms of its country of origin in the country where it was active,
leading to risks of ‘social dumping’ and a race to the bottom in terms of labour and social

EU economic and competition policies

## S. 297

legislation. After several amendments successfully pushed forward by the European Parliament,
including the removal of the ‘country of origin’ principle, the Council ﬁnally adopted the Services
Directive (Council 2006c), which had to be transposed by all EU member states by the end of
2009.
The Services Directive aims to create a real internal market for services by 2010 by supporting
the freedom of establishment for providers in all member states and the freedom of service provision between member states. Examples of services included in the Directive are consultancies,
advertising, tourism and plumbing. A number of services were excluded from the Directive: 
non-economic services of general interest; electronic communications services; transport; health
care; audiovisual services; activities connected with the exercise of ofﬁcial authority; social
services relating to social housing; childcare; and aid for persons in need. Yet many public service
defence organisations fear that social and health services are ultimately going to be affected by
EU competition and liberalisation policies. Because social and health services have undergone
various processes of reform, in particular through outsourcing to the private sector or publicprivate partnerships, an increasing number of activities performed by those services could be
considered by the European Commission as ‘economic in nature’, and thus fall under the scope
of EU state aid and competition rules. This would have a dramatic impact on the public nature
of those services. Since the early 2000s, public service trade unions (for example, the European
Federation of Public Service Unions), left-wing parties and the European Parliament have 
thus called for a ‘public service charter’ or legal framework to be adopted at EU level in order
to guarantee the protection of public social services in the name of social and territorial
cohesion, and to explicitly exclude health, education and social housing services from the scope
of EU competition rules (EP 2004). The Treaty of Lisbon, ratiﬁed in 2009, is accompanied by
a protocol on ‘services of general interest’, which reiterates that national, regional and local
authorities have primary responsibility and discretion for providing, commissioning and
organising SGIs that meet the needs of users and respect the diversity of geographical, economic,
social and cultural situations in the member states.
Conclusion
In this chapter the impacts of EU macroeconomic and competition policies on territorial
development and spatial planning were examined. These policies have signiﬁcant, although
under-researched, impacts. First, the economic geography and urban and regional territorial
development patterns of the EU have been influenced by the SEM and EMU, although
researchers have found it very hard to disentangle these impacts from wider processes of
globalisation and economic and territorial restructuring. There is little evidence to suggest that
the SEM and EMU have markedly helped to reduce territorial disparities in the EU; indeed, they
often conflict with the policy objectives of EU Cohesion Policy (including its territorial
dimension) and EU environmental policy.
Second, the Single Market and EU competition policy have radically altered the structural
context for the action of public authorities in member states, particularly through state aid rules,
public procurement rules and the liberalisation of public services. Although EU competition
policy has not, so far, affected directly the content of planning, it has indirectly inﬂuenced urban
development procedures (Korthals Altes 2006; van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004), by constraining
the kind of policy instruments that may be used by public authorities to change the ownership
and use of land or affect urban development and regeneration. Evers (2005: no page number)
makes the point that the very idea of planning may be a problem from the perspective of EU
competition policy: ‘planning rules often work by distorting the land market in order to achieve
certain social, economic or environmental goals [. . .]. In this sense, planning itself is suspect’.
268
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 298

EU economic and competition policies, which epitomise the primacy of a liberal economic
approach to EU integration, thus often conﬂict with the objectives and ethos of other EU (and
national) policies, in particular regional, environmental and spatial planning policies. The tension
between the objectives of EU competition policy and those of EU Cohesion Policy is of
particular importance for the subject matter of European spatial planning. While the EU has
developed an EU-wide form of regional policy (described in Chapter 18), it has in parallel
increasingly constrained ‘the scope for national policy-makers to control economic transactions
on their territories’ (Young and Wallace 2000: 112) through its macroeconomic and competition
policies. Additionally, EU-led liberalisation processes have modiﬁed and at times threatened the
framework for the delivery of public services that are key in promoting social and territorial
cohesion. For planners, crucial issues consequently arise from EU economic integration and
competition policies: on the one hand they have to deal with the ‘unintended impacts’ of 
the European economic integration project, such as negative environmental externalities
(Chapter 21) or social tensions in regions affected by economic restructuring (Bremm and Ache
1993); on the other hand, the intervention capacity of planners and policy-makers in spatial and
territorial development has increasingly been constrained in the name of EU competition policy.
Further reading
For a general overview of EU economic integration and competition policies, see Cini and
McGowan 1998; Hitiris 2005; McDonald and Dearden 2005; Molle 2006, and Al Agraa 2007.
The only comprehensive study of the inﬂuence of EU state aid rules on regional aid at present
is provided by Wishlade 2003 (the ﬁnal chapter summarises the ﬁndings of the research).
Numerous websites provide more background and explanation, particularly: the European
Commission DG Single Market, http://ec.europa.eu/internal_market/index_en.htm; and websites
dealing with competition policy and state aid, http://ec.europa.eu/competition/, and services of
general interests, http://ec.europa.eu/services_general_interest/index_en.htm.

## S. 299

EU Cohesion Policy and EU
urban policy
Europe sees its future as striking a balance between competition and cooperation, collectively trying
to steer the destiny of the men and women who live in it. Is this easily done? No. Market forces
are powerful. If we left things to their own devices, industry would be concentrated in the north
and leisure pursuits in the south. But these market forces, powerful though they may seem, do not
always pull in the same direction. Man’s endeavour and political aspiration is to try to develop a
balanced territory.
(Jacques Delors, President of the European Commission 
1985–95, quoted in CEC 2008s: 9)
Introduction
There are economic, social and political arguments in favour of a regional policy at the EU level.
Although on the global stage the EU is a prosperous continent, there are signiﬁcant wealth
disparities between its member states and its regions, which have been increased by successive
enlargements and the process of market integration. In 2006, the GDP of the richest region in
the EU (Inner London) was 336 per cent of that of the EU-27 average GDP per capita, while
that of the poorest region, Nord-Est in Romania, was 25 per cent of the EU average (Eurostat
2009). Regional inequalities are far wider in the EU than in the US or Japan. Wealth is mainly
concentrated in the central area of Europe, described in the ESDP as the ‘Pentagon’, delineated
by Hamburg, Milan, Munich, London and Paris (see Chapter 5). Economically, such levels of
disparities threaten the ‘competitiveness’ of the European economy as a whole on the global
stage. Politically, such disparities threaten the political consensus around the European project
of political and economic integration. If there is no commitment from the Union to tackle
regional inequalities, then the ‘poorer’ regions and countries will feel that they do not beneﬁt
from European integration. ‘It is impossible not to display collective concerns for those who may
lose from the process; and it is impossible for those who are perceived to gain not to be willing
to compensate those who lose’ (Cheshire 1995: 32).
Such considerations led to the development and expansion of an EU regional policy supported
by speciﬁc EU ﬁnancial instruments: the Structural and Cohesion Funds. While the overall size
of the budget dedicated to these funds is modest in relation to the combined GDP of all EU
member states, it represents one-third of the overall EU budget and is thus the second largest
budget line after the Common Agricultural Policy. EU regional policy (now commonly called
Cohesion Policy) is the main ﬁeld of EU policy that targets ﬁnancial resources geographically
on the basis of criteria deﬁned at the European level. It consequently has a direct spatial impact
on the development of European regions and on the structure of the EU territory. It is also
perhaps the most well known of EU policies for those involved in local and regional territorial
Chapter 18
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 300

development, since it provides a sizeable source of funding for EU cities and regions – the 
‘pot of gold at the end of the rainbow’ (Williams 1996: 114). In this chapter the development
of a regional policy at EU level and the current instruments of this policy – the Structural and
Cohesion Funds – are presented. Specific attention is paid to the impact of EU-funded
programmes in urban areas, and to the linkages between Structural Fund programmes and
spatial planning in the member states (in particular regional planning). The chapter ends with
a critical discussion of the achievements and territorial impact of EU regional policy to date.
The development of a regional policy at EU level
Regional policy has been carried out by national governments on efﬁciency and equity grounds,
in order to address the spatial and geographical inequalities in economic prosperity, activity and
social welfare that exist between regions, in other words to tackle the uneven spatial
development caused by market forces in capitalist economies. Governments experimented with
various forms of regional policies after the Second World War (e.g. in the UK, Italy, Germany
and France) to encourage a better distribution of economic activities across the national
territory and the development of less prosperous regions. Such policies relied on the use of
geographically targeted subsidies in support of productive investment and job creation, tax
breaks, the ﬁnancing of infrastructure, the decentralisation of public institutions and support
for the creation of new poles of industrial activity. Approaches to regional policies varied
strongly among member states depending on their political structures and historical traditions
(e.g. federal or centralised state) (Balchin et al. 1999). In the immediate post-war years, regional
policies in Europe were thus mostly a national matter. The Treaty of Rome of 1957 made no
speciﬁc provision for a European ‘regional policy’. In the favourable post-war economic climate
of the 1950s, there was a general belief that market integration would help weaker regions to
develop. However, two EU funding instruments that are still in place today were created in 1958
and 1962 (although they were not initially designed as tools to tackle regional spatial
inequalities). These are the European Social Fund (ESF) and the European Agricultural Guidance
and Guarantee Fund (EAGGF).
In 1973, a report published by the European Commission on The Regional Problems in the
Enlarged Community (CEC 1973; known as the ‘Thompson Report’) took stock of the ﬁrst
waves of industrial restructuring and the slowdown of post-war economic growth, and
concluded that although the objective of continuous expansion set in the Treaty had been
achieved, its balanced and harmonious nature had not. The report’s authors suggested that a
Community regional policy should be set up on ‘moral, environmental and economic grounds’
(CEC 1973: 4) to reduce concentration in congested regions, reduce disparities across the
Community, ensure the development of less developed and declining regions and reduce
structural imbalances. At the same time the accession of Denmark, Ireland and the United
Kingdom to the EC generated pressures for the creation of a compensation mechanism for
countries that were net contributors to the EC budget and did not beneﬁt so much from
European agricultural subsidies.
As a result the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) was established in 1975 for
a three-year period with a budget of EUR 1.3 billion, aiming at correcting the main regional
imbalances within the Community. Between 1975 and 1984, 95 per cent of the budget allocated
to the ERDF was distributed to the member states in accordance with a national quota system
through which the fund reimbursed part of the costs of investment projects carried out by
national governments. At the end of the 1970s, a very small section of the ERDF was earmarked
to support Community-wide pilot projects, later followed by larger ‘Integrated Mediterranean
Programmes’ in Greece, Italy and France. In 1984 the Commission proposed to increase the

EU Cohesion Policy and EU urban policy

## S. 301

‘non-quota’ part of the ERDF and to develop a Community-wide (instead of a national)
procedure for the designation of eligible areas. Two priorities (called ‘objectives’) were identiﬁed
to focus the use of the Structural Funds: the support to ‘regions lagging behind’ and the
conversion of declining industrial areas. A new requirement for a ‘regional development plan’
was also introduced as a prerequisite for a region to receive ERDF support, moving away from
an approach so far dominated by ad hoc individual projects decided by national governments.
During the 1975–85 period, the total budget for the ERDF increased from ECU 258 million to
2,290 million (from 4.8 to 7.5 per cent of the EU budget).
In 1986 EU regional policy was given a formal legal foundation through the introduction of
a new objective in the Single European Act, that of economic and social cohesion, which aims
to reduce disparities between the levels of development of the various regions and the
backwardness of the least favoured regions or islands, including rural areas. This was followed
in 1988 by a major reform of the functioning of the Structural Funds. Two main factors led to
this change. First, EU ofﬁcials and national experts began to acknowledge that market integration through the Single Market programme (described in Chapter 17) would not ‘naturally’
bring about economic convergence between European regions, and would actually increase the
pressures for restructuring in less-favoured regions. Various reports recommended the creation
of an EU ‘structural policy’ to support adjustments in structurally weak regions and countries
(Cecchini et al. 1988; Delors 1989; Padoa-Schioppa 1987). Second, the accession to the EU 
of Greece in 1981 and of Spain and Portugal in 1986 signiﬁcantly increased the extent of
interregional inequalities in the EU. The southern enlargements and the completion of the Single
Market programme thus strengthened the political will of the member states and of the Commission to take action at the EU level to reduce disparities between the various regions.
In 1988 four key principles were introduced to guide the allocation and management of the
Funds and they still apply. They are: concentration on the poorest and most backward regions;
multi-annual programming; additionality; and the involvement of regional and local partners.
The 1988 reform additionally established priority development objectives and common criteria
for designating eligible regions at European level in order to concentrate resources on the less
prosperous areas. Five priority objectives were deﬁned (see Table 18.2), some of which were
geographically targeted on the basis of speciﬁc indicators, while others applied to the whole
Community territory. The resources allocated to the Structural Funds for the period 1989–93
were doubled. The year 1992 marked another signiﬁcant turning point for EU Cohesion Policy:
the Treaty of Maastricht reinforced the importance given to ‘economic and social cohesion’ 
as a primary goal of the EU by establishing a new ﬁnancial instrument, the Cohesion Fund. 
This was intended to support transport infrastructure and environmental improvements in the
poorest member states of the EU (Greece, Portugal, Spain and Ireland). In 1992 the European
Council again doubled the budget allocated to Cohesion Policy: by 1999, the Structural Funds
accounted for nearly 33 per cent of the total EU budget.
With the prospect of enlargement to the east, EU Cohesion Policy had to undergo a new series
of reforms in 1999 and 2006. Regional disparities in the EU-15 in the mid-1990s were
signiﬁcant: the GDP per head in NUTS II regions varied from 37 to 196 per cent of the EU
average. More than 19 per cent of the population of the EU-15 lived in areas with a per capita
GDP of less than 75 per cent of the Community average, primarily in southern Europe but also
eastern Germany and parts of the UK (CEC 1996c). Various studies showed that in an enlarged
EU of 25 or 27, interregional disparities would signiﬁcantly increase, since the accession
countries had an average GDP of about 30 per cent of the EU-15 average. A huge increase in
the budget allocated to Cohesion Policy to deal with enlargement was politically unacceptable
to most of the EU-15 member states. In the run-up to the 2004 enlargement the political debates
on the reform of EU cohesion and agricultural policies comprised three main points of view:
272
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 302

the ‘East’ wants the highest priority, because it suffers obviously from the most acute
problems; the ‘South’ wishes to keep enough funding to tackle the problems not yet solved;
and the ‘North and the West’, which must foot the bill, refuse to pay for both.
(Drevet 2007: 152)
In March 1999, the Agenda 2000 reform package was agreed, ﬁxing a ceiling for the Union’s
budget and allocating EUR 213 billion to Structural and Cohesion policies for 2000–06. The
ten new countries that entered the EU in 2004 began to receive Structural and Cohesion funding,
which replaced the pre-accession aid granted by the EU prior to enlargement (see Chapter 12).
For the period 2007–13, EU Cohesion Policy was reformed again. The six-year negotiations
leading to the reform were complex and politically difﬁcult (Bachtler and Wishlade 2004, 2005).
There were considerable tensions between the three viewpoints. The qualitative focus of EU
Cohesion Policy was shifted towards the prioritisation of measures supporting growth, jobs and
competitiveness, as explained in the next section. Table 18.1 provides an overview of the
historical development of EU regional policy (see also CEC 2008s).
The instruments of EU Cohesion Policy (2007–13)
EU Cohesion Policy is funded in so-called ‘programming periods’ of six years, at the end of which
reforms take place. The current programming period runs from 2007 to 2013. The total budget
earmarked for Cohesion Policy for this six-year period is EUR 347 billion, i.e. 35.7 per cent of
the total EU budget at 2007 prices. EU Cohesion Policy is divided into programmes, called

## S. 303

‘objectives’, which are supported by three types of funds (see Molle 2007: Chapter 7 for a detailed
overview of the structure of EU Cohesion Policy, as well as the website of DG REGIO).
Funds and objectives
EU Cohesion Policy is supported by the Structural Funds and the Cohesion Fund. The Structural
Funds comprise the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) and the European Social
Fund (ESF). Prior to 2007 there were two additional Structural Funds that no longer exist: the
Financial Instrument for Fisheries Guidance (now replaced with the European Fisheries policy,
which is no longer considered a Structural Fund); and the European Agricultural Guidance 
and Guarantee Fund (replaced by the European Agricultural Guarantee Fund and the European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development under EU agricultural policy; see Chapter 20).
In 1992, the Maastricht Treaty created a new fund, the Cohesion Fund, to support speciﬁcally
274
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Box 18.1 The funds of EU Cohesion Policy (2007–13)
European Regional Development Fund (ERDF)
Created in 1975, the ERDF is intended to help to redress the main regional imbalances in the
Community through participation in the development and structural adjustment of regions whose
development is lagging behind and in the conversion of declining industrial regions. The ERDF
ﬁnances: infrastructures of research and innovation, telecommunications, environment, energy
and transport; direct aid to investments in companies (SMEs); and ﬁnancial instruments (capital
risk funds, local development funds, etc.) to support regional and local development. The rate
of ERDF co-ﬁnancing varies depending on the region (usually between 50 and 75 per cent).
European Social Fund (ESF)
Created in 1957, the ESF focuses on four key areas in line with the European Employment
Strategy: increasing adaptability of workers and enterprises; enhancing access to employment
and participation in the labour market; reinforcing social inclusion by combating discrimination and facilitating access to the labour market for disadvantaged people; and promoting
partnership for reform in the ﬁelds of employment and inclusion. The ESF does not ﬁnance
infrastructure, and its use is not spatially targeted (i.e. it covers the entire territory of all EU
member states).
Cohesion Fund
The Cohesion Fund was created in 1992 by the Maastricht Treaty to help the poorest member
states of the then EU-15 to overcome their infrastructural handicaps. The Cohesion Fund only
applies to member states that have a gross national product (GNP) of less than 90 per cent
of the EU average: this includes all the new member states that joined in 2004 and 2007, as
well as Greece, Portugal and (on a transitional basis until 2013) Spain. The Fund supports
interventions that contribute to the trans-European transport networks (Chapter 19) and the
improvement of the environment (Chapter 21), for example, projects improving the supply of
drinking water, waste water treatment and renewable energy. The Fund can contribute up to
85 per cent of total public expenditure.

## S. 304

the poorer countries of the EU. Box 18.1 gives an overview of the three funds available under
EU Cohesion Policy for the current (2007–13) programming period. In addition to these three
funds, the Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance (IPA) is used to support the development and
preparation of candidate countries or potential candidate countries (see Chapter 12), namely
Croatia, Turkey, the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia and potential candidate countries
in the Western Balkans.
In the 2000–06 period there were nine different objectives, Community Initiatives and programmes, which were simpliﬁed into three for the period 2007–13 (see Table 18.2). For the
2007–13 programming period, EU Cohesion Policy is organised in three ‘objectives’: the ‘convergence’ objective, the ‘regional competitiveness and employment’ objective, and the ‘European
Territorial Cooperation’ objective. EU Cohesion Policy is characterised by a very high concentration of resources on the poorest member states and regions, which are targeted under the
convergence objective, while at the same time still maintaining some degree of funding for 
all EU regions under the competitiveness and European Territorial Cooperation objectives. 
To determine the eligibility of particular regions, Europe is divided into various groups of regions
corresponding to the NUTS classiﬁcation explained in Chapter 3. The Commission bases

## S. 307

its decision on statistical data. The convergence objective includes the poorest regions of the 
EU-27: regions at level 2 of the NUTS classiﬁcation whose GDP per inhabitant is less than 
75 per cent of the Community average. These regions appear in dark grey on Figure 18.1. 
The competitiveness and employment objective covers all regions that are not covered by the
convergence objective or by transitional assistance (in light grey on Figure 18.1 – NUTS 1 or
NUTS 2 regions depending on the member states). Transitional assistance systems have been
devised to ‘phase out’ EU assistance for regions that beneﬁted from much ﬁnancial assistance
before the enlargement, and to ‘phase in’ NUTS 2 regions that were covered by the former
Objective 1 but whose GDP exceeds 75 per cent of the average GDP of the former EU-15. The
‘European Territorial Cooperation’ objective includes the former INTERREG programmes and
has three strands (see Chapter 16): cross-border cooperation, transnational cooperation and
interregional cooperation. The areas covered by this objective are visualised in Figures 16.1 
and 16.2. Each of the three objectives is ﬁnanced by one or more of the three funds referred to
above, as shown in Table 18.3.
Design and management of EU Cohesion Policy
Since the 1988 reform of the Structural Funds, four overarching principles have guided the
Commission’s approach to Cohesion Policy. First, the largest part of the ﬁnancial assistance is
concentrated on assisting the least prosperous EU regions with the lowest GDP per head. Second,
ﬁnancial assistance is subject to programming, which means it is channelled on the basis of multiannual strategic regional development programmes, rather than ad hoc and uncoordinated
national projects. Third, the design and implementation of EU Cohesion Policy is characterised
by a partnership between the EU institutions, national governments, sub-national public authorities and other stakeholders (business associations, trade unions, voluntary sector, environmental
associations), as explained in Box 18.2. Finally, according to the additionality principle, EU
funds should not replace, but complement, national expenditure (projects are never funded 100
per cent by EU funds but also have to make use of national and regional co-funding sources).
Box 18.2 summarises the process of shaping and implementing Cohesion Policy during one
programming period, from design to evaluation. It is important to note the prominent role of
national and regional authorities in the process, in particular in the selection of projects that
receive EU funding. The structures for implementing the programmes ﬁnanced by EU funds are
complex, and vary from one member state to another, so no attempt is made to describe them
in detail here (see Molle 2007: Chapter 9, as well as the ‘In Your Country’ section of the DG
REGIO website).
The types of projects that are funded under the three objectives vary. The primary purpose
of EU ﬁnancial support is not to ‘redistribute money’ but to ‘strengthen the economic base in
recipient regions, including human capital formation’ (CEC 1996c: 10), i.e. giving regions the
‘structural assets’ they need to speed up their economic development and improve the living
standards of their inhabitants:

EU Cohesion Policy and EU urban policy
279
Box 18.2 How is Cohesion Policy shaped and implemented?
Setting the strategic framework for EU Cohesion Policy for a 6-year
programming period
Prior to the beginning of a programming period, political bargaining and negotiations take place
between EU member states to agree on the overall EU budget (on the basis of proposals by
the Commission) and the share dedicated to EU Cohesion Policy and each of its priority
objectives. The Council and European Parliament are the ﬁnal decision-making bodies for the
EU budget. This is a very political process: ‘decisions about the spatial targeting of EU regional
policy have, in effect, become decisions about the distribution of Community funding between
the member states’ (Wishlade 2003: 145–6).
In preparation for the 2007–13 programming period, the Commission proposed Community strategic guidelines on cohesion, which contain the overarching principles and priorities of
Cohesion Policy that frame the national and regional programmes for a six-year period. These
guidelines have to be agreed by the Council and the Parliament, as well as the overarching rules
for the use of the Structural and Cohesion Funds. The criteria that deﬁne the eligibility of areas
for particular objectives and funds are agreed through a general regulation; additional regulations are made for each of the three funds.
Preparing national and regional programmes
Since 2007, each EU member state, in partnership with the Commission, regional and local
authorities and socio-economic partners, prepares a National Strategic Reference Framework
(NSRF), which deﬁnes the strategy chosen by the member state (in line with the Strategic
Guidelines) and proposes a list of Operational Programmes (OPs). OPs are national or, more
often, regional development strategies with a deﬁned and coherent set of thematic priorities
that will guide the selection of projects that will receive Structural or Cohesion Fund support.
NSRFs and OPs have to be submitted to the Commission for approval (the Commission can
request changes or modiﬁcations). For the 2007–13 period, around 450 OPs were adopted by
the European Commission.
Implementing the operational programmes
Member states and regions are responsible for the implementation of the OPs. To do so,
member states have to set up one ‘managing authority’ (MA) for each OP. The MA can be a
national, regional or local public authority or public/private body (this is left for the member
states to decide). In Spain, for example, the managing authority is the Ministry of Economics;
in France, the préfectures de régions; in England the regional development agencies; in Germany
the Länder administration, and in Poland the councils of the voivodeship. In addition, member
states have to appoint certiﬁcation and auditing bodies that verify statements of expenditure
and oversee the efﬁcient running of the management and monitoring systems.
Managing authorities are responsible for selecting the projects that will receive funding 
(on the basis of calls for project applications), ensuring that products and services are delivered
by project partners, monitoring progress, sending ﬁnancial and strategic reports to the
Commission at regular intervals. Under the ‘European Territorial Cooperation’ objective, in
which programmes cut across national boundaries, the managing authorities are assisted by 
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 309

First, it means that programmes have to be constructed as economic development (or
regeneration) packages. Second, the emphasis is on public investment in the broad sense of
the term. Investment can be in infrastructure, in training and in boosting the productive
potential of businesses in the region. Third, subsidies to individual ﬁrms or income support
for the poor or the unemployed are not acceptable use of the Funds. Fourth, programmes
need to ﬁt into and reinforce other economic development initiatives.
(Begg 1998: 629)
Over the years the operations funded by Cohesion Policy have given more emphasis to 
‘softer’ investments in human resources and research and innovation as shown in Figure 18.2,
while the relative share of funding dedicated to infrastructural investments has decreased.
According to the Community Strategic Guidelines on Cohesion 2007–2013 (Council 2006a),
the new generation of programmes have to earmark a certain proportion of the resources for
investments linked to the Lisbon Strategy for Growth and Jobs, such as research and innovation,
infrastructures of European importance, industrial competitiveness, renewable energies, energy
efﬁciency, eco-innovations, entrepreneurship and human resources. In convergence regions, these
priorities must receive 60 per cent of the total available funding; in other regions, 75 per cent.
This has been assessed critically by some, who argue that in the wake of neoliberal policymaking in Europe, the primary conceptualisation of the term cohesion ‘has become detached
from its redistributive origins and incorporated in a discourse of competitiveness and growth’
(Rumford 2000: 183; see also Chorianopoulos and Iosiﬁdes 2006).
The changes of focus of EU regional policy have to be understood within the context of
developments in regional economics, economic geography and regional studies that show 
that ‘geography matters’ (CEC 2008s: 2). These developments have fed into new policy thinking ‘in particular on the factors that inﬂuence economic development, and on how these can be
affected through policy’ (ESPON 2005a: 116). These theoretical debates are too complex 
to be explored within the scope of this book (for an overview of the debates on ‘territorial
innovation’ see Camagni 2002; Lagendijk 2006; Moulaert and Sekia 2003). The notions of
280
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Box 18.2 continued
cross-border or transnational ‘technical secretariats’ and monitoring and steering committees
that bring together representatives from the member states involved.
The organisations in charge of individual projects funded with support from the Structural
and Cohesion Funds have to implement their projects and claim EU funding within a deﬁned
time frame. At the beginning of each programming period, annual ﬁnancial allocations are 
made to each programme. MAs have to spend the allocated funds (i.e. provide proof of
payment) by the end of the second year following the year in which they are allocated (the
so-called ‘N+2 rule’). Failure to do so means that money will be returned to the budget of the
European Commission. The role of the Commission in the implementation process is to pay
the certiﬁed expenditure to the member states, monitor that the funds are used properly
alongside the managing authorities and verify the control systems. All programmes are subject
to an independent evaluation prior to their start (ex ante), at mid-term, and after their completion (ex post) (see Molle 2007: 195 for a more detailed analysis of the ‘policy cycle’ in EU
Cohesion Policy).
Source: authors’ own, based on DG REGIO website.

## S. 310

‘regional competitiveness’ – to be achieved through the valorisation of the region’s endogenous
(growth) potential and resources – and ‘territorial capital’ lie behind much of current policy
thinking, both within the European Commission and among member states. During the 2000–06
programming period ‘strategies have become more articulated and deﬁned on the basis of an
underlying development paradigm based on the stimulation of competitiveness through the 
full exploitation of endogenous potentials’ (ESPON 2005a: 4). The notion of regional competitiveness covers very diverse aspects: productive competitiveness, ﬁscal competitiveness, wage 
cost competitiveness, competitiveness in quality, etc. (ESPON 2006c: 34). The key ‘drivers’ of
regional competitiveness can be classiﬁed into three main categories (ESPON 2006c: 31): ‘traditional’ factors (economic diversity, specialisation, human capital, connectivity/accessibility);
‘modern’ factors (synergy, proximity, ‘milieux’, quality of life, creativity and innovation); and
‘governance’-related factors (strategy and vision capability, mobilisation and inclusion capability, implementation capability). Policy interventions can focus on any combination of these
three types. Over the past two decades both national and EU regional policy instruments have
increasingly favoured the last two aspects, encouraging the creation of a suitable framework for
interaction and innovation through networks and clusters, ‘in which companies, organizations
and public agencies are able to explore joint solutions to problems shared in common’ by
exchanging information and interactively learning from one another (Diez 2001: 908).
EU Cohesion Policy and spatial planning in the member 
states
In this section the linkages between EU Cohesion Policy and spatial planning are discussed from
two perspectives: ﬁrst, the relationship between the evolution of EU regional policy and that of
the ‘European spatial planning’ agenda described in Chapter 14; second, the linkages between
EU regional policy programmes, regional governance and national regional policy in the
member states.
The instruments of EU regional policy were never part of a real ‘European spatial planning
strategy’ that would proactively seek to exert direct inﬂuence on the spatial location of economic
activities across EU countries. In that regard it is telling that the transformations of EU regional
policy described in the first part of this chapter happened relatively separately from the
development of a ‘European spatial planning’ agenda described in Chapter 14. In the 1990s,

## S. 311

as the ESDP was being prepared by experts from the member states, for a brief period of time
supporters of the ESDP agenda for a more balanced territorial development of the EU proposed
the idea that the ESDP principles could underpin the future allocation of Structural and
Cohesion Funds. A number of member states, however, strongly opposed this idea, in particular
net beneﬁciary countries from southern Europe that were concerned that this might lead to a
diversion of Structural Fund spending away from them (Faludi and Waterhout 2002). However,
even if ‘the actual extent of the innovation introduced by the ESDP as regards the Structural
Funds was limited, the thinking behind this document has left some legacy, leading to the concept
of “territorial cohesion”’ (Polverari et al. 2005: v) that made its way into the Second and Third
Reports on Economic and Social Cohesion (CEC 2004a, 2007b). The ESDP concepts additionally inﬂuenced the thematic priorities of the programmes that had an explicit spatial planning
dimension, INTERREG IIC (1994–99) and IIIB (2000–06) (discussed in Chapters 16 and 22).
However, the integration of these former Community Initiatives into the territorial cooperation
objective after 2007 has diluted the ‘spatial planning’ dimension of these programmes in favour
of the objective of growth, jobs and competitiveness.
If one looks at the inﬂuence of EU Cohesion Policy and the Structural Fund programmes on
regional governance and regional planning in the member states, both EU evaluations and
academic research have shown that beyond socio-economic impacts, there are noticeable
indirect, ‘qualitative’ impacts from such programmes. The Third Cohesion Report acknowledges
that beyond the net impact of EU Cohesion Policy on GDP or employment, ‘its added value
arises from other aspects, like the contribution made to regional development by factors such
as strategic planning, integrated development policies, partnership, evaluation and the exchange
of experience, know-how and good practice between regions’ (CEC 2004a: 138). After the 1988
reform, the Commission has promoted a more comprehensive approach to regional development
supported by the Structural Funds, mainly through requirements for integrated strategic regional
planning, emphasis on partnership between the Commission, national and regional/local
government and other regional actors, and integration of economic development, environmental
and social objectives. Structural Funds programming has thus had an important impact on
innovation in governance and policy-making at the local and regional level. Academic
researchers have investigated these impacts in speciﬁc national and regional contexts in more
depth (see, for example, Boland 2001; Evans 2002 and Roberts 2003 on the UK; and Gualini
2001, 2004a on Italy).
The discipline associated with the EU Structural Fund programming cycle, evaluation and
partnerships, and the thematic emphasis of EU-funded programmes have inﬂuenced working
practices as well as understandings of regional policy in the member states, leading to
institutional learning (Leonardi 2005; Paraskevopoulos et al. 2006). The ESPON 2.2.1 report
(2005a) identified five qualitative effects at the micro level: the deployment of economic
development resources; the promotion of a strategic dimension in policy-making; the introduction of new types of intervention; enhanced partnership; and the promotion of new learning
and innovation dynamics. The ESPON 2.2.1 study concludes that by favouring ‘bottom-up’
approaches to policy-making and delivery, Structural Fund programmes:
contribute to the strengthening and empowerment of the regional and local levels of
governance. This also involves facilitating local-level dialogue through the implementation
of horizontal partnership and by the creation of sub-national and often local organisations
with speciﬁc functions associated with Structural Fund implementation. Thus governance
measures have important indirect spatial impacts as they provide fertile ground for local
and regional spatial development action.
(ESPON 2005a: 21)
282
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 312

In countries that did not have a regional tier of government with established regional economic development or spatial planning strategies, EU Cohesion Policy has led to the setting up
of new forms of regional administration and programming, as in Ireland and Poland (see Chapter
23). Regional programming, however, does not necessarily mean that ‘regional planning’ is
established, i.e. the development of an integrated spatial strategy at the regional level. In Ireland,
for example, the regional units that were created for the purpose of managing the Structural
Funds have not become the focus of regional spatial planning. Besides, EU regional policy and
the regional policies carried out by the member states themselves do not necessarily coincide.
Often ‘the logic of Structural Funds is a logic of quick realization of projects and quick spending . . . The link with a global strategy of regional development, if it exists, is often lost’ (Magnier
2004: 111). The relationship between European regional policy and national regional policies
was investigated in the ESPON 2.2.1 project (2005a: Chapter 7), on the basis of criteria such
as: the extent to which national regional policies are based on cross-sectoral, multi-annual
programmes and partnership-based strategies; policy content (whether emphasis is placed 
on the objectives of equity or efﬁciency); and spatial targeting (the area designation process and
the coincidence between Structural Funds and regional aid maps). The report concludes that in
a majority of countries, the two policies can be considered as ‘separated’ (Austria, Belgium,
Denmark, France, Western Germany, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and the UK); in others 
the two policies are coincident or overlapping (East Germany, Greece, Ireland, the Italian
Mezzogiorno, Portugal and Spain); and in a further cluster of countries the two sets of policies
do not coincide but are closely interrelated either due to the geographical scope, or due to the
overall approach and strategies implemented (Italian Centre-North, Finland and Sweden)
(ESPON 2005a: 128–9). There is a clear core-periphery picture, with separated policies in the
core of Europe and more related policies in the peripheral parts of the EU-15, which are the areas
receiving the highest amount of EU funding (in terms of Structural Fund share of a region’s GDP).
So ‘the amount of Structural Fund money allocated to a country matters as regards the leverage
effects the Structural Funds have on national regional policies’ (ESPON 2005a: 130).
Several EU member states have increasingly attempted to improve the coherence and coordination of their national regional policies with EU regional policy in various ways, by aligning
programming periods, area eligibility, administrative management structures, and so on. In
France, for example, cooperation between the central state and the regional tier of government
takes the shape of a system of contracts that channel public investment towards various jointly
negotiated strategic priorities (Contrat de Projet Etat-Région, or CPER). Between 1982 and 2006,
four generations of CPER were implemented. A report commissioned by the French government
on the linkage between EU Structural Funds and regional policies in France (Magnier 2004)
showed that until the 2000–06 programming period the coordination between both processes
was questionable: the CPER and Operational Programmes were characterised by different
terminologies and thematic priorities; the geographical zoning and eligibility areas of the 
CPER and Objective 2 did not match; and implementation was characterised by the complexity
of procedures and the multiplicity of funding streams.
EU Cohesion Policy and cities: the added-value of urban 
actions
In line with the principle of subsidiarity, the EU has no speciﬁc competence for urban affairs
and there are no direct provisions for urban policy in the Treaties, as it is generally argued that
urban problems are best solved at the local or regional level. Yet many EU sectoral policies affect
urban areas, in particular the Structural and Cohesion Fund programmes, transport policy and
