## S. 313

environmental legislation. Some of the Commission’s Directorates-General started to develop
an ‘urban dimension’ in their activities and agenda (Atkinson 2001: 392), for example in the
ﬁeld of urban environmental management, urban energy policies, research on urban themes, etc.
(see CEC 2007j for an overview). In the 1990s DG Environment developed a speciﬁc urban
sustainability agenda described in Chapter 21. From 1989 onwards, experimental programmes
speciﬁcally targeting urban areas to promote integrated urban regeneration were set up under
the framework of EU regional policy: the Urban Pilot Projects, followed by the Community
Initiative URBAN I and II, which are presented brieﬂy in this section. DG REGIO also sought
to improve the knowledge base about European cities by launching an Urban Audit in 1997 to
assess the state of individual EU cities (CEC 2000f, 2000g) (explained in Chapter 6).
In 2005 the European Commission established an Interservice Group on Urban Development,
chaired by DG REGIO, in which other Commission DGs are represented. The Interservice
Group pursues three objectives: to promote an integrated approach to sustainable urban
development when programming and implementing the assistance of the Structural Funds; to
identify the initiatives under the various Community policies aiming to support the sustainable
development of urban areas and to ensure the necessary cooperation between the Commission
services in this respect; to ensure partnership between the Commission, the European Parliament
(EP), the Committee of the Regions (CoR) and associations of towns and urban areas, and to
establish regular dialogue allowing exchanges of views and the gathering of observations from
these bodies on the consideration given to the urban dimension (CEC 2007i).
The emergence of an EU ‘urban agenda’ in the 1990s (see Table 18.4) generated some
attention among researchers (Atkinson 2001, 2002; Dellatsima 2003; Frank 2005; Tofarides
2003; Williams 1996). There was speculation as to whether the emerging EU ‘urban agenda’
was going to pave the way for a formalisation of EU competences in urban planning and policies.
It became clear, however, that most member states were strongly opposed to that possibility,
while supporting the idea of exchanges of knowledge and European cooperation on urban policy
issues. In the 2000s the European ministers responsible for urban policy began to meet regularly
through informal meetings (Lille in 2000, Rotterdam in 2004, Bristol in 2005, Leipzig in 2007).
In 2005, at the Bristol meeting under the UK presidency, the ministers signed an agreement
known as the ‘Bristol Accord’, which introduced the concept of ‘sustainable communities’
(mostly used in the UK since). The document (ODPM 2006) identiﬁes Europe-wide principles
and characteristics of ‘sustainable communities’ and emphasises the importance of sharing good
practice between member states. The Leipzig Charter on Sustainable European Cities, a document agreed in 2007 that sets out common principles and strategies for urban development
policies (EU Ministers 2007b), builds upon the recommendations set out in the Territorial
Agenda of the European Union (see Chapter 14). The Charter seeks to support the establishment
of a balanced European polycentric urban structure and promotes the concept of integrated
urban development strategies for sustainable development. It recommends making greater use
of ‘holistic strategies and coordinated action by all persons and institutions involved in the urban
development process which reach beyond the boundaries of individual cities’, improving the
coordination between sectoral policy areas, ensuring ‘that those working to deliver these
policies at all levels acquire the generic and cross-occupational skills and knowledge needed to
develop cities as sustainable communities’ (EU Ministers 2007b: 2). In the Charter the Ministers
committed themselves to initiate a political debate in their country on the integration of the
principles and strategies of the Leipzig Charter into national, regional and local development
policies (EU Ministers 2007b: 1).
The various urban initiatives and exchanges of good practices encouraged by the EU (and
more recently by the member states) have led to the production and dissemination of an ‘acquis

## S. 315

urbain’, a term introduced at the Rotterdam ministerial meeting and used by the Commission
(CEC 2009i). The term is deﬁned by Atkinson and Rossignolo (2009) as encompassing a body
of ‘urban knowledge’, a repertoire of ‘good practices’ and an associated common European
‘urban methodology’ of intervention that does not, however, constitute a body of accumulated
law as the acquis communautaire does. This acquis urbain has been acknowledged by the
Commission (CEC 2007i, j).
The ‘urban focus’ in the mainstream Structural and Cohesion Funds 
programmes (1989–2006)
Urban areas (both cities and smaller towns) have, since the beginning of the operation of the
Structural Funds, been included in the areas eligible for EU funding, presently under the two
objectives of convergence and competitiveness, formerly under Objective 1 and 2 (CEC 2006h).
An overview of the actions funded by the Structural and Cohesion Funds in urban areas in 
the previous programming periods is provided in CEC 1997b and ESPON 2006h. Three recent
studies have investigated the ‘urban dimension’ of the 2000–06 Structural Funds programmes
(EPRC/Nordregio 2002a, 2002b; ESPON 2006h; Polverari et al. 2005). In the mainstream
programmes, different types of approaches to urban areas have been identiﬁed: some approaches
favour direct support to urban areas facing particular problems through infrastructural projects
of urban renewal involving physical construction of buildings and transport, complemented by
training and employment initiatives; others favour economic development strategies focusing 
on ‘growth poles’; ﬁnally, some involve urban-rural strategies focused on supporting small towns
through social, cultural and economic infrastructure (Polverari et al. 2005).
The ESPON study (2006h) identiﬁed four types of spatial effects of Structural Fund activities
in urban areas: effects on morphology (e.g. in terms of concentration of functions, reduction of
spatial disparities, re-utilisation of larger sites); effects on functional or economic specialisation
(for example, strengthening of existing proﬁle or division of labour between various places,
development of new proﬁle/niche); effects on connectivity, accessibility and transport (for
example, improvement of links, removal of bottlenecks, development of hub functions); 
and strengthening of urban cooperation with other urban or rural areas. Structural Funds 
additionally ‘have had a strong inﬂuence on administrative procedures’ (ESPON 2006h: 83). 
The governance impact arising from Structural Fund interventions in urban areas can be twofold: partnerships leading to new cooperation networks, organisational innovation and
institutional learning; and citizens’ participation and identity-building for the inhabitants
(ESPON 2006h: 83).
The Structural Funds may have strong effects on the economic and social context of the urban
areas, on changes in the quality of the urban environment and the accessibility of a place. Yet
the fact that Structural Funds are spent in cities does not mean that programmes systematically
support the development of comprehensive, integrated urban development strategies. The analysis
undertaken by the ESPON 2.2.3 project showed that there were actually few occasions where
Structural Fund programmes within Objective 1 and Objective 2 regions included an explicitly
urban dimension or speciﬁcally addressed particular urban problems through an integrated urban
development approach. The ESPON study concluded that ‘given that the Structural Funds
programmes have been drafted as regional economic development programmes, urban issues 
are not among the core issues to be dealt with in mainstream programmes’ (ESPON 2006h: 105).
In the 1990s DG REGIO decided to set up speciﬁc programmes dedicated to integrated urban
development and regeneration, in order to encourage European cities to apply new approaches
to tackle their urban problems.
286

## S. 316

EU programmes in support of area-based actions for urban
regeneration (1989–2006)
Between 1988 and 2006 a small part of the budget earmarked for the ERDF funded speciﬁc
‘urban’ programmes: the Urban Pilot Projects (1989–93), the Community Initiative URBAN I
(1994–99) and URBAN II (2000–06). The budget of these initiatives (Table 18.5) was very small
in comparison with the amount of funding dedicated to the mainstream programmes.
The Urban Pilot Projects (UPPs), launched in 1989, aimed to support innovative ways of
tackling problems of urban disadvantage and unemployment in cities (CEC 1997b). They 
were funded under ‘Article 10’ of the ERDF regulations, which allowed the Commission to use
up to 1 per cent of the annual ERDF budget to provide support for studies or innovative pilot
schemes concerning regional development at Community level. During the 1990s, the European
Commission made good use of this opportunity to fund innovative demonstration projects 
and studies on spatial development (CEC 1991, 1994a). Between 1990 and 1999, 59 Urban 
Pilot Projects were implemented in two waves. They involved urban regeneration interventions
such as the economic and physical revitalisation of historical city centres, and some of the funded
UPPs were sustained beyond the funding period and have acted as catalysts for further regeneration in the targeted areas. The experiment of the UPPs gave rise to a programme that lasted
for two programming periods, i.e. 12 years: the Community Initiative URBAN. The Community
Initiative was launched in 1994 with two objectives: to promote the design and implementation
of innovative, area-based strategies of physical, economic and social regeneration in small and
medium-sized towns and in the deprived neighbourhoods of major conurbations; and to reinforce and share knowledge and experience on regeneration and sustainable urban development
in the European Union.
Under URBAN I (1994 and 1999) a total of 118 programmes were designated, in which the
ERDF co-funded a series of inter-linked projects on social, economic and environmental issues
associating physical improvements works (e.g. rehabilitation of public spaces or renovation of

## S. 317

buildings to accommodate economic and social activities, but no housing), economic and labour
market actions (support for SMEs, training) and actions to combat social exclusion (provision
of cooperative services). The targeted deprived urban neighbourhoods (chosen on the basis of
speciﬁc indicators of deprivation) had, on average, 27,000 people. Under URBAN II (2000–06),
70 programmes were funded across the EU, covering areas containing some 2.2 million people.
The areas covered were considerably smaller than other types of Structural Funds programme
regions. Almost half of programmes were located in inner-city areas, and a third were in areas
at the periphery of urban agglomerations, often difﬁcult to access, on large social housing estates
or abandoned industrial sites (ESPON 2006h: 105). For an overview of the URBAN I and II
programmes and examples of funded projects, see CEC 2003c.
The URBAN programmes promoted an integrated area-based approach to urban problems,
which was innovative in many member states where urban issues had traditionally been tackled
on a sectoral basis, with a separation between physical, social, economic, environmental, transport and security measures. The URBAN programmes additionally emphasised a communityled partnership approach: programmes were delivered not by government agencies alone, but
by partnerships between local governments, businesses, public agencies, the community and
voluntary sectors, who jointly deﬁned challenges, strategy, priorities and resource allocation 
to projects. In 2002 a speciﬁc programme was set up to identify good practice and promote
exchanges of experience between the various URBAN programmes across Europe: the European
programme for sustainable urban development, or URBACT.
What is the ‘added value’ speciﬁcally brought by these EU programmes as opposed to local,
regional or national urban regeneration programmes? The EU funding channelled to URBAN
programme areas was often quite signiﬁcant in relation to the size and population of the area
concerned. Funded operations delivered visible improvements in the physical environment, in
employment opportunities, in services to businesses and individuals in target areas. However,
the cost of managing the programmes was very high and for small-scale organisations, the
technical and administrative expertise required was time and resource consuming (EKOS 2000).
Beyond these tangible impacts, ofﬁcial evaluations and academic analyses of the URBAN
programmes converge in concluding that the most signiﬁcant positive impact, or ‘added value’,
of URBAN is the nature of the new urban regeneration approaches which were encouraged in
the programme areas. The required integration of physical, social and economic interventions
in the framework of a coherent strategy led to new forms of partnerships and cooperation
between and within organisations at the local level, including the participatory involvement of
various sections of the local communities (ESPON 2006h). There is evidence that the lessons
from the small-scale URBAN programmes have been applied across other cities and have fed
into the deﬁnition of new types of local, regional and national urban policies (Frank et al. 2006).
A number of scholars have investigated the qualitative impacts of the URBAN programmes in
terms of change in local governance networks and institutional arrangements as well as urban
policy paradigms in speciﬁc cities (among others Carpenter 2006; Dukes 2008; Frank et al. 2006;
Güntner and Halpern 2006; Halpern 2005; Marshall 2005; Murtagh and McKay 2003;
Tofarides 2003).
What most of these studies have shown is that the extent to which URBAN programmes have
led to changes in local governance arrangements and urban regeneration approaches very much
depends on the pre-existing traditions of ‘urban policies’ and area-based interventions in the
countries and cities under scrutiny (Carpenter 2006). The URBAN programmes were inﬂuenced
by French, British and Dutch approaches to urban regeneration, so it should not be surprising
that according to existing research, in cities located in those countries, URBAN programmes
have not triggered a signiﬁcant overhaul in urban policy approaches. An evaluation of the impact
of URBAN I in the UK argued that ‘it is not possible to conclude that the URBAN process was
288
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 318

more efﬁcient than other processes’ and it is not clear ‘if URBAN has facilitated a more strategic
view of local regeneration’, although it has fostered greater community involvement (EKOS
2000). By contrast, in countries that did not have a tradition of area-based urban interventions
integrating social, economic and physical measures, or in countries with a hierarchical and
centralist tradition of urban planning where the position of local authorities was relatively weak,
there is evidence that the URBAN approach has triggered more signiﬁcant changes in urban
policies at local level, and in certain cases, at national level. In southern European countries, 
in particular, there is evidence that the URBAN programmes have promoted new forms of
integrated urban regeneration that were unknown in such contexts (for Greece and Italy, see
Chorianopoulos 2002, 2006; Frank et al. 2006; Koutalakis 2003).
The URBAN Community Initiative was not pursued beyond 2006. Political reasons partly
explain this: some countries invoked the subsidiarity principle to question the legitimacy, and
added value, of EU action in urban policy matters. In the current programming period 2007–13,
the ‘urban focus’ and lessons from URBAN were integrated into the three mainstream objectives
of EU Cohesion Policy (explained in the next section). The Leipzig Charter on Sustainable European Cities signed by the member states in 2007 also integrated lessons from URBAN, stressing
that special attention should be paid to deprived neighbourhoods within cities through integrated
urban regeneration strategies.
The ‘urban focus’ in the programming period 2007–13
The ‘urban agenda’ of the EU is not likely to evolve into the formalisation of an EU competence
in urban policy issues, but rather through attention being paid to the ‘urban dimension’ of
Community policies and specific initiatives in particular Commission DGs, and through
increased support to exchanges of good practice and networking on urban regeneration and
sustainable urban development. The Interservice Group on Urban Development set up by the
Commission in 2005 has produced a two-part guide (CEC 2007i, 2007j) that lists all EU policies
and initiatives with direct and indirect implications for the sustainable development of urban
areas for the period 2007–13. The guide – essential reading for planners – provides a useful
overview of the current EU policies that have an impact on urban areas.
The Community Strategic Guidelines on Cohesion Policy for 2007–13 explicitly refer to
urban areas, listing three main types of actions: to promote cities as motors of regional
development; to promote internal cohesion inside the urban areas and improve the situation of
crisis districts; and to promote a more balanced development between the economically strongest
cities and the rest of the urban network. The need to encourage integrated plans and strategies
for sustainable urban development was stressed, on the basis of lessons learned from URBAN.
A preliminary assessment of the ‘urban dimension’ of approved Operational Programmes in the
member states (CEC 2008k) estimates that a total of EUR 21.1 billion will be spent on urban
development actions between 2007 and 2013 (6.1 per cent of the total EU Cohesion Policy
budget). Additionally, in partnership with the European Investment Bank and other ﬁnancial
institutions three new initiatives have been set up by the Commission, establishing urban
development funds and new financial engineering instruments: JESSICA (Joint European
Support for Sustainable Investment in City Areas); JASPERS (Joint Assistance in Supporting
Projects in European Regions); and JEREMIE (Joint European Resources for Micro to medium
Enterprises). These programmes will provide additional loan resources for business formation
and development in cities and regions.
While the URBAN Community Initiative was discontinued, the URBACT programme was
maintained and expanded, as a European framework programme and resource centre to
support exchange of good practice and the dissemination of knowledge on all issues related

EU Cohesion Policy and EU urban policy

## S. 319

to sustainable urban development. It offers an online database of documents, available at http://
urbact.eu.
What have the Structural and Cohesion Funds achieved?
Over the past 15 years EU regional policy has become one of the most evaluated policies in
Europe, due to a combination of the large amounts of expenditure at stake, the contested
decisions on its use and pressures for more accountability in spending (Bachtler and Wren 2006).
Evaluation focuses on three main questions: are EU structural interventions appropriate,
effective in reaching their objectives, and economically efﬁcient (Molle 2007)? Since 1988 the
evaluation of programmes is compulsory at three points in the programming cycle: ex ante, midterm and ex post. Evaluation methods vary across programmes and across member states, but
include impact assessment, econometric modelling of outcomes and the use of quantiﬁed
performance indicators that link ﬁnancial inputs to outputs, outcomes and results (for example,
in terms of job creation) (Bachtler and Wren 2006; Murtagh and McKay 2003).
Every three years the Commission has to submit a report to the European Parliament and
the Council on the progress made towards achieving economic and social cohesion. Since 1996
the European Commission has therefore published regular Reports on Social and Economic
Cohesion (CEC 1996c, 2001h, 2004a, 2007b). They summarise the estimated quantitative
impacts of the Structural and Cohesion Fund programmes. The primary indicator used by the
Commission to monitor the economic development of regions and member states is GDP per
capita, i.e. the economic value of all goods and services produced within a given area over a
given period of time. GDP per capita is often expressed in purchasing power parities, which
makes different prices comparable across member states. In Commission reports and statistics,
GDP is usually expressed as a percentage of the EU average (the average being 100 per cent).
‘Convergence’ is deﬁned by the Commission as the reduction of the gap between the countries,
or regions, with the highest and the lowest average GDP. The main ﬁndings from the Cohesion
reports are summarised below. It has been argued that data presented in the Cohesion reports
are used ‘to support both supporters and dissenters of the view that regional policies can help
poorer regions catch up with wealthier ones’ (Peters 2003: 334).
EU Cohesion Policy and national convergence between member states
The main ﬁnding that emerges from both Commission-led and independent research is that EU
regional policy interventions seem to have supported convergence between member states at
national level, although the extent of this contribution is debated. Disparities in GDP per head
between the member states of the EU-15 narrowed signiﬁcantly in the 1980s and 1990s,
primarily because of the catching up of the then four Cohesion countries: Spain, Portugal, Greece
and Ireland. Their rate of GDP growth was higher than that in the rest of the EU through 
the period; between 1983 and 1995 their income per head increased from 66 to 74 per cent 
of the Community average (CEC 1996c: 5). During the 1995–2005 period this trend continued:
the GDP per capita of Greece moved from 74 to 88 per cent of the EU-27 average, that of Spain
from 91 to 102 per cent, and that of Ireland from 102 to 145 per cent. In Portugal, however,
the GDP per capita was only 74 per cent of the EU-27 average in 2005 (CEC 2007b).
In those countries the added value of EU intervention was assessed by the Commission as
having been high (Mairate 2006). In other words, EU funds have allowed things to happen that
would not have happened otherwise (Molle 2007). The Structural and Cohesion Funds brought
about a general improvement in basic infrastructure and the economic base: in Greece, the
number of towns with a waste water treatment system doubled between 1989 and 1999, while
290
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 320

14,000 km of major roads were built or upgraded in Spain (CEC 1996c). Between 2000 and
2006, transfers from the Funds amounted, on average, to an estimated 60 per cent or so of total
public capital expenditure in Portugal, 48 per cent in Greece and 24 per cent in Spain (CEC
2007b: 143). Evaluating quantitatively the extent of the impact of EU funds on the GDP growth
of these four Cohesion countries is a complex matter, however, and various studies come up
with different results. The First Cohesion Report (CEC 1996c) estimated that EU support
increased growth by 0.5 per cent per year over the 1989–93 period (corresponding to the
creation or maintenance of over 500,000 jobs) and by at least 0.5 per cent over the 1994–99
period in those four countries. Ireland went through a spectacular take-off from the mid-1980s,
and it is claimed that the Structural Fund support has played a key role in supporting rapid
growth, although some economists have argued that other factors, such as high levels of foreign
direct investment from outside the EU played a key role in this. In the 1980s the impact of the
Structural Funds was less clear in Greece and Italy.
The Third Cohesion Report concluded that as a result of EU structural interventions, ‘GDP
in real terms in 1999 was some 2.2 per cent higher in Greece than it otherwise would have been,
while in Spain, the ﬁgure was 1.4 per cent, in Ireland, 2.8 per cent and in Portugal, 4.7 per cent’
(CEC 2004a: 148). Differences in performances are explained by ‘the high degree of openness
of the Irish and Portuguese economies in relation to Greece and Spain, especially the latter
because of the larger size of its internal market’ (CEC 2004a: 148). In the new German Länder,
the effect of Structural Fund intervention on GDP is estimated to have been signiﬁcant (raising
the level by around 4 per cent in 1999) (CEC 2004a). In the new member states of central and
eastern Europe, from 1995 to 2005 the growth of GDP per head exceeded the average rate in
the EU-27 (except in Cyprus), although growth rates varied markedly between countries. The
three Baltic States, Poland, Hungary and Slovakia have had growth rates about double the EU
average; Bulgaria and Romania saw their economies contract in the second half of the 1990s,
but since 2000 they have both grown by on average 6 per cent a year (CEC 2007b: 4). Generally
countries with a very low GDP per head are catching up faster. However, the Fourth Cohesion
Report (CEC 2007b) estimated that assuming a continuation of those growth rates, it would
take more than 15 years before Poland and, most especially, Bulgaria and Romania would reach
a GDP per head of 75 per cent of the EU-27 average.
A trade-off between national and regional convergence?
While a signiﬁcant process of catching-up between member states happened in the 1980s and
early 1990s, the First Cohesion Report had already noted that income disparities between regions
remained largely unchanged at the European scale: between 1983 and 1995, in the 25 richer
regions income per head rose marginally from 140 per cent of the Union average to 142 per cent
while it increased from 53 to 55 per cent in the 25 poorest regions. In the 1983–95 period,
regional income disparities within member states actually widened in all member states except
the Netherlands (CEC 1996c). In the Cohesion countries, the growth that took place concentrated
in the capital cities and a few dynamic regions, reinforcing existing regional disparities. In
Portugal, GDP per capita in Lisbon increased from 81 per cent to 96 per cent of the EU average
between 1983 and 1993, while in the neighbouring Alentejo it fell from 48 to 42 per cent. In the
Third Cohesion Report, the Commission consequently acknowledged that there seems to be a
‘trade-off’ between national convergence and regional convergence (CEC 2004a: 149).
In the Fourth Cohesion Report (CEC 2007b) the Commission argued that for the ﬁrst time
there seems to be evidence of convergence occurring at the regional level too: over the period
1995–2004, disparities in GDP per head between the top 20 per cent of NUTS 2 regions in the
EU-27 and the bottom 20 per cent decreased. However, the situation of different regions varies

## S. 321

enormously; for example, Romania and Poland recorded a decline in employment in the
majority of regions. Moreover, if one looks at interregional disparities within member states,
convergence is not occurring: regional inequalities have remained stable over the period
1995–2004 in Germany, France, Greece, Spain, Italy, Belgium and Finland, while in other
countries they have increased. ‘Much of the regional convergence which has occurred at EU level
is a consequence of convergence of low income countries rather than of low income regions as
such’ (CEC 2007b: 10).
This apparent conﬂict between national and regional convergence has policy implications 
for EU Cohesion Policy as such, but also for planners in the member states and for the types of
regional policies that national governments should pursue. In terms of ‘territorial cohesion’, 
the Fourth Cohesion Report suggested that in the EU-27 there is less territorial concentration
of GDP in the traditional core of Europe, due to the emergence of new growth centres such as
Dublin, Madrid, Helsinki, Stockholm, Warsaw, Prague, Bratislava and Budapest. Yet there is
evidence of an increasing concentration of economic activity and GDP in capital city regions
and large metropolitan areas within the new member states (CEC 2003c, 2007b). The ESPON
Project 2.2.1, which analysed the interrelationship between the Structural Funds and the spatial
concepts of territorial cohesion and polycentricity, concluded that for structural policies to
support polycentric development they need to address the issue of polycentricity versus
monocentricity in a much more explicit fashion than is the case in today’s Structural Fund
programmes (ESPON 2005a).
To summarise, while the national GDP levels of EU member states have been gradually
converging (expressed in percentage of the EU average), there is no clear-cut evidence of longterm regional convergence within the EU, and there is actually evidence that in many countries
interregional disparities have markedly increased. Additionally, although EU structural and
Cohesion fund interventions have contributed to the ‘catching up’ of Cohesion countries, the
extent of that contribution in quantitative terms is debated.
The lack of overwhelming evidence on the effectiveness of EU Cohesion Policy can be
explained in different ways: ﬁrst, problems with the evaluation methodologies themselves;
second, the limited size of the EU budget and the role played by the member states through their
own regional policies; third, the inﬂuence of other forces, trends and EU policies on regional
economic development trajectories; and ﬁnally, the very rationale underpinning the EU Cohesion
Policy, which has been questioned by some economists. There have been extensive criticisms
about the methodologies and macroeconomic models adopted by the Commission to evaluate
programmes. These controversies will not be discussed here. (For an overview of the debates
see the special issue of Regional Studies introduced by Bachtler and Wren 2006; Molle 2007:
Ch. 10; and CEC 2003c: 138.)
The supporters of interregional transfers as a means to reduce disparities in the EU argue that
the size of the budget allocated to the Structural and Cohesion Funds in relation to the extent
of regional disparities in the EU is too limited, in absolute terms, to achieve real progress towards
of cohesion, especially after the eastern enlargement. The existence of an EU regional policy
should not obscure the fact that the various domestic policies of the member states are, in terms
of resources, still the primary instruments for achieving economic, social and territorial cohesion:
‘solidarity in the Union begins at home’ (CEC 1996c: 6). National public spending in the EU
member states accounts on average for 40 to 60 per cent of national GDP, while the share of
the EU budget dedicated to EU Cohesion Policy is about 0.4 per cent of the EU total GDP. As
detailed in Chapter 17, however, member states are constrained in the exercise of their regional
and urban policies by EU competition policy and the ‘state aid rules’.
Furthermore, many commentators argue that the economic forces of globalisation and
European integration that have caused increasing disparities between regions (discussed in
292
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 322

Chapters 4 and 17) are simply too strong for regional policies to counteract them (Peters 2003:
334). Additionally, a number of other EU sectoral policies, aside from EU Cohesion Policy itself,
tend to go against the objective of cohesion (at least in the short and medium term); examples
are the Common Agricultural Policy (Chapter 20), market integration and liberalisation policy
(Chapter 17). Other policies favour the territorial cohesion of the EU as a whole but not that
of national and regional territories (e.g. the Trans-European Transport Networks; see Chapter
19). Molle (2007: Chapter 11) analysed the impact of various EU policies on economic, social
and territorial cohesion. He concludes that in the past, the total sum of EU policies seems to
have leaned towards a negative impact on cohesion, but at present the balance seems to be on
‘neutral’ (2007: 274).
Finally, a number of economists have radically questioned the overall efﬁcacy and economic
efﬁciency of EU Cohesion Policy (Boldrin and Canova 2001; Ederveen et al. 2002; Puga 2002),
and have even argued that Structural Fund expenditure may actually be hindering, rather than
helping, the cohesion process (Braunerhjelm et al. 2000; Midelfart-Knarvik and Overman 2002).
For those authors, the solution to regional inequalities lies with market mechanisms and private
investment ﬂows. EU regional policy is deemed to be counterproductive and should therefore
be stopped, because EU Structural and Cohesion Funds trigger a culture of ‘aid dependency’,
which stiﬂes the search for innovation and competitiveness. Some economists have argued that
EU Cohesion Policy may even negatively affect overall economic growth in the EU ‘if it drains
resources from those wealthy and innovative regions that are the main engines of economic
growth’ (Maystadt 2000: 4). Research ﬁndings in economic geography and regional economics
tend to show that efﬁciency gains tend to accrue from the existence of economic agglomeration
(Martin 1999). If one accepts such an argument, ‘Europe as a whole may be better off (macro-)
economically if it keeps concentrating infrastructure investments in the Blue Banana core of
Europe’ (Peters 2003: 32). This, however, contradicts the Cohesion Policy objective of a more
equalised distribution of economic activity across European space.
It seems clear that there is a conﬂict between the objective of economic competitiveness and
that of social and territorial cohesion, which translates into necessary trade-offs between
growth and equity in EU policy interventions. Some critically question whether there is a genuine
political will to put the objective of cohesion ﬁrst on the European agenda and advocate a much
stronger role for EU regional policy. Others criticise the very effectiveness and efﬁciency of EU
regional policy as a way to reduce uneven development levels in Europe, and would support its
abolition, or at least it re-nationalisation (meaning that the EU member states themselves 
would again be in charge of deciding on where and how to allocate regional policy funding)
(HM Treasury et al. 2003). Whatever perspective is chosen, it is perhaps important to conclude
this discussion by going back to the political and symbolic importance of EU Cohesion Policy
in the process of EU integration. The transfer of EU funds to poorer member states can help
‘buying off the poor countries’ concession for further moves toward integration that rich member
states decide to pursue’ (Gemitis and Kafkalas 1993: 1). Cohesion Policy also plays a key role
in increasing the visibility of the EU to European citizens, as all projects receiving support from
EU funds have to be signposted with the European ﬂag. In that sense the Structural Funds ‘have
rather less to do with real economic cohesion or the eradication of regional disparities than to
do with a modest budgetary redistribution to facilitate the continued development and smooth
running of the EU’ (Allen 2000: 264).
Conclusion
In the global context EU regional policy is a unique form of interregional redistribution 
between 27 countries. It has become a key element in support of the EU political and economic

EU Cohesion Policy and EU urban policy

## S. 323

integration project. Yet as discussed in the previous section, the overall effectiveness and efﬁ-
ciency of EU Cohesion Policy is controversial. There is no consensus among experts as to
whether existing EU Cohesion Policy has been effective in reducing growing regional disparities
in the EU. In spite of these uncertainties, that some form of EU regional policy will continue in
the future is not really in question, ‘if only because ensuring cohesion is a Treaty obligation 
and the Structural Funds are the designated instrument in the Treaty’, yet ‘the philosophy, scale,
spread and instruments to be used are open to the debate’ (Begg 2003: 173). In September 
2007 the Commission launched a public consultation on the challenges and future of Cohesion
Policy post-2013, within the larger context of negotiations over the future EU budget. An
independent report to assess the effectiveness of Cohesion Policy to date and make proposals
for reform post-2013 was published in 2009 (Barca 2009). The report makes a strong case 
for the Union to continue allocating a large share of its budget ‘to the provision of European
public goods through a place-based development strategy aimed at both core economic and
social objectives’ (Barca 2009: vii), However, in light of the EU political dynamics to date, 
the overall structure of Cohesion Policy is unlikely to change signiﬁcantly; it will continue to
concentrate on the poorest regions, with a limited increase in the overall budget. A number of
key issues were identiﬁed in the Regions 2020 report (which analyses the future challenges 
for EU regions; see CEC 2008l) and the report by Barca (2009) as central to the debate on future
EU regional policy: economic globalisation; climate change; demographic change and ageing;
and rising energy prices – to which one could add the global ﬁnancial crisis.
Further reading
The primary and indispensable source of information on EU regional policy is the website of
the Directorate-General for Regional Policy of the European Commission: http://ec.europa.eu/
regional_policy/index_en.htm. The best and most up-to-date academic textbook on EU Cohesion
Policy is Molle 2007. It evaluates Cohesion Policy alongside other EU policy inﬂuences on social,
economic and territorial cohesion. For a good overview of the changes in EU Cohesion Policy
after the eastern enlargement, and the implications of those changes for various EU member
states, see Baun and Marek 2008. On the ‘urban focus’ in EU Cohesion Policy see the thematic
webpage of DG REGIO, http://ec.europa.eu/regional_policy/themes/urban/index_en.htm and the
website of the URBACT II programme, http://urbact.eu/. For a source of ‘urban practices’ see
the European Urban Knowledge Network (EUKN) website, www.eukn.org/.

Sectoral policies and their territorial effects

## S. 324

EU transport policy
Trans European Networks (TENs) are in fact a means by which the original objectives of the Treaty
of Rome, and the European integration sought by its promoters, may be brought close. Just as the
Roman roads were essential to bind the Roman Empire together, and the railways were essential
to the continental expansion of the USA in the 19th century, so TENs will bind Europe together
with a mixture of old and new transport modes and communication technologies.
(Williams 1996: 168)
There has always been an obvious conﬂict between the main goal of transport policy, which consists
in satisfying the needs for the mobility of goods and persons at the least cost for the community,
and that of a regional policy, which consists in seeking balanced territorial development.
(Robert et al. 2001: 143)
Introduction
The argument for the intervention of the EU in transport and infrastructure policy is rooted in
the three main goals of the EU: competitiveness, cohesion and sustainability. Transport is
considered to be key to economic development, and is also a fundamental aspect of social and
territorial cohesion. The transport sector is a major contributor to air pollution and greenhouse
gas emissions, and the environmental impacts of large-scale transport infrastructure on natural
habitats and landscapes are signiﬁcant. While transport policy was mentioned in the Treaty of
Rome in 1957, it was only in the late 1980s that the EU began to actively develop a comprehensive strategy for transport policy, including infrastructure development. This chapter outlines
the development of, and the rationale for, an EU transport policy. Particular attention is paid
to the Trans-European Transport Networks (TEN-T), a programme of infrastructure development that has signiﬁcant territorial development and spatial planning implications at the
European and regional level. The contribution of EU transport policy (in particular of the TEN-
T) to economic, social and territorial cohesion, and to the achievement of more environmentally
sustainable forms of mobility in Europe, is discussed in the ﬁnal two sections of the chapter.
The development of EU transport policy
Unlike environmental or regional policy (discussed in Chapters 18 and 21), the ﬁeld of transport
was mentioned from the outset in the Treaty of Rome (1957) as an area of Community competence. In spite of this Treaty basis, until the mid-1980s transport policy was considered by
many as one of the least successful domains of the European integration project (Stevens 2004).
In spite of several proposals for transport policy by the Commission and the European
Parliament in the 1960s and 1970s, the Council of Ministers approved only a relatively small

## S. 325

body of rules. The deadlock in the Council was partly explained by two conﬂicting views on
transport that made unanimous decision-making between the six founding member states very
difﬁcult: while one group supported an ‘Anglo-Saxon’ approach to transport (i.e. market-led
with little intervention by public authorities and free choice of transport for carriers and clients),
a second group – West Germany and France in particular – supported strong public intervention
in transport (Stevens 2004; Te Brommelstroet and Nowak 2008). Table 19.1 provides an
overview of the development of contemporary European transport policy.
Up to the late 1980s, European transport legislation was of a regulatory nature, focusing 
on three objectives: the harmonisation of technical standards in various transport sectors; the
liberalisation of the transport industry; and the control of state aids to transport companies.
Harmonisation of technical and safety standards throughout the EU sought to remove incompatibilities between technical standards, e.g. air trafﬁc control, dimensions of heavy goods vehicles
and train signalling systems, as well as to harmonise social legislation, pollution standards and
safety matters in the transport industry. Under the Single Market programme (see Chapter 17),
the liberalisation of transport involved the opening up of national markets to competition from
operators of other member states. Road hauliers, airlines and shipping companies are now free
to carry people and goods from one EU country to another, but also between destinations within
a member state that is not their own (these rights are known as ‘cabotage’). There have been
attempts at opening freight and passenger rail transport and inland waterway transport to
competition, although the process has been politically contested and hence slow. Finally, as
discussed in Chapter 17, under EU competition policy the EU has restricted the type of aid that
public authorities can give to private companies so that aid that gives a transport company a
competitive advantage over its rivals is, in principle, forbidden.
For three decades the EU did not have a policy for transport infrastructure per se, but in the
space of a few years, a clear European transport policy emerged, including the development of
a pan-European infrastructure programme (Stevens 2004; Te Brommelstroet and Nowak 2008).
In 1979 the Commission published a memorandum on the role of the EC in the development
of European infrastructure (CEC 1979), in which it argued that the Council of Ministers had
failed to implement the Treaty of Rome in the domain of transport policy. The European Round
Table of Industrialists (ERT), a powerful business group inﬂuenced by transport and automobile
industries, successfully inﬂuenced the EU transport agenda in the 1980s by commissioning a
series of reports (ERT 1984, 1987, 1989, 1991, 1992) that argued that European infrastructure
was not competitive compared to that of other global players such as the USA, because of
‘border crossings’, ‘missing links’ and ‘bottlenecks’ (Peters 2003). The ERT called for a more
proactive role of the EU in infrastructure development (te Brommelstroet and Nowak 2008).
The European Parliament (EP), which at the time had a very limited role in decision-making in
comparison to the powers of the Council, also began to press for action. In 1983, following an
EP challenge, the Court of Justice ruled that the Council had failed to adopt a common transport
policy, although there was no speciﬁed time scale to do so in the Treaty. During the early 1990s
the EU did contribute to the development of transport infrastructure in the member states
through the use of the ERDF and the Cohesion Fund (see Chapter 18) for transport projects
(amounting to EUR 16 billion for 1975–91).
The political implications of the ECJ ruling were signiﬁcant (te Brommelstroet and Nowak
2008), combined with the impetus given by the Single Market programme in the second half of
the 1980s. In order to enable the free movement of goods, persons and services across borders
– the cornerstones of the Single Market Programme (see Chapter 17) – a more integrated
transport network became a priority as ‘the key to an integrated Europe’ (Banister et al. 1995:
xiii). Yet there are numerous missing links, inconsistencies, incompatibilities and bottlenecks
between the national networks of the member states, which were developed during the

## S. 327

nineteenth and twentieth centuries as an integral part of the territorial consolidation of modern
nation-states. Until the mid-1990s, there had been little attention to the functioning of the
European transport system as a whole. Policy-makers and planners ‘tended to develop segmented
solutions to merging bottlenecks by looking for speciﬁc local or modal solutions without due
regard to the connectivity of the transport system across different regions, sectors and modes’
(Nijkamp 1993: 7). Jacques Delors, the then president of the European Commission, acted as
a champion for the development of an EU policy in transport infrastructure, which would create
employment in peripheral areas and link them to central regions of Europe, as argued in the
White Paper on Growth, Competitiveness and Employment (CEC 1994b) (te Brommelstroet and
Nowak 2008).
The initial arguments in support of the development of an EU policy of infrastructure
development were thus related to economic considerations and the Single Market. Differences
between member states in the quality and provision of transport infrastructure can form
competitive distortions (CEC 1992b). Through the ‘pooling’ of infrastructure investments to
develop European-wide transport networks, scale economies and cost savings can be achieved
(Banister et al. 2000: 23). The conventional wisdom underpinning EU regional policy has been
that investments in modern and efﬁcient transport infrastructure are vital for the economic
development of disadvantaged peripheral regions and their convergence towards the average
levels of incomes and well-being in the EU. Besides, transport is a key economic sector in its
own right, representing 7–8 per cent of the EU GDP and providing jobs for 4.1 per cent of all
employed Europeans (Bosch 2003: 7).
In 1990 the Commission (CEC 1990b) advocated the creation of trans-European networks
in transport, telecommunications, and energy to underpin the implementation of the internal
market, through the promotion of the interconnection and inter-operability of national
networks. The subsequent Green Paper on The Impact of Transport on the Environment (CEC
1992a) and the White Paper Towards a New Framework for Sustainable Mobility (CEC
1992b) laid the foundation for a coherent European transport policy. The White Paper identiﬁed
serious imbalances in Europe’s transport infrastructure system: an increasing saturation of
transport networks; an unbalanced distribution between different modes of transport; regional
disparities in infrastructure provision; and a lack of capital investment in infrastructure. The
Commission proposed the development of a European framework for ‘sustainable mobility’ ‘in
order to ensure that transport continues to fulﬁl its economic and social functions under the most
favourable environmental conditions, while safeguarding the freedom of choice for the user’ and
to ‘contribute to social and economic cohesion in the Community and to the prosperity of the
peripheral regions’ (CEC 1992a: 43).
The Treaty of Maastricht, signed in 1992, formalised the proposals made by the Commission
by introducing a new title on ‘Trans-European Networks’ in transport, telecommunications and
energy. The early part of the 1990s, with the formalisation of a dual agenda on ‘sustainable
transport’ and on the ‘Trans-European Networks’, consequently marked a turning point in the
development of a comprehensive European transport policy, or ‘Common Transport Policy’.
In 1995 the European Commission adopted an action programme for transport policy for
the period 1995–2000 with a detailed legislative timetable (CEC 1995). This action programme
was modiﬁed with the 2001 White Paper on European Transport Policy for 2010: Time to
Decide (CEC 2001i), which forms the backbone of EU action in transport until 2010. The White
Paper includes an action plan of 60 measures aimed at bringing about substantial improvements
in the quality and efﬁciency of transport in Europe, and at gradually breaking the link between
transport growth and economic growth (a process known as ‘decoupling’), in order to reduce
pressures on the environment. The integration of environmental sustainability objectives into
EU transport policy has been of growing importance. In June 2009 the Commission published
298
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 328

a communication on the Future on Transport (CEC 2009f) to prepare for the new EU transport
strategy post-2010 in the face of challenges such as ageing, migration, increasing mobility, fossil
fuel scarcity and climate change.
Actors and instruments of the Common Transport Policy
The main actors involved in EU transport policy are the Commission’s DG Transport and Energy
(DG TREN), the Council of Ministers on Transport, Telecommunications and Energy and the
European Parliament (in particular its Committee on Transport and Tourism). Within the
Commission, transport is also a concern of DG Research, DG Environment, DG Competition
and DG REGIO. Since the Single European Act of 1986, decisions on EU transport policy are
normally taken under the co-decision procedure (described in Chapter 11). In the Council,
transport-related decisions are taken by qualiﬁed majority vote, with the exception of measures
that are liable to have a major impact on living standards, employment and the operation of
transport facilities, which have to be agreed unanimously. Outside the formal EU institutions,
various industrial pressure groups (such as the European Round Table of Industrialists or the
Community of European Railways) and environmental groups also work to inﬂuence the EU
transport policy agenda. 
EU interventions in transport policy can be broadly classiﬁed into two types (Robert et al.
2001: 55). The ﬁrst is intervention directly connected with the Common Transport Policy.
Formal EU legislation aims at ‘harmonising the tax, technical and social provisions of the member states which block the competition between the companies of the transport sector’ and at
developing the internal transport market through a ‘controlled liberalisation of markets’ (Robert
et al. 2001: 55). But EU action also involves the development of guidelines for the TEN-T 
and the selection of projects of Community interest (discussed in the following section). The
development of Intelligent Transport Systems is also part of the list of priority projects, with the
Galileo system for satellite radio navigation and the European rail trafﬁc management system,
and the Community co-ﬁnancing of pilot programmes related to the promotion and exchanges
of good practices. Second, there are a number of interventions in the context of other Community
policies that contribute directly to objectives of the Common Transport Policy: the co-ﬁnancing
of transport infrastructure investments through the Structural Funds and Cohesion Fund; the
activities of the European Investment Bank; and the transport-related research taking place under
the EU Seventh Framework Programme for Research and Technological Development (e.g. on
alternative motor fuels, next generation of vehicles or demand management).
Altogether, the areas of EU action in transport are numerous: air, road, road safety, rail,
maritime, inland waterways, intelligent transport systems, urban transport, satellite navigation
system, security and safety, passenger rights, state aid, research, international relations, crosscutting actions to support environmentally sustainable transport, transport infrastructure
development, transport pricing, and so on. Many of these areas are relevant for spatial planning
and have territorial implications. The scope of this book does not allow for a detailed coverage
of all these aspects of EU transport policy. In the remainder of this chapter the focus will
therefore be on two aspects of particular relevance and implication for spatial planning and
territorial development across Europe: the Trans-European Transport Networks and their
contribution to regional development and the cohesion of the European territory; and EU
activities to encourage an environmentally sustainable mobility in Europe.
The Trans-European Transport Networks (TEN-T): an overview
The European Union is now directly involved in the ﬁnancing of investments aimed at the
development and modernisation of transport infrastructure in the member states, through

EU transport policy

## S. 329

projects co-funded by the Structural and Cohesion Funds, loans from the European Investment
Bank and European Investment Fund, and through the Trans-European Transport Networks
(TEN-T) programme. The TEN-T is the element of EU transport policy that is of most direct
relevance to spatial planners: ‘given that the TENs predate and vastly outweigh (both in
monetary and political terms) the EU’s European Spatial Development Perspective (ESDP), the
TENs can indeed be considered the EU’s ﬁrst and primary de facto spatial development
intervention’ (Peters 2003: 321). The TEN-T is an ambitious plan to integrate 27 national
transport infrastructure networks into a coherent European system by eliminating bottlenecks,
building missing links, interconnecting networks and stimulating the development of intermodal
and interoperable networks. Interconnectivity refers to the horizontal cohesion and interconnection between different national, regional and local networks and accessibility at different
scales. Intermodality refers to the sequential use of different means of transport through the
transport cycle. Interoperability refers to operational and technical integration and forms of
coordination that enable the functioning of interconnectivity and intermodality at different
spatial levels by the various users and actors.
At the Essen Council in December 1994, the EU heads of state and government selected 14
TEN-T priority transport projects and eight energy projects. The transport projects (numbered
1 to 14 in Plate 8) include high-speed and conventional train links, motorways, airports, seaport
infrastructure and combined transport. In 1996 the European Parliament and the Council
approved guidelines to identify further projects of ‘common interest’ (Council 1996a), and 
a series of new priority projects were subsequently added to the Essen list (see Plate 8). These
priority projects qualify for speciﬁc ﬁnancial and technical assistance from the EU. The process
of selecting priority projects was a highly political exercise ‘from the bottom up’, characterised
by pressures from national governments or industrial lobbies pushing for their national wish
lists (Peters 2003; Te Brommelstroet and Nowak 2008; van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004). This
means that the list of priority projects resembles ‘more a mosaic than a coherent network’,
connecting national subsystems rather than giving birth to a genuine functional European 
system (Robert et al. 2001: 65). The selected projects have a highly symbolic role in the sense
that all countries are represented and most of them link two or more countries (Banister et al.
2000: 71).
There are currently 30 priority projects (illustrated in Plate 8), of which 18 are railway
projects, three are mixed rail-road projects, two are inland waterway projects and one focuses
on maritime transport along new ‘motorways of the sea’. The initial TEN-T guidelines published
in 1996 planned for a completion date of 2010 for the priority projects. It soon became clear
that this was an unrealistic time frame, and this has been revised to 2020 (Council 2004a), by
which date it is envisaged that the TEN-T will include 89,500 km of roads, 94,000 km of
railways (including 20,000 km of high-speed lines), 11,250 km of inland waterways (including
210 inland ports), 294 seaports and some 366 airports. Completing the network by 2020
involves upgrading existing infrastructure as well as building ‘missing links’, i.e. 4,800 km of
road and 12,500 km of rail tracks (CEC 2005d).
The primary responsibility for ﬁnancing and implementing the TEN-T priority projects lies
with the member states, with ﬁnancial and technical support from the Community, as detailed
below. As of 2008 progress was slow. Only four priority projects were completed: the Øresund
or Öresund ﬁxed link connecting Sweden and Denmark (see Figure 1.2); Milan’s Malpensa
Airport; the Cork-Dublin-Belfast-Stranraer railway line between Ireland and the UK; and the
Betuwe railway line linking Rotterdam to the German border. Two more were nearing
completion: the PBKAL (Paris-Brussels-Köln (Cologne)-Amsterdam-London) high-speed rail link
and the UK West Coast Main Line. Sections of the remaining projects have been completed –
for example, the ﬁrst phase of the TGV East in France and the Madrid-Barcelona high-speed
300
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 330

rail link (see CEC 2008m for an overview of the state of the projects). One-third of the total
investment necessary for the priority projects had been carried out by 2008; another third is
planned during 2007–13; and the remainder will take place after 2013. The Commission has
recognised that it will be difﬁcult to meet the 2020 deadline for some of the most complex projects such as the Alpine crossings (CEC 2008m). The intention is now to propose new 
TEN-T guidelines in 2010. In 2009, the Commission published a Green Paper that reviews 
the TEN-T policy and sets out options for its future development (CEC 2009g).
The already completed TEN-T projects have had spatial impacts on their surrounding
regions by changing the accessibility of particular locations, and thus affecting nodes of economic
activity. Recent developments in the Lille city-region in northern France (e.g. the Euralille
development) were heavily inﬂuenced by the positioning of Lille on the high-speed rail lines
connecting Paris, London and Brussels. Additionally, TEN-T can inﬂuence the choice of transport mode made by people and businesses, the mobility behaviour of individuals and the 
locational preferences of companies (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 55). New forms of mobility 
and commuting across borders have, for example, emerged as a result of the development 
of a dense network of high-speed rail links in north-west Europe, something that is discussed 
in the sociological study of ‘free-moving professionals’ called Eurostars and Eurocities (Favell
2008).
Financial, procedural and technical issues have slowed progress. Figure 19.1 illustrates the
various sources of funding that are used to ﬁnance the TEN-Ts. In 2008, the total cost of
completing the 30 priority axes by 2020 was estimated at EUR 397 billion. If all the other
projects that have been identiﬁed as being of common interest but not included in the priority
list were actually included, the total cost of completing the TEN-T network would be about 
EUR 900 billion (CEC 2008m). The EU contributes to the funding of the TEN-T through (i)
the TEN-T budget, (ii) the Cohesion and Structural Funds, and (iii) loans and guarantees of the
European Investment Bank (EIB). With these sources combined, the share of the EU in the
overall TEN-T investment was 29 per cent for the period 1993–2006 and is expected to reach
around 27 per cent for the period 2007–13 (CEC 2008m). The TEN-T budget line amounts to

## S. 331

approximately EUR 8 billion for 2007–13 (compared to EUR 4.4 billion in 2000–06 and EUR
2.2 billion in 1993–99), which will fund feasibility studies, contributions towards guarantees
or interests for loans and, in certain strictly deﬁned cases, infrastructure investments (up to 20
per cent of the total costs in speciﬁc cross-border projects). TEN-T-related infrastructure
investments are additionally co-funded by the Structural and Cohesion Funds (amounting to a
total of EUR 20 billion in the period 2000–06 and EUR 43 billion in 2007–13, i.e. 11 per cent
of the total investment needs).
While the share of the EU budget dedicated to transport infrastructure increased visibly after
1992, this role should not be overplayed: the lion’s share of the investment required for the TEN-
T comes from the member states and private investors. So the TEN-T budget ‘will not make
anything happen that could not otherwise go ahead on the basis of national sources of public
or private ﬁnance’ (Williams 1996: 172). Yet the level of investment in transport infrastructure
has fallen in most member states: in the 1980s, EU countries used to invest, on average, 1.5 per
cent of their GDP on transport infrastructure, while the current EU average is less than 
1 per cent (CEC 2008m). The Commission has developed guidance and instruments to assist
member states in ﬁnding the necessary funding through user charging and the participation 
of private capital.
Implementation of the TEN-T has also been delayed by coordination, planning and management problems, especially across borders. The absence of common legal structures and differences
in planning systems make the planning of transfrontier infrastructure developments difﬁcult and
lengthy. As a response to these difﬁculties, the Trans-European Transport Network Executive
Agency was set up in 2005. It is responsible for assisting the member states in managing the
technical and ﬁnancial implementation of the TEN-T. The Commission additionally appointed
a number of European coordinators (currently eight) to support those TEN-T projects that need
a strong push in order to overcome difﬁculties in the planning and construction phases.
In 1995 the Transport Council commissioned a ‘Transport Infrastructure Needs Assessment’
of the candidate countries (TINA 1999). This assessment identiﬁed the network elements to be
included in the TEN-T that were extended to the new accession countries. A number of ‘pan-
European corridors’ were selected to receive priority funding from the EU, most of them linking
the capital cities of the new member states. Also, a ‘high level expert group’ was commissioned
to work on the extension of major trans-European transport axes to the countries and regions
neighbouring the enlarged EU. The expert group identiﬁed ﬁve major transnational axes (High
Level Group 2005): ‘Motorways of the Seas’ linking the Baltic, Barents, Atlantic, Mediterranean,
Black and Caspian sea areas; a ‘Northern axis’ to connect the northern EU with Norway to the
north and with Belarus and Russia and beyond to the east; a ‘Central axis’ to link the centre of
the EU to Ukraine and the Black Sea and through an inland waterway connection to the Caspian
Sea; a ‘South Eastern axis’ to link the EU through the Balkans and Turkey to the Caucasus and
the Caspian Sea as well as to Egypt and the Red Sea; and a ‘South Western axis’ to connect the
south-western EU with Switzerland and Morocco and beyond, including the trans-Maghrebin
link connecting Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia. In January 2007 the European Commission
adopted Guidelines for Transport in Europe and Neighbouring Regions (CEC 2007k), which
integrate the ﬁve transport axes for international trade recommended by the 2005 High Level
Group report.
EU transport policy, regional development, cohesion and 
polycentricity in the EU
EU transport policy and the TEN-T in particular are embedded in discourses on growth,
competitiveness, cohesion and sustainability which are partly complementary, yet also partly
302
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 332

competing (Peters 2003). Although the initial arguments for the TEN-T were of economic
nature, more attention was paid over the years to their environmental and ‘cohesion’ dimension.
Transport infrastructure and services are important driving forces for territorial development
in Europe. As seen in Chapter 17, the liberalisation of network industries in the EU (e.g. industries organising ﬂows of energy, information, goods or people through a network, for example
electricity, telecommunications or postal services) has had ambiguous territorial impacts, as
liberalisation can often be accompanied by a detrimental impact on peripheral regions unless
strong measures to safeguard public services and a minimum level of ‘universal service’ are taken.
The territorial impact of the liberalisation of the transport sector is not addressed in detail here
(see EP 1998b; Robert et al. 2001: 58–63), but the British experience of liberalisation of
passenger rail transport, for example, showed that private companies selected the most proﬁtable
connections and neglected secondary connections and socially difﬁcult districts (Robert et al.
2001). The liberalisation and privatisation of transport services in the EU, coupled with
common trends across EU member states such as the decrease in public funding for transport
infrastructure, the increasing importance of public-private partnerships in infrastructure delivery
and the emphasis on transport solutions that are commercially viable, to name but a few, are
factors that all negatively affect the potential of the TEN-T to contribute to achieve socioeconomic and territorial cohesion.
At the same time, the TEN-T programme and the transport infrastructure investments
funded under EU Cohesion Policy are based on the assumption that improvements in the
accessibility of peripheral and ‘lagging behind’ regions will lead to their economic development
and their convergence with more prosperous regions of the EU (CEC 1998b: Vickerman et al.
1999). This economic justiﬁcation, however, has been deeply contested among economists and
transport experts (Peters 2003) because of the complexities and uncertainties surrounding the
causal links between transport infrastructure investments and regional economic development.
Indeed, several authors have argued that greater integration of the European territory through
improved infrastructure networks could actually lead to a greater divergence in economic
potential between regions.
Transport infrastructure investment and regional development: the debate
Traditional arguments in support of infrastructure-based economic development strategies are
based on the potential for exploitation of economies of scale arising from improved transport
infrastructure, leading to commodity price reductions:
better infrastructure will lead to lower transport costs or to a wider range of choice and
more competition. Improved access to input materials and to markets will cause ﬁrms in a
region, ceteris paribus, to be more productive, more competitive and hence more successful
than those in regions with inferior accessibility.
(Vickerman et al. 1999: 1–2)
Yet while improvements in the transport links between central and peripheral regions (and
associated reductions in transport costs) may make it easier for local ﬁrms to market their
products outside the region, it also enables producers from the central regions to access
previously peripheral markets, thereby exposing vulnerable local markets to competition (CEC
1998b; Sichelschmidt 1999; Vickerman et al. 1999). In Italy, it has been argued that ‘improved
transport during the 1950s, and reduced delivery costs, between north and south removed a 
key factor sheltering ﬁrms in the south from northern competition and helped to accelerate 
de-industrialisation’ (CEC 1998b: 4). The ‘brain drain’ of young workforce out of the region
may also be encouraged.

EU transport policy

## S. 333

Second, the mere presence of infrastructure alone does not in itself support economic
development, since the problems of areas ‘lagging behind’ or suffering from economic and
industrial decline can be explained by many issues: global restructuring of particular industrial
sectors; problems of labour productivity or lack of qualiﬁed workforce; lack of research and
development infrastructure; inadequate business support services; environmental damage and
problems of land availability; and other factors. The ESPON project 2.1.1 that investigated the
impact of EU transport policy on cohesion concluded that:
if one considers that under normal economic circumstances the long-term growth of
regional economies is in the range between two and three percent per year, [the] additional
regional economic growth of less than one or two percent over twenty years [attributed to
transport infrastructure investments] is almost negligible.
(ESPON 2004a: 16)
Large increases in regional accessibility translate into only very small increases in regional
economic activity, although this varies depending on the already existing level of accessibility
of a region:
•
for regions in the European geographical core and a highly developed transport and telecommunications infrastructure, additional gains in accessibility through larger airports,
extra motorways or high-speed rail lines will bring only a little additional incentive for
economic growth;
•
for regions at the European periphery or in the accession countries that suffer from a remote
geographical location and an underdeveloped transport infrastructure, a gain in accessibility
through a new motorway or rail line may bring signiﬁcant progress in economic development, although the opposite may happen ‘if the new connection opens a formerly isolated
region to the competition of more efﬁcient or cheaper suppliers in other regions’ (ESPON
2004a: 16).
Therefore, as recognised by the Commission (CEC 1998b: 4), transport improvements have to
be combined with other factors for growth to take place.
Third, the so-called ‘corridor’ and ‘shadow’ effects will reduce the effectiveness of an
infrastructure-led policy of regional development. The ‘corridor effect’ arises when a linear
infrastructure, for example a high-speed rail link, channels trafﬁc through a region without
bringing substantial beneﬁts to the region due to the lack (or scarcity) of stops between largescale urban centres. A new infrastructure link can improve the accessibility between regions but
not within regions if there are no (or few) linkages with sub-regional secondary networks.
‘Shadow effects’ arise when a new transport infrastructure diverts trafﬁc away from older routes
and other modes, in central or in peripheral regions, thus contributing to the decline of certain
networks in parts of these regions.
The contribution of the TEN-T to cohesion
In light of the arguments raised above, it has therefore been debated whether the TEN-T will
stimulate the economic development of peripheral regions, contribute to EU cohesion or instead
fuel forms of spatial polarisation. It is too early to draw any ﬁnal conclusions on the impact of
TEN-T on the social, economic and territorial cohesion of Europe, since the TEN-T network
envisaged by EU policy-makers is far from being realised. However, several studies have
concluded that the TEN-T investments, in their current form, will strengthen the economic
304
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 334

competitiveness and cohesion of the EU territory as a whole, but not necessarily contribute to
social and territorial cohesion within member states. In the initial list of TEN-T priority
projects, links between the ‘highest-level centres of decision making and creativity’ (Vickerman
et al. 1999: 2) were prioritised. This is because the ﬁnancial contribution of the EU to TEN-T
project costs is relatively low, which creates pressures to ‘concentrate on routes that serve a well
established existing market or transport ﬂow because these are where it will be easier to attract
private sector ﬁnance’ (Williams 1996: 182). Standard appraisal methods for large-scale infrastructure projects in terms of cost beneﬁt analysis tend to assign a high proﬁtability to projects
generating intensive use. The demand for new infrastructure, and the commercial viability of
such infrastructure, is typically highest in urbanised regions with higher population densities –
which are often the most developed regions. The preference given to high-speed rail, while
justiﬁed from an environmental point of view as it provides an alternative to congested road
and air in core regions, also serves as a means of reinforcing the dominance of the major urban
centres (Vickerman et al. 1999: 6).
In regions outside the European core, the selected TEN-T priority projects, because of their
very nature as projects of ‘European importance’, tend to link the major cities to the higherlevel European networks, which will increase the disparities between these cities and their
hinterlands (ESPON 2004a; Vickerman et al. 1999). The success or failure of the TEN-T policy
in that regard:
does not depend only on the number of kilometres of motorways or of high-speed railway
lines built in the context of the main network, it also depends on a more effective use of
infrastructures already in place and of a better interconnection between the TEN-T and the
secondary networks and between the various transport modes involved in the TEN-T.
(Robert et al. 2001: 144)
Developing appropriate interconnections between the TEN-Ts and secondary transport
networks should therefore, from a spatial planning point of view, be an important policy
objective in peripheral regions. In southern and central European member states, the concentration of investment on major networks carries the danger of shifting funding away from
secondary networks. However, in the new member states there is evidence that the use of the
Cohesion Fund has shifted from a focus on peripheral, lagging regions (i.e. upgrading of rural
roads and general modernisation of infrastructure) towards a modernisation of key, nationallevel infrastructures ‘so that these Cohesion countries might be more competitive with regard
to other EU member states’ (ESPON 2004a; Peters 2003: 325–6).
In terms of cohesion at the intra-regional or urban level, the contribution of individual TEN-
T projects to social cohesion is not evident either. The link between transport infrastructure and
social cohesion (or exclusion) is twofold:
Inadequacies in transport provision (either in terms of access to the system itself or the level
of service provided by the system) may create barriers limiting certain individuals and
groups from fully participating in the normal range of activities, including key activities 
such as employment, education, health care and shopping. . . . The transport system itself
may generate disbeneﬁts (in the form of environmental and social externalities) that bear
disproportionately on certain individuals and groups.
(Centre for Transport Studies et al. 2006)
Poor access to adequate transport is both a contributing cause and a result of social exclusion
(Cass 2005; Lucas 2004; Preston and Rajé 2007; Rajé 2003; SEU 2003). If not carefully planned
