EU transport policy
305
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 335

and thought through, large-scale transport projects (of which the TEN-T are an example) can
have a variety of negative impacts on social equity and reinforce social exclusion that outweigh
the positive impact that may be generated by increased access to jobs and services. Such negative
effects can include, for example, the ‘severance’ of physical space and communities, eviction
through compulsory purchase, displacement to make way for commercial development, and
increase in real-estate prices resulting in long-term gentriﬁcation and displacement. Additionally,
the affordability of the transport services provided as part of the TEN-T is an issue, in particular
in the case of high-speed rail services. With the general decline in public expenditure as a proportion of the overall GDP in many European countries, the ability to cover the costs of transport infrastructure from sources other than governmental tax revenue is of growing importance,
in particular in a period of ‘credit crunch’. This raises the question of ‘the extent to which the
costs of individual schemes can be recovered through user charges and the consequences for
society as a whole of any signiﬁcant shift towards this form of ﬁnancing’ (Grant-Muller et al.
2001: 256–7). Yet the social implications of the mobility opportunities offered by TEN-T have
been mostly ignored by policy-makers (Jensen and Richardson 2004: 220).
This discussion of the contribution of the TEN-T to cohesion should end with remarks on
their contribution to territorial cohesion and to the ESDP objective of a more balanced
distribution of infrastructure and activity across space. Although there are clear links between
transport infrastructure and the ﬁrst and second objectives of the ESDP, i.e. ‘polycentric spatial
development and new urban-rural relations’ and ‘parity of access to infrastructure and knowledge’, Williams (1996: 180) stressed that the development of the TEN-T proceeded ‘without
the broader perspective of EU spatial policy because of the political urgency attached to the
programme’. This may seem somehow paradoxical, since in the context of spatial planning at
the EU scale the TEN-T have an obvious resonance:
if asked what spatial planning issues legitimately should be addressed in a spatial plan for
the whole of the EU, it is likely that most local planners or other citizens would readily
accept that transport infrastructure is one such issue.
(Williams 1996: 168)
If one looks at the relation between TEN-Ts and the objective of polycentricity, for reasons
outlined above it becomes clear that while the TEN-Ts may improve the polycentricity of the
European territory as a whole, they tend to increase disparities within nation-states (ESPON
2004a), leading to a ‘dualisation of the territory’ (Robert et al. 2001) that contradicts the goal
of the ESDP to achieve a balanced polycentric urban system (ESPON 2004a: 21). So the scale
at which polycentricity is conceptualised has an inﬂuence on the type of transport investments
that are advocated by policy-makers:
The storyline of polycentricity continues to hold something in stock for everyone, which
means that EU decision-makers wishing to justify high-speed rail connections between 
national capitals are as justiﬁed to do so in the name of polycentric urban development as
are regional policy-makers arguing for improved road connections between secondary cities.
(Peters 2003: 328)
EU transport policy and sustainability
Transport is a major contributor to environmental problems of all kinds: acid rain and air
pollution; loss of natural habitats and biodiversity; emission of greenhouse gases fuelling
climate change; deterioration of the urban environment; and noise pollution. There are
306
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 336

numerous interactions between the quality of the environment and transport infrastructures and
systems, whether it is the modal split of trafﬁc or the location of transport infrastructures in
relation to centres of population and to natural spaces (Robert et al. 2001: 75). Transport
demand is closely linked to economic growth. There has been a continuous increase in trafﬁc
in Europe over the past four decades, due to rising income and mobility levels, increased social
and leisure time, technological developments and the process of European economic integration
(Banister et al. 2000). Intra-EU passenger transport has grown more than the population (which
means a greater mobility per person) but less than GDP. Road is still by far the most important
mode, accounting for more than 83 per cent of total intra-EU passenger transport. Air transport
growth has been the most dynamic over the past years (CEC 2009g). The sustained growth in
passenger and freight transport over the past decades has generated severe problems in terms
of lack of physical capacity (i.e. congestion), consumption of land, impacts on human health
and environmental externalities (see Chapter 21 for a discussion of the concept) (Banister 
et al. 2000).
From the early 1990s the European Commission began to press for the integration of
environmental protection objectives into the deﬁnition and implementation of EU transport
activities. The 1992 Green Paper on The Impact of Transport on the Environment deﬁned 
a new objective of ‘sustainable mobility’ for EU transport policy, i.e. containing the impact of
transport on the environment while allowing transport to continue to fulﬁl its economic and
social functions in the context of the Single Market, as well as contribute to social and economic
cohesion and the creation of new opportunities for peripheral regions (CEC 1992a). As discussed
above, reconciling these aims is no easy task. Banister et al. (2000) provide a good discussion
of the instruments that can be used by policy-makers at various scales to achieve sustainable
mobility: market-based instruments (such as taxes on fuel); regulation-based instruments 
(e.g. ﬁxing emission standards); lifestyle-based instruments (e.g. eco-labelling); and research 
and development. Additionally, spatial planning as such has a role to play in achieving sustainable mobility by helping to reduce the demand for travel through a better integration and
mix of functions and land uses. The EU has used a combination of all these instruments in its
activities. The most significant EU actions to promote ‘sustainable mobility’ are briefly
summarised here:
•
In line with the requirements of the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Directive (see
Chapter 21), all projects funded under the TEN-T are subject to an assessment of their likely
environmental impacts. When signiﬁcant impacts are identiﬁed, preventative and remedial
measures have to be integrated into the planning and implementation of the projects.
Although the TEN-T favour rail over road and air, high-speed projects do have signiﬁcant
environmental impacts, for example loss of habitats and biodiversity. It has also been shown
that building more rail links alone will have a marginal effect on modal shift, unless other
measures (such as road charging) are taken at the same time. In 2005 the Commission
estimated that on current trends, CO2 emissions from transport will be 38 per cent greater
in 2020 than today, and that completing the 30 priority axes will slow down this increase
by only about 4 per cent (CEC 2005d). The contribution of the TEN-T to the objective of
sustainable mobility will be limited if they do not lead to a shift from road and air to rail
(Banister et al. 2000: 219).
•
A variety of EU actions have sought to encourage modal shift from road to other modes of
transport. The Marco Polo programme, with a budget of EUR 450 million for the 2007–13
period, funds transnational projects that promote the shift of freight transport from roads
to sea, rail and inland waterways. Under the TEN-T programme, the development of the
‘Motorways of the Sea’ is intended to help to reduce road congestion by developing

EU transport policy
307
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 337

maritime transport along the coasts of the EU. The EU has also supported the development
of more environmentally friendly forms of urban transport, as advocated in the 2007 Green
Paper on Urban Mobility (CEC 2007l). The CIVITAS Initiative helps cities across Europe
to test innovative strategies for sustainable urban transport.
•
In 2008 the Commission proposed a package of measures for ‘greening transport’. This
includes, among other things, an inventory that summarises EU actions to date and a
Communication with proposals for future EU initiatives (CEC 2008n). Guidance on the
internalisation of the social and environmental costs of transport through infrastructure
charging has been produced (CE Delft 2008). This should in the long term stimulate 
a transfer of road trafﬁc towards other less polluting modes. If a European energy tax on
non-renewable fossil fuels could be agreed in future, this would considerably strengthen the
policy (see Chapter 21). However, such a measure may conﬂict with social equity and social
cohesion objectives and with the goal of balanced spatial development, because the increase
in transport cost that would be generated ‘is most unfavourable for lagging regions, rural
regions and peripheral regions, those who are in general less afﬂuent than the centres’
(ESPON 2004a: 264).
How can the combined impacts of these EU measures be assessed to date? A study commissioned
by DG TREN in 2009 to assess the economic, social and environmental impacts of the
Common Transport Policy since 2000 (Steer Davies Gleave 2009) concluded that the objective
of a modal shift towards rail transport stated in the 2001 White Paper has not been achieved
if measured in terms of total transport demand across the EU. The White Paper on European
Transport Policy for 2010 (CEC 2001i) also emphasised decoupling growth in transport
demand from GDP growth and reducing transport emissions. Neither of these objectives have
been achieved to date. However, there has been some progress towards decoupling the growth
of transport from greenhouse gas emissions. While emissions have continued to rise, the growth
has been slower than trafﬁc growth and there has been a signiﬁcant reduction in the emissions
of speciﬁc pollutants thanks to progress on fuel efﬁciency. But there is little progress towards a
shift away from fossil fuels and the car dependency of European societies.
This conclusion is not surprising. A major dilemma characterises EU transport policy: on the
one hand the EU, through the TEN-T and other funds, supports new infrastructure investments
(which create more demand for travel and more mobility); on the other hand the EU advocates
sustainable mobility and protection of the environment (which implies containing new
investments) (Banister et al. 2000: 63). The modal focus of the TEN-T projects on rail and water
has to be put into perspective with the transport infrastructure investments funded by the
Structural and Cohesion Funds across the EU since the 1980s. These funds have ﬁnanced many
more transport projects than the TEN-T budget does, and have disproportionately favoured
investments in road infrastructure (in 1994–99 over two-thirds of the Cohesion Fund and ERDF
spent on transport infrastructure in Objective 1 areas went to roads [Peters 2003]).
Conclusion
In the 1990s, the emphasis of EU transport policy shifted towards a comprehensive strategy for
‘sustainable mobility’ including a policy on infrastructure development through the TEN-T. EU
transport policy now has a central place in the European integration project, supported by
important strategies, legislation, funding and tangible infrastructure projects. As discussed in this
chapter, while the TEN-Ts have the potential to contribute to the cohesion of the EU territory
as a whole, their contribution to cohesion within the member states and to the objective of
‘sustainable mobility’ is questionable. Altogether ‘growth and competitiveness considerations
308
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 338

continue to win over redistributive and environmental aims’ in EU transport policy (Peters 
2003: 321).
The ESPON study (2004a) concluded that the three fundamental goals of economic efﬁciency,
spatial equity and environmental sustainability cannot be favoured at the same time in EU
transport policy and that trade-offs are inevitable. This is because, as discussed in this chapter,
‘transport as an agent of enhancing competitiveness conﬂicts with transport as an agent of
improving accessibility and cohesion’, and ‘transport as a source of welfare through mobility
conﬂicts with the need to control harmful effects on the environment’ (ESPON 2004a: 264). To
reconcile economic efficiency and spatial equity objectives, the report suggests that one
possibility for the future is to revise policy measures ‘in the direction of a more balanced spatial
development, if one is willing to give up a certain degree of economic efﬁciency’, or instead to
leave EU transport policy unchanged in favour of a higher degree of efﬁciency, which means
that spatial imbalances should then ‘be equalized by transfer payments to those regions suffering
from losses’ (ESPON 2004a: 264). Spatial planning at the national and regional level has, in
any case, a key role to play to complement the EU TEN-T policy by ensuring the development
of secondary networks.
Further reading
A concise introduction to the development and politics of EU transport policy is provided by
Stevens 2004. For a full overview of all the aspects of EU transport policy, see the website of
DG TREN, which includes most recent policy documents: http://ec.europa.eu/transport/index_
en.htm. For an overview of the TEN-T, see the website of the TEN-T executive agency: http://
tentea.ec.europa.eu. The yearly statistical pocketbook published by Eurostat, Energy and
Transport in ﬁgures, offers up-to-date reviews of transport trends in the EU. It is available from
the Eurostat website (http://epp.eurostat.ec.europa.eu).

## S. 339

EU agriculture and rural
development policy
The view that there is something special about agriculture, something that distinguishes it from other
sectoral activities and merits it receiving advantageous treatment, whilst not commanding such
strong support as in the early days of the EC, still strikes a chord with EU decision-makers.
(Nugent 2006: 456)
Introduction
Agriculture has long been at the heart of European Union policy. It holds a dominant position
and one that is closely interlinked with spatial development and environmental concerns.
Agriculture policy involves the largest number of Commission ofﬁcials, the most extensive and
complex collection of EU committees and advisory bodies, and the largest share of Community
spending. Its beneﬁts and impacts are a matter of constant debate, particularly the effects on
consumer prices, the environment, the landscape and the social composition of rural areas.
Change, rather than stability, has been the hallmark of EU agricultural and rural policy. Early
approaches involving complex mechanisms to support the price of farm produce led to massive
surpluses and other market distortions. Since the early 1990s, price support has slowly given
way to policies that subsidise farm incomes directly, promote more environmentally friendly
practices and support rural communities. There has also been increasing interest in the broader
concept of rural development, which has made the link with territorial issues more explicit. This
chapter summarises the historical development of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) with
particular reference to its spatial impacts. It starts with an introduction to EU rural areas and
their economies.
The rural territory of the EU
More than three-quarters of the EU territory is ‘rural’ – where agriculture and forestry remain
the dominant forms of land use. The European Commission estimates that 27 per cent of the
EU-27 population live in predominantly rural areas and another 37 per cent in intermediate rural
areas (both deﬁned at the NUTS 3 level) (CEC 2007m: 50).1 Although agriculture is the
dominant land use in the EU, and particularly in central and eastern Europe, it makes only a
small contribution to the economy of most member states. While 44 per cent of EU land is
deﬁned as ‘utilised agricultural areas’ managed by farmers, agriculture accounts for only 7.4 per
cent of employment in the EU-27 and only 3.8 per cent in the EU-15. Agriculture accounts for
12.7 million full-time equivalent workers in the EU (and a much larger number of actual
workers), with a signiﬁcant share of that workforce in Romania (2.6 million) and Poland 
(2.3 million). The total output of agriculture was worth EUR 134 billion of gross value added
Chapter 20
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 340

(GVA) in 2006 (CEC 2008p: 322). This is only 1.8 per cent as a proportion of total GVA for
the EU-27 in comparison to, for example, 6.5 per cent for the construction sector and 28 per
cent for business and ﬁnancial services.2 However, income from agriculture has risen steadily
for the EU as a whole since the 1950s and continues to do so, up 12 per cent in 2006 on 2000
income. Part of this is explained by the fall in labour input and the increasing intensity of
farming, with fewer people producing more, and particularly so in the new member states 
(CEC 2008p: 324).
Why have a Common Agricultural Policy?
For almost 40 years, the development of rural areas in the EU has been strongly inﬂuenced 
by the CAP. It has the biggest share of the EU budget (around 54.6 per cent in 1998, down to
around 44 per cent in 2007). The total EU budget in 2007 was EUR 126.5 billion (1.08 per
cent of the EU gross national income). Commitments for agricultural policy represented 44 per
cent of the EU budget (in 2009) totalling EUR 56 billion; of this EUR 42.7 billion was spent
on direct aids and market measures and EUR 12.4 billion on rural development. The CAP was
conceived in the years following the Second World War when agriculture was a more signiﬁcant
sector of the economy (with about a 25 per cent share of employment in the then six member
countries), but when productivity was low and countries relied heavily on food imports from
other parts of the world. Rural poverty was widespread. Agricultural interests were comparatively powerful stakeholders in national and regional governments. After times of shortage
and rationing, the objective to become self-sufﬁcient in food production, or ‘food security’, was
high on the political agenda. Other non-Community countries were able to ask for high prices
since the demand for food is inelastic (demand for food does not fall much even if prices go up).
So a single market for agriculture was proposed, based on three main principles: common guaranteed prices for agricultural products; ﬁnancial incentives to farmers to increase productivity;
and a ‘Community preference’, meaning that tariffs would be applied to goods from nonmember countries. Nugent (2006: 470) argues that there is a fourth unofﬁcial operating principle: that the policy should make allowance for national variations. In practice member state
governments have had considerable discretion to vary the application of the CAP.
The Treaty of Rome (Article 33) deﬁned the objectives of the CAP as increasing productivity,
ensuring a fair standard of living for the agricultural community, stabilising agricultural
markets, and assuring supplies and reasonable prices. Rapid progress was made on these
objectives. Productivity soared, the EU became a net exporter of food, and agricultural wages
improved substantially. At the same time, the signiﬁcance of agriculture as an employer and as
a sector in national economies declined rapidly. Despite the decline, agriculture has maintained
its dominant position in EU policy because of the primary need for food production and the
vulnerability of incomes in the face of natural environmental conditions. Agriculture has had
strong support from some member states, particularly France (a major beneﬁciary of the CAP),
rural landowners and agricultural interest groups such as the Committee of Agricultural
Organisations in the European Union/General Confederation of Agricultural Co-operatives in
the EU (COPA/COGECA).
Agricultural pressure groups have been successful, argues Nugent (2006: 462), partly because
they have no ‘natural opponents’:
Whereas farmers constitute a clear section of the population with a readily identiﬁable common
sectoral interest, consumers and environmentalists do not have such a group consciousness, 
or are widely dispersed and, in consequence, are just not so easy to mobilise or organise.

EU agriculture and rural development policy

## S. 341

This is less true in the 2000s than it was in the 1950s but the agricultural lobby remains
formidable in size, power and ability to operate inside the EU institutions. Those who beneﬁt
(for example, through increased land values) have become powerful friends of the CAP.
However, support for the original formulation of the CAP was weakened by its effect on rising
prices of food and other products, the creation of expensive surpluses, and the growing political
salience of environmental and food safety issues (Nugent 2006). As the original rationale for
the CAP has become less valid, other social and environmental arguments about the need to
maintain rural communities and to husband the land resource have come to the fore. In parallel
the CAP has evolved through a series of quite radical reforms to address weaknesses and remain
credible.
Evolution of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP)
Table 20.1 summarises the main stages in the evolution of the CAP. The ﬁrst CAP market
regulations and common prices were adopted in the 1960s. CAP measures were almost entirely
price support mechanisms. Fixed subsidised prices were up to 40 per cent more than world
prices, and about 70 per cent of products were supported. Although well intentioned, ‘the overall
impact of the CAP in many respects has been disastrous’ (Dinan 1998: 73). Guaranteed prices
led to massive over-production and surpluses, which had to be stored or converted to animal
feed or required the export of products, often with further expense and subsidy. Agricultural
employment fell rapidly, and small farms suffered from competition and did not beneﬁt so much
from increases in average income. The size of holdings increased, and the use of intensive farming
methods became more widespread, leading to environmental damage. Understandably, such
contradictions were roundly criticised, indeed ridiculed. The CAP was also the ﬁrst target for
sustained criticism (for its protection tendencies) from international competitors, particularly the
United States, which saw the support mechanisms (and sometimes dumping of products on
world markets) as a threat to their own agriculture. Restrictions in the form of thresholds and
quotas were applied in the 1980s to reduce over-supply. These rules also increased the complexity of the CAP with variations of incentives and controls in each food sector. By this time
the CAP had grown to 60 per cent of the EU budget. Reforms were agreed in 1988 and 1992
that linked the CAP much more to the reality of the market, reduced direct support, provided
incentives for greater diversity in crops and took 15 per cent of arable land out of production
as ‘set-aside’. Direct support for agricultural production shifted to income support for farmers.
Thus, by the 1990s the form and operation of the CAP had gone through many changes and
it had become quite different to its starting point, although the underlying principles remained
the same: to support an agricultural economic sector and those employed in it (Buckwell 1997).
The ﬁrst major challenge to this principle came with the MacSharry reforms of 1992 (named
after the then Commissioner for Agriculture). These are widely regarded as the primary milestone in agricultural policy, signalling a change in direction from support to production (the
‘productivist CAP’) to support for farming incomes, wider rural development, and agrienvironmental measures (explained further below) (Dammers and Keiner 2006; Gallent et al.
2008; van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004).
Also in 1992 the Commission’s Directorate-General for Regional Policy (DG REGIO) began
to fund the Community Initiative Leader (Liaison entre actions de développement de l’économie
rurale) as one of a number of Cohesion Policy initiatives funded through the Structural Funds
(see Chapter 18). Leader supported rural economies indirectly by co-ﬁnancing projects that
stimulated organisational capacity of local actors usually through partnership and networking
(CEC 2000h: 7). The accent was very much on joining up across sectors, notably agriculture,
environment and tourism. Projects sought to coordinate actions mainly to enhance the rural
312
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 342

heritage and improve the economy and job prospects. Evaluations of Leader were positive, with
potential for ‘profound change from a passive to an active attitude . . . among many local actors’
(Shucksmith et al. 2005: 192). Leader has been closely aligned to the territorial dimension and
the creation of integrated territorial development strategies at the local level with planning
authorities often playing a key role. It has been through three iterations (Leader I, II and +) and
it is now an axis of the rural development policy, as explained below.
Agenda 2000
During the 1990s, international negotiations intended to liberalise trade through the General
Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) increased the pressure for a more fundamental reform
of the CAP. The USA and other international players including developing countries pressed hard
for the EU to reduce subsidies (and effectively subsidise exports) to its farmers. The argument

EU agriculture and rural development policy

Table 20.1 Milestones in the development of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP)
1957
Treaty of Rome deﬁned aims of the CAP: to increase agricultural productivity; ensure a fair
standard of living for the farm community; achieve stability of markets; guarantee regular
supplies; ensure reasonable prices to consumers
1958
Stresa Conference deﬁned principles of CAP: a single market for agriculture with common
guaranteed prices for farmers; Community preference over imports, ﬁnancial solidarity
1962
First market regulations of the CAP
1968
Entry into force of common prices for agricultural products under the CAP
1972
First socio-structural directives
1980
Principle of production targets agreed
1984
Introduction of milk quotas
1985
Commission Green Paper on Perspectives for the CAP
1988
Reforms: introduction of budgetary discipline and of set-aside scheme
1991
MacSharry Report on the CAP (after the name of the then Commissioner for Agriculture)
1992
Treaty of Maastricht: made it a priority to integrate environmental protection aspects into
all EU policies, including the CAP
1992
Reform of the CAP (‘MacSharry reform’): cuts in prices in various sectors and introduction
of a small amount of direct payment to farmers
1996
European Conference on Rural Development in Cork: Cork Declaration: A Living
Countryside
1997
Commission’s proposals (Agenda 2000) for dealing with the impact of enlargement on the
budget of EU regional and agriculture policies. Proposals for second pillar: rural
development
1999
Berlin Council agreement on EU budget and Agenda 2000 reforms in light of enlargement.
First regulations governing rural development pillar of CAP
2002
Brussels Agreement ﬁnalised the CAP negotiating chapter for enlargement. The European
Council agreed that the accession countries in the enlargement process would not receive
the same levels of support as existing members
2003
Major reform of the CAP: new system of single farm payments (income support) and
decoupling of aid and production and strengthened rural development policy
2005 or 2007
‘Single farm payment’ entered into force
2007–13
New programming period for CAP funding: simpliﬁcation of system with only two funds:
‘European Agricultural Fund for Guarantee’ and ‘European Agricultural Fund for Rural
Development’
2007
New review (‘health check’) of CAP launched
Source: authors’ own.

## S. 343

was only partially resolved with the conclusion in 1994 of the ‘Uruguay Round’ of international
talks. By the mid-1990s the prospect of substantial increases in agricultural land and workers
through EU enlargement provided further motivation for changing the system alongside
commitments to incorporate environmental concerns in EU sectoral policies (Lenschow 1999,
2002; Steurer 2005). In 1996 the Agriculture Commissioner joined with many other stakeholders
to agree the Cork Declaration: A Living Countryside (European Conference on Rural Development 1996), which called for the ‘introduction of broader rural development objectives’ into the
CAP. It promoted sustainable rural development as the ‘fundamental principle which underpins
all rural policy in the immediate future and after enlargement’ (point 1).
This commitment was elaborated in Agenda 2000 (CEC 1997c), the Commission’s proposals
for dealing with enlargement and its impact on the EU budget. The proposals for the CAP were
to further reduce production subsidies and to strengthen the new objectives for agricultural
policy – the incorporation of environmental and rural development goals, particularly the
creation of alternative employment in rural areas. These have been followed through in actions
although they have not found their way into the Treaty.3 A Berlin Summit of 1999 partially
carried forward the Agenda 2000 proposals although it was agreed that more work was needed
if international competitors were to be assuaged and the potentially costly consequences of
enlargement avoided. In 2002 the European Council agreed that the accession countries in the
enlargement process would not receive the same levels of support as existing members. In the
same year, a new reform package proposed a major ‘decoupling’ of ﬁnancial support from
production, and this was agreed in 2003. This is the point at which more critical steps are taken,
bringing, as Nugent (2006: 476) explains:
an end to the array of payment based on levels of output and production; support for
farmers to be based on a single direct payment . . . payments to farmers to be dependent
on them observing environmental, food safety and animal welfare standards; [and] savings
in direct payments to be re-directed towards rural development and environmental
protection schemes.
Bryden (2000) explains how a shift to rural development has other advantages: it is more
easily defended in the critical negotiations in the World Trade Organisation (WTO), and because
it requires a level of co-ﬁnancing by member states (as in Cohesion Policy) this effectively reduces
the demand for funding. The attention to rural development in agricultural policy has close
parallels with the agenda for European spatial planning expressed in the European Spatial
Development Perspective (ESDP) (CSD 1999) with its attention to policy coordination and the
territorial dimension. It is a:
shift from sectoral to territorial policy involving attempts to integrate the various sectoral
policies at regional and local levels; . . . [and] more generally a shift from an approach based
on subsidising declining sectors including agriculture, ﬁshing and mining, to one based on
strategic investments to develop new activities.
(Bryden 2000: 26)
At the end of 2007, DG Agriculture and Rural Development published further proposals for
reform under the banner of ‘health check’, this time to help streamline and modernise, rather
than ‘re-reform’, the CAP (CEC 2007o). It continues the trend of strengthening rural development policy, although arguments about the balance between support for rural development and
product support continue, as explained below. It is also noteworthy that throughout the many
reviews and reforms of the operation of the CAP the Treaty provisions setting out the general
objectives for EU agricultural policy have remained the same, as shown in Box 20.1.
314
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 344

The instruments of agricultural and rural development policy
Since 2005, the agricultural expenditure is ﬁnanced by two funds: the European Agricultural
Guarantee Fund (EAGF), which ﬁnances direct payments to farmers and measures to regulate
agricultural markets such as intervention and export refunds; and the European Agricultural
Fund for Rural Development (EAFRD), which ﬁnances the rural development programmes of
the Member States.
The CAP first pillar: economic instruments
The elements of Agenda 2000 implemented in 2003 introduced a CAP with two pillars. The
ﬁrst is primarily the direct support to farmers, the single payment scheme (SPS).4 The second is
directed to rural development and explained below. The principle of the SPS in the ﬁrst pillar
is that farmers should receive the same amount of support irrespective of the crops they grow
or production levels – sometimes described as the ‘decoupled’ principle. Member states are
allocated a maximum amount of funding or ‘national ceiling’ to be used for support, but have
considerable discretion in the timing and method of its implementation.

EU agriculture and rural development policy
315
Box 20.1 Treaty provisions for agriculture and rural areas
The actions of the Commission on agriculture and rural policy are guided, as in other sectors,
ﬁrst by the objectives set out in the Treaties. Article 38 of the Consolidated Treaty on the
Functioning of the European Union (TFEU) (in its 2008 consolidated version integrating the
modiﬁcations listed in the Treaty of Lisbon signed in 2007 and ratiﬁed in 2009) (formerly Article
32 of the TEC) states that: ‘The Union shall deﬁne and implement a common agriculture and
ﬁsheries policy’. Article 39 of the TFEU of 2008 (formerly Article 33 of the TEC) states that
the objectives of the common agricultural policy shall be:
(a)
to increase agricultural productivity by promoting technical progress and by ensuring the
rational development of agricultural production and the optimum utilisation of the factors of
production, in particular labour;
(b) thus to ensure a fair standard of living for the agricultural community, in particular by
increasing the individual earnings of persons engaged in agriculture;
(c)
to stabilise markets;
(d) to assure the availability of supplies;
(e)
to ensure that supplies reach consumers at reasonable prices.
The Treaty further states that the application of the Common Agricultural Policy must take
account of variations in agricultural activity and disparities between regions. These objectives
are the same as those expressed in the TEC (summarised above), and despite the substantial
shift in policy, the term ‘rural development’ is still not used in the Treaties, something that the
Treaty of Lisbon ratiﬁed in 2009 did not change. The Lisbon Treaty also offers no proposals
to include reference to rural development (European Communities 2008). However, under
Article 3 the objective of promoting ‘the economic, social and territorial cohesion’ (explained
further in Chapter 8) involves ‘reducing disparities between the levels of development of
various regions . . . [and] particular attention shall be paid to rural areas . . .’ (Article 174,
emphasis added).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 345

The amount of payment is determined historically with reference to payments made to the
same farm under the old production-linked schemes, or the member state can opt to claim for
a whole region and then share out the funding pro rata by the land area of each farm. In both
cases there is some continuity in the spatial distribution of the CAP from the old regime despite
the change in approach. The 12 new member states have a much simpler regime where a
standard payment is made per hectare. Difﬁcult negotiations over changes to the CAP led to
provisions that allow member states to make exceptions to the SPS (within limits) to ensure that
the farming of certain produce continues, including some cereals and seeds, and the farming 
of livestock. This allows a proportion of the ‘ceiling’ to remain ‘coupled’, that is, linked to
production. Quotas have also been retained in the dairy sector and will only be reduced incrementally over time. The rationale for retaining controls is in part a territorial one. The European
Environmental Bureau (EEB) (2008) has warned of unwanted consequences of liberalisation
policies for the environment, such as rapid intensiﬁcation as farmers chase the most proﬁtable crops. There are also interconnections with the socio-economic structure of regions. A very
good example is the continuation of support for 2.5 million olive growers in the south of Europe,
for which a special aid scheme exists to avoid dramatic changes to the landscapes and
livelihoods.
In order to receive their payments, farmers must fulﬁl certain conditions known as ‘crosscompliance’. The land must be kept in ‘good agricultural and environmental condition’ and must
meet management standards in respect of ‘the protection of the environment, public, animal 
and plant health; and animal welfare’. Furthermore, EU agricultural policy emphasises the role
of farmers as ‘stewards of the countryside’ and has required since 1998 the incorporation of
environmental objectives into the CAP. This role is encouraged by cross-compliance and by
various agri-environmental measures. Other funding is available for energy crops, sufﬁcient to
encourage a very rapid increase in the area planted up to 1.2 million hectares in 2006.
The direct aids described above account for about 68 per cent of the CAP budget (in 2008).
Figure 20.1 shows how the ‘decoupled’ element of state aid has increased since 2006 in contrast
to aid tied to production. It also shows the relative amount spent on the second pillar: rural
development.
The CAP second pillar: rural development
The second pillar supports rural development. It is much more ‘spatial’ or territorial in its
operation than the ﬁrst pillar, linking policy to the particular characteristics of regions and the
communities in which it intervenes (CEC 2001j). In its ﬁrst iteration between 2000 and 2006
the second pillar on rural development was a very small component of funding, about one ninth
of direct aid. Although its share of funding has grown (as shown in Figure 20.1) it still has by
far the smaller share of the budget (CEC 2008q).
The instruments for rural development used by member states in the ﬁrst round included
investments on farms, early retirement for farmers, promotion of young farmers, and, of
particular interest for this book, agri-environmental measures, support for less favoured areas
and ‘adaptation and development of rural areas’. All of these instruments existed prior to the
creation of the second pillar but were bundled together within the notion of rural development.
Up to 10 per cent of the national ceiling can be used for agri-environment measures, that is, to
pay farmers for providing additional environmental beneﬁts (public goods) that are beyond
‘normal’ good husbandry. These may include less intensive pasture systems, organic agriculture,
preservation and enhancement of landscape features such as hedgerows and woods, and the
conservation of natural habitats (CEC 2003d). The mid-term review of the ﬁrst round of rural
development came to positive conclusions about the measure for ‘adaptation of rural areas’. 
316
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 346

A great variety of types of project were funded, for instance, diversiﬁcation of employment,
accessibility and public transport improvements, small-scale infrastructure, village facilities, and
the improvement of the capacity of rural communities to tackle problems. The review noted the
interplay of CAP and Structural Funds in rural areas and called for more explicit coordination.
As a result, spending from 2007 is directed by the Community Strategic Guidelines for Rural
Development for the Period 2007–13, which applies to all Community actions in rural areas
(Council 2006d). The Guidelines have four themes or axes of rural development policy as shown
in Box 20.2. Member states must spread the funding across all the axes and, in contrast to the
ﬁrst pillar, member states must routinely ﬁnd co-ﬁnancing for projects from government or
private sources.
The fourth, or ‘Leader’, axis is of particular interest and takes forward the succession of
Leader initiatives (I, II and +) since 1992 in the same vein. From 2007 it funds projects in
relatively small areas of homogenous character having in general a population of no more than
10,000, with implementation through partnerships of local stakeholders or ‘local action groups’
(LAGs). During the period of Leader+ 893 LAGs spent about EUR 5 billion, 45 per cent of
which came from the EU. Local action groups have had considerable leeway in proposing
projects for funding and it would be impossible to do justice to the range of activity here.
Examples include improving wine production while achieving environmental improvements and
tourism development in the Serres LAG in Greece, and encouraging sustainable agricultural
practices to maintain landscape character on Menorca (CEC 2007n).5

## S. 347

Cohesion Policy and rural areas
The creation of the Leader axis in the rural development pillar of the CAP is an indication of
the increasing integration of rural policy and action in the Commission. DG Agriculture and
Rural Development is not the sole player in rural matters, and the involvement of other DGs
in rural affairs is increasing. The Directorate-General for Trade has played a critical role in the
reform of the CAP because it is the main representative at the WTO. The Directorates-General
for regional/Cohesion Policy and environment also have a strong interest in domestic agricultural
and rural issues. Integration is assisted (although not guaranteed) by the Community Strategic
Guidelines on Rural Development (Council 2006d), and the guidelines for spending of the
Structural Funds also call for ‘complementarity and consistency with other Community policies’
(Council 2006e: 10). The 2008 Green Paper on Territorial Cohesion (CEC 2008c) stresses the
strong connection between the objective of territorial cohesion and the performance of rural
areas (especially where they are also border regions).
What have agricultural and rural development policy 
achieved?
The CAP has had a signiﬁcant impact on the transformation of European agriculture and,
through that, on the character of the European countryside. Overall beneﬁts and costs are
summarised in Box 20.3. Of the ﬁve main aims in the Treaty the CAP has made much progress
with productivity, agricultural incomes, food security and self-sufﬁciency in the EU. While food
is plentiful it has not achieved the ﬁfth aim of reasonable prices as cheaper imports have been
excluded and the burden of dealing with surplus production has been met largely by the
taxpayer.
The beneﬁts have come at considerable ﬁnancial, environmental and social costs. Because 
the CAP has taken such a large share of the EU budget there has also been an opportunity cost,
318
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Box 20.2 Four axes of rural development policy,
2007–13
1
Improving the competitiveness of the agricultural and forestry sector – involving measures
to ‘target human and physical capital in the agriculture, food and forestry sectors (promoting
knowledge transfer and innovation) and quality production’.
2
Improving the environment and the countryside – involving measures to protect and enhance
natural resources, as well as preserving farming of high nature value and forestry systems and
cultural landscapes in Europe’s rural areas.
3
Improving the quality of life in rural areas and diversiﬁcation of the rural economy including

conditions for growth and job creation in all sectors and the diversiﬁcation of economic
activities’.
4
Taking forward the Leader initiative on ‘innovative governance through locally based, bottomup approaches to rural development’.

## S. 348

as the Commission has not been able to fund other potentially worthwhile objectives. The direct
costs are multiplied through the unintended consequences, especially for the environment, an
example of which is given in Box 20.4. The CAP has been slow to respond to these challenges.
The disadvantages were widely understood in the 1980s, but the interests behind the CAP and
particularly the national interests of the big farming countries in the old EU-15 (France,
Ireland, Italy and Spain) have slowed reform.
Environmental impacts or ‘interaction’
Because they are so closely bound together, it is more correct to speak of the ‘interaction’ of
farming and the environment than of ‘impacts’. ‘Farming forms part of an ecosystem rather than

EU agriculture and rural development policy
319
Box 20.3 Benefits and costs of the CAP
Beneﬁts:
•
strongly encouraged the modernisation of European agriculture with great gains in
productivity;
•
helped achieve the goal of self-sufﬁciency in the production of basic agricultural products in
Europe;
•
helped reach stability in food markets and improved the ‘food security of Europe’;
•
increased farmers’ average incomes;
•
cushioned some farmers from underlying trends in the restructuring of the rural economy;
•
encouraged farmers to maintain or improve the rural environment by cross-compliance
requirements linking payments to good husbandry of the land and through additional agrienvironmental measures.
Costs:
•
imposed a very large burden on the EU budget with opportunity costs;
•
created massive surpluses that have been difﬁcult to dispose of and maintained artiﬁcially high
prices for consumers;
•
encouraged productivity at the cost of environmental sustainability (although now much less
so);
•
encouraged a fall in employment in agriculture and through that contributed to rural outmigration and its social consequences;
•
beneﬁted large holdings and fostered economic dependency among small producers;
•
disproportionately beneﬁted producers in northern Europe and increased intensity and
specialisation;
•
disadvantaged developing countries that have had less access to EU markets;
•
contributed to territorial disparities across the EU.
The actual impacts vary considerably from place to place. There are extensive Commissionled evaluations of aspects of agriculture and rural development policy available on the DG
Agriculture and Rural Development website under ‘evaluation’ (see also CEC 2005e).
Source: based on CEC (2001j); Nugent (2006); Shucksmith et al. (2005).

## S. 349

being external to it’ (EEA 2006b: 18). But the operation of agriculture (and thus the character
of rural areas) is now less tied to local concerns than to the demands of international actors.
This is described as the ‘disembedding of agriculture from its regional and local context’ (Gallent
et al. 2008: 108). Power over agriculture is increasingly concentrated in few hands, particularly
food retailers: ‘in 2003 there were approximately 110 supermarket buying–in desks in the EU
that mediated the ﬂow of produce from Europe’s 3.2 million farmers to its 160 million
consumers . . .’ (Gallent et al. 2008: 109, drawing on Grievink 2003). The effect of the
loosening relations between agriculture and its locality is accentuated by the CAP and its
‘productivist’ or industrial model of agriculture, which has encouraged more intensive farming
of land, extensive use of pesticides, artiﬁcial fertilisers and, in much of Europe, irrigation.
The CAP has tended to reinforce intensification trends that have caused widespread
environmental damage including water pollution, soil contamination, erosion, desertiﬁcation,
loss of habitat and biodiversity, and irrevocable changes to the rural landscape. Agriculture also
320
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Box 20.4 Selected environmental issues in agriculture
Air quality and climate change
Agriculture contributed 10.1 per cent of total greenhouse gas emissions in the EU-15 in 2002.
The main greenhouse gases emitted by agriculture are nitrous oxide (N2O) and methane (CH4),
both of which have a far greater global warming potential than carbon dioxide (CO2). Agriculture also consumes fossil fuels for farm operations, thus emitting carbon dioxide. Emissions
of nitrous oxide and methane that arise from the use of mineral nitrogen fertilisers and manure
storage (when manure nitrogen is converted into nitrous oxide) decreased by 8.7 per cent
between 1990 and 2002, due to less use of nitrogenous fertiliser.
Ammonia emissions mainly occur as a result of volatilisation from livestock manure. Within
the EU-15 emissions of ammonia from agriculture decreased by 9 per cent between 1990 and
2002, resulting from a reduction of livestock numbers across Europe (especially cattle) and,
again, the lower use of nitrogenous fertilisers.
Water quantity
Irrigation enables the growth of the agricultural sector in many member states, but it also 
puts pressure on water resources. The depletion of water resources can decrease groundwater 
and river levels, which in turn can inﬂuence aquatic and riparian ecosystems negatively. The
irrigable area in the EU-12 increased from 12.3 million to 13.8 million hectares between 1990
and 2000, which is an increase of 12 per cent. In France, Greece and Spain there was an increase
of 29 per cent. There are large regional variations in water use for irrigation ranging from 1,636
million m3/year in the Sevilla region (south-west Spain) to 0 m3/year in Northern Ireland (the
United Kingdom). During the 1990s, the reported water allocation rates for irrigation decreased
across the EU-15 member states. This indicates a likely reduction in water application rates per
hectare of irrigated land, implying an increase in water efﬁciency, even though agricultural water
consumption remains high in the south.
Source: this is an edited version of text from EEA 2006b: 21–4. The report provides many other examples of the
relationship between agriculture and the environment, and the impacts of trends and policies.
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 350

makes a contribution to climate change and air pollution, and the increasing impact on transport as agricultural products are shipped longer distances is often overlooked (EEA 2006b).
There have also been concerns about consumer safety, the quality of some food products
(especially in the context of foot and mouth disease) and genetically modiﬁed foods. This is not
all as a consequence of the CAP. Economic and social trends would probably have taken
agriculture in this direction in any case, but too often the CAP is seen as facilitating negative
effects. One result is the accusation that the CAP is the source of EU subsidised pollution. Two
examples of the relationship between agriculture and environment are summarised in Box 20.4.
Changes since the late 1990s have started to ameliorate negative consequences by addressing
the externalities of agricultural production. Some measures such as set-aside have had very
positive impacts on biodiversity. But the European Environmental Bureau (EEB 2008: 4) warns
that ‘the CAP is far from an instrument that will inspire more sustainable farming practices’ and
that further liberalisation, market forces and the lifting of quotas could lead to sharp increases
in production with consequent environmental damage. The EEB has developed an agenda for
a Sustainable Common Agricultural Policy (SCAP) including, for example, permanent set-aside,
charges for all water use, and full decoupling of aids from production.
Performance indicators for agri-environmental measures were introduced in 2000 by the
European Commission and the European Environment Agency (EEA), and they conﬁrm that
trends in agriculture are as much driven by external factors as they are by the CAP. The way 
that member states implement (or do not implement) Community policy is crucial (EEA 2006b).
The Commission’s 2007 ‘health check’ (CEC 2007o) is considering the environmental challenges
for agriculture, including the maintenance of the beneﬁts of set-aside (which is being abolished),
bio-energy production, water management and biodiversity. It proposes, among other things,
incentives and more cross-compliance requirements for adaptation to climate change and better
water management.
Territorial impacts
The spatial or territorial impacts of the CAP have been the subject of studies by DG REGIO
(CEC 2001j) and ESPON (ESPON 2004b), particularly the effects of the spatial distribution of
funding. Because price support is directly linked to levels of farm output, ‘the regions and farms
producing more, receive also the bulk of price support’ (CEC 2001j: 5). In 1991 about 80 per
cent of spending went to 20 per cent of farmers (CEC 2001j: 6). Therefore there is a very clear
territorial divide and concentration of price support, with regions in northern Europe (which
tend to have the larger and more efficient units) receiving the most. The analysis also
demonstrated that direct payments (introduced by the 1992 reforms) are more efﬁcient in raising
agricultural incomes than production support; they increased farm incomes by some 18 per cent.
However, the spatial distribution remained the same because direct aids were based on the
subsidies that farmers had received under the previous regime. Also, direct payments cost more
to deliver because of the need to collect more detailed information on farming households.
A more extensive assessment of the territorial impact of the CAP across the EU-15 (ESPON
2004b; Shucksmith et al. 2005) was directly related to questions of balanced development raised
in the ESDP and emerging policy on territorial cohesion. The project was charged primarily with
assessing the extent to which the CAP contributed to EU Cohesion Policy. The general ﬁndings
are much the same as those noted above in terms of the distribution of the CAP and its bias to
more prosperous accessible regions. The authors of the report conclude that:
In aggregate the CAP works against the ESDP objectives of balanced territorial development,
and does not support the objectives of economic and social cohesion. Moreover, in terms of

EU agriculture and rural development policy

## S. 351

polycentricity at the EU level, Pillar 1 of the CAP appears to favour core areas more than it assists
the periphery of Europe, and at a local level CAP favours the more accessible areas. In recent
years the CAP has undergone a series of reforms. Some of these have begun to ameliorate these
conﬂicts of objectives. For example, direct income payments tend to be distributed in a manner
more consistent with cohesion than market prices support. Similarly, higher levels of Pillar 2
payment are associated with more peripheral regions of the EU than is the case with Pillar 1
support. Nevertheless, there is considerable scope for both Member States and the Commission
to make the CAP more consistent with the objectives of the ESDP.
(Shucksmith et al. 2005: 197–8)
The ﬁndings underlying these conclusions, like those of the previous Commission study, are that
the CAP tends to favour larger agricultural units, which in turn tend to be concentrated in the
more prosperous core areas of the EU. The general effect for both pillars at the level of NUTS
3 can be seen in Plate 9. The support for the ﬁrst pillar is concentrated in the northern regions
of the Benelux, Denmark, France, Germany and the UK. The distribution of support can be
compared to other factors explained in Chapter 4, although the researchers report that there is
no significant relationship with the distribution of gross domestic product. It should be
emphasised that there are great variations of prosperity within these regions. The researchers
note that the skewed distribution of CAP funding is unsurprising. The ﬁrst pillar was never
intended to be a cohesion policy, and as noted above, the general territorial and environmental
consequences of the CAP had been known for some time.
The ﬁndings for rural development policy in the second pillar are less conclusive but show
that support is higher in the more peripheral regions. Nevertheless, overall the CAP has ‘uneven
territorial effects across the EU which run counter to cohesion objectives’ (ESPON 2004b: 18).
Rural development policy is still very closely aligned in the minds of policy makers with
agricultural policy, and in the CAP ‘the agro industrial model still dominates’ (Shucksmith 
et al. 2005: 193). The ﬁndings suggest that agri-environmental measures have been successful
in encouraging more environmentally sustainable land management practices and may make an
indirect contribution to social cohesion through income support and the improvement of the
economy of rural areas.
The CAP is not by any means the only factor driving change: ‘there is a longer-term,
underlying process of agricultural restructuring onto which policies are layered’ (Shucksmith 
et al. 2005: 190). EU policies have an important impact, but agriculture is changing in response
to other factors. Policy may slow down this process, or it may speed it up. There is also a need
to be aware of the potential territorial consequences of a reduction of CAP support for rural
areas. For example, Van Ravesteyn and Evers (2004) note in relation to the Netherlands that
a reduction in support for farming could lead to pressures for further increases in productivity
and greater conﬂict between the demands of agriculture and urban development.
Multifunctionality and the future of rural areas
Rural policy rhetoric from the late 1990s has been about moving towards a ‘post-productivist
countryside’, that is, a re-embedding of agriculture in its regional territory. Central to embeddedness is the concept of multifunctionality. Agriculture is inherently multifunctional, it produces
food but also directly or indirectly contributes to the local ecology and social and economic life
of rural communities (Dobbs and Pretty 2004; van der Ploeg et al. 2000). Recognising and
increasing positive multifunctional effects of agriculture are now widely promoted, and
diversiﬁcation of farm activity is one manifestation of this. Nevertheless, most policy and actions
remain directed to economic production – the promotion of other products and services that
322
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 352

complement agricultural activity (Bohman et al. 1999; Piorr et al. 2007). Such actions recognise
the reality of the consumption countryside but continue to place agriculture and its economic
rationale at the centre, allocating other social, environmental and economic goals as subsidiary
aims (OECD 2001, 2007b). Furthermore, the notion of multifunctionality is widely understood
as gaining positive externality effects from primary agricultural production. An alternative view
from a territorial perspective understands multifunctionality as a product of the competing
demands for rural space and opportunities arising from the wider territorial capital or potential
of rural regions beyond farming. The central functions here are the many socio-economic,
environmental and cultural facets of rural territory, providing: space for economic production
(including but not limited to agriculture); ‘living spaces’ through the provision of housing and
other requirements of community; ecological space for ﬂora and fauna; places of consumption
of recreation and leisure and aesthetic appreciation; and spaces that represent cultural identity
and belonging (Brandt and Vejre 2003; Gallent et al. 2008; Tress and Tress, 2003). The CAP
and its implementation by member states is embedded in the notion of agricultural multifunctionality, rather than the more diverse functions of wider rural development (Marsden and
Sonnino 2008).
From this perspective there is a prominent need for a spatial planning approach in the sense
of a coordinating activity that manages the spatial impact of sector-led policies in rural regions.
A territorial perspective also gives more emphasis to the interdependences of urban and rural
areas, and the difﬁculties associated with meeting urban demands for additional non-agricultural
activities that may conﬂict with other rural functions (Gallent et al. 2004; Stead and Nadin
2000). These pressures include urban sprawl, competition for rural housing from commuters,
demands for recreational use of the countryside, the need to supply energy and the disposal of
waste. The future of rural areas hinges on how these challenges are addressed, particularly the
exploitation of the territorial capital of rural regions, environmental sustainability and accessibility. Of these recurring themes two will be paramount in the early 2000s: the ‘demographic
crisis’, with depopulation threatening the survival of some settlements in remote rural regions;
and climate change, with the strong probability of changes in temperature and precipitation
severe enough to require widespread changes to patterns of agriculture and production methods.
These issues cannot be seen in isolation and much will depend on the extent to which integrated
solutions are found. A spatial planning approach can help to consider rural development issues
in an integrated manner.
Conclusion
Great changes have occurred in agriculture and the socio-economic make-up of the European
countryside since the 1950s. This is true for most of the EU, but care is needed in making any
generalisations. To take just two countries, France and the UK have implemented current 
CAP measures in very different ways, reﬂecting their contrasting agricultural and rural economic
geography and long-standing attitudes towards supporting farming (Lowe et al. 2002). 
The conditions in the new member states are different again: agriculture generally plays a more
dominant role in the economy in these countries; incomes are lower; and farming is undergoing rapid change as it is opened up to global forces. EU measures have helped to improve
environmental performance but only slowly (Shucksmith et al. 2005). In the old EU-15
agricultural production is higher, factors of production are used more efﬁciently and in some
areas technical innovations have driven an intensive industrial mode of agriculture. Overall, the
area of land used for productive farming has decreased and so have many rural populations.
Farming has a lower share of national economic activity – less than 3 per cent in many countries
– and a much smaller workforce – below 5 per cent of the working population in most of the

EU agriculture and rural development policy

## S. 353

EU-15. The average income of farmers is now much closer to that of other employment sectors,
but there are far fewer of them – and rural poverty is still common in much of Europe.
In some ways rural territories are much less signiﬁcant than they once were, and many areas
are facing considerable challenges. However, looking ahead they also have advantages. Rural
areas can offer an environment and quality of life that is attractive for more footloose investment
– particularly for SMEs, the self-employed and knowledge-based industries – and they make an
increasing contribution to job creation (Marsden et al. 1993; Nadin et al. 2004). Above all, the
countryside in much of Europe now exhibits a far more diversified range of activities,
encompassing, in particular, more leisure and tourism functions. The role of the countryside has
shifted from primarily production to consumption. Dammers and Keiner (2006: 7–8) sum up
these trends in four categories:
growth of alternative farm activities and production of high quality food; increasing
signiﬁcance of employment in service industries, manufacturing and high technology;
emergence of new users of rural areas (outdoor recreation, tourism); and repopulation of
rural areas, especially by retired people.
These trends have varying effects depending on the type of rural area in question, but the
common theme is the steady turn towards diversiﬁcation of rural economies together with
continued intensiﬁcation and even ‘industrialisation’ of agricultural production. In this context
in the early 1990s Marsden et al. (1993: 3) argued that ‘the study of the countryside has been
almost exclusively restricted to inward-looking analyses of agriculture . . . these analyses have
produced a partial outlook that is of diminishing relevance to advanced capitalist societies’. 
The narrow productivist, agri-industrial understanding of rural areas continues to dominate.
Thus agriculture continues to be disconnected from the social and wider economic context of
the territory on which it takes place. The process of moving from a sectoral CAP to an integrated
rural policy has slowly reinforced the ties between agriculture and rural communities and territories at large, and opens the prospect for the ‘re-territorialisation’ of rural policy, through
regional development programmes emphasising endogenous development and partnerships.
From a rural planning and environmental sustainability point of view, the CAP reforms of the
past decade are moving in the right direction, even if critics argue that they do not go far enough.
Further reading
For an introduction to the CAP see The Common Agricultural Policy Explained by DG
Agriculture and Rural Development (CEC 2007m). The CAP and the Regions by Shucksmith
et al. (2005) is based on ESPON project 2.1.3 The Territorial Impact of the CAP (ESPON
2004b). Both publications provide a thorough assessment of the CAP from a spatial perspective,
including explanations of the main components of agricultural and rural development policy.
Van Ravesteyn and Evers (2004) provide a summary of the CAP and an explanation of how it
has influenced spatial development in the Netherlands. Gallent et al. 2008 provide an
introductory text on Rural Planning that is concerned mainly with the UK, though many
chapters are of more general relevance.
324
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 354

EU environmental policy 
and sustainability
The Amsterdam Treaty establishes that ‘environmental protection requirements must be integrated
into the deﬁnition and implementation of Community Policies and activities in particular with a view
to promoting sustainable development’. The Cardiff Summit in June 1998 endorsed a Commission
communication, which – among other things – identiﬁes energy, transport and agriculture as priority
sectors, on which integration efforts should focus. These sectors are at the same time key sectors
for spatial planning and development . . . In other words, sustainable environment and sustainable
spatial development condition each other, most of the negative environmental impacts having their
origin in territorial imbalances induced by competing and conﬂicting demands on given spatial
potentials.
(CEC 1998a: 19)
Introduction
The activities of the EU in the ﬁeld of environmental policy started in the 1970s and expanded
rapidly. A large part of member state action on the environment stems from EU directives and
regulations. More importantly for the subject matter of this book, EU environmental legislation
was the ﬁrst ﬁeld of EU law that had a direct impact on national planning procedures and
practice, through the 1985 Directive on the ‘Assessment of the effects of certain public and
private projects on the environment’ (Environmental Impact Assessment Directive).
This chapter presents the development of, and rationale for, an EU environmental policy. 
It highlights its key principles and the main stakeholders involved in the production 
and implementation of EU environmental legislation and activities. The breadth of themes and
issues covered by EU environmental action means that it is virtually impossible to talk about
one EU environmental policy (McCormick 2001: 11). The focus in this chapter will therefore
be selectively on those areas of EU policy and law which are of relevance for spatial planning
and territorial development. The chapter ends with a discussion of the relationship between
spatial planning and the EU sustainable development and climate change agendas.
The development of EU environmental policy
The Treaty of Rome (1957) did not refer to ‘environmental policy’, nor did it grant European
institutions the competence to act in this ﬁeld. Its Article 2 referred to ‘a continuous and balanced
expansion’ as a primary objective for the EEC, reﬂecting the post-war consensus about a
sustained, rather than a sustainable growth (Williams 1996: 169). The early priority of the
European integration project was economic, as explained in Chapter 17. The beginning of an
identiﬁable EU environmental policy can be traced to the 1972 summit meeting of the EC Heads
of State and Government in Paris, which led to a declaration on environmental policy and,

Chapter 21

## S. 355

a year later, to the adoption of the ﬁrst European ‘Environmental Action Programme’. The need
for an environmental policy at EU level was initially justiﬁed on economic grounds, as a corollary
to the construction of an integrated common market in which any form of distortion to
competition and trade within the EU would be removed (McCormick 2001), including different
national environmental standards. The harmonisation of environmental regulations that began
at EU level in the 1970s was thus initially legitimised as subservient to trade policy, rather than
grounded in environmental protection objectives per se (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 2).
Between 1972 and 1987 European environmental legislation was thus legally based on two
generic articles of the Treaty of Rome that allowed the EC to act if it was necessary for the
establishment or functioning of the Common Market. These articles did not allow for bold
environmental objectives to be pursued, because legislation based on them required unanimity
in the Council of Ministers and had to be justiﬁed on the basis of trade-related arguments (Knill
and Liefferink 2007: 9). Nevertheless, by the mid-1980s around 200 pieces of environmental
legislation had been passed in the ﬁeld of water, waste, chemical and air pollution control.
Member states with more advanced and restrictive national environmental legislation, such as
Germany, Denmark and the Netherlands, were particularly proactive in pushing for higher
environmental standards at European level.
In the late 1980s the justiﬁcation for further developments in EU environmental policy 
began to shift towards environmental protection objectives per se. From the late 1970s onwards
there had been growing public awareness of the cross-border nature of various forms of
environmental pollution, for example air pollution and acid rain. It became clear that ‘the biggest
producers’ of air pollution were not its ‘biggest victims’ (McCormick 2001: 4). This begged 
for new forms of cooperation between national governments and regulatory agencies across
national borders, in order to overcome the ineffectiveness of isolated action against sources of
pollution. Additionally, there was increasing evidence that the process of EU integration itself
had intensiﬁed pressures on the environment, as illustrated by the environmental impact of the
Common Agricultural Policy (CAP). Through a system of subsidies to production, the CAP
encouraged intensive forms of farming characterised by high chemical input leading to environmental damage (see Chapter 20). Equally, the liberalisation of the transport sector (see Chapter
17) and improvements in trans-European transport networks (Chapter 19), while considerably
improving the mobility of Europeans (in particular through cheaper air travel) have contributed
to a signiﬁcant increase in CO2 emissions (McCormick 2001: 4).
As a result the beneﬁts of a European, rather than a purely national, approach for dealing
with environmental problems became increasingly accepted. In 1987 environmental policy 
was formally enshrined in the Single European Act as an area of EU competence in its own 
right independent of trade policy (Knill and Liefferink 2007). The Maastricht Treaty of 1992
made the adoption of EU environmental policy by the Council subject to qualiﬁed majority
voting (instead of unanimity). The Amsterdam Treaty of 1997 strengthened the role of the European Parliament by making environmental policy subject to the co-decision procedure (see
Chapter 11), and introduced for the ﬁrst time the objective of ‘balanced and sustainable development’ as a key objective of the EU, which is still present in the Treaty of Lisbon that was signed
in 2007.
During the 1990s the areas covered by EU environmental legislation and policies were
gradually expanded from air, water, waste and chemical pollution control to new issues such
as habitat conservation, environmental information, CO2 emissions and climate change.
Paradoxically, after 1987 the ‘increasing legal and institutional “anchoring” of EU environmental policy’ coincided with ‘the stagnation of its political dynamics’ (Knill and Liefferink
2007: 20), leading to a ‘cooling off of the environmental policy boom’ that had marked the
1970–1980s (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 23). It is true to say that the degree of importance of
326

environmental issues on the EU agenda has varied greatly over the past 20 years, depending on
factors such as the general economic context or the ‘green credentials’ of the country holding
the EU presidency. The key milestones in the development of an EU environmental policy are
summarised in Table 21.1. In the early part of the twenty-ﬁrst century, the global challenges
posed by climate change rose to prominence on the EU agenda.

1985
Directive 85/337/EEC on the assessment of the effects of certain public and private
projects on the environment (‘Environmental Impact Assessment Directive’)
1986
Single European Act: gave the EC a formal competence in environmental policy (new 
Title on the environment and principle of integration of environmental objectives into
other EC policies)
1987
World Commission on Environment and Development report Our Common Future 
(the Brundtland report) and emergence of the concept of sustainable development
1990
European Commission Green Paper on the Urban Environment: marking the start of
reﬂection on urban sustainability (see Box 21.2)
1992
Directive 92/43/EEC on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild fauna and ﬂora
(‘Habitats Directive’)
1992
Treaty on the European Union (Maastricht Treaty): ‘sustainable development’ as key 
EU objective and EU environmental policy subject to qualiﬁed majority vote (instead of
unanimity) in the Council
1994
Beginning of the work of the European Environment Agency
1997
Treaty of Amsterdam: expansion of the co-decision procedure to environmental policy,
reinforcing the role of the European Parliament.
1994
Adoption of the Fifth EC Environmental Action Programme Towards Sustainability
(1993–2001)
2000
Directive 2000/60/EC establishing a framework for the Community action in the ﬁeld of
water policy (‘Water Framework Directive’)
2001
Directive 2001/42/EC on the assessment of the effects of certain plans and programmes on
the environment (‘Strategic Environmental Assessment Directive’), effective from 2004
2001
Adoption of the Sixth EU Environmental Action Programme (2002–12)
2003
Directive 2003/87/EC establishing a scheme for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading
(European Emission Trading Scheme, which started in 2005)
2004
Directive 2004/35/CE on environmental liability with regard to the prevention and
remedying of environmental damage (enforcing the ‘polluter pays principle’)
2006
Revised EU Sustainable Development Strategy
Jan 2008
‘Climate action and renewable energy package’: Commission’s proposals to achieve agreed
EU objectives in the ﬁght against climate change up to 2020 and beyond
Source: authors’ own.
