## S. 357

The principles of EU environmental policy
The objectives of EU environmental policy, according to the Treaty of Lisbon (ratiﬁed in 2009),
are preserving, protecting and improving the quality of the environment, protecting human
health, promoting a prudent and rational utilisation of natural resources, and promoting
measures at international level to deal with regional or worldwide environmental problems 
(in particular climate change). The achievement of these objectives is guided by a number of
principles spelled out in the Treaty: the precautionary principle; the principles of action at the
source and of ‘prevention rather than cure’; and the ‘polluter pays principle’. These principles
are not exclusive to EU environmental action but are generally accepted principles of
environmental policy in many countries. The political importance and practical implementation
of these principles has varied over time (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 35; McCormick 2001:
Chapter 3).
Early EU environmental action focused on remedial action against existing pollution and 
on combating environmental damage at the source where it originates (Knill and Liefferink
2007: 15). This principle has underpinned ‘emission-based’ EU environmental legislation, 
for example in relation to exhaust pipe ﬁlters. EU environmental policy is now driven by a
preventive approach to environmental damage – trying to prevent damage before it happens.
The precautionary principle goes further than the prevention principle since it requires, in theory,
that activities that are potentially harmful to the environment be strictly controlled or even
forbidden, even if there is not yet any conclusive scientiﬁc evidence of their harmfulness. Good
examples of the partial application of this principle are the various EU regulations, currently
among the strictest worldwide, controlling the conditions under which a party may develop, use
or market a genetically modiﬁed organism (GMO) or a food product derived from GMOs,
which is a subject of high public controversy in Europe.
The ‘polluter pays principle’ is a widely accepted principle of environmental policy and
international environmental law that was adopted in 1972 by the member countries of the
OECD as a non-binding principle according to which ‘he or she who pollutes the environment
or creates a risk to it shall bear the costs for the prevention, removal of and compensation for
environmental damage’ (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 15; OECD 1975). It is an economic principle: costs that would otherwise be borne by parties other than the polluter are charged to the
polluter who, as a result, ‘internalises’ such costs (see Box 21.1). This is intended to act as an
incentive for the polluter to adopt more environmentally friendly approaches. The ‘polluter pays
principle’ is supposed to be economically efﬁcient, not distorting competition and not requiring
assistance from public funds. The principle has been implemented in various EU member states
through different techniques, such as taxes on the emission of polluting substances. However,
in practice the polluters liable for environmental harm cannot always be identiﬁed easily. This
is, for instance, the case with land that has been contaminated many years ago.
The EU environmental policy-making process
The main actors involved in the production of EU environmental policy are the European
Commission, the Council of Ministers of the Environment, the European Parliament, national
experts and environmental and industrial lobbies (see Chapters 9 and 10). The implementation
of EU environmental policy involves many more actors at national, regional and local levels.
Monitoring and data gathering additionally rely on the activities of national environmental
ministries and agencies as well as the European Environment Agency. The European Commission is the key player in environmental policy formulation (see Chapter 11). The Directorate-
General speciﬁcally in charge of environmental affairs is DG Environment, which has a
328

EU environmental policy and sustainability

Box 21.1 Understanding the concepts of ‘environmental
externalities’ and ‘market failure’ in environmental
economics
The harmful impacts on the environment and on human health caused by the emissions of pollutant
substances and the depletion of natural resources have led economists to address environmental
problems within the framework of neo-classical welfare economics. Classical economic theory contends that in competitive markets, prices drive optimal decision-making by economic agents and
lead to an efﬁcient allocation of resources. In certain cases, however, markets ‘fail’ to do so.
Because it is difﬁcult to attach a monetary price to some environmental goods such as ‘air’ or
‘landscape’, which are perceived to be in common ownership and to exist in unlimited quantity,
individuals and ﬁrms are induced to use more resources than is socially desirable, leading to
resource depletion. This is considered by mainstream economists as a ‘market failure’ and has been
called the ‘tragedy of the commons’ (Hardin 1991). Pollution is considered as an ‘externality’ not
included in a ﬁrm’s production costs or in the consumer’s costs. As long as no measures are taken,
those costs will be shifted to other individuals, other social groups or to future generations. For
neo-classical economists, environmental problems such as pollution and resource depletion can
be solved through the ‘internalisation of externalities’: the price of a good should fully reﬂect its
overall cost of production or consumption including its environmental costs (i.e. use of natural
resources or pollution). In other words, polluters should pay for the environmental consequences
of their activities. Economists advocate market-based instruments (such as taxes and charges)
rather than regulation through standards, because pollution charges are applied equally to all
economic agents for every unit of pollutant produced and are considered to be compatible with
free market competition.
The neo-classical economic approach to environmental problems is based on the assumption
that the resource base of the earth is limitless and that ultimately important natural functions will
be undertaken by man through technological progress. These assumptions have been criticised by
environmental scientists (see, for example, Meadows et al. 1972) and economists from alternative
schools of thought (e.g. ecological economics) who have stressed that certain natural resources
vital to human life and activities are in limited quantities, are not renewable and cannot (so far) be
artiﬁcially created by human technology. The ecological economist Daly (1991: 35) used a powerful
image to illustrate the fact that neo-classical economics deals only with the question of allocation
and efﬁciency, but ignores the primary question of the optimal ‘scale’ of human activity:
the micro allocation problem is analogous to allocating optimally a given amount of weight in
a boat. But once the best relative location of weights has been determined, there is still the
question of the absolute amount of weight that the boat should carry. . . . Optimally loaded
boats will still sink under too much weight – even though they may sink optimally!
Ecological economists thus focus on the question of the ‘limit to growth’, deﬁned by the regenerative or absorptive capacity of the ecosystem (Boulding 1966; Daly 1991), and argue that the
only way to regulate the scale of the economy in relation to the global ecosystem is through the
setting of environmental standards. The total stock of natural capital has to be maintained by
depleting non-renewable resources at a rate corresponding to the creation of renewable substitutes. The use of renewable resources should not exceed their regeneration rate, and waste
emissions should not exceed the renewable assimilative capacity of the earth (Daly 1991).
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 359

permanent staff of 500 ofﬁcials, making it a small DG in comparison to, for example, DG
Agriculture (which has almost double that number of staff). The main role of DG Environment
is to initiate and draft new environmental legislation, and ensure that agreed measures are
implemented by EU member states. DG Environment is often considered less inﬂuential within
the Commission than DG Industry or DG Agriculture, something that raises a major challenge
for the design of new environmental policy:
Many (if not most) environmental problems are products of energy use, transportation
policy, agricultural practices, public investment and infrastructure patterns, and economic
activity in general. Thus, any ‘environmental’ problems need the cooperation of the DGs
concerned with those sectors, which are generally much less sympathetic to environmental
considerations than is DG [Environment].
(Sbragia 2000: 298–9)
Many interest groups constantly seek to inﬂuence EU environmental decision-making processes (see Chapter 10), for example, business groups representing particular industries and
environmental NGOs campaigning for environmental issues, such as the European Environmental Bureau (EEB), an umbrella organisation based in Brussels representing 140 environmental NGOs.
Once new environmental legislation has been drafted by the Commission, the Council of
Ministers of the Environment and the European Parliament will decide upon it through the 
co-decision procedure (see Chapter 11). The European Parliament has usually supported a
‘greener line’ than the Council (Burns 2005) and has pushed for more stringent environmental
regulations, particularly in the areas of food and consumer safety. In the Council most
environmental legislation is taken by qualiﬁed majority voting, with a number of exceptions that
require unanimity because of their ‘sensitive nature’, e.g. provisions of a ﬁscal nature and
measures ‘affecting town and country planning’. Within the Council, the importance of environmental issues on the EU agenda has often been inﬂuenced by ‘environmentally advanced’ member
states such as Germany, the Netherlands or Denmark. The application of the subsidiarity
principle (explained in Chapter 2) to the environmental policy ﬁeld has been widely debated and
contested. In the 1990s, some member states, such as the UK, argued that certain EU environmental directives had gone beyond what could be justiﬁed at the European level. Soil pollution,
for example, was argued to be a matter for national action rather than EU action (unlike water
or air pollution).
Once EU environmental legislation has been passed, it has to be implemented by EU member
states. The role of national and sub-national levels of government in policy implementation is
crucial. The implementation process involves two stages: formal transposition into domestic
legislation, and practical application within each member state (Knill and Liefferink 2007). 
The European Commission can open infringement procedures against member states or private
parties for problems of transposition or compliance, and NGOs and individual citizens 
can report infringements to the Commission. Yet environmental policy is by far the ﬁeld of EU
activity in which the implementation of EU law is the most problematic. There are signiﬁcant
differences in the degree of transposition of, and of compliance to, EU environmental law
between member states (see inter alia Knill and Liefferink 2007: Chapters 7 and 9), with evidence of a widespread ‘implementation deﬁcit’, that is, an incorrect or incomplete transposition,
or the incorrect application of law (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 148–9). The Commission 
lacks adequate resources for a close monitoring of the application of environmental law
(Sbragia 2000) and has on the whole been rather reluctant to use its legal powers to bring 
a member state before the European Court of Justice (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 147–8). 
330
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 360

In practice, the monitoring of implementation failures relies to a considerable extent on complaints by citizens or interest groups. The European Environment Agency, founded in 1994, has
no powers of enforcement, unlike its counterparts in other countries such as the Environmental
Protection Agency in the USA. The Environmental Liability Directive, which took effect in 2007
and is referred to below, might in future support a better enforcement of EU environmental
legislation.
Approaches and instruments in EU environmental policy
National environmental policies have been characterised in terms of typologies of ‘regulatory
approaches’ or ‘paradigms’. The UK and Germany, for instance, have had very different views
on how to achieve environmental protection. While German policy-makers tend to focus on the
uniform application of ‘best available technology’ to reduce pollution emissions, UK policymakers seek to deﬁne levels above which environmental harm becomes signiﬁcant: the objective
is ‘not the prevention of emissions at (almost) any price, but rather the deﬁnition of an optimal,
i.e. most cost-effective, way of managing the environment’ (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 36;
Sbragia 2000). These differences between ‘technology-oriented’ versus ‘cost-oriented’ national
approaches have been reﬂected in EU environmental policy, leading in practice to a combination
of approaches.
Another tension has permeated EU environmental policy: between ‘command and control’
approaches based on the deﬁnition of uniform, binding standards offering little room for
manoeuvre to individual members, and more ﬂexible, ‘context oriented forms of environmental
governance’ (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 38). Initially the EU relied heavily on regulatory
environmental standards to control emissions at the source (through maximum emission levels
for pollutants or the prescriptive use of speciﬁc technology) or to control the concentration of
speciﬁc substances in the environment (Knill and Liefferink 2007). From the 1990s a more
ﬂexible approach aiming at promoting voluntary changes in patterns of producer and consumer
behaviour gained currency, based on self-regulation instruments, voluntary schemes and marketbased economic incentives (Héritier 2002; Jordan et al. 2003, Knill and Lenschow 2000, 2007:
Chapter 8; Lenschow 2005; Sbragia 2000,). Examples are the eco-label and emission trading
schemes described below, although instruments belonging to this approach remain a minority
within the wider range of instruments used by the EU in its environmental policies (Héritier
2002). Contemporary EU environmental policy is consequently a hybrid model of ‘voluntary
cooperation and hierarchical intervention’ (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 55).
The most well-known and visible instrument of EU environmental policy is the environmental
legislation produced by the EU, which is binding on member states. By 2000, 700 environmental
policy measures, including 200 legal acts (directives or regulations), had been passed by the EU
(Knill and Liefferink 2007: 198). But EU environmental policy is not limited to the production
of law. Policy instruments include: policy guidelines, which set priorities for EU action; research
and monitoring of environmental information at the EU level (see Chapter 6); financial
instruments in support of environmental projects; voluntary schemes of environmental action;
active participation in international negotiations and agreements on environmental issues; and
the integration of environmental objectives into other EU sectoral policies.
EU environmental legislation
The bulk of EU environmental law is passed through directives and regulations, with a
preference for directives, or so-called ‘framework directives’, which deﬁne goals or targets but
leave the choice of means to achieve them open for member states. In the ﬁeld of environmental

## S. 361

policy, however, the boundary between directives and regulations has been blurred: while some
directives include very detailed requirements and leave little room for manoeuvre by member
states, some regulations allow for ﬂexibility in implementation (Knill and Liefferink 2007: 46).
EU environmental law has primarily been of a regulatory nature, applied to the ﬁelds of water
pollution, air pollution, waste management, controls of chemicals and noise reduction. It
should be noted that the Treaty gives the option to member states to adopt more stringent
protective measures so long as they are compatible with the Treaty.
Environmental Action Programmes and strategies
The political priorities of the EU in terms of environmental policy have, since 1973, been
expressed in multi-annual ‘Environmental Action Programmes’ (EAPs), which are non-binding
strategic documents containing guidelines for action by EU institutions and member states and
proposals for new EU legislation. The Sixth Environmental Action Programme (CEC 2001f;
Council 2001a) covers the period 2002–12, and identiﬁes four priority areas for EU action:
climate change; nature and biodiversity; environment, health and quality of life; natural
resources and waste. The EU has also produced other non-binding environmental strategies, as
discussed below.
Research and monitoring of environmental information
The EU started to gather information on the state of the European environment in 1985 through
the CORINE project, which gave rise to the establishment of a comprehensive land cover
database using satellite imagery (see Chapter 6). The European Environment Agency, created
in 1994, is now in charge of gathering environmental data in the EU to provide a scientiﬁc basis
for environmental policy-making and monitoring, and produces regular reports on the state of
the environment (EEA 1995, 2007). Under the ‘Framework Programmes for Research’, which
fund European research in all subject areas, many research projects on environmental issues have
been funded.
Financial instruments
In comparison with other policy sectors, only a very small part of the EU budget is speciﬁcally
dedicated to environmental protection. The environment budget line of the EU financial
framework for 2007–13 earmarks EUR 2.1 billion for the environment, more speciﬁcally for
the LIFE+ programme (which funds biodiversity and habitat conservation projects across the
EU). As well as the environment budget line, it should be stressed that the Structural and
Cohesion Funds (Chapter 18) and the agri-environmental programmes (Chapter 20) contribute
to environmental protection and quality in the EU.
Voluntary schemes of environmental action
From the 1990s onwards, a small number of schemes based on the voluntary participation of
speciﬁc target groups have been set up by the EU. The EU ‘Eco-label’ scheme, created in 1992,
seeks to encourage businesses to market products and services that are kinder to the environment, by marking them with a ﬂower-shaped logo. Its success to date has been relatively limited
(682 labels awarded as of October 2008 in the whole of the EU). The EU Eco-Management and
Audit Scheme (EMAS) allows organisations in the public and private sectors to go through a
voluntary audit to evaluate and improve their environmental performance.
332
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 362

EU participation in international negotiations and agreements
The EU as a political entity can negotiate and sign international agreements with third parties
in the ﬁeld of environmental policy. A number of international conventions have been signed
in the areas of water pollution, air pollution and the protection of ﬂora and fauna. The EU has
become a key player in the international negotiations that have taken place under the UN
Framework Convention on Climate Change (the so-called ‘Kyoto negotiations’). In 1997 the EU
Environment Council managed to agree on a common EU position, committing the EU to
reducing its emissions of greenhouse gases by 15 per cent by 2010. In spite of divergent positions
between the member states, the EU has thus gradually begun to speak with one voice in
international negotiations on environmental issues.
The integration of environmental objectives into EU sectoral 
policies
Since the Amsterdam Treaty of 1997 the Commission has elaborated strategies for the
integration of environmental protection requirements into EU policies such as energy, transport
and agriculture, stressing the need for horizontal coordination between various Commission DGs
and vertical coordination between various tiers of government from local to European level
(Lenschow 2002). Yet progress towards the ‘greening of EU policies’ has been very uneven. In
the ﬁeld of agriculture, since the mid-1990s environmental considerations have formed a key
component in the successive reforms of the CAP, which have encouraged more environmentally
friendly farming practices (see Chapter 20). In the ﬁeld of energy policy, in which EU action
has primarily focused on energy security and the integration of European energy markets
(McCormick 2001), new EU measures have been taken to encourage energy efﬁciency and
renewable energy. In the case of EU Cohesion Policy (Chapter 18), projects supported by the
Structural and Cohesion Funds must take into account environmental protection requirements
and be subject to an environmental impact assessment. In spite of these efforts to integrate
environmental considerations into sectoral policy areas, the ‘greening of EU policies’ has been
rather slow and limited. There are many reasons for this: the political priority given to economic
growth, employment and competitiveness, the relatively weak position of DG Environment vis-
à-vis other DGs in the Commission, and the sometimes reluctant attitudes of member states
(Sbragia 2000: 299).
Areas of EU environmental policy of relevance for spatial
planning and territorial development
This section provides an overview of the environmental policy activities of the EU that are of
particular relevance for spatial planning and territorial development. The ESPON project 2.4.1
(ESPON 2006i) analysed the territorial impact of European environmental policy and concluded
that the ﬁelds of air, civil protection and environmental accidents, climate change, nature and
biodiversity, noise, soil, waste and water had the most explicit spatial planning dimension and
territorial relevance (ESPON 2006i: 8). The impact of EU environmental legislation on spatial
planning can be felt in several ways (CEC 1998a): through restrictions on, or changes in, the
use of land arising from nature protection objectives (e.g. the Birds and Habitats Directive);
through a direct inﬂuence on the planning control and development process (e.g. the EIA
Directive); and through inﬂuence on the location of development (e.g. in the case of polluting
industries) or on the necessity of development.

## S. 363

Waste
The mass production of waste has accompanied the increase in production and consumption
that has characterised capitalist economic development in the twentieth century. EU legislation
in this ﬁeld emerged in the mid-1970s. There is as yet no single, uniﬁed ‘EU waste policy’ but
rather a series of measures in the ﬁelds of waste disposal, recycling, transfrontier transport of
waste, location of waste storage, treatment and disposal units, management of landﬁlls, and the
handling of toxic, hazardous and radioactive waste (CEC 2005f; McCormick 2001: Chapter 6).
The EU has gradually become more proactive in encouraging waste prevention, reduction 
and recycling (CEC 2005f), with Commission proposals for a European directive that would
set recycling standards and oblige EU member states to develop national waste prevention
programmes.
Two aspects of EU waste legislation are of particular relevance for spatial planners: the
requirements to produce waste management plans and the Directive on landﬁll. Since Directive
75/442/EEC (Council 1975) member states are required to draw up waste management plans
covering the whole of their territory. The revised Directive 2006/12/EC (Council 2006f) sets out
the general requirements for these waste management plans, which are an important tool for
developing and implementing regional waste management policies based on principles of
proximity and self-sufﬁciency for disposal operations (CEC 1998a). In EU member states, ‘waste
management planning has become a permanent element in public planning efforts’ (CEC
2003e: 5), with national, regional and local waste management plans integrated within the
system of spatial plans and policy (for example, in the UK with the Planning Policy Statement
10: Planning for Waste Management (ODPM 2005)).
The second issue of particular relevance for planners is that of landﬁll. In 2001, 69 per cent
of all European municipal waste was buried in landﬁll, 25 per cent incinerated and only 10 per
cent composted (McCormick 2001: 167). Landﬁll deteriorates the environment through land 
and water contamination by gas, chemicals and heavy metals, and poses very high risks for
human health in the areas concerned. The location of landﬁll sites and the frequent public opposition to such sites are signiﬁcant issues for local planners. The regulation of landﬁll by the EU
was a very contentious issue on the EU legislative agenda in the 1990s. Directive 99/31/EC on
the landﬁll of waste was eventually adopted (Council 1999b). It introduced stringent requirements for the management of different types of landﬁll sites and set up a system of operating
permits for such sites. Existing landﬁll sites may not continue to operate unless they comply 
with the provisions of the Directive. A study of the implementation of the Landﬁll Directive
(Golder Europe 2005) has shown that the quantity of municipal solid waste continues to increase 
across Europe, but that the proportion that goes to landﬁll is in most cases decreasing, although
illegal landﬁlls continue to be a problem in some member states.
Air
Various EU directives aimed at combating air pollution have focused on regulating emissions
at the source (vehicles, large combustion plants and power stations), on controlling fuel quality
and on setting air quality standards (McCormick 2001: Ch. 7). Concentration limits can have
a direct impact on activities in urban or industrial areas. The Netherlands was the only
European country in which EU Directive 1999/30/EC on air quality (Council 1999a) was
integrated into national legislation via the planning system, effectively linking land use planning
and air quality and thereby limiting the possibility of development in areas where air quality
exceeds the limits set by the EU. As this affected areas in 75 per cent of this highly urbanised
country, this is also a good example of the lack of consideration that is often given to the
implications of new EU directives in implementation (Waterhout 2007; see Chapter 23).
334
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 364

The release of greenhouse gases (CO2, SO2 and CFCs) into the atmosphere has become a
global issue. The EU has committed its member states to collectively reduce their emissions of
greenhouse gases under the Kyoto Protocol signed in 1997 (see section on climate change). The
Commission’s strategy is to encourage action in all the economic sectors that produce such gases,
i.e. transport, energy, industry and agriculture. Some of the advocated policy measures, such as
the encouragement of renewable energy use and low-carbon buildings, have significant
implications for spatial planning.
Water
Several EU directives have established quality standards for different types of water: surface and
underground water, fresh and marine waters, bathing and drinking water. These directives have
regulated the discharge of toxic substance into watercourses and have had an impact on the scope
and intensity of economic activity in the areas concerned (e.g. agricultural practices restricted
by the Nitrates Directive). The 2001 Water Framework Directive (Council 2001b) expanded the
scope of water protection to all waters. It has a clear spatial planning dimension as it requires
member states to establish and implement ‘river basin management plans’ based on river
catchment areas vulnerable to pollution. The Water Framework Directive requires the spatial
identiﬁcation of all activities liable to cause pollution or other impacts on water bodies,
contributing to a more holistic approach to water management (Howe and White 2002). In many
countries, this introduces a new spatial scale for planning that cuts across local and regional
administrative boundaries, although some countries such as France had already established water
management strategies at the scale of river basins. In many parts of Europe, such plans have a
clear cross-border (or even transnational) dimension as many European rivers cross national
borders. Since 1950 the International Commission for the Protection of the Rhine has brought
together nine European countries to work on the joint protection of the river, and has acted as
a model for other forms of cross-boundary cooperation focused on river management.
Development may have to be restricted if it causes a deterioration of water quality: in the
Netherlands, since the entire country ‘falls under one of four designated river basin districts,
plans for urban growth may come into conﬂict with objectives of the Water Framework
Directive’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 118). Another crucial aspect of EU water policy
relates to the management of ﬂood risk. The large-scale ﬂooding episodes that affected many
European countries in 2002 and 2005 highlighted the need for coordinated responses at
European level. Directive 2007/60/EC on the assessment and management of flood risks
(Council 2007b) entered into force on 26 November 2007, requiring member states to assess
by 2011 all watercourses and coastlines to determine whether they may be at risk from
ﬂooding, to map the ﬂood risks in these areas by 2013 and to prepare ﬂood risk management
plans by 2015. Such plans will have an obvious spatial planning dimension as they may include
restrictions on, or speciﬁc provisions for, urban development in ﬂood-prone areas.
Chemicals, industrial risks and land contamination
The artiﬁcial manufacturing of chemicals has been a major characteristic of the development of
industrial economies since the 1950s, and more than 100,000 compounds are used in industry
today. Two categories of chemicals are harmful for human health and the environment: toxic
chemicals, which are poisonous to humans and wildlife, and hazardous substances (corrosive,
ﬂammable or reactive) (McCormick 2001). The earliest European chemical legislation dates back
to 1967. Thirty years later, over half of all EU environmental laws are ‘directed at some elements
of the chemical problem’ (McCormick 2001: 155): regulating the use, storage, handling,

## S. 365

packaging and labelling of dangerous chemicals; limiting the release of harmful substances into
the environment; and controlling the shipment and disposal of chemical waste. Following the
Seveso disaster in 1976 (an industrial accident in a chemical manufacturing plant in Italy), the
so-called ‘Seveso Directive’ (82/501/EEC, Council 1982) sought to prevent major industrial
accidents and limit their consequences by imposing land use planning obligations on member
states with regard to the location of chemical plants. The provisions of the Seveso Directive were
strengthened with the ‘Seveso II’ Directive in 1996 (96/82/EC, Council 1996b), which directly
affects land uses around thousands of industrial establishments where dangerous substances 
are handled.
EU legislation on pollution control has, for more than two decades, treated each component
of the environment in isolation, with separate directives for water, air and soil pollution. This
approach was criticised for potentially encouraging pollution ‘to be shifted between different
environmental media rather than ensuring protection of the environment as a whole’ (Gillies
1999: 26). A new form of integrated pollution control was consequently introduced by the
Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control (IPPC) Directive (1996/61/EC, Council 1996c
amended into Directive 2008/1/EC), modelled on the British Integrated Pollution Control Act
of 1990 (Sbragia 2000: 310–311). Directive 96/61 deﬁnes pollution as:
the direct or indirect introduction as a result of human activity, of substances, vibrations,
heat or noise into the air, water or land which may be harmful to human health or the
quality of the environment, resulting in damage to material property, or impair or interfere
with amenities and other legitimate uses of the environment.
(Council 1996c)
The IPPC Directive sets common rules for permitting and controlling industrial installations in
sectors such as energy, mineral extraction, chemical industry, waste processing or food
production. These industries require a permit from competent authorities in the member states,
and are subject to emission ceilings based on ‘Best Available Technology’. About 52,000
installations are covered by the IPPC Directive in the EU. In many countries such permits are
delivered by speciﬁc authorities that are distinct from the authorities involved in the formal land
use planning system.
The contamination of land by pollutants released by industrial and agricultural activities has
been a major problem in many EU member states, particularly in the old manufacturing
regions of western and central Europe. The question of liability for contaminated land is a crucial
issue for all the stakeholders involved in land development and urban regeneration (Williams
1996: 193): developers, investors, land owners and planners. When a piece of land has been
contaminated by harmful substances, who should be liable for the clean-up and decontamination
costs? To what extent do ‘public sector bodies accept risk of liability from past pollution by
industrial processes and enterprises no longer in existence’, and how does a developer deal with
the risk of ‘inheriting liabilities with the acquisition of land’ (Williams 1996: 193)? In many cases
national civil liability regimes cover damages to persons and property, but not to the wider
environment, and vary from one European country to the other. Differences in civil liability
regimes are potentially capable of distorting the free operation of the land development,
insurance and investment markets in the Single Market: it is thus an issue that can be subject
to EU action (Williams 1996: 193). In 2004 the Environmental Liability Directive (2004/35/EC)
was adopted (Council 2004b). The Directive, which became effective in April 2007, implements
the ‘polluter pays principle’ by deﬁning circumstances under which economic operators are liable
for environmental damage (i.e. damage to protected species and habitats, water and soil) and
336

Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 366

by proposing two types of remedial measures: the restoration of the environment to its baseline
condition and land decontamination measures to restore soil quality. The implementation of the
Directive tends to falls within the remit of several agencies, such as nature protection agencies
and (in the case of land contamination) local authorities.
Protection of nature and biodiversity
EU legislation in this domain includes measures to protect natural habitats, ecosystems and biodiversity in response to the pressures of agriculture, industry and urbanisation. The various
directives taken in this ﬁeld have had signiﬁcant spatial planning consequences through the
designation of sensitive areas, which are subject to protection measures and restrictions on land
use and development. The 1979 Directive on the conservation of wild birds (EEC/79/409,
Council 1979) was one of the ﬁrst pieces of EC legislation concerned with nature conservation
rather than pollution (Williams 1996: 192). It requires member states to deﬁne ‘special protection areas’ to preserve the natural habitats of birds. It was followed in 1992 by the Habitats
Directive (EEC/92/43, Council 1992), which requires the protection of a certain number of plant
and animal species and the designation of sites of community importance and special areas of
conservation. Member states are responsible for developing appropriate land use planning
policies to respect the obligations attached to habitat protection under these two directives.
The network of sites designated under the Birds Directive and the Habitats Directive form
the Europe-wide ‘Natura 2000’ ecological network that is shown on Plate 10. It comprises over
26,000 protected areas across the member states covering a total area of around 850,000 km2
(more than 20 per cent of the EU territory). It is the largest network of protected areas in the
world, and constitutes the ﬁrst experience in EU-wide ‘zoning’ and territorial management based
on shared responsibility between member states and the Commission (CEC 1998a). However,
the relative and total amount of land covered by Natura 2000 sites varies hugely from one
member state to another: in Spain, most NUTS3 regions have 20–40 per cent of their territory
covered, while in France, the UK and Germany the coverage is much lower (ESPON 2006i: 24;
see Plate 10).
Natura 2000 is not a system of strict nature reserves where all human activities are excluded.
It cannot be, because, among other reasons, most of the land is privately owned. However, 
the implications of area designation under the EU Birds and Habitat Directives for land use 
and urban development in the areas concerned are signiﬁcant. Once an area is designated as a
natural habitat, major constraints on development apply: ‘permitting development is no longer
a matter of national discretion, but has to be referred to the European Commission’ (van
Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 119). Development is permitted only if a signiﬁcant public interest
exists, and it is subject to compensatory measures to replace natural habitats. In the Netherlands,
for example, the European Commission considered that the planned expansion of the Port of
Rotterdam would affect a designated area of dunes and sandbanks. The project was allowed to
go forward after being granted exception from the provisions of the Habitats Directive on the
grounds that the port was of vital importance to the Dutch economy and was an essential
multimodal node in the TEN-T network (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004), but it had to be
accompanied by environmental mitigation and compensatory measures.
Environmental impact assessment and strategic environmental
assessment
The 1985 Directive on Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) (EEC/85/337, Council 1985)
(amended in 1997 with Directive 97/11) entered into force in July 1988. This is certainly the

## S. 367

piece of EU legislation that has had the most visible impact on national planning to date. The
EIA Directive requires member states to:
adopt all measures necessary to ensure that, before consent is given, projects likely to have
signiﬁcant effects on the environment by virtue, inter alia, of their nature, size or location
are made subject to an assessment with regard to their effects.
(Council 1985)
‘Development consent’ is the generic term used to refer to the authorisation granted by public
authorities before development projects carried out by private developers and public authorities
can go ahead, e.g. planning permission in the UK, permis de construire in France and licencias
urbanísticas in Spain. Only development projects of a certain size and scope are subject to an
EIA. Annex I of the EIA Directive lists projects that require a full assessment (such as chemical
and steel works, oil reﬁneries, power stations, nuclear installations, airports, motorways, highspeed rail links, ports and inland waterways). Annex II deﬁnes other types of projects that are
subject to a simpliﬁed assessment ‘where member states consider that their characteristics so
require’. The Directive deﬁnes the scope of the assessment as well as the information, public
participation and consultation requirements that have to be part of the EIA process.
Incorporation of the EIA Directive into national law has taken various forms. In the UK, for
example, the EIA Directive was incorporated into national law by the Town and Country
Planning (Assessment of Environmental Effects) Regulations 1988, and became a routine procedure in the development control process. A developer must submit, alongside an application
for planning permission, an EIA ‘statement’ (i.e. a report often produced by specialised expert
consultants) that outlines the likely environmental impacts of the project and the proposed
remedial or compensatory actions. It should be stressed that the EIA process does not prevent
a project from obtaining planning permission even if the EIA statement shows that it will damage
the environment, as decision-makers can decide to prioritise particular projects on economic or
social grounds. The EIA Directive thus sets out a procedural obligation, not a prescribed
outcome. For that reason EIA has been criticised by environmental protection groups as a rather
token measure.
In 1996 the European Commission decided to take the concept of EIA one step further and
presented a proposal for an environmental assessment of plans and programmes called strategic
environmental assessment (SEA). The SEA Directive was adopted in 2001 (2001/42/EC, Council
2001c) and came into force in July 2004. The purpose of the SEA Directive is to ensure that
the environmental consequences of certain plans and programmes are identiﬁed and assessed
during the plan preparation process, prior to ﬁnal adoption. The public and environmental
authorities can give their opinion, and results of the consultations are supposed to be taken into
account in the plan-making procedure. The SEA Directive was gradually transposed into
national planning law by the member states. Many different kinds of plans are subject to SEA,
some within the planning system and some belonging to other policy sectors (waste management
or environmental protection plans).
Access to environmental information
The EU Directive on Access to Environmental Information (90/313, Council 1990) gives the
public extensive rights of access to environmental information. Anyone requesting ‘information
on the state of the environment as well as on activities, policies and measures positively or
negatively affecting the environment, both on paper and in electronic form’ (Knill and Liefferink
2007: 130), should be able to obtain it from public authorities without having to justify their
338
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 368

request. The Directive has had a major impact in countries whose administrative cultures did
not allow public access to environmental information (Sbragia 2000: 309), introducing more
transparency and formal access standards. This has beneﬁted environmental pressure and
action groups in their activities.
Environmental sustainability, climate change and spatial planning: 
towards better policy integration?
Critics have sometimes argued that the EU has favoured the sectoral regulation of individual
environmental issues, with few overarching policies dealing with environmental protection in
an integrated way (Knill and Liefferink 2007). Yet various developments over the past decade
have paved the way for more integrated strategies aimed at achieving ‘sustainable development’
and addressing climate change, the two most prominent issues on the EU environmental policy
agenda in the twenty-ﬁrst century. Such integrated strategies potentially give a more explicit role
to spatial planning policies at various scales, and more generally to the coordination of EU and
national sectoral policies.
Urban planning and the EU sustainable development agenda
The objective of ‘sustainable development’ appeared in the EU Treaties with the 1997 Treaty of
Amsterdam. The concept of sustainable development was deﬁned in the 1987 Brundtland Report
Our Common Future as ‘development that meets the need of the present generation without
compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs’ (UNCED 1987). The
1992 United Nations Conference on Environment and Development in Rio, or ‘Earth Summit’,
adopted the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development (UNCED 1992) and the
‘Agenda 21’ programme for sustainable development. The European Commission took these
principles on board in preparing the ﬁrst EU strategy for sustainable development (CEC 2001k),
approved in June 2001 by the European Council in Gothenburg, and revised in 2006 (Council
2006g). Together with the Lisbon Agenda (see Chapter 17), the Gothenburg agenda sets out
high-level objectives for the EU (commonly called the ‘Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda’). The
Gothenburg strategy sets objectives and lists concrete actions to achieve by 2010 in seven key
priority areas: climate change and clean energy; sustainable transport; sustainable consumption
and production; conservation and management of natural resources; public health; social inclusion, demography and migration; global poverty and sustainable development challenges.
It is interesting to note that the emergence of a sustainable development agenda in the European Commission in the 1990s, led largely by DG Environment, remained separate from the
parallel development of the ‘European spatial planning’ and ESDP agenda involving DG
REGIO. This is surprising, considering that there are strong interrelationships between sustainable development and spatial planning. Between 1990 and 1998 DG Environment was
instrumental in stimulating the emergence of an ‘urban sustainability’ agenda that explicitly
included recommendations of relevance to urban planning (Box 21.2).
The Fifth and Sixth EU Environmental Action Programmes (EAPs) both refer to land-use
planning and spatial policies as key instruments to achieve environmental sustainability. This
marks a signiﬁcant shift from previous Action Programmes, which had focused on sectoral
pollution problems. The Fifth EAP (Council 1993) stressed the potential of spatial planning
instruments (regional management plans, integrated transport plans and integrated coastal
management plans) in various types of geographical areas (coastal areas, urban areas and rural
areas) as key instruments to promote the integration of environmental objectives into all policy
sectors. The actions foreseen within the Sixth EAP (Council 2001a) include the preparation

## S. 369

Box 21.2 The ‘urban agenda’ of DG Environment in 
the 1990s
The European Commission’s DG Environment (previously called DG XI) gave the impetus to
an ‘urban sustainability’ agenda at the EU level in the early 1990s, starting with a Green Paper
on the Urban Environment (CEC 1990a), which reviewed the causes of urban environmental
degradation and criticised dominant planning and urban development paradigms based on monofunctionalism, suburban sprawl, reliance on the car, greenﬁeld developments and out-of-town
shopping centres. The Green Paper made urban planning recommendations, for example that
strict zoning should be avoided in favour of mixed uses of urban space. Although urban policies
per se are not a competence of the EU, the Green Paper proposed guidelines for a Community
strategy for the urban environment and listed the existing Community instruments that could
be developed for that purpose: environmental legislation; recommendations and guidelines;
research and training; ﬁnancial assistance through the ERDF; economic and ﬁscal measures; and
an ‘urban impact assessment’ of Community policies. Following the Green Paper, in 1991 an
Expert Group on the Urban Environment was set up, consisting of representatives from national
ministries, independent experts and Commission ofﬁcials. Various pan-European initiatives
emerged: in 1993 the European Sustainable Cities Project and the European Sustainable Cities
and Towns Campaign, in 1994 the Charter of European Sustainable Cities and Towns, to name
but a few. The Sustainable Cities and Towns Campaign led to the publication of the European
Sustainable Cities Report (EU Expert Group on the Urban Environment 1996).
In 1997, the European Commission adopted a communication entitled Towards an Urban
Agenda in the European Union (CEC 1997d), which proposed a strengthened EU ‘urban agenda’
along the following lines:
The intention is not to develop Europe wide urban policies for matters which are best
dealt with at a local or regional scale. However, since it is clear that cities in the European
Union are facing a number of common problems, there are also opportunities at the
European scale to share and facilitate potential solutions. This would not require additional
powers at the European level. Rather, much can be achieved through a more focused
approach using existing instruments at national and Community level and enhanced
cooperation and co-ordination at all levels.
(CEC 1997d: 3)
This was followed in 1998 by a communication entitled Sustainable Urban Development in the
European Union: a Framework for Action (CEC 1998c), which identiﬁed 24 speciﬁc actions and
proposed the creation of an ‘Interservice group’ in the Commission to examine the urban
implications of EU policies. Following the publication of the Sixth EU Environmental Action
Programme, the Commission published an EU Thematic Strategy on the Urban Environment (CEC
2006i), which forms the building block of the EU’s current approach to urban environmental
issues. The European sustainable urban development agenda was revived by the member states
themselves, as the EU ministers responsible for urban development endorsed the Leipzig Charter
on Sustainable European Cities at an informal ministerial meeting in 2007 (see Chapter 18).
For more information see the thematic website of DG Environment on the urban environment: http://ec.europa.eu/
environment/urban/home_en.htm.
340

## S. 370

(following extensive consultation with various stakeholders) of seven ‘thematic strategies’
addressing key environmental issues that require a holistic approach: clean air; soil protection;
pesticides; the marine environment; waste prevention and recycling; natural resources; and the
urban environment. Some of these strategies are particularly relevant for spatial planning
activities at EU, national, regional and local level, in particular the Thematic Strategy on the
Urban Environment (CEC 2006i).
The Thematic Strategy on the Urban Environment aims to promote the adoption of
integrated approaches to urban environmental management by national, regional and local
authorities. While it recognises that according to the subsidiarity principle, local authorities have
the primary responsibility for improving the urban environment (CEC 2006i: 3), the strategy
acknowledges that the urban environment needs action at all levels: regional, national, as well
as European. Yet it is argued that EU action should not take the form of binding EU legislation
– something that was explicitly rejected by most member states and local authorities when the
strategy was prepared ‘given the diversity of urban areas and existing national, regional and local
obligations, and the difficulties linked to establishing common standards on all urban
environment issues’ (CEC 2006i: 4). The strategy argues that the EU can support member states
and local authorities ‘by promoting Europe’s best practices, facilitating their widespread use
throughout Europe and encouraging effective networking and exchange of experiences between
cities’ (CEC 2006i: 3). The Thematic Strategy thus proposes the following measures: production
of guidance on integrated environmental management and sustainable urban transport plans by
the Commission; support for an EU-wide exchange of best practices through networking and
demonstration projects and a network of ‘National Focal Points’ on urban issues (URBACT;
see Chapter 18); training and capacity building for local and regional authorities on urban
management issues funded by the LIFE+ programme; and ﬁnally, drawing on other Community
Support Programmes (e.g. Cohesion Policy and the 7th Framework Programme for Research)
to address the problems facing urban areas.
The EU climate change agenda
Climate change is likely to affect many regions of Europe, in particular coastal zones, mountainous areas, and the Mediterranean and Arctic regions. The European Commission began to
refer to climate change in the mid-1980s with a series of documents on the ‘greenhouse effect’,
i.e. the impact of the release of large quantities of greenhouse gases (carbon dioxide (CO2),
methane, chloroﬂuorocarbons (CFCs), nitrous oxide (NO) and ozone) in the atmosphere. The
United Nations created the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change in 1988. In 1990, 
for the ﬁrst time a joint Council of EU energy and environment ministers managed to reach a
common EU position proposing the stabilisation of CO2 emissions at 1990 levels by the year
2000 and the sharing of the burden of emission reductions between EU member states. Yet it is
only with the Fifth EAP (1993–2000) that climate change was put at the heart of the EU environmental policy agenda. The Commission and member state governments began to ‘assert collective positions in global regime-building’ (Sbragia 2000: 313), making the EU a key stakeholder
in international environmental politics and the negotiations taking place under the United
Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). In 1997, the EU Environment
Council committed the EU to a 15 per cent reduction in CO2, methane and NO emissions by
2010 (from 1990 levels) – a target that was eventually cut down to 8 per cent by 2012 when
the Kyoto Protocol was signed. Each member state will contribute to this effort according to its
relative wealth, with national emission targets ranging from minus 20 per cent for richer
countries to plus 20 per cent for poorer ones. In March 2007, the EU Heads of state and
government made a unilateral, binding agreement to cut greenhouse gases by 20 per cent by 2020

## S. 371

compared to 1990 levels. Reaching these targets for emission reduction will be a challenge, as
this depends on measures taken at national levels in key sectors of the economy that lie outside
the remit of environment ministers (e.g. energy and transport). In 2005, the share of renewable
electricity usage (including hydroelectric power) in Europe was just below 15 per cent.
EU policy on climate change is based on a series of directives promoting energy efﬁciency
and the use of renewable energy, which are complemented by a new ‘emissions trading scheme’
introduced at EU level (Peeters and Deketelaere 2006). The concept of emissions trading introduces a new regulatory philosophy in EU environmental policy (Sbragia 2000). The EU, initially
very sceptical towards emissions trading, became a global leader in the ﬁeld (see Skjaerseth and
Wettestad 2008 for an in-depth analysis of the factors leading to this change). The idea of an
EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) was gradually developed between 1998 and 2001. The
ETS Directive was adopted in 2003 (2003/87/EC, Council 2003d), and the Emissions Trading
System began running on 1 January 2005, becoming ‘the ﬁrst international emissions trading
scheme ever’ (Skjaerseth and Wettestad 2008: 1).
The ETS, which only applies to large industrial installations so far (e.g. power stations or oil
reﬁneries), created a market for carbon emission permits. The system is based on national
allocation plans ﬁxing maximum limits for CO2 emissions within a deﬁned period (Skjaerseth
and Wettestad 2008); the subject of tough political bargaining. Within these set limits, a system
of tradable permits allows companies to buy and sell ‘emission rights’ held in electronic
registries set up by the member states and overseen by a central administrator at EU level. 
The idea behind the ETS, which is based on the concept of the ‘internalisation of environmental
costs’ explained in Box 21.1, is that industrial manufacturers will be forced to improve their
environmental performance and reduce greenhouse gases in order to lower their costs. The ETS
currently covers about 10,000 industrial plants across the EU and covers 40 per cent of the EU’s
total CO2 emissions. Under the current ETS, ﬁrms are allocated a number of free carbon permits.
From 2013 onwards all permits will have to be bought. In 2013–20 the EU member states should
be able to generate about EUR 461 billion from the ETS (BBC News 2008), which could be
invested in carbon capture technology, renewable energy sources and development aid in third
countries.
So far the scheme has been moderately successful, criticised in particular for being based on
national emissions ceilings that do not constrain industrial activity enough (Skjaerseth and
Wettestad 2008). The other major ﬂaw of the current ETS system is that many sectors that are
high contributors to CO2 emissions are not part of it, e.g. transport, housing, agriculture and
waste. There are proposals to take the implementation of the ETS further, for example through
the inclusion of air transport and a single EU-wide cap on emissions, although these are very
controversial and opposed by industrial lobby groups.
The ETS is not the sole instrument for achieving CO2 emission reduction. Policies promoting
the use of renewable energy and biofuels, energy efﬁciency, and low carbon technologies will
also have to play a key role. This is where spatial planning can be important, in providing a
framework for the use of land and for urban development patterns that help to achieve CO2
emission reduction. In some EU countries, such as the UK, the planning system is increasingly
used to promote renewable energy use, for example through incentives or obligations set upon
developers to integrate low carbon technologies in new-build developments. At EU level there
have been recurring proposals by the Commission to introduce an EU-wide ‘energy tax’ on the
use of non-renewable energy and on CO2 emissions, which would have a signiﬁcant impact on
road trafﬁc and air transport; yet such a tax has been consistently opposed by several member
states and various industrial lobbies.
342
Sectoral policies and their territorial effects
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 372

Conclusion
Over the past three decades environmental issues have been given increasing attention on the
EU agenda. While the member states continue to have different environmental ‘policy styles’
(McCormick 2001: 8), the bulk of their environmental policies and legislation now stems 
from EU legislation. In spite of uneven levels of implementation, EU environmental legislation
is in many areas the most stringent in the world and has succeeded in limiting the most harmful
forms of chemical pollution (e.g. in water). Joint action and burden-sharing have encouraged
national governments ‘to take action where otherwise they may have been reluctant’, and
progressive governments have increased the pressure for a more ambitious set of objectives
(McCormick 2001: 2). The 2004 and 2007 enlargements of the EU have led to an expansion
of EU environmental standards to 12 new member states, which had to integrate the environmental acquis communautaire very quickly as a condition for EU accession (see Chapter 12).
This was a difﬁcult process in countries with a legacy of poor environmental regulations and
heavy industrial pollution (VanDeveer and Carmin 2005).
Despite these achievements, the state of the environment in many parts of Europe is
deteriorating, although this varies across sectors and countries. While water quality is now good
overall, there is to date a lack of signiﬁcant progress in the ﬁght against greenhouse gas emissions
and climate change. The integration of environmental protection objectives into EU sectoral
policy has not progressed enough, as the goal of environmental sustainability has remained 
subservient to the goal of economic growth, competitiveness and employment, which has
preoccupied member states’ governments most. It is telling, in that regard, that issues of EU
environmental policy rarely make it to the European Council (the summit meeting of heads of
states and governments) but are primarily decided upon by Council meetings of the ministers of
the environment. Key challenges thus remain: the ‘implementation deﬁcit’, the integration of
environmental policy goals into EU sectoral policies, renewable energy use, and the role of the
EU in global concerted action to address climate change. Spatial planning certainly has a key
role to play in the pursuit of more sustainable, low carbon forms of human settlements in Europe,
by encouraging more compact urban models based on mixed uses, public transport, ecological
building and design standards.
Further reading
The most concise introductions to EU environmental policy are McCormick 2001, Jordan 2005
and Knill and Liefferink 2007. The key reference website covering all aspects of EU
environmental policy in great depth is the website of the European Commission’s DG Environment, which acts as a gateway to all legislation, policies and initiatives taken by the EU in all
ﬁelds of environmental action: http://ec.europa.eu/environment/index_en.htm. The website of the
European Environment Agency is an invaluable source of publications, data, maps and reports
of relevance for spatial planners interested in environmental issues: www.eea.europa.eu.

## S. 374

Towards new forms of
territorial governance?
Part VI brings together the key elements of the previous parts of the book to discuss in depth
how governance arrangements and the organisation of spatial planning in nation-states, regions
and local authorities have changed as a consequence of their engagement with the EU and other
European partners, and under the inﬂuence of the EU activities described in previous sections.
Chapter 22 analyses the effects that the practices of European territorial cooperation described
in Chapter 16 have had on planning in the member states, their regions and cities, and discusses
whether changes to domestic planning practices or policies, a re-scaling of planning strategies
and the convergence of planning policies and systems across Europe have been witnessed as a
result of territorial cooperation. Chapter 23 focuses on the combined impacts on the domestic
planning systems of EU member states of all the types of European activities described in 
Parts IV and V. It provides a synthetic discussion of the channels of Europeanisation of spatial
planning illustrated by three country-speciﬁc case studies (the UK, the Netherlands and Poland).
In Chapter 24, the evolution of ‘planning cultures’ in Europe in the context of Europeanisation
processes is discussed, as well as the changes in the planning professions and planning education
that have arisen as a result of territorial cooperation and European integration processes. The
book concludes, in Chapter 25, with a critical reﬂection on experiences in European spatial
planning to date, before considering future perspectives on European spatial planning as
‘practice’ and as a ‘ﬁeld of research’. It re-emphasises the need for a European dimension to
spatial planning and for a ‘spatialisation’ of European policy.

Part VI

## S. 375

The relevance of European
transboundary cooperation 
for spatial planning
On the whole, the available evidence seems to suggest the following tentative conclusion. First, crossborder and transnational programmes have often helped to overcome institutional inertia, mobilising
ﬁnancial resources, stimulating economic, social and cultural exchanges and new relationships.
Second, they have facilitated policy transfer and institutional adaptation through networks. . . .
Territorial cooperation has encouraged new public perceptions of regions, and the creation of new
‘regional identities’, institutions and cross-border governance systems: in some cases, local
authorities have entered a ﬁeld previously reserved for central state actors.
(Barca 2009: 97–8)
Introduction
The generic concept of ‘transboundary cooperation’ refers to forms of cooperation across
national borders between cities, regions, national governments and other agencies or
organisations at various geographical scales to pursue policy objectives and projects in ﬁelds as
varied as transport, cultural and economic cooperation, environmental management, and so on.
Since 1999 the term trans-European cooperation refers specifically to the three strands 
of cooperation programmes (cross-border, transnational and interregional) funded by EU
Cohesion Policy, also known as INTERREG (introduced in Chapter 16). Since the INTERREG
Community Initiative was created in 1990 to support cross-border cooperation, the ﬁnancial
support for trans-European cooperation has steadily expanded. In particular there has been a
signiﬁcant growth in cooperation on spatial development issues and spatial planning across
national borders under the transnational strand of the INTERREG programmes (IIC, later IIIB
and IVB), which now involve networks of thousands of professionals (Dühr and Nadin 2007).
Additionally, there have been increasing ﬂows and exchanges of knowledge within transnational
expert networks of urban or regional professionals (De Jong and Edelenbos 2007), and through
numerous networks such as Eurocities (see Chapter 10). As a result, many local and regional
authorities across the EU are now routinely involved in some forms of cooperation across
national borders and have become increasingly exposed to planning practices from other
countries through international networks (Dühr and Nadin 2007).
Given the ﬁnancial support available and the number of actors involved, it may therefore be
reasonable to expect that the various forms of EU-funded transboundary cooperation have had
some effect on the agenda for planning and spatial development policies at the national,
regional and local levels. INTERREG has been justiﬁed on the grounds that cooperation will
bring beneﬁts to the cities and regions involved by allowing them to tackle issues that require
coherent management across national borders more effectively, to address ‘border effects’ and
the ‘costs of non-coordination’ of the spatial impacts of sector policies (Robert et al. 2001) or
to overcome the disadvantages of being located in a peripheral region through cooperation with
Chapter 22
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 376

European partners. It has been argued, however, that the reality of achievements through
transnational territorial cooperation may not have matched these ambitious expectations, and
that the results and impacts of such forms of cooperation have been uneven across Europe (Dühr
and Nadin 2007).
In this chapter the impacts of EU-funded territorial cooperation on domestic spatial planning
practices and policies are discussed.1 The main focus is on EU-funded transnational cooperation
programmes, as between 1997 and 2006 these programmes were most explicitly focused on
spatial planning and spatial development (although some reference is also made to cross-border
cooperation in this chapter). Three possible types of effects of transnational cooperation on
spatial planning systems, policies and practices can be identiﬁed (Colomb 2007; Dühr and Nadin
2007):
•
changes to domestic planning practices or policies as a result of transnational cooperation;
•
a re-scaling of planning strategies to take account of supranational spatial development
trends; and
•
the convergence of planning policies or systems across Europe in response to international
inﬂuences and/or cooperation across national borders.
These three types of effects are considered in turn, following a discussion of the conceptual and
analytical frameworks that can be used to understand them.
Why do actors cooperate across borders and what is the 
added value of cooperation?
There are many reasons why organisations from the public, non-proﬁt and private sector become
involved in cooperation across national borders. The early literature on European inter-city
networks has shown that the rationale for local authorities to participate in such international
networks varied from the hope of enhanced access to EU ﬁnancial resources to the search for
a stronger inﬂuence over EU policy and for direct engagement with EU institutions bypassing
national policy channels (Camhis and Fox 1993; Marshall 2005; Phelps et al. 2002; Ward 
and Williams 1997) (see Chapter 10). Likewise, different motivations can be identiﬁed as to why
actors and their organisations become involved in INTERREG cooperation projects. For some,
INTERREG programmes have been perceived in an opportunistic way as an alternative source
of European funding to be tapped into, and the cross-border, transnational or interregional
dimension of the programmes was not the main incentive. For many partners engaged in European cooperation projects, however, INTERREG has provided a real opportunity to address
issues that require, or at least beneﬁt from, cooperation with other actors outside their own
member state.
Various types of spatial development issues are addressed through territorial cooperation. 
The most signiﬁcant are cross-border or transnational issues that affect an area cutting across
national and regional borders, such as water and air pollution or transboundary transport infrastructure. These call for new forms of cooperation, for example cooperation in transnational
river basins (such as the Rhine) to improve the planning of land uses in ﬂood-prone areas and
jointly manage ﬂood risk. However, in practice cooperation on spatial development issues that
cut across borders is less in evidence than exchanges of experience on issues that do not have
a direct transnational impact (see Chapter 2). Arguably most participants in transnational
cooperation are simply seeking comparative ideas about how problems within their own
jurisdictions could be improved – particularly in sectors where there are a limited number of
expert organisations within individual countries – and wishing to demonstrate and raise

## S. 377

awareness of their own practice (Benz and Fürst 2002; Dühr and Nadin 2007; Millar 2005).
Such exchange can be beneficial on common issues such as urban regeneration, heritage
protection and urban transport, through sharing and transfer of knowledge and good practice,
and the joint realisation of common pilot projects. The diversity of issues addressed by European transnational cooperation projects is matched by the variety of project partnerships, in
terms of geographical spread and types of actors involved. Figure 22.1 illustrates the various
geographical conﬁgurations of cooperation structures commonly found in INTERREG transnational cooperation projects – the level of intensity of cooperation is assumed to increase
towards the bottom of the diagram.
Political scientists have traditionally analysed cross-national exchanges and forms of
cooperation under the label of ‘policy transfer’, a concept that refers to the process whereby
‘knowledge about policies, administrative arrangements, institutions and ideas in one political
setting (past or present) is used in the development of policies, administrative arrangements,
institutions and ideas in another political setting’ (Dolowitz and Marsh 2000). International
policy transfers have in recent times been facilitated by the development of information and
communication technologies and global mobility, and encouraged by international organisations
(e.g. the World Bank or the EU), global consultancies, international policy networks and other
‘policy entrepreneurs’ (Dolowitz and Marsh 2000). However, until now the processes at stake
within INTERREG programmes have not ﬁtted easily into the traditional concept of ‘policy
348

Projects aiming at the sharing of experience, gathering together partners
undergoing similar problems (e.g. other metropolitan areas), or working with the
same issues (implicitly: partners which do not necessarily share geographical
contiguity or functional relationships).
Unbalanced cooperation
Any project in which the great majority of partners belong to the same country.

Unbalanced
Add-on projects
Well-established national cooperation structures cooperating with one
another on transnational projects, implying the need to adapt national forms of
interaction to a new structure.
‘Add-on’
Axial cooperation

numerous possible aims (such as infrastructure development, tourism
development, flood protection, water quality preservation etc.).
Axial
Transnational regional cooperation
Projects based on an existing or emerging (transnational) functional region, or
on a localised transnational cluster of enterprises – usually characterised by
the relative spatial proximity of the partners.
Regional

## S. 378

transfer’ (Colomb 2007), because they involve voluntary, multilateral processes of cooperation,
with exchange of experience taking place over a period of several years between various types
of actors mainly at sub-central levels of government. The processes at stake in European
territorial cooperation projects could therefore more aptly be described as ‘lesson-drawing’
(Dolowitz and Marsh 2000; Rose 1993), that is, actors voluntarily engage in an active search
for new ideas and partners as response to a problem, but do not necessarily change their policies
and practices as a result, as discussed below.
Over recent years, the ‘added value’ of transnational cooperation has been increasingly
conceptualised in terms of ‘learning’ that can help to change mindsets and raise cultural
awareness, and perhaps may even prompt policy innovations and institutional adaptation in the
participating organisations. This shift in understanding was prompted by the recognition that
despite obvious benefits of cooperation for many of the participating organisations, the
comparatively small INTERREG funds (in comparison to ‘mainstream’ programmes) rarely
translated into quantiﬁable socio-economic effects (such as better accessibility of a border region
through transport infrastructure). Rather, INTERREG funding was predominantly used for
‘soft’ measures, intended to help overcoming legal, administrative and institutional differences
across Europe that present barriers to more institutionalised forms of cooperation. This was
particularly the case for the transnational and interregional strands of INTERREG III, where
funding was considerably more limited than for many of the cross-border programmes. It is now
frequently assumed that the potential impacts of European territorial cooperation, be they
materialised through changes in practices, in policies or in strategies, will result from a process
of organisational, social or policy learning between the actors involved.
Böhme et al. (2003) identiﬁed several types of learning that can take place in INTERREG
projects:
•
individual learning, which allows the project partners to get to know each other and individually learn through the cooperation experience;
•
intra-organisational learning, with experiences from the cooperation project being disseminated and used within the wider organisations of the project partners, and
•
inter-organisational learning at the regional, national or transnational scale, which implies
that experiences and knowledge gained through the cooperation project are used outside
the partner organisation, e.g. by involving other regional actors.
Individuals involved in cooperation projects and networks ﬁrst learn from other individuals with
whom they interact in such projects. Individual learning may give rise to organisational learning
if ‘individuals acting on behalf of an organisation and interacting with others in the organisation
learn in such a way that the beliefs, attitudes, or values of relevant organisational members
change and . . . organisation behaviour changes’ (Wolman and Page 2002: 478). Organisational
learning has to be manifested through changes in routines, practices and policies (Böhme et al.
2003). Various factors can provide an incentive or disincentive to learning in institutions, for
example ‘routines, standard operating procedures, the organisational culture, and the human
resources of an organisation’ (van Bueren et al. 2002: 266). Transnational learning arises when
partners from different countries learn from each other, when knowledge gets transferred from
one country to the other and/or when new knowledge gets generated jointly.
In transboundary projects such as those funded by INTERREG, the management and
patterns of communication between partners determine the quality of cooperation. Additionally,
the type of spatial issues dealt with and the geography of the project partnership will probably
inﬂuence the intensity of cooperation and the potential scope of learning between partners
(Colomb 2007; Lähteenmäki-Smith and Dubois 2006). In projects that focus on truly
