## S. 379

‘transnational’ issues, such as transnational river basin management, the learning process is likely
to be focused on ﬁnding joint solutions that affect the transnational project area as a whole,
whereas in projects focusing on ‘common issues’ (for example, methods of urban regeneration),
learning may be more often concentrated on each partner’s own region (Böhme et al. 2003;
Lähteenmäki-Smith and Dubois 2006). Spatial closeness between partners, similarities between
institutional structures, between spatial patterns and development challenges are therefore
crucial ingredients in the development of a productive cooperation partnership (ESPON 2006f;
Lähteenmäki-Smith and Dubois 2006). As a consequence, different degrees of ‘intensity’ of
transnational cooperation, and potentially of transnational learning, have been identiﬁed,
ranging from mere exchanges of experience and know-how to the pooling of resources to tackle
a common problem or to the joint realisation of a transnational investment or transnational
spatial strategy (Colomb 2007).
For policy evaluators, politicians and researchers interested in understanding the impact of
transboundary cooperation programmes, determining a causal relationship between learning and
policy outcomes or policy change is not easy. To what extent, for example, does the process of
transnational learning contribute to the achievement of speciﬁc spatial policy objectives such as
polycentric development or territorial cohesion, or to improved spatial and territorial development policies? Transnational cooperation does increase opportunities for policy learning, but
this does not necessarily translate directly (or even indirectly) into actual transfer, let alone into
identiﬁable policy impacts. Transnational territorial cooperation has developed very much as
an exercise of ‘learning by doing’ without much attention given, until recently, to the added value
of EU funding to such cooperation networks. The frameworks and approaches currently used
by the European Commission to monitor and evaluate European territorial cooperation programmes have in recent years been increasingly criticised for not being suitable to capture the
nature and scope of ‘soft’ learning processes at stake in transboundary cooperation projects
(Colomb 2007) and the policy and institutional changes that may arise as a result. Like the mainstream Structural Fund programmes (see Chapter 18), INTERREG programmes are mainly
required to report on quantiﬁable outputs and results, such as infrastructure investments or the
creation of jobs (Mairate 2006). Evaluation methods based on direct outputs will not capture
and assess the form and extent of learning outcomes (Colomb 2007).
Approaches and methods drawn from the sociological literature on social and policy learning
(Hall 1993; Jenkins-Smith and Sabatier 1993; Wolman and Page 2002), organisational sociology
as well the sociology of innovation (Halpern 2005) have been considered to be particularly useful
in examining the link between learning and policy change (Colomb 2007). The political scientist
P. Hall (1993: 278) has conceptualised policy change as ‘social learning’, deﬁned as ‘a deliberate
attempt to adjust the goals and techniques of policy in response to past experience and new
information’. Social learning might occur at different ‘levels and settings’ of policy, from a mere
change in ‘instrument settings’ in the light of new knowledge to changes in policy instruments
and settings or even to ‘paradigm shifts’, involving wholesale changes in instruments and
hierarchy of goals (Hall 1993). Learning can therefore be of different ‘orders’, as argued by Stone
(2004): shallow, tactical or instrumental learning as opposed to deeper social or policy learning.
Sociological approaches can help researchers analyse how policy-makers, ofﬁcials, civil servants
and other professionals may improve their knowledge through networks and cooperation with
other actors, as well as understand how this will inﬂuence policy formation and implementation
(Evans and Davies 1999; James and Lodge 2003). De Jong and Edelenbos (2007: 702), for
example, in their analysis of a transnational expert community engaged in multilateral learning
in the ﬁeld of area-based urban regeneration, sought to make explicit ‘what planning concepts
gain predominance in the debate in the course of time and what patterns of social interaction
cause the changes’.
350
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 380

The effects of European territorial cooperation on planning 
practices, policies and systems
There is as yet no comprehensive assessment offering compelling evidence of the impacts of
territorial cooperation on planning practices and policies across Europe, or even on individual
member states and regions, so the conclusions presented here are far from deﬁnitive. Although
transnational cooperation has been supported by the EU institutions for more than a decade,
there have been comparatively few analyses of its effects (with the exception of ofﬁcial programme evaluations, ESPON 2005a and reports published by the German Bundesamt für
Bauwesen und Raumordnung – see Görmar et al. 2005; Huebner and Stellfeldt-Koch 2009;
Müller et al. 2005). This can be partly explained by the difﬁculties that arise when trying to ﬁnd
ways of evaluating outcomes. Conventional, indicator-led evaluation approaches used in the
mainstream Structural Fund programmes fail to grasp the degree and intensity of cooperation
processes between different stakeholders across national borders, and the qualitative learning
and policy change that may arise as a result in the long term (Colomb 2007; ESPON 2005a;
Taylor et al. 2004). More qualitative evaluation methods have been proposed (Böhme et al.
2003; Colomb 2007), although the identiﬁcation of causal links between transnational cooperation and subsequent changes in practices and policies remains a difﬁcult and elusive task.
From transnational learning to changes in planning policies and 
practices
INTERREG IIC and IIIB programmes were foreseen to be the instrument through which the
key policy objectives of the ESDP (CSD 1999) would be applied (see Chapter 16). A small
number of studies have investigated whether INTERREG programmes have led to the diffusion and implementation of spatial planning concepts from the ESDP to plans and policies 
at national, regional and local levels. Researchers have analysed the themes and concepts 
mentioned in the strategic documents framing INTERREG IIIB programmes (Pedrazzini 2005;
Waterhout and Stead 2007) or used in project applications (Dabinett 2006). Waterhout and
Stead (2007) examined the extent to which the ESDP inﬂuenced the 13 INTERREG IIIB
cooperation areas, by looking at the preparation of the guidelines by the European Commission,
the preparation of strategic documents framing cooperation themes in each INTERREG IIIB
programme, and the selection of INTERREG IIIB project applications. They conclude that ESDP
concepts such as polycentricity and urban-rural relationships have been taken on board in the
INTERREG III guidelines published by the Commission (CEC 2000i) (which set out the general
objectives, eligibility criteria and thematic priorities for all programmes) and in all 13
INTERREG IIIB programme documents. However, this textual or rhetorical ‘conformance’ was
not matched by ‘performance’ in the project applications submitted and selected in INTERREG
IIIB programmes. In other words, while projects were required to comply with the ESDP ‘on
paper’, in order to ensure that the project would be granted funding, references to the ESDP in
project applications were vague and often not translated into concrete realisations in projects.
Other studies have shown that transnational cooperation projects have increased project
partners’ awareness and knowledge of speciﬁc spatial policy concepts from the ESDP (such as
‘polycentricity’) and inﬂuenced the way in which participants view their neighbours and the
position of their region in the wider European context (Dabinett 2006; ESPON 2005a;
Lähteenmäki-Smith and Dubois 2006; LRDP 2003). However, only rarely have ideas about
polycentricity been implemented or translated into actual planning policies in partner cities or
regions (ESPON 2005a).
Transnational cooperation does not necessarily lead to changes in spatial development
policies, nor, by extension, to changes in territorial development patterns. The ﬁrst generation

## S. 381

of transnational programmes (INTERREG IIC) were widely accepted as having been experimental, and indeed they achieved few concrete results, besides improving mutual knowledge and
creating a common understanding of spatial planning issues (Pedrazzini 2005). Most projects
involved local or sub-regional authorities, which shared experiences on common issues of local
character. Only a small number of projects displayed a strategic dimension and aimed to develop
transnational spatial visions, transnational corridors or transnational ﬂood protection initiatives
(LRDP 2003). For INTERREG III programmes (2000–06) the European Commission called for
more concrete, ambitious cooperation projects that would lead to clear territorial impacts (CEC
2000i). Yet again few projects dealt with themes of a ‘true’ transnational character (Dühr and
Nadin 2007; ESPON 2006f), with the majority of projects focusing on exchanges of experiences
and pilot investments around ‘common issues’. It should be stressed, however, that the outcomes
and impacts of cooperation have differed from one INTERREG IIIB programme cooperation
area to another (ESPON 2006f), with certain areas displaying a much higher ‘intensity’ of
cooperation (e.g. the Baltic Sea area) than others.
There is some evidence of changes arising within partner organisations’ ‘policy styles’ as a
result of participation in transnational cooperation projects (Lähteenmäki-Smith and Böhme
2004). Cooperation may also generate new understanding of certain concepts such as polycentricity, which sometimes (albeit rarely) may be translated into operational decisions regarding
spatial structure (Lähteenmäki-Smith and Böhme 2004; Lähteenmäki-Smith and Dubois 2006).
Other recorded effects are changes in practices and methods of ‘doing things’ (e.g. new
approaches to urban regeneration); changes in ways of conceptualising policies (different scales
or new integrated concepts) (Böhme et al. 2004; Janin Rivolin and Faludi 2005; Pedrazzini 2005;
Zaucha and Matczak 2007); and changes in the nature of the partnerships set up with other
actors (e.g. involvement of new types of local actors as a result of INTERREG requirements).
Such changes seem to have been particularly noticeable in the context of southern European
countries, where alongside the ESDP, European initiatives such as INTERREG and URBAN
have generated pressures for change in domestic spatial planning practices and urban policies,
in terms of approaches, paradigms and institutional capacity (Giannakourou 2005; Janin
Rivolin 2003; Pedrazzini 2005) (see Italian case study in Chapter 23). In the context of the new
member states, Jancˇicˇ (2005) for Slovenia, and Zaucha and Szydarowski (2005) and Zaucha
(2007) for Poland have shown that transnational cooperation has clearly inﬂuenced national
planners and planning guidelines in the two countries, although in Poland interest in such forms
of cooperation has recently been weakening (Zaucha 2007).
The 2009 report prepared by the German Bundesamt für Bauwesen und Raumordnung,
based on the analysis of around 500 INTERREG transnational projects (Huebner and Stellfeldt-
Koch 2009), identiﬁed four types of impact: mobilisation of ﬁnancial resources; innovation in
the ﬁelds of brands, standards and procedures; quality management; and the development of
regional governance. The report emphasised the ‘enabling’ character of INTERREG funding in
overcoming administrative or political obstacles, and valued ‘intercultural competences’ as
important beneﬁts of INTERREG. As regards follow-on investments, the report concluded that
‘small-scale investments ameliorated by INTERREG lead in the long term to a greater beneﬁt
derived from the money invested through the effect chain of consumption, income and
investment’ (Huebner and Stellfeldt-Koch 2009: 31). Yet such impacts are likely to occur on a
medium- to long-term basis only, or as ‘marginal elements within more general policy trends’
(Böhme et al. 2003: 30). They are thus difﬁcult to separate from other possible sources of inﬂuence, such as reforms launched by national governments or other EU policies and programmes.
Quite often transnational cooperation projects act as a source of creativity and inspiration from
which individual partners can draw at a later point in time to respond to challenges faced by
their organisation (Dabinett 2006).
352
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 382

The ﬁrst generation of cross-border programmes (the INTERREG A strand) did not achieve
signiﬁcant tangible results in many cases. Rather, reportedly many projects concentrated on
exchanges of experiences and ‘getting to know each other’, rather than developing common
products (LRDP 2003). Cooperation structures need time to mature and for trust to evolve. In
cross-border areas where intensive patterns of cooperation were already established in the 1970s
and 1980s before the existence of INTERREG programmes (as, for example, along the Dutch-
German or Belgian-Dutch-German borders), many concrete projects and activities with a visible
impact on the territorial development of the area concerned were funded. Cooperation between
cultural, educational and health services in cross-border ‘Euroregions’, for example in the
‘Grande Région Saar-Lor-Lux+’, have expanded the opportunities for residents to beneﬁt from
services across national borders and for businesses to develop their activities. At the Franco-
Belgian border, the metropolitan authorities of Lille Métropole, Tournai, Mouscron, Kortrijk,
Leper and Roeselare have cooperated since 1991 in the ﬁelds of cross-border public transport,
water resources management, economic development, spatial data and spatial planning studies.
In 1998, a non-binding cross-border spatial planning strategy was published, and in 2008 a
European grouping of territorial cooperation (EGTC) ‘Lille-Kortrijk-Tournai’ was set up (see
Chapter 16). These examples tend to be exceptions, as there is to date no obvious evidence of
signiﬁcant changes in planning policy or practice across Europe that could be directly accounted
to INTERREG projects. Where there are effects, they are in the ﬁrst and second categories
deﬁned by Hall (1993), i.e. changes in ‘instrument settings’ and/or in policy instruments in the
light of new knowledge.
Towards a rescaling of planning practices and perspectives?
The arguments for and against spatial planning summarised in Chapter 2 emphasised the
importance of ‘transnationality’ arising from the changing spatial reality of socio-economic and
environmental relationships in an integrated Europe. This has prompted an expectation that
alongside mutual learning such cooperation should, to some degree, lead to a rescaling of the
planning perspective in the sense of a reformulation of the spatial planning agenda (at regional
and national levels) to account for the transnational dimension and the reality of socio-spatial
relations and enhanced awareness of connectedness across national boundaries. Transnational
cooperation should ideally shape the attention of actors and agencies to the transnational scale.
However, the empirical evidence available on INTERREG cooperation has so far done little to
suggest that a transnational rescaling of planning perspectives may have indeed taken place.
There are few examples of the development of joint transnational planning strategies, or spatial
visions that could have helped to raise awareness for the transnational dimension of spatial
development (see Chapter 15). The beneﬁts of cooperating across administrative boundaries are
evident, for instance in the areas of coastal zone management and the management of transnational river basins and interregional transport infrastructure. Few projects, however, have to
date responded to such issues, and instead frequently focus on issues of common interest rather
than those that are of truly transnational concern.
Dühr and Nadin (2007) investigated the extent of a rescaling of planning perspectives in the
participating countries and regions of the INTERREG IIIB North-West Europe programme (one
of 13 transnational cooperation areas in the 2000–06 funding period, which covered all or parts
of Belgium, France, Germany, Ireland, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and Switzerland). On the
basis of an analysis of the relevant spatial planning and regional economic development
strategies in the countries and regions covered by the programme and many interviews with
stakeholders, they concluded that at the end of the programming period (2000–06) there was
little evidence of an increased ‘awareness’ of the transnational dimension to spatial development

## S. 383

in north-west Europe. There were few references to transnational issues in the national and
regional planning strategies, and while the professional practitioners were well aware of the
transnational scale, they reported that in practice their planning horizon was limited by
domestically oriented political priorities, resources and other reasons (Dühr and Nadin 2007).
This lack of positive outcomes may be surprising given the amount of transnational
cooperation activity under way, but there has been, until recently, little debate and analysis about
transnational planning (with some notable exceptions mentioned above). At best, participants
recognised the value of transnational cooperation on issues that were of shared interest. However, in most cases such issues were essentially domestic in nature, that is, they could be tackled
effectively without the involvement of foreign partners. Many funded projects were spontaneous
responses that relied on pre-existing personal contacts and the interests of individuals. There has
been little in the way of strategy, and the spatial visions that were prepared for some of the
cooperation areas have arguably not ﬁlled this gap. There is therefore little evidence to suggest
that a signiﬁcant adaptation or reorientation of domestic planning policy and practices to the
transnational scale (in the sense of a transnational ‘rescaling’ of spatial planning) has taken place
to date. At least in north-west Europe, not even a ‘weak’ Europeanisation – in the sense of shifting the preferences and strategies of actors involved in cooperation – appeared to have taken
place by the mid-2000s (Dühr and Nadin 2007). The change of focus and reforms of EU
Cohesion Policy for the funding period 2007–13 does not, at ﬁrst sight, seem to signpost a major
change in this regard.
Does European territorial cooperation lead to a convergence of 
planning systems, policies and practices?
European territorial cooperation programmes offer a platform for the transnational communication, dissemination and subsequent transformation of different concepts, cultures and ideas
of spatial planning horizontally across EU member states, regions and cities. When territorial
cooperation and learning does take place, the question arises as to whether the gradual ‘emulation’ between policies and practices might ultimately lead to a form of convergence between
planning practices, policies and systems across Europe. Does European territorial cooperation
foster a gradual homogenisation of planning practices through the diffusion of ‘good practices’
and dominant spatial policy concepts (Jensen and Richardson 2004) or does it rather support
the continuing existence of a heterogeneous set of planning practices (Colomb 2007)? This is a
most difﬁcult effect to understand. How and why does convergence (or divergence) take place?
In what ways and in what degree? For what reasons? And has territorial cooperation played a
part in these processes? Transboundary territorial cooperation may be only one of several factors
potentially leading to the dissemination of similar practices and policies across Europe. Other
possible sources of inﬂuence could include the EU regulatory framework (e.g. environmental
regulations) and wider processes of globalisation and international ‘policy transfer’.
Since INTERREG IIC and IIIB programmes were explicitly presented as instruments to
promote the application of the ESDP, it could be hypothesised that the principles and objectives
of INTERREG guidelines and programme documents, and the criteria used to select projects,
would have subtly encouraged the dissemination of concepts such as ‘polycentricity’, possibly
leading to the gradual development of a ‘consensual knowledge community’ in European
planning (Stone 2004: 560). However, the ofﬁcial documents framing INTERREG IIC and 
IIIB programmes were formulated in relatively generic ways, with a wide-ranging set of
‘thematic priorities’ broadly in line with the three ESDP objectives but also reﬂecting the various
interests of the constituent member states and regions in the 13 programme areas. As a result
the topics and content of the hundreds of transnational cooperation projects funded under these
354
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 384

programmes have been extremely diverse and wide-ranging. As Waterhout and Stead (2007)
showed, at project level there is little evidence of ESDP concepts being ‘implemented’ through
detailed policies. Although INTERREG programme documents are framed by powerful
concepts, past the application stage project partners have some leeway to depart from the formal
rhetoric of the funding application. Concepts such as ‘polycentricity’ or ‘urban-rural partnerships’ are ‘speciﬁc enough to lead to a mode of thought, but not speciﬁc enough to preclude a
variety of different interpretations for different people and contexts’ (De Jong and Edelenbos
2007: 702). These concepts become ‘constructed, contested, reproduced and institutionalised
across multi-levels of governance’ (Böhme et al. 2004). Local partners will interpret and transform such generic concepts to suit their own agenda (Adams 2008; Gløersen et al. 2007;
Zonneveld and Stead 2007). This instrumentalisation of so-called ‘European’ planning ideas may
occur as part of a domestic political process of legitimisation to persuade other actors of the
desirability of a policy change or innovation. As recent political science research on cognitive
modes of Europeanisation has shown, local, regional and national actors can use ‘Europe’
creatively as a resource or as an argument to achieve speciﬁc objectives of domestic change
(Radaelli 2004). Examples and best practices ‘extant in other national contexts, other regions,
other municipalities, other policy areas, other eras or combinations of each of those’ are used
as legitimising arguments in this struggle to reframe the dominant policy discourse and adapt
the institutional framework in a direction of their liking (De Jong and Mamadouh 2002: 23–4).
Altogether it is thus doubtful whether ‘an increasing commonality among the partners at a
generic conceptual level’, expressed by the widespread dissemination of concepts such as polycentricity, implies ‘growing policy convergence at home’ (De Jong and Edelenbos 2007: 704).
Dabinett, in his study of cooperation projects in the North Sea Area, concluded that it is difﬁcult
to identify the development of ‘a shared view of spatial planning’ or the ‘extension of practices
to the wider Euro meta-region’ (Dabinett 2006: 290). Existing research tends to show that if
there is a convergence of planning policies at European level, it is more a rhetorical one than
an actual homogenisation of practices. Planning practices are ‘cultural’ and embedded in particular traditional ways of doing things and the predominant social model. Although domestic
planning will be inﬂuenced in similar ways in the member states, inﬂuences are reinterpreted in
the dominant terms of the domestic social model and tradition may temper innovation (Nadin
and Stead 2008). Rather than convergence, some authors have proposed to use the term ‘con-
ﬂuence’ between planning systems (Böhme and Waterhout 2008), which is not synonymous with
uniformisation or homogenisation, but rather describes a situation where there are some
common or similar responses to the engagement with EU inﬂuences.
Reviewing the effects of European territorial cooperation 
on planning to date
While transboundary collaboration has expanded rapidly, knowledge of its effects (or effectiveness) has not. This is particularly the case for transnational cooperation on spatial planning, for
which EU funding was ﬁrst made available in 1997, yet where to date limited effects in terms
of transfer, rescaling and convergence of planning practices, policies and strategies are observed.
The methodological challenges raised by the evaluation of forms of cooperation and processes
of learning within cooperation projects, and the difﬁculties in establishing clear causal links
between transnational learning and changes in approaches, policies and practices, can partly
explain the paucity of evidence. But what evidence is available shows few signs of radical changes
in planning practices, of a transnational ‘rescaling’ of planning perspectives or of a convergence
of planning systems and policies as a result of European territorial cooperation. The ‘border
effects’ that cooperation programmes were supposed to overcome are still acutely present in

The relevance of transboundary cooperation
355
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 385

many parts of Europe: ‘European integration notwithstanding, borders are still important for
the way people perceive social-political questions’ (De Vries 2008: 50). Territorial cooperation
through INTERREG funding, it seems, has to date not been able to change this fundamentally,
and planning practices and systems are slow to change, let alone converge. A number of factors
can be identiﬁed that may be responsible for this.
First, the dominance of competition (rather than cooperation) between cities and regions is
an important factor explaining the weak political dimension of transnational cooperation in many
parts of Europe. Interregional competition is fuelled by political rivalries, historic differences 
and the importance of market forces in directing economic growth (Dühr and Nadin 2007). These
deep-seated attitudes inﬂuence the themes selected for cooperation, with more interest in ‘softer’,
less competitive, themes such as culture or tourism. Besides, transnational issues tend to do much
less well than smaller-scale cross-border issues because they are less visible. Thus politicians 
will (after domestic concerns) give priority to cross-border working if they are located in a 
border region, and in any case to projects that will lead to ‘visible’ (usually physical) outcomes,
for example a cross-border transport link. Crucially, many politicians are not convinced of the
potential beneﬁts of transnational cooperation, as the long-term nature of transnational spatial
development and the limited competences and missing institutions for spatial planning at the EU
and transnational levels present a considerable disadvantage in the political sphere. But there is
considerable variation across regions, and transnational cooperation tends to be more developed
and politically supported where there are historical links or a long-standing tradition of cooperation across national borders, as, for example, in the Baltic Sea region.
Second, challenges to European territorial cooperation arise from the difﬁculties often
encountered by stakeholders when working to establish a governance platform for engaging in
cooperation both externally (with governments or organisations in other countries) and internally
(within their own organisation) (Dühr and Nadin 2007). Externally, it is widely recognised that
the main practical challenge in transboundary working of any kind lies in bridging across widely
contrasting institutional arrangements for spatial planning in different countries and regions.
Numerous comparative studies have illustrated the sharp differences in structures of government, law, procedures and planning cultures even in adjacent territories (CEC 1997a). There can
also be considerable variations within countries (as between the German and Swiss speaking
cantons in Switzerland). These differences continue to be a signiﬁcant issue even for long-standing
cooperation initiatives, and can seriously hinder progress on new cooperation projects. Little
progress has been made on creating formal transnational governance arrangements that might
facilitate transnational working on spatial planning, in spite of repeated calls by stakeholders
involved in long-standing cooperative working for the creation of bodies and instruments with
legal status and autonomy at the transnational level. The EU Regulation on a European grouping of territorial cooperation (EGTC), adopted in 2006 (Council 2006b; see Chapter 16), is
addressing this requirement. If such formal groupings become more widespread in future,
national and regional stakeholders are likely to become more attuned to these new arrangements
as an effective approach to transnational problem solving.
Besides the difﬁculties of working with external governments and organisations, the necessity
for a partner organisation to establish an effective platform for transnational cooperation within
its own institutional context can be a challenge. Within a country or a region, as in the context
of metropolitan city regions, many difﬁculties are frequently experienced when trying to develop
a common agenda among competing interests across traditional administrative boundaries
(Healey 2007; Salet et al. 2003). Within organisations themselves, technical capacity, political
leadership, and human and ﬁnancial resources are important. Although transnational cooperation can offer considerable personal beneﬁts, it makes severe demands on staff resources that
are often difﬁcult to meet. A lack of expertise in intergovernmental working, including language
356
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 386

skills, often presents signiﬁcant challenges for successful transnational cooperation, from the
creation of projects to the communication of results. Support from senior ofﬁcials or politicians
is also crucial, but may require some changes in mindsets: ‘in the view of many external
observers, INTERREG projects consist of a succession of superﬂuous meetings, from which
nothing visible emerges at all’ (Huebner and Stellfeldt-Koch 2009: 33). The limited involvement
of senior policy-makers in local authorities participating in cooperation projects has often meant
that the shift from individual to organisational learning as a result of participation in
INTERREG projects has remained limited (Dabinett 2006).
Finally, an important barrier to more effective transnational cooperation may arise from the
formal requirements and functioning of funding programmes themselves (Dühr and Nadin 2007;
Waterhout and Stead 2007). INTERREG IIC and IIIB projects have traditionally been developed
in a bottom-up manner within the framework of rather broad thematic guidelines deﬁned for
each programme area, with little steer from ‘above’ (i.e. national or EU institutions). The main
actors involved in these projects have been mostly local and regional public authorities. While
this may mean that transnational issues have not been given much attention to date, numerous
positive beneﬁts also arise from such a situation, not least the large number of participants who
have been able to engage in cooperation in a way that they want. Yet the selection of projects
is carried out by programme ‘steering committees’ made up of representatives from the national
and regional governments involved in the programme. As a result, the process of project selection
is often inﬂuenced by political considerations and power structures, since decision-makers tend
to prioritise national interests and the search for maximum returns in terms of European funding
gained, rather than concentrating on spatial planning or territorial development criteria
(Waterhout and Stead 2007). These transnational programme committees have wide scope in
interpreting the notion of ‘transnationality’, although they must work within the framework of
cooperation zones, the programme objectives and project selection criteria (Dühr and Nadin
2007). The result is that overall many INTERREG projects across Europe have addressed transnationality in only a weak way, with a preference for networking and exchanges of experience
on thematic issues of a common nature rather than ‘truly transnational’ problems. Despite some
successful projects, the transnational approach therefore remains rather hazy and unconsolidated
(Dühr and Nadin 2007).
Conclusion
The ﬁndings presented in this chapter point towards a limited impact of European territorial
cooperation programmes and projects on spatial planning systems, policies and practices in the
EU member states to date. While there are no comprehensive analyses of the effects of transnational territorial cooperation, the results of individual programme analyses and case studies
point to a contrast between the achievements of programmes and projects and the ambitious
objectives for transnational cooperation. However, the discussions in this chapter should not
be interpreted as a failure of European territorial cooperation (in particular transnational cooperation) per se, but as a reminder that ‘Europeanisation’ is not a smooth or inevitable process
and that the development of shared strategies and actions across boundaries will not emerge of
its own accord but that cooperation structures require time to mature. The embeddedness of
planning in national and regional cultures, the persistence of a territorial mindset ﬁxed on
domestic concerns, of sectoral boundaries and ‘inter-place’ competition should not be underestimated. Institutions change only very slowly and attachments to longstanding territories are
very strong.
From a spatial planning point of view – both at European level and at local and regional level
– the limited evidence on the achievements and added value of past initiatives does not mean

The relevance of transboundary cooperation
357
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 387

that European transboundary cooperation should be abandoned. However, some realism needs
to be injected into expectations, based on a fuller analysis of the value of qualitative strategic
impacts of cooperation. A lesson from progress so far is that transnational cooperation efforts
should be focused on areas where there are real and signiﬁcant transnational problems that can
be addressed by a spatial planning approach. This might mean much more targeting of funding
and closer speciﬁcation of the transnational and cross-border zones. It implies long-term
ﬁnancial support to cooperation programmes and not short-term ad hoc projects. It will also
require a politicisation of the issues securing the political support of actors at the local, regional,
national and EU level, if transnational territorial cooperation is to become truly valued as a key
mechanism in spatial planning. This may mean a need for more of a top-down approach, rather
than the bottom-up project deﬁnition that has dominated so far. It will also require more use
of the legal and institutional arrangements facilitating cooperation at the transnational level
(such as EGTC) (Dühr and Nadin 2007). Understanding the effects of EU-supported territorial
cooperation better and improving the knowledge base on learning that is taking place through
cooperation will also require that future programme and project evaluation and monitoring
approaches pay more attention to the qualitative and soft impacts of cooperation, rather than
continuing to try to measure socio-economic impacts.
It must also be acknowledged that the ‘rules of the game’ of territorial cooperation have
changed. In response to the Lisbon-Gothenburg Strategy (European Council 2000a; European
Council 2001), the objective of territorial cooperation has shifted strongly in favour of the
promotion of innovation, economic competitiveness and the knowledge economy. This has raised
fears among European spatial planning advocates that the integrated ‘spatial development’ and
‘territorial cohesion’ approach championed over the past decade through the ESDP, INTERREG
IIIB and ESPON might be sidetracked, and that the more thematic focus of the new transnational
strand of cooperation programmes will not provide a strong incentive for cooperation on truly
transnational spatial development issues (Dühr and Nadin 2007). The changing focus of the 
new transnational cooperation programmes away from an ‘ESDP-spatial development’ approach
under INTERREG IIIB to a ‘Lisbon-Growth and Jobs’ priority under INTERREG IVB, begs 
the question as to what the ultimate rationale of future territorial cooperation in the EU will be.
Will cooperation be directed to the logic of competitiveness (Leitner and Sheppard 1999), 
merely perpetuating a form of ‘competition through collaboration’ whereby local and regional
actors use enhanced knowledge and best practice ‘to distance themselves from less “networked”
neighbours’ (Phelps et al. 2002: 214)? Or will cooperation foster new forms of territorial solidarity, territorial cohesion and sustainable spatial planning at transnational scales not experienced
before?
Further reading
There is at present no comprehensive analysis of the impacts of territorial cooperation on spatial
planning systems and policies. The Special Issue on ‘The Europeanization of spatial planning
through territorial cooperation’ (Dühr et al. 2007), on which this chapter draws, gives an insight
into different perspectives on territorial cooperation. ESPON (2005a, 2006f) and INTERACT
have funded some studies that try to analyse the impacts of INTERREG cooperation (available
on the programme websites). The German Bundesamt für Bauwesen und Raumordnung (BBR)
has published a number of studies on the effects of the five transnational cooperation
programmes in which Germany is involved and on the relevance of the ESPON programme for
Germany (see Görmar et al. 2005; Hartz and Saad 2007; Huebner und Stellfeldt-Koch 2009;
Müller et al. 2005; available on the BBR website: www.bbr.bund.de).
358
Towards new forms of territorial governance

## S. 388

The Europeanisation of domestic
planning systems
By participating in the different initiatives of EU territorial governance (from the Integrated
Mediterranean Programmes of the middle 1980s, to the Structural Funds mainstream, to
INTERREG, Leader and Urban Community initiatives), all South European countries are developing
innovations in planning which seem not to have precedent in their ‘urbanism tradition’. This affects
not only the local level as the most directly concerned, but also the regional and national institutions,
albeit in different forms.
(Janin Rivolin 2005: 25)
Introduction
A signiﬁcant part of the research carried out by planning scholars in the ﬁeld of European spatial
planning in the past decade has focused on identifying the inﬂuence of the activities of the EU
on domestic planning systems and on institutions, policies and processes of urban and regional
planning. At the crossroad between political science, European integration studies and spatial
planning research, a new ﬁeld of investigation on the ‘Europeanisation of spatial planning’ and
the emergence of a European spatial policy ﬁeld consequently emerged in the 1990s. Research
into EU inﬂuences on domestic spatial planning has taken various directions. Some scholars have
focused on the speciﬁc impacts of particular EU initiatives and programmes or on the planning
implications of particular pieces of EU legislation (for example, Beunen 2006; Howe and White
2002). (Chapter 18 reviews research on the effects of the URBAN initiative, Chapter 22
summarises the research on the effects of territorial cooperation programmes drawing on the
special issue of Planning Practice and Research introduced by Dühr et al. 2007.) Others have
focused on: the domestic impact of Structural Fund programmes on regional and urban
governance and policies (Gualini 2001, 2004a); the inﬂuence of the ESDP on national, regional
and local planning practices and policies (ESPON 2007b; Faludi 2001a, 2003, 2004b; Shaw and
Sykes 2003, 2005); or the impacts of a wide ranging set of EU policies on one particular country
(Tewdwr-Jones and Williams 2001; van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004).
As Part V of the book has demonstrated, many EU sectoral policies influence spatial
development patterns, spatial planning policies and ‘the way spatial planning decisions are made
at any given administrative level’ (Böhme and Waterhout 2008: 234). In addition, Chapters 15
and 16 have presented the specific instruments and initiatives of spatial planning at the
transnational and European scale. The present chapter takes stock of the combined inﬂuence
of all the types of EU activities analysed in this book and provides a brief discussion of their
overall effects on the domestic planning systems of a number of EU countries. There is at present
no comprehensive analysis of the Europeanisation of the spatial planning systems of the 27 EU
member states on which this chapter could draw. Rather than being exhaustive, this chapter

## S. 389

therefore provides a snapshot of the state of research in a limited number of country-speciﬁc
case studies. These were chosen on the basis of the availability of published research in English,
and aim to illustrate the discussion on spatial planning change in different parts of the EU: the
UK, the Netherlands and Poland. For each of these, the inﬂuence of EU activities on spatial
planning is brieﬂy considered at three levels of planning: national, regional and local.
The Europeanisation of domestic spatial planning: 
an introduction
As discussed in Chapter 8, Europeanisation is a multi-faceted term that has been the subject of
many deﬁnitions by political scientists and European integration scholars. The Europeanisation of spatial planning can be conceptualised as a discursive and cognitive process whereby
certain normative concepts and ‘spatial planning ideas’ are encouraged by EU institutions,
programmes and documents such as the ESDP, and through debate among actors from across
the EU, but are then reshaped by local actors (Böhme et al. 2004; Dabinett and Richardson
2005; Janin Rivolin and Faludi 2005; Jensen and Richardson 2004). In line with the deﬁnition
proposed by Radaelli (2004: 3), in this chapter Europeanisation is thus understood as a 
process of:
a) construction, b) diffusion and c) institutionalisation of formal and informal rules,
procedures, policy paradigms, styles, ‘ways of doing things’ and shared beliefs and norms
which are ﬁrst deﬁned and consolidated in the EU policy process and then incorporated in
the logic of domestic (national and subnational) discourse, political structures and public
policies.
Böhme and Waterhout (2008) conceptualise the ‘Europeanisation of planning’ as a threefold
process (Figure 23.1) which encompasses:
1
the emergence of ‘planning for Europe’ (i.e. the idea of spatial planning at the European
scale, which corresponds to the second meaning of ‘spatial planning’ discussed in 
Chapter 3);
2
the inﬂuence of ‘planning for Europe’ on ‘planning in Europe’ (i.e. on spatial planning
systems, policies and practices in the EU member states) and on EU policies;
3
the inﬂuence of EU sectoral policies and European integration on ‘planning in Europe’.
This chapter focuses on the second and third dimension of Böhme and Waterhout’s conceptualisation. ‘Planning for Europe’ encompasses the various initiatives, strategies, programmes and
instruments for the spatial planning and territorial development of the EU territory as a whole,
or of large transnational areas within it (described in Part IV of the book). ‘Planning in Europe’
refers to the diversity of spatial planning systems and practices that exist across EU member
states (Böhme 2002) (see Chapter 13). While ‘planning for Europe mainly deals with providing
the means for the Europeanization of planning in Europe’ (Böhme and Waterhout 2008: 232),
the two ﬁelds have mutually inﬂuenced each other.
Tewdwr-Jones and Williams (2001), the authors of one of the ﬁrst systematic studies of the
EU’s inﬂuence on a national planning system, stress the methodological challenges that exist for
researchers who attempt to study the inﬂuence of the EU on domestic planning systems and
practices. The methods used by planning scholars to date have included studying the content
of national, regional and local planning policy documents, as well as interviewing politicians,
ofﬁcials and planners at various tiers of government, to try and identify evidence of EU
360
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 390

inﬂuence. However, channels of inﬂuence in the EU’s multi-level governance system (discussed
in the following section) are so varied that identifying when and how inﬂuence has occurred is
a challenging task: ‘the EU seems to have a hand in so many different policy areas in so many
different ways, making it extremely difﬁcult to get a complete picture’ (van Ravesteyn and Evers
2004: 139). This explains why most studies are limited to a particular sector, region or theme.
Inﬂuence may have been very gradual, may have taken place over a long time, and may not have
been recognised by practitioners themselves (for instance, the European origin of much
environmental legislation currently in place in EU member states has often been forgotten).
Of particular relevance to this book is the difﬁculty of tracing the inﬂuence of non-legislative,
non-binding EU initiatives such as action frameworks, guidelines or strategies – for example,
the ESDP. Assuming that one ﬁnds evidence of the presence of certain speciﬁc European spatial
planning concepts in domestic planning documents – what the authors of the ESPON project
2.3.1 (2007b) call ‘conformance’ (i.e. thematic correspondence) – it is not easy to demonstrate
whether such concepts are translated into planning policies and practices of spatial development
on the ground and have actually been used in decision-making (what Faludi (2000b) calls
‘performance’) (see Chapter 13). Planning documents may be ‘Europeanised’ in their discourse,
but this could be merely rhetorical and does not necessarily mean that planning practices are
Europeanised as a result. In some cases, processes of Europeanisation can lead to the institutionalisation of a European dimension in domestic policies and practices. Institutionalisation is
deﬁned as ‘a process in which recurring patterns of the agent’s behaviour lead to valued 
and stable organizations, procedures and beliefs’ (Waterhout 2007: 312). To be able to talk
about institutionalisation, there have to be visible changes in planning strategies, policies or
organisational structures – something that rarely happens quickly but takes place over time.
Finally, it is not easy to distinguish EU inﬂuences from other global trends that have affected

## S. 391

the exercise of planning at all scales: the Europeanisation of planning ‘should not be considered
as simply a form of domestic response to EU policies or the spatial planning discourse, but rather
as a response to general processes of upscaling due to European integration, EU enlargement,
and, more generally, globalization’ (Böhme and Waterhout 2008: 243). In some countries, such
as the UK and Spain, domestic pressures for more devolution and regional autonomy have
interacted with processes of Europeanisation to produce new arrangements for the governance
of regional planning and territorial development (Shaw and Sykes 2005).
The channels of the Europeanisation of spatial planning
Before introducing country-speciﬁc case studies, it is helpful to identify the different types of
channels of EU inﬂuence on ‘planning in Europe’, that is, spatial planning in the member states.
The ways in which the EU inﬂuences national and sub-national planning systems and practices
does not only happen through the implementation of EU regulations and legislation but also
through funding programmes (e.g. the Structural Funds), cooperation initiatives (INTERREG
and ESPON – see Chapter 16), the production of a new discourse on European spatial planning through initiatives such as the ESDP which leads to the circulation of ‘planning ideas’, and
so on. Rather than considering the inﬂuence of the EU on spatial planning as a one-directional,
linear process, it is thus much more appropriate to consider domestic change in planning
systems, policies and practices in the EU as a result of discursive and circular processes. 
Ideas are shared among the member states during the preparation of EU law and policy, and
subsequently such ‘European’ ideas are being (re-)interpreted within domestic contexts. Crossfertilisation of planning thoughts also occurs through exchanges in territorial cooperation programmes and projects and by working with colleagues in other European regions. A typology
of ‘European inﬂuences’ on domestic spatial planning in Europe is proposed in Table 23.1. Four
broad categories are identiﬁed.
The ﬁrst two categories encompass EU sectoral policies that have an inﬂuence on the spatial
organisation and development of the European territory at various scales. In the ESDP a spatial
impact means that ‘Community measures modify the spatial structure and potentials in the economy and society thereby altering land use patterns and landscapes’, or ‘inﬂuence the competitive
position or spatial signiﬁcance of a city or region within the European economic system and
settlement pattern’ (CEC 1998a: 13). EU policies with spatial impacts can be sub-divided into
two types: those that are spatially deﬁned and targeted on a geographical basis (such as EU
Cohesion Policy) and those that are not spatially deﬁned and apply across the whole EU territory
(such as EU competition policy). The third category corresponds to EU legislation that has a
direct impact on domestic planning legislation and practices. Only few items of EU legislation
actually have such a direct impact.
The fourth category includes speciﬁc initiatives, programmes and instruments for the spatial
planning of the EU territory as a whole or of large transnational areas within it (‘planning for
Europe’). The initial impact of such initiatives has been to encourage domestic planning actors
to ‘think European’ and position their city, region or country in a bigger territorial frame of reference – what Böhme and Waterhout (2008) deﬁne as ‘reﬂexive integration’ and what Williams
(1996: 97) has called ‘spatial positioning’. These initiatives have also encouraged the wide
circulation of certain ‘spatial policy concepts’, such as polycentricity, across Europe (Jensen 
and Richardson 2004). This has led, in some cases, to the types of processes of ‘cognitive’ 
(or ‘horizontal’) Europeanisation discussed in Chapter 8. The transformations of planning
practices witnessed as a result are not ‘the product of a forced compliance to EU models but 
the outcome of complex socialization and learning processes enabling domestic actors to
experience new ideas and practices and to adapt their methods and strategies accordingly’
362
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 392

(Giannakourou 2005: 319). The ESPON 2.3.1 project (2007b) on the application of the 
ESDP showed that the ESDP’s inﬂuence on national and regional planning policy varied highly
between member states and regions. It concluded that while national planning policy guidelines
in the member states are generally ‘in line with’ the ESDP principles (i.e. exhibiting a thematic
correspondence), the ESDP has rarely led to explicit changes in spatial development institutions,
policies and practices: ‘no stone has been turned because of the ESDP’ (Faludi 2001b: 50).
However, there are many cases of coherence of policies in the ESDP and policies in the member
state because they both reﬂect current orthodoxies in planning (ESPON 2007b). In several
countries the ESDP has been used by actors at the sub-state level ‘as a source of inspiration and
justiﬁcation in the development of spatial policies’ (ESPON 2007b: 15). The spatial planning
concepts of the ESDP are vague and ﬂexible enough to be reinterpreted, and sometimes
contested, by local and regional actors to suit their own agenda (Jensen and Richardson 2004;
Shaw and Sykes 2003, 2005). Actors use certain concepts ‘to try to make sense of a particular
situation, justify a certain choice or solve a speciﬁc problem’ and in so doing, they often ‘reinterpret concepts, principles and strategies from their own perspective, and perhaps also ignore
or reject other parts of the same document that are less relevant for their task’ (Waterhout and
Stead 2007: 398–9). The concept of ‘polycentricity’, for example, has been open to multiple
interpretations to serve different agendas:
polycentricity is thus perceived as either a Trojan horse of globalization and the competitiveness agenda, or as a tool of resistance which can enhance social inclusion; preserve territorial diversity; and resist ‘genericization’ (i.e. the homogenisation of place) and the erosion
of local productive systems and local identities.
(Shaw and Sykes 2004: 299)
Bearing in mind the complexities of tracing the inﬂuences of EU initiatives and policy on
member states’ planning systems, the next sections will give a brief overview of the discussion
on domestic change in three EU member states: the UK, the Netherlands and Poland.
The United Kingdom: Europeanisation that dares not
speak its name?
From the mid-1990s onwards British researchers have investigated the impacts of EU activities
on the UK’s planning systems at the national, regional and local levels. The Royal Town Planning
Institute (RTPI) commissioned a study to assess the impact of the EU on British land use planning
(Davies et al. 1994), followed in 1998 by an analysis commissioned by the then Department of
Environment, Transport and the Regions (now the Department of Communities and Local
Government) (DETR 1998a). Tewdwr-Jones and Williams (2001) later published their study on
the effect of the EU on planning in the UK. Note that competence for spatial planning in the UK
varies in the four countries (England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland) such that they have
their own ‘national’ policy statements (Cullingworth and Nadin 2006). The inﬂuence of the
European agenda on these national policies and debates has also varied from time to time.
The emergence of research into the European dimension of British planning corresponded to
a period of debates on the reform of the planning system and to a change in the attitude of the
UK government towards the EU, following the election of the New Labour government in 1997
(Nadin 2007). In the ﬁnal years of the ESDP process (1997–99), UK planners consequently
played a considerably more proactive role than under the previous Conservative government,
which had frequently been described as ‘Euro-sceptic’ (Faludi and Waterhout 2002), although
during that time individual experts from the UK had played their part in European spatial
planning debates. During the Thatcher era, the ‘European dimension’ of planning was largely

## S. 395

ignored in policy documents and debates (Tewdwr-Jones et al. 2000). In his ﬁrst policy statement Modernising Planning (DETR 1998b), the then planning minister Richard Caborn
explicitly called for ‘thinking in terms of EU space and spatial relationships’ and the need to
‘relate to non-British modes of planning thought’ (quoted in Tewdwr-Jones and Williams 2001:
62). This coincided with a shift in thinking about the essence of planning in the UK, which led
New Labour to promote a signiﬁcant reform of the planning system and embrace the concept
of ‘spatial planning’ (as opposed to land use planning) in the revised Planning and Compulsory
Purchase Act of 2004.
Tewdwr Jones and Williams (2001) conclude that the most signiﬁcant impacts of the EU have
come from EU sectoral legislation (in particular environmental law) and from the operation of
the EU Structural Funds, rather than from European spatial planning initiatives per se. The
authors identiﬁed over 50 EU sectoral measures of particular importance for British planning.
They additionally argued that the impact of the EU on British planning in the late 1980s and
for most of the 1990s was much greater at local level than at the level of national and regional
policies. They considered the EU impact on national and regional planning policy to be
noticeable in some policy sectors but ‘the degree to which this impact has been acknowledged
in planning statements has varied markedly between topics and over time’ (Tewdwr Jones and
Williams 2001: 75). Areas of EU inﬂuence on national planning policy were nature conservation,
transport, coastal planning, planning and pollution control, and business and industry. However
the European origin of various initiatives was often deliberately left unmentioned in ofﬁcial texts,
in part because of a political will to ‘keep Europe at a distance from the British planning system
and to treat European spatial planning as a separate process outside Britain’s statutory land use
planning system’ (Tewdwr-Jones and Williams 2001: 76). The reforms of the planning system
in England that took place in the early part of the 2000s included more explicit reference to the
European dimension in revised national policy statements on the need, for example, for regional
spatial strategies to take account of intergovernmental and European Union legislation, policies,
programmes and funding regimes that impact on the regions (DETR 2000; ODPM 2004). This
included the ESDP and the results of the INTERREG IIIB and ESPON programmes. Even so,
the UK government did not set any ‘speciﬁc requirement to take any particular part of the ESDP
on board, whether in terms of the broad policy options or indeed the more procedural issues
of horizontal and vertical integration’ (Shaw and Sykes 2005: 189).
The New Labour government brought about some fundamental changes to the politicaladministrative organisation of the UK, with increased devolution (decentralisation) of government functions (including planning powers) to Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland and London
and new bodies for economic development and spatial planning at the regional tier in England.
It is in this context that European initiatives such as INTERREG and the ESDP have been
mobilised by regional actors to develop their own strategic spatial planning agenda (Shaw and
Sykes 2003, 2005). In the north-west region of England, for example, ESDP ideas and policy
messages were used by various stakeholders to develop and produce their regional spatial strategy (GONW 2003), which included references to the north-west’s position in relation to Europe
(through maps and diagrams) and placed emphasis on improving transnational links (Dühr
2007; Shaw and Sykes 2005). In Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales, the ESDP was used to
help inform the development of new national spatial planning strategies and frameworks.
In terms of the EU’s impact on British planning at the local level, Bishop et al. (2000) and
Tewdwr-Jones and Williams (2001) have classiﬁed EU inﬂuences according to ﬁve main types
of planning activities in which local authorities routinely engage. These EU inﬂuences have put
constraints on development in speciﬁc areas, created the need for new types of plans or new types
of content in existing statutory development plans and inﬂuenced speciﬁc development decisions
(Tewdwr-Jones and Williams 2001: 50). EU activities have therefore strongly inﬂuenced the
366
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 396

exercise of statutory planning functions by local authorities. But they have also led UK planning
authorities to engage in new activities outside statutory functions, such as cross-border and
transnational networking and participation in European cooperation projects. Some regions or
cities have engaged relatively early on in cross-border cooperation, for example Kent with
northern France and Belgium across the English Channel (Church and Reid 1999). Others have
been proactive in forging alliances at the local level to bid for EU Structural Funds for projects
to address a range of social, economic and environmental problems within their local areas.
Local planning policy was, in some instances, even framed to support the case for Structural
Fund assistance (such as in Wales). Structural Funds programmes have encouraged new forms
of collaborative governance and partnerships across sectors and boundaries, sometimes leading
to more integrated territorial development, albeit with different degrees of success in different
regions (Gore 2008). This brief review demonstrates that there have been numerous interconnections between European spatial planning and planning in the UK, especially in terms of
new ideas inﬁltrating into the reform of the formal system.
The ebbs and flows of the Europeanisation of spatial
planning in the Netherlands
The relationship between Dutch spatial planning and the EU has been well researched over the
past decade, under the inﬂuence of an active group of researchers who have investigated the
emerging ﬁeld of European spatial planning and the Europeanisation of Dutch planning early
on. The most comprehensive overview of how the EU has affected spatial development and
spatial policies in the Netherlands is a 2004 report commissioned by the Dutch spatial research
institute, entitled Unseen Europe (van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004). This comprehensive report
highlighted the signiﬁcant, yet often ‘unseen’ effects of EU legislation and policies on various
aspects of spatial planning and various types of geographical areas in the Netherlands. The
report, which had political resonance in the Netherlands, was commissioned in reaction to the
increasing number of articles in Dutch newspapers that highlighted the impact of EU policies
on Dutch spatial development and planning (Waterhout 2007): ‘EU air quality standards
frustrating residential building plans; expansion of the Westerschelde Container Terminal on a
derelict stretch of coastal land being held back by an EU habitat designation; Dutch citizens
proﬁting from their right to free settlement by purchasing houses just over the German border’
(van Ravesteyn and Evers 2004: 11).
As with the UK, when analysing the Europeanisation of Dutch planning researchers 
have distinguished between the national level of planning policy and guidance on the one hand
and the planning activities of regional and local authorities on the other. The Dutch spatial
planning system belongs to the ‘comprehensive, integrated approach’ identiﬁed in the EU
Compendium of Spatial Planning Systems and Policies (CEC 1997a, and see Chapter 13),
characterised by an elaborate and sophisticated system of successive national spatial planning
documents and policies. The Netherlands is one of the six founding member states of the EU,
and Dutch spatial planners at national level (i.e. ofﬁcials of the Ministry of Housing, Spatial
Planning and the Environment) have a long history of being keen supporters of the development
of a ‘European spatial planning agenda’. In 1957 senior planners had voiced the idea of
integrating a spatial planning title in the emerging EEC Treaty (Zonneveld 2005c). Fifty years
later, it was a French-Dutch initiative that led to the ﬁrst informal meeting of European spatial
planning ministers in 1989, prompting the start of the ESDP drafting process in which Dutch
planners and experts played a prominent role (Faludi 2004a; Faludi and Waterhout 2002). In
the mid-2000s, when the ESDP lost momentum, the Dutch took the lead in organising a meeting
of EU spatial planning ministers in November 2004 that launched the debate on territorial

The Europeanisation of domestic planning systems

## S. 397

cohesion (Faludi and Waterhout 2005) and led to the elaboration of the Territorial Agenda of
the European Union (see Chapter 15).
Paradoxically, however, Zonneveld (2005c) shows that while national planners have been
strongly engaged in European debates, Dutch national spatial planning policies have not necessarily demonstrated a strong European dimension. From 1960 until the 2000s, when a planning
reform introduced new planning instruments, national planning policy has been expressed in
national ‘memoranda’ (or guidelines) on spatial planning that contain principles of spatial
organisation framing spatially relevant policies (e.g. housing and land) and the zoning of land at
regional (provinces) and local (communities) level (Zonneveld 2005c). Zonneveld (2005c) 
and Waterhout (2007) provide an overview of the successive phases in the ‘Europeanisation’ 
of Dutch national spatial planning policy, based on an analysis of the successive national spatial
planning guidelines. Their research shows the ‘ebb and ﬂow’ of the idea of Europe in national
planning policy and the importance of domestic institutional and political factors in inﬂuencing
these phases. In the 1960s the Second Memorandum started to represent the Dutch territory ‘as
part of an internally coherent North-West European urban domain for which a set of maps was
designed’ (Zonneveld 2005c: 7). In the 1970s domestic urban issues dominated the planning
agenda and the European scale disappeared from the Third Memorandum: ‘on the policy maps
the neighbouring countries were entirely white like the terra incognita of the nineteenth century’
(Zonneveld 2005c: 7). In the late 1980s, Europe was back on the national planning agenda. The
Fourth Memorandum (1988) went as far arguing that, as so many EC policy domains had become
spatially relevant, ‘spatial planning should become an identiﬁable part of one of the portfolios in
the European Commission’ (quoted in Zonneveld 2005c: 8). Yet Europe was primarily seen as a
setting for national policy, with no detailed analysis of spatial development trends in neighbouring
countries and no attempt to deﬁne cross-border cooperation priorities (Zonneveld 2005c: 8).
In 1997 a small Unit for International Affairs was set up within the Dutch National Spatial
Planning Agency that pushed for a stronger engagement with European issues in the preparation
of the Fifth Memorandum (VROM 2000; Zonneveld 2005c). In the ﬁnal version of the Fifth
Memorandum published in 2001, the analytical part makes ample reference to the European
context, but again this dimension is not reﬂected in the policy and strategy part of the document.
This discrepancy is partly explained by the fact that the preparation of national planning
memoranda are not the sole responsibility of the spatial planning ministry, but also involve other
sectoral ministries (in particular Economic Affairs, Transport, Agriculture and Nature) that
pursue their own agenda and may have more sceptical views about ‘Europe’ (Priemus 1999;
Waterhout 2007; Zonneveld 2005c). As a result, in spite of the strong engagement of Dutch
planners in the ESDP process, ESDP ideas have not been visibly taken up in national planning
documents because they were considered to be already part of existing policies or found to be
too abstract to be of help for solving policy problems (Waterhout 2007: 324).
In April 2002, before the Fifth Memorandum was approved by the Dutch Parliament, the
centre-left governing coalition was replaced by a centre-right government that had a more critical
and reluctant approach to EU integration than its predecessor. In terms of planning, the new
government had a distinctly pro-development approach, favouring economic growth and the
global competitiveness of the Dutch economy. The unapproved Fifth Memorandum was transformed by the new government into a National Spatial Strategy (VROM et al. 2004), which
emphasised competition and economic growth, played down the European dimension and made
no reference to a potential common planning agenda with the countries and regions surrounding
the Netherlands, or of a strategy towards the European Union and its policies (Zonneveld
2005c). This has led Zonneveld (2005c) and Waterhout (2007) to argue that the 2004 National
Spatial Strategy is a step backwards in the Europeanisation of Dutch national spatial planning
policy.
368
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 398

A new ‘episode of Europeanisation’ of Dutch spatial planning was identiﬁed by Waterhout
as starting in 2005. This was triggered by a seemingly technical issue concerning the legal
transposition of a piece of EU legislation that had unexpected impacts upon domestic spatial
planning. The Netherlands was the only European country in which the EU Directive 1999/
30/EC on air quality (see Chapter 21) was integrated into national legislation via the planning
system, namely through the Dutch decision Besluit Luchtkwaliteit, which came into force on 1
January 2005. This means that urban development in areas where air quality exceeds the limits
set by the EU has to be constrained. Such areas represent 75 per cent of the country, so the
implications are signiﬁcant:
from 2005 the Council of State started to block building and infrastructure projects in all
areas with insufﬁcient air quality (in particular around motorways and in city centres).
Obviously, in a country that suffers from road congestion . . . and a shortage of housing,
such a measure has a considerable impact.
(Waterhout 2007: 323)
The Netherlands, through its then environment minister, was the only EU member state that
voted against tougher air quality norms by the year 2015 at the Environment Council of October
2006. Due to the impact of the air quality directive, the impact of EU policies on spatial planning
in the Netherlands ‘for the ﬁrst time raised awareness beyond the domain of experts and turned
into a wider political concern’ (Waterhout 2007: 311). A new unit for international affairs was
created in the ministry of planning (VROM) to monitor sectoral EU policy developments and
their spatial impacts and ensure better participation in the EU comitology system (Waterhout
2007).
At sub-central level, Dutch local and regional authorities have been keen participants in EU
programmes of cross-border and transnational cooperation in the ﬁeld of spatial planning
(Zonneveld 2005c). Participation in INTERREG projects (in particular in the transnational
programmes North-West Europe and North Sea) has been high, with projects covering a multitude of issues such as water management and land use, green corridors and urban regeneration.
Additionally, EU sectoral policies have affected spatial developments at the regional and local
level in the Netherlands in many ways, as discussed in detail by van Ravesteyn and Evers (2004).
Impacts appear to have been more pronounced in rural areas (in particular due to EU
environmental legislation), and less so in urban areas (although the above mentioned air quality
legislation has changed this). Van Ravesteyn and Evers additionally argue that EU competition,
transport and environment policies have a strong impact on the main Dutch hubs, namely the
port of Rotterdam and Schiphol airport in Amsterdam, putting high constraints on their future
expansion (see Chapter 21).
EU accession and the Europeanisation of planning in 
Poland
In central and eastern European countries the impacts of EU policies and legislation started to
be felt several years before the formal accession of these countries to the EU in 2004 and 2007.
This is because the adaptation of the legal and administrative systems of those countries 
was a pre-requisite for accession, in order to comply with the three ‘Copenhagen criteria’ and
with the EU acquis communautaire (see Chapter 12 for a full definition of these terms)
(Schimmelfennig and Sedelmeier 2005). This EU ‘accession conditionality’ and the strict
conditions for EU membership have inﬂuenced institutional reforms, administrative restructuring
and governance patterns in central and eastern European countries in ways that were
