## S. 419

progress has been made since the late 1980s in engaging member states in debates about the
future organisation of the EU territory, effects have been variable and often subtle (or at least
difﬁcult to measure), with the question of how spatial coordination can be achieved most
effectively within existing power structures still to be answered. Often questions about spatial
coordination between sectoral policies at national and regional levels are not being addressed
in the member states themselves.
The objective of territorial cohesion, included in the Treaty of Lisbon, may change this 
and provide more scope for coordinated EU action in the area of territorial development and
cooperation. Even so, the paradigm shift in the 2000s towards the competitiveness agenda of
the EU has prompted a stronger emphasis on sectoral and thematic approaches supporting 
the growth, innovation and jobs agenda. Less attention is given to spatial planning as a coordinating mechanism. EU funding for territorial cooperation through INTERREG programmes 
has also been affected by this, with limited consideration given to the value of strategic development frameworks or spatial visions to coordinate cooperation projects. Much transnational
cooperation has developed in a bottom-up way and as an exercise of ‘learning-by-doing’, 
and although the activities may not have led to a rescaling of the planning perspectives of
participants, they have at least raised the awareness for the ‘spatial positioning’ of their own
territories in the wider European space. Cooperation structures take time to evolve, and critical
issues for cooperation (such as on joint economic development initiatives) require trust between
partners. Territorial cooperation is a learning process, and the real effects of it may be seen only
in the years to come.
Yet it is perhaps also in territorial cooperation initiatives that the tensions between the EU’s
objectives for competitiveness and cohesion come to the fore most acutely. EU Cohesion Policy
has traditionally been concerned with overcoming regional disparities within the European
territory, which were seen as obstacles to economic and social cohesion. The ESDP additionally
advocated the objective of the ‘polycentric development’ of the EU as a form of territorial cohesion. Following EU enlargement and with the agreement on the Lisbon-Gothenburg Agenda, 
the objective of improving the economic competitiveness of the EU has become dominant. 
As a result, the primary objective of more balanced development (the cohesion objective) has
been joined in the 2007–13 Cohesion Policy period by policies that promote economic growth
uniformly across the EU (the competitiveness objective) (Dühr and Nadin 2007). This places
support for structurally weak regions next to an emphasis on strengthening strong areas. These
potentially contradictory objectives are presented in the Third Cohesion Report (CEC 2004c)
as a question of appropriate scale, by emphasising the need to reduce disparities within the EU
in order to accelerate the growth of the EU as a whole (i.e. at the global scale). Yet, in practice
there are considerable tensions between the competitiveness and cohesion objectives, especially
in relation to the scale at which they should be pursued. The past two decades have shown that
increases in overall levels of economic prosperity and competitiveness at the European and
national levels have often been accompanied by a widening of interregional disparities within
member states. Cooperation programmes such as INTERREG are increasingly understood as
tools that merely help overcome the barrier effect of national borders in order to strengthen
economic competitiveness, rather than to achieve cohesion.
For spatial planners who have engaged extensively in cooperation on spatial development
issues with other EU regions, this neoliberal turn is of considerable concern. Economic
development is given precedence over social and environmental issues. This may be a shortsighted approach that risks undermining the ‘European social model’, commonly seen as a
considerable strength in comparison with other global economies. It may also result in
irreversible environmental damage if the polarisation tendencies around Europe’s large urban
390
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 420

areas and unsustainable transport growth are given free rein. The objective of territorial
cohesion in the ‘Lisbon Treaty’ may give new stimulus to the debate on the added value of
territorial cooperation, for instance in relation to the implementation of transnational strategies
such as those for the Baltic Sea Region or the Danube corridor. It may also help to more ﬁrmly
inject a spatial dimension into EU policy-making, but whether it will succeed in doing so will
depend on how territorial cohesion is understood and implemented. Will this be accepted by
the EU institutions and the member states as the leading paradigm over and above economic
competitiveness, alongside environmentally sustainable development?
Looking forward: perspectives on European spatial 
planning
In a speculative paper about the future of European spatial planning written in 2002, John Zetter
(previously a senior planner in the UK national administration and as national representative
involved in the preparation of the ESDP) pretended to look back on progress from a viewpoint
in 2010 (Zetter 2002). In the aftermath of the adoption of the ESDP, Zetter painted a rather
positive picture, but acknowledged that the future ‘trajectory’ of European spatial planning until
2010 would be determined by the following main factors: the state of the EU and its Treaties;
the European Commission; the ‘ESDP institutions’; the state of spatial planning; and ‘the state
of the world’.
In 2009, at the time of ﬁnalising this book, the future of European spatial planning is less
certain than predicted by Zetter in 2002, though the conditions that determine its future remain
the same, particularly the support of key actors. First and foremost, a precondition for the future
of European spatial planning is the future of ‘planning’ (as state intervention in the management
of space) in EU member states. Despite the supranational character of the EU as a political
system, member states continue to have considerable control over the processes of EU policymaking, be it in the Council for decisions on EU sector policies or through intergovernmental
initiatives on spatial planning. Over recent years, neoliberal agendas have had considerable
inﬂuence on state institutions and policies, including in many countries a shift in attitudes to
planning and especially public sector-led development. In central and eastern Europe, the very
idea of planning is not popular. In other countries, such as the UK, strategic spatial planning
has enjoyed a revival after a period of mainly project-led approaches, but this is difﬁcult to
establish in a culture dominated by a regulatory style of planning and strong sectoral interests.
In countries where national and regional planning has been more concerned with cohesion
objectives and balanced development, such as in France and Germany, the inﬂuence of neoliberal
governmental attitudes are now being felt in planning. The renaming of the French national
planning agency from DATAR to DIACT is, for example, more than a purely semantic move
from ‘aménagement du territoire et action régionale’ (national spatial planning and regional
action) to ‘aménagement et compétitivité des territoires’ (spatial planning and competitiveness);
rather, it symbolises a more fundamental shift in thinking. In countries that traditionally did
not have a strong strategic planning system, forms of regional and sub-regional strategic planning are now being set up to deal with the problems that previous years of largely unregulated
urbanisation had caused, for example, in some autonomous Communities of Spain, such as
Catalonia. And following a phase of spatial planning with a decidedly international outlook
during the 1990s in pioneering countries such as Denmark and the Netherlands spatial planning
has become more inward-looking. Concerns over domestic governance arrangements (prompted
by decentralisation, or local or regional government reforms) have become dominant, while the
international dimension has been reduced largely to considerations of economic competitiveness

## S. 421

in the comparative perspective. The long-standing variations in how planning is understood and
conducted in EU countries still stand, but now with varying degrees of consideration given to
strategic forms of planning. Convergence of approaches is not expected or necessary, but if the
spatial impacts of sector policies and the qualities of place are to ﬁnd a ﬁrmer foothold in the
EU agenda, then more consensus is needed on the role, scope and appropriate tools for spatial
planning at that level.
From a pan-European perspective, there are several trends that would call for stronger spatial
planning intervention – not least climate change and the resulting need to reconsider urban
development patterns. Other trends, such as the international economic competitiveness of the
EU, are used to argue for a pruning of planning and regulation in favour of market solutions,
though the banking crisis that emerged from 2008 has rather undermined these arguments. In
short, if the beneﬁts of spatial planning are recognised by EU member states, then this is likely
to colour the debates at EU level towards more spatial coordination. If, however, the neoliberal
turn gains dominance in the member states and domestic planning systems are increasingly under
pressure, then the future of European spatial planning will look considerably less promising.
Spatial planning at EU level will also be inﬂuenced by how the ‘European integration
project’ proceeds, and whether the institutional stalemate of the past years can be overcome and
decision-making structures facilitated, as foreseen by the Lisbon Treaty. The future of the
European project involves debates about institutional reforms, about further economic and
political integration (and the pace thereof), and about the ‘democratic deﬁcit’ and the need to
bring the EU closer to its citizens. Future enlargements of the EU may raise new issues, and at
the very least will further increase the diversity of planning systems and policies in the EU. In
2002, Zetter prophesied that diversity would become more important in the EU policy agenda
and that this in turn would give a role for European spatial planning in measuring the impact
of EU policy on different places and making best use of territorial capital with the European
Commission and the old member states paying more attention to spatial planning in order to
retain some control over the spending of regional funding. While the latter remains to be seen,
the complexity of policy- and decision-making in the EU, and the sharing of power and
competences between four (or in some countries up to six) levels of government makes it very
clear that the future of European spatial planning, however deﬁned, is inextricably linked to
questions of territorial governance.
The hopes of Commission ofﬁcials, members of the European Parliament and national
government experts who believe in the value of a spatial development framework for the EU
are now pinned on the notion of territorial cohesion. Much will depend on how this is deﬁned
and interpreted in relation to EU Cohesion Policy post-2013. As discussed in Faludi (2007a),
the term is complex, ambiguous, and can serve various agendas, some more geared towards the
dominant ‘competitiveness discourse’, some more focused on territorial, spatial and social justice.
In any case the heyday of European spatial planning in the 1990s, when DG REGIO gave much
support to spatial development initiatives, seems to be over, with the enthusiasm of the
Commission stumped by fears of the member states over an expansion of EU competences. The
Commission’s efforts to achieve policy coordination are likely in future to be directed towards
instruments other than spatial planning, as the recent examples of the Maritime Strategy and
the EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region show. Spatial development still plays an important
role in these initiatives, but it is packaged into wider ambitions to improve territorial governance
in the EU, while also contributing to the EU’s growth and competitiveness agenda. And while
for large transnational territories ‘strategy’ is likely to play a strong role in the EU Cohesion
Policy of the future, it remains to be seen how far spatial planners will be engaged in the quest
for territorial cohesion or whether the emphasis will be more on ‘structural projects’ as in the
392
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 422

current 2007–13 programming period. The European Parliament has traditionally been a
strong supporter of a European spatial planning approach and transnational cooperation, but
it is not certain that it will remain a steady force in European spatial planning. Following the
2009 election the Parliament’s agenda has been recast in line with the priorities of the new
composition of members.
Alongside the support of key institutions to the European spatial planning agenda, continuation of Community funding for territorial cooperation is crucial – and is likely if it is fully
connected to the objective of territorial cohesion. Although the effects of INTERREG
cooperation appear to have been variable, EU funding has at the very least succeeded in engaging
local and regional authorities in the EU policy framework. This is expected to lead to processes
of Europeanisation, whereby the EU becomes an important frame of reference for action.
Cooperation with European partners is also expected to result in learning effects that can lead
to more institutionalised forms of cooperation and perhaps prompt policy change or institutional
adaptation in the participating organisations. Perhaps ultimately the scale of trans-European
cooperation will also lead to a rescaling of planning perspectives of those involved in territorial
cooperation projects, to take account of the transnational dimension of spatial planning more
effectively. Experiments with spatial visions and other strategic development frameworks at the
transnational level will prove important in agreeing on the key issues for future cooperation.
This is particularly so as transnational territorial cooperation is expected to play an important
role in implementing the EU’s objective of territorial cohesion post-2013, and this is likely to
involve a strategic approach to cooperation.
There are thus many factors that may inﬂuence the EU institutions in their attitude towards
transnational spatial planning. Yet the medium- to long-term impacts of trends at the end of
the 2000s, in particular the global banking crisis, are difﬁcult to foresee. One scenario is that
in a period of great uncertainty, parts of the private sector industry will increasingly value the
greater certainty provided by spatial planning strategies so as to limit the risks to their investments. Support for coordinated spatial planning approaches at pan-European level may also
come from other large-scale development trends, notably climate change, demographic change
and immigration, and from related policy debates such as the sustainable urban development
agenda. Moreover, as Kunzmann (2008: 13) has argued ‘energy geopolitics may, in the end, be
more inﬂuential on territorial development than national state policies or the European Union
when it comes to inﬂuencing mobility patterns and residential behaviour’. If the dominant 
trends of metropolisation, fragmentation and polarisation in the core of Europe, depopulation
of rural areas, adaptation to the effects of climate change and mitigation of its sources are to
be addressed, then coordinated and integrated responses need to be found, and this will require
considerable political support and powerful instruments.
European spatial planning and territorial cooperation 
as a field of research
European spatial planning and territorial cooperation is far from being a coherent ﬁeld of study.
There is at present no comprehensive overview of the effects of territorial cooperation on spatial
planning systems, policies and practices, and, consequently, the inﬂuence of the Union and of
EU policy on spatial development patterns and planning systems in the member states is not
always well understood. The ESPON programme has contributed to increasing our understanding of spatial development trends in Europe and the territorial inﬂuences of EU policies,
but the results of the projects have been variable. Moreover, as Kunzmann (2008: 20) points
out, ‘despite a plethora of academic publications on urban and regional development our knowledge of the various factors inﬂuencing spatial development in Europe remains limited’.

## S. 423

Generally speaking, research efforts in the ﬁeld of European spatial planning and territorial
cooperation have to date been mostly based on individual case studies or bi- and multilateral
comparisons. What this variety of individual research results means is that the available
empirical material often merely allows an illustration of certain points in individual locations,
but does not really lend itself to gain a comprehensive understanding of the variety of responses
to EU inﬂuences.
The focus on case studies and individual and partial analyses is understandable from the
researcher’s perspective, as comprehensive analyses have considerable resource implications.
Moreover, the European inﬂuences on planning are complex and methodologically not easy to
separate and measure. Planning systems are dynamic and subject to change, and while this may
sometimes be in response to EU ideas or through mutual learning processes, it is difﬁcult to
isolate different sources of inspiration that lead to planning reform or more subtle changes in
practices or policies.
Overall, however, the scale of research activity under way in the ﬁeld of European spatial
planning and territorial cooperation adds up to a considerable collective effort. After two
decades of practical experiences and research efforts, more conceptual and analytical clarity to
understanding the role of the EU and neighbouring countries on shaping planning systems,
practices and policies would be desirable. There is research that should be done on ‘new’
substantive issues on which we have limited understanding. Kunzmann (2008: 20), for example,
argued for more analysis ‘on the complex energy issue of mobility and logistic ﬂows, on the
implications of geopolitical developments on space, or the inﬂuential role of value systems on
locational choice and on bridging sector policies with spatial policies’.
But besides research in planning (on substantive issues), there is also a need to do more
research on planning, in particular its role at the transnational and European level. Here, the
thinking about spatial planning is strongly inﬂuenced by the different planning cultures in the
EU, but spatial planning at EU level is much more complex and less clearly regulated by legal,
regulatory and ﬁnancial frameworks than within member states. Consequently, research efforts
could be directed, for example, at evaluating the impact of spatial planning practice more,
investigating ways of cooperation, and comparing the approaches of different systems and
cultures in inﬂuencing European and transnational initiatives.
It will also be important to develop more appropriate conceptual and theoretical frameworks
and practical methodologies that are capable of grasping the dynamics and processes of
‘Europeanisation’ (understood in a wide sense as deﬁned in Chapter 8), and the impact of
cooperation programmes and the ‘circulation of spatial planning ideas’ in Europe on various
levels of planning in the member states. In this, researchers should strive for scientiﬁc rigour,
to avoid repeating the currently fashionable discourses of EU institutions on the value of
exchanges of good practice without solid evidence of the processes of social learning and policy
transfer that may (or may not) result from this. This will require more dialogue between
researchers from different disciplines, e.g. planning, political science and public policy, sociology
and geography, among others.
Where to, European spatial planning and territorial 
cooperation?
It is impossible to end this book with ﬁrm conclusions, especially in regard to possible future
trajectories. Much will depend on the combination of different factors, as explained above.
Different scenarios about the future are possible. So we prefer to end with quoting Klaus
Kunzmann, recently retired professor of European spatial planning, who suggested that:
394
Towards new forms of territorial governance
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 424

Planners should remain humble and patient, as well as ambitious and committed. On the
one hand they should acknowledge that their power to guide spatial development and to
defend greenﬁeld consumption against infrastructural development and property speculation
is quite limited. While on the other hand, they should not surrender to neo-liberal attitudes
and short-term incrementalism. It is still the task of planners to develop longer-term visions
and to seek spatial justice. Without allies in the ‘real’ world however their visions and
concerns will not be heard. Thus they must seek, wherever possible, to construct strategic
alliances with those who have more inﬂuence in society.
(Kunzmann 2008: 21)

## S. 426

Sources of EU official documents 
and information
Official EU documentation – online resources
The ofﬁcial European Union’s website, http://europa.eu/, has links to all up-to-date EU-related
information, documents and data. See in particular:
•
the ‘Easy reading’ corner, with accessible publications on the EU: http://ec.europa.eu/
publications/index_en.htm;
•
the gateway to all online EU documentation at http://europa.eu/documents/index_en.htm,
including links to: all EU law (Eur-Lex), legislation under discussion and case law;
documents common to all EU institutions; documents and reports by the individual
institutions (accessible via their respective websites).
Most EU publications are now freely available in electronic format online, but paper copies of
many publications can be ordered from the EU bookshop – http://bookshop.eu.int/ – or from
the Office of Official Publications of the European Communities at http://publications.
europa.eu/.
The Ofﬁcial Journal of the European Union (OJ) is the authoritative publication for all new
EU legislation and ofﬁcial texts. It is published every working day in all ofﬁcial languages of
the EU. It consists of two related series (L for legislation and C for information and notices)
and a supplement (S for public procurement). There is also an electronic section to the C series.
See http://publications.europa.eu/ofﬁcial/index_en.htm.
Ofﬁcial EU statistics can be accessed via the website of Eurostat, the Statistical Ofﬁce of the
European Communities at http://epp.eurostat.ec/europa.eu.
Europe Direct helps citizens to ﬁnd answers to questions about the EU and their rights:
http://ec.europa.eu/europedirect/.
Other useful online sources of European information
To ﬁnd relevant academic literature on EU issues and policies, Google Scholar or the two major
social sciences bibliographical databases – International Bibliography of the Social Sciences
(IBSS) and Social Sciences Citation Index (usually available electronically via university libraries)
– are useful starting points.
This online tutorial introduces how to use the internet and web resources for European
studies: www.intute.ac.uk/resources.html.
A list of EU grants and funding per topic can be found at http://ec.europa.eu/grants/
index_en.htm. A database of funding opportunities from the European Union for young people
and those who work with them is available at www.eurodesk.org/.

Many continental European planning systems make use of zoning, which effectively allocates
development rights through a binding regulation plan. Conversely, the UK systems, which have no
such zoning, are usually described as land use planning systems.
4
Spatial development trends in Europe
1
See also www.citymayors.com/statistics/urban_2006_1.html.
2
Neoliberalism is:
a theory of political economic practices that proposes that human well-being can best be
advanced by liberating individual entrepreneurial freedoms and skills within an institutional
framework characterized by strong private property rights, free markets and free trade. The role
of the state is to create and preserve an institutional framework appropriate to such practices. 
[. . .] State intervention in markets (once created) must be kept to a bare minimum because,
according to the theory, the state cannot possibly possess enough information to second-guess
market signals (prices) and because powerful interest groups will inevitably distort and bias state
interventions (particularly in democracies) for their own beneﬁt.
(Harvey 2005: 2).
3
The wider effects of large-scale transport infrastructure investments may only materialise in the
medium to longer term and may require additional action to fully reap their beneﬁts. For instance,
a study by the OECD in 2003 showed that deeper economic integration as a consequence of transport
infrastructure improvements were below expectations in the Öresund Region. The report identiﬁed
national differences in institutions and legislation as the main barriers to the integration of the two
labour markets. The toll, different currencies and the regulations of housing markets, as well as
mental barriers, were also considered to be of importance. The report concludes that efforts for
integration have to go beyond the provision of a ﬁxed link: ‘The new region has great potential but
increased physical accessibility per se is not sufﬁcient to reap the beneﬁts of cross-border integration’
(OECD 2003: 16). In general, however, it is difficult to isolate the economic effects of the
infrastructure development from accompanying measures such as public initiatives or business
integration.
4
It should be noted that there is no ofﬁcial map that shows the high-speed rail network in Europe.
There is one freely available at http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/07/High_Speed_
Railroad_Map_Europe_2008.gif. that comes closest to being comprehensive. Figure 4.2 is based on
this map, although it should be considered as indicative, as changes may occur, for instance following track upgrades.

## S. 429

5
After 15 years of negotiation, in 2007 agreement to construct the 18km Fehmarn Belt crossing across
the Baltic Sea to connect Germany and Denmark was ﬁnally reached. Construction will start in 2011
and is expected to be completed in 2018. The cost for the project is estimated to be EUR 5 billion.
The bridge will be ﬁnanced by state-guaranteed loans, which will be paid by the road and train fees.
Denmark will be solely responsible for guaranteeing the funding of the project, whereas German
participation will be limited to the development of the land-based facilities on the German side.
Denmark will own the bridge alone, allowed to keep the fees after the loans have been paid, and get
work opportunities for the toll station. Once completed, the bridge will shorten the rail journey from
Hamburg to Copenhagen to 3.5 hours. It is the largest planned infrastructure project in northern
Europe, and will be the main connection between Hamburg Metropolitan Region and the Öresund
Region (www.fehmarnlink.com/uk). It is, however, also expected that the Fehmarn Belt crossing will
increase the relative peripherality of Finland as Sweden and Denmark become better connected (see
Williams 1996: 92).
6
Spatial data and spatial information
1
There are frequent criticisms of the use of NUTS data to determine the eligibility of regions for EU
Cohesion Policy funding (see for instance Bachtler 1998).
7
European integration and the European Union as a political
system
1
In political science, a distinction is commonly drawn between the three political dimensions of polity,
politics and policy, and this is also a useful distinction for the purposes of this book and to
understand which theoretical approaches may be suitable to understand which aspects of the EU
political system. Polity refers to the formal dimension of politics, that is, the structure of norms, the
way in which procedures are regulated and the institutions in which politics takes place. In the
national context, the notion is frequently associated with ‘statehood’, which is characterised by
territoriality, sovereignty, legitimacy and the monopoly of governance. In the EU context, it refers
to the political community of the EU and its institutions. Policy is the substance-based dimension of
politics, whereas politics refers to the procedural dimension, or rather the decision-making processes
and the enforcing of goals and interests.
8
Theoretical approaches to EU governance and policy-making
1
In a parallel line of argument, Brown and Duguid (2001) propose the term ‘networks of practice’,
and Wenger (1998) the term ‘communities of practice’. These networks or communities of practice
are characterised by regular communication (e.g. through the exchange of best practices) through
which involved actors learn from one another. Epistemic communities, in comparison, may more
deliberately aim at creating new knowledge than communities or networks of practice (which are
more strongly aimed at increasing skills in a given practice).
10 Non-EU actors, organisations and networks
1
In the Commission’s European Governance: A White Paper (CEC 2001e) ‘civil society’ is deﬁned as
‘trade unions and employers’ organisations (social partners); non-governmental organisations;
professional associations; charities; grass-roots organisations; organisations that involve citizens in
local and municipal life with a particular contribution from churches and religious communities’.
2
Note that the European Council of Spatial Planners retains the abbreviation from its former title of
European Council of Town Planners (ECTP), which it is commonly known by.
3
The International Federation of Surveyors was set up in 1878 as the Fédération Internationale des
Géomètres and has members in more than 100 countries. Its work is allocated among 10 commissions, one of which is concerned with spatial planning and development. See www.ﬁg.net.
4
The Europa website includes a special section devoted to public consultations: ‘Your Voice in
Europe’. See http://ec.europa.eu/yourvoice/consultations/index_en.htm.
5
The openness of the policy process varies by sector. For example, in agriculture interest groups are
very well established, and the relationships with the EU are strongly institutionalised. In the relatively
new and low-proﬁle domains of territorial cooperation relationships are still being formed (see
Chapter 8 on the theoretical approach to policy networks).
400
Notes
Downloaded by [ETH Zurich] at 05:22 20 September 2016

## S. 430

6
The agencies that have been particularly important are the Délegation Interministérielle à l’Aménagement et à la Compétitivité des Territoires (DIACT), until 2005 known as the Délegation à
l’Aménagement du Territoire et à l’Action Régionale (DATAR), France; the Bundesamt für Bauwesen
und Raumordnung (BBR) and the Bundesministerium für Verkehr, Bau und Stadtentwicklung
(BMVBS), Germany; and the Ministerie van Volkshuisvesting, Ruimtelijke Ordening en Milieubeheer
(VROM), Netherlands.
7
The decision to go ahead with the preparation of the ESDP was made under the Belgian Presidency
in 1993. See Chapter 14.
12 Widening, deepening and broadening the EU
1
The term ‘eastern enlargement’ is used for practical purposes in this chapter to refer to the 2004 and
2007 enlargements, yet it should be noted that two Mediterranean countries, Cyprus and Malta, also
joined the EU in 2004.
15 The instruments: spatial strategies and visions
1
Strategic spatial strategies are more accurately referred to as being applied, rather than implemented.
For a discussion of the two concepts, see Chapter 13.
2
This is not just a semantic difference, as the concepts of ‘space’ and ‘territory’ are not interchangeable.
Schön (2005: 389) explains that space includes the concept of territory ‘but additionally addresses
spatial themes . . . like the spatial organisation of society, relationships between territories, ﬂows of
people and goods, networks, corridors, and settlement structures’. Space, accordingly, is related 
to analyses and future oriented strategic thinking (i.e. the notion of spatial planning for the EU
territory), whereas territory (including sovereignty over territory) is decisive for the implementation
of spatial or territorial policies. The main difference between the terms ‘spatial’ and ‘territorial’ can
thus be seen as relating to the competence over decisions affecting territories.
3
In the INTERREG IIC and IIIB funding periods, CADSES denoted the ‘Central European, Adriatic,
Danubian, South-Eastern European Space’ transnational cooperation area. In the funding period
2007–13, the CADSES transnational cooperation area was divided into two separate programme
areas: the Central Europe Programme (CENTRAL) and the South East European Space (SEES).
16 The instruments: territorial cooperation
1
At the Council of Europe’s initiative, the European Outline Convention on Transfrontier Cooperation
between Territorial Communities or Authorities concerning Interterritorial Cooperation came into
force on 21 May 1980 (Madrid). The Madrid Convention created a legal basis for intergovernmental
agreements, thereby promoting and facilitating cooperation between the regional administrative
bodies of the contract states. This umbrella agreement for the intensiﬁcation and advancement of
relations between neighbours is based on two principles: the inviolability of the regional
administrative bodies’ areas of responsibility, as determined by the states’ domestic laws (Article 2),
and the priority procedure of domestic law over the ﬁnal agreement (Article 3, paragraph 4). The
European Outline Convention was not set up as a direct legal basis for transnational cooperation,
and it therefore requires instruments in the form of further intergovernmental agreements. Examples
of such agreements include the Benelux agreement of 1986, the Anholt agreement of 1991 between
Germany and the Netherlands, the Vienna agreement of 1993 between Italy and Austria, the Rome
agreement of 1993, the Bayonne agreement of 1993 between France and Spain, the Mainz agreement
of 1996 (Germany–Belgium) as well as the Karlsruhe agreement of 1996 (France–Germany–
Luxembourg–Switzerland).
17 EU economic and competition policies
1
The Schengen zone is now composed of 25 EU member states (without the UK and Ireland) and three
non-EU countries, Iceland, Norway and Switzerland. Only half of these countries fully apply all the
provisions of the Schengen agreement, i.e. common border control and visa provisions.
2
The ESPON research programme paid surprisingly little attention to the territorial impact of EU
economic integration policies, apart from ESPON Study 3.4.2 (2006), which focused on simulations
and forecasts rather than the extensive analysis of past trends.

## S. 431

20 EU agriculture and rural development policy
1
The EU uses the OECD’s method for deﬁning rural areas. This involves two stages: ﬁrst, local units
with a population density of less than 150 inhabitants per square kilometer are deﬁned as rural.
Second, NUTS 2 and NUTS 3 regions are placed in three classes; predominantly rural if more than
50 per cent of the population are living in rural local units (less then 150 inhabitants per square
kilometre); intermediate if 15 to 50 per cent of the population live in rural local units; and
predominantly urban if less than 15 per cent live in local rural units. For a full explanation see the
Commission Report on Rural Development in the European Union: Statistical and Economic
Information (CEC 2008q: 3).
2
Figures are for 2007. Source is Eurostat (CEC 2008o). GVA is the difference between the value of
goods and services produced and the cost of the raw materials that went into their production. For
an explanation of EU-27 and EU-15 see Chapter 3.
3
The requirement for the environment to be considered in agricultural policy is met by other Treaty
provisions. The Lisbon Treaty carries forward the Treaty of Rome provisions word for word.
4
The information here is a very brief summary of complex regulations. The sources are the ‘fact sheets’
provided by DG Agriculture and Rural Development and available on its website at http://ec.europa.
eu/agriculture/capreform.
5
Many examples of Leader projects are given on the website of the European Observatory of Rural
Areas at http://ec.europa/leaderplus, which also has details of various publications setting out good
practices in rural development.
22 The relevance of European transboundary cooperation 
for spatial planning
1
The content of this chapter is largely inspired by previous work by Dühr and Nadin (2007) 
and Colomb (2007) in a special issue of the journal Planning Practice and Research on the
Europeanisation of spatial planning.

## S. 485

Blue Banana (Brunet 1989)
Golden Triangle (Williams 1993)
Central Regions (CEC 2001h)
Pentagon (Schön 2000, CEC 2007b)
Plate 6 Core-periphery models of Europe: shifting conceptualisations of the core
Source: Base map: Europe at night (urban areas visualised by light sources), courtesy NASA/Goddard Space Flight Center
Scientiﬁc Visualization Studio, 2001, downloaded from the Visible Earth Website at http://visibleearth.nasa.gov. Modiﬁed by
the authors.
Note: the Golden Triangle is deﬁned by London, the Ruhr and Paris. The Central Regions are identiﬁed in the Second
Cohesion Report (CEC 2001h) as bounding 14 per cent of the EU-15 land area, 33 per cent of its population and 47 per cent
of its GDP. The Pentagon, bounded by London, Hamburg, Milan, Munich and Paris, covers 20 per cent of the EU-15 land 
area, 40 per cent of its population and 50 per cent of its GDP (Schön 2000 and also Fourth Cohesion Report, CEC 2007b). 
A variation in the Third Cohesion Report (CEC 2004a) includes northern England.
